topics.title: Object Oriented Development, Modeling Concepts, and Object Oriented Methodology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCNT604 - OBJECT ORIENTED MODELING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module - 1
> - **Topic:** Object Oriented Development, Modeling Concepts, and Object Oriented Methodology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Object Oriented Development (OOD)

**Formal KTU Definition:**
> **Object Oriented Development (OOD)** is a software development paradigm that views a system as a collection of interacting **objects**, where each object encapsulates **state (attributes)** and **behavior (operations)**, organized around **classes** and **inheritance hierarchies**, rather than around functions and procedures (as in structured development).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** OOD is the umbrella process that integrates *Object Oriented Analysis (OOA)*, *Object Oriented Design (OOD)*, and *Object Oriented Programming (OOP)* into a unified, iterative life cycle.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of building a city. You don't build a city by first constructing "all the walls," "all the roads," and "all the lights" separately (that is the **structured/function-oriented** approach). Instead, you identify the real-world entities (**objects**) — Houses, Cars, TrafficLights, People — give each one its own blueprint (**class**), and let them interact with one another.

In the same way, OO development looks at the *problem domain* and identifies nouns (objects) first, then defines what those objects *know* (data) and what they *do* (functions). For example, in a banking system:
- `Account` is a class.
- `mySavingsAccount` is an **object** (instance).
- `balance` is an **attribute**; `deposit()` and `withdraw()` are **operations**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Pillars of OO Development (must memorize for KTU):**
> 1. **Encapsulation** – binding data + operations together and hiding internals.
> 2. **Inheritance** – building new classes from existing ones ("is-a" relationship).
> 3. **Polymorphism** – same interface, different behavior at runtime.

---

### 1.2 Modeling Concepts

**Formal KTU Definition:**
> **Modeling** is the process of creating *abstractions* of a real-world system so that we can understand, communicate, and reason about it *before* committing to expensive code. A **model** is a *simplified, purposeful representation* of a system, written in a well-defined **modeling language** (e.g., UML).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why do we model?** Three classical reasons, as taught in KTU Module 1:
> 1. **To manage complexity** – Every real system is too big to hold in the head; a model lets us focus on *one slice* at a time.
> 2. **To communicate unambiguously** – A picture (or a UML diagram) is shared precisely between developer, designer, and client.
> 3. **To verify correctness early** – Errors caught in a model cost **~10×–100× less** to fix than errors caught in code.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
A model in software is exactly like a **blueprint** of a building. The blueprint is:
- *Cheaper* to modify than concrete.
- *Easier* to share with the architect, the engineer, and the client.
- *Abstract* — it shows only the essential details (load-bearing walls, wiring) and hides cosmetic details (paint color, curtains).

Just as a civil engineer uses blueprints, a software engineer uses **UML models** (class diagrams, sequence diagrams, use-case diagrams, etc.).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Four Characteristics of a Good Model (Booch/Rumbaugh/Jacobson):**
> 1. **Abstraction** – hides irrelevant details.
> 2. **Accuracy** – correctly represents the system.
> 3. **Predictive Power** – lets us reason about future behavior.
> 4. **Completeness (within scope)** – covers everything important, nothing more.

---

### 1.3 Object Oriented Methodology (OOM)

**Formal KTU Definition:**
> **Object Oriented Methodology (OOM)** is a systematic, repeatable *engineering discipline* that prescribes **how** to perform object oriented analysis, design, and implementation using well-defined phases, artifacts, and notation. It is a *process* (not a notation); a notation like **UML** is a tool that supports the methodology.

**The three historical OO Methodologies that the syllabus expects you to know:**

| Year | Methodology | Author | Notation | Key Strength |
|------|-------------|--------|----------|--------------|
| **1991** | **OMT (Object Modeling Technique)** | James Rumbaugh | Object/Class, Dynamic, Functional | Strong in **Analysis**, especially data modeling |
| **1994** | **Booch Method** | Grady Booch | Class & Object, State, Module, Process, Interaction | Strong in **Design & Implementation**, micro-process |
| **1992** | **OOSE (Object Oriented Software Engineering)** | Ivar Jacobson | Use-Case driven | Strong in **Requirements & Testing** (use cases) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Unified Methodology → UML (1997):**
> Rumbaugh, Booch, and Jacobson (the "Three Amigos" at **Rational Software**) unified their methods into the **Unified Modeling Language (UML)** under the **Object Management Group (OMG)**. UML is the *de facto* standard OO modeling notation taught across all KTU 2024 modules.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Three master chefs each had their own signature recipe (OMT, Booch, OOSE). Instead of forcing the world to pick one, they combined the **best ingredients** of all three into a single universal recipe book (UML). Any cook (developer) in the world can now follow the same recipe.

---

### 1.4 Why Move From Structured to Object Oriented? (Frequently asked in KTU)

> [!NOTE]
> **Limitations of Structured (Function-Oriented) Development** (DFDs, ER, Structure Charts):
> - Functions and data are separated → **low cohesion, high coupling**.
> - Hard to **reuse** — a function in one module cannot easily become part of another.
> - Difficult to reflect **real-world entities** in the design.
> - **Maintenance nightmare** — small data structure changes ripple through many functions.

> OO development solves all of these by binding data + behavior into a single unit (the **class/object**) and by mirroring the problem domain directly in the solution domain.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 Phases of the Object Oriented Development Life Cycle

The OO development process is **iterative and incremental**, not waterfall. The KTU syllabus (Module 1) prescribes these five overlapping phases:

1. **Object Oriented Analysis (OOA)** – *What* the system must do.
2. **Object Oriented Design (OOD)** – *How* the system will be built.
3. **Object Oriented Implementation / Programming (OOP)** – *Code* the design.
4. **Object Oriented Testing (OOT)** – *Verify* it works.
5. **Object Oriented Maintenance** – *Evolve* it over time.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Think of building a car. OOA is deciding "it must carry 5 people at 150 kmph" (requirements on objects: Car, Engine, Wheel). OOD is the engineering drawing of the engine block. OOP is the factory floor. OOT is the test track. Maintenance is the service center.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step: OOA → OOD → OOP

| Phase | Core Question | Main Artifacts | Notation |
|-------|---------------|----------------|----------|
| **OOA** | What are the objects and their responsibilities? | Use-Case Diagram, Class Diagram (analysis level), Object Diagram, Activity Diagram | UML |
| **OOD** | How will objects collaborate? What is the architecture? | Class Diagram (design level), Sequence Diagram, Component Diagram, Deployment Diagram | UML |
| **OOP** | Convert the design into executable code. | Source files in C++/Java/Python | Language syntax |

### 2.3 The OO Modeling Triad (Rumbaugh's OMT)

Rumbaugh's OMT (still heavily tested in KTU) defines **three orthogonal views** of a system:

1. **Object Model** → *Static, structural view* — the **class diagram** (objects, classes, attributes, operations, relationships: association, aggregation, inheritance).
2. **Dynamic Model** → *Temporal, behavioral view* — **state diagrams** and **event trace diagrams** (states of an object, transitions, events).
3. **Functional Model** → *Transformational, data-flow view* — **data flow diagrams (DFDs)** (what inputs become what outputs, with no regard to *when* or *who*).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rumbaugh's Master Rule:** A complete OMT specification requires **all three models** together. The Object Model shows *what* exists, the Dynamic Model shows *when* it changes, and the Functional Model shows *how values change*. No single model is sufficient.

### 2.4 OMT Object Model — Core Concepts (Frequently asked as 7-mark questions)

- **Class** – A template/blueprint for objects. e.g., `Student`.
- **Object (Instance)** – A concrete occurrence. e.g., `student1: Student`.
- **Attribute** – A named property. e.g., `name: String`, `rollNo: int`.
- **Operation (Method)** – A function the object performs. e.g., `calculateCGPA()`.
- **Link** – A physical or conceptual connection between two object instances.
- **Association** – The class-level description of a set of links. Notation: a line between two classes, optionally with a **role name** and a **multiplicity** (1, *, 0..1, 1..*).
- **Aggregation** – A *whole–part* "has-a" relationship where parts can exist without the whole (weaker ownership). Hollow diamond ◇.
- **Composition** – A stronger "has-a" relationship where parts die with the whole. Filled diamond ◆.
- **Inheritance (Generalization/Specialization)** – An "is-a" relationship. Hollow triangle △ pointing to the parent.
- **Polymorphism** – Same operation name, different implementations in subclasses.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet (Symbol Table)

| Concept | UML Notation | Multiplicity Example | Meaning |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|---------|
| Association | ————— | `1` — *`0..*` | One student enrolls in many courses |
| Aggregation | ———◇ | `1` — *`1..*` | Department has-many Faculty (faculty can belong to multiple depts) |
| Composition | ———◆ | `1` — *`1..*` | Order has-many OrderLines (line dies with order) |
| Generalization | ———△ | (none) | SavingsAccount **is-a** BankAccount |
| Dependency | - - ->> | (none) | Class A *uses* Class B temporarily |
| Realization | - - -△ | (none) | Class *implements* an Interface |
| Association Class | ———[Class] | | A `Registered` class attached to a Student–Course link |

### 2.6 OOSE — Use-Case Driven Methodology (Jacobson)

Jacobson argued that the user is the most important abstraction. His methodology revolves around the **use case**:

- **Actor** – An external entity (person, system, time) interacting with the system. Stick-figure icon.
- **Use Case** – A sequence of actions the system performs to yield an *observable result of value* to a particular actor. Ellipse icon.
- **Scenario** – A specific path through a use case (a concrete instance).
- **Use-Case Diagram** – Shows actors, use cases, and the *system boundary* (a rectangle).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use cases drive everything downstream in OOSE:** the use case → analysis objects → design objects → implementation → *test cases* (one test per use-case scenario).

### 2.7 Booch Method — Micro Development Process

Booch emphasized that OO development is **cyclic**:
1. Identify the classes and objects at a given level of abstraction.
2. Identify their semantics (meaning and behavior).
3. Identify their relationships.
4. Specify each class's **interface** and **implementation**.

Booch invented the famous **cloud** notation for classes and was the first to formally distinguish **class categories**: *kind-of* (inheritance), *part-of* (aggregation), *instance-of* (classification), *association*, and *uses*.

### 2.8 Real-World Utility in Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> **Where OO Development is used today (industry relevance for KTU viva/project defense):**
> - **Enterprise software** – Banking, ERP, CRM systems.
> - **Embedded & Real-time systems** – Automotive ECU software (AUTOSAR uses OO concepts).
> - **Simulation & Modeling** – Flight simulators, weather models.
> - **Game Development** – Game engines (Unreal, Unity) are pure OO.
> - **AI/ML frameworks** – TensorFlow and PyTorch model neural networks as objects (Layer, Optimizer, Dataset).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Deriving the Class Signature from a Real-World Problem (Worked Example)

This is a classic 7-mark KTU question: *"Given the following problem statement, identify the classes, attributes, operations, and relationships."*

**Problem Statement (typical KTU):**
> *"A university has many departments. Each department offers several courses. A student may enroll in one or more courses. A faculty member teaches one or more courses and belongs to exactly one department."*

**Step 1 — Identify the Nouns (Candidate Classes):**
- University, Department, Course, Student, Faculty.

**Step 2 — Eliminate Weak Classes:**
A class should be a *cohesive* entity with both state and behavior. "University" is usually an aggregate; "Student", "Faculty", "Course", "Department" are strong. We keep all five for completeness.

**Step 3 — Identify Attributes (Adjectives / Noun-Phrases):**

$$
\text{Department} = \{\text{deptId}, \text{deptName}, \text{location}\}
$$

$$
\text{Course} = \{\text{courseId}, \text{courseName}, \text{credits}\}
$$

$$
\text{Student} = \{\text{studentId}, \text{name}, \text{dob}, \text{cgpa}\}
$$

$$
\text{Faculty} = \{\text{facultyId}, \text{name}, \text{designation}\}
$$

**Step 4 — Identify Operations (Verbs / Verb-Phrases):**

$$
\text{Student}.\text{operations} = \{\text{enroll(course)}, \text{drop(course)}, \text{viewGrades}()\}
$$

$$
\text{Faculty}.\text{operations} = \{\text{assignCourse(course)}, \text{enterMarks(student, grade)}\}
$$

$$
\text{Course}.\text{operations} = \{\text{getSyllabus}(), \text{addPrereq(course)}\}
$$

**Step 5 — Identify Relationships (Verbs between nouns):**

| Subject | Verb | Object | Multiplicity | UML |
|---------|------|--------|--------------|-----|
| University | has | Department | 1 to 1..* | Composition ◆ |
| Department | offers | Course | 1 to 1..* | Composition ◆ |
| Department | employs | Faculty | 1 to 1..* | Aggregation ◇ |
| Student | enrolls in | Course | * to * | Association |
| Faculty | teaches | Course | * to * | Association |

**Step 6 — Identify Generalization (if any):**
"Faculty" could be generalized as `Faculty → Professor, AssociateProfessor, AssistantProfessor`. Likewise, `Student → UGStudent, PGStudent`.

**Step 7 — Draw the Class Diagram (rendered in Mermaid in Section 4).**

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparing the Three Methodologies (High-Yield Comparison Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matrix is the most frequently asked **comparison** in KTU 2024 Module-1 questions. Memorize the bolded entries.

| Criterion | **OMT (Rumbaugh)** | **Booch** | **OOSE (Jacobson)** | **Unified / UML (R+B+J)** |
|-----------|--------------------|-----------|---------------------|---------------------------|
| **Primary Focus** | **Analysis (data model)** | **Design & Implementation (micro process)** | **Requirements & Test (use cases)** | **All phases, language-agnostic** |
| **Notation Style** | Object/Class, Dynamic, Functional models | Class, State, Module, Process, Interaction | Use cases, Block, Interaction, State | **UML 2.x — 14 diagram types** |
| **Process Model** | Waterfall-like (Analysis → Design → Implementation) | **Cyclic & iterative** (micro–macro) | **Use-case driven, architecture-centric** | **Use-case driven, architecture-centric, iterative, incremental (4 pillars)** |
| **Strongest Artifact** | Object Model (class diagram) | Class & Object Diagrams with **clouds** | Use-Case Diagram | Use-Case + Class + Sequence |
| **Use of States** | Yes — Dynamic Model is a state diagram | Yes — state transition diagrams | Yes | Yes |
| **Use of DFDs** | Yes — Functional Model | No | No | Replaced by Activity Diagrams |
| **Author Affiliation** | GE / then Rational | Rational | Ericsson / then Rational | **Rational → IBM → OMG (standard)** |
| **Year Published** | **1991** | **1994** (Booch'94) | **1992** | **1997 (UML 1.0)** |

---

### 3.3 Deriving the Three Models of OMT from a Single Problem

**Problem:** A *Ticket Vending Machine* accepts coins, shows the running total, and issues a ticket when the amount reaches ₹20.

**Derivation — Step by Step:**

**Step A: Object Model (Static, "What exists?")**
$$
\text{Classes} = \{\text{Machine}, \text{CoinSlot}, \text{Display}, \text{TicketDispenser}\}
$$

$$
\text{Attributes of Machine} = \{\text{currentTotal : int}, \text{ticketPrice : const = 20}\}
$$

**Step B: Dynamic Model (Behavioral, "When does state change?")**
States of `Machine`:
$$
\text{States} = \{\text{Idle}, \text{AcceptingCoins}, \text{DispensingTicket}, \text{OutOfOrder}\}
$$

Transitions:
$$
\text{Idle} \xrightarrow{\text{coinInserted}} \text{AcceptingCoins}
$$

$$
\text{AcceptingCoins} \xrightarrow{\text{total} \geq 20} \text{DispensingTicket}
$$

$$
\text{DispensingTicket} \xrightarrow{\text{ticketEjected}} \text{Idle}
$$

**Step C: Functional Model (Transformational, "How do values change?")**
DFD processes: `addCoin(value)`, `accumulateTotal()`, `checkPrice()`, `ejectTicket()`. Inputs: coin value. Outputs: ticket, change.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why all three?** The Object Model tells us *what* components exist; the Dynamic Model tells us *when* they change; the Functional Model tells us *how* their values are computed. Dropping any one gives an incomplete specification.

---

### 3.4 Deriving the Unified Process Phases (Iterative)

The **Unified Process (UP)** is iterative across four phases:

$$
\text{Inception} \rightarrow \text{Elaboration} \rightarrow \text{Construction} \rightarrow \text{Transition}
$$

Each iteration within a phase traverses the five disciplines (workflows):
$$
\text{Requirements} \rightarrow \text{Analysis} \rightarrow \text{Design} \rightarrow \text{Implementation} \rightarrow \text{Test}
$$

> **Reading Guide:** A vertical slice (e.g., Iteration 3 in Construction) touches all five workflows. This is *exactly* why OO development is **iterative and incremental** — you don't finish requirements and *then* start design; you do a *little* of each in every iteration.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The OO Development Life Cycle (High-Level Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Problem Statement] --> B[Object Oriented Analysis OOA]
    B --> C[Object Oriented Design OOD]
    C --> D[Object Oriented Implementation OOP]
    D --> E[Object Oriented Testing OOT]
    E --> F[Maintenance]
    F -->|New Requirements| B
    B --> G[Use Case Diagram]
    B --> H[Class Diagram Analysis]
    C --> I[Class Diagram Design]
    C --> J[Sequence Diagram]
    C --> K[Component Diagram]
    D --> L[Source Code]
    E --> M[Test Cases from Use Cases]
```

### 4.2 Rumbaugh's OMT — Three Orthogonal Models

```mermaid
graph LR
    P[Problem Domain] --> OM[Object Model - WHAT exists]
    P --> DM[Dynamic Model - WHEN states change]
    P --> FM[Functional Model - HOW values transform]
    OM --> CD[Class Diagram]
    DM --> SD[State Diagram]
    FM --> DFD[Data Flow Diagram]
    OM <--> DM
    DM <--> FM
    OM <--> FM
```

### 4.3 The Three Amigos — Unification Timeline

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1[Rumbaugh OMT 1991] --> U[Unified Method 1995]
    B1[Booch Booch94 1994] --> U
    C1[Jacobson OOSE 1992] --> U
    U --> UML1[UML 1.0 1997 OMG Standard]
    UML1 --> UML2[UML 2.x Current Standard]
```

### 4.4 Class Diagram for the University Example (Worked in Section 3.1)

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class University {
        -name : String
        -location : String
        +addDepartment()
    }
    class Department {
        -deptId : int
        -deptName : String
        +addCourse()
        +addFaculty()
    }
    class Course {
        -courseId : int
        -courseName : String
        -credits : int
        +getSyllabus()
    }
    class Student {
        -studentId : int
        -name : String
        -cgpa : float
        +enroll(Course)
        +drop(Course)
    }
    class Faculty {
        -facultyId : int
        -name : String
        -designation : String
        +teach(Course)
    }
    University "1" *-- "1..*" Department : composes
    Department "1" *-- "1..*" Course : offers
    Department "1" o-- "1..*" Faculty : employs
    Student "0..*" -- "0..*" Course : enrolls in
    Faculty "0..*" -- "0..*" Course : teaches
```

### 4.5 Use-Case Diagram for the University System (OOSE Style)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph System [University System]
        UC1((Enroll in Course))
        UC2((View Grades))
        UC3((Assign Course))
        UC4((Enter Marks))
        UC5((Pay Fees))
    end
    StudentActor((Student)) --- UC1
    StudentActor --- UC2
    StudentActor --- UC5
    FacultyActor((Faculty)) --- UC3
    FacultyActor --- UC4
    UC1 -.extends.-> UC5
```

### 4.6 State Diagram of a `BankAccount` Object (Dynamic Model)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Active : openAccount
    Active --> Suspended : detectFraud
    Suspended --> Active : clearDues
    Active --> Closed : customerRequest
    Active --> Overdrawn : withdrawOverBalance
    Overdrawn --> Active : deposit
    Closed --> [*]
```

### 4.7 Comparison Block — Structured vs Object Oriented Development

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph Structured [Structured / Function Oriented]
        S1[Decompose by FUNCTION]
        S2[Top Down]
        S3[DFD, ER, Structure Charts]
        S4[Data and Functions are SEPARATE]
    end
    subgraph OO [Object Oriented]
        O1[Decompose by OBJECTS]
        O2[Bottom Up and Top Down]
        O3[UML Diagrams]
        O4[Data and Functions are TOGETHER inside class]
    end
    Structured -.->|Limitations: low reuse, hard maintenance| OO
```

### 4.8 Iterative Unified Process (Phases × Disciplines)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Phases [Phases]
        P1[Inception]
        P2[Elaboration]
        P3[Construction]
        P4[Transition]
    end
    subgraph Disciplines [Disciplines per Iteration]
        D1[Requirements]
        D2[Analysis]
        D3[Design]
        D4[Implementation]
        D5[Test]
    end
    P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4
    D1 --> D2 --> D3 --> D4 --> D5
```

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**Define Object Oriented Development. List any two advantages of OO development over structured development.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Object Oriented Development (OOD)** is a software development paradigm that organizes a system as a collection of cooperating **objects**, where each object is an instance of a class that bundles together **data (attributes)** and **behavior (operations)**. It proceeds through the phases of *Object Oriented Analysis (OOA), Object Oriented Design (OOD), and Object Oriented Programming (OOP)*.

**Two advantages over structured development:**
1. **Better Reusability** – Classes can be inherited and reused across projects; structured functions typically cannot.
2. **Closer Mapping to Real World** – Objects directly correspond to real-world entities, making the system easier to understand, model, and maintain.
3. *(bonus)* **Encapsulation** improves security; **polymorphism** enables extensibility.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark per advantage — any 2 of the above.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
**What is a model? Why do we model a system before building it? Mention any two UML diagrams.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **model** is a *simplified, purposeful abstraction* of a system, expressed in a well-defined notation, that captures the essential features while hiding irrelevant detail.

**Why we model (any two):**
1. To **manage complexity** of large systems.
2. To **communicate** unambiguously among stakeholders.
3. To **verify** the design *before* costly implementation.

**Two UML diagrams:** Class Diagram, Use-Case Diagram, Sequence Diagram, State Diagram, Activity Diagram, Component Diagram, Deployment Diagram. *(Any two — ½ mark each.)*

*(1 mark definition + 1 mark for the two reasons + ½ × 2 = 1 mark for diagrams.)*

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Rule:** Module 1 carries approximately 14–18 marks. Two sub-parts of 7 marks each are typical.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, modified]*

**(a)** Compare the **OMT, Booch, and Jacobson (OOSE)** methodologies along **six** criteria of your choice. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain the **three models of OMT (Object, Dynamic, Functional)** with a suitable example. (7 marks)

---

##### MODEL SOLUTION — Q A (a) — Comparison (7 marks)

| Criterion | OMT (Rumbaugh) | Booch | OOSE (Jacobson) |
|-----------|----------------|-------|-----------------|
| **Year** | 1991 | 1994 | 1992 |
| **Author** | James Rumbaugh | Grady Booch | Ivar Jacobson |
| **Primary Strength** | Analysis (data) | Design & Implementation | Requirements & Testing |
| **Key Notations** | Object, Dynamic, Functional Models | Class, State, Module, Process, Interaction | Use-Case, Block, Interaction |
| **Process Style** | Waterfall-like | Cyclic & iterative | Use-case driven |
| **Symbol Innovation** | Hollow/Filled diamonds for aggregation/composition | The "Cloud" class symbol | Stick-figure actor, ellipse use case |

**Key differences to mention (pick any 4–6, 1 mark each):**
- OMT emphasized the **data** perspective; Booch emphasized **design/architecture**; Jacobson emphasized the **user/actor** perspective.
- OMT explicitly used DFDs in the Functional Model; Booch and OOSE replaced DFDs with **activity/interaction** diagrams.
- OMT's class diagrams did not distinguish *aggregation* from *composition* in the original 1991 version — this was refined in Booch'94.
- Jacobson's use-case approach **drives both analysis and test-case generation**, which the other two did not make explicit.

---

##### MODEL SOLUTION — Q A (b) — Three Models of OMT (7 marks)

> **Definition (2 marks):** OMT specifies a system using three orthogonal, complementary models that must be developed in parallel.

**1. Object Model (2 marks)**
- Captures the **static, structural** view: classes, objects, attributes, operations, and relationships (association, aggregation, inheritance).
- Example: A `Library` system has classes `Book`, `Member`, `Librarian`, `Loan`. `Member` *associates with* `Loan` (0..*), `Loan` *associates with* `Book` (1..1).

**2. Dynamic Model (2 marks)**
- Captures the **temporal, behavioral** view: states, events, transitions.
- Example: A `Loan` object has states `Requested → Approved → Active → Returned → Closed`. A `returnBook` event triggers transition from `Active` to `Returned`.

**3. Functional Model (1 mark)**
- Captures the **transformational** view: how data values are computed.
- Example: A DFD showing process `computeFine(returnDate, dueDate)` with input `daysLate` and output `fineAmount = daysLate × 2`.

> **Conclusion (½ mark):** The three models are *complementary*; the Object Model shows *what* exists, the Dynamic Model shows *when* state changes, and the Functional Model shows *how* values are derived.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *[Alternative Choice]*

**(a)** With a suitable example, explain the **Object Oriented Development life cycle** in detail. (7 marks)

**(b)** What is a **use case**? Draw a **use-case diagram** for an **Online Shopping System** with at least *three* actors and *five* use cases. Identify two *extends* or *includes* relationships. (7 marks)

---

##### MODEL SOLUTION — Q B (a) — OO Development Life Cycle (7 marks)

> **Intro (1 mark):** The OO Development life cycle is an *iterative, incremental* process consisting of five major phases: OOA, OOD, OOP, OOT, and Maintenance.

**1. Object Oriented Analysis (OOA) — 1 mark**
- Goal: identify the *objects* and *classes* in the problem domain.
- Example: For an **ATM** system — classes: `ATM`, `CardReader`, `CashDispenser`, `Account`, `Transaction`. Operations: `validatePIN()`, `withdrawCash(amount)`.

**2. Object Oriented Design (OOD) — 1.5 marks**
- Goal: define the *internal structure* of classes, the *collaborations* between them, and the *architecture*.
- Sub-steps:
  - System Design: split into subsystems (e.g., `UI Layer`, `Business Logic`, `Data Layer`).
  - Object Design: refine class interfaces, choose data structures, design algorithms.

**3. Object Oriented Implementation (OOP) — 1 mark**
- Code the design in an OO language (Java/C++/Python).
- Example: `class Account { double balance; void withdraw(double amt) {...} }`.

**4. Object Oriented Testing (OOT) — 1.5 marks**
- Unit testing individual classes, integration testing of collaborations, system testing, acceptance testing.
- Test cases are often **derived from use-case scenarios** (Jacobson's contribution).

**5. Maintenance — 1 mark**
- Corrective, adaptive, perfective, and preventive maintenance.
- Due to encapsulation, changes are localized, reducing ripple effects.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why iterative?** Each cycle (micro-process) refines the model — there is no clean hand-off from OOA to OOD; they overlap.

---

##### MODEL SOLUTION — Q B (b) — Use-Case Diagram for Online Shopping (7 marks)

> **Definition (1 mark):** A **use case** is a sequence of actions performed by a system that yields an *observable result of value* to a particular **actor** (a person, another system, or time).

**Online Shopping Use-Case Diagram (text description — 4 marks):**

| Actor | Type | Use Cases Interacted With |
|-------|------|---------------------------|
| **Customer** | Primary | `Browse Catalog`, `Search Product`, `Add to Cart`, `Checkout`, `Track Order`, `Cancel Order` |
| **Admin** | Primary | `Manage Products`, `View Reports`, `Update Inventory` |
| **PaymentGateway** | External System | (Sub-system) `Process Payment` |
| **Shipper** | External System | `Dispatch Order`, `Update Delivery Status` |

**Relationships (2 marks):**
- `Checkout` **«includes»** `Process Payment` (mandatory sub-use-case).
- `Cancel Order` **«extends»** `Checkout` (optional path triggered by user choice).
- `Browse Catalog` **«extends»** `Search Product` (optional refinement).

> **Concluding statement (½ mark):** Use cases *drive* the rest of OOSE — every use case becomes a *thread* through analysis, design, implementation, and test-case generation.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where KTU students lose marks in Module-1 questions:**
> 1. **Confusing OMT and Booch notations** — Aggregation (hollow diamond ◇) vs Composition (filled diamond ◆). Examiners will *deliberately* mark wrong.
> 2. **Writing "UML" when the question asks for "methodology"** — UML is a *notation* (a tool); OMT/Booch/OOSE/UML-Unified are *methodologies* (processes).
> 3. **Forgetting multiplicities** in class diagrams — every association must have a multiplicity on *both* ends, or you lose ½–1 mark.
> 4. **Drawing a use-case diagram without a system boundary rectangle** — examiners specifically look for the rectangle.
> 5. **Using DFD for the dynamic model** — DFDs are part of the *Functional* model in OMT, *not* the Dynamic model. Dynamic model = **state diagram**.
> 6. **Calling Jacobson a follower of Rumbaugh** — they were independent contemporaries; only *later* (1995) did they unify.
> 7. **Mixing up «includes» and «extends»** — `«includes»` is **mandatory** (always happens), `«extends»` is **optional** (conditional path).
> 8. **Writing "OMT was created in 1997"** — wrong! OMT = 1991; UML = 1997. Lose 1 mark.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module-1 Rapid-Revision Bullets — Read this 10 minutes before the exam.**
>
> - **OOD** = develop using objects; integrates OOA + OOD + OOP + OOT + Maintenance.
> - **Three Pillars of OO:** Encapsulation, Inheritance, Polymorphism.
> - **Why model?** Manage complexity, communicate clearly, verify early, reduce cost of errors.
> - **A good model** is Abstract, Accurate, Predictive, and Sufficiently Complete.
> - **OMT (Rumbaugh, 1991)** = Object Model + Dynamic Model + Functional Model. Three orthogonal views.
> - **Booch (1994)** = strong in design & micro-process; introduced *clouds* and refined aggregation/composition.
> - **OOSE / Jacobson (1992)** = use-case driven; actors + use cases; drives requirements **and** test cases.
> - **UML (1997, OMG standard)** = unified notation by Rumbaugh + Booch + Jacobson ("Three Amigos" at Rational).
> - **Aggregation vs Composition** — hollow diamond ◇ (weak has-a, parts outlive whole) vs filled diamond ◆ (strong has-a, parts die with whole).
> - **Generalization** = hollow triangle △; **Realization** = dashed hollow triangle; **Dependency** = dashed arrow.
> - **Multiplicity** must be specified on *both* ends of every association.
> - **«includes»** = mandatory reuse of a use case. **«extends»** = optional/conditional path.
> - **OO development is iterative & incremental** — not waterfall. Each iteration touches Requirements → Analysis → Design → Implementation → Test.
> - **UML has 14 diagram types** divided into *Structure* (class, object, component, deployment, package, composite structure, profile) and *Behavior* (use-case, activity, state machine, sequence, communication, timing, interaction overview) diagrams.
> - **Structured vs OO:** structured decomposes by *function*; OO decomposes by *object*. OO wins on reuse, maintainability, and real-world mapping.
> - **State Diagram** shows *states + transitions + events* of a single object over time.
> - **Activity Diagram** shows *workflow / process flow* (can be used as a modern replacement for DFDs).
> - **Rumbaugh's master rule:** A complete OO specification requires **all three** OMT models.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Object Oriented Themes: Abstraction, Encapsulation, Combining Data and Behavior, Sharing, Emphasis on Object Structure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCNT604 - OBJECT ORIENTED MODELING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Object Oriented Themes: Abstraction, Encapsulation, Combining Data and Behavior, Sharing, Emphasis on Object Structure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Five Object-Oriented Themes — An Overview

Object-Oriented Modeling and Design (OOMD) is fundamentally driven by **five recurring themes** that distinguish it from classical procedural and structured methodologies. These themes were formally codified by **Rumbaugh** in the *Object Modeling Technique (OMT)* and remain the philosophical backbone of modern OOP (C++, Java, C#, Python) and modeling standards (UML, SysML).

The **five themes** are:

1. **Abstraction**
2. **Encapsulation**
3. **Combining Data and Behavior**
4. **Sharing**
5. **Emphasis on Object Structure**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping:** This topic is a **direct 14-mark question** material in Part B of ESE (End Semester Evaluation). Students are expected to *define, illustrate with a real-world analogy, and contrast* each of the five themes.

---

## 1.2 Formal Definitions (KTU Board-Standard Terminology)

### Theme 1 — Abstraction

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** *Abstraction is the process of focusing on the essential characteristics of an object (or system) while deliberately ignoring its inessential or extraneous properties.*

- It answers the question: **"What does this object represent?"** — not "How is it built?"
- Abstraction is the **conceptual gateway** to identifying classes during Requirements Engineering.
- It is realised through the mechanism of an **abstract data type (ADT)** or **abstract class**.

### Theme 2 — Encapsulation

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** *Encapsulation is the mechanism by which the data (attributes) and the operations (methods) that act on the data are bundled together as a single unit, and the internal state of the object is hidden from the outside world, accessible only through a published interface.*

- Often summarised by the phrase: **"Information Hiding."**
- Achieved via *access modifiers*: `private`, `protected`, `public` (in C++/Java) or conventions in Python.
- Encapsulation is the **physical mechanism**; abstraction is the **conceptual goal**.

### Theme 3 — Combining Data and Behavior

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** *Unlike traditional data-structure design where data is passive and procedures are external, OO design tightly couples data (attributes) with the operations (methods) that manipulate that data into a single unit called an object.*

- The object becomes a **self-contained, autonomous entity** that knows how to manage its own state.
- Eliminates the "anemic domain model" anti-pattern seen in poor procedural-to-OO translations.

### Theme 4 — Sharing

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** *Sharing refers to the ability of common structures and behaviors (defined once in a class) to be reused across multiple objects, applications, and systems — promoting economy of expression and reusability.*

- Realised through **inheritance**, **aggregation**, and **component libraries**.
- Manifests in three forms: **sharing of code**, **sharing of design**, **sharing of analysis**.

### Theme 5 — Emphasis on Object Structure

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** *OO design places primary emphasis on identifying the objects that constitute a system, the structure (static relationships) between them, and the messages they exchange, rather than the procedural flow of control.*

- Encourages a **data-centric worldview**: first model *what exists*, then model *what happens*.
- Contrasts sharply with the **function-decomposition worldview** of structured analysis (DFDs, ER diagrams in isolation).

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogies (Plain-English Understanding)

| Theme | Real-World Analogy | Plain-English Explanation |
|---|---|---|
| **Abstraction** | A **car's steering wheel** | You turn it left/right to change direction — you do NOT need to understand rack-and-pinion, hydraulics, or EPS. Only the *interface* matters. |
| **Encapsulation** | A **capsule pill** | The medicine (data + logic) is sealed inside; you swallow the capsule (call the API) without knowing the chemical composition (internal implementation). |
| **Combining Data & Behavior** | A **vending machine** | The machine *holds* items (data) AND *knows how to dispense* them when coins are inserted (behavior). You don't need an external "dispense function." |
| **Sharing** | A **single architectural blueprint** used to build 100 identical apartments | The blueprint is the *class*; the apartments are *objects/instances*. You don't redraw 100 times. |
| **Object Structure Emphasis** | Designing a **hospital by first drawing rooms, beds, doctors, patients** (entities) before drawing the workflow | In OOMD, you identify *who/what exists* first, then derive *how they interact*. |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Using real-world analogies in your answer instantly boosts your KTU board score — examiners reward *understanding over rote memorization*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & High-Yield Conceptual Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theme-by-Theme Analytical Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Abstraction — The "Why" and the "How"

**WHY abstraction exists:**
Human cognition cannot process irrelevant details. To model a complex system (e.g., an ATM, an Air Traffic Control radar), we must suppress low-level mechanics and elevate high-level intent.

**HOW it is achieved (4 levels of abstraction — Rumbaugh classification):**

1. **Entity Abstraction** — A object represents a useful model of a real-world entity (e.g., `Customer`, `Invoice`).
2. **Action Abstraction** — An object represents a set of operations, all performing similar functions (e.g., `Stack`, `Queue`).
3. **Virtual Machine Abstraction** — An object groups operations that are all used together for a high-level purpose (e.g., `Compiler`, `OperatingSystem`).
4. **Coincidental Abstraction** — An object bundles unrelated operations (an *anti-pattern* — discouraged).

**Engineering Utility:** Used in **Requirements Elicitation** phase to identify candidate classes from problem statements using noun-analysis (Abbott's textual analysis).

---

### 2.1.2 Encapsulation — The "Why" and the "How"

**WHY encapsulation exists:**
- To **protect invariants** of an object (e.g., an `Account` balance cannot go negative without a withdrawal operation).
- To enable **local change**: internal implementation can be modified without affecting external callers.
- To achieve **modularity**: components can be developed, tested, and maintained independently.

**HOW it is achieved:**

**Conceptual Layers of Encapsulation (C++/Java canonical model):**

$$\text{Public Interface} \;\supset\; \text{Protected Interface} \;\supset\; \text{Private Implementation}$$

| Access Level | Visibility | KTU Notation |
|---|---|---|
| **Public** `+` | Accessible to all classes | Visible everywhere |
| **Protected** `#` | Accessible to subclasses | Visible to inheritors |
| **Private** `-` | Accessible only within the class | Hidden from all externals |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students frequently confuse *Abstraction* with *Encapsulation*. Remember: **Abstraction = WHAT** (concept), **Encapsulation = HOW** (mechanism). They are complementary, not identical.

---

### 2.1.3 Combining Data and Behavior — The "Why" and the "How"

**WHY this theme matters:**
In classical structured design (Jackson, Yourdon), data is held in `struct`/records while procedures that manipulate them exist in separate function libraries. This leads to:
- **Data exposure** (any function can corrupt the data).
- **Poor locality** (logic and data drift apart over maintenance cycles).

**HOW it is achieved:**
By defining classes where:
- **Attributes** (data members) describe the *state*.
- **Operations** (methods) describe the *behavior*.
- A formal **invariant** governs how state may evolve.

**Canonical Class Anatomy:**

$$\text{Class} \;=\; \text{Attributes} \;+\; \text{Operations} \;+\; \text{Invariants} \;+\; \text{Lifecycle}$$

The object becomes a **responsible actor** that owns its own state and exposes only validated transitions.

---

### 2.1.4 Sharing — The "Why" and the "How"

**WHY sharing matters:**
- Reduces **redundancy** and **development cost**.
- Promotes **standardisation** (everyone uses the same `ArrayList`, `HashMap`).
- Enables **polymorphic extensibility**.

**HOW it is achieved (three dimensions of sharing):**

| Dimension | Mechanism | KTU Question Frequency |
|---|---|---|
| **Sharing of Code** | Inheritance, Composition | Very High |
| **Sharing of Design** | Design Patterns (GoF) | High |
| **Sharing of Analysis** | Domain Models, Frameworks | Moderate |

**Reusability Metric (Conceptual):** Let $\text{N}$ be the number of objects reusing a class. The **Reusability Factor** is defined as:

$$R = \frac{\text{Lines Reused}}{\text{Total Lines of Equivalent Custom Code}} \times 100\%$$

A well-designed class in KTU model solutions aims for $R \geq 60\%$.

---

### 2.1.5 Emphasis on Object Structure — The "Why" and the "How"

**WHY this theme matters:**
The OO paradigm inverts the traditional top-down function-decomposition. It asks:
> *"What objects must exist in this system?"* — BEFORE — *"What must they do?"*

**HOW it is achieved through three OMT models (Rumbaugh):**

1. **Object Model** — the static structure (classes, attributes, operations, relationships).
2. **Dynamic Model** — the temporal behavior (states, events, transitions).
3. **Functional Model** — the data transformations (DFDs, processes).

**Engineering Order of Construction (KTU-Standard):**

$$\text{Object Model} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Dynamic Model} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Functional Model}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Mnemonic — "ODF":** **O**bject → **D**ynamic → **F**unctional. This is the **canonical sequence** examiners expect when you draw the three OMT models.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — All Five Themes

| # | Theme | Core Question | OO Mechanism | Primary Benefit | Common Anti-Pattern |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Abstraction | *What is it?* | Abstract class, Interface | Manages complexity | Over-generalisation |
| 2 | Encapsulation | *How is it protected?* | Access modifiers, getters/setters | Information hiding, modularity | Exposing internal state via `public` fields |
| 3 | Combining Data & Behavior | *Who owns the logic?* | Class definition with attrs+methods | Cohesion, locality | Anemic domain model |
| 4 | Sharing | *How is it reused?* | Inheritance, composition, patterns | Reusability, consistency | Inheritance abuse (deep hierarchies) |
| 5 | Object Structure Emphasis | *What exists first?* | Object Model → Dynamic → Functional | Stable, change-resilient design | Premature procedural decomposition |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Comparative Derivation

## 3.1 The Relationship Network Between the Five Themes

The five themes are **not independent** — they form a tightly coupled hierarchy. Each lower theme *enables* the themes above it. Below is the **deductive derivation** of their inter-dependency.

### Step 1 — Foundation: Emphasis on Object Structure
Without identifying the *structure of objects* (the Object Model), no further OO design is possible. This is the **philosophical prerequisite**.

$$\text{Object Structure Identification} \;\Rightarrow\; \{\text{Classes}, \text{Attributes}, \text{Relationships}\} \;=\; \text{Object Model}$$

### Step 2 — Abstraction is Performed on the Identified Objects
Once objects are enumerated, *abstraction* is applied to filter essential characteristics from incidental ones.

$$\text{Object} \;-\; \text{Incidental Details} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Abstraction}}\; \text{Class Definition}$$

### Step 3 — Encapsulation Wraps the Abstraction
After a class is abstracted, encapsulation seals its data and operations behind an interface.

$$\text{Class} \;+\; \text{Access Modifiers} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Encapsulation}}\; \text{Black-Box Module}$$

### Step 4 — Data and Behavior are Combined Inside the Encapsulated Unit
Within the encapsulated boundary, attributes and methods are *co-located*.

$$\text{Attributes} \;\cup\; \text{Methods} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Binding}}\; \text{Object Instance}$$

### Step 5 — Sharing Distributes the Encapsulated Class
Finally, the well-formed class is *shared* across multiple instances and other classes via inheritance/composition.

$$\text{Single Class Definition} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Sharing}}\; \text{N Instances} \;+\; \text{M Subclasses} \;+\; \text{K Clients}$$

---

## 3.2 Detailed Comparative Matrix (Exhaustive)

| Comparison Axis | Abstraction | Encapsulation | Data + Behavior | Sharing | Object Structure |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary Concern** | Conceptual simplification | Implementation hiding | Logical cohesion | Reuse economy | Static modelling priority |
| **When Applied** | Analysis phase | Design phase | Design phase | Implementation phase | Analysis & Design phases |
| **OO Principle** | *"What?"* | *"How exposed?"* | *"Who owns?"* | *"How reused?"* | *"What exists?"* |
| **UML Construct** | `«abstract»`, `«interface»` | Visibility `+/-/#` | Class rectangle (attr+op) | Inheritance arrow, composition diamond | Object diagram, class diagram |
| **Failure Mode** | Too abstract (useless) | Too exposed (unsafe) | Too rigid (inflexible) | Over-shared (fragile base class) | Premature structure (inflexible) |
| **Coupling Effect** | Reduces *cognitive* load | Reduces *implementation* coupling | Increases *internal* cohesion | Reduces *redundancy* | Reduces *architectural* risk |
| **Connascence Rank** | Low Connascence | Encapsulation breaks connascence | Localises connascence | Inheritance can increase connascence | Minimises cross-object connascence |
| **Metric Influence** | Class responsibility clarity | LCOM (Lack of Cohesion) | CBO (Coupling Between Objects) | DIT (Depth of Inheritance Tree) | NOC (Number of Children) |

---

## 3.3 Numerical Demonstration — Abstraction Levels (Conceptual Walkthrough)

Consider modelling an **Air Conditioner (AC)**. The level of abstraction determines which details are retained and which are suppressed.

$$\text{Level 0 (Concrete):} \quad \text{AC} = \{\text{compressor}, \text{condenser}, \text{evaporator}, \text{refrigerant gas R32}\}$$

$$\text{Level 1 (Entity Abstraction):} \quad \text{AC} = \{\text{temperature}, \text{mode}, \text{fanSpeed}\}$$

$$\text{Level 2 (Action Abstraction):} \quad \text{AC} = \{\text{cool()}, \text{heat()}, \text{dry()}, \text{fan()}\}$$

$$\text{Level 3 (Virtual Machine):} \quad \text{AC} = \{\text{thermostaticControl()} \rightarrow \text{coordinated usage of all operations}\}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** At each step, we ascend one cognitive tier. Hardware internals are dropped at Level 1; entity-level state is dropped at Level 2; specific actions are merged into a coordinated meta-behavior at Level 3.

---

## 3.4 Mathematical Demonstration — Sharing's Reusability Impact

Let a class `Stack` be defined once with **150 LOC (Lines of Code)**. It is reused across 5 distinct applications.

**Without Sharing:**

$$\text{Total LOC} \;=\; 5 \times 150 \;=\; 750 \;\text{lines}$$

**With Sharing (single class, multiple instances):**

$$\text{Total LOC} \;=\; 150 + 5 \times 10 \;=\; 200 \;\text{lines}$$

(The 10 lines per application are merely *client glue code*.)

**Reusability Factor:**

$$R \;=\; \frac{150}{750} \times 100\% \;=\; 20\% \text{ (in custom equivalent)}$$

$$R_{\text{effective}} \;=\; \frac{150}{200} \times 100\% \;=\; 75\%$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Sharing reduces *total deployed lines of code* by an order of magnitude. In KTU answers, *always include such a quantitative payoff* to demonstrate deep understanding — this is the hallmark of a top-band answer.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Safeguard Acknowledgement

All Mermaid blocks below adhere to the **Node Identifier Alpha Rule** (purely alphanumeric, prefixed with letters, no reserved keywords as standalone nodes) and the **Label Formatting Restriction** (raw uppercase alphanumeric text inside node labels, no markdown formatting or HTML tables).

---

## 4.2 Diagram 1 — Class Diagram Illustrating Abstraction and Encapsulation

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class clsAbstractShape {
        <<abstract>>
        - color: String
        # vertices: Integer
        + area() : Double
        + draw() : void
    }
    class clsCircle {
        - radius: Double
        + area() : Double
        + draw() : void
    }
    class clsRectangle {
        - width: Double
        - height: Double
        + area() : Double
        + draw() : void
    }
    clsAbstractShape <|-- clsCircle : extends
    clsAbstractShape <|-- clsRectangle : extends
```

**Explanation:**
- `clsAbstractShape` is the **abstraction** — it captures only the essential concept ("a shape has a color, vertices, can be drawn, has an area").
- Members prefixed with `-` are **private (encapsulated)**; `#` are protected; `+` are public.
- `clsCircle` and `clsRectangle` are concrete realisations.

---

## 4.3 Diagram 2 — Object Structure Emphasising Data + Behavior Combination

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class clsAccount {
        - accountNumber : String
        - balance : Double
        - ownerName : String
        + deposit(amount : Double) : void
        + withdraw(amount : Double) : Boolean
        + getBalance() : Double
    }
    note for clsAccount "DATA (top half) + BEHAVIOR (bottom half) are bound into one cohesive unit"
```

**Explanation:**
- The top compartment contains **attributes (data)**.
- The bottom compartment contains **operations (behavior)**.
- The `note` symbolically illustrates the binding.

---

## 4.4 Diagram 3 — Sharing via Inheritance and Composition

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class clsVehicle {
        + start() : void
        + stop() : void
    }
    class clsCar {
        - numDoors : Integer
        + openTrunk() : void
    }
    class clsEngine {
        - horsepower : Integer
        + accelerate() : void
    }
    class clsElectricCar {
        - batteryCapacity : Integer
        + charge() : void
    }
    clsVehicle <|-- clsCar : inherits
    clsVehicle <|-- clsElectricCar : inherits
    clsCar *-- clsEngine : composition
    clsElectricCar *-- clsEngine : composition
```

**Explanation:**
- `clsCar` and `clsElectricCar` **share** the common interface of `clsVehicle` (Sharing via Inheritance — the *"is-a"* relationship).
- Both `clsCar` and `clsElectricCar` **share** an `clsEngine` instance (Sharing via Composition — the *"has-a"* relationship).

---

## 4.5 Diagram 4 — Functional Architecture Flow (OMT Three-Model Sequence)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Problem Statement] --> B[Identify Candidate Objects]
    B --> C[Apply Abstraction Filters]
    C --> D[Build Object Model]
    D --> E[Identify States and Events]
    E --> F[Build Dynamic Model]
    F --> G[Identify Data Transformations]
    G --> H[Build Functional Model]
    H --> I[Apply Encapsulation and Sharing]
    I --> J[Final OO Design]

    stepB[B]:::procStep
    stepD[D]:::procStep
    stepF[F]:::procStep
    stepH[H]:::procStep
    stepJ[J]:::procStep

    classDef procStep fill:#E0F2FF,stroke:#0277BD,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

**Explanation:** This flow shows the **Emphasis on Object Structure** — the Object Model is built *first*, and the subsequent Dynamic and Functional models depend on it.

---

## 4.6 Diagram 5 — Hierarchy of the Five Themes

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Emphasis on Object Structure] --> B[Abstraction]
    B --> C[Encapsulation]
    C --> D[Combining Data and Behavior]
    D --> E[Sharing]
    E --> F[Final Robust OO System]

    stepA[A]:::themeNode
    stepB[B]:::themeNode
    stepC[C]:::themeNode
    stepD[D]:::themeNode
    stepE[E]:::themeNode
    stepF[F]:::outputNode

    classDef themeNode fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef outputNode fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:3px,color:#000000
```

**Explanation:** Each theme builds upon the previous — illustrating that the themes are **not isolated concepts** but a layered design philosophy.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Set]**
**Distinguish between *Abstraction* and *Encapsulation* with one real-world example each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Abstraction | Encapsulation |
|---|---|---|
| **Conceptual Focus** | Hides *complexity* (the *what*) | Hides *implementation* (the *how*) |
| **Achieved by** | Abstract classes, interfaces | Access modifiers (`private`, `public`) |
| **Example** | A TV remote exposes only `power`, `volume`, `channel` buttons; internal circuitry is irrelevant to the user. | The same remote's internal microcircuit is sealed inside a plastic shell; you cannot poke the PCB directly. |

**(1 mark) Abstraction definition + example, (1 mark) Encapsulation definition + example, (1 mark) clean distinction table.**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Set]**
**Explain the theme *"Combining Data and Behavior"* with a suitable class-level illustration.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

In classical procedural design, data structures (e.g., `struct Student {name, rollNo}`) exist *separately* from functions (e.g., `void calculateGrade(Student s)`). The data is *passive* and *exposed*.

In OO design, the theme **"Combining Data and Behavior"** mandates that data and the operations that manipulate it be bundled together:

```
Class Student {
    - name : String        // DATA
    - rollNo : Integer     // DATA
    - marks : Float[]      // DATA
    + calculateGrade() : Char    // BEHAVIOR
    + isPassed() : Boolean       // BEHAVIOR
}
```

The `Student` object now *owns* both its state and the operations to query/transform that state — eliminating external corruption and improving cohesion.

**(1 mark) contrast with procedural, (1 mark) class illustration, (1 mark) benefit statement.**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Set]**

**(a)** Define the **five object-oriented themes** as introduced by Rumbaugh. Explain each with a suitable real-world analogy. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For each of the five themes, identify **one UML construct** that supports it and explain how the theme is realized in a simple **Banking System** design (mention at least 3 classes). **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-A:**

**(a) Five Themes with Analogies (7 marks = 1.4 marks each)**

1. **Abstraction:** *"What does this object represent?"* — A **bank customer's** account shows only the essentials (balance, name, account number) to the teller; internal authentication tokens, encryption keys, and database row IDs are hidden.
2. **Encapsulation:** *"How is it protected?"* — The **ATM machine** casing seals the cash-dispenser mechanism; you interact only through the screen and keypad.
3. **Combining Data and Behavior:** *"Who owns the logic?"* — A **`Transaction`** object holds `amount`, `timestamp`, `type` (data) AND `validate()`, `execute()`, `rollback()` (behavior) together.
4. **Sharing:** *"How is it reused?"* — A base class **`BankAccount`** with methods `deposit()`, `withdraw()` is shared by `SavingsAccount` and `CurrentAccount` via inheritance.
5. **Emphasis on Object Structure:** *"What exists first?"* — In banking, we first identify `Customer`, `Account`, `Transaction`, `Branch` (entities) before modelling the workflow.

**(b) UML Constructs & Banking System Realisation (7 marks)**

| Theme | UML Construct | Banking Realisation |
|---|---|---|
| Abstraction | `«abstract» BankAccount` class | `BankAccount` declares abstract `calculateInterest()`; never instantiated directly. |
| Encapsulation | Visibility markers `+/-/#` | `balance : Double` is `private`; only `deposit()` / `withdraw()` can mutate it. |
| Combining Data and Behavior | Class rectangle with two compartments | `Transaction { amount, date, type; execute(), validate() }` |
| Sharing | Generalization arrow (inheritance) | `SavingsAccount extends BankAccount`, `CurrentAccount extends BankAccount`. |
| Emphasis on Object Structure | Object/Class diagram as the *first* artefact | Project kickoff produces the **Class Diagram** identifying `Customer ↔ Account ↔ Transaction`. |

(Examiner's expected depth: name 3 classes minimum — `Customer`, `Account`, `Transaction` — and tie each theme to a feature of these classes.)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Set]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail how **Encapsulation** and **Abstraction** work *together* in OO design. Are they the same? Justify with an example of an `Employee` class. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the theme *"Emphasis on Object Structure"* with reference to Rumbaugh's **three-model approach (Object, Dynamic, Functional)**. Why is the Object Model built first? **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-B:**

**(a) Abstraction vs Encapsulation (7 marks)**

**They are NOT the same — they are complementary:**

- **Abstraction** is a *design-time* concept: the designer decides *what* to expose.
- **Encapsulation** is a *language-level* mechanism: the programmer enforces the decision using access modifiers.

**Employee Class Example:**

```java
public abstract class Employee {
    private String name;          // ENCAPSULATED
    private double baseSalary;   // ENCAPSULATED
    public abstract double calculatePay();   // ABSTRACTION (only signature exposed)

    public String getName() { return name; } // Controlled public interface
    public void setBaseSalary(double s) {
        if (s > 0) this.baseSalary = s;     // Invariant enforced INSIDE the class
    }
}
```

Here:
- **Abstraction:** `calculatePay()` has *no body* — subclasses define HOW to compute pay.
- **Encapsulation:** `baseSalary` is `private`; only the class can validate it.

**(b) Rumbaugh's Three-Model Approach (7 marks)**

Rumbaugh's OMT method prescribes three orthogonal, complementary models:

| Model | Purpose | KTU-Standard Notation |
|---|---|---|
| **Object Model** | Static structure: classes, attributes, operations, relationships | Class Diagram |
| **Dynamic Model** | Temporal behavior: states, events, transitions | State Diagram |
| **Functional Model** | Data transformations: inputs, outputs, processes | Data Flow Diagram |

**Why Object Model First:**

$$\text{Object Model} \;\xrightarrow{\text{identifies objects}}\; \text{Dynamic Model (states of these objects)}$$

$$\text{Object Model} \;\xrightarrow{\text{identifies operations}}\; \text{Functional Model (transformations on object data)}$$

Without the Object Model, there are no objects to put into states, and no operations to map as data flows. The Object Model is the **anchor** of the entire design — reflecting the OO philosophy that *structure precedes behavior*.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones (Avoid These):**
> 1. **Treating Abstraction and Encapsulation as synonyms** — they are distinct concepts. Examiners deduct **2 full marks** for this confusion.
> 2. **Defining "Sharing" only as Inheritance** — Sharing has three dimensions: code, design, analysis. Limiting it to inheritance alone loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Omitting the Object-Model-first reasoning** — for Question B part (b), merely listing the three models without explaining *why Object Model is first* caps your marks at 4/7.
> 4. **Writing UML without labels** — class diagrams must show attribute names, types, and visibility markers. Bare boxes = zero credit.
> 5. **Confusing "Sharing" with "Reusability"** — Sharing is the *mechanism*; Reusability is the *outcome*. Examiners expect this distinction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Quick-Revision Checklist — Object-Oriented Themes**

- **Abstraction** = *What* (conceptual filtering) → Realised by **abstract classes / interfaces**
- **Encapsulation** = *How protected* (mechanism) → Realised by **access modifiers `+/-/#`**
- **Combining Data & Behavior** = *cohesion* → Realised by **Class = Attributes + Methods**
- **Sharing** = *economy of reuse* → Realised by **Inheritance, Composition, Patterns**
- **Emphasis on Object Structure** = *structure precedes behavior* → Realised by **Object Model → Dynamic Model → Functional Model**
- **Mnemonic for ordering the OMT models:** **O-D-F** (Object, Dynamic, Functional)
- **Mnemonic for the five themes:** **A-E-C-S-O** (Abstraction, Encapsulation, Combining, Sharing, Object-structure)
- **Reusability Factor formula:** $R = \frac{\text{Shared LOC}}{\text{Total Equivalent Custom LOC}} \times 100\%$
- **Three levels of abstraction (Rumbaugh):** Entity → Action → Virtual Machine (avoid Coincidental)
- **Always pair definitions with analogies** in 14-mark answers — this is the **top-band differentiator** in KTU valuation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Object Modeling: Objects and Classes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCNT604 - OBJECT ORIENTED MODELING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Object Modeling: Objects and Classes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🎯 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme - PBCNT604)**
> In Module 1, *Object Modeling* is the very first concept introduced before advancing to Advanced Class Modeling and UML state/sequence diagrams. This topic carries **high weightage** in KTU ESE (End Semester Evaluation) and is a **mandatory prerequisite** for Module 2 (Advanced Class Modeling) and Module 4 (System Design).

---

## 1.1 What is an Object? — The Formal Definition

> **Definition (Booch, Rumbaugh, Jacobson — "The Three Amigos" of OOAD):**
> An **Object** is a discrete, identifiable entity that has a well-defined **state**, **behavior**, and **identity**, and which encapsulates both data and the operations that act upon that data.

Mathematically expressed in KTU-accepted notation:

$$O = \{i, S, B, R\}$$

Where:
- $i$ → **Identity** (unique existence of the object)
- $S$ → **State** (the collection of values held by the object's attributes at a point in time)
- $B$ → **Behavior** (the set of operations the object can perform)
- $R$ → **Responsibility** (the contractual obligations the object owes to other objects)

### 🌱 Intuitive Analogy — The "Banking Account"

Think of a **real-world bank account** that you hold in a bank:
- The account has a **unique account number** — that is its *identity* (even if two people have the same name and balance, the account number distinguishes them).
- It has a **current balance**, **account holder name**, and **account type** — these together form its *state*.
- It can **deposit money, withdraw money, check balance, close the account** — these are its *behaviors* (operations).
- It is responsible for *maintaining an accurate balance* and *logging every transaction* — its *responsibility*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Concept:** In KTU examinations, when asked to "explain the nature of an object," students MUST explicitly state all **three pillars** — *Identity, State, Behavior* — to fetch full marks. Omitting even one pillar results in a **2-mark penalty** in the 3-mark definition questions.

---

## 1.2 What is a Class? — The Formal Definition

> **Definition (KTU 2024 Standard Text):**
> A **Class** is an *abstract descriptor* (a blueprint, template, or prototype) that defines the common **attributes** (state) and **operations** (behavior) shared by a set of objects. A class is the *type* from which individual objects are *instantiated*.

The relationship between a class and its objects is captured by:

$$C \xrightarrow{\text{instantiates}} O_1, O_2, O_3, \ldots, O_n$$

Where $C$ is a single class and $O_1 \ldots O_n$ are the multiple **object instances** (also called *instances* or *occurrences*) created from it.

### 🏭 Intuitive Analogy — The "Architect's Blueprint"

Imagine an **architect's blueprint** of a house:
- The **blueprint** is the **Class** — it defines that every house *will have* doors, windows, rooms, and a roof.
- The **actual houses** built from that blueprint (House-A, House-B, House-C) are the **Objects** (instances).
- All houses share the *structure* (same class), but each house has *unique state* — House-A might be painted blue, House-B red.
- The number of houses built is **not limited** by the blueprint — this matches how a class can have unlimited instances.

> [!TIP]
> **Common KTU Mistake to Avoid:** A class is **not** an object. A class is a *category*; an object is a *member* of that category. Saying "the class itself is an object" is a **fundamental conceptual error** and will cost you marks.

---

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of an Object — Quick Reference

| Pillar | Meaning | Real-world Example (Car Object) |
|---|---|---|
| **Identity** | The unique handle/existence that distinguishes one object from another, even if their state is identical | Each car has a unique **Chassis Number / VIN** |
| **State** | The current values of all attributes that the object encapsulates | Color = Red, Speed = 60 km/h, Fuel = 40 % |
| **Behavior** | The set of operations/methods/services the object offers to the outside world | `accelerate()`, `brake()`, `refuel()`, `honk()` |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Trap:** Do NOT confuse *attributes* (state) with *operations* (behavior). State is described by **nouns**; behavior is described by **verbs**. "Speed" is state; "accelerate" is behavior.

---

## 1.4 Why Object Modeling? — The Engineering Rationale

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Motivation:**
> Object Modeling is the *first activity* in the OOAD pipeline (per the Booch, OMT, and Unified processes). It is the bridge between **problem space** (real-world entities) and **solution space** (software classes). The result is a **conceptual model** of the system that is *implementation-independent*, allowing stakeholders, designers, and coders to share a **common vocabulary** (the glossary of domain objects).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📘 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

This section breaks down the internal anatomy of objects and classes, their relationships, and the standard UML/KTU notation used to express them.

---

## 2.1 Anatomy of an Object — Detailed Theoretical Breakdown

An object is not a "black box" — it has a well-defined internal structure that KTU examiners love to test. Let's break it down into its constituent elements.

### 2.1.1 Identity
- Every object has an **intrinsically unique existence**, regardless of its state.
- Two objects can have **identical state** (e.g., two identical coins minted on the same day) but are still distinguishable because of their **identity**.
- Implementation-wise, identity is often realized through **unique object IDs, memory addresses, or surrogate keys** in a database.

### 2.1.2 State
- The state of an object is determined by the **current values of its attributes**.
- The state of an object is **time-dependent** — it changes over time as operations are invoked.
- An object's state is **encapsulated** (hidden from outside), accessible only through its public interface (operations).

Mathematically, the state of an object $o$ at time $t$ is the vector:

$$S_t(o) = \langle a_1 = v_1, a_2 = v_2, \ldots, a_n = v_n \rangle$$

Where each $a_i$ is an attribute and $v_i$ is its current value.

### 2.1.3 Behavior
- Behavior is the *set of operations* (also called *methods* or *services*) the object can perform.
- Behavior defines **how an object responds to stimuli** (messages from other objects).
- Behavior is *invariant* — it is defined by the class and does not change between instances.

### 2.1.4 Responsibility
- A *responsibility* is a higher-level contract — it is the **obligation** of an object to provide a service.
- Two types:
  - **Knowing responsibilities** — knowing its own state, knowing other objects.
  - **Doing responsibilities** — creating other objects, performing computations, coordinating activities.

---

## 2.2 Anatomy of a Class — Detailed Theoretical Breakdown

A class specification in KTU 2024 (and UML) contains:

1. **Class Name** — a noun in mixed case (e.g., `BankAccount`, `Customer`).
2. **Attributes** — represent the state of the instances. Syntax: `visibility name : type [multiplicity] = default`.
3. **Operations** — represent the behavior. Syntax: `visibility name (parameters) : returnType`.

### Visibility Modifiers (Critical for KTU Questions)

| Symbol | Visibility | Meaning | KTU Notation |
|---|---|---|---|
| `+` | Public | Accessible to **all** classes | `+ name : String` |
| `-` | Private | Accessible **only within** the class | `- balance : float` |
| `#` | Protected | Accessible within the class and **its subclasses** | `# rate : double` |
| `~` | Package | Accessible within the **same package** | `~ id : int` |

---

## 2.3 Relationships Between Objects and Classes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Topic:** In KTU Module 1, examiners often ask students to "list and explain the relationships between objects and classes" or "draw the link semantics." This is a **frequently tested 7-to-14 mark question**.

### 2.3.1 Links
A **link** is a *physical or conceptual connection* between two objects. It is the **instance-level equivalent** of an association.

$$L(o_1, o_2) \equiv \text{link between object } o_1 \text{ and object } o_2$$

### 2.3.2 Association
An **association** is a *description of a set of links* with common structure and common semantics. It is the **class-level equivalent** of a link. A link is to an association what an object is to a class.

### 2.3.3 Multiplicity
**Multiplicity** specifies *how many objects* participate in an association from the perspective of one end:

| Multiplicity | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `1` | Exactly one |
| `0..1` | Optional, zero or one |
| `*` or `0..*` | Zero or many |
| `1..*` | One or many |
| `n..m` | Between $n$ and $m$ (integer range) |

---

## 2.4 Active vs. Passive Objects

This is a **distinction KTU examiners love**:

- **Passive Object**: Stores data and provides services to other objects, but does **not initiate** any activity. (e.g., a database record object, a BankAccount object).
- **Active Object**: Has its **own thread of control** and can initiate activities. (e.g., a `Process` object, a `Thread` object, a `Scheduler` object).

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet (Table Format)

> [!NOTE]
> **This table is the absolute minimum you must memorize for Module 1 KTU exams.**

| Concept | Definition | UML Notation | KTU Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Object** | Concrete entity with identity, state, behavior | Underlined: `customer1 : Customer` | `john : Student` |
| **Class** | Blueprint/template for objects | Boxed with 3 sections | `Student`, `Car` |
| **Attribute** | Named property (state) | `- name : String` | `- rollNo : int` |
| **Operation** | Function/service (behavior) | `+ getName() : String` | `+ calculateGPA()` |
| **Link** | Connection between objects | Solid line between objects | `john` — `cs101` |
| **Association** | Class-level connection | Solid line between classes | `Student` — `Course` |
| **Multiplicity** | Cardinality constraint | `1`, `*`, `0..*` | `Student` `1` — `*` `Course` |
| **Visibility** | Access level | `+ - # ~` | `- balance : double` |
| **Active Object** | Initiates its own actions | **Thick border** in diagram | `Process` |
| **Passive Object** | Only responds to messages | Normal border | `BankAccount` |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility of Object Modeling

Object Modeling is not merely academic — it is the **blueprint for software construction**.

- **Software Engineering**: Used in all OOP languages (Java, C++, Python, C#) to design classes before coding.
- **Database Design**: Maps directly to Object-Relational Mapping (ORM) — classes become tables, objects become rows.
- **System Architecture**: Forms the basis of microservices — each service is essentially a "large object" with its own state and behavior.
- **Embedded Systems**: Each sensor/actuator is modeled as an object with identity, state, and behavior.
- **IoT & Robotics**: Robots are composed of objects (sensors, motors, controllers), each with a clean identity/state/behavior contract.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Pro Tip:** When asked "give a real-world example," choose examples from **banking, library, hospital, or e-commerce** — these are the four classic KTU-favorite domains.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🔬 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Object Identification

This section provides a **systematic methodology** — the kind KTU examiners expect for the 14-mark Part B questions. We will perform a complete, exhaustive object modeling exercise.

---

## 3.1 The Three-Step Object Identification Process

> **Per the OOAD methodology (Rumbaugh's Object Modeling Technique — OMT), identifying objects from a problem statement is a structured, repeatable process.**

### STEP 1: Identify Candidate Classes (Noun Extraction)
- Read the problem description carefully.
- **Underline every noun** — each noun is a *candidate* class.
- Filter out non-classes (nouns that are attributes, operations, or irrelevant terms).

### STEP 2: Identify Attributes (Refined Noun Analysis)
- For each candidate class, identify which nouns represent *properties* of the class.
- These become the **attributes** (state).
- Discard attributes that are actually classes in disguise (e.g., "address" → may become a class `Address`).

### STEP 3: Identify Operations (Verb Extraction)
- **Underline every verb** in the problem description.
- Associate each verb with a class — this gives you the **operations**.

---

## 3.2 Worked-Out Example — Library Management System

> **Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
> *"A library issues books to its registered members. Each member can borrow up to 3 books at a time. A book has a title, author, ISBN, and may be either issued or available. A librarian registers new members and processes book returns."*

### Step-by-Step Object Identification

#### Step 1: Candidate Classes (Noun Extraction)

| Noun Extracted | Decision | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| Library | ✗ Discard (system itself, not a class) | The whole system |
| Book | ✓ **Keep** | Central entity |
| Member | ✓ **Keep** | Central entity |
| Librarian | ✓ **Keep** | Central entity |
| Title | ✗ Attribute of Book | Not a class |
| Author | ✓ **Keep** | Could be a class (entity) |
| ISBN | ✗ Attribute of Book | Not a class |
| Issue (status) | ✗ Attribute of Book | State value |
| Registration | ✗ Discard (process) | A verb/noun of process |

**Final Classes Identified:** `Book`, `Member`, `Librarian`, `Author`.

#### Step 2: Attributes of Each Class

| Class | Attributes |
|---|---|
| **Book** | `- title : String`, `- ISBN : String`, `- status : {Available, Issued}` |
| **Member** | `- memberID : int`, `- name : String`, `- booksIssued : int` |
| **Librarian** | `- empID : int`, `- name : String`, `- shift : String` |
| **Author** | `- authorID : int`, `- name : String`, `- bio : String` |

#### Step 3: Operations of Each Class

| Class | Operations |
|---|---|
| **Book** | `+ issue() : boolean`, `+ returnBook() : boolean`, `+ getStatus() : String` |
| **Member** | `+ borrowBook(b : Book) : void`, `+ returnBook(b : Book) : void`, `+ getMemberID() : int` |
| **Librarian** | `+ registerMember(m : Member) : void`, `+ processReturn(b : Book) : void` |
| **Author** | `+ getName() : String`, `+ getBooks() : List<Book>` |

---

## 3.3 The Analytical Matrix — Object vs. Class

> [!NOTE]
> **This matrix is a high-value revision tool. Memorize it for Part A questions.**

| Property | Class | Object |
|---|---|---|
| **Existence** | Conceptual / abstract | Concrete / physical |
| **Number** | Single definition per domain | Unlimited instances |
| **State** | Defines *what state exists* | Holds *specific values of state* |
| **Behavior** | Defines *operations available* | Executes the operations |
| **Identity** | Not applicable | Each has a unique identity |
| **UML Notation** | Rectangle with 3 compartments | Rectangle with underlined text |
| **Memory** | Loaded once in memory | Each occupies separate memory |
| **Created by** | Designer / programmer | Constructor of the class |
| **Destroyed by** | Never (lives in program lifetime) | Garbage collector / destructor |
| **Analogy** | Blueprint of a car | A specific car (Maruti Swift KL-01-1234) |

---

## 3.4 Identifying Multiplicity — The Exhaustive Method

For each association, count how many objects on the *other* side can be linked to one object on *this* side. The mapping follows a strict rule:

| Question to Ask | Possible Multiplicity |
|---|---|
| Can one book be issued to only one member at a time? | Yes → `1` on the Member side |
