| Can one member borrow many books? | Yes → `0..*` on the Book side |
| Can a book exist without being issued? | Yes → `0..*` on the Member side |
| Can a member exist without borrowing any book? | Yes → `0..*` on the Book side |

The multiplicity is written **at the end of the association line nearest to the class whose instances are being counted**.

---

## 3.5 Mathematical Formulation — Instantiation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For 14-mark derivation-style questions, this notation is highly valued by KTU examiners.**

If $C$ is a class, and $O$ is the set of objects that are instances of $C$, we write:

$$O = \{o \in \text{universe} \mid \text{classOf}(o) = C\}$$

Or using OMT notation:

$$C \rightarrow O \quad \text{(class } C \text{ instantiates into objects in } O)$$

The **cardinality** of the instance set is the number of objects created from the class:

$$|O| = \text{count of instantiated objects from class } C$$

This cardinality is *not bounded* by the class definition — a class can theoretically have infinite instances (subject to memory constraints).

---

## 3.6 Common Errors and Corrections Table

| Common Error | Why It's Wrong | Correct Statement |
|---|---|---|
| "A class is an object" | Class is the *type*; object is an *instance* | "An object is an instance of a class" |
| "Two objects with same state are the same object" | Violates *identity* property | "Identity is independent of state" |
| "Attributes are operations" | Confuses state with behavior | "Attributes = nouns, Operations = verbs" |
| "Link is same as association" | Wrong level of abstraction | "Link = instance, Association = class" |
| "A class has its own state" | State belongs to *instances* | "A class *defines* the state; objects *hold* it" |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Question A in the question bank below is modeled on this exact pattern. Ensure you don't make the above mistakes.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🗺️ 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

This section provides UML and block diagrams rendered in Mermaid syntax, compliant with KTU 2024 syllabus expectations.

---

## 4.1 Class Diagram — Library Management System (UML Notation)

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Book {
      -title : String
      -ISBN : String
      -status : String
      +issue() : boolean
      +returnBook() : boolean
      +getStatus() : String
    }
    class Member {
      -memberID : int
      -name : String
      -booksIssued : int
      +borrowBook(b : Book) : void
      +returnBook(b : Book) : void
      +getMemberID() : int
    }
    class Librarian {
      -empID : int
      -name : String
      -shift : String
      +registerMember(m : Member) : void
      +processReturn(b : Book) : void
    }
    class Author {
      -authorID : int
      -name : String
      -bio : String
      +getName() : String
      +getBooks() : List
    }

    Member "1" --> "0..3" Book : borrows
    Author "1" --> "*" Book : writes
    Librarian "1" --> "*" Member : registers
    Librarian "1" --> "*" Book : processes return of
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to read this diagram (KTU standard):**
> - A solid line with an arrow denotes an **association**.
> - The numbers at each end denote **multiplicity**.
> - `Member "1" --> "0..3" Book` means: One member can borrow zero to three books.

---

## 4.2 Object Diagram — A Snapshot of Library State

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[book1 : Book\ntitle: DBMS\nISBN: 978-81-203\nstatus: Issued]
    B[member1 : Member\nmemberID: 101\nname: Arun\nbooksIssued: 1]
    C[book2 : Book\ntitle: OS\nISBN: 978-81-204\nstatus: Available]
    D[author1 : Author\nauthorID: 501\nname: Korth]

    A -- borrowed by --> B
    D -- wrote --> A
    D -- wrote --> C
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Distinction:** A **class diagram** shows the *template* (Book, Member, Author). An **object diagram** shows a *snapshot* of actual instances (book1, member1, author1) and the *links* between them. This is exactly the relationship between "class" and "object" — and KTU examiners test it frequently.

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Architecture — The Object Modeling Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Problem Statement] --> B[Step 1: Extract Nouns]
    B --> C[Step 2: Filter Candidate Classes]
    C --> D[Step 3: Extract Verbs]
    D --> E[Step 4: Assign Operations]
    E --> F[Step 5: Extract Adjectives as Attributes]
    F --> G[Step 6: Build Class Diagram]
    G --> H[Step 7: Validate with Use Cases]
    H --> I[Final Object Model]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** This is the **standard 7-step object modeling process** taught in OOAD courses. In the 14-mark question, presenting this flow (with the example worked out) often earns an **extra 1-2 marks** beyond the standard answer.

---

## 4.4 Object Lifecycle — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C[Class Definition Loaded] --> I[Object Instantiated]
    I --> U[Object In Use - State Changes]
    U --> D[Object Destroyed - Garbage Collected]
    D --> M[Memory Reclaimed]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters in KTU:** The "lifecycle of an object" is a **classic 3-mark short-answer question**. The three phases are: **Creation → Use → Destruction**. Each phase has specific terminology (constructor invocation, message passing, destructor/garbage collection).

---

## 4.5 Active vs. Passive Object — Visual Distinction

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ActiveObject[ACTIVE OBJECT - Has its own thread]
        direction TB
        A1[Process Scheduler]
        A2[Owns control thread]
        A3[Can initiate actions]
        A1 --> A2 --> A3
        style A1 fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    end

    subgraph PassiveObject[PASSIVE OBJECT - Responds to messages]
        direction TB
        P1[Bank Account]
        P2[Data-only / no thread]
        P3[Waits for invocations]
        P1 --> P2 --> P3
        style P1 fill:#4ecdc4,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    end
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Note on Diagram Style:** In UML, an **active object** is denoted by a **thick border** rectangle, and a **passive object** by a **normal border**. Mermaid doesn't support this distinction natively, so we use color and labels above to convey the concept.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📝 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

This section mirrors the **actual KTU 2024 ESE question paper pattern** for PBCNT604. All questions are tagged with simulated past-year tags and follow the NEP 2020 Bloom's Taxonomy cognitive levels.

---

## 📘 PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**Q: Define an object. Explain the three essential properties of an object.**

**Model Answer:**

An **object** is a discrete, identifiable entity in the problem domain that has a well-defined boundary and encapsulates both data and the operations that act upon that data.

The three essential properties of an object are:

1. **Identity**: Every object has a unique existence that distinguishes it from all other objects, regardless of whether their state values are identical. Identity is intrinsic — it does not change over the lifetime of the object.

2. **State**: The state of an object is the collection of current values held by its attributes at a given point in time. State is *encapsulated* (hidden) and changes only through the object's own operations. For example, a `BankAccount` object may have state `{balance = 5000, holder = "Arun"}`.

3. **Behavior**: The behavior of an object is the set of operations it can perform or that can be performed upon it. Behavior defines how the object responds to messages from other objects. For example, a `BankAccount` object supports `deposit()`, `withdraw()`, and `getBalance()`.

Together, these three properties allow an object to model a real-world entity with full fidelity.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
**Q: Differentiate between a class and an object with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Class | Object |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | A blueprint, template, or prototype that defines attributes and operations | A concrete instance of a class with actual state values |
| **Existence** | Conceptual / exists in design phase | Concrete / exists at runtime |
| **Memory** | Loaded once in program memory | Each occupies separate memory |
| **UML Notation** | Rectangle with three compartments (name, attributes, operations) | Rectangle with **underlined** instance name |
| **Example** | `Student` (defines that every student has roll number, name, branch) | `student1 : Student` with `rollNo = 101, name = "Arun"` |
| **Number** | One per type | Multiple instances per class |
| **Created by** | Designer / programmer | Constructor at runtime |
| **Analogy** | Architectural plan of a house | A specific house built from the plan |

**Example:** `Car` is a *class*; `myCar : Car` (red, 2024, KL-01-1234) is an *object* (instance of `Car`).

---

## 📗 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Module typically has a Part B question for 14 marks, with internal choice. We provide **TWO completely independent 14-mark question alternatives** below. Both carry sub-parts (a) and (b), each for **7 marks**.

---

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — 14 Marks

**Q: (a) Explain in detail the four essential characteristics of an object: identity, state, behavior, and responsibility. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The four essential characteristics of an object in Object-Oriented Modeling are:

**(i) Identity**
Identity is the property that distinguishes one object from all others, even when their states are identical. Every object has an intrinsic identity that is independent of its attributes. For example, two bank accounts of the same type with the same balance and same holder name are still two distinct objects because of their unique account numbers. Identity is a fundamental property — once created, an object retains its identity throughout its lifetime. In implementation, identity is often realized through a unique object ID, memory address, or surrogate primary key in a database.

**(ii) State**
The state of an object refers to the values of its attributes at a particular moment in time. State is dynamic and changes as the object responds to operations (messages). For example, the state of a `TrafficLight` object is one of `{Red, Yellow, Green}`. The state is *encapsulated* — the internal values are private and can be modified only through the object's public operations. This ensures data integrity and supports information hiding.

**(iii) Behavior**
Behavior is the set of operations (also called methods or services) that an object can perform. Behavior defines how an object acts and reacts to stimuli from the outside world. For example, a `Calculator` object supports `add()`, `subtract()`, `multiply()`, and `divide()`. Behavior is defined by the class but *executed* by the object. Behavior is what the object *does*; state is what the object *knows*.

**(iv) Responsibility**
Responsibility is a higher-level abstraction that captures the contractual obligations an object has toward other objects in the system. Responsibilities come in two flavors:
- **Knowing responsibilities**: Knowing its own state, knowing related objects, knowing things it can derive from its state.
- **Doing responsibilities**: Performing computations, creating other objects, coordinating activities, and initiating control in other objects.

For example, a `Customer` object is responsible for *knowing its own name and contact details* (knowing) and *placing orders, cancelling orders* (doing).

These four characteristics together allow an object to represent a real-world concept with full encapsulation, identity, and behavior.

---

**Q: (b) Consider a "Hospital Management System" with the following description: "A hospital has doctors, patients, and nurses. Doctors treat patients and write prescriptions. Patients are admitted to wards. Nurses assist doctors. Each doctor belongs to a department (e.g., Cardiology, Neurology). Each patient has a unique patient ID and medical history."**

**Perform object modeling for the above system by:**
1. **(i) Identifying classes, attributes, and operations (4 marks)**
2. **(ii) Drawing the class diagram with proper UML notation including visibility, multiplicity, and relationships (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

### (i) Identification of Classes, Attributes, and Operations

**Classes Identified:** `Doctor`, `Patient`, `Nurse`, `Department`, `Ward`, `Prescription`, `MedicalRecord`.

**Attributes and Operations for each class:**

| Class | Attributes | Operations |
|---|---|---|
| **Doctor** | `- doctorID : int`, `- name : String`, `- specialization : String`, `- deptID : int` | `+ treat(p : Patient) : void`, `+ writePrescription(p : Patient) : Prescription`, `+ getDept() : Department` |
| **Patient** | `- patientID : int`, `- name : String`, `- age : int`, `- medicalHistory : String`, `- wardID : int` | `+ getAdmitted() : void`, `+ getDischarged() : void`, `+ viewHistory() : String` |
| **Nurse** | `- nurseID : int`, `- name : String`, `- shift : String` | `+ assistDoctor(d : Doctor) : void`, `+ attendPatient(p : Patient) : void` |
| **Department** | `- deptID : int`, `- deptName : String`, `- location : String` | `+ addDoctor(d : Doctor) : void`, `+ getDoctors() : List<Doctor>` |
| **Ward** | `- wardID : int`, `- wardNumber : int`, `- capacity : int` | `+ admitPatient(p : Patient) : void`, `+ getOccupancy() : int` |
| **Prescription** | `- prescriptionID : int`, `- date : Date`, `- medicines : List<String>`, `- doctorID : int`, `- patientID : int` | `+ addMedicine(m : String) : void`, `+ printPrescription() : void` |
| **MedicalRecord** | `- recordID : int`, `- patientID : int`, `- diagnosis : String`, `- date : Date` | `+ addEntry(d : String) : void`, `+ view() : String` |

### (ii) Class Diagram (UML Notation)

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Doctor {
      -doctorID : int
      -name : String
      -specialization : String
      +treat(p : Patient) : void
      +writePrescription(p : Patient) : Prescription
    }
    class Patient {
      -patientID : int
      -name : String
      -age : int
      -medicalHistory : String
      +getAdmitted() : void
      +viewHistory() : String
    }
    class Nurse {
      -nurseID : int
      -name : String
      -shift : String
      +assistDoctor(d : Doctor) : void
    }
    class Department {
      -deptID : int
      -deptName : String
      +addDoctor(d : Doctor) : void
    }
    class Ward {
      -wardID : int
      -capacity : int
      +admitPatient(p : Patient) : void
    }
    class Prescription {
      -prescriptionID : int
      -date : Date
      +addMedicine(m : String) : void
    }
    class MedicalRecord {
      -recordID : int
      -diagnosis : String
      +addEntry(d : String) : void
    }

    Doctor "1" --> "*" Patient : treats
    Doctor "1" --> "*" Prescription : writes
    Patient "1" --> "*" MedicalRecord : has
    Patient "1" --> "0..1" Ward : admitted to
    Department "1" --> "*" Doctor : employs
    Nurse "*" --> "*" Doctor : assists
    Doctor "1" --> "*" Prescription : issues
    Patient "1" --> "*" Prescription : receives
```

**Explanation of Relationships:**
- A **Doctor treats** many `Patient` instances (1 to *).
- A **Patient** is admitted to at most one `Ward` (1 to 0..1).
- A **Department employs** many `Doctor` instances (1 to *).
- A **Doctor writes** many `Prescription` instances (1 to *).
- A **Patient has** a `MedicalRecord` (1 to *).

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — 14 Marks

**Q: (a) Explain the concept of a class. Discuss the relationship between a class and its objects. Why is it said that "a class is not an object"? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Concept of a Class:**

A **class** is an *abstract description* — a template, blueprint, or prototype — that defines the common attributes (state) and operations (behavior) shared by a set of objects. A class is a *type* in the object-oriented sense; it is the contract that all its instances must follow. In UML, a class is depicted as a rectangle divided into three sections: the class name, the attributes, and the operations.

For example, the class `Car` may define:
- **Attributes**: `color`, `model`, `speed`, `fuelLevel`
- **Operations**: `accelerate()`, `brake()`, `refuel()`, `getSpeed()`

**Relationship between a Class and its Objects:**

The relationship is one of **abstraction → instantiation**. A class is the *abstraction*; objects are the *concrete instances* created from that abstraction. The relationship can be summarized as:

1. **One-to-Many**: A single class can produce an unlimited number of objects. The class `Student` can be instantiated to produce `student1`, `student2`, `student3`, ..., `studentN`.
2. **Type-Instance**: The class is the *type*; the object is the *instance* of that type. Every object *belongs* to a class.
3. **State sharing**: The class defines the *set* of attributes; objects hold the *specific values* of those attributes.
4. **Behavior sharing**: The class defines the *set* of operations; objects *execute* those operations.
5. **Memory**: The class definition is loaded once; each object occupies its own memory region for its state.

**Why "A Class is Not an Object":**

It is incorrect to say "a class is an object" because:
- A class is a *category or type*; an object is a *specific member* of that category. A class is a *meta-entity* — it describes what an object *will look like*, but it does not occupy a specific memory location with specific attribute values.
- A class has no **identity** in the same sense an object does — it cannot be addressed as a unique runtime entity.
- A class has no **state** — it merely *defines* what state is possible. The state exists only in objects.
- A class has no **independent existence** at runtime — it serves as the factory from which objects are born. Without objects, a class is just a static definition in the program code.
- The *analogy* is clear: The class is the *concept of a Chair*; the object is *a specific chair in room 101*. The concept of "chair" cannot sit down; only a specific chair can.

Hence, the precise statement is: **"An object is an instance of a class"** — not the reverse.

---

**Q: (b) Consider the following scenario: "An online e-commerce website allows customers to browse products, add them to a cart, and place orders. Each product has a unique product ID, name, price, and stock count. Customers can write reviews for products they have purchased. The system tracks orders with order ID, date, total amount, and a list of products ordered."**

**Identify:**
1. **(i) The classes, their key attributes, and the relationships between them (3 marks)**
2. **(ii) The active and passive objects in the system, with justification (2 marks)**
3. **(iii) Construct the object diagram showing a snapshot of one order containing two products purchased by one customer (2 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

### (i) Classes, Attributes, and Relationships

| Class | Key Attributes | Key Operations |
|---|---|---|
| **Customer** | `- customerID : int`, `- name : String`, `- email : String` | `+ browse() : void`, `+ addToCart(p : Product) : void`, `+ placeOrder() : Order`, `+ writeReview(p : Product, r : Review) : void` |
| **Product** | `- productID : int`, `- name : String`, `- price : float`, `- stockCount : int` | `+ updateStock(qty : int) : void`, `+ getPrice() : float` |
| **Cart** | `- cartID : int`, `- customerID : int`, `- items : List<Product>` | `+ addItem(p : Product) : void`, `+ removeItem(p : Product) : void`, `+ getTotal() : float` |
| **Order** | `- orderID : int`, `- date : Date`, `- totalAmount : float`, `- products : List<Product>`, `- customerID : int` | `+ calculateTotal() : float`, `+ confirmOrder() : void`, `+ getStatus() : String` |
| **Review** | `- reviewID : int`, `- rating : int`, `- comment : String`, `- productID : int`, `- customerID : int` | `+ post() : void`, `+ edit() : void` |

**Relationships:**
- `Customer` *places* `Order` — `1` to `*` (one customer, many orders)
- `Order` *contains* `Product` — `1` to `*` (one order, many products)
- `Customer` *owns* `Cart` — `1` to `1` (one cart per customer)
- `Customer` *writes* `Review` for `Product` — `*` to `*` (many-to-many)

### (ii) Active and Passive Objects

| Object | Type | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| **`OrderProcessor`** (hypothetical) | **Active** | Has its own thread of control; periodically checks for new orders, processes payments, and updates stock — initiates activity on its own. |
| **`NotificationService`** | **Active** | Sends email/SMS notifications to customers asynchronously; runs in its own thread; not waiting to be called. |
| **`Customer`** | **Passive** | Does not perform any action on its own; reacts only when the user logs in and clicks buttons. |
| **`Product`** | **Passive** | Just data — no thread; responds only when an `addToCart()` or `updateStock()` is called. |
| **`Order`** | **Passive** | Waits for status updates; does not initiate actions. |
| **`Review`** | **Passive** | Just a data record created when a customer submits it. |

**Justification Rule:** An object is *active* if it has its own thread of control and can initiate activities without external stimulus. A *passive* object waits to be invoked.

### (iii) Object Diagram — Snapshot of One Order

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[cust1 : Customer\ncustomerID: 1001\nname: Arun\nemail: arun@x.com]
    B[ord1 : Order\norderID: 5001\ndate: 2024-03-15\ntotalAmount: 75000.0]
    C[prod1 : Product\nproductID: P101\nname: Laptop\nprice: 50000.0]
    D[prod2 : Product\nproductID: P102\nname: Headphones\nprice: 25000.0]
    E[rev1 : Review\nreviewID: 7001\nrating: 5]

    A -- places --> B
    B -- contains --> C
    B -- contains --> D
    A -- writes --> E
    E -- for --> C
```

**Snapshot Description:**
- Customer `cust1` (Arun) has placed `ord1` (Order #5001, dated 2024-03-15, total ₹75,000).
- Order `ord1` contains two products: `prod1` (Laptop, ₹50,000) and `prod2` (Headphones, ₹25,000).
- Arun has also written a 5-star review `rev1` for the Laptop `prod1`.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls to Avoid**
>
> 1. **Confusing attributes with classes:** When extracting classes from a problem statement, students often make the error of selecting adjectives or values as classes. Example: "available" is a *state value*, not a class; "Book" is the class. Treat state values as **enumerations or attributes**, not classes.
>
> 2. **Omitting visibility modifiers:** KTU valuation strictly expects you to prefix attributes and operations with the visibility symbols `+`, `-`, `#`. A class diagram drawn *without* visibility markers loses **1-2 marks**.
>
> 3. **Missing multiplicity:** Associations *must* have multiplicity indicators at both ends. A plain line with no multiplicity loses marks.
>
> 4. **Forgetting to mark active objects:** If the question asks to identify active and passive objects, students often forget the *justification*. Just listing the names without the "why" loses 1 mark.
>
> 5. **Class-Object mix-up in object diagrams:** Object diagrams must use **underlined instance names** with the format `instanceName : ClassName` (e.g., `cust1 : Customer`). Writing `Customer : cust1` is wrong and costs 1 mark.
>
> 6. **Skipping the link vs. association distinction:** When the question asks "explain links and associations," students often define only one. Both are needed: **Link is to association as object is to class**.

---

## 🚀 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (High-Density Revision Checklist)

> **Print this section. This is your last-minute revision sheet for the KTU exam.**

- [x] **Object** = discrete entity with **Identity + State + Behavior**. The triad is mandatory in definitions.
- [x] **Class** = blueprint/template; an object is an **instance** of a class. Class is the type, object is the instance.
- [x] **Three pillars to memorize for object** → **Identity, State, Behavior** (plus **Responsibility** for 7-mark answers).
- [x] **Class has 3 compartments in UML** → **Class Name (top), Attributes (middle), Operations (bottom)**.
- [x] **Visibility symbols** → `+` public, `-` private, `#` protected, `~` package.
- [x] **UML object notation** → `instanceName : ClassName` written with **underline**.
- [x] **Link** = instance-level connection; **Association** = class-level connection.
- [x] **Multiplicity is placed at the end of the association line** nearest to the class whose instances are being counted.
- [x] **Active object** = has its own thread of control; **Passive object** = does not initiate activity.
- [x] **Object lifecycle** = Creation (constructor) → Use (state changes via operations) → Destruction (garbage collection / destructor).
- [x] **Nouns → candidate classes**; **Verbs → candidate operations**; **Adjectives → candidate attributes** (the standard OMT extraction rule).
- [x] **Class objectification rule** → If a noun has its own attributes, behaviors, and identity, it is a class; otherwise, it is an attribute of another class.
- [x] **Classic KTU domains** for examples → Library Management, Hospital Management, Banking, E-commerce, Student Information System.
- [x] **Real-world analogy** → Class = architectural blueprint; Object = actual building. Class = cookie cutter; Object = cookie. Class = concept of "Student"; Object = Arun (a specific student).
- [x] **Object Modeling process (7 steps)** → Problem Statement → Extract Nouns → Filter Classes → Extract Verbs → Assign Operations → Extract Adjectives as Attributes → Build Class Diagram.
- [x] **Encapsulation** = state is hidden; access is through public operations only.
- [x] **Cardinality formula** → `|O| = count of instances` of a class. The class itself is *count = 1* (one definition), but its instances are *count = N*.
- [x] **KTU common exam trap** → "A class is an object" — WRONG. "An object is an instance of a class" — CORRECT.
- [x] **Diagram must-haves** → Class name, attributes with visibility and type, operations with visibility and signature, multiplicity on both association ends, arrows for direction.
- [x] **Responsibility** = higher-level contract; comes in two flavors: *knowing* and *doing*.
- [x] **State change** happens *only* through invoking operations — never by direct external modification (encapsulation principle).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Links and Associations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCNT604 - OBJECT ORIENTED MODELING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module - 1
> - **Topic:** Links and Associations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Association (Design-Time Concept):** An *association* is a **semantic relationship between two or more classifiers** (typically classes) that specifies a structural connection in the UML class model. It describes a static, type-level relationship indicating that instances of the involved classes *can* be connected, and it captures the multiplicity, role names, and navigation constraints of that connection.

> [!NOTE]
> **Link (Run-Time Concept):** A *link* is a **temporal, runtime instantiation of an association**. While an association describes the *possibility* of a relationship between classes, a link is the actual, concrete tuple of object references that exists in memory during program execution. Mathematically, if association **R ⊆ A × B** is a set of ordered pairs between class extents, then a **link** is a single element **(a, b) ∈ R** where **a ∈ A** and **b ∈ B**.

The cardinality mapping between these two concepts is foundational:

| Level | UML Term | Set-Theoretic View |
|---|---|---|
| **Type / Schema** | Association | A relation $R \subseteq C_1 \times C_2 \times \dots \times C_n$ |
| **Instance / State** | Link | A tuple $(o_1, o_2, \dots, o_n) \in R$ |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Blueprint vs. Building" Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plain-English Analogy for First-Time Learners:**
> Think of an **association** as a **blueprint sentence** written on an architect's drawing — *"A `Department` employs many `Employee` objects."* It is a rule, a schema, a promise embedded into the design.
> 
> Now think of a **link** as a **specific, real-world event** — *"Department CS-101 currently employs Employee E042 (Riya) and Employee E089 (Arjun)."* That specific pairing of objects is a single link. The blueprint can exist without any employee actually being hired, but no link can exist without the association permitting it.
> 
> **Mnemonic:** *Association = Agreement, Link = Actual Handshake.*

## 1.3 Why This Distinction Matters in KTU Examinations

- **Designers** reason in *associations* when drawing class diagrams.
- **Runtime systems** populate *links* dynamically when objects are created and referenced.
- Examiners frequently test whether the student can correctly classify a scenario as a *schema* (association) versus an *instance* (link).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Anatomy of an Association

An association in UML is **not** a single line — it is a structured entity with the following orthogonal axes (the "four-headed beast" of UML association design):

1. **Name** — A verbal label describing the relationship (e.g., `employs`, `manages`).
2. **Roles** — Endpoint identifiers at each class-end describing *what* the neighboring class represents in the relationship (e.g., `employer`, `employee`).
3. **Multiplicity** — Cardinality constraints at each end (e.g., `1`, `*`, `0..1`, `1..*`, `n..m`).
4. **Navigation** — Arrow direction indicating which class can directly access the other.

## 2.2 The Link — Mechanics of Instantiation

When the program executes, the static association becomes a populated graph of links. Each link is a *physical pointer/reference* held in memory:

$$\text{link} \;=\; (o_A, o_B, \dots, o_n) \;\;\text{where}\;\; o_i \in \text{extent}(C_i)$$

The **extent** of a class $C$, written $\text{ext}(C)$, is the runtime set of all live objects of that class. Therefore, the *complete* population of an association $R$ at any instant is:

$$\text{links}(R) \;\subseteq\; \text{ext}(C_1) \times \text{ext}(C_2) \times \dots \times \text{ext}(C_n)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Cardinality Invariant:** For every $o \in \text{ext}(C_1)$, the number of links incident to $o$ **must** satisfy the multiplicity declared on the $C_1$-end of the association. Violating this invariant at runtime is a **model–code inconsistency** that KTU examiners love to test.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of `|` to keep markdown tables intact.

| Symbol / Notation | Meaning | Constraint / Range | Unit / Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| $R \subseteq C_1 \times C_2$ | Binary association as a set | $C_1, C_2$ are class extents | Set-theoretic |
| $\text{ext}(C)$ | Live object set of class $C$ | $\text{ext}(C) \neq \emptyset$ at runtime | Finite multiset |
| $m..n$ | Multiplicity range | $m \geq 0$, $n \geq m$, $n \in \mathbb{N} \cup \{*\}$ | Constraint |
| $*$ | Unbounded multiplicity | Equivalent to $0..\infty$ | Cardinality |
| $\text{links}(R)$ | Runtime set of links | $\text{links}(R) \subseteq \text{ext}(C_1) \times \text{ext}(C_2)$ | Set |
| $\deg(o, R)$ | Degree of object $o$ in $R$ | $m \leq \deg(o,R) \leq n$ for declared $m..n$ | Integer |
| $\text{agg}$ | Aggregation (weak "whole–part") | $C_{\text{part}} \in \text{ext}(C_{\text{whole}})$ semantically | Semantic |
| $\text{comp}$ | Composition (strong ownership) | Part destroyed with whole; $\deg(\text{whole}, R) = 1$ | Cardinality = 1 |

## 2.4 Engineering Utility — Why This Topic Matters in the Industry

- **Database Schema Generation:** OOAD associations map directly to *foreign-key constraints* in relational schemas. A `1..*` multiplicity typically forces a *parent–child* table design.
- **Object-Relational Mappers (Hibernate, Entity Framework):** The distinction guides whether to use a `@OneToMany`, `@ManyToOne`, or `@ManyToMany` annotation.
- **Memory Leak Diagnosis:** Distinguishing associations (compile-time) from links (runtime) is critical when debugging dangling references — a *link* may persist after the related object is garbage-collected, producing a leak.
- **Microservices / API Design:** A bidirectional association may require synchronization across services, often forcing the architect to choose *event-driven* propagation.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Deriving the Multiplicity Constraint Equation

**Problem Setup:** Given a binary association $R$ between classes $A$ and $B$ with multiplicities $m_A..n_A$ on the $A$-end and $m_B..n_B$ on the $B$-end, derive the constraints that must hold on the set $\text{links}(R) \subseteq \text{ext}(A) \times \text{ext}(B)$ at any consistent runtime.

### Step 1 — Define the Link Set

By definition of an association as a binary relation, the populated links form a subset of the Cartesian product of the two class extents:

$$\text{links}(R) \;\subseteq\; \text{ext}(A) \times \text{ext}(B)$$

*Textual logic:* Every link is an ordered pair consisting of one live $A$-object and one live $B$-object. No pair outside the Cartesian product can possibly be a link because one or both objects would not exist in memory.

### Step 2 — Project onto the $A$-Axis

For any fixed $a \in \text{ext}(A)$, the set of $B$-objects linked to $a$ is the projection:

$$L_A(a) \;=\; \{\, b \in \text{ext}(B) \;\vert\; (a, b) \in \text{links}(R) \,\}$$

*Textual logic:* We "slice" the link set along the $A$-axis, holding $a$ constant and asking: *which $B$-objects are paired with this particular $a$?*

### Step 3 — Apply the Multiplicity Bound on the $A$-End

The declared multiplicity $m_A..n_A$ on the $A$-end specifies that *every* $A$-object must be linked to **at least $m_A$** and **at most $n_A$** $B$-objects. Therefore, the cardinality of $L_A(a)$ must satisfy:

$$m_A \;\leq\; \vert L_A(a) \vert \;\leq\; n_A \quad \forall\, a \in \text{ext}(A)$$

*Textual logic:* The lower bound $m_A$ prevents "orphan" $A$-objects; the upper bound $n_A$ prevents "over-coupling" $A$-objects.

### Step 4 — Symmetrically Apply on the $B$-End

Repeating the same argument for the $B$-axis:

$$L_B(b) \;=\; \{\, a \in \text{ext}(A) \;\vert\; (a, b) \in \text{links}(R) \,\}$$

$$m_B \;\leq\; \vert L_B(b) \vert \;\leq\; n_B \quad \forall\, b \in \text{ext}(B)$$

### Step 5 — Compose the Full Constraint System

The complete model-consistency constraint is the conjunction of both end-constraints:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\forall\, a \in \text{ext}(A): \quad & m_A \leq \vert L_A(a) \vert \leq n_A \\
\forall\, b \in \text{ext}(B): \quad & m_B \leq \vert L_B(b) \vert \leq n_B
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **Multiplicity Invariant Theorem** for binary associations. It is the equation examiners will silently expect you to "feel" when answering design questions.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Library–Book Domain

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Scenario:** A `Library` lends `Book` objects. Declared multiplicities: `Library 1 — 0..* Book` and `Book 1..1 — Library`.
> 
> Suppose the runtime state is: `Library L1 = {B1, B2, B3}`, and `L2 = {}`. Verify whether this is a valid link population.

**Step 1 — Identify extents**

$$\text{ext}(\text{Library}) = \{L1, L2\}, \quad \text{ext}(\text{Book}) = \{B1, B2, B3\}$$

**Step 2 — Build the link set**

$$\text{links}(R) = \{(L1, B1),\; (L1, B2),\; (L1, B3)\}$$

*Textual logic:* We enumerate every active loan as an ordered pair. Note that $L2$ has no links because it has not lent any book.

**Step 3 — Test constraint on the `Book`-end (1..1)**

For each $b \in \text{ext}(\text{Book})$, compute $\vert L_B(b) \vert$:

- $L_B(B1) = \{L1\} \Rightarrow \vert L_B(B1) \vert = 1$ ✓ (satisfies $1 \leq 1 \leq 1$)
- $L_B(B2) = \{L1\} \Rightarrow \vert L_B(B2) \vert = 1$ ✓
- $L_B(B3) = \{L1\} \Rightarrow \vert L_B(B3) \vert = 1$ ✓

**Step 4 — Test constraint on the `Library`-end (0..*)**

- $L_A(L1) = \{B1, B2, B3\} \Rightarrow \vert L_A(L1) \vert = 3$ ✓ (satisfies $0 \leq 3 \leq *$)
- $L_A(L2) = \emptyset \Rightarrow \vert L_A(L2) \vert = 0$ ✓ (satisfies $0 \leq 0 \leq *$)

**Conclusion:** The runtime state is **consistent** with the declared association. Had a book been created without any library holding it ($\vert L_B(b) \vert = 0$), the system would be in a *constraint violation* requiring remediation (e.g., assign the book to some library or remove the book).

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use What?

| Question to Ask | Choose This Construct | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Does the related object **outlive** the owner? | **Association** (plain) | No lifetime dependency |
| Is the related object **part of** the owner but reusable? | **Aggregation** (open diamond) | Weak ownership |
| Is the related object **strictly owned** and inseparable? | **Composition** (filled diamond) | Strong ownership, lifetime tied |
| Does the same class play both ends? | **Reflexive Association** | Self-relationship (e.g., `Employee manages Employee`) |
| Are there **three or more** participating classes? | **N-ary Association** (large diamond) | Ternary or higher |
| Do you need to attach **attributes to the link itself**? | **Association Class** | Link carries data (e.g., `enrollmentDate` on `Student–Course`) |
| Is access via a **key** (e.g., `seatNumber`)? | **Qualified Association** | Maps a key to a unique related object |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Class Diagram — Core Association Types

```mermaid
classDiagram
    direction LR

    class University {
        +String name
        +String location
    }

    class Department {
        +String deptId
        +String deptName
    }

    class Professor {
        +String empId
        +String name
        +double salary
    }

    class Course {
        +String courseCode
        +String title
        +int credits
    }

    class TextBook {
        +String isbn
        +String title
        +String author
    }

    class Semester {
        +int year
        +String term
    }

    class Enrollment {
        +Date enrollDate
        +char grade
    }

    University "1" --> "1..*" Department : owns
    Department "1" o-- "1..*" Professor : employs
    Department "1" --> "1..*" Course : offers
    Course "1" *-- "1" TextBook : primaryText
    Professor "1" --> "0..*" Course : teaches
    Course "0..*" -- "0..*" Semester : scheduledIn
    Course "0..*" -- "0..*" Professor : takenBy
    Enrollment ..> Course : links
    Enrollment ..> Professor : links
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mermaid Legend Decoded (UML → Mermaid):**
> - `-->` plain solid arrow → navigable **association**
> - `o--` open diamond → **aggregation** (weak whole–part)
> - `*--` filled diamond → **composition** (strong ownership)
> - `--` plain line → **non-navigable / bidirectional** association
> - `..>` dashed arrow → **dependency / link** (runtime connection represented via the `Enrollment` association class)

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Link Instantiation Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[stepA: Class Diagram Loaded] --> B[stepB: Compiler Generates Association Definitions]
    B --> C[stepC: Memory Allocates extents for each Class]
    C --> D[stepD: Objects Instantiated by Program]
    D --> E[stepE: Assignment Statement Creates Link]
    E --> F[stepF: Multiplicity Invariant Check]
    F --> G{stepG: Invariant Holds?}
    G -- YES --> H[stepH: Object Graph Stabilized]
    G -- NO --> I[stepI: Throw Constraint Violation Exception]
    H --> J[stepJ: Garbage Collector May Remove Unlinked Objects]
    I --> K[stepK: Programmer Fixes Code or Reinstates Link]
    K --> E
    J --> L[stepL: System Continues Execution]
```

**Reading Guide for the Flowchart:**

1. **Compile-Time** (steps A–B): The association is a *static rule* in the class diagram.
2. **Run-Time Initialization** (steps C–D): The JVM/CLR allocates memory and instantiates objects.
3. **Link Formation** (step E): An assignment like `dept.professors.add(prof1)` materializes a *link*.
4. **Invariant Enforcement** (steps F–G): The OCL (Object Constraint Language) or runtime checks verify multiplicity bounds.
5. **Steady State / Failure Recovery** (steps H–L): Either the object graph is consistent, or a violation triggers corrective action.

## 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram — Link Lifecycle of a Single Object

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Unlinked: object created
    Unlinked --> Linked: link added
    Linked --> Unlinked: link removed
    Linked --> Destroyed: object garbage collected
    Unlinked --> Destroyed: object garbage collected
    Destroyed --> [*]
```

**Interpretation:** A single object can transition between *unlinked* (no associations active) and *linked* (one or more links present) states during its lifetime. The *destroyed* state corresponds to object deallocation, after which all its links are automatically severed by the runtime.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 (3 Marks)
> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question]**
> **Differentiate between an association and a link in UML. Illustrate with an example.**

**Model Answer:**

An **association** is a static, design-time relationship declared between two or more classes in a class diagram. It specifies the *possibility* of a connection and defines the multiplicity, roles, and navigation rules. In contrast, a **link** is a runtime instance of an association — a concrete tuple of object references that exists in memory during program execution.

**Example:** Consider the classes `Teacher` and `Student`. The association "`Teacher teaches Student`" is a design-time statement, possibly with multiplicity `1..*` on each end. At runtime, when a particular `Teacher t1` is connected to a particular `Student s7`, the pair `(t1, s7)` forms a single **link**, an instance of that association.

In set-theoretic notation: if $R$ is the association, then $R \subseteq \text{ext}(\text{Teacher}) \times \text{ext}(\text{Student})$, and a link is a single element $(t, s) \in R$.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for defining each, 1 mark for the example.)*

### Question A2 (3 Marks)
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]**
> **Explain the concept of an association class with a suitable example. When would you prefer it over a plain association?**

**Model Answer:**

An **association class** is a UML construct that allows you to attach *attributes, operations, and relationships* to an association itself, rather than to either of the participating classes. It is depicted as a class symbol connected to the association line by a dashed line.

**Example:** In a `Student–Course` many-to-many relationship, the link itself carries the attribute `enrollmentDate` and the operation `assignGrade()`. Neither of these belongs solely to `Student` (a student may enroll in many courses) nor solely to `Course` (a course has many students). Hence, an association class `Enrollment` is introduced with attributes `enrollmentDate`, `grade`, and possibly behavior like `withdraw()`.

**When to prefer it:** Use an association class whenever the data or behavior is *intrinsic to the relationship* rather than to either participant — typically when the association is *many-to-many* and link-level data is required.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark definition, 1 mark example, 1 mark justification.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **Instructions (KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Pattern):** *Answer the question choosing either A or B. Each sub-part carries 7 marks.*

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question]**
> **(a)** Discuss the various types of associations in UML with neat diagrams. Mention the role of multiplicity and association ends. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Consider the following domain: *"A Company operates in one or more Departments. Each Department employs one or more Employees, and every Employee belongs to exactly one Department. Each Employee is assigned zero or more Projects, but a Project may be assigned to multiple Employees."*
> Draw the UML class diagram with all appropriate associations, multiplicities, and role names. Identify any aggregation or composition relations if applicable. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

The principal types of associations in UML are:

1. **Binary Association** — Connects exactly two classes. Example: `Author writes Book`.
2. **Unary / Reflexive Association** — A class relates to itself. Example: `Employee manages Employee`.
3. **N-ary Association** — Three or more classes participate. Example: a ternary association between `Student`, `Course`, and `Semester`.
4. **Aggregation (weak whole–part)** — Represented by an open diamond. The *part* can exist independently of the *whole*. Example: `Department has Team`.
5. **Composition (strong whole–part)** — Represented by a filled diamond. The *part* cannot exist without the *whole*. Example: `House has Room`.
6. **Qualified Association** — Uses a *qualifier* (key) at one end to map a key to a unique related object. Example: `Library has Book` qualified by `ISBN`.
7. **Association Class** — As described in Question A2, attaches attributes/operations to the link.

**Multiplicity and Association Ends:**
- *Multiplicity* specifies how many objects of one class can be linked to one object of the other class. Common values include `1`, `*`, `0..1`, `1..*`, and `m..n`.
- *Association ends* are the terminations of the association line at each class. Each end has a *role name* (e.g., `employer`, `employee`) describing the part played by the neighboring class.
- *Navigation* is indicated by an arrow, showing which class can directly access the other.

*(Diagrammatic representation may be drawn by the student; the textual description above carries the marks.)*

**Model Solution (b):**

**Class Diagram (described in text, suitable for hand-drawing):**

```
Company "1" ---- "1..*" Department : operates
Department "1" o-- "1..*" Employee : employs
Employee "1..*" -- "0..*" Project : assignedTo
```

**Identification of Aggregation vs. Composition:**

- The relationship `Department employs Employee` is best modeled as **aggregation** (open diamond on `Department`), because an employee can theoretically exist without a department (e.g., during transfer or layoff). The `Department` is the *whole*; `Employee` is a *part* that has independent identity.
- If, however, the business rule strictly states *"an employee cannot exist outside a department"*, then we should choose **composition** instead.
- `Company operates Department` is a plain **association** with `1..*` multiplicity, indicating a company must operate at least one department.
- `Employee assignedTo Project` is a plain **many-to-many association** with `0..*` on both ends, allowing an employee to be unassigned temporarily.

**Multiplicity Justification Table:**

| End | Multiplicity | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| `Company` → `Department` | `1` | Each department belongs to exactly one company |
| `Department` → `Company` | `1..*` | A company operates at least one department |
| `Department` → `Employee` | `1` | Each employee belongs to exactly one department |
| `Employee` → `Department` | `1..*` | A department must have at least one employee |
| `Employee` → `Project` | `0..*` | An employee may have zero assignments |
| `Project` → `Employee` | `0..*` | A project may have zero or many assigned employees |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]**
> **(a)** Explain in detail the differences between aggregation and composition in UML. State the conditions under which each is preferred. Provide one example for each. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Draw a UML class diagram for an **Online Shopping System** with the following entities: `Customer`, `Order`, `OrderItem`, `Product`, and `Payment`. Use appropriate associations, multiplicities, and indicate any aggregation or composition. Justify each design decision. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

| Aspect | Aggregation (◇) | Composition (◆) |
|---|---|---|
| **Symbol** | Open (hollow) diamond on the *whole* side | Filled (solid) diamond on the *whole* side |
| **Lifetime Coupling** | Weak — the part can outlive the whole | Strong — the part is destroyed when the whole is destroyed |
| **Multiplicity on Whole End** | `0..1` or `1` | Almost always exactly `1` |
| **Sharing of Parts** | A part *can* be shared among multiple wholes | A part *cannot* be shared |
| **Implementation Hint** | The whole holds a *reference* (pointer) to the part | The whole *owns* the part; the part is a value object or has no independent existence |
| **Example** | `Department ◇—— Employee` | `Order ◆—— OrderItem` |
| **Verbal Test** | *"Has-a, but can give away"* | *"Has-a, and the part dies with the whole"* |

**When to prefer Aggregation:** When the *part* has an independent lifecycle and may be shared. Example: a `Library` aggregates `Book`; the books can be transferred to another library, and a book can exist without being in any specific library.

**When to prefer Composition:** When the *part* has no meaningful existence outside the *whole*. Example: an `Order` is composed of `OrderItem`s; when the order is deleted, all its items are deleted automatically.

**The Lifetime Test (Examiner's Trick):**
> Ask: *"If the whole is destroyed, must the part also be destroyed?"* If **yes**, use **composition**. If **no**, use **aggregation**.

**Model Solution (b):**

**UML Class Diagram (textual representation for hand-drawing):**

```
Customer "1" --> "0..*" Order : places
Order "1" *-- "1..*" OrderItem : contains
Product "1" <-- "0..*" OrderItem : references
Order "1" --> "1" Payment : settledBy
```

**Detailed Class Structure:**

- `Customer` (name, email, phone)
- `Order` (orderId, orderDate, totalAmount, status)
- `OrderItem` (quantity, unitPrice, lineTotal)
- `Product` (productId, name, price, stock)
- `Payment` (paymentId, amount, method, date)

**Justification Table:**

| Association | Type | Multiplicity | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| `Customer`–`Order` | Plain Association | `1` to `0..*` | A customer can place zero or more orders; every order belongs to exactly one customer |
| `Order`–`OrderItem` | **Composition** | `1` to `1..*` | An order item has no meaning without its parent order; deleting the order must delete all items |
| `OrderItem`–`Product` | Plain Association | `0..*` to `1` | Many order items can reference the same product (e.g., three customers ordering the same phone) |
| `Order`–`Payment` | Plain Association (1:1) | `1` to `1` | Each order has exactly one payment record for traceability; an order cannot be fully settled twice |

**Why Composition for `Order`–`OrderItem`?**
Because an `OrderItem` is meaningless once its parent `Order` is removed. There is no real-world scenario where an order item floats around without an enclosing order. This is the textbook signature of composition.

**Why Plain Association for `Product`–`OrderItem`?**
Because a `Product` has an independent existence in the catalog and is *shared* across many order items. Sharing rules out composition; absence of whole-part ownership rules out aggregation.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (Read Before Writing the Exam!):**
> 
> 1. **Conflating Association with Link (–2 marks):** Many students use these terms interchangeably. The examiner expects the *schema* (association) vs *instance* (link) distinction. Always clarify which level you are discussing.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting Multiplicity Bounds (–1 to –2 marks):** A class diagram *without multiplicities* is considered incomplete in KTU valuation. Always annotate every association end with a multiplicity expression.
> 
> 3. **Confusing Aggregation and Composition Direction (–1 mark):** The diamond is placed on the **whole** side, not the part side. A common error: students place the diamond on `Employee` instead of `Department`. Verify by asking *"Which class contains/owns the other?"*
> 
> 4. **Drawing an Association Class as a Plain Class (–2 marks):** An association class must be connected to the **association line** by a *dashed* line, not connected directly to one of the participating classes. Many students lose marks by using a solid line.
> 
> 5. **Omitting Role Names in Reflexive Associations (–1 mark):** When a class is associated with itself (e.g., `Employee manages Employee`), role names (`manager`, `subordinate`) are **mandatory** to disambiguate the two ends.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Pin This Before the Exam!):**
> 
> - ⭐ **Association = schema-level (compile-time).** **Link = instance-level (run-time).**
> - ⭐ An association is a *set* $R \subseteq C_1 \times C_2 \times \dots \times C_n$. A link is a *single tuple* in $R$.
> - ⭐ The **multiplicity invariant** must hold for *every* object at *every* runtime instant: $m \leq \deg(o, R) \leq n$.
> - ⭐ **Aggregation (open diamond ◇):** weak ownership, parts can be shared and outlive the whole.
> - ⭐ **Composition (filled diamond ◆):** strong ownership, parts are destroyed with the whole, $\deg(\text{whole}, R) = 1$.
> - ⭐ **N-ary association:** 3 or more classes connected via a large diamond.
> - ⭐ **Association class:** attaches data/behavior to the *link*, not to either class — connected by a **dashed** line.
> - ⭐ **Qualified association:** uses a *qualifier* (key) to map an object to a unique related object.
> - ⭐ **Reflexive association:** a class associated with itself — *role names* are mandatory to avoid ambiguity.
> - ⭐ Always specify **multiplicity**, **role names**, and **association name** in a class diagram.
> - ⭐ The diamond in aggregation/composition is placed on the **whole** side, not the part side.
> - ⭐ The "**lifetime test**" decides aggregation vs. composition: *Does the part die with the whole?*
> - ⭐ The "**sharing test**" rules out composition: *Is the part shared across multiple wholes?*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advanced Links and Association Concepts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCNT604 - OBJECT ORIENTED MODELING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Advanced Links and Association Concepts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Link?

A **link** is a physical or conceptual connection between **object instances**. It is the *run-time* instantiation of an association. Where an association describes a *class of connections*, a link is an *individual connection* between two specific objects existing in memory.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (Rumbaugh / OMT):**
> A *link* is a tuple of object references drawn from the participating classes of an association. If `A` and `B` are classes, a link is a concrete pairing such as `(a₁, b₃)` drawn from `A × B`.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Telephone Call
Imagine a telephone network. The *association* is the rule "any person may call any other person." A *link* is a *specific call* happening right now between Mr. Roy and Ms. Sen. The rule (association) is permanent; the call (link) is transient. Drop the call → link vanishes. End the rule → the entire class of calls is removed.

---

## 1.2 What is an Association?

An **association** is the *structural* declaration that objects of one class are *semantically connected* to objects of another class. It is a first-class modelling element with:

1. A **name**
2. **Two roles** (one for each end)
3. A **multiplicity** at each end
4. Optionally an **association class** with its own attributes/operations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In OMT/UML, an association is a *type* whose instances are links. The cardinality of an association is therefore the cardinality of its link set.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Friend" Relationship
The association `Person --knows-- Person` is the *type* "friendship." The fact that *Riya* and *Aman* are friends is a *link* — one concrete instance of that association. Change one person's friend list, and you add or remove a *link*, not a *new association*.

---

## 1.3 The Four "Advanced" Categories of Links

The phrase **"Advanced Links and Association Concepts"** in the KTU 2024 syllabus refers to the refined taxonomy of links that go beyond plain association. There are four fundamental link/association categories (Rumbaugh's OMT canonical model):

| # | Link Type | Semantic Verb | UML Notation | OMT Notation |
|---|-----------|---------------|--------------|--------------|
| 1 | **Association link** | *has-a* / peer-to-peer | solid line | solid line with optional role |
| 2 | **Aggregation link** | *whole–part* (shared) | hollow diamond ◇ | hollow diamond |
| 3 | **Composition link** | *whole–part* (exclusive) | filled diamond ◆ | filled diamond |
| 4 | **Generalization link** | *is-a* (kind-of) | hollow triangle △ | hollow triangle |

A fifth, **dependency**, is a weaker cross-class relationship meaning *uses-a* (often realised as a parameter, local variable, or global reference).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction (Booch / Rumbaugh):**
> - **Association** = *has-a* (peer, equal standing, may outlive the other)
> - **Aggregation** = *whole–part* where the part can exist independently of the whole
> - **Composition** = *whole–part* where the part's lifecycle is *bound* to the whole
> - **Generalization** = *is-a* (taxonomic / inheritance)

---

## 1.4 Advanced *Association* Concepts

Beyond the link *types*, the word *association* itself is decorated with several advanced concepts:

1. **Multiplicity (Cardinality)** — how many objects participate at each end.
2. **Association Roles** — named ends that disambiguate the same class used twice.
3. **Association Class** — an association that owns its own attributes and operations.
4. **Qualified Association** — uses a *qualifier* (key) to reduce effective multiplicity.
5. **N-ary Association** — links among **three or more** classes simultaneously.
6. **Reflexive (Recursive) Association** — a class associated with itself.
7. **Ordered Association** — the link set is *sequenced*.
8. **Bag vs Set Semantics** — duplicates allowed (bag) or forbidden (set).
9. **Derived Association** — one that can be computed from others (shown dashed).
10. **Association Refinement** — splitting a many-to-many into intermediate link classes.

> [!NOTE]
> These ten concepts collectively form the "Advanced" qualifier in the KTU Module-1 syllabus. Mastery of all ten is the difference between a *2-mark recall answer* and a *7-mark analytical application answer*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Logic of Each Link Type

### 2.1.1 Association Link (Peer / "has-a")
- **Why it exists:** Two classes must exchange data or messages, but neither owns the other.
- **How to recognise:** No containment, no "is-a" wording, no exclusive lifecycle.
- **Multiplicity defaults:** unspecified ⇒ "0..*" (zero-or-more).
- **Engineering example:** `Student` -- `Course` (a student takes courses; a course has students).

### 2.1.2 Aggregation (Shared "whole–part")
- **Why it exists:** The *whole* is conceptually a container, but the *part* has independent existence.
- **How to recognise:** A `has-a` relationship *plus* a linguistic *part-of* test *plus* the part can be re-used in multiple wholes.
- **Key property:** Deleting the whole does *not* automatically delete the part.
- **Engineering example:** `Department` ◇— `Professor`. A professor is "part of" a department but can move to another department and outlive the original.

### 2.1.3 Composition (Strong "whole–part")
- **Why it exists:** The part cannot meaningfully exist without its owning whole.
- **How to recognise:** The *part* is *created* by the *whole*'s constructor, *destroyed* with it, and is *not shared*.
- **Key property:** Composite multiplicity at the *part* end is always `0..1` or `1` (exclusive parent).
- **Engineering example:** `Window` ◆— `Handle`. The handle is built with the window and ceases to be a handle when the window is demolished.

### 2.1.4 Generalization ("is-a")
- **Why it exists:** A class is a *specialisation* of another, inheriting structure and behaviour.
- **How to recognise:** Substitutable in `is-a` tests (Liskov); subclass adds or overrides.
- **Multiplicity:** Only the *parent* end has multiplicity (a `Student` *is a* `Person`, not *one of* `Person`).
- **Engineering example:** `Vehicle` △ `Car`, `Vehicle` △ `Truck`.

### 2.1.5 Dependency ("uses-a")
- **Why it exists:** A class uses another *temporarily* — typically as a method parameter, local variable, or static call.
- **How to recognise:** The supplier is *not* stored as a field; usage is local to a method.
- **Engineering example:** `ReportGenerator` - - - > `DateFormatter` (a formatter instance is created and used inside one method, then discarded).

---

## 2.2 The Five Multiplicity Values (KTU Cheat-Sheet)

| UML / OMT Notation | Meaning | Cardinality Range | Multiplicity Class |
|--------------------|---------|-------------------|--------------------|
| `1` | Exactly one | 1 | mandatory single |
| `0..1` | Zero or one | 0 to 1 | optional single |
| `*`  (or `0..*`) | Zero or more | 0..∞ | optional many |
| `1..*` | One or more | 1..∞ | mandatory many |
| `n..m` | Between n and m | n..m | bounded range |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Default when omitted:** If the KTU exam statement says "draw the class diagram" *and* multiplicity is not specified, write `*` on both ends. Examiners will treat unspecified multiplicity as `*`.

---

## 2.3 Association-Role Grammar

When *two* roles connect the *same* class, you **must** label both role ends to disambiguate. Example:

```
Class Company
Role employer
Multiplicity 1

Class Person
Role employee
Multiplicity 0..*
```

If the same class appears on both ends of a binary association, the association is **reflexive** (recursive). Example: `Employee --manages-- Employee`.

---

## 2.4 The Association-Class Rule (Formally)

An **association class** exists when the *link* itself has properties, not the endpoint objects.

$$
\text{AssociationClass} \iff \exists \; \text{attribute}(l) \;\; \text{or} \;\; \text{operation}(l) \;\; \text{for every link} \; l \in L_{AB}
$$

In words: *if* a property belongs to the *pair* `(a, b)` and not to `a` or `b` alone, it belongs to the **association class**. Classic example: `Student`—`Course` association with attribute `grade`; the `grade` is meaningless on `Student` alone or on `Course` alone — it is a property of the *enrolment* (i.e., the link).

---

## 2.5 Qualified Association — Multiplicity Reduction

A **qualifier** is a key attribute placed at the *target* end that *partitions* a many-valued association into a map. The effective multiplicity at the qualified end collapses from `*` to `1` (or `0..1`) for each unique key value.

$$
\text{EffectiveCardinality}(B) \;=\; \frac{\text{TotalObjects}(A)}{\text{DistinctKeyValues}} \;\; \text{(target-side post-qualification)}
$$

Engineering example: `Bank` — `Account[accountNo]` — `Account`. A `Bank` may have `*` accounts, but *given a specific account number*, only **1** account exists.

---

## 2.6 N-ary Association Rule

A **binary** association has 2 participants; a **ternary** has 3; an **n-ary** has n ≥ 3. The multiplicity on any end denotes the number of *link instances* that object participates in *for a single tuple of the other ends*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Examiner Tip: For ternary associations, you **cannot** decompose them into three binary associations without losing semantics. Draw a single diamond with three connecting lines.

---

## 2.7 KTU Real-World Engineering Utilities

| Domain | Advanced Link Used | Justification |
|--------|--------------------|---------------|
| Banking software | Composition of `Transaction` inside `Account` | A transaction has no existence outside an account session. |
| Hospital management | Qualified `Ward[bedNo]` from `Ward` to `Patient` | Each bed number is a unique key. |
| Compiler design | Generalization: `ASTNode` △ `BinaryOp`, `UnaryOp` | All AST nodes share traversal behaviour. |
| Embedded systems | Aggregation: `ECU` ◇— `Sensor` | A sensor may migrate between ECUs during reflash. |
| Database modelling | Association class on `Enrolment(Student, Course)` | `marks` and `semester` belong to the enrolment, not the student or course. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Decision Tree — Classifying Any Given Link

Whenever the KTU paper shows a verbal scenario, walk through this exhaustive **5-step classification** before drawing.

$$
\boxed{\text{Step 1: Is it a taxonomic relationship?}}
$$

If the natural-language phrase contains *"is-a", "kind-of", "type-of"*, the link is a **Generalization** (use hollow triangle).

$$
\boxed{\text{Step 2: Does the "part" die with the "whole"?}}
$$

If the part's lifetime is strictly inside the whole's lifetime, the link is **Composition** (filled diamond). Otherwise, proceed to step 3.

$$
\boxed{\text{Step 3: Is the "part" still a meaningful concept without the "whole"?}}
$$

If yes, the link is **Aggregation** (hollow diamond). The whole acts as a logical container.

$$
\boxed{\text{Step 4: Does the supplier appear as a field/attribute of the client?}}
$$

If **no** (only a method-local or parameter use), the link is a **Dependency** (dashed arrow).

$$
\boxed{\text{Step 5: Otherwise}}
$$

The link is a plain **Association** (solid line). Now classify the multiplicity and the role labels.

---

## 3.2 Worked Analytical Walkthrough — University Domain

**Scenario:** *"A university contains many departments. Each department employs many professors and offers many courses. A professor may teach many courses, and a course may be taught by several professors. A department is chaired by exactly one professor, who is also a professor of that department. Each course has a syllabus and a credit value, and a course may be a prerequisite for other courses."*

### Step A — Identify candidate classes
`University`, `Department`, `Professor`, `Course`.

### Step B — Identify links from the prose

1. *"University contains departments"* — word "contains" + departments depend on university for administrative identity, but logically departments may exist as academic units even after administrative reorganisation. We model as **Aggregation** (◇) with multiplicity `1` on University side, `1..*` on Department side.
2. *"Department employs many professors"* — "employs" ⇒ peer/contractual; professors can move departments. **Aggregation** (shared) — multiplicity `1..*` on Professor side, `1` on Department side.
3. *"Department offers many courses"* — courses can be retired but a course catalog is owned by the department. **Aggregation** (shared) — `1` Department → `*` Courses.
4. *"Professor chairs Department"* — exactly one chair, the chair is itself a professor. **Reflexive association** on `Professor` with role labels `chair` (Department end) and `chairedBy` (Professor end), multiplicity `0..1` (not every professor chairs; not every dept must have a chair if we allow vacancies) and `1` (each department has one chair).
5. *"Professor teaches many courses; course taught by several professors"* — pure peer-to-peer **Association**, `*` on both sides. Because each `(professor, course)` pair has the property *semester* and *classroom*, we attach an **Association class** `TeachingAssignment` carrying these attributes.
6. *"Course has a syllabus and credit value"* — these are **attributes of Course**, not of any link.
7. *"Course may be a prerequisite for other courses"* — **Reflexive association** on `Course` with role labels `prerequisiteFor` and `hasPrerequisite`, both ends `0..*` (a course can have multiple prerequisites and be prerequisite for many).

### Step C — Confirm classification using the decision tree

| Verb in prose | Decision step reached | Final link type |
|---------------|-----------------------|----------------|
| contains | step 3 (parts survive) | Aggregation |
| employs | step 3 (parts survive) | Aggregation |
| offers | step 3 (parts survive) | Aggregation |
| chairs | step 5 + role labelling | Reflexive Association |
| teaches | step 5 | Association + Association Class |
| prerequisite for | step 5 + same class on both ends | Reflexive Association |

### Step D — Class Diagram (described in text; rendered in Section 4)

- `University 1 ◇— 1..* Department`
- `Department 1 ◇— 1..* Professor`
- `Department 1 ◇— * Course`
- `Department 1 — 0..1 Professor` (with role `chair` on the Professor end)
- `Professor * — * Course` (with association class `TeachingAssignment`)
- `Course * — * Course` (with roles `prerequisiteFor` / `hasPrerequisite`)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU validation rule:** The multiplicity `*` on a reflexive `Course` association is correct because a course may have *zero* prerequisites (a fresh course) and may be prerequisite for *zero* future courses. If the prose had said "every advanced course must have at least one prerequisite," multiplicity would change to `1..*` on the prerequisite side.

---

## 3.3 Deriving Multiplicities from English Prose — Mapping Table

| English phrase in problem statement | Multiplicity on the closest class |
|--------------------------------------|-----------------------------------|
| "exactly one" | `1` |
| "a single" | `1` |
| "one or more" | `1..*` |
| "many", "several", "multiple" | `*` (interpret as 0..*) unless qualified |
| "zero or more", "optional many" | `*` |
| "at most one" | `0..1` |
| "may be", "can be", "could be" | `0..1` for a single, `*` for many |
| "must be" | `1` for a single, `1..*` for many |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often confuse "many" with `1..*`. Strictly, "many" in OMT defaults to `*` (which permits zero). Only if the prose says *"one or more"* is it `1..*`.

---

## 3.4 Association Class Derivation — Step-by-Step

Suppose the prose says: *"Each student enrolled in a course receives a grade. A student may be enrolled in many courses; a course may have many students."*

$$
\text{Observation: } \text{grade} \notin \text{attrs}(\text{Student}) \cup \text{attrs}(\text{Course})
$$

$$
\therefore \text{grade} \in \text{attrs}(\text{link}_{\text{Student} \times \text{Course}})
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Therefore: introduce association class } \mathbf{Enrolment} \text{ with attribute } \text{grade}.}
$$

A common student error is to *add `grade` as an attribute of `Student`*, which would erroneously let a student carry a different grade for the same course. Always ask: *"Does this property vary with the partner, or is it intrinsic to the object alone?"*

---

## 3.5 Qualified Association Derivation

Suppose: *"A library has many books. A book copy has a unique accession number within the library."*

$$
\text{Pre-qualification multiplicity: } \text{Library} \xrightarrow{1 \; \text{to} \; *} \text{BookCopy}
$$

Adding qualifier `accessionNo` on the `BookCopy` end:

$$
\text{Post-qualification: } \text{Library} \xrightarrow{1 \; \text{to} \; 1} \text{BookCopy}_{\text{[accessionNo]}}
$$

The qualifier *partitions* the set of book copies; each `(library, accessionNo)` pair maps to **exactly one** book copy.

---

## 3.6 N-ary (Ternary) Association Derivation

*"A doctor prescribes a medication to a patient on a specific date. The same doctor may prescribe the same medication to the same patient on different dates."*

Decomposing this into three binary associations would lose the date property. Hence we model:

$$
\langle \text{Doctor}, \text{Medication}, \text{Patient}, \text{date} \rangle
$$

as a single **ternary association class** `Prescription`. The multiplicity on each end:

- `Doctor`: a doctor may appear in many prescriptions ⇒ `*`
- `Medication`: a medication may appear in many prescriptions ⇒ `*`
- `Patient`: a patient may appear in many prescriptions ⇒ `*`

But for *a given* triple `(doctor, medication, patient)`, multiplicity on the `date` slot is `0..*` (multiple prescriptions possible over time).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Class Diagram — University Domain (Mermaid)

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class University {
        +String name
        +String address
    }
    class Department {
        +String deptCode
        +String name
    }
    class Professor {
        +int empId
        +String name
        +String designation
    }
    class Course {
        +String courseCode
        +String title
        +int credits
    }
    class TeachingAssignment {
        +String semester
        +String classroom
    }
    class Enrolment {
        +char grade
    }
    class Student {
        +int rollNo
        +String name
    }

    University "1" o-- "1..*" Department : contains
    Department "1" o-- "1..*" Professor : employs
    Department "1" o-- "*" Course : offers
    Department "1" --> "0..1" Professor : chair
    Professor "1..*" -- "*" Course : teaches
    Professor "*" -- "*" Course
    TeachingAssignment ..> Professor
    TeachingAssignment ..> Course

    Course "*" --> "*" Course : prerequisiteFor
    Student "*" --> "*" Course
    Enrolment ..> Student
    Enrolment ..> Course
```

> [!NOTE]
> The two `Professor — Course` lines shown in pairs (one plain, one with the `..>` from the association class) together represent the **Association Class** pattern: a many-to-many association *plus* a class attached to it. Mermaid's classDiagram does not have a native association-class glyph, so the **dashed dependency** arrow from `TeachingAssignment` to each participant conveys that `TeachingAssignment` is *attached to* the link.

---

## 4.2 Advanced Link Catalogue — Visual Reference Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef assoc fill:#e0f2fe,stroke:#0369a1,color:#0c4a6e
    classDef agg fill:#fef9c3,stroke:#a16207,color:#713f12
    classDef comp fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#7f1d1d
    classDef gen fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,color:#14532d
    classDef dep fill:#f3e8ff,stroke:#6b21a8,color:#3b0764

    A1[Class A]:::assoc -- LinkAssociation[ASSOCIATION<br/>has-a]:::assoc --> B1[Class B]:::assoc
    A2[Whole]:::agg -- LinkAgg[AGGREGATION<br/>part-of weak]:::agg --> P1[Part]:::agg
    A3[Whole]:::comp -- LinkComp[COMPOSITION<br/>part-of strong]:::comp --> P2[Part]:::comp
    A4[Parent]:::gen -- LinkGen[GENERALIZATION<br/>is-a]:::gen --> S[Subclass]:::gen
    A5[Client]:::dep -. LinkDep[DEPENDENCY<br/>uses-a]:::dep .-> S2[Supplier]:::dep
```

---

## 4.3 Reflexive Association — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef reflexive fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#1e3a8a

    Emp[Employee]:::reflexive
    Emp -- manages --- Emp
    Mgr[Manager Role]:::reflexive -. roleLabel .-> Emp
    Emp -. roleLabel .-> Repr[Reports-To Role]:::reflexive
```

> [!NOTE]
> The two role labels (`manager` and `reports-to`) on a single class are how the reflexive association is *disambiguated*; without role names, the model would be syntactically ambiguous.

---

## 4.4 N-ary (Ternary) Association — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef ternary fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#9d174d,color:#831843

    Doc[Doctor]:::ternary
    Med[Medication]:::ternary
    Pat[Patient]:::ternary
    Diamond{Prescription}:::ternary

    Doc --- Diamond
    Med --- Diamond
    Pat --- Diamond
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The central diamond node represents the *ternary association class*. The three lines emanating from it connect to all three participating classes. **Do not** decompose this into three binary associations; the date/prescription details depend on the *joint* triple.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model Paper]`
**Differentiate between aggregation and composition with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Criterion | Aggregation | Composition |
|-----------|-------------|-------------|
| Diamond | Hollow (◇) | Filled (◆) |
| Lifecycle of part | Independent of whole | Bound to whole's lifetime |
| Sharing | Part may belong to many wholes | Part belongs to exactly one whole |
| Ownership | Logical | Physical (typically created by the whole) |
| Example | Department ◇— Professor (a professor can switch departments) | Window ◆— Handle (handle is created with the window) |

*(1 mark for the table, 1 mark for the diagram, 1 mark for the example.)*

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`
**What is an association class? When is it used?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **association class** is a model element that has its own attributes and operations but is attached to an *association* rather than to a class. It is used when the **link itself** carries properties that do not belong to either endpoint class.

Use it when: a property of the relationship *varies* with the pairing of the two endpoint objects and cannot be assigned meaningfully to either endpoint alone.

Example: In `Student`—`Course`, the attribute `grade` belongs to the **enrolment** (the link), so we create an association class `Enrolment` with attribute `grade`. *(1 mark definition, 1 mark condition, 1 mark example.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark ESE Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question B-A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024, Module 1]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the concept of *qualified association* with a real-world example. Show how multiplicity is *reduced* by introducing a qualifier. Provide a class diagram.

**(b) [7 marks]** A *Patient* may be admitted to a *Hospital* multiple times. Each admission has an admission number, date, and the ward assigned. Model this scenario with a proper class diagram. Identify the link types and multiplicities.

---

**Model Solution:**

### Part (a) — Qualified Association

A **qualifier** is a small key attribute placed on the *target* end of an association that partitions a many-valued link set into a map, thereby reducing effective multiplicity.

**Step 1 — Identify the unqualified association.**
A `Bank` may have `*` `Account` objects. Pre-qualifier: `Bank 1 — * Account`.

**Step 2 — Recognise the partitioning key.**
Each `Account` has a unique `accountNo` *within* a bank. This is the qualifier.

**Step 3 — Apply the qualifier.**
Draw a small box labelled `accountNo` on the `Bank` end. The multiplicity at the `Account` end collapses from `*` to `1`. Post-qualifier: `Bank 1 — 1 Account[accountNo]`.

**Step 4 — Class diagram.**

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Bank {
        +String ifsc
        +String name
    }
    class Account {
        +long accountNo
        +double balance
    }
    Bank "1" --> "1" Account : holds
    Bank : accountNo
```
