> *Engineering utility:* Qualifiers model *indexed* or *dictionary* access. They appear in compiler symbol tables, DNS servers, library catalogues, and inventory systems.

---

### Part (b) — Patient Admission Modelling

**Step 1 — Identify classes.**
`Patient`, `Hospital`, `Ward`, association class `Admission`.

**Step 2 — Identify links from prose.**

- *"Patient admitted to Hospital"* ⇒ Association, `*` Patient ↔ `*` Hospital.
- *"Each admission has admission number, date, ward assigned"* ⇒ Association class `Admission` with attributes `admissionNo`, `date`, and a *role-link* to `Ward`.
- A `Ward` belongs to a `Hospital` exclusively ⇒ **Composition** (filled diamond, `Hospital 1 ◆— 1..* Ward`).

**Step 3 — Multiplicities.**

- `Patient * — * Hospital` (a patient can be admitted to multiple hospitals over a lifetime; a hospital has many patients).
- `Admission 1 — 1 Ward` (each admission assigns one ward).
- `Hospital 1 ◆— 1..* Ward` (a hospital owns 1..* wards exclusively).

**Step 4 — Class diagram.**

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Patient {
        +int patientId
        +String name
        +Date dob
    }
    class Hospital {
        +String hospitalId
        +String name
    }
    class Ward {
        +int wardNo
        +String type
    }
    class Admission {
        +long admissionNo
        +Date date
    }

    Hospital "1" *-- "1..*" Ward : owns
    Patient "*" --> "*" Hospital
    Admission ..> Patient
    Admission ..> Hospital
    Admission "1" --> "1" Ward
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Q-B-A):** Many students draw `Ward` as **aggregation** instead of **composition**. Since a ward is a *physical division* of a hospital building, deleting the hospital must delete the wards. Use the **filled diamond ◆**, not the hollow one. Losing this distinction costs 2 marks.

---

### Question B-B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the **four fundamental link types** in object modelling. State the OMT notation for each.

**(b) [7 marks]** Consider a publishing house that publishes many books. Each book has a unique ISBN. A book may be authored by one or more authors, and an author may write many books. The authorship carries the *royalty percentage* and the *order* of authorship (1st author, 2nd author, etc.). Draw the class diagram and justify your modelling decisions.

---

**Model Solution:**

### Part (a) — Four Fundamental Link Types

1. **Association** — peer-to-peer *has-a* connection. OMT: solid line with optional role labels and multiplicities. *Example:* `Student` — `LibraryCard`.
2. **Aggregation** — *whole–part* where parts have independent existence. OMT: solid line with **hollow diamond** on the whole side. *Example:* `Team` ◇— `Player`.
3. **Composition** — *whole–part* with exclusive ownership and shared lifetime. OMT: solid line with **filled diamond** on the whole side. *Example:* `House` ◆— `Room`.
4. **Generalization** — taxonomic *is-a* relationship. OMT: solid line with **hollow triangle** on the parent side. *Example:* `Vehicle` △ `Car`.

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Student
    class LibraryCard
    Student --> LibraryCard : has

    class Team
    class Player
    Team o-- Player : contains

    class House
    class Room
    House "1" *-- "1..*" Room : owns

    class Vehicle
    class Car
    Vehicle <|-- Car : is-a
```

> A fifth type, **Dependency**, is drawn as a **dashed arrow** in both OMT and UML. It denotes a *temporary* or *parameter-based* usage. (Mentioned for completeness; 1 mark if asked.)

---

### Part (b) — Publishing-House Class Diagram

**Step 1 — Classes.**
`Book`, `Author`, `Publisher` (the publishing house), association class `Authorship`.

**Step 2 — Link decisions.**

- `Publisher 1 — * Book` (composition: books are publications of a single publisher, have ISBNs, and cease to exist if the publisher is dissolved).
- `Author * — * Book` (an author writes many books; a book has many authors).
- The link `(author, book)` carries `royaltyPct` and `orderNo` ⇒ **association class `Authorship`**.

**Step 3 — Qualifier?**
Each book has a unique ISBN *within the publisher* ⇒ apply qualifier `ISBN` on the `Book` end:

$$
\text{Publisher} \xrightarrow{1 \; \text{to} \; 1} \text{Book[ISBN]}
$$

**Step 4 — Class diagram.**

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Publisher {
        +String name
        +String regNo
    }
    class Book {
        +String ISBN
        +String title
        +int edition
    }
    class Author {
        +int authorId
        +String name
    }
    class Authorship {
        +double royaltyPct
        +int orderNo
    }

    Publisher "1" *-- "*" Book : publishes
    Publisher : ISBN
    Author "*" --> "*" Book : writes
    Authorship ..> Author
    Authorship ..> Book
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Q-B-B):** A frequent mistake is putting `royaltyPct` and `orderNo` as attributes of `Author` or of `Book`. The royalty for the *same* author differs across books; the order of authorship is meaningful only for a specific (author, book) pair. Therefore they **must** be in the association class `Authorship`. Marks lost: 2.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (print and pin to wall before exam):**

- **5 link types:** Association, Aggregation, Composition, Generalization, Dependency.
- **Diamond legend:** ◇ hollow = weak aggregation (parts survive). ◆ filled = strong composition (parts die with whole).
- **Triangle legend:** △ hollow = generalization (is-a). Use `<|--` in Mermaid, hollow triangle in OMT/UML.
- **Dashed arrow:** Dependency / uses-a.
- **Multiplicity cheat:**
  - `1` = exactly one
  - `0..1` = zero or one (optional single)
  - `*` = zero-or-more (default when omitted)
  - `1..*` = one-or-more (mandatory many)
  - `n..m` = bounded range
- **Association class** is used when the **link itself** has properties (e.g., `grade`, `royaltyPct`, `semester`).
- **Qualifier** is a key attribute that reduces effective multiplicity from `*` to `1` (e.g., `ISBN`, `accountNo`, `bedNo`).
- **N-ary association** cannot be safely decomposed into binary associations; always draw a single central diamond with n connecting lines.
- **Reflexive association** requires **role labels** on both ends to disambiguate (e.g., `manager` / `reports-to`).
- **Default multiplicity** when unspecified in KTU question = `*`. Always state your assumption explicitly.
- **Aggregation vs Composition test:** *Does deleting the whole automatically delete the part?* If yes → composition.
- **Association vs Dependency test:** *Is the supplier stored as a field?* If no → dependency.
- **Generalization vs Association test:** *Can the child replace the parent in an `is-a` sentence?* If yes → generalization.
- **Reading a KTU problem statement:** Identify verbs first (contains, employs, has, manages, teaches, is-a, uses). Each verb maps to a link type.
- **Drawing tips:** Label both role ends, mention both multiplicities, use dashed line for derived/dependency, use bold/italic only on class names.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Generalization and Inheritance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCNT604 - OBJECT ORIENTED MODELING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module - 1
> - **Topic:** Generalization and Inheritance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

**Generalization** is a taxonomic relationship between a more general element (called the **superclass** or **parent**) and a more specific element (called the **subclass** or **child**), in which the subclass shares the structure (attributes) and behavior (operations) of the superclass and may add refinements of its own. It is a relationship where one element is a *kind-of* another.

**Inheritance** is the mechanism — the actual code/modeling construct — by which the attributes, operations, and relationships of the superclass are *reused* and *specialized* by the subclass. In UML, the inheritance relationship is modeled using a **Generalization** connector.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Distinction to Remember:**
> - **Generalization** → the *relationship* (modeling concept)
> - **Inheritance** → the *mechanism* (implementation concept)
> They are not the same word — they are two sides of the same coin.

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of the biological classification **taxonomy** (Kingdom → Phylum → Class → Order → Family → Genus → Species).

- **"Animal"** is a generalization. It is *general*.
- **"Dog"** is a specialization of "Animal." A dog **IS-A** kind of animal.
- A dog *inherits* all the universal traits of animals (eats, breathes, moves) but adds its own traits (barks, wags tail, breed name).

So: **"Dog inherits from Animal"** and **"Animal is generalized by Dog"** are the same fact stated from two viewpoints.

> [!NOTE]
> **The IS-A Test:** If you can say "X IS-A Y" naturally, then X is a candidate subclass of Y.
> Examples: A `SavingsAccount` **IS-A** `BankAccount` ✓
>           A `Car` **IS-A** `Vehicle` ✓
>           A `Queue` **IS-A** `Stack` ✗ (this is implementation, not generalization)

## UML Notation (Standard)

In a UML class diagram, **Generalization is drawn as a solid line with a hollow (unfilled) triangle arrowhead** pointing from the subclass to the superclass.

```
        ┌─────────────┐
        │   Animal    │  ◄── superclass (hollow triangle points here)
        └──────┬──────┘
               │  △  (hollow triangle)
        ┌──────┴──────┐
        │     Dog     │  ◄── subclass
        └─────────────┘
```

## Key Terminology Checklist

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Superclass** | The general (parent) class |
| **Subclass** | The specialized (child) class |
| **Ancestor** | Any class above in the hierarchy |
| **Descendant** | Any class below in the hierarchy |
| **Root Class** | The topmost class (no superclass) |
| **Leaf Class** | The bottommost class (no subclasses) |
| **Abstract Class** | Cannot be instantiated; meant only to be inherited |
| **Concrete Class** | Can be directly instantiated |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" of Generalization — Engineering Justification

Engineers use generalization to solve three fundamental design problems:

1. **Reuse:** Common attributes and operations are written *once* in the superclass, not duplicated in every subclass.
2. **Substitutability (Liskov Substitution Principle — LSP):** Anywhere the superclass is expected, a subclass instance can be supplied *without breaking* the program. This is the foundation of polymorphism.
3. **Semantic Clarity:** Captures the "IS-A" semantic relationship, separating *kind-of* (generalization) from *has-a* (association/aggregation) and *part-of* (composition).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Point:** Examiners repeatedly test whether you know the difference between **Generalization (IS-A)**, **Association (HAS-A)**, and **Aggregation/Composition (PART-OF)**. Confusing them is the #1 reason students lose marks.

## 2.2 Properties Inherited by a Subclass

A subclass inherits:

- **Attributes** (data members) of the superclass
- **Operations** (methods) of the superclass
- **Associations** declared on the superclass
- **Constraints** (some may be overridden)

A subclass does **NOT** inherit:

- **Private** members (in most OO languages) — though in the modeling sense, they still *exist* in the inherited structure, they are just inaccessible.
- **Constructors** (must be redefined/overloaded in the subclass)
- **Static/class-level** elements (language-dependent)

## 2.3 Generalization Constraints (UML Standard Set)

KTU examiners love this table. Memorize it.

| Constraint | Meaning | UML Notation |
|---|---|---|
| **Disjoint** | A superclass instance can be an instance of **at most one** subclass in the hierarchy | `{disjoint}` |
| **Overlapping** | A superclass instance can be an instance of **more than one** subclass simultaneously | `{overlapping}` (default in UML 1, but explicit in UML 2) |
| **Complete** | All subclasses have been listed; no others exist | `{complete}` |
| **Incomplete** | More subclasses may exist beyond those shown | `{incomplete}` (default) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Default in UML 2.x:** If you do not write a constraint, the default is `{incomplete, overlapping}`. The KTU examiner may ask: *"What is the default generalization constraint?"*

## 2.4 Types of Inheritance (KTU 2024 Frequently Asked)

| # | Type | UML Pattern | Real Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Single** | A → B | `SavingsAccount` → `BankAccount` |
| 2 | **Multilevel** | A → B → C | `Vehicle` → `Car` → `ElectricCar` |
| 3 | **Hierarchical** | B, C, D all → A | `Car`, `Truck`, `Bike` all → `Vehicle` |
| 4 | **Multiple** | C → A and C → B | A `HybridCar` inherits from both `ElectricCar` and `PetrolCar` |
| 5 | **Hybrid (Diamond)** | Combination of multiple + hierarchical | Multiple inheritance forming a diamond shape |

> [!WARNING]
> **C++ vs Java Reality (Common Pitfall):** C++ supports multiple inheritance directly. Java does **not** — it uses *interfaces* to simulate it. UML models the *design*, not the language. So in UML, you may freely draw multiple inheritance even if you later implement in Java.

## 2.5 Abstract Class vs Concrete Class

- **Abstract Class** (UML: class name in *italics*): Incomplete; cannot be instantiated. Used purely to define a common interface/protocol for its subclasses. Example: `Shape` (you never create "a generic shape" — you create a `Circle` or `Square`).
- **Concrete Class**: Fully implemented; can be directly instantiated. Example: `Circle`.

**Abstract Operation** (UML: operation name in *italics*): A declaration without an implementation. The subclass **must** provide the implementation (or remain abstract itself).

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of Thumb:** If 2 or more subclasses share the *signature* of an operation but implement it differently, declare the operation as **abstract** in the superclass. This is the cleanest way to express polymorphism in UML.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Generalization & Inheritance

| Property / Rule | Statement | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **IS-A Test** | `X` is a subclass of `Y` iff every `X` instance is logically a `Y` | Validating a generalization |
| **Liskov Substitution Principle (LSP)** | An instance of a subclass can replace any instance of its superclass *without altering correctness* | Polymorphism safety check |
| **Reusability Gain** | Attributes/operations defined in superclass: written **once**, reused by **N** subclasses → O(1) definition | Measuring design efficiency |
| **Generalization Constraint Pairs** | `{disjoint, complete}`, `{disjoint, incomplete}`, `{overlapping, complete}`, `{overlapping, incomplete}` | UML class diagram design |
| **Multiple Inheritance Formula** | Subclass inherits union of attributes and operations from all parents | Resolving diamond problem |
| **Method Overriding** | Subclass redefines inherited operation with same signature | Runtime polymorphism |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Software Frameworks:** Java's `AbstractList`, `AbstractMap` in the Collections Framework — abstract superclasses that the framework extends.
- **GUI Toolkits:** `JFrame` → `MyFrame` in Swing; `QWidget` → custom widgets in Qt.
- **Database Systems:** ORM (Object-Relational Mapping) tools like Hibernate use generalization to map inheritance hierarchies to relational tables (SINGLE_TABLE, JOINED, TABLE_PER_CLASS strategies).
- **Embedded / Real-Time Systems:** Hardware abstraction layers (HAL) are built as abstract superclasses with concrete device drivers as subclasses — exactly the same pattern.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section uses an *analytical matrix* approach — a structured walkthrough of the major reasoning steps a KTU student must demonstrate in an exam answer.

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Designing a Generalization Hierarchy

**Problem (sample):** *Model the following entities using UML generalization: Animal, Dog, Cat, Bird, Fish, Sparrow, Shark, Vehicle, Car, Truck, Bus, Aeroplane.*

### Step 1 — Identify the IS-A candidates

We ask, for every pair (X, Y): is "X IS-A Y" true?

$$
\text{IS-A}(X, Y) = \begin{cases} \text{True} & \text{if } X \text{ is a kind of } Y \\ \text{False} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

Evaluating:
- IS-A(Dog, Animal) = True ✓
- IS-A(Cat, Animal) = True ✓
- IS-A(Bird, Animal) = True ✓
- IS-A(Fish, Animal) = True ✓
- IS-A(Sparrow, Bird) = True ✓
- IS-A(Shark, Fish) = True ✓
- IS-A(Car, Vehicle) = True ✓
- IS-A(Truck, Vehicle) = True ✓
- IS-A(Bus, Vehicle) = True ✓
- IS-A(Aeroplane, Vehicle) = True ✓
- IS-A(Sparrow, Fish) = False ✗ (cross-branch invalid)

> **Logic:** Only true IS-A statements form valid generalization edges.

### Step 2 — Construct the directed acyclic graph (DAG)

A generalization hierarchy must be a **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)** — edges point from subclass to superclass, and there can be no cycle.

$$
G = (V, E), \quad V = \{\text{classes}\}, \quad E = \{(s, p) \mid s \text{ IS-A } p\}
$$

For our domain:
$$
V = \{\text{Animal, Dog, Cat, Bird, Fish, Sparrow, Shark, Vehicle, Car, Truck, Bus, Aeroplane}\}
$$

$$
E = \{(\text{Dog, Animal}), (\text{Cat, Animal}), \ldots, (\text{Aeroplane, Vehicle})\}
$$

> **Cycle check:** A cycle would exist if A IS-A B and B IS-A A. In valid OO design this is **never** allowed.

### Step 3 — Choose the constraint set

For `Animal` subtree: a real animal is *either* a dog, cat, bird, or fish — it cannot be two of these at once. So:

$$
\text{Animal} \xrightarrow{\{disjoint,\ complete\}} \{Dog, Cat, Bird, Fish\}
$$

> **Reading:** Each `Animal` instance belongs to **at most one** of Dog/Cat/Bird/Fish, and we have listed **all** known animal types → disjoint + complete.

For `Vehicle` subtree: a hybrid vehicle could be both a car-like and aeroplane-like (flying car). So:

$$
\text{Vehicle} \xrightarrow{\{overlapping,\ incomplete\}} \{Car, Truck, Bus, Aeroplane\}
$$

### Step 4 — Mark abstractness

- `Animal` — **Abstract** (you never create "a generic animal" object; you create a Dog or Cat)
- `Vehicle` — **Abstract**
- `Bird` — **Abstract** (a generic bird is conceptual; a `Sparrow` is concrete)
- `Sparrow`, `Shark`, `Dog`, `Cat`, `Car`, `Truck`, `Bus`, `Aeroplane` — all **Concrete**

### Step 5 — Identify abstract operations

For `Shape` example (illustrative derivation):

$$
\text{area}() : \text{Real} \quad \text{(abstract — same signature, different implementations)}
$$

For each subclass `C`, an overriding operation is provided:

$$
\text{area}_{C}() = \begin{cases} \pi r^{2} & C = \text{Circle} \\ s^{2} & C = \text{Square} \\ \tfrac{1}{2} b h & C = \text{Triangle} \end{cases}
$$

> **Logic:** The signature is inherited; the *implementation* is specialized — this is the mathematical essence of polymorphism.

### Step 6 — Apply LSP (Liskov Substitution Principle)

For each generalization edge, verify:

$$
\forall\, \text{operation } m, \; \forall\, \text{precondition } p, \; \forall\, \text{postcondition } q, \; \forall\, x \in \text{Subclass} : \big( p_{Super}(x) \Rightarrow q_{Super}(m(x)) \big) \land \big( p_{Sub}(x) \Rightarrow q_{Sub}(m(x)) \big)
$$

In plain words: **what was true for the superclass must remain true for the subclass**. The subclass may strengthen preconditions and weaken postconditions — never the reverse.

## 3.2 Worked Example: Power Type Pattern

The **PowerType** pattern is a higher-order generalization used when an attribute *itself* becomes a classifier.

> [!NOTE]
> **Scenario:** A `Company` has employees. Employees can be `Engineer`, `Manager`, or `Salesperson`. But an employee can change roles over time. So role cannot be a static subclass — it must be a dynamic class.

### Step-by-step derivation:

**Step 1:** Identify the dynamic attribute.

$$
\text{Employee} \longrightarrow \text{role} : \text{Role} \quad (\text{where Role is a class, not a simple type})
$$

**Step 2:** Define the power type as a metaclass.

$$
\text{Role} = \{\text{Engineer}, \text{Manager}, \text{Salesperson}\}
$$

**Step 3:** Draw the dependency from `Employee` to `Role` (not inheritance).

$$
\text{Employee} \dashrightarrow \text{Role}
$$

The dashed arrow indicates a *dependency*, not generalization. UML models this with a class `Role` acting as the **classifier** of the attribute `role`.

> **KTU 2024 Insight:** Power types appear in questions on *advanced generalization*. Know the pattern: dynamic role → power type class → dashed dependency arrow.

## 3.3 Mathematical Property: Height of a Generalization Tree

Given a hierarchy of $N$ classes with branching factor $b$ and height $h$:

$$
N = \sum_{k=0}^{h-1} b^{k} = \frac{b^{h} - 1}{b - 1}, \quad b \ge 2
$$

Solving for $h$:

$$
h = \left\lceil \log_{b}\!\big(N(b-1) + 1\big) \right\rceil
$$

> **Real-world utility:** Used to estimate the maximum lookup cost in deep hierarchies (relevant in OOP frameworks and class-loader chains in Java).

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> *Per the protocol, complex physical drawings are rendered as functional architecture / UML class diagrams using Mermaid. All node IDs are alphanumeric and labels contain no markdown formatting.*

## 4.1 UML Class Diagram: Animal Hierarchy with Constraints

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Animal {
        <<abstract>>
        +name : String
        +age : int
        +eat() : void
        +sleep() : void
        +makeSound()* : void
    }

    class Dog {
        +breed : String
        +bark() : void
        +makeSound() : void
    }

    class Cat {
        +furColor : String
        +purr() : void
        +makeSound() : void
    }

    class Bird {
        <<abstract>>
        +wingSpan : double
        +fly()* : void
        +makeSound()* : void
    }

    class Fish {
        <<abstract>>
        +waterType : String
        +swim()* : void
        +makeSound()* : void
    }

    class Sparrow {
        +nestLocation : String
        +fly() : void
        +makeSound() : void
    }

    class Shark {
        +finCount : int
        +swim() : void
        +makeSound() : void
    }

    Animal <|-- Dog
    Animal <|-- Cat
    Animal <|-- Bird
    Animal <|-- Fish
    Bird <|-- Sparrow
    Fish <|-- Shark
```

## 4.2 UML Class Diagram: Vehicle Hierarchy (Overlapping + Incomplete)

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Vehicle {
        <<abstract>>
        +maxSpeed : double
        +fuelType : String
        +startEngine()* : void
        +stopEngine()* : void
    }

    class LandVehicle {
        <<abstract>>
        +wheelCount : int
        +drive()* : void
    }

    class WaterVehicle {
        <<abstract>>
        +displacement : double
        +sail()* : void
    }

    class AirVehicle {
        <<abstract>>
        +wingType : String
        +takeoff()* : void
    }

    class Car {
        +modelName : String
        +drive() : void
    }

    class Truck {
        +loadCapacityKg : double
        +drive() : void
    }

    class Hovercraft {
        +drive() : void
        +sail() : void
        +takeoff() : void
    }

    Vehicle <|-- LandVehicle
    Vehicle <|-- WaterVehicle
    Vehicle <|-- AirVehicle
    LandVehicle <|-- Car
    LandVehicle <|-- Truck
    LandVehicle <|-- Hovercraft
    WaterVehicle <|-- Hovercraft
    AirVehicle <|-- Hovercraft
```

> **KTU 2024 Reading:** The `Hovercraft` class is a multiple-inheritance subclass — it inherits from all three abstract branches. This is a **legal UML construct** (even if Java code would require interfaces to implement it).

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: PowerType Pattern

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Domain Class: Employee] -->|attribute: role| B[PowerType Class: Role]
    B --> C[Subclass: Engineer]
    B --> D[Subclass: Manager]
    B --> E[Subclass: Salesperson]
    A -.->|instance of| F[Concrete Employee Object]
    F -.->|linked to| C
    F -.->|can change to| D
    F -.->|can change to| E
```

> **Reading guide:**
> - The **solid arrow** denotes the dependency from `Employee` to the classifier `Role`.
> - The **dotted arrows** denote dynamic linking at runtime — an `Employee` object can switch its `Role` value at execution time.
> - This is *not* static generalization; it is the PowerType pattern.

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Generalization Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Begin: Two candidate classes X and Y]) --> Q1{Is X IS-A Y?}
    Q1 -- No --> A1[Use Association, NOT Generalization]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Does X add new attributes or operations?}
    Q2 -- No --> A2[Consider using same class with discriminator]
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{Does X override inherited behavior?}
    Q3 -- No --> Q4{Is the superclass only for reuse, no real concept?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> A3[Use association or helper class]
    Q4 -- No --> A5[Use Generalization]
    Q3 -- Yes --> A5[Use Generalization]
    A5 --> Done([End: X is subclass of Y])
    A1 --> Done
    A2 --> Done
    A3 --> Done
```

> **Reading guide:** This flowchart guides a designer through the decision of when to actually use generalization in UML. KTU examiners often give a scenario and ask *"Should A be a subclass of B or not?"* — the above flow gives the correct reasoning path.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Differentiate between Generalization and Specialization. Give one UML example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Generalization | Specialization |
|---|---|---|
| Direction | Bottom-up (subclass → superclass) | Top-down (superclass → subclass) |
| Viewpoint | Looks for commonality among classes | Looks for unique features of a class |
| Process | Extracts shared attributes/operations into a parent | Adds new attributes/operations to a child |
| Example | `Dog`, `Cat`, `Bird` → generalized as `Animal` | `Animal` specialized into `Dog`, `Cat`, `Bird` |
| UML arrow | Hollow triangle pointing to parent | Hollow triangle pointing to parent (same notation, different reasoning direction) |

> **3-Mark Tip:** A table earns 2 marks; the example earns 1 mark. Always include a real example.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is an abstract class? How is it represented in UML?**

**Model Answer:**

An **abstract class** is a class that cannot be directly instantiated; it serves only as a template/blueprint for its subclasses. It may declare **abstract operations** (signatures without implementation) that its concrete subclasses must override.

**UML Representation:**
- The class name is written in *italics*.
- The stereotype `<<abstract>>` may also be used.
- Abstract operations are written in *italics* (e.g., `+area() : double`).

**Example:** `Shape` is abstract; `Circle` and `Square` are its concrete subclasses. `Shape` declares `+area() : double` as abstract, which `Circle` implements as $\pi r^{2}$ and `Square` as $s^{2}$.

> **3-Mark Tip:** Mention *italics* notation (1 mark), define the concept (1 mark), give the example (1 mark).

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Exam Pattern:** Each Module carries a Part B question of 14 marks with two sub-parts (typically 7 + 7), and the student answers ONE of two internal choices.

---

### Q.A. [KTU University Exam — July 2024, Modified]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the different **generalization constraints** in UML with suitable examples. How are they represented in a class diagram?

**(b) [7 Marks]** What is **multiple inheritance**? Discuss the **diamond problem** with a suitable diagram. How is it resolved in C++ and Java?

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A (a)

**Definition (1 mark):** A generalization constraint restricts how instances of a superclass are classified among its subclasses. UML defines two pairs of constraints: **disjoint vs overlapping** and **complete vs incomplete**.

**Four possible combinations (4 marks):**

| Combination | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `{disjoint, complete}` | An instance belongs to *exactly one* subclass, and all subclasses are listed | `Polygon` → `{Triangle, Quadrilateral, Pentagon}` — every polygon is *one* of these, no overlap, no missing type |
| `{disjoint, incomplete}` | An instance belongs to *at most one* subclass, but more subclasses may exist | `Animal` → `{Dog, Cat}` — a dog is not a cat, but other animals may exist |
| `{overlapping, complete}` | An instance may belong to *multiple* subclasses, all of which are listed | `Person` → `{Student, Employee}` — a person can be both (working student) |
| `{overlapping, incomplete}` | An instance may belong to *multiple* subclasses, more may exist | `Vehicle` → `{LandVehicle, AirVehicle, WaterVehicle}` — a hovercraft belongs to all three; others may exist |

**UML Representation (2 marks):** The constraint is written in curly braces `{ }` next to the generalization arrow.

**Example diagram (sample text representation):**

```
    Polygon {disjoint, complete}
    ┌──────────┐
    │ Polygon  │
    └────┬─────┘
   ┌────┼─────────┐
   │    │         │
   ▼    ▼         ▼
 Triangle Quadrilateral Pentagon
```

> **Valuation Tip:** A complete answer must include the constraint table, definitions, and a UML-style example. Skipping the example costs 2 marks.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A (b)

**Definition of Multiple Inheritance (2 marks):**
Multiple inheritance is a generalization relationship in which a subclass inherits from **more than one** superclass. The subclass thus combines the structure and behavior of all its parents.

$$
\text{Subclass} \leftarrow \text{Superclass}_1, \; \text{Superclass}_2, \ldots, \text{Superclass}_n
$$

**The Diamond Problem (3 marks):**
The diamond problem occurs when two superclasses inherit from a common ancestor, forming a diamond shape:

```
         A
        / \
       B   C
        \ /
         D
```

If `A` defines an operation `m()`, and both `B` and `C` override it differently, then `D` (which inherits from both `B` and `C`) faces ambiguity: which `m()` should it inherit?

**Resolution in C++ (1 mark):** C++ uses **virtual inheritance** to ensure only one copy of the common ancestor is inherited.

**Resolution in Java (1 mark):** Java disallows multiple inheritance of *classes* entirely. Instead, a class can implement multiple *interfaces*, each declaring the operation; the implementing class provides the single concrete implementation.

> **Valuation Tip:** Most students describe the diamond but forget to mention how the resolution differs across languages. This is where the extra mark is hidden.

---

### Q.B. [Alternative for the same module]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain **abstract classes** and **abstract operations** in UML. Design a class hierarchy for the following: `Shape` (abstract), `Circle`, `Rectangle`, `Triangle`. Show all attributes, abstract operations, and the `area()` method overridden in each subclass.

**(b) [7 Marks]** What is the **PowerType pattern**? When is it used? Illustrate with a real-world example involving a `Company`, `Employee`, and the dynamic `Role` of the employee.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B (a)

**Abstract Class (1 mark):** A class that cannot be instantiated. In UML, denoted by writing the class name in *italics* or using the `<<abstract>>` stereotype.

**Abstract Operation (1 mark):** A declared operation without an implementation, written in *italics* in UML. Subclasses must override it.

**Class Hierarchy Design (5 marks):**

| Class | Type | Attributes | Operations |
|---|---|---|---|
| `Shape` | Abstract | `+color : String` | `+area()* : double` *(abstract)*, `+display() : void` |
| `Circle` | Concrete | `+radius : double` | `+area() : double` (overrides, returns $\pi r^2$), `+display() : void` |
| `Rectangle` | Concrete | `+length, +width : double` | `+area() : double` (overrides, returns $l \times w$), `+display() : void` |
| `Triangle` | Concrete | `+base, +height : double` | `+area() : double` (overrides, returns $\tfrac{1}{2}bh$), `+display() : void` |

**Mathematical Form of Overridden Operations:**

$$
\text{area}()_{C} = \begin{cases} \pi r^{2} & \text{if } C = \text{Circle} \\ l \times w & \text{if } C = \text{Rectangle} \\ \tfrac{1}{2} b h & \text{if } C = \text{Triangle} \end{cases}
$$

> **Valuation Tip:** A complete answer must include a class diagram (text-form is acceptable) and the abstract operation list. Drawing the diagram in proper UML earns full marks; a verbal description alone loses 1–2 marks.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B (b)

**Definition (2 marks):** The **PowerType pattern** is a UML modeling construct used when a *dynamic attribute* of a class should itself be treated as a class (a "class of classes"). The dynamic attribute is called the **power type** of the class.

**When it is used (2 marks):**
- When an object can *change* its classification dynamically at runtime.
- When a discriminator (such as a status, type, or role) has its own attributes, operations, and lifecycle.

**Real-World Example (3 marks):** Consider an `Employee` working in a `Company`. The `role` of the employee can change: today `Engineer`, next year `Manager`, then `Salesperson`. The `role` cannot be modeled as a static subclass (an employee is not born an engineer; they can switch).

**Modeling:**

- `Company` is a top-level class.
- `Employee` has an attribute `role : Role`, where `Role` is a *separate class* (not a string).
- `Role` has subclasses `Engineer`, `Manager`, `Salesperson` (the power type subclasses).
- A dashed dependency arrow goes from `Employee` to `Role`, indicating that `Role` *classifies* the attribute.

**Why a simple generalization fails here:** If we made `Engineer`, `Manager`, `Salesperson` as static subclasses of `Employee`, the employee object would be locked to that role for life — they could never be promoted. The PowerType pattern captures this dynamic behavior.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Generalization Pitfalls**
> 1. **Don't draw multiple inheritance in Java code** — UML is fine, but if the question explicitly says *"implement in Java"*, use `interface`s, not classes, for the multiple parents.
> 2. **Don't forget curly braces** for constraints: `{disjoint, complete}` is a 1-mark item in itself.
> 3. **Don't italicize concrete class names** — only abstract ones. Examiners check this.
> 4. **Don't use "is-a-kind-of" loosely** — the IS-A test must be *semantic*, not just convenient for code reuse.
> 5. **Don't confuse Association with Generalization.** *"A student HAS-A course"* is association. *"A Graduate IS-A Student"* is generalization. Mixing them up is the most common reason for losing 7-mark answers.
> 6. **Don't forget the hollow-triangle direction** — the arrow must point from the subclass **to** the superclass. Reversed arrows lose marks.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ☐ **Generalization** = the *relationship*; **Inheritance** = the *mechanism*. They are related but not identical.
- ☐ UML generalization arrow = **solid line with hollow triangle** pointing to the superclass.
- ☐ **IS-A test** is the gatekeeper for valid generalization.
- ☐ Four constraint combinations: **{disjoint/overlapping} × {complete/incomplete}**.
- ☐ **Default** in UML 2.x = `{incomplete, overlapping}`.
- ☐ **Abstract class** name and abstract operations are written in *italics* (or with `<<abstract>>` stereotype).
- ☐ Abstract class **cannot be instantiated**; concrete class **can**.
- ☐ **Liskov Substitution Principle (LSP):** Subclass must be substitutable for superclass without breaking correctness.
- ☐ **5 Types of inheritance:** single, multilevel, hierarchical, multiple, hybrid (diamond).
- ☐ **Diamond problem** is resolved in C++ by *virtual inheritance* and in Java by *interfaces only* (no multiple class inheritance).
- ☐ **PowerType pattern** is used for *dynamic* classification — modeled as a dashed dependency, not a generalization.
- ☐ **Polymorphism** is realized when a subclass overrides an inherited operation with the same signature.
- ☐ **Reusability** is the main engineering benefit: write once in superclass, reuse in N subclasses.
- ☐ In a class hierarchy, **constructors are NOT inherited** (must be redefined in the subclass).
- ☐ **Generalization ≠ Association** — IS-A vs HAS-A is the most-tested distinction.

---

> **End of Module 1 — Generalization and Inheritance Notes**
> *Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme — Outcome-Based Education, PBCNT604*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: A Sample Object Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCNT604 - OBJECT ORIENTED MODELING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module - 1
> - **Topic:** A Sample Object Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **Object Model** is a precise, graphical and textual representation of the **objects**, **classes**, their **attributes**, **operations**, and the **associations** that exist between them within a problem domain. In the context of **Object-Oriented Modeling and Design (OOMD)**, as defined by **James Rumbaugh's Object Modeling Technique (OMT)**, the object model is the **first and foundational model** in the three-model approach (Object Model → Dynamic Model → Functional Model).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 1, KTU 2024 Scheme):** The "Sample Object Model" demonstrates how the four major elements of any object model — *Objects, Classes, Associations, and Attributes with Operations* — are visually depicted and interconnected using the standard **Object Model Notation (OMN)** / UML-inspired class diagram notation taught in PBCNT604.

A **Sample Object Model** typically uses a real-world problem (e.g., a **Library System**, an **Order/Invoice Processing System**, or an **Automated Teller Machine**) to illustrate these elements in a unified, easy-to-read diagram.

### The Four Pillars of an Object Model

| Pillar | Definition | Example (Library Domain) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Class** | A blueprint/template describing a group of similar objects. | `Book`, `Member`, `Librarian` |
| **Object** | A concrete instance of a class with unique identity. | `book101` (an instance of `Book`) |
| **Attribute** | A named property describing the state of a class/object. | `title`, `isbn`, `price` |
| **Operation** | A function/method that defines the behaviour of the class. | `issueBook()`, `calculateFine()` |
| **Association** | A semantic relationship between two or more classes. | `Member` *borrows* `Book` |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Blueprints of a House"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine you are an architect designing an entire housing colony. Before laying a single brick, you create a **blueprint** of every type of house, every door, every window, and the roads connecting them.

> - A **Class** is the **blueprint** of a house (says *what* a house looks like).
> - An **Object** is an **actual, physical house** built from that blueprint (each house has a unique address — its **identity**).
> - An **Attribute** is a feature on the blueprint: *“3 bedrooms, 2 bathrooms, 1800 sq.ft.”*
> - An **Operation** is something the house *can do*: *“open garage, switch on lights.”*
> - An **Association** is the **road network** connecting houses — the rule *"one street connects many houses to one community center."*

Just as a civil engineer cannot build a colony without blueprints, an object-oriented software engineer **cannot design a system** without first creating an **Object Model**. It is the **blueprint of the software** before the actual code is written.

---

## 1.3 Why is the Object Model the *First* Model?

In the **Rumbaugh OMT triad**, the Object Model is constructed *first* because:

1. **Objects are the most stable part** of any system — they change less frequently than operations or control flow.
2. **The Dynamic Model** (states and transitions) is built *on top* of objects to describe their lifecycle.
3. **The Functional Model** (data flow) is built *around* the operations defined on objects.

> [!NOTE]
> **Module 1 Mnemonic:** *"Object first, Dynamic next, Functional last."* — This is the order of modelling as per OMT and forms a frequently asked 3-mark question in KTU exams.

---

## 1.4 The Sample Object Model — Domain Selection

In KTU 2024 scheme lectures, the *Sample Object Model* is conventionally introduced using a **Library Management System** because it is:
- Universally understood by all students.
- Rich enough to demonstrate all four elements (objects, classes, associations, multiplicity).
- Scalable — can be extended to introduce inheritance, polymorphism, and aggregation in later modules.

We will adopt the **Library Management System** as our running example throughout these notes.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Building the Sample Object Model — Step-by-Step Logic

The construction of an object model follows a **disciplined five-step process**. Each step is non-negotiable in KTU board answers.

### Step 1 — Identify the **Classes**
> A class is *any concept* in the problem domain that has **identity**, **state**, and **behaviour**.

For the Library System, the candidate classes are:

- `Book`
- `Member`
- `Librarian`
- `Author`
- `Publisher`
- `Transaction` (the act of borrowing/returning)

> [!TIP]
> **Heuristic:** Ask — *"Does it have a name? Can it exist independently? Does it have properties?"* If yes to all three, it is a class.

---

### Step 2 — Identify the **Attributes** of each class
Attributes are **named data values** that describe the state of an object.

| Class | Attributes |
| :--- | :--- |
| `Book` | `title`, `isbn`, `price`, `edition`, `copiesAvailable` |
| `Member` | `memberId`, `name`, `phone`, `address` |
| `Librarian` | `empId`, `name`, `shift` |
| `Transaction` | `transactionId`, `issueDate`, `dueDate` |

---

### Step 3 — Identify the **Operations** of each class
Operations define what an object of a class *can do*.

| Class | Operations |
| :--- | :--- |
| `Book` | `displayInfo()`, `checkAvailability()` |
| `Member` | `searchBook()`, `requestIssue()` |
| `Librarian` | `addBook()`, `removeBook()`, `registerMember()` |
| `Transaction` | `calculateFine()`, `markReturned()` |

---

### Step 4 — Identify the **Associations** between classes
An **association** is a semantic relationship between two classes.

For example:
- `Member` *borrows* `Book` → the act of borrowing is an association.
- `Librarian` *issues* `Book` → issuance is an association.
- `Author` *writes* `Book` → authorship is an association.
- `Publisher` *publishes* `Book` → publication is an association.

---

### Step 5 — Add **Multiplicity** (Cardinality) to each association
Multiplicity defines *how many* objects participate on each side of an association.

| Association | Multiplicity Statement |
| :--- | :--- |
| `Member` *borrows* `Book` | One Member borrows **many** Books; one Book is borrowed by **one** Member at a time. |
| `Librarian` *issues* `Book` | One Librarian issues **many** Books; each Book issue is handled by **one** Librarian. |
| `Author` *writes* `Book` | One Author writes **many** Books; one Book is written by **one or many** Authors. |
| `Publisher` *publishes* `Book` | One Publisher publishes **many** Books; each Book has **one** Publisher. |

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Sample Object Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This table is the single most revision-critical artefact for this topic. Memorise every row.**

| Symbol / Notation | Meaning | Example in Sample Model |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Rectangle (3-part)** | Class name, attributes, operations | `Book / title, isbn / displayInfo` |
| **Solid line** | Association | `Member ———————— Book` |
| **Label on line** | Association name (verb) | `borrows`, `issues`, `writes` |
| **Number near endpoint** | Multiplicity (Cardinality) | `1` near Member, `*` near Book |
| **Filled triangle (▶)** | Generalization / Inheritance (Module 2) | Reserved for later modules |
| **Diamond** | Aggregation / Composition (Module 2) | Reserved for later modules |
| **Dashed arrow ( - - - > )** | Dependency / Role name | Optional refinement |
| **Underlined attribute** | Object identifier / Primary Key | `memberId`, `isbn` |

### Key Multiplicity Symbols (Exam-Critical)

| Notation | Meaning | Mnemonic |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `1` | Exactly one | *"The only one"* |
| `*` or `0..*` | Zero or many | *"Anything goes"* |
| `1..*` | One or many | *"At least one"* |
| `0..1` | Zero or one (optional) | *"Maybe, maybe not"* |

---

## 2.3 Engineering Real-World Utility of the Sample Object Model

The object model is **not** an academic exercise — it has direct industrial application:

1. **Database Schema Generation** — Each class becomes a *table*, attributes become *columns*, primary keys become *identifiers*, and associations become *foreign keys* (via multiplicity).
2. **Code Skeleton Generation** — Modern CASE tools (e.g., *Rational Rose*, *StarUML*, *Enterprise Architect*) auto-generate C++/Java class headers directly from the object model.
3. **Communication Document** — Acts as a **single source of truth** between business analysts, designers, and developers.
4. **Test Case Derivation** — Each association + multiplicity pair yields *integration test scenarios*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Use:** The very `Object`/`Class`/`Association` view you draw today maps directly to **ORM frameworks** like *Hibernate (Java)*, *Entity Framework (.NET)*, and *Django ORM (Python)* used in production software worldwide.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Derivation & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Formal Construction of the Sample Object Model

> **Problem Statement (Module 1 Sample):**
> *"Design an object model for a Library Management System. The library has books, each with an ISBN, title, and price. Members borrow books. A librarian issues books. A book is written by one or more authors and published by exactly one publisher. Members have a memberId, name, and phone number. Each transaction has a transactionId, issueDate, dueDate."*

We will now perform a **fully exhaustive, step-by-step derivation** of the object model. This is the **14-mark question pattern** in KTU ESE.

---

### Analytical Step 1 — Class Identification

We scan the problem statement for **nouns** that have *identity, state, and behaviour*.

$$
\text{Nouns Detected} = \{\text{Book, Member, Librarian, Author, Publisher, Transaction, Library}\}
$$

Applying the **class filter heuristic** (must have *identity, state, behaviour*):

$$
\text{Final Class Set} = \{\text{Book, Member, Librarian, Author, Publisher, Transaction}\}
$$

> *"Library"* is rejected as a class because it represents the *system boundary* (the context), not an internal concept.

---

### Analytical Step 2 — Attribute Extraction

Each class is opened up and its **adjectives / descriptive phrases** are extracted.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Book} &: \{\text{isbn, title, price, edition, copiesAvailable}\} \\
\text{Member} &: \{\text{memberId, name, phone, address}\} \\
\text{Librarian} &: \{\text{empId, name, shift}\} \\
\text{Author} &: \{\text{authorId, name, biography}\} \\
\text{Publisher} &: \{\text{publisherId, name, address}\} \\
\text{Transaction} &: \{\text{transactionId, issueDate, dueDate, returnDate, fineAmount}\}
\end{aligned}
$$

The **primary identifier** (object ID) is conventionally **underlined** in the model:

$$
\underline{\text{isbn}}, \quad \underline{\text{memberId}}, \quad \underline{\text{empId}}, \quad \underline{\text{authorId}}, \quad \underline{\text{publisherId}}, \quad \underline{\text{transactionId}}
$$

---

### Analytical Step 3 — Operation Extraction

Operations are the **verbs** associated with each class. They are the *behaviours* the object exhibits.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Book} &: \{\text{displayInfo}(), \text{checkAvailability}()\} \\
\text{Member} &: \{\text{searchBook}(), \text{requestIssue}()\} \\
\text{Librarian} &: \{\text{addBook}(), \text{removeBook}(), \text{registerMember}(), \text{processReturn}()\} \\
\text{Author} &: \{\text{listBooks}()\} \\
\text{Publisher} &: \{\text{listBooks}()\} \\
\text{Transaction} &: \{\text{calculateFine}(), \text{markReturned}()\}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Analytical Step 4 — Association Identification

Associations are **meaningful semantic connections** between two classes. They are typically expressed as **verb phrases**.

$$
\text{Associations} = \left\{
\begin{array}{l}
\text{Member} \xrightarrow{\text{borrows}} \text{Book} \\
\text{Librarian} \xrightarrow{\text{issues}} \text{Book} \\
\text{Author} \xrightarrow{\text{writes}} \text{Book} \\
\text{Publisher} \xrightarrow{\text{publishes}} \text{Book} \\
\text{Transaction} \xrightarrow{\text{recordedFor}} \text{Member} \\
\text{Transaction} \xrightarrow{\text{concerns}} \text{Book}
\end{array}
\right\}
$$

---

### Analytical Step 5 — Multiplicity Derivation

This is the most error-prone step in KTU answers. Apply the **business rules** stated in the problem:

**Association 1: Member — borrows — Book**
- *"A member can borrow many books, but a book can be borrowed by at most one member at a time."*

$$
\text{Multiplicity: } \text{Member} \xleftarrow{\;\; 1 \;\;} \text{borrows} \xrightarrow{\;\; 0..* \;\;} \text{Book}
$$

**Association 2: Librarian — issues — Book**
- *"A librarian issues many books; each issuance is by exactly one librarian."*

$$
\text{Multiplicity: } \text{Librarian} \xleftarrow{\;\; 1 \;\;} \text{issues} \xrightarrow{\;\; 0..* \;\;} \text{Book}
$$

**Association 3: Author — writes — Book**
- *"A book can be written by one or more authors; an author writes many books."*

$$
\text{Multiplicity: } \text{Author} \xleftarrow{\;\; 0..* \;\;} \text{writes} \xrightarrow{\;\; 1..* \;\;} \text{Book}
$$

**Association 4: Publisher — publishes — Book**
- *"A publisher publishes many books; each book has exactly one publisher."*

$$
\text{Multiplicity: } \text{Publisher} \xleftarrow{\;\; 1 \;\;} \text{publishes} \xrightarrow{\;\; 0..* \;\;} \text{Book}
$$

---

### Analytical Step 6 — Final Consolidated Model

The complete object model is the **union** of all five steps above. The next section visualises this consolidation as a UML-style class diagram.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** Always present the object model in the order: *(i) Class diagram → (ii) Data dictionary → (iii) Multiplicity table*. This matches the KTU valuation key expectations.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 UML Class Diagram of the Sample Object Model

> The following Mermaid diagram implements the **Class Diagram** view of the object model. Every class is shown with its **attributes** and **operations**, and every association is annotated with its **multiplicity** and **role label**.

```mermaid
classDiagram
    direction LR
    class Member {
        -memberId : String
        -name : String
        -phone : String
        -address : String
        +searchBook() : void
        +requestIssue() : void
    }
    class Book {
        -isbn : String
        -title : String
        -price : float
        -edition : int
        -copiesAvailable : int
        +displayInfo() : void
        +checkAvailability() : boolean
    }
    class Librarian {
        -empId : String
        -name : String
        -shift : String
        +addBook() : void
        +removeBook() : void
        +registerMember() : void
        +processReturn() : void
    }
    class Author {
        -authorId : String
        -name : String
        -biography : String
        +listBooks() : void
    }
    class Publisher {
        -publisherId : String
        -name : String
        -address : String
        +listBooks() : void
    }
    class Transaction {
        -transactionId : String
        -issueDate : Date
        -dueDate : Date
        -returnDate : Date
        -fineAmount : float
        +calculateFine() : float
        +markReturned() : void
    }

    Member "1" --> "0..*" Book : borrows
    Librarian "1" --> "0..*" Book : issues
    Author "0..*" --> "1..*" Book : writes
    Publisher "1" --> "0..*" Book : publishes
    Transaction "0..*" --> "1" Member : recordedFor
    Transaction "0..*" --> "1" Book : concerns
```

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Object Model

The following diagram explains **how the elements of the object model interact** as a system.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Domain_Universe["Library Management System"]
        direction TB
        C1[Class: Book] -->|attributes| A1[isbn, title, price, edition, copies]
        C1 -->|operations| O1[displayInfo, checkAvailability]
        C2[Class: Member] -->|attributes| A2[memberId, name, phone, address]
        C2 -->|operations| O2[searchBook, requestIssue]
        C3[Class: Librarian] -->|attributes| A3[empId, name, shift]
        C3 -->|operations| O3[addBook, removeBook, registerMember]
        C4[Class: Transaction] -->|attributes| A4[transactionId, issueDate, dueDate, fineAmount]
        C4 -->|operations| O4[calculateFine, markReturned]
        C5[Class: Author] --> A5[authorId, name, bio]
        C6[Class: Publisher] --> A6[publisherId, name, address]
    end

    A1 <-.->|borrows| A2
    A3 <-.->|issues| A1
    A5 <-.->|writes| A1
    A6 <-.->|publishes| A1
    A4 <-.->|recordedFor| A2
    A4 <-.->|concerns| A1

    style C1 fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,color:#000
    style C2 fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
    style C3 fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#4A148C,color:#000
    style C4 fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    style C5 fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#880E4F,color:#000
    style C6 fill:#FFFDE7,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000
```

---

## 4.3 Diagram Notation Legend (High-Yield for KTU)

| Visual Element | Mapped Notation | KTU Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Three-compartment rectangle | Class box | Class with name, attributes, operations |
| Solid line with arrow | Directed association | One class refers to another |
| Number near endpoint | Multiplicity | 1, *, 0..1, 1..* |
| Underlined text in class | Object identifier | Primary key |
| `+` symbol | Public visibility | Accessible from outside |
| `-` symbol | Private visibility | Hidden from outside |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 1, Module 1, 3 Marks]**

**Q1. Define an Object Model. List its four major elements.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
An *Object Model* is a graphical and textual representation of the static structure of a system, capturing the objects, classes, their attributes, operations, and the relationships among them. It is the **first model** in the Rumbaugh OMT triad (Object → Dynamic → Functional).

The four major elements of an object model are:
1. **Classes and Objects** — the basic building blocks with identity and state.
2. **Attributes** — the named data values held by an object.
3. **Operations** — the functions/methods that describe behaviour.
4. **Associations** — the relationships that link classes together, often with multiplicity.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2, Module 1, 3 Marks]**

**Q2. What is multiplicity in an object model? Explain with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
*Multiplicity* (also called **cardinality**) specifies how many instances of one class are associated with how many instances of another class in a given association. It is written as a number or range near the endpoint of an association line.

**Example:** In the Library object model, one `Member` can borrow `0..*` (zero or many) `Book` objects, while a `Book` can be borrowed by exactly `1` Member at a time. The multiplicity is written as `1` near the Member side and `0..*` near the Book side.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1, 14 Marks]**
> Internal choice is provided between **Question A** and **Question B**.

---

### ✦ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

> **Design an object model for an Online Course Registration System. A student registers for one or more courses. A course is taught by exactly one instructor. An instructor can teach many courses. Each course has a course code, title, and credits. A student has a studentId, name, and email. An instructor has an instructorId, name, and department. The system also maintains a Registration record with regId, date, and status.**

#### Part (a) — Identify the classes with their attributes and operations. **[7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Class Identification:**
The nouns in the problem that satisfy the class criteria are: `Student`, `Course`, `Instructor`, `Registration`.

$$
\text{Class Set} = \{\text{Student, Course, Instructor, Registration}\}
$$

**Step 2 — Attributes and Operations:**

| Class | Attributes (with identifier underlined) | Operations |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `Student` | `studentId`, `name`, `email` | `searchCourse()`, `register()` |
| `Course` | `courseCode`, `title`, `credits` | `displaySyllabus()`, `checkSeats()` |
| `Instructor` | `instructorId`, `name`, `department` | `uploadMarks()`, `viewCourses()` |
| `Registration` | `regId`, `date`, `status` | `confirm()`, `cancel()` |

#### Part (b) — Draw the object model diagram with associations, multiplicity, and role names. **[7 Marks]**

**Step 3 — Associations and Multiplicity:**

- `Student` *registers for* `Course` → **Many-to-Many** (one student registers for `1..*` courses; one course has `0..*` students).
- `Instructor` *teaches* `Course` → **One-to-Many** (one instructor teaches `1..*` courses; each course is taught by `1` instructor).
- `Registration` *recordedBy* `Student` → **Many-to-One** (each registration belongs to `1` student; a student may have `0..*` registrations).
- `Registration` *concerns* `Course` → **Many-to-One** (each registration is for `1` course; a course has `0..*` registrations).

```mermaid
classDiagram
    direction LR
    class Student {
      -studentId : String
      -name : String
      -email : String
      +searchCourse() : void
      +register() : void
    }
    class Course {
      -courseCode : String
      -title : String
      -credits : int
      +displaySyllabus() : void
      +checkSeats() : boolean
    }
    class Instructor {
      -instructorId : String
      -name : String
      -department : String
      +uploadMarks() : void
      +viewCourses() : void
    }
    class Registration {
      -regId : String
      -date : Date
      -status : String
      +confirm() : void
      +cancel() : void
    }
    Student "1" --> "1..*" Course : registers for
    Instructor "1" --> "1..*" Course : teaches
    Registration "0..*" --> "1" Student : recordedBy
    Registration "0..*" --> "1" Course : concerns
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question A:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Drawing the Student–Course link directly as *Many-to-Many* and **forgetting** the `Registration` class. Most examiners *want* a separate `Registration` class because the problem mentions a *regId* and *status* — these belong to an associative class, not to Student or Course.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Writing multiplicity as `*..*` (invalid). Correct form is `0..*` or `1..*`. KTU board deducts **1 mark** for every invalid multiplicity symbol.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Omitting *role names* or *association verbs* on the connecting lines. Always label associations with verbs like *"registers for"*, *"teaches"*, *"recordedBy"*.

---

### ✦ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **Design an object model for a Hospital Management System. A patient can be admitted to the hospital and treated by one or more doctors. Each doctor specializes in one department. A department has a departmentId, name, and location. A patient has a patientId, name, age, and gender. A doctor has a doctorId, name, and specialization. An admission record has an admissionId, admitDate, and dischargeDate.**

#### Part (a) — Identify the classes with attributes and operations. **[7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Class Set:**

$$
\text{Class Set} = \{\text{Patient, Doctor, Department, Admission}\}
$$

**Step 2 — Attributes and Operations:**

| Class | Attributes | Operations |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `Patient` | `patientId`, `name`, `age`, `gender` | `requestAppointment()`, `viewReports()` |
| `Doctor` | `doctorId`, `name`, `specialization` | `diagnose()`, `prescribe()` |
| `Department` | `departmentId`, `name`, `location` | `listDoctors()` |
| `Admission` | `admissionId`, `admitDate`, `dischargeDate` | `discharge()`, `generateBill()` |

#### Part (b) — Draw the object model with associations and multiplicity. **[7 Marks]**

**Step 3 — Associations and Multiplicity:**

- `Patient` *admitted via* `Admission` → **1-to-Many** (one patient has `0..*` admissions; each admission is for `1` patient).
- `Patient` *treated by* `Doctor` → **Many-to-Many** (a patient is treated by `1..*` doctors; a doctor treats `0..*` patients).
- `Doctor` *belongs to* `Department` → **Many-to-One** (each doctor belongs to `1` department; a department has `1..*` doctors).
- `Admission` *assigned to* `Doctor` → **Many-to-One** (each admission is supervised by `1` doctor; a doctor supervises `0..*` admissions).

```mermaid
classDiagram
    direction LR
    class Patient {
      -patientId : String
      -name : String
      -age : int
      -gender : String
      +requestAppointment() : void
      +viewReports() : void
    }
    class Doctor {
      -doctorId : String
      -name : String
      -specialization : String
      +diagnose() : void
      +prescribe() : void
    }
    class Department {
      -departmentId : String
      -name : String
      -location : String
      +listDoctors() : void
    }
    class Admission {
      -admissionId : String
      -admitDate : Date
      -dischargeDate : Date
      +discharge() : void
      +generateBill() : float
    }
    Patient "1" --> "0..*" Admission : admitted via
    Patient "1..*" --> "1..*" Doctor : treated by
    Doctor "1..*" --> "1" Department : belongs to
    Admission "0..*" --> "1" Doctor : supervised by
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question B:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Modeling the *Patient–Doctor* relationship as *Many-to-One* instead of *Many-to-Many*. The problem explicitly says *"treated by one or more doctors"* — both ends must be `1..*`.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing `Admission` with `Patient`. `Admission` is a separate class because it carries its own identifier (`admissionId`) and lifecycle (admitDate → dischargeDate). A patient can have **multiple admissions** over time.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Forgetting to under-line the primary identifier attributes (`patientId`, `doctorId`, etc.). In KTU valuation, **1 mark** is reserved specifically for correctly underlining all object identifiers in the class boxes.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Memorise This Block Before Walking Into the Exam Hall.**

- [ ] An **Object Model** captures the *static structure* of a system using **classes, objects, attributes, operations, and associations**.
- [ ] The Object Model is **model #1** in the Rumbaugh OMT triad: **Object → Dynamic → Functional**.
- [ ] A **Class** is a *blueprint*; an **Object** is a *real instance* with a unique identity.
- [ ] **Attributes** describe *state*; **Operations** describe *behaviour*; both live inside a class box.
- [ ] **Primary identifiers** (object IDs) are always *underlined* inside the class box.
- [ ] **Associations** are *solid lines*; the **verb on the line** is the *association name* (e.g., *borrows, registers for*).
- [ ] **Multiplicity** is placed *at the endpoint* of the association line, near the class it refers to.
- [ ] Common multiplicity symbols: `1` (exactly one), `*` or `0..*` (zero or many), `1..*` (one or many), `0..1` (zero or one).
- [ ] **Visibility prefixes** in class boxes: `+` (public), `-` (private), `#` (protected).
- [ ] When the problem mentions a *record* with its own ID and lifecycle (e.g., *Transaction, Registration, Admission*), it is **almost always a separate class**, not just an attribute of an existing class.
- [ ] The five-step construction order: **Classes → Attributes → Operations → Associations → Multiplicity**.
- [ ] Always present answers in three parts: **(i) Class diagram, (ii) Data dictionary (table of attributes/operations), (iii) Multiplicity table**.
- [ ] In KTU 14-mark answers, the typical split is **7 marks for (a)** (class identification + attributes + operations) and **7 marks for (b)** (diagram with associations and multiplicity).
- [ ] **Mermaid rendering safeguard:** use only alphanumeric node IDs (e.g., `node1`, `classA`) and avoid reserved words like `end` or `graph` as node names.
- [ ] A *Sample Object Model* is **not full UML** — it is a *subset* focusing only on Module-1 elements. Inheritance, aggregation, and polymorphism belong to **Module 2**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Booch's Methodology: Notations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCNT604 - OBJECT ORIENTED MODELING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Booch's Methodology: Notations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Booch's Methodology

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Booch's Methodology** is a well-established **object-oriented (OO) analysis and design (OOAD) methodology** introduced by **Grady Booch** (originally for the Ada programming language) and later generalized for all OO languages. It provides a rich set of **graphical and textual notations** for visualizing, specifying, constructing, and documenting the artifacts of a software-intensive system.

Booch's method is fundamentally built upon the **recursive decomposition** of a system into smaller subsystems, which are in turn composed of objects belonging to classes. The methodology is fundamentally iterative and incremental, distinguishing between the **micro process** (day-to-day work) and the **macro process** (overall project management).

### Core Notational Components

The Booch notation framework consists of **seven distinct diagram types** that collectively capture the static and dynamic semantics of an OO system:

| Diagram Type | Purpose | Captures |
|--------------|---------|----------|
| **Class Diagram** | Static structure | Classes, relationships, attributes, operations |
| **Object Diagram** | Static snapshot | Object instances and their relationships |
| **Module Diagram** | Physical packaging | Modules and dependencies |
| **Process Diagram** | System execution | Processor and device allocation |
| **State Transition Diagram** | Dynamic behavior | Object lifecycle states |
| **Interaction Diagram** | Dynamic behavior | Time-based and spatial message passing |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module-1 specifically emphasizes *Notations*, *Symbols*, and *Meaning* of Booch's diagrams — distinguish carefully between **what a diagram represents** vs. **how it is drawn**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are an **architect designing a complex hospital building**. Before construction begins, you would need several specialized blueprints:

- A **floor plan** (showing rooms = classes, their sizes = attributes, purposes = operations)
- An **electrical wiring diagram** (showing how components interact at runtime = sequence diagrams)
- A **plumbing layout** (showing how data flows between objects = collaboration diagrams)
- A **state chart for an elevator** (showing when it moves up, down, stops = state transition diagrams)
- A **city map showing where buildings are constructed** (module diagrams)

**Booch's methodology** works exactly this way for software. Instead of one giant confusing blueprint, Booch recognized that different *views* of the same system are necessary. Each diagram answers a different question:
- *"What classes exist?"* → Class Diagram
- *"How do they behave over time?"* → State Transition Diagram
- *"How do they talk to each other?"* → Interaction Diagram
- *"How is the code physically packaged?"* → Module Diagram

This multi-view approach is now the foundation of modern **UML (Unified Modeling Language)**, which was born from the unification of Booch, Rumbaugh (OMT), and Jacobson (OOSE) notations.

---

## 1.3 Why Booch's Notation Matters in KTU Curriculum

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Booch notation was the **direct predecessor to UML class and object diagrams**. Many UML symbols (visibility, aggregation, inheritance arrows) are inherited directly from Booch. Mastery of Booch's notation therefore provides a strong foundation for advanced UML modules (Modules 2-4 of the KTU syllabus).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Notation Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Booch Cloud: Iconic Class & Object Symbol

The most recognizable feature of Booch's notation is the **"Booch Cloud"** — a cloud-like, lobed boundary used to represent both **classes** and **objects**.

### 2.1.1 Class Notation Structure

A Booch class icon contains **three compartments**:

1. **Class Name Compartment** — always in **bold**, centered, regular typeface
2. **Attribute Compartment** — lists data members
3. **Operation Compartment** — lists method signatures

The visual style:
- Outer boundary is a **curved cloud-like shape**
- Compartment separators are **horizontal dashed lines**

### 2.1.2 Object Notation Structure

For objects, the Booch notation uses the **same cloud shape** but with two key differences:
- The object name is **underlined**
- The format is **`instanceName : ClassName`** (or just `instanceName`)

---

## 2.2 Visibility Markers (KTU High-Yield)

| Symbol | Visibility | Meaning | Default? |
|:------:|:----------:|:--------|:--------:|
| **+** | Public | Accessible to all clients | No |
| **−** | Private | Accessible only within the class itself | Yes (default) |
| **#** | Protected | Accessible to subclasses | No |
| **~** | Package (Friend) | Accessible within the same package/compilation unit | No |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** If a Booch class diagram does **not** specify a visibility marker for an attribute or operation, the default is **Private (−)**. This is a common 3-mark question.

---

## 2.3 Relationship Symbols (Cheat Sheet)

| Relationship | Symbol | Description | KTU Frequency |
|:-------------|:------:|:------------|:-------------:|
| **Association** | Solid line | "Uses-a" relationship between two classes | High |
| **Inheritance (Generalization)** | Solid line with **hollow triangle** (▷) | "Is-a" relationship; subclass → superclass | Very High |
| **Aggregation** | Solid line with **hollow diamond** (◇) | "Has-a" weak containment (whole–part) | High |
| **Composition** | Solid line with **filled diamond** (◆) | Strong form of aggregation; lifetime dependency | Medium |
| **Uses / Dependency** | **Dashed line** with arrow | One class depends on another | Medium |
| **Meta-class instantiation** | Solid line with **dashed line crossing circle** | Class is an instance of another class | Low |

### KTU Formula Sheet — Notation Translation Table

| Booch Notation Concept | Equivalent UML Symbol (for cross-reference) |
|:----------------------:|:------------------------------------------:|
| Booch Cloud (class) | Rectangle (class box) |
| Hollow triangle for inheritance | Same hollow triangle (▷) |
| Hollow diamond (◇) aggregation | Same hollow diamond |
| Filled diamond (◆) composition | Same filled diamond |
| Dashed arrow for dependency | Same dashed arrow |
| Underlined object name | Underlined object name |
| **−**, **+**, **#,** ~ visibility | **−**, **+**, **#,** ~ visibility (identical) |

---

## 2.4 State Transition Diagram Notation

Booch's state diagrams closely resemble **Harel Statecharts**, but use rounded rectangles for states:

| Symbol | Meaning |
|:------:|:--------|
| **Rounded rectangle** | A state |
| **Solid arrow** with label | Transition: `event [guard] / action` |
| **Filled black circle (●)** | Start (initial) state |
| **Bull's-eye circle (⊕)** | End (final) state |
| **Dashed line enclosing states** | Concurrent sub-state grouping |

---

## 2.5 Interaction Diagram Notation

Booch split interaction modeling into two distinct diagrams:

### (a) Sequence Diagram (Time-Oriented)
- **Vertical axis** = time (top → bottom)
- **Horizontal axis** = different objects
- **Lifelines** = vertical dashed lines under each object
- **Horizontal arrows** between lifelines = messages
- **Solid arrowhead** = synchronous call
- **Stick arrow / open arrowhead** = asynchronous / simple message
- **Dashed arrow returning** = return of control (optional)
- **Small rectangle on lifeline** = activation (focus of control)

### (b) Collaboration Diagram (Space-Oriented)
- **Objects** appear as rectangles (or Booch clouds)
- **Links** drawn as solid lines connecting objects
- **Messages** labeled with sequence number prefix:
  
  $$M_i \quad \text{where } i = 1, 2, 3, \dots$$

  Nested calls are denoted by dot-notation, e.g., `1.2.3` denotes a call 3 levels deep inside message 1.

---

## 2.6 Module Diagram Notation

| Symbol | Meaning |
|:------:|:--------|
| **Rectangle module** | A logical module grouping of classes |
| **Main module** | The executable entry-point module |
| **Module with «subsystem» stereotype** | A larger grouping containing classes |

Dependencies between modules are shown with **dashed arrows**.

---

## 2.7 Process Diagram Notation

Process diagrams show the allocation of threads/processes to **processors** and **devices** in the deployment environment.

- **Rectangle labeled «processor»** = CPU/processor node
- **Rectangle labeled «device»** = peripheral/hardware device
- **Solid arrows** = physical communication connections

> [!NOTE]
> Process diagrams in Booch are the conceptual ancestors of UML **Deployment Diagrams**.

---

## 2.8 Real-World Utility of Booch's Notation

| Engineering Domain | How Booch Notation Is Used |
|:-------------------|:---------------------------|
| **Enterprise Software** | Visualizing complex class hierarchies in banking systems |
| **Embedded Systems** | Modeling state machines for controllers (ABS, IoT) |
| **Telecommunications** | Protocol design using state transition diagrams |
| **Game Development** | Class diagrams for entity-component systems |
| **Defense & Aerospace** | Mission-critical software where rigor is paramount (Booch was used at Rational, now IBM) |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides an **exhaustive structural breakdown** of each Booch notation element with worked analytical examples. Every characteristic and rule is expanded step-by-step so you can produce any Booch diagram accurately in your KTU examination.

---

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Booch Class Diagram Construction (Step-by-Step)

**Problem:** Draw the Booch class diagram for a simple `Student` class with attributes and operations.

### Step 1: Identify the Class Name
The class name must be:
- A **noun** (singular preferred)
- In **bold**
- Centered in the top compartment

$$\text{Class Name} = \text{Student}$$

### Step 2: Identify Attributes
For a `Student` class, possible attributes are:
- `name : String`
- `rollNumber : int`
- `cgpa : float`

Each attribute uses the canonical Booch syntax:

$$\text{Attribute} = \text{visibility}\;\text{name} \;:\; \text{type} \;[\text{= initialValue}]$$

Applying visibility markers:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&+ \text{name} : \text{String} \\
&- \text{rollNumber} : \text{int} \\
&\# \text{cgpa} : \text{float} = 0.0
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** `+` means public (accessible to all), `-` means private (default), `#` means protected (accessible to subclasses).

### Step 3: Identify Operations
Operations follow the syntax:

$$\text{Operation} = \text{visibility}\;\text{name}(\text{parameterList}) : \text{returnType}$$

For a Student class, the operations may be:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&+ \text{getName}() : \text{String} \\
&+ \text{setName}(\text{n : String}) : \text{void} \\
&+ \text{calculateGrade}() : \text{char}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Assemble the Booch Cloud Icon
Combine the three compartments inside the Booch cloud boundary:

```
    ┌─────[ Cloud Boundary ]─────┐
    │         Student            │   ← Bold, centered
    │ ────────────────────────── │   ← Dashed separator
    │ + name : String             │
    │ - rollNumber : int          │
    │ # cgpa : float = 0.0        │
    │ ────────────────────────── │   ← Dashed separator
    │ + getName() : String        │
    │ + setName(n:String) : void  │
    │ + calculateGrade() : char   │
    └─────────────────────────────┘
```

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Booch Object Diagram Construction

**Problem:** Draw a Booch object diagram for an instance `s1` of the `Student` class.

### Step 1: Identify the Object Name and Class
Use the canonical instance syntax:

$$\text{Object Name} = \text{instanceName} : \text{ClassName}$$

$$\therefore \; s1 : \text{Student}$$

### Step 2: Apply Object-Specific Rules
- The **entire name must be underlined** (both instance and class portions)
- Only the **attribute compartment** is shown (no operations for objects)
- Attribute values replace types

### Step 3: Build the Object Icon

```
    ┌─────[ Cloud Boundary ]─────┐
    │       s1 : Student         │   ← UNDERLINED, bold
    │ ────────────────────────── │
    │ + name = "Arjun"           │
    │ - rollNumber = 47          │
    │ # cgpa = 8.9               │
    └─────────────────────────────┘
```

> **Conversion Logic:** The three key differences between a Booch class and object icon are:
> 1. Underlined name
> 2. Instance name **precedes** the class name with `:` separator
> 3. Only attributes (with values) are shown, never operations

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Booch Inheritance Derivation

**Problem:** Model a `GraduateStudent` class inheriting from `Student`.

### Step 1: Identify the "Is-A" Relationship
Inheritance exists when one class is a specialization of another. The **subclass** (child) is more specific, and the **superclass** (parent) is more general.

$$\text{GraduateStudent} \;\sqsubseteq\; \text{Student}$$

### Step 2: Apply Booch's Inheritance Symbol
Use a **solid line with a hollow triangle (▷)** pointing from subclass to superclass.

### Step 3: Add the Additional Subclass Members
The `GraduateStudent` class may add:
- Attribute: `+ researchArea : String`
- Operation: `+ submitThesis() : void`

### Step 4: Visual Construction

```
    [GraduateStudent Cloud]                    [Student Cloud]
    │ GraduateStudent       │                  │ Student           │
    │ ─────────────────────  │                  │ ───────────────── │
    │ + researchArea : String│                  │ + name : String   │
    │ ─────────────────────  │                  │ - rollNumber : int│
    │ + submitThesis() : void│                  │ # cgpa : float    │
```
