Remember: **Aggregation = Hollow diamond (parts survive), Composition = Filled diamond (parts die with whole)**. Another frequent mistake is using `*` notation for both — the diamond shape is mandatory. Examiners deduct up to 2 marks for not using the correct UML symbol.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Before Every Exam**

- ✅ **Object-Oriented Modeling** is a language-agnostic way of designing software using objects that combine data and behavior.

- ✅ **Rumbaugh's OMT** defines **three models**: Object (static), Dynamic (temporal), Functional (transformational). All three must be **consistent**.

- ✅ **Four core OO concepts** = Abstraction + Encapsulation + Inheritance + Polymorphism. Use the mnemonic **"A-E-I-P"** = "**A**ll **E**ngineers **I**nherit **P**olymorphism."

- ✅ **Class vs. Object:** Class is the *blueprint*; Object is the *instance* with allocated memory.

- ✅ **Three structural relationships:**
  - **Association** → solid line (independent)
  - **Aggregation** → hollow diamond ◇ (weak whole–part)
  - **Composition** → filled diamond ◆ (strong whole–part, lifecycle tied)

- ✅ **OMT four phases** = Analysis → System Design → Object Design → Implementation. Each phase has **distinct deliverables**; mention them in the exam.

- ✅ **Three Amigos of OOAD** = Rumbaugh + Booch + Jacobson. Their unified work became **UML** under the **OMG** standardization.

- ✅ **Abstraction** = showing *what*, hiding *how*. **Encapsulation** = bundling + access control. They are **not synonyms**.

- ✅ **Polymorphism** = one interface, many implementations. Achieved via method **overriding** (runtime) and **overloading** (compile-time).

- ✅ **Noun-Verb rule in OOA:** Nouns in the problem statement become **classes**; verbs become **operations or relationships**. Apply this in any exam derivation.

- ✅ **Dynamic Model** uses **State Diagrams**; **Functional Model** uses **DFDs**; **Object Model** uses **Class Diagrams**. Do not mix them up.

- ✅ **Identity** is the property that distinguishes an object from all others, even if its state is identical to another object. This is what makes OOP different from traditional record-based programming.

- ✅ **Inheritance represents an "is-a" relationship**; **Association/Aggregation/Composition represent "has-a" or "uses-a" relationships**. Distinguishing these is a classic exam question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Events and States
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCNT604 - OBJECT ORIENTED MODELING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Events and States

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Events

In the context of **Object-Oriented Modeling and Design (OOMD)** as per the KTU 2024 syllabus, an **Event** is defined as a noteworthy occurrence that has a significant impact on the state of an object, and which the object must respond to in a defined manner. Formally, an event is a one-time occurrence of a stimulus from one object to another, or from the system boundary to an object.

Mathematically, an event can be denoted as:

$$E = \langle \text{Object}_{source}, \text{Object}_{target}, \text{Trigger}, \text{Parameters} \rangle$$

Where:
- $\text{Object}_{source}$ = the originator of the event
- $\text{Object}_{target}$ = the receiver of the event
- $\text{Trigger}$ = the signal/call that fires the event
- $\text{Parameters}$ = optional data payload carried by the event

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Events are a core construct of the **Dynamic Model** in OMT (Rumbaugh's methodology). They are the *external triggers* that cause the state of an object to change. Without events, state machines would have no input mechanism.

### 1.2 Formal Definition of States

A **State** is an abstraction of the attribute values and links of an object. More rigorously, a state represents a condition or situation during the life of an object during which it satisfies some condition, performs an activity, or waits for an event. The state of an object is defined by the set of values of its attributes at a given instant.

$$S = \{ s \mid s = \text{condition}(\text{attributes}, \text{links}) \text{ holding over a time interval} \}$$

A state has **duration** (unlike an event, which is instantaneous).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Real-World Analogy — A Traffic Light System**

Think of a **traffic light controller** as an object. Its life cycle is filled with discrete situations:

- **State** = The *color* it is currently showing (Red, Green, Yellow). While in the "Red" state, the light remains red for a *duration* of time (e.g., 30 seconds). This is a **state**.

- **Event** = An *occurrence* like "timer expired" or "emergency vehicle detected". These are instantaneous triggers that cause the light to change its color. These are **events**.

- **Transition** = The actual *change* from Red → Green when the event "timer-expired" occurs.

Similarly, an **ATM machine object** can be in states like `Idle`, `CardInserted`, `PINEntered`, `DispensingCash`. The event "card-inserted" causes a transition from `Idle` to `CardInserted`. The event "wrong-PIN-3-times" may transition to `Locked`.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook:** *Events are the causes, States are the effects.* A state **lasts**; an event **happens**.

## 1.4 Why Events and States Matter in OOD

| Aspect | Events | States |
|---|---|---|
| **Temporal Nature** | Instantaneous (zero duration) | Has duration (non-zero interval) |
| **Purpose** | Trigger state changes | Capture conditions/object behavior over time |
| **Diagram Role** | Labels on transitions in state diagrams | Nodes/boxes in state diagrams |
| **Engineering Utility** | Model inter-object communication | Model object lifecycle & control flow |
| **KTU Exam Weight** | 40% of Module 2 | 60% of Module 2 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric (OMT Notation):** A state diagram must have exactly **one initial state** and may have one or more **final states** per object lifecycle.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Taxonomy of Events

Events in OMT/OOMD are classified into **four primary categories**. KTU frequently tests this classification.

### A. Signal Event
A signal event is the most common type. It represents the explicit receipt of a signal (message/call) from another object or the external environment.

**Example:** `button-pressed()`, `order-received(orderID)`

$$E_{signal} = \text{Object receives an asynchronous notification}$$

### B. Call Event
A call event represents the synchronous invocation of an operation on the object.

$$E_{call} = \text{Object receives a method call (synchronous)}$$

### C. Change Event
A change event is fired when a condition on an attribute becomes true. It is denoted using the `when` keyword.

$$E_{change} = \text{when}(\text{condition on attribute}) \rightarrow \text{fire transition}$$

**Example:** `when(temperature > 100°C)` — fires only when temperature crosses the threshold.

### D. Time Event
A time event is fired after a specified duration has elapsed from a reference point.

$$E_{time} = \text{after}(\Delta t) \rightarrow \text{fire transition}$$

**Example:** `after(30 seconds)` — fires the event 30 seconds after entering a state.

## 2.2 Detailed Components of a State

A fully-qualified state in OMT may consist of the following sub-parts:

| Sub-component | Notation | Meaning | When Executed |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Entry Action** | `entry / action` | Activity performed upon entering the state | On state entry |
| **Exit Action** | `exit / action` | Activity performed upon leaving the state | On state exit |
| **Do Activity** | `do / activity` | Ongoing work while in the state | During the state |
| **Internal Transition** | `event / action` | Event handled without leaving the state | While in state, no state change |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Critical Distinction:** An *internal transition* does **not** cause a state change, but it does cause the state to re-trigger its entry and exit actions. This is a common exam pitfall.

## 2.3 Transition Logic — The 5-Tuple Model

A state transition is the heart of dynamic modeling. A transition is formally defined as a 5-tuple:

$$T = \langle S_{source}, E, G, A, S_{target} \rangle$$

Where:
- $S_{source}$ = Source state
- $E$ = Triggering event
- $G$ = Guard condition (boolean expression)
- $A$ = Action performed during transition
- $S_{target}$ = Target state

**Triggering Condition for Transition:**

$$T_{fires} \iff (E \text{ occurs}) \land (G = \text{true})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A transition fires **only if** both the event occurs AND the guard condition evaluates to `true`. If only the event occurs but the guard is `false`, the transition is silently ignored.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Notation | Formal Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Event** | `event-name(args)` | Instantaneous occurrence | `button-pressed()` |
| **State** | `StateName` | Condition with duration | `Idle`, `Active` |
| **Transition** | `E [G] / A` | Event-Guard-Action tuple | `timeout [balance>0] / debit()` |
| **Guard Condition** | `[condition]` | Boolean expression | `[temp > 100]` |
| **Action** | `/ action()` | Atomic, non-interruptible | `/ log("done")` |
| **Activity** | `do / activity` | Can be interrupted | `do / processData()` |
| **Initial State** | Filled black circle | Start of lifecycle | `●` |
| **Final State** | Bull's-eye | End of lifecycle | `◉` |
| **Change Event** | `when(cond)` | Fires on condition becoming true | `when(mode=ON)` |
| **Time Event** | `after(t)` | Fires after duration | `after(5 sec)` |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** Events and states form the basis of **finite state machines (FSMs)** used in embedded systems (washing machines, elevators), protocol design (TCP state machine), UI controllers, and real-time control systems.

## 2.5 State Diagrams — Construction Rules (KTU Board Pattern)

The KTU examiners expect students to follow these rules when drawing state diagrams:

1. **Every state diagram has exactly one initial state** (filled circle), no exceptions.
2. **Final states are optional** — multiple final states are allowed (e.g., success vs. failure).
3. **State names must be unique** within a given state diagram.
4. **Transitions are directed arcs** — never undirected lines.
5. **Self-loops represent internal transitions** — common in KTU questions.
6. **Composite/Nested states** are advanced; KTU typically tests flat (1-level) state diagrams.

## 2.6 Event Inheritance and Polymorphism

In OMT, events can be organized into hierarchies using inheritance:

$$E_{sub} \text{ inherits from } E_{parent} \implies \text{Handling } E_{parent} \text{ also handles } E_{sub}$$

This mirrors the Generalization relationship in class diagrams and is a high-yield KTU topic.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Transition Firing Rule

We derive the exact condition under which a transition fires, which is essential for analyzing KTU problems with guard conditions.

**Step 1: Define the universe of events occurring in time.**

Let $E(t)$ be a boolean function representing whether event $E$ has occurred at time $t$:

$$E(t) = 
\begin{cases} 
1 & \text{if event } E \text{ occurs at instant } t \\
0 & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}$$

**Step 2: Define the guard condition as a function of object attributes.**

Let $G(a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_n)$ be the guard expression evaluated on the current attribute values:

$$G: \mathbb{A}_1 \times \mathbb{A}_2 \times \cdots \times \mathbb{A}_n \rightarrow \{0, 1\}$$

**Step 3: The system is currently in source state $S_{source}$.**

Let $C(t)$ denote the current state of the object at time $t$.

$$C(t) = S_{source} \quad \text{(precondition for transition to be eligible)}$$

**Step 4: Compose the firing condition.**

The transition fires if and only if **all three** conditions hold simultaneously:

$$F_{fire} = (C(t) = S_{source}) \land (E(t) = 1) \land (G(a_1, \ldots, a_n) = 1)$$

**Step 5: Apply the action and update state.**

Upon firing, the action $A$ executes atomically, and the state updates to $S_{target}$:

$$C(t^+) = S_{target} \quad \text{where } t^+ = t + \epsilon, \epsilon \to 0^+$$

The "$\epsilon$" represents the infinitesimal time after the event, reflecting the **instantaneous** nature of the transition.

**Step 6: The final system equation.**

$$\boxed{C(t^+) = 
\begin{cases}
S_{target} & \text{if } F_{fire} = 1 \\
C(t) & \text{if } F_{fire} = 0
\end{cases}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This derivation is the foundation of all reactive system design. It is mathematically equivalent to a **Mealy Machine** transition function:
> $$\delta: S \times E \times G \rightarrow S \times A$$
> where $\delta$ is the state-transition function.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Event-State Examples (KTU-Pattern)

The following matrix maps common real-world objects to their events and states — a frequent KTU question pattern.

| Object | Key States | Triggering Events | Notable Transitions |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Elevator** | Idle, Moving Up, Moving Down, Doors Open | `floorButtonPressed(floor)`, `doorSensorTriggered`, `timerExpired` | `Idle → MovingUp` (on `floorButtonPressed`) |
| **Vending Machine** | Idle, Coin Inserted, Item Selected, Dispensing | `coinInserted(amount)`, `selectionMade(code)`, `itemDispensed` | `ItemSelected → Dispensing` (on `selectionMade`) |
| **TCP Connection** | LISTEN, SYN_SENT, ESTABLISHED, CLOSE_WAIT, CLOSED | `SYN`, `ACK`, `FIN`, `RST` (timeouts) | `ESTABLISHED → CLOSE_WAIT` (on `FIN`) |
| **Phone Call** | Idle, Ringing, Connected, On Hold | `incomingCall()`, `answerPressed()`, `holdPressed()`, `endPressed()` | `Connected → OnHold` (on `holdPressed`) |
| **Login Session** | LoggedOut, Authenticating, LoggedIn, Locked | `credentialsEntered()`, `authSuccess`, `authFailure`, `timeoutExpired` | `Authenticated → Locked` (on `failureCount > 3`) |

## 3.3 State Reduction Theorem (For KTU Advanced Problems)

In some KTU problems, students are asked to **minimize** a state diagram. The number of equivalent states can be found using the **partition refinement algorithm**:

$$n_{reduced} \leq n_{original}$$

For a state machine with $n$ states and $k$ distinct event types, the maximum number of transitions is bounded by:

$$T_{max} = n \times k$$

And the minimum number of states for a meaningful FSM is:

$$n_{min} = 2 \quad (\text{one state plus one final state, or two alternating states})$$

These bounds are useful in KTU problems asking about diagram correctness or minimization.

## 3.4 Worked Example: Door Object State Diagram Analysis

**Problem:** A door has two states (`Open`, `Closed`). It transitions to Open on event `pull()` and to Closed on event `push()`. The door logs every transition.

**Solution Derivation:**

**Step 1:** List states.
$$S = \{ S_{Open}, S_{Closed} \}$$

**Step 2:** List events.
$$E = \{ e_{pull}, e_{push} \}$$

**Step 3:** Define transitions.
$$T_1 = \langle S_{Closed}, e_{pull}, G_1 = \text{true}, A_1 = \text{log("opened")}, S_{Open} \rangle$$
$$T_2 = \langle S_{Open}, e_{push}, G_2 = \text{true}, A_2 = \text{log("closed")}, S_{Closed} \rangle$$

**Step 4:** Apply firing rule.
The transition $T_1$ fires if and only if $C(t) = S_{Closed}$ and $e_{pull}$ occurs. Since $G_1$ is always true, the condition simplifies to:

$$T_1 \text{ fires } \iff C(t) = S_{Closed} \land E(t) = e_{pull}$$

**Step 5:** Final state equation.

$$C(t^+) = 
\begin{cases}
S_{Open} & \text{if } C(t) = S_{Closed} \land E(t) = e_{pull} \\
S_{Closed} & \text{if } C(t) = S_{Open} \land E(t) = e_{push} \\
C(t) & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}$$

This is a clean KTU-ready solution.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid State Diagram — ATM Object Lifecycle

The following state diagram illustrates an ATM's dynamic behavior using the Mermaid `stateDiagram-v2` syntax. All node IDs are alphanumeric to comply with Mermaid's parsing rules.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> atmStateIdle
    atmStateIdle --> atmStateCardInserted: cardInserted
    atmStateCardInserted --> atmStatePinEntered: pinEntered
    atmStateCardInserted --> atmStateCardRetained: invalidPin [attempt = 3]
    atmStatePinEntered --> atmStateSelecting: pinValidated
    atmStatePinEntered --> atmStateCardRetained: invalidPin
    atmStateSelecting --> atmStateDispensing: amountSelected [balance >= amount]
    atmStateSelecting --> atmStateInsufficient: amountSelected [balance < amount]
    atmStateDispensing --> atmStatePrinting: cashDispensed
    atmStatePrinting --> atmStateIdle: receiptPrinted / ejectCard
    atmStateInsufficient --> atmStateSelecting: cancelPressed
    atmStateCardRetained --> [*]
```

**Diagram Explanation (for the KTU answer sheet):**

1. **Initial pseudo-state** `[*]` flows into `atmStateIdle` — the ATM is ready and waiting.
2. The event `cardInserted` transitions the ATM to `atmStateCardInserted`.
3. Upon `pinEntered`, the ATM moves to `atmStatePinEntered`; the guard condition `[attempt = 3]` blocks the user after 3 wrong PINs and moves to `atmStateCardRetained` (a final state).
4. From `atmStateSelecting`, the guard conditions `[balance >= amount]` and `[balance < amount]` route the flow to either `atmStateDispensing` or `atmStateInsufficient`.
5. After `receiptPrinted`, the action `/ ejectCard` runs and the ATM returns to the idle state.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Event–State Architecture Flow

For students who prefer a top-down architectural view, the following Mermaid flowchart shows how events, guards, actions, and state updates are interconnected within a single transition step.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Current State of Object] --> B{Event Occurs in System}
    B -- No --> A
    B -- Yes --> C{Guard Condition Evaluates to True}
    C -- No --> A
    C -- Yes --> D[Execute Transition Action]
    D --> E[Apply Exit Actions of Old State]
    E --> F[Update State to Target State]
    F --> G[Apply Entry Actions of New State]
    G --> H[Resume Do Activity in New State]
    H --> A
```

**Block-Level Interpretation:**

- **Block A** is the "starting point" — the object exists in some state.
- **Block B** checks if an event has been signaled.
- **Block C** is the *guard check* — a critical filter.
- **Block D** executes the action attached to the transition.
- **Blocks E and G** are the exit and entry actions (omitted if not defined).
- **Block H** is the new state's ongoing behavior (e.g., `do / activity`).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Drawing Tip:** When asked to draw a state diagram in the exam, always label transitions in the form `event [guard] / action` and place the **initial state as a filled black circle** and the **final state as a bull's-eye (circle within a circle)**. Examiners specifically check for these.

## 4.3 Conceptual Hierarchy Diagram — Event Classification

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    evtRoot[Events in OMT] --> evtA[Signal Event]
    evtRoot --> evtB[Call Event]
    evtRoot --> evtC[Change Event]
    evtRoot --> evtD[Time Event]
    evtA --> exA1[Example: buttonPressed]
    evtB --> exB1[Example: computeTotal]
    evtC --> exC1[Example: when temp exceeds limit]
    evtD --> exD1[Example: after 10 seconds]
```

This block diagram reinforces the four-part event taxonomy discussed in Section 2.1 — a frequently tested KTU topic.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define an event. List and briefly explain the different types of events in the OMT dynamic model.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

An **event** is a noteworthy, instantaneous occurrence that triggers a state change in an object or signals a transition between objects. Events are the dynamic stimuli in the OMT model.

The four types of events are:
1. **Signal Event** — A notification received asynchronously from another object. *Example: `alarmTriggered()`*
2. **Call Event** — A synchronous operation invocation. *Example: `calculateTotal(price, qty)`*
3. **Change Event** — Fires when a condition on an attribute becomes true. *Example: `when(speed > 120)`*
4. **Time Event** — Fires after a specified duration. *Example: `after(30 seconds)`*

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is a state in object-oriented modeling? Differentiate between an action and an activity in a state diagram.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **state** is a condition or situation in the life of an object during which it satisfies some condition, performs some activity, or waits for an event. States have a time duration.

| Aspect | Action | Activity |
|---|---|---|
| **Duration** | Atomic / instantaneous | Has duration / interruptible |
| **Notation** | `/ action` | `do / activity` |
| **Interruptible** | No | Yes |
| **Example** | `/ setCounter(0)` | `do / processPayment` |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Always include the initial state** (filled black circle) — students lose 2 marks for omitting it.
> 2. **Do not confuse action with activity** — actions are atomic, activities are not.
> 3. **Guard conditions must be enclosed in square brackets `[ ]`** — using parentheses `( )` is a common mistake that costs 1 mark.
> 4. **For 14-mark questions, draw the diagram neatly and label all transitions** — unlabeled transitions get 0 marks for that transition.
> 5. **Mention entry/exit actions explicitly** when they are part of the question — examiners look for the `entry/` and `exit/` keywords.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern]

**A washing machine controller has the following behavior:**
- It starts in the `Idle` state.
- On event `startPressed(washType)`, it enters the `FillingWater` state.
- Once water level reaches the threshold (change event `when(level >= threshold)`), it transitions to `HeatingWater`.
- When temperature reaches 60°C (`when(temp >= 60)`), it transitions to `Washing` (do activity: `do / agitateMotor`).
- After 30 minutes (`after(30 minutes)`), it transitions to `Rinsing`.
- After rinsing, it transitions to `Spinning`.
- After spinning, the cycle ends (final state).
- A `cancelPressed` event from any state returns the machine to `Idle` and stops all operations.

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:** Draw the complete state diagram for the washing machine controller. Label all states, events, guards, and actions.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:** Explain the role of change events and time events in this state diagram with specific references to the transitions.

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) — Solution:**

The state diagram has the following components:

**States (6 total):**
- `Idle` (initial state)
- `FillingWater`
- `HeatingWater`
- `Washing`
- `Rinsing`
- `Spinning`
- Final state (after Spinning)

**Transitions (with event-guard-action labels):**

1. `Idle → FillingWater`: Event = `startPressed(washType)`, Action = `/ openInletValve`
2. `FillingWater → HeatingWater`: Event = `when(level >= threshold)`, Action = `/ closeInletValve, startHeater`
3. `HeatingWater → Washing`: Event = `when(temp >= 60)`, Action = `/ startMotor`
4. `Washing → Rinsing`: Event = `after(30 minutes)`, Action = `/ stopMotor, openDrain`
5. `Rinsing → Spinning`: Event = `after(10 minutes)`, Action = `/ closeDrain, startSpin`
6. `Spinning → Final`: Event = `after(5 minutes)`, Action = `/ stopSpin, beep`

**Cancel transitions from every non-idle state to `Idle`:**
- Event = `cancelPressed`, Action = `/ emergencyStop, drainWater`

**ASCII Representation of the State Diagram (for the answer sheet):**

```
    [●] --> Idle
    Idle --startPressed(washType)/openInletValve--> FillingWater
    FillingWater --when(level>=threshold)/closeInletValve--> HeatingWater
    HeatingWater --when(temp>=60)/startMotor--> Washing
    Washing --after(30 min)/stopMotor--> Rinsing
    Rinsing --after(10 min)/closeDrain--> Spinning
    Spinning --after(5 min)/beep--> [(○)]
    Idle, FillingWater, HeatingWater, Washing, Rinsing, Spinning --cancelPressed/emergencyStop--> Idle
```

**Mark Allocation (7 marks):**
- 1 mark: Initial state and final state correctly drawn
- 2 marks: All 6 intermediate states correctly named
- 3 marks: All transitions correctly labeled with event-guard-action
- 1 mark: Cancel transitions from all states

**Part (b) — Solution:**

In this state diagram, **change events** and **time events** play distinct but complementary roles:

1. **Change Events** — These are based on **attribute values reaching a threshold**, not on a fixed time. In the washing machine:
   - `when(level >= threshold)` fires when the water sensor detects sufficient water. This is robust because the actual time to fill depends on water pressure, which varies. Using a change event ensures the machine always proceeds when the **physical condition** is met, regardless of how long it took.
   - `when(temp >= 60)` similarly ensures the water is genuinely hot enough before washing begins.

2. **Time Events** — These are based on **fixed durations**, irrespective of external conditions. In the washing machine:
   - `after(30 minutes)` for washing means the agitation runs for a fixed duration, regardless of how clean the clothes are.
   - `after(10 minutes)` for rinsing, `after(5 minutes)` for spinning.

3. **Engineering Trade-off** — The washing machine uses **change events for safety/critical conditions** (water level, temperature) where waiting for a fixed time could damage components or underperform, and **time events for procedural durations** where the cycle length is empirical and predetermined.

This hybrid use of change and time events is a hallmark of well-designed reactive systems and is a KTU-favored discussion point.

**Mark Allocation (7 marks):**
- 3 marks: Correct explanation of change events with washing machine context
- 3 marks: Correct explanation of time events with washing machine context
- 1 mark: Engineering trade-off / why both types are used

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]

**A library book reservation system tracks a book's lifecycle. The book can be in one of the following states: `Available`, `Reserved`, `CheckedOut`, `Overdue`, `Returned`.**

- A book starts in `Available`.
- `reserveBook()` from `Available` → `Reserved`.
- `checkOut()` from `Reserved` → `CheckedOut`.
- A `dueDateReached` event with guard `[currentDate > dueDate]` from `CheckedOut` → `Overdue`.
- `returnBook()` from `CheckedOut` → `Returned`.
- `returnBook()` from `Overdue` → `Returned` (with a fine calculated).
- `cancelReservation()` from `Reserved` → `Available`.
- After `Returned`, the system calculates the fine (do activity) and returns to `Available` after the fine is paid (event `finePaid`).

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:** Construct the state transition table for this system. Identify all states, events, guards, and actions.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:** Draw the state diagram and explain how the guard condition `[currentDate > dueDate]` is critical for the system's correctness.

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) — Solution: State Transition Table**

| Source State | Event | Guard Condition | Action | Target State |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `Available` | `reserveBook()` | `true` | `/ markReserved` | `Reserved` |
| `Reserved` | `checkOut()` | `true` | `/ setDueDate, updateLog` | `CheckedOut` |
| `Reserved` | `cancelReservation()` | `true` | `/ clearReservation` | `Available` |
| `CheckedOut` | `dueDateReached` | `[currentDate > dueDate]` | `/ flagOverdue, sendNotice` | `Overdue` |
| `CheckedOut` | `returnBook()` | `true` | `/ calculateBaseFine = 0` | `Returned` |
| `Overdue` | `returnBook()` | `true` | `/ calculateFine(daysLate * rate)` | `Returned` |
| `Returned` | `finePaid` | `[fineAmount > 0]` | `/ updateAccount, clearFine` | `Available` |
| `Returned` | (auto) | `[fineAmount = 0]` | `/ autoClearFine` | `Available` |

**Mark Allocation (7 marks):**
- 2 marks: All states correctly identified
- 2 marks: All events correctly identified
- 2 marks: All guards and actions correctly identified
- 1 mark: Neat tabular format

**Part (b) — Solution:**

**State Diagram (Textual Representation):**

```
    [●] --> Available
    Available --reserveBook()/markReserved--> Reserved
    Reserved --checkOut()/setDueDate--> CheckedOut
    Reserved --cancelReservation()/clearReservation--> Available
    CheckedOut --returnBook()/baseFine=0--> Returned
    CheckedOut --dueDateReached[currentDate > dueDate]/flagOverdue--> Overdue
    Overdue --returnBook()/calcFine--> Returned
    Returned --finePaid[fine>0]/updateAccount--> Available
    Returned --auto[fine=0]/autoClear--> Available
```

**Explanation of the Critical Guard Condition `[currentDate > dueDate]`:**

The guard condition is essential for the system's correctness for the following reasons:

1. **Temporal Accuracy** — The transition to the `Overdue` state should **only** occur when the actual current date exceeds the due date. Without the guard, the system would mark books as overdue prematurely, leading to incorrect fine calculations and user complaints.

2. **Event-Guard Decoupling** — The event `dueDateReached` may be generated by a periodic timer (e.g., a daily cron job). The timer may fire on the same day as the due date but at a time when the book is still legitimately within the loan period. The guard `[currentDate > dueDate]` filters out these spurious events, ensuring the transition only fires on **strict inequality**.

3. **Correctness of Fine Calculation** — In the `Overdue` state, the fine is calculated as $\text{fine} = \text{daysLate} \times \text{rate}$. If the book enters `Overdue` one day early due to a missing guard, the user would be incorrectly fined. The guard thus acts as a **safety net** for financial correctness.

4. **Boundary Case Handling** — When `currentDate == dueDate`, the guard evaluates to `false`, and the book remains in `CheckedOut` — this is the correct behavior, as the book is technically due **at the end** of that day.

**Mark Allocation (7 marks):**
- 2 marks: Correct state diagram with all transitions
- 3 marks: Correct explanation of the guard's role
- 2 marks: Engineering/financial correctness justification

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Events and States (Module 2)**

- ☐ **Event** = Instantaneous occurrence that triggers a state change. 4 types: Signal, Call, Change, Time.
- ☐ **State** = Condition with duration; defined by attribute values of an object.
- ☐ **Transition 5-tuple**: $\langle S_{source}, E, G, A, S_{target} \rangle$. Fires when $E$ occurs AND $G = \text{true}$.
- ☐ **Action** is atomic (`/ action`); **Activity** is interruptible (`do / activity`).
- ☐ **Entry action** = `entry/`, **Exit action** = `exit/`. These run on every entry/exit.
- ☐ **Change event** = `when(condition)` — fires on condition becoming true.
- ☐ **Time event** = `after(duration)` — fires after fixed duration.
- ☐ **Guard condition** = `[boolean expression]` — must be in square brackets.
- ☐ **State diagram rules**: 1 initial state (filled circle), optional final states (bull's-eye), unique state names, directed transitions only.
- ☐ **Internal transition** = self-loop; does not trigger state exit/entry.
- ☐ **Event inheritance** = events can be organized in generalization hierarchies; handling a parent event handles all children.
- ☐ **Dynamic Model** in OMT = state diagrams + event flow + scenarios.
- ☐ **Common KTU drawing mistakes**: omitting initial state, using `( )` instead of `[ ]` for guards, confusing action with activity, missing final state.
- ☐ **Mathematical model**: Transition fires iff $C(t) = S_{source} \land E(t) = 1 \land G = 1$.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "E-S-T-A-G"**: **E**vent, **S**tate, **T**ransition, **A**ction, **G**uard. These five are the cornerstones of every KTU Module 2 question on this topic.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCNT604 - OBJECT ORIENTED MODELING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Operations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Object-Oriented Modeling and Design (OOMD)** under the KTU 2024 Scheme, an **Operation** is formally defined as a **service or function that an object (or class) can be requested to execute**, representing the *behavioral* facet of a class in the object model. Operations are declared in a class and are inherited by all instances (objects) of that class.

According to the **Object Modeling Technique (OMT)** notation popularized by Rumbaugh, Blaha, Premerlani, Eddy and Lorensen, every operation in a class is characterized by its:

- **Name** — a unique identifier within the class scope.
- **Signature** — the formal parameter list and return type.
- **Visibility** — the access modifier (public `+`, private `-`, protected `#`, package `~`).
- **Polymorphic behavior** — whether the operation may be overridden in subclasses.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition:** *"An operation is a procedure or transformation that an object performs or that is performed on an object. It is a specification of behavior common to all instances of a class."* — This exact phrasing is frequently asked in KTU 2-mark and 3-mark questions.

In the **UML Class Diagram notation**, operations occupy the **third compartment** of the class rectangle, immediately below attributes. The full syntax is:

```
visibility name (parameter-list) : return-type {property-string}
```

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **Banking System** as a real-world analogy. When you (an *object* of type *Customer*) approach a bank's front desk, you can only ask the bank to perform certain well-defined *services*: **deposit money, withdraw money, check balance, transfer funds, open an account, close an account**. You cannot ask the bank to "fly" or "sing" — those are not legitimate services.

In this analogy:
- The **Bank** is the **Class**.
- The **specific branch** you walk into is the **Object (instance)**.
- The list of services displayed at the counter (deposit, withdraw, etc.) are the **Operations**.
- The **actual bank teller performing the service** is the **Method** (the implementation behind the operation).

> [!NOTE]
> **Operations ≠ Methods.** A *method* is the **implementation (body of code)** of an operation. An *operation* is the **declaration/signature** of behavior. A single operation can be implemented by many different methods (one in each subclass) — this is the foundation of **polymorphism**.

## 1.3 Why Operations Are Central to OOMD

Operations transform the object model from a *passive data structure* into a *living, computational system*. They:

1. Encapsulate behavior alongside data (attributes) inside the class.
2. Enforce the **encapsulation principle** by controlling how an object's state is mutated.
3. Provide the **public interface** through which objects collaborate (via messages).
4. Enable **polymorphism** when subclasses override inherited operations.

> [!TIP]
> In the KTU 2024 syllabus for PBCNT604, Module 2 emphasizes how operations, when combined with attributes, fully define the **state-behavior contract** of an object in the object model.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of an Operation — Five Logical Components

The formal UML/OMT specification decomposes every operation into **five distinct parts**, each carrying semantic weight in the object model:

1. **Operation Name** — A descriptive identifier that must be unique within the declaring class. Follows identifier rules of the implementation language (Java, C++, Python).
2. **Visibility Modifier** — Controls access scope:
   - `+` **Public** — accessible to any class.
   - `-` **Private** — accessible only within the defining class.
   - `#` **Protected** — accessible within the class and its subclasses.
   - `~` **Package** — accessible within the same package (UML-specific).
3. **Parameter List** — Ordered tuple of typed inputs enclosed in parentheses. Each parameter is written as `name : Type`, optionally followed by a direction tag: `in`, `out`, `inout`.
4. **Return Type** — The data type of the value produced by the operation. Written after a colon following the parameter list. May be `void` (no return value).
5. **Property String** — Curly-brace enclosed metadata describing algorithmic properties: `{query}`, `{abstract}`, `{leaf}`, `{concrete}`, `{isQuery}`.

## 2.2 Classification of Operations (KTU Frequently Tested)

Operations are taxonomically classified along several orthogonal axes:

### A. By Purpose / Functional Role

| Category | Description | Example |
|----------|-------------|---------|
| **Constructor** | Initializes a newly created object | `+Account(name:String, bal:Real)` |
| **Destructor** | Releases resources held by the object before destruction | `-~Account()` |
| **Query (Accessor)** | Reads object state without mutating it (read-only) | `+getBalance(): Real` |
| **Modifier (Mutator)** | Alters the internal state of the object | `+deposit(amount:Real): void` |
| **Iterator** | Traverses a collection of objects in the class | `+next(): Item` |
| **Factory Method** | Creates and returns instances of the class (or related classes) | `+createSavingsAccount(): Account` |

### B. By Polymorphic Behavior

- **Abstract Operation** — declared in a superclass without implementation; *must* be overridden.
- **Concrete Operation** — fully implemented in the declaring class.
- **Overridden Operation** — re-declared in a subclass with same signature but different behavior.
- **Overloaded Operation** — same name, different parameter list within the same class.

### C. By Object Reference Direction

- **Class-Scope Operation (Static Operation)** — underlined in UML; invoked on the class itself, not on an instance.
- **Instance Operation** — the default; requires an object instance to invoke.

## 2.3 Notation in the Object Model Diagram

The full UML/OMT notation for an operation in a class box is:

```
visibility name ( parameter : Type = defaultValue , ... ) : ReturnType { properties }
```

**Comprehensive Example** — A `BankAccount` class with its operations:

```
+----------------------------+
|        BankAccount         |
+----------------------------+
| -accountNumber : String    |
| -balance       : Real      |
| -owner         : Customer  |
+----------------------------+
| +BankAccount(num:String)   |
| +deposit(amount:Real):Bool |
| +withdraw(amount:Real):Bool|
| +getBalance():Real {query} |
| +transfer(to:BankAccount,  |
|           amount:Real):Bool|
| +~getTotalAccounts():Int   |
+----------------------------+
```

The underline beneath `+~getTotalAccounts():Int` indicates it is a **class-scope (static) operation**.

## 2.4 Operations and Messages — The Collaboration Engine

In OOMD, objects **collaborate** by sending **messages** to one another. A message is essentially a *request* to invoke an operation on the target object. The triplet is:

> *sender object → message → receiver object → operation execution → reply (optional)*

This message-passing mechanism is the **dynamic runtime counterpart** of statically declared operations.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet — Operation Modeling

| Concept | UML Notation | Semantics | KTU Weightage |
|---------|--------------|-----------|----------------|
| Operation Declaration | `+op(p:T):R` | Public operation returning R | **High** |
| Static Operation | `+op():R` (underlined) | Class-scope operation | **High** |
| Abstract Operation | *name* in *italics* or `{abstract}` | No implementation in this class | **High** |
| Query Operation | `{query}` suffix | Read-only, no side effects | Medium |
| Parameter Direction | `in/out/inout` | Specifies data flow direction | Medium |
| Polymorphic Operation | Same signature in subclass | Late binding, dynamic dispatch | **High** |
| Operation Signature | `name(parameter types)` | Unique identifier for overloading | **High** |
| Constraint on Operation | `{postcondition: ...}` | OCL-style state invariant | Low |
| Visibility Order | `+, #, -, ~` | Public → Protected → Private → Package | Medium |
| Method vs Operation | Operation = signature, Method = body | KTU examiners love this distinction | **High** |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Operations form the **behavioral backbone** of every major software engineering paradigm:

- **Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP)** — Business rules are encoded as operations on `Order`, `Invoice`, `Inventory` classes.
- **Embedded Systems** — Sensor reading operations in `IoTDevice` classes.
- **Compilers** — `Tokenizer`, `Parser`, `CodeGenerator` classes with operations like `getNextToken()`, `parseExpression()`.
- **Web Frameworks** — `Controller` operations mapped to HTTP endpoints (REST `GET`, `POST`, `PUT`, `DELETE`).
- **AI/ML Pipelines** — `Model` class with operations `train()`, `predict()`, `evaluate()`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The *quality* of an object's interface is judged by the **cohesion** of its operations — a well-designed class has operations that are tightly related to its responsibility (the **Single Responsibility Principle**).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Operation Signature Canonical Form

Starting from first principles of OMT, an operation is a *mathematical function* over the object's state. We can formalize the canonical signature as follows.

### Step 1 — State Space Definition

Let an object $o$ of class $C$ possess a state vector $\mathbf{s}_o$ at time $t$:

$$\mathbf{s}_o(t) = \{a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_n\}$$

where each $a_i$ is the value of attribute $i$ in $C$.

### Step 2 — Operation as a State Transition

An operation $op$ invoked on $o$ at time $t$ produces a new state at time $t+\Delta t$ and optionally a return value:

$$op : \mathbf{s}_o(t) \times \mathbf{params} \rightarrow \mathbf{s}_o(t+\Delta t) \times \mathbf{return}$$

The textual reasoning is: *the operation consumes the current state and the input parameters, and produces a new state plus an optional reply*.

### Step 3 — Decomposition into Signature Parts

The **operation signature** is the syntactic projection of this function:

$$\Sigma(op) = \big\langle \text{visibility},\ \text{name},\ \big\{ (p_i : T_i,\ \text{dir}_i) \big\}_{i=1}^{k},\ R,\ \text{properties} \big\rangle$$

where:
- $p_i$ is the $i$-th parameter name.
- $T_i$ is the $i$-th parameter type.
- $\text{dir}_i \in \{\text{in},\ \text{out},\ \text{inout}\}$ is the direction.
- $R$ is the return type.
- `properties` describes metadata such as `{query}`, `{abstract}`, etc.

### Step 4 — Two Operations Are Equal If and Only If Signatures Match

This is the **canonical equivalence relation** that governs overloading and overriding:

$$op_1 \equiv op_2 \iff \big(\text{name}_1 = \text{name}_2\big) \wedge \big(\{T_1, \ldots, T_k\}_1 = \{T_1, \ldots, T_k\}_2\big) \wedge (R_1 = R_2)$$

Note that *return type alone does not* distinguish overloads in languages like Java, but *parameter types* are the **decisive element**.

## 3.2 Polymorphism Through Operation Overriding — Analytical Derivation

Let $C$ be a superclass and $C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_n$ be its subclasses. Suppose $C$ declares an abstract operation $op$:

$$op : C \rightarrow R \quad \text{(abstract)}$$

Each subclass $C_i$ provides its own concrete implementation:

$$op_i : C_i \rightarrow R \quad \text{(concrete, overrides)}$$

The **polymorphic dispatch** at runtime is governed by the rule:

$$\text{dispatch}(op, o) = op_i \quad \text{where} \quad o \in C_i$$

**Worked Example** — Geometry hierarchy:

```
              Shape
               |
    +----------+----------+
    |          |          |
 Circle    Rectangle   Triangle
```

Define an operation `area()` in `Shape` (abstract) and override it in each subclass. The signature derivation:

| Class | Operation | Signature | Implementation |
|-------|-----------|-----------|----------------|
| `Shape` | `+area(): Real` | `Σ = {+, area, (), Real, {abstract}}` | Undefined |
| `Circle` | `+area(): Real` | `Σ = {+, area, (), Real, {concrete}}` | $\pi \cdot r^2$ |
| `Rectangle` | `+area(): Real` | `Σ = {+, area, (), Real, {concrete}}` | $w \cdot h$ |
| `Triangle` | `+area(): Real` | `Σ = {+, area, (), Real, {concrete}}` | $\frac{1}{2} \cdot b \cdot h$ |

The **operation `area()` is the polymorphic contract**; the **methods** $\pi r^2$, $wh$, and $\frac{1}{2}bh$ are the **concrete realizations**.

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Construction of an Operation-Rich Class

Following the **GRASP (General Responsibility Assignment Software Patterns)** methodology, the derivation of operations on a class proceeds in five steps:

1. **Identify the class's responsibility** — what does the class *know* and *do*?
2. **Enumerate candidate operations** — verbs that describe responsibilities.
3. **Classify each operation** — constructor, query, modifier, etc.
4. **Assign visibility** — public for the external interface, private for internal helpers.
5. **Refine the signature** — assign types to parameters and return value; add property strings.

**Worked Analytical Walk-through** — Building a `Library` class:

- **Step 1** — Responsibility: *manage a catalog of books and member borrowings*.
- **Step 2** — Candidate operations: `addBook`, `removeBook`, `searchByTitle`, `issueBookToMember`, `returnBook`, `getAvailableCopies`.
- **Step 3** — Classification:
  - `addBook`, `removeBook` → Modifiers
  - `searchByTitle`, `getAvailableCopies` → Queries
  - `issueBookToMember`, `returnBook` → Modifiers with complex pre/post-conditions
- **Step 4** — Visibility: All public except internal helper `-validateMemberStatus()`.
- **Step 5** — Final signatures:
  - `+addBook(book:Book): void`
  - `-removeBook(isbn:String): Bool`
  - `+searchByTitle(title:String): List<Book> {query}`
  - `+issueBookToMember(isbn:String, memberId:Int): Bool`
  - `+returnBook(isbn:String, memberId:Int): Bool`
  - `+getAvailableCopies(isbn:String): Int {query}`
  - `-validateMemberStatus(memberId:Int): Bool`

## 3.4 Mathematical Distinction: Operation vs Method

This is a **highly tested KTU 2024 topic** worth full marks if derived correctly.

| Aspect | Operation | Method |
|--------|-----------|--------|
| Definition | The *declaration* of behavior | The *implementation* of an operation |
| What it specifies | Signature only (name, parameters, return type) | Executable code (the algorithm) |
| Where it exists | Class definition, in the model | Object instance, at runtime |
| One-to-many? | One operation may have many methods | One method implements exactly one operation |
| Polymorphism | The operation is the polymorphic contract | The methods are the polymorphic realizations |

Formal statement: Let $\mathcal{O}$ be the set of operations on a class and $\mathcal{M}$ the set of methods implementing them. In a class hierarchy with $n$ subclasses:

$$|\mathcal{M}| \geq |\mathcal{O}|$$

and in the presence of polymorphism,

$$|\mathcal{M}| = |\mathcal{O}| \times (1 + n_{\text{overriding}})$$

where $n_{\text{overriding}}$ is the number of subclasses that override the operation.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Operation Anatomy Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    opBlock["Operation Block"]
    opBlock --> vis["Visibility Modifier"]
    opBlock --> name["Operation Name"]
    opBlock --> params["Parameter List"]
    opBlock --> ret["Return Type"]
    opBlock --> props["Property String"]
    vis --> visPub["+ Public"]
    vis --> visPro["# Protected"]
    vis --> visPri["− Private"]
    vis --> visPkg["~ Package"]
    params --> pName["name : Type"]
    params --> pDir["Direction: in / out / inout"]
    params --> pDef["Default Value"]
    ret --> rType["Primitive / Class / void"]
    props --> pQry["query"]
    props --> pAbs["abstract"]
    props --> pLeaf["leaf"]
    props --> pStat["static / underlined"]
```

## 4.2 Operation Classification Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    root["Operations on a Class"]
    root --> byPurpose["By Purpose"]
    root --> byPoly["By Polymorphism"]
    root --> byScope["By Scope"]
    byPurpose --> ctor["Constructor"]
    byPurpose --> dtor["Destructor"]
    byPurpose --> qry["Query Accessor"]
    byPurpose --> mut["Modifier Mutator"]
    byPurpose --> iter["Iterator"]
    byPurpose --> fact["Factory Method"]
    byPoly --> abs["Abstract Operation"]
    byPoly --> con["Concrete Operation"]
    byPoly --> ovr["Overridden Operation"]
    byPoly --> oload["Overloaded Operation"]
    byScope --> instOp["Instance Operation"]
    byScope --> statOp["Class Scope Static Operation"]
```

## 4.3 Message-Passing and Operation Invocation Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Sender as Sender Object A
    participant Receiver as Receiver Object B
    participant Operation as Operation Declared in Class B
    participant Method as Method Implementing Operation
    Sender->>Receiver: send message opName arg1 arg2
    Receiver->>Operation: look up operation in class
    Operation->>Method: bind and dispatch to method
    Method->>Method: execute body
    Method-->>Receiver: return value if any
    Receiver-->>Sender: optional reply
```

## 4.4 Polymorphic Operation Dispatch Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    shape["Class Shape"]
    shape --> absOp["Operation area abstract"]
    absOp --> dispatch{"Runtime Dispatch on Actual Type"}
    dispatch --> circ["Circle area equals pi r squared"]
    dispatch --> rect["Rectangle area equals w times h"]
    dispatch --> tri["Triangle area equals half b h"]
    circ --> objC["object is type Circle"]
    rect --> objR["object is type Rectangle"]
    tri --> objT["object is type Triangle"]
```

## 4.5 Class Diagram with Operations — Library Management System

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Book {
        -title : String
        -isbn : String
        -author : String
        -copies : int
        +Book(title:String, isbn:String)
        +getTitle() : String
        +getIsbn() : String
        +isAvailable() : bool
        +decrementCopies() : void
        +incrementCopies() : void
    }
    class Member {
        -memberId : int
        -name : String
        -borrowedBooks : List~Book~
        +Member(id:int, name:String)
        +borrowBook(b:Book) : bool
        +returnBook(b:Book) : bool
        +getBorrowedCount() : int
    }
    class Library {
        -catalog : Map~String,Book~
        -members : Map~int,Member~
        +addBook(b:Book) : void
        +removeBook(isbn:String) : bool
        +searchByTitle(title:String) : List~Book~
        +issueBook(isbn:String, id:int) : bool
        +returnBook(isbn:String, id:int) : bool
        +getAvailableCopies(isbn:String) : int
    }
    Library "1" --> "*" Book : manages
    Library "1" --> "*" Member : registers
    Member "1" --> "*" Book : borrows
```

## 4.6 Operation vs Method — Conceptual Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classBox["Class Definition"]
    classBox --> opDecl["Operation Declaration"]
    classBox --> mthImpl1["Method Implementation in Class"]
    classBox --> mthImpl2["Overridden Method in Subclass A"]
    classBox --> mthImpl3["Overridden Method in Subclass B"]
    opDecl -. same signature .-> mthImpl1
    opDecl -. same signature .-> mthImpl2
    opDecl -. same signature .-> mthImpl3
    mthImpl1 --> note1["The interface contract"]
    mthImpl2 --> note2["The polymorphic variant"]
    mthImpl3 --> note3["Another polymorphic variant"]
```
