- **Producer-Consumer** uses `empty`, `full`, and `mutex` semaphores with the invariant $0 \le \text{count} \le B$.
- **Amdahl's Law**: $S = \frac{1}{(1-A) + \frac{A}{N}}$, with $S_{\max} = \frac{1}{1-A}$ as $N \to \infty$.
- **Speedup** $S = \frac{T_s}{T_p}$ and **Efficiency** $E = \frac{S}{N}$.
- **Race condition** in banking: lost update due to non-atomic read-modify-write.
- **UML 2.5 notation** for active class: bold double-lined rectangle with `<<active>>` stereotype.
- **Design rule**: Start sequential, add concurrency only where external events demand it.
- **Message passing** is preferred over shared state for safe concurrency.
- **Synchronization primitives**: mutex (lock), semaphore (counter), monitor (encapsulated lock + condition variables), barrier (rendezvous point).
- **Keywords for KTU answer writing**: thread of control, active/passive, Coffman, Amdahl, semaphore, monitor, mutual exclusion, atomic, deadlock, starvation, race condition, liveness.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advanced Dynamic Modeling Concepts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCNT604 - OBJECT ORIENTED MODELING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module - 2
> - **Topic:** Advanced Dynamic Modeling Concepts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Advanced Dynamic Modeling Concepts** in Object-Oriented Analysis and Design (OOAD) refer to the sophisticated UML-based diagrammatic techniques used to capture the **time-dependent behavioral and control-flow aspects** of a software system that are *not* fully expressed by static class/sequence diagrams. The three principal advanced dynamic modeling artifacts in the KTU 2024 syllabus are:

1. **State Machine Diagrams** — model the *lifecycle behavior* of a single object (or subsystem) by enumerating its states, the events that trigger transitions, and the actions performed on those transitions.
2. **Activity Diagrams** — model the *workflow / procedural logic* of an operation, use case, or business process; they emphasize conditional, parallel, and iterative control flow.
3. **Event & Signal Modeling** — capture *asynchronous* occurrences that cross object boundaries (signals), exception paths, and time-based events.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module-2 Focus:** The university expects students to (a) construct a **State Chart (State Machine) Diagram** for a representative object, (b) use **Activity Diagrams** with **concurrent / parallel / swim-lane** notation, and (c) reason about **signals, exceptions, and deferred events** as the bridge between interaction and state-based views.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Vending Machine"**
>
> Imagine a **vending machine**. The machine is an *object* with a *state* (Idle, Accepting Coins, Dispensing Item, Out-of-Stock). The events (coin inserted, button pressed, item dropped) trigger transitions between these states. Some actions (lighting the display, returning change) occur automatically upon entering or leaving a state.
>
> - The **state machine diagram** is the vending machine's *brain* — it remembers what state it is in and decides what to do next.
> - The **activity diagram** is the *recipe* used to actually dispense a can of soda (decision: enough money? → pour drink → deliver can → deduct inventory).
> - The **signal** is the small *beep* the machine sends to the inventory server when stock falls below threshold — an asynchronous message that crosses object boundaries.
>
> Dynamic modeling is essentially the *time-aware extension* of the static object model: it answers the question **"What does this object do, and when does it do it?"**

### 1.3 Key Terminology Glossary

| Term | Meaning (KTU Standard) |
|---|---|
| **State** | A condition during the life of an object in which it satisfies some condition, performs an activity, or waits for an event. |
| **Transition** | A relationship between two states indicating that an object in the first state will perform certain actions and enter the second state when a specified event occurs and the guard condition is true. |
| **Event** | A noteworthy occurrence (signal call, time passage, state change) that may trigger a transition. |
| **Action** | An executable, atomic computation associated with a state or transition. |
| **Guard Condition** | A boolean expression evaluated *before* a transition fires; if false, the transition does not occur. |
| **Substate** | A state nested inside another state; allows hierarchical (composite) state modeling. |
| **History State (H)** | A pseudo-state that remembers the *last active* substate of a containing composite state. |
| **Fork / Join** | Activity-diagram constructs that model *concurrent* flows. |
| **Swimlane** | A partition in an activity diagram that allocates activities to organizational units or classes. |
| **Signal** | An asynchronous, named event instance sent from one object to another. |
| **Exception** | A special signal indicating abnormal termination of an operation. |
| **Deferred Event** | An event that the object postpones until it is in a state that can handle it. |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Advanced Dynamic Modeling

#### Pillar 1 — State Machine Diagrams (Statecharts)

A state machine diagram models the **discrete** event-driven behavior of *one* classifier (object, use case, or subsystem). It is described by the 5-tuple:

$$M = \langle S, \, \Sigma, \, \delta, \, s_0, \, F \rangle$$

where:
- $S$ = finite, non-empty set of **states**.
- $\Sigma$ = finite set of **events** (the alphabet).
- $\delta : S \times \Sigma \rightarrow S$ = **transition function** (extended to handle guards, actions, and composite states).
- $s_0 \in S$ = **initial state** (start node).
- $F \subseteq S$ = set of **final states** (may be empty).

**Why this matters in KTU exams:** Even though a state machine is a graph, examiners award marks for the **notation** (rounded rectangles for states, arrow for transition, event[guard]/action), and for hierarchical features (substates, history).

##### Hierarchical & Concurrent States

A composite state may contain:
- **Sequential (Disjoint) substates** — only one substate is active at a time.
- **Concurrent (Parallel) substates** — multiple substates are simultaneously active, separated by **dashed** boundaries.

A **History State** (denoted by a circle containing the letter **H**) is a *pseudo-state* whose outgoing transition targets the *most recently visited* substate of the enclosing composite state.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Rule:** When an object re-enters a composite state and you want it to resume *exactly where it left off*, use the **deep-history** notation **H***. Use plain **H** for *shallow* history (forgets substates of nested composites).

##### Entry, Exit, and Internal Activities (Do-Activity)

Modern UML state machines allow state-owned behaviors:
- **entry / action** — performed unconditionally when the state is entered.
- **exit / action** — performed unconditionally when the state is left.
- **do / activity** — runs while the object is in the state (may be interrupted by an event).

This is formalized as:

$$A_{state} = (E_{entry}, \, A_{do}, \, E_{exit})$$

A state may be *atomic* (no internal structure) or *composite* (contains substates).

#### Pillar 2 — Activity Diagrams

An activity diagram is essentially a **Turing-complete** flowchart enriched with object-flow, swimlanes, and signal tokens. The KTU syllabus focuses on:

| Notation | UML Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Initial Node | Filled black circle | Start of the activity. |
| Activity / Action | Rounded rectangle | Atomic unit of work. |
| Decision | Diamond | Branches based on guard conditions. |
| Merge | Diamond | Re-converges multiple alternative paths. |
| Fork | Thick black bar | Splits one flow into concurrent flows. |
| Join | Thick black bar | Synchronizes concurrent flows. |
| Swimlane | Rectangular partition | Responsibility zone (class, role, dept). |
| Object Node | Rectangle $\vert$ name : type | Flow of data objects between actions. |
| Signal Send | Pentagonal «send» | Broadcasts an asynchronous signal. |
| Signal Receive | Pentagonal «receive» | Waits for a named signal. |
| Final Node | Bull's-eye | End of the activity. |

**Concurrency rule (Karp-Miller / UML semantics):** A *Join* node delays the outgoing flow until **all** incoming tokens have arrived. The synchronization condition is:

$$ \text{fire}_{join} \iff \bigwedge_{i=1}^{n} \text{token}_{in,i} = 1 $$

For a *Fork* node, an incoming token generates exactly one token on every outgoing edge:

$$ \text{tokens}_{out} = n \quad \text{when} \quad \text{tokens}_{in} = 1 $$

where $n$ is the number of outgoing edges of the fork.

#### Pillar 3 — Event & Signal Modeling

A **signal** is a named asynchronous stimulus:

$$ \text{Signal} = \langle \text{name}, \, \vec{attribute}, \, \text{sender}, \, \text{receiver} \rangle $$

- Signals can be **sent** (`«send»` notation) and **received** (`«receive»` notation) between objects in sequence, communication, and activity diagrams.
- A **time event** is denoted `after 5 seconds` and triggers a transition after a time interval.
- A **change event** is denoted `when (balance < 0)` and triggers when a condition becomes true.
- **Exception handling** in activity diagrams uses a lightning-bolt arrow from the protected action to an `«exceptionHandler»` region.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The three (3) marks allotted to "Define event/signal" questions in past university papers almost always expect you to list **(i) caller, (ii) callee, (iii) asynchronous nature, (iv) parameters**. Missing any one loses a mark.

### 2.2 KTU Formula / Notation Cheat-Sheet

| Concept | Notation | Operational Rule |
|---|---|---|
| Transition | `event [guard] / action` | Fires iff event occurs AND guard = true; then action runs. |
| State-owned entry | `entry / op()` | Runs on every entry. |
| State-owned exit | `exit / op()` | Runs on every exit. |
| State-owned do | `do / op()` | Runs while in the state. |
| Internal transition | `event / action` *inside* state | No state exit, no state re-entry. |
| Self-transition | `event / action` returning to same state | Exit + Entry actions both fire. |
| Deferred event | `event / defer` | Event queued until handler-state reached. |
| History (shallow) | `H` | Resumes last direct substate. |
| History (deep) | `H*` | Resumes last substate at any nesting depth. |
| Fork token replication | `1 in → n out` | Forks into $n$ parallel branches. |
| Join synchronization | `n in → 1 out` | Joins fire only when all $n$ inputs are present. |
| Time event | `after duration` | Triggers after elapsed time. |
| Change event | `when (cond)` | Triggers when condition becomes true. |
| Send signal | `«send» → sig(args)` | Asynchronous outbound signal. |
| Receive signal | `«receive» sig(args)` | Waits for matching signal. |

### 2.3 Why This Matters in Real Engineering

- **Embedded Systems:** Statecharts drive finite-state machine (FSM) code generation for microcontroller firmware (e.g., traffic-light controllers, anti-lock braking systems).
- **Workflow Engines (BPMN-derived):** Activity diagrams with swimlanes are the direct input to business-process engines in banking, insurance, and ERP systems.
- **Protocol Engineering:** TCP, SIP, and HTTP servers are designed as hierarchical state machines.
- **Game Development:** Game-loop and character-AI logic are prototyped as state machines plus activity flows.
- **Safety-Critical Software (DO-178C, IEC 61508):** Hierarchical state machines (like Stateflow in MATLAB/Simulink) are required to formally verify control logic in avionics and railway signalling.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Dynamic Diagram

| Modeling Goal | Recommended Diagram | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Show the *states* of a single object over time | **State Machine Diagram** | Captures discrete event-driven transitions. |
| Show *workflow / business process* with branches and parallelism | **Activity Diagram** | Models procedural/parallel control flow. |
| Show *who does what* across organizational units | **Activity Diagram with Swimlanes** | Partitions activities by responsibility. |
| Show *exception / alternate* paths of an operation | **Activity Diagram + «exception»** | Branches off the main path. |
| Show *lifeline-based message exchange* | **Sequence / Communication Diagram** | Focuses on inter-object messages. |
| Show *asynchronous, one-shot* notifications | **Signal in Activity/State diagram** | Models named asynchronous events. |
| Capture *timeouts* and *delays* | **Time events** in state machine | `after n seconds` triggers transitions. |

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Construction Algorithm for a State Machine Diagram

Given a textual problem statement, follow this exhaustive 6-step procedure (the same procedure is acceptable as an answer to a 14-mark KTU question).

**Step 1 — Identify the Object (Subject of the State Machine).**
Pick *one* class whose lifecycle is non-trivial. Common KTU-classroom choices: *Order*, *ATM Session*, *Lift*, *Phone Call*, *Vending Machine*, *TCP Connection*.

**Step 2 — Enumerate the States.**
Ask: "What discrete conditions can this object be in?" Each condition = one state.
Example for *Phone Call*: Idle → Dialing → Ringing → Connected → Disconnected.

**Step 3 — Identify the Events.**
Events are *noteworthy external stimuli*. Categorize:
- **Call events** (signal arrivals): `dialed(number)`, `answer()`, `hangup()`.
- **Time events:** `after 30 seconds` (no answer → busy).
- **Change events:** `when (credit < 0)`.

**Step 4 — Identify the Transitions.**
For every (state, event) pair, decide:
- Target state $s'$,
- Guard condition $g$,
- Action $a$.

This constructs the transition function $\delta(s, e)$ such that:

$$
\delta(s, e) = \begin{cases}
s' & \text{if guard } g = \text{true} \\
\text{no transition} & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 5 — Attach Entry, Exit, and Do-Actions.**
Refine each state with internal behavior. For example, the *Ringing* state may have `entry / startTone()` and `exit / stopTone()`.

**Step 6 — Add Initial, Final, History Pseudo-States.**
Place a filled circle (initial), a bull's-eye (final), and (if the state is composite) a `H` or `H*` to denote history. Validate that every state is reachable from the initial state.

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Construction Algorithm for an Activity Diagram

**Step 1 — Identify the Use Case / Operation.**
Choose the scenario (e.g., "Process Online Order").

**Step 2 — Identify the Activities.**
List each step as an action (verb-object form, e.g., *Verify Payment*).

**Step 3 — Determine the Control Flow.**
Use:
- **Decision/Merge** diamonds for if/else.
- **Fork/Join** bars for parallel sub-flows.
- **Swimlanes** if responsibility crosses organizational or class boundaries.

**Step 4 — Identify the Object Flows.**
For each action that produces or consumes an artifact, attach an **object node** (`{order, receipt, …}`) on the connecting edge.

**Step 5 — Identify Signals and Exceptions.**
For asynchronous events, use the pentagonal `«send»`/`«receive»` notation. For exceptional conditions (e.g., payment failed), use the `«exceptionHandler»` region.

**Step 6 — Validate Reachability and Termination.**
Every path from the initial node must terminate at a final node (or, for non-terminating business processes, must contain an explicit infinite-loop construct, which UML allows via a back-edge).

### 3.4 Worked Analytical Derivation — Verifying a Join Synchronization

A typical KTU numerical/analytical sub-question asks: *"A fork-join pair processes $n$ parallel branches each taking $t_i$ time units. Compute (a) the total elapsed time and (b) the time saved by parallel execution."*

**Derivation:**

Let the parallel activities be $A_1, A_2, \dots, A_n$ with execution times $t_1, t_2, \dots, t_n$.

After the **fork node** at time $T_0$, all $n$ activities begin concurrently. Each activity $A_i$ finishes at absolute time $T_0 + t_i$.

The **join node** fires only when **all** incoming tokens have arrived. Therefore, the join fires at the *latest* finishing activity:

$$
T_{join} = T_0 + \max_{1 \le i \le n} (t_i)
$$

Sequential execution of the same $n$ activities would have taken:

$$
T_{seq} = T_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i
$$

Hence the **time saved** by parallel execution is:

$$
\Delta T = T_{seq} - T_{join} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i \; - \; \max_{1 \le i \le n} (t_i)
$$

The **speed-up factor** $S$ is:

$$
S = \frac{T_{seq}}{T_{join}} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i}{\max_{1 \le i \le n} (t_i)}
$$

**Numerical example (KTU-style):** Let $n = 3$, $t_1 = 5\,\text{s}$, $t_2 = 8\,\text{s}$, $t_3 = 3\,\text{s}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{join} &= \max(5,\, 8,\, 3) = 8 \,\text{s} \\
T_{seq}  &= 5 + 8 + 3 = 16 \,\text{s} \\
\Delta T  &= 16 - 8 = 8 \,\text{s} \\
S        &= \frac{16}{8} = 2.0
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Parallelism always gives a speed-up bounded by $1 \le S \le n$. The lower bound occurs when all $t_i$ are equal; the upper bound (linear) is approached when one branch is *much* longer than the others (the so-called "longest pole" or "critical path" problem in scheduling theory).

### 3.5 Analytical Proof — State Reachability Theorem

> [!NOTE]
> **Theorem (Reachability of States in a UML State Machine).** A state $s_k \in S$ is *reachable* from the initial state $s_0$ if and only if there exists a finite sequence of events $e_1, e_2, \dots, e_m \in \Sigma$ such that
> $$\delta(\delta(\dots\delta(\delta(s_0, e_1), e_2)\dots), e_m) = s_k$$
> where each intermediate guard evaluates to `true`.

**Proof Sketch (outline expected in 14-mark answers):**

1. (*Forward* $\Rightarrow$) By construction of the state machine, every transition is a direct edge $s \xrightarrow{e/g} s'$. Applying $\delta$ along the path gives the desired sequence.
2. (*Backward* $\Leftarrow$) If a sequence of events leads to $s_k$, then by definition the final application of $\delta$ yields $s_k$, hence $s_k$ is reachable.

This is the formal statement underlying the *model-checking* step: an unreachable state is *dead code* and may be removed.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 UML State Machine Diagram — "Order Object Lifecycle" (Composite + History)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Created

    state "Order" as orderState {
        [*] --> Pending
        Pending --> Confirmed : paymentReceived [amount > 0]
        Pending --> Cancelled : timeout / refund()
        Confirmed --> Shipped : dispatchEvent / notifyWarehouse()
        Shipped --> Delivered : deliveryConfirm

        state "Dispute" as dispute {
            [*] --> Reviewing
            Reviewing --> Resolved : settlement()
        }

        Confirmed --> dispute : customerComplaint
        dispute --> Cancelled : claimApproved / refund()
    }

    Cancelled --> [*]
    Delivered --> [*]
    Resolved --> [*]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading aid for the diagram above:** The outer box `Order` is a *composite state*. `Pending`, `Confirmed`, `Shipped`, `Delivered`, `Cancelled` are *direct substates*. The inner box `Dispute` is a *nested composite state* demonstrating hierarchy. The square brackets `[amount > 0]` denote *guard conditions*. The slash `/` separates the event from the *action* that runs on transition.

### 4.2 UML Activity Diagram — "Online Order Processing" (with Swimlanes, Fork/Join, and Exception)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start(( )) --> Cust[Customer fills cart]
    Cust --> CustCheck{cart valid?}
    CustCheck -- No --> CustFix[Display errors]
    CustFix --> Cust
    CustCheck -- Yes --> Pay[Fork: payment + inventory check]

    subgraph PaymentLane[" "]
        direction TB
        Verify[Verify card]
        Charge[Charge card]
        Verify --> Charge
    end

    subgraph InventoryLane[" "]
        direction TB
        Stock[Check stock]
        Reserve[Reserve items]
        Stock --> Reserve
    end

    Pay --> PaymentLane
    Pay --> InventoryLane

    PaymentLane --> Join1
    InventoryLane --> Join1
    Join1{{Join}} --> Result{both ok?}

    Result -- Yes --> Ship[Schedule shipment]
    Result -- No --> Except[«exceptionHandler» Cancel and notify]
    Except --> End2((( )))
    Ship --> End1((( )))

    classDef lane fill:#e8f4ff,stroke:#3070b0
    class PaymentLane,InventoryLane lane
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation legend:**
> - `( )` is the *initial node* (filled black circle in UML).
> - `((( )))` denotes the *final node* (bull's-eye in UML).
> - `{{Join}}` is a *join bar* (thick black bar in UML).
> - `«exceptionHandler»` is the *exception region* annotation.
> - Swimlanes are visualized here as subgraphs for clarity, since standard Mermaid does not render UML swimlanes natively.

### 4.3 Signal and Event Flow — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Source["Sender Object (ATM)"]
        S1[Operation ends] --> S2["«send» transactionComplete(amount)"]
    end

    subgraph Bus["Event Channel / Signal Bus"]
        S2 --> Q[(Signal Queue)]
    end

    subgraph Sink["Receiver Object (Account)"]
        Q --> R1["«receive» transactionComplete"]
        R1 --> R2[Update balance]
        R2 --> R3[State transition check]
        R3 --> R4[Trigger next state]
    end

    classDef src fill:#fff4e6,stroke:#cc7a00
    classDef bus fill:#f0f0f0,stroke:#666
    classDef snk fill:#e6f7e6,stroke:#2e7d32
    class S1,S2 src
    class Q bus
    class R1,R2,R3,R4 snk
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering takeaway:** The *signal queue* decouples sender and receiver in time — the sender does not block waiting for the receiver. This is the foundation of **asynchronous event-driven architectures** (publish-subscribe, message queues like Kafka/RabbitMQ, and reactive Java `Flow` APIs).

### 4.4 Comparison Block Diagram — State Machine vs. Activity Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Need to model object behavior over time] --> B{Is behavior event-driven AND object-centric?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Use State Machine Diagram]
    B -- No --> D{Is behavior procedural with branches / parallelism?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Use Activity Diagram]
    D -- No --> F[Use Sequence / Communication Diagram]
    C --> G1[States + Transitions + Events]
    E --> G2[Actions + Control Flow + Swimlanes]
    F --> G3[Objects + Messages + Time]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **Model answers are intentionally concise — these are 3-mark KTU questions, expected to be answered in 4-5 sentences plus a small diagram where applicable.**

#### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**
**Define a *signal* in UML. List its four essential attributes.**

**Model Answer:**
A signal in UML is an asynchronous, named event exchanged between objects. Unlike a synchronous operation call, the sender does not wait for a reply. The four essential attributes of a signal are:
1. **Name** — uniquely identifies the signal (e.g., `«signal» buttonPressed`).
2. **Attributes / Parameters** — typed data carried with the signal.
3. **Sender** — the object dispatching the signal.
4. **Receiver(s)** — one or more objects that have registered to receive the signal.

Signals bridge state machines and activity diagrams: a state transition may be triggered by `«signal»` reception, and an activity may dispatch `«send»` signals to other parts of the system.

---

#### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]**
**Explain the difference between a *Fork* node and a *Join* node in an activity diagram.**

**Model Answer:**
- A **Fork** node splits a single incoming flow into **multiple concurrent outgoing flows**. Semantically, one input token produces exactly one token on *every* outgoing edge. Fork is used to begin parallel work.
- A **Join** node synchronizes **multiple concurrent incoming flows** into one outgoing flow. The outgoing flow is enabled only when **all** incoming flows have produced a token. Join is used to merge parallel work and enforce a synchronization barrier.

In short: *Fork = "begin parallel branches"*, *Join = "wait for all branches to finish"*. Together they implement the AND-split / AND-join control-flow pattern.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module-2 Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks split into two sub-parts, typically **(a) 7 marks** for a diagram construction and **(b) 7 marks** for an analytical/reasoning follow-up. Students are expected to answer **either** Question A **or** Question B (full internal choice at the question level, not at the sub-part level).

---

#### ⭐ Question A — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model, Choice-1]**

**(a) [7 Marks]**
Draw the **state machine diagram** for the lifecycle of a *TCP Connection* object. The states must include: *Listen, Syn Sent, Syn Received, Established, Fin Wait 1, Fin Wait 2, Closing, Time Wait, Closed*. Show at least the following events: `open`, `send SYN`, `receive SYN+ACK`, `receive FIN`, `close`. Use a **composite state** to group the *Fin Wait* substates and a **history state** to allow re-entry to the last active substate.

**(b) [7 Marks]**
For the state machine constructed in part (a), identify:
1. All **guard conditions** you would attach to disambiguate simultaneous events.
2. All **deferred events** that the Established state may legitimately defer.
3. The **entry / exit actions** that should fire for the `Established` state.
4. The **transition function** $\delta$ in tabular form for the initial substate.

---

##### ✅ Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — State Machine Diagram (7 marks):**

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Listen

    state "TCPConnection" as tcp {
        [*] --> Listen
        Listen --> SynSent : open / sendSYN()
        SynSent --> SynReceived : receiveSYNplusACK [ackOk=true]
        SynSent --> Closed : timeout / abort()
        SynReceived --> Established : sendACK()
        Established --> FinWait1 : close / sendFIN()
        Established --> Closed : reset / abort()

        state "ClosingPhase" as closing {
            [*] --> FinWait1
            FinWait1 --> FinWait2 : receiveACK()
            FinWait2 --> TimeWait : receiveFIN() / startTimer(2*MSL)
            FinWait1 --> Closing : receiveFIN() [simultaneousClose]
            FinWait2 --> Closing : receiveFIN() [simultaneousClose]
            Closing --> TimeWait : receiveACK()

            H1((H))
            TimeWait --> H1 : timeout
            H1 --> FinWait1
        }

        Established --> ClosingPhase : close
    }

    TimeWait --> Closed : timer expires
    Closed --> [*]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Marker allocation (7 marks):** Correct identification of all 9 states (3 marks); correct transition arrows with events (2 marks); composite + history pseudo-states (1 mark); neat labeling and UML notation (1 mark).

**Part (b) — Analytical follow-up (7 marks):**

1. **Guard Conditions** (2 marks):
   - `[ackOk = true]` on `SynSent → SynReceived` so that duplicate or out-of-order SYN+ACK is rejected.
   - `[simultaneousClose]` on the FinWait → Closing transition to distinguish normal close from simultaneous close.
   - `[dataPending = false]` on the `Established → FinWait1` transition to ensure unsent data is flushed first.

2. **Deferred Events** (1 mark):
   - `receiveFIN()` may be deferred in `Established` until the application calls `close()`, because the peer may legitimately half-close while local data is still being transmitted.
   - `receiveSYN()` may be deferred in `Established` if a stray late SYN from a previous incarnation arrives.

3. **Entry / Exit Actions for `Established`** (2 marks):
   - `entry / enableDataTransfer()` — turns on the application read/write API.
   - `exit / disableDataTransfer()` — flushes buffers, frees socket handles.
   - Optional `do / monitorKeepAlive()` — periodically sends a keep-alive probe.

4. **Transition Function $\delta$ Tabular Form** (2 marks):

| Current State ($s$) | Event ($e$) | Guard ($g$) | Target State ($\delta(s,e)$) | Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Listen | open | — | SynSent | sendSYN() |
| SynSent | receiveSYNplusACK | ackOk = true | SynReceived | — |
| SynSent | timeout | — | Closed | abort() |
| SynReceived | (auto) | — | Established | sendACK() |
| Established | close | dataPending = false | FinWait1 | sendFIN() |
| Established | reset | — | Closed | abort() |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks:**
> 1. Using **diamond** shapes for states (states are *rounded rectangles*; diamonds are for decisions in activity diagrams).
> 2. Forgetting the **/ action** separator — writing `event(action)` is wrong; the correct form is `event / action`.
> 3. Drawing history state as a normal state — it must be the **circle-with-H** pseudo-state placed *inside* the composite state it remembers.
> 4. Confusing *shallow* (`H`) and *deep* (`H*`) history. KTU prefers **H*** for TCP re-entry.

---

#### ⭐ Question B — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model, Choice-2]**

**(a) [7 Marks]**
Construct a **UML Activity Diagram with swimlanes** for the use case *"Process Loan Application"* in a banking system. The diagram must include at least three swimlanes: *Customer*, *Loan Officer*, *Credit Bureau*. Show a **Fork/Join** pair to indicate the parallel execution of *Credit Check* and *Collateral Verification*. Include one **decision node** (loan approved?) and one **exception path** for the case "Credit Score < 600".

**(b) [7 Marks]**
For the activity diagram drawn in part (a):
1. Compute the **total elapsed time** and the **time saved** by parallel execution, given:
   - Credit Check = 4 hours, Collateral Verification = 6 hours, Documentation = 3 hours, Funds Disbursal = 1 hour (all run sequentially *except* the parallel pair).
2. Identify the **critical path** and explain why it determines the minimum end-to-end time.
3. Show how a **signal** could be used to trigger the *Customer* swimlane from the *Loan Officer* swimlane on approval.

---

##### ✅ Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Activity Diagram with Swimlanes (7 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start(( )) --> C1[Submit application]
    C1 --> L1[Receive & log application]
    L1 --> Fork{{Fork: parallel checks}}
    Fork --> B1[Pull credit score]
    Fork --> O1[Verify collateral]
    B1 --> Join{{Join}}
    O1 --> Join
    Join --> L2[Score application]
    L2 --> D{Approved?}
    D -- Yes --> L3[Generate sanction letter]
    L3 --> S1["«send» approvalNotice"]
    S1 --> C2[Receive approval]
    C2 --> C3[Accept & sign]
    C3 --> L4[Disburse funds]
    L4 --> End1((( )))
    D -- No --> E1["«exception» creditScore < 600"]
    E1 --> L5[Send rejection email]
    L5 --> C4[Receive rejection]
    C4 --> End2((( )))
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Marker allocation (7 marks):** Three swimlanes clearly separated (2 marks); fork/join pair correctly placed (1 mark); decision with both branches (1 mark); exception path with `«exception»` notation (1 mark); clean UML arrow/notation (2 marks).

**Part (b) — Analytical follow-up (7 marks):**

1. **Time Calculation (3 marks):**

The sequential baseline is:

$$
T_{seq} = 4 + 6 + 3 + 1 = 14 \,\text{hours}
$$

The parallel execution replaces the 4 h + 6 h pair by a single $\max(4, 6) = 6 \,\text{h}$ segment:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{par} &= \max(4,\, 6) + 3 + 1 \\
        &= 6 + 3 + 1 \\
        &= 10 \,\text{hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence the **time saved** is:

$$
\Delta T = T_{seq} - T_{par} = 14 - 10 = 4 \,\text{hours}
$$

The **speed-up factor** is:

$$
S = \frac{T_{seq}}{T_{par}} = \frac{14}{10} = 1.4
$$

2. **Critical Path (2 marks):**
The critical path is the *longest* sequence of dependent activities from the initial to the final node. It is:

$$
\text{Submit} \rightarrow \text{Verify collateral (6 h)} \rightarrow \text{Score (3 h)} \rightarrow \text{Disburse (1 h)} = 10 \,\text{h}
$$

The critical path determines the **minimum possible end-to-end duration** because *no* amount of additional parallelism on non-critical activities can shorten the schedule.

3. **Signal Trigger (2 marks):**
The *Loan Officer* swimlane dispatches an asynchronous signal on approval:

```
«send» → approvalNotice(applicationId, approvedAmount, timestamp)
```

The *Customer* swimlane contains a `«receive» approvalNotice` action. Because it is asynchronous, the Loan Officer is **not blocked** — the customer can be notified later via push notification, SMS, or email without holding up other applications.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks:**
> 1. Using a *single* diamond for both decision and merge — UML allows reuse, but for clarity KTU expects *separate* decision and merge nodes when they are far apart.
> 2. Forgetting the **join bar** in the activity diagram — examiners award 1 full mark specifically for the correct `Join` placement.
> 3. Confusing *speed-up* with *efficiency* ($\eta = S/n$). For $n = 2$ parallel branches, $S = 1.4$ implies $\eta = 0.7$ (i.e., 70 % parallel efficiency). Mentioning this earns a bonus mark.
> 4. Drawing *signals* as ordinary method calls — they must be denoted with the **pentagonal** `«send»`/`«receive»` stereotype.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this as the last 5-minute glance before entering the exam hall.**

- [x] **State Machine Diagram** = states + transitions + events + actions + guards. Subject is *one* object.
- [x] **Activity Diagram** = actions + control flow (decision, fork, join) + swimlanes + object nodes. Subject is a *workflow / use case*.
- [x] Transition syntax: `event [guard] / action`. Missing any one component is a -1 mark deduction.
- [x] **Composite state** = a state that contains substates. Draw with an enclosing rounded rectangle.
- [x] **History pseudo-state** = `H` (shallow) or `H*` (deep) circle inside a composite state.
- [x] **Concurrent substates** are separated by a *dashed* boundary line.
- [x] **Entry / Exit / Do-actions** belong to a state; internal transitions do *not* trigger exit-then-entry.
- [x] **Deferred event** syntax: inside state body write `event / defer`.
- [x] **Fork** = one input, $n$ outputs (parallel split). **Join** = $n$ inputs, one output (parallel merge). Join fires iff all $n$ tokens have arrived.
- [x] **Time event** = `after duration`. **Change event** = `when (booleanExpr)`.
- [x] **Signal** = asynchronous, named, typed event; drawn with pentagonal `«send»` / `«receive»` notation.
- [x] **Exception handler** = lightning-bolt arrow leading to `«exceptionHandler»` action region.
- [x] **Swimlane** = partition by responsibility (class, role, department). UML allows both *horizontal* and *vertical* swimlanes.
- [x] **Critical path** in an activity diagram = longest chain of dependent activities. Determines minimum project duration.
- [x] **Speed-up factor** $S = T_{seq}/T_{par}$. **Parallel efficiency** $\eta = S/n$ where $n$ is the number of parallel branches.
- [x] When asked to "draw", **always** include the initial node (filled circle) and at least one final node (bull's-eye).
- [x] For KTU 14-mark questions, the standard split is **7 marks diagram + 7 marks analytical follow-up**. Allocate time accordingly.
- [x] For an asynchronous signal, **never** draw a return arrow — signals are one-way.
- [x] If a question says "show concurrency", you *must* include a fork-join pair; if it says "show organizational responsibility", you *must* include swimlanes.
- [x] Reachability theorem: a state is reachable iff there exists a sequence of events and guard-true evaluations leading from $s_0$.

> **End of Notes — Advanced Dynamic Modeling Concepts (Module 2, PBCNT604, KTU 2024 Scheme)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: A Sample Dynamic Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCNT604 - OBJECT ORIENTED MODELING AND DESIGN > - **Module:** Module Module - 2 > - **Topic:** A Sample Dynamic Model # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Dynamic Model (Per Rumbaugh OMT Methodology)** > The **Dynamic Model** represents the time-dependent (temporal) behaviour of the system — the changing states of objects over time and the events (causes) that trigger those state changes. It consists of **state diagrams**, **event traces (scenarios)**, and the **synchronisation of events between objects**. It answers the question: *"What happens to the object as time passes and as events occur?"* A **Sample Dynamic Model** is the practical, worked demonstration of how the dynamic model of a real system is constructed. It walks the analyst through a complete iteration of: 1. **Identifying events** from the use cases. 2. **Preparing event traces** (single-threaded time-ordered sequences of events). 3. **Drawing state diagrams** for each important class. 4. **Matching events** between objects (event synchronisation). 5. **Reviewing** the model for completeness, correctness, and consistency. > [!NOTE] > **Three Models of OMT (Rumbaugh) — Recap** > | Model | Captures | Key Diagram | > |---|---|---| > | **Object Model** | Static structure (classes, relations, attributes) | Class diagram, object diagram | > | **Dynamic Model** | Temporal behaviour (states, events, transitions) | **State diagram, event trace** | > | **Functional Model** | Transformations on data (processes, data flow) | Data flow diagram (DFD) | > The three models are interrelated: the **Object Model** is the *backbone*; the **Dynamic Model** is the *heart* (the control); the **Functional Model** is the *body* (the computations). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!TIP] > **The "Movie vs. Photograph" Analogy (Rumbaugh)** > Imagine your system under analysis as a building. > - The **Object Model** is the *blueprint* of the building — it shows walls, doors, rooms (static structure). > - The **Dynamic Model** is a *movie* of the building in use — it shows people opening doors, lights switching on, lifts moving (temporal behaviour). > - The **Functional Model** is the *electrical/plumbing schematic* — it shows how energy/water is transformed and routed. > > A **Sample Dynamic Model** is therefore a *short movie clip* of the system: we pick a scenario, list every event that occurs in order, freeze the movie at the *interesting moments* (the **states**), and draw arrows between them (the **transitions**). **A second analogy** — a **Traffic Light Controller**: - The light has **states** (RED → GREEN → YELLOW → RED). - It does not know the time; it merely *responds* to an event (a timer tick, `after(30s)`). - A **dynamic model** for the light = state diagram + the event that drives the transitions. ## 1.3 Key Terminology (Board-Exam Standard) | Term | Formal Meaning | Notation | |---|---|---| | **Event** | An instantaneous occurrence that may change an object's state or invoke an action | `e(eventName)` | | **State** | A condition of an object during which it satisfies some condition, performs an action, or waits for an event | Rounded rectangle | | **Transition** | A change of state caused by an event | Directed arrow | | **Guard** | Boolean condition on a transition (fired only if TRUE) | `[x > 0]` | | **Action** | Atomic computation executed as a result of a transition | `a / expression` | | **Activity** | Computation that takes time (a state may contain one) | `do / expression` | | **Initial / Final State** | Entry / termination pseudo-state | Filled / bullseye circle | > [!NOTE] > **Boundary Metric for Sample Models** > A "Sample" dynamic model in textbooks typically considers only the **3 – 5 most important classes** of the system (those with significant dynamic behaviour). Static, value-only classes (e.g., `Address`, `Date`) are **omitted** from state diagrams because they have no interesting states. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Five-Stage Construction Process A **Sample Dynamic Model** is built by executing the following ordered steps. Each step has a verifiable deliverable. ### Stage 1 — Preparation: Identify Candidate Events - Scan every **use case** from the use-case diagram. - For each step in the use-case text, ask: *"Does this event change the state of any object?"* - If **yes** → include in event list. If **no** → it is a pure functional action (goes to the functional model). - Classify each event: - **External event** (from an actor) — e.g., `pressOk`. - **Internal event** (from another object) — e.g., `doorClosed`. - **Temporal event** (after some time) — e.g., `after(30s)`. - **Change event** (when a Boolean condition becomes true) — e.g., `when(weight > 100)`. ### Stage 2 — Prepare Event Traces (Scenarios) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Event Trace Definition (Board Favourite)** > An **event trace** is a time-ordered, single-threaded, end-to-end list of events and states for **one path through one scenario** of one use case. It is the textual form of the dynamic model before the diagrammatic form is built. A trace looks like: ``` [t0] state: objectX is in state S0 [t1] event: e1 arrives [t2] state: objectX is in state S1 [t3] event: e2 arrives ... ``` ### Stage 3 — Build State Diagrams for Individual Classes - For each class with significant dynamic behaviour, build a **state diagram**. - Use **Harel state-chart** notation (states as rounded boxes, transitions as arrows labelled `event [guard] / action`). - **Start from the initial state** (state when the object is created). - Add **accepting states** (Bullseye) for termination of the object's useful life. - Add **do-activities** for states that perform work. ### Stage 4 — Match Events Between State Diagrams > [!WARNING] > **Synchronisation Rule (Frequently Tested)** > Whenever the **same event** appears as a transition in **two or more state diagrams**, the receiving object must **listen (have that event as its own incoming transition)**. The sender's action sends the event; the receiver's transition consumes it. Mismatch = inconsistent dynamic model. Steps to match: 1. Take each event in the event list. 2. Decide: *Which object generates it?* (the **sender**) 3. Decide: *Which object(s) consume it?* (the **receiver(s)**) 4. Verify the receiver has a transition labelled with that event. 5. If a receiver has the event but no useful effect → remove the transition (cleanup). ### Stage 5 — Review for Completeness & Correctness - **Reachability**: Can every state be reached from the initial state? - **Reachability of all events**: Is every event used in at least one state diagram? - **Consistency**: Do the matched events between objects form a coherent flow? - **Conciseness**: Are there unused states or events? (Prune them.) - **Multiple paths**: Are the traces complete? Any path through the use case is still a valid event trace. ## 2.2 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet Since Dynamic Modelling is a **conceptual and diagrammatic** discipline (not numerical), the "formula sheet" takes the form of a **notation reference** and a **transformation table**. ### Table 2.2.1 — Harel State-Diagram Notation (High-Yield) | Symbol | Meaning | |---|---| | Filled black circle | **Initial state** (object creation) | | Rounded rectangle | **State** — possibly labelled `do / activity` | | Bullseye circle | **Final / accepting state** (object destruction) | | Arrow `A → B` | Transition from state A to state B | | Label `e` | Transition triggered by event `e` | | Label `e [g]` | Transition triggered by `e` only if guard `g` is true | | Label `e [g] / a` | Transition triggered by `e`; if guard holds, perform action `a` | | Label `a` | State activity performed while in the state | | Label `after(n)` | Temporal event (occurs `n` time units after entry) | | Label `when(c)` | Change event (occurs when Boolean condition `c` becomes TRUE) | ### Table 2.2.2 — Decision Table: Which Sub-Model Owns an Event? | If the event ... | Belongs to ... | |---|---| | Changes the state of an object | **Dynamic Model** (state diagram) | | Transforms data (input → output) without state change | **Functional Model** (process in DFD) | | Defines a structural relationship | **Object Model** (class diagram) | ### Table 2.2.3 — Common Standard Metrics in Dynamic Analysis | Metric | Typical Value (Sample Model) | Why | |---|---|---| | Number of classes with state diagrams | **3 – 5** | Only "interesting" classes | | Number of events in an event trace | **5 – 15** per scenario | One scenario per trace | | Number of states per class | **3 – 7** | Averages in textbook samples | | Number of scenarios per use case | **1 main + 2–3 alternatives** | Normal + edge cases | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility of the Dynamic Model** > In real-time, embedded, and control-system design (a KTU-favoured application area), the dynamic model is the **primary design artifact** — the object and functional models are *secondary*. Examples: elevator controllers, vending machines, mobile-phone call-flow, ATM transaction flows, anti-lock braking systems (ABS), and traffic-light controllers. # 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix — Building a Sample Dynamic Model > [!NOTE] > We will build a **Sample Dynamic Model** for a classic KTU case study — the **Automated Teller Machine (ATM)** — used by Rumbaugh in his canonical OMT book. The use case chosen is **"Withdraw Cash"**. ## 3.1 Step 1 — Write the Use-Case (Starting Point) ``` Use-Case: Withdraw Cash Actor: Customer, Bank Computer Flow: 1. Customer inserts card. 2. ATM reads card → sends cardNumber to Bank Computer. 3. Bank Computer validates card → returns "valid" or "invalid". 4. If valid: ATM requests PIN. 5. Customer enters PIN. 6. ATM sends PIN to Bank Computer. 7. Bank Computer validates PIN → returns "ok" or "bad". 8. If ok: ATM requests amount. 9. Customer enters amount. 10. ATM sends amount to Bank Computer. 11. Bank Computer checks balance, dispenses cash, debits account. 12. ATM prints receipt, ejects card. 13. Customer takes card and cash. ``` ## 3.2 Step 2 — Identify the Candidate Events Going through the use case line by line and asking *"does this change an object's state?"*: | Step | Event | Type | Sender | Receiver | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | `cardInserted` | External | Customer | ATM | | 2 | `cardRead(cardInfo)` | Internal | ATM | BankComputer | | 3 | `cardValid` / `cardInvalid` | Internal | BankComputer | ATM | | 4 | `requestPin` | Internal | ATM | Customer | | 5 | `pinEntered` | External | Customer | ATM | | 6 | `pinSent(pin)` | Internal | ATM | BankComputer | | 7 | `pinOk` / `pinBad` | Internal | BankComputer | ATM | | 8 | `requestAmount` | Internal | ATM | Customer | | 9 | `amountEntered` | External | Customer | ATM | | 10 | `withdraw(amount)` | Internal | ATM | BankComputer | | 11 | `cashDispensed` / `insufficientFunds` | Internal | BankComputer | ATM | | 12 | `receiptPrinted` | Internal | ATM | ATM | | 12 | `cardEjected` | Internal | ATM | Customer | | 13 | `cashTaken` | External | Customer | ATM | | 13 | `cardTaken` | External | Customer | ATM | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exhaustive Expansion — Every Row Justified** > - Step 1 → state change of `ATM` from `Idle` to `Card-Inserted` → event qualifies. > - Step 2 → no state change, only data transmission → still qualifies because it triggers a *response* (a state change in BankComputer: `Validating` → `Authenticated`). > - Step 7, 11 → branching events (`pinOk` vs `pinBad`) — each must be a **separate** transition in the receiver's state diagram. This is a frequently missed KTU point. > - Step 12, 13 → multiple events, one per atomic action. ## 3.3 Step 3 — Prepare an Event Trace (Main Success Scenario) ``` t0 ATM created → state: Idle t1 event: cardInserted → state: Card-Inserted t2 event: cardRead(cardInfo) → state: Validating-Card t3 event: cardValid → state: Waiting-PIN t4 event: pinEntered → state: Validating-PIN t5 event: pinOk → state: Waiting-Amount t6 event: amountEntered → state: Validating-Amount t7 event: withdraw(amount) → state: Processing-Withdrawal t8 event: cashDispensed → state: Dispensing-Cash t9 event: receiptPrinted → state: Printing-Receipt t10 event: cardEjected → state: Ejecting-Card t11 event: cardTaken → state: Idle t12 event: cashTaken → state: Idle (final) ``` ## 3.4 Step 4 — Build a State Diagram for the Class `ATM` Applying the rules of Harel state-diagrams, the ATM has the following states and transitions: **ATM States (justification — the "Why")** - `Idle` — initial state; ATM is doing nothing; waiting for `cardInserted`. - `Card-Inserted` — card has been inserted, but not yet read. - `Validating-Card` — actively communicating with Bank Computer. - `Waiting-PIN` — prompt on screen; do-activity: `display "Enter PIN"`. - `Validating-PIN` — sending PIN to bank. - `Waiting-Amount` — prompt on screen; do-activity: `display "Enter Amount"`. - `Validating-Amount` — asking bank for funds. - `Processing-Withdrawal` — internal handling. - `Dispensing-Cash` — counting and delivering notes. - `Printing-Receipt` — peripheral action. - `Ejecting-Card` — final step before return to `Idle`. - `Error` — exceptional state (e.g., card invalid → terminal state for this session). **Transition Table (the "How")** | # | Source State | Event | Guard | Action | Target State | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Idle | cardInserted | — | readCard | Card-Inserted | | 2 | Card-Inserted | cardRead | cardInfo not null | sendToBank | Validating-Card | | 3 | Validating-Card | cardValid | — | promptPin | Waiting-PIN | | 4 | Validating-Card | cardInvalid | — | ejectCard, beep | Error | | 5 | Waiting-PIN | pinEntered | — | sendPinToBank | Validating-PIN | | 6 | Validating-PIN | pinOk | — | promptAmount | Waiting-Amount | | 7 | Validating-PIN | pinBad | attempts < 3 | ejectCard | Error | | 8 | Waiting-Amount | amountEntered | amount > 0 | sendToBank | Validating-Amount | | 9 | Validating-Amount | cashDispensed | — | dispense | Dispensing-Cash | | 10 | Validating-Amount | insufficientFunds | — | display "No funds" | Waiting-Amount | | 11 | Dispensing-Cash | cashDispensed | — | printReceipt | Printing-Receipt | | 12 | Printing-Receipt | receiptPrinted | — | ejectCard | Ejecting-Card | | 13 | Ejecting-Card | cardTaken | — | reset | Idle | | 14 | Ejecting-Card | after(30s) | — | swallowCard | Idle | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mathematical Justification of "Multiple Branches"** > In a state diagram, a state may have **N outgoing transitions** with **mutually-exclusive events or guards**. This is essentially a **switch / case construct** in procedural programming: > $$ \text{nextState} = \begin{cases} S_1 & \text{if } e = e_1 \text{ (or } g_1 \text{ TRUE)} \\ S_2 & \text{if } e = e_2 \text{ (or } g_2 \text{ TRUE)} \\ \vdots & \\ S_n & \text{otherwise (default)} \end{cases} $$ > This is why **state diagrams map directly to a finite-state-machine (FSM)** implementation in any programming language. ## 3.5 Step 5 — State Diagram for Class `BankComputer` (Receiver Side) The same matching process yields the `BankComputer`'s state diagram. Since the bank computer handles many sessions in parallel, it is **multi-object** — typically modelled with a sub-diagram for each session (or with a sub-state). The single-session state diagram: ``` ┌──────────────┐ (initial)──► │ Authenticating │ ──cardInvalid──► (return) └──────┬───────┘ cardValid ▼ ┌──────────────┐ │ ValidatingPIN │ ──pinBad (attempts < 3)──► ValidatingPIN └──────┬───────┘ pinOk ▼ ┌──────────────┐ │ DebitingAcct │ ──insufficientFunds──► DebitingAcct (return fail) └──────┬───────┘ withdrawOK ▼ ┌──────────────┐ │ Dispensing │ ──► (return cashDispensed) └──────────────┘ ``` ## 3.6 Step 6 — Match Events Between Objects (Synchronisation Check) For each event, we verify both ends: | Event | Sender (in diagram of …) | Receiver (in diagram of …) | Status | |---|---|---|---| | `cardInserted` | (Actor: Customer) | ATM | ✓ Matched | | `cardRead` | ATM | BankComputer | ✓ Matched | | `cardValid` | BankComputer | ATM | ✓ Matched | | `cardInvalid` | BankComputer | ATM | ✓ Matched | | `pinEntered` | (Actor: Customer) | ATM | ✓ Matched | | `pinOk` / `pinBad` | BankComputer | ATM | ✓ Matched | | `amountEntered` | (Actor: Customer) | ATM | ✓ Matched | | `withdraw` | ATM | BankComputer | ✓ Matched | | `cashDispensed` | BankComputer | ATM | ✓ Matched | | `insufficientFunds` | BankComputer | ATM | ✓ Matched | | `cardTaken` | (Actor: Customer) | ATM | ✓ Matched | | `cashTaken` | (Actor: Customer) | ATM | ✓ Matched | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Self-loop / Autonomous events (e.g., `after(30s)` in row 14)** > Some events are **self-generated** by the object itself (temporal events). They have *no sender*. This is allowed and is a common examiner question: *"What is the difference between an external event and a temporal event?"* > - **External event** — originates from an actor or another object. > - **Temporal event** — originates from a clock internal to the object. > > A clean sample model **labels all temporal events explicitly** with `after(n)`. ## 3.7 Step 7 — Review and Refine Final review checklist applied: | Check | Result | |---|---| | Every state reachable from `Idle`? | ✓ (verified by trace) | | Every event used in ≥ 1 state diagram? | ✓ (12 / 12 used) | | All events matched between sender & receiver? | ✓ | | Any redundant state? | `Processing-Withdrawal` collapses into `Validating-Amount` → pruned | | Any extra state? | `Error` retained as safety net | | Concise? | 11 states in ATM, 4 in BankComputer — within textbook norms | > [!NOTE] > **Final Counts (Sample Model Realisation)** > $$\text{Number of state diagrams} = 2 \quad (\text{ATM, BankComputer})$$ > $$\text{Number of events in trace} = 13$$ > $$\text{Number of states in ATM} = 11$$ > $$\text{Number of transitions in ATM} = 14$$ > These are within the typical bounds for a **Sample Dynamic Model** as defined by Rumbaugh. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Dynamic-Model Construction ```mermaid flowchart TD start([Start Dynamic Modelling]) --> uc[Use-Case
Textual Description] uc --> ie[Step 1: Identify
Candidate Events] ie --> evList[(Event List)] evList --> et[Step 2: Build Event Traces
One per Scenario] et --> traceDB[(Event Traces
Main + Alternatives)] traceDB --> sd[Step 3: Build State
Diagrams per Class] sd --> sdSet[(Set of State
Diagrams)] sdSet --> match[Step 4: Match Events
Between Objects] match --> consistent{All events
matched?} consistent -- No --> fix[Reconcile Senders
and Receivers] fix --> sdSet consistent -- Yes --> review[Step 5: Review
Reachability / Conciseness] review --> done([Sample Dynamic Model
Complete]) style start fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff style done fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff style consistent fill:#fff2cc,color:#000000 style evList fill:#cfe2f3,color:#000000 style traceDB fill:#cfe2f3,color:#000000 style sdSet fill:#cfe2f3,color:#000000 ``` ## 4.2 State Diagram of the ATM Class (Harel Notation) ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Idle Idle --> CardInserted: cardInserted / readCard CardInserted --> ValidatingCard: cardRead ValidatingCard --> WaitingPIN: cardValid / promptPin ValidatingCard --> Error: cardInvalid / ejectCard WaitingPIN --> ValidatingPIN: pinEntered ValidatingPIN --> WaitingAmount: pinOk / promptAmount ValidatingPIN --> Error: pinBad and attempts less than 3 WaitingAmount --> ValidatingAmount: amountEntered and amount greater than 0 ValidatingAmount --> DispensingCash: cashDispensed ValidatingAmount --> WaitingAmount: insufficientFunds DispensingCash --> PrintingReceipt: receiptPrinted PrintingReceipt --> EjectingCard: cardEjected EjectingCard --> Idle: cardTaken / reset EjectingCard --> Idle: after 30s / swallowCard Error --> Idle: after 5s ``` ## 4.3 Event-Sync Matrix (How Events Move Between Objects) ```mermaid flowchart LR Customer((Customer
Actor)) -- cardInserted --> ATM Customer -- pinEntered --> ATM Customer -- amountEntered --> ATM Customer -- cardTaken --> ATM Customer -- cashTaken --> ATM ATM -- cardRead --> BC[Bank Computer] ATM -- pinSent --> BC ATM -- withdraw --> BC BC -- cardValid --> ATM BC -- cardInvalid --> ATM BC -- pinOk --> ATM BC -- pinBad --> ATM BC -- cashDispensed --> ATM BC -- insufficientFunds --> ATM style Customer fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff style ATM fill:#cfe2f3,color:#000000 style BC fill:#cfe2f3,color:#000000 ``` ## 4.4 Block Diagram of an Event Trace (Time on the X-axis) ```mermaid flowchart LR s0([t0]) -->|cardInserted| s1([t1]) s1 -->|cardRead| s2([t2]) s2 -->|cardValid| s3([t3]) s3 -->|pinEntered| s4([t4]) s4 -->|pinOk| s5([t5]) s5 -->|amountEntered| s6([t6]) s6 -->|withdraw| s7([t7]) s7 -->|cashDispensed| s8([t8]) s8 -->|cardTaken| s9([t9]) s9 -->|cashTaken| sf([t10 final]) style s0 fill:#fff2cc,color:#000000 style sf fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]` **Define an event trace. List any four types of events used while building a dynamic model.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > An **event trace** is a *time-ordered, end-to-end list of events and states* for a single scenario (path) through a single use case. It is the textual form of the dynamic model and serves as the basis for constructing the state diagrams of individual classes. **Four types of events:** 1. **External event** — originates from an actor (e.g., `cardInserted`). 2. **Internal event** — originates from another object in the system (e.g., `cardValid`). 3. **Temporal event** — occurs after a fixed time (`after(n)`). 4. **Change event** — occurs when a Boolean condition becomes true (`when(c)`). *(½ mark for definition; ½ mark each for the four types = 3 marks)* --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **What is meant by "matching events" between state diagrams? Why is it important?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Matching events** is the process of verifying that for every event that is *sent* by one object, there is a corresponding *receive* transition in the consuming object. (1 mark) > > **Importance:** > 1. Ensures the dynamic model is **internally consistent** — no event is "lost" in transit. (1 mark) > 2. Reveals **missing transitions** in a class's state diagram (the model is incomplete). (½ mark) > 3. Helps **identify the sender and receiver** of every interaction — a prerequisite for OOD class design. (½ mark) --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, ESE Module Choice Pattern) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Sample Dynamic Model** > - **Common Loss Zone 1 (≈ 3 marks):** Students write state diagrams **without first listing the event trace**. Board examiners expect the trace as a deliverable in Step 2 of the process. Lose 3 marks if omitted. > - **Common Loss Zone 2 (≈ 2 marks):** Students omit the **initial state (filled circle)** and **final/accepting state (bullseye)**. Lose 2 marks each. > - **Common Loss Zone 3 (≈ 2 marks):** Students do not **match events** between the two state diagrams. Lose 2 marks. > - **Common Loss Zone 4 (≈ 1 mark):** Students forget the **guard condition** syntax `[condition]` on branched transitions. > - **Always draw both the event trace AND the state diagram** in two adjacent boxes; the examiner scans for them in the first 30 seconds of valuation. --- ### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** *With a suitable example, explain the process of building a Sample Dynamic Model. List the events generated when a customer inserts a card into an ATM. (7 marks)* **Model Answer:** The process of building a Sample Dynamic Model consists of **five iterative steps** (Rumbaugh, OMT methodology): **Step 1 — Identify candidate events from use cases.** Scan every use case. For each step ask: *"Does it change the state of an object?"* If yes, record it. Classify it as external, internal, temporal, or change event. *(1 mark)* **Step 2 — Prepare event traces.** An event trace is a time-ordered list of events and the resulting states of the *primary* object for one path through one scenario. Each use case generates one main trace and several alternative traces (for exceptions). *(1 mark)* **Step 3 — Build state diagrams for each class.** For each class with significant dynamic behaviour, draw a Harel state diagram with initial state, final state, and labelled transitions `event [guard] / action`. *(1 mark)* **Step 4 — Match events between state diagrams.** Verify that every event emitted by one object is consumed by another. This is the "**inter-object event synchronisation**" step. *(1 mark)* **Step 5 — Review and refine.** Check reachability, completeness, and conciseness. Remove redundant states; add missing ones. *(1 mark)* **Events for ATM card-insert scenario (2 marks):** 1. `cardInserted` — Customer → ATM 2. `cardRead(cardInfo)` — ATM → Bank Computer 3. `cardValid` / `cardInvalid` — Bank Computer → ATM 4. `pinEntered` — Customer → ATM 5. `pinOk` / `pinBad` — Bank Computer → ATM 6. `amountEntered` — Customer → ATM 7. `cashDispensed` / `insufficientFunds` — Bank Computer → ATM 8. `cardEjected` — ATM → Customer 9. `cashTaken`, `cardTaken` — Customer → ATM --- **(b)** *Draw the state diagram for an ATM machine handling the "Withdraw Cash" use case. Explain the significance of guard conditions and temporal events in the diagram. (7 marks)* **Model Answer:** (Show the state diagram of the ATM — refer to Section 4.2 of this notes sheet. The diagram carries 3 marks; explanation carries 4 marks.) **Explanation — Guard Conditions (2 marks):** - A **guard** is a Boolean condition that must be TRUE for the transition to fire. - They are written in square brackets, e.g., `pinBad [attempts < 3]`. - Guards model **business rules** of the system — e.g., "after 3 wrong PINs, the card is swallowed". - They allow **mutually exclusive branches** out of a state (similar to `if / else if`). **Explanation — Temporal Events (2 marks):** - A **temporal event** is generated by a clock internal to the object, *not* by an external actor. - Notation: `after(n)` — fires `n` time units after entering the state. - Use case in the ATM: `Ejecting-Card → Idle: after(30s) / swallowCard` — if the customer does not take the card in 30 seconds, the ATM swallows it. - Temporal events model **timeouts** and **safety behaviour**, essential in real-time / control systems. --- ### Question B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** *Explain the concept of an event in OMT with classification. Distinguish between action and activity. (7 marks)* **Model Answer:** **Concept of Event (3 marks):** An **event** is an instantaneous occurrence that takes place at a point in time. In OMT, an event is the **stimulus** that causes a transition in an object's state diagram. Events have no duration. Each event is associated with a single point on the event trace. **Classification (3 marks):** 1. **External Event** — originates outside the system, from an actor (e.g., user clicks a button). 2. **Internal Event** — sent from one object to another inside the system (e.g., `orderPlaced` from `Order` to `Inventory`). 3. **Temporal Event** — generated by a clock within an object (e.g., `after(60s)`). 4. **Change Event** — generated when a Boolean condition becomes true (e.g., `when(temperature > 100)`). **Action vs. Activity (1 mark):** - **Action** — an *atomic, instantaneous* computation associated with a transition. Notation: `e / action`. - **Activity** — a *non-atomic, time-consuming* computation associated with a state. Notation: `do / activity`. - Analogy: a *photograph* vs a *video clip*; a checkmark vs a progress bar. --- **(b)** *Consider the use case "Lift travels from floor 1 to floor 5". List all candidate events. Prepare an event trace and draw the state diagram for the Lift class. (7 marks)* **Model Answer:** **Candidate Events (2 marks):** 1. `floor1ButtonPressed` (External — User → Lift) 2. `floor5ButtonPressed` (External — User → Lift) 3. `motorOn` (Internal — Controller → Motor) 4. `floorReached(floorN)` (Internal — Sensor → Controller) 5. `doorClosed` (Internal — Door → Controller) 6. `doorOpen` (Internal — Door → Controller) 7. `motorOff` (Internal — Controller → Motor) 8. `after(2s)` (Temporal — for door open / close delay) **Event Trace (2 marks):** ``` t0 state: Idle t1 event: floor5ButtonPressed t2 state: Moving t3 event: floorReached(2) t4 state: Moving (passing floors 2,3,4) t5 event: floorReached(5) t6 state: DoorsOpening t7 event: doorOpen t8 state: DoorsOpen (do / wait 2s) t9 event: after(2s) t10 state: DoorsClosing t11 event: doorClosed t12 state: Idle ``` **State Diagram (3 marks):** ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Idle Idle --> Moving: floor5ButtonPressed / motorOn Moving --> DoorsOpening: floorReached(5) / motorOff Moving --> Moving: floorReached(2 or 3 or 4) / continue DoorsOpening --> DoorsOpen: doorOpen DoorsOpen --> DoorsClosing: after 2s DoorsClosing --> Idle: doorClosed Idle --> [*] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Board Marking Tip** > The self-loop on `Moving` (`floorReached(2/3/4) / continue`) is *frequently missed*. Always show intermediate states/events; examiners allocate **½ mark** for this completeness. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — "A Sample Dynamic Model"** - [x] A **Sample Dynamic Model** is a worked example illustrating the *five-stage process* of dynamic modelling (Rumbaugh). - [x] The **Dynamic Model** captures *time-dependent* behaviour: states, events, transitions. - [x] **Five stages**: (1) identify events from use cases, (2) prepare event traces, (3) build per-class state diagrams, (4) match events between objects, (5) review. - [x] **Event types** to remember: External, Internal, Temporal, Change. - [x] **Event trace** = time-ordered, single-threaded, end-to-end list of events and states for one scenario. - [x] **State diagram notation (Harel)**: rounded rectangle = state, arrow = transition, filled circle = initial, bullseye = final. - [x] **Action** is *atomic* (`/ action`); **Activity** takes time (`do / activity`). - [x] **Guard** condition: written in square brackets `[condition]`; enables branched transitions. - [x] **Event matching** = inter-object synchronisation — every emitted event must have a receiving transition. - [x] Only classes with **significant dynamic behaviour** get state diagrams (typically **3–5** in a sample). - [x] Standard metrics: **3–7 states** per class, **5–15 events** per trace, **3–5** state diagrams per sample. - [x] Common **forgotten** items in exams: initial state, final state, guard syntax, self-loops, intermediate floor / step events, and the event-match matrix. - [x] The three OMT models are interrelated: **Object Model = backbone**, **Dynamic Model = heart (control)**, **Functional Model = body (computations)**. - [x] The dynamic model is the **primary artifact** in real-time and control systems; it is the *first* model to be built in such systems. - [x] Two crucial board-favourite **diagrams** to draw: (i) the **event trace** (textual or arrow-chain) and (ii) the **state diagram** of the primary class. > [!TIP] > **One-Line Mantra for the Hall** > *"Identify events → trace scenarios → draw per-class state diagrams → match events → review — that's a Sample Dynamic Model."*

topics.title: Relationship of Object and Dynamic Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCNT604 - OBJECT ORIENTED MODELING AND DESIGN > - **Module:** Module - 2 > - **Topic:** Relationship of Object and Dynamic Models** # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In the **Object Modeling Technique (OMT)** methodology (the foundation of OOAD as prescribed in the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus), a complete software system is described using **three complementary models**: 1. **Object Model** → Describes the *static, structural* aspects (classes, attributes, operations, relationships, multiplicities). 2. **Dynamic Model** → Describes the *temporal, behavioral* aspects (states, events, transitions, actions). 3. **Functional Model** → Describes the *data transformation* aspects (DFDs, processes, data flow). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core Definition (KTU Board Standard):** > The **relationship between the Object Model and the Dynamic Model** states that the two models are **complementary views of the same underlying system**. The classes of the object model are the *carriers* of the behavior described in the dynamic model. Operations declared on a class in the object model correspond to the *actions* executed during state transitions in the dynamic model, while *events* in the dynamic model correspond to *operations* invoked on objects of the class in the object model. Together, both models are used to construct a **script (trace)** that demonstrates the integrated, time-ordered behavior of the system. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition) Imagine a **theatrical play**: - The **Object Model** is like the **cast list and scriptbook** — it tells you *who the characters are* (classes/objects), *what properties they have* (attributes like age, role), and *what actions they are capable of performing* (operations like `speak()`, `fight()`, `exit()`). - The **Dynamic Model** is like the **stage directions and lighting cues** — it tells you *when* a character is in which mood/scene (state), *what triggers* a scene change (event), and *what the character does* when the cue arrives (action). The **relationship** is this: the stage directions (dynamic model) only make sense *with reference to* the cast list (object model). A cue "Character A exits stage left" is meaningless unless we know who Character A is. Conversely, the cast list (object model) cannot fully describe the play without showing how characters *change and react* over time. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes that *no single model is sufficient*. The object model gives structure, the dynamic model gives life, and the **script (trace)** is where they meet to describe a complete scenario. --- ## 1.3 Why the Relationship Matters in Software Engineering | Aspect | Object Model Role | Dynamic Model Role | |---|---|---| | **Time** | Time-independent (snapshot) | Time-dependent (sequence) | | **Focus** | What objects exist & how they relate | How objects react to events | | **Representation** | Class diagram | State diagram | | **Concurrency** | Implicit | Explicit (parallel states, forks) | > [!IMPORTANT] > The **object model is the primary model** (often called the "backbone"), while the dynamic and functional models provide additional behavioral and transformational views. However, the dynamic model only becomes meaningful when mapped back to object model classes. --- ## 1.4 Key Vocabulary for KTU Examinations | Term | Meaning | |---|---| | **Script (Trace)** | A time-ordered sequence of events between objects showing a complete scenario. | | **State** | A condition during the life of an object when it satisfies some condition, performs an activity, or waits for an event. | | **Event** | A stimulus that triggers a state transition. | | **Action** | An operation performed by an object in response to an event. | | **Class Scope Attribute** | A single attribute shared by all instances of a class. | | **Concurrent States** | Independent substates active simultaneously within a composite state. | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 The Core Correspondence Rules The relationship between the Object Model and the Dynamic Model is governed by **five foundational correspondence rules**. These are heavily tested in KTU exams. ### Rule 1: *Same Classes Appear in Both Models* The classes identified in the object diagram are the **same classes** whose lifecycle is described in the dynamic model. The dynamic model does not introduce new classes — it only describes the *behavior over time* of classes already defined in the object model. ### Rule 2: *Operations in the Object Model Become Actions in the Dynamic Model* When the state diagram of a class specifies an action (e.g., `calculate interest()`), this action corresponds to an **operation of that class** declared in the object model. > **Mapping:** `Object Model Operation` ↔ `Dynamic Model Action` ### Rule 3: *Events in the Dynamic Model Become Operations in the Object Model* An event (e.g., `deposit(amount)`) sent to an object in the dynamic model corresponds to an **operation of the receiving class** in the object model. The event is essentially a *call* to that operation. > **Mapping:** `Dynamic Model Event` → `Object Model Operation (on receiving class)` ### Rule 4: *States in the Dynamic Model Organize the Operations of a Class* A class in the object model may have many operations. The dynamic model groups these operations into **states** — i.e., a class responds to certain events only in certain states. This is called **state-dependent behavior**. ### Rule 5: *A Script (Trace) Uses Both Models Together* A script is constructed by selecting a scenario, then stepping through the events between objects. Each event in the script refers to an operation defined in the object model, and the state changes that result are recorded using the dynamic model. --- ## 2.2 Building a Script — The Practical Bridge A **script** is the integration mechanism between the two models. The procedure is: 1. **Start** with a starting object and a stimulus event from outside the system. 2. **Each event** is sent to a receiver object. The dynamic model of the receiver's class determines whether the event is accepted (in the current state) or ignored. 3. **If accepted**, the object transitions to a new state and may perform an action (which is an operation on the same or another object → which triggers another event in the script). 4. **Stop** when no further events are generated. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked "How are the object and dynamic models related?", the most-credited answer is: *"The operations on a class in the object model become the actions and events in the dynamic model, and the events in the dynamic model invoke operations defined in the object model. A script (trace) combines both models to describe a complete scenario."* --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet | Concept | Object Model | Dynamic Model | Linkage | |---|---|---|---| | **Unit of structure** | Class | State | One class → one state diagram | | **Element of behavior** | Operation | Action / Event | Operations ↔ Actions/Events | | **Cardinality** | Multiplicity | Not applicable | — | | **Time dimension** | Absent | Present | — | | **Primary diagram** | Class diagram | State diagram | — | | **Integration artifact** | — | — | **Script (Trace)** | | **Visibility** | Public/Private | Internal/External events | Operations correspond to public actions | --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility This dual-model relationship is foundational in: - **Embedded systems** (e.g., an ATM controller): the object model lists `Account`, `CardReader`, `CashDispenser`; the dynamic model shows the state of an `ATM` (Idle → CardInserted → PINVerified → Dispensing → Idle). - **Telecommunications** (e.g., a phone call): the object model has `Call`, `Phone`, `Switch`; the dynamic model describes the `Call` state machine (Dialing → Ringing → Connected → Ended). - **Web applications** (e.g., an e-commerce `Order`): object model defines `Order`, `Cart`, `Payment`; dynamic model describes the `Order` lifecycle (Pending → Confirmed → Shipped → Delivered → Cancelled). > [!IMPORTANT] > In **NEP 2020 / KTU 2024 OBE practice**, when designing a system you must explicitly state: *"For class X, what is its state diagram? Which operations in the class diagram are the actions/transitions?"* This satisfies the CO (Course Outcome) requirement of integrating structural and behavioral views. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Derivations ## 3.1 The Mathematical/Logical Derivation of the Mapping Function While this topic is largely conceptual, the *mapping between models* can be expressed as a formal relation. This is a favourite KTU 14-mark question. ### 3.1.1 Formal Definition of the Object Model Let: $$ O = (C, A, R, M) $$ where: - $C = \{c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_n\}$ = set of classes - $A = \{a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_p\}$ = set of attributes - $R = \{r_1, r_2, \ldots, r_q\}$ = set of relationships - $M$ = set of multiplicities For each class $c_i$, we define its **operation set**: $$ Ops(c_i) = \{op_{i,1}, op_{i,2}, \ldots, op_{i,k_i}\} $$ ### 3.1.2 Formal Definition of the Dynamic Model For each class $c_i$, let its **state diagram** be defined as: $$ D(c_i) = (S_i, E_i, \delta_i, s_{0,i}, A_i) $$ where: - $S_i = \{s_{i,1}, s_{i,2}, \ldots\}$ = set of states of $c_i$ - $E_i = \{e_{i,1}, e_{i,2}, \ldots\}$ = set of events accepted by $c_i$ - $\delta_i : S_i \times E_i \rightarrow S_i$ = transition function - $s_{0,i}$ = initial state of $c_i$ - $A_i \subseteq Ops(c_i)$ = set of actions of $c_i$ ### 3.1.3 The Mapping Between the Two Models **Step 1 — Event-to-Operation Mapping:** Each event $e_{i,j}$ accepted by class $c_i$ corresponds to an operation on the sending class that *generates* the event, and the event itself is treated as a call to an operation on $c_i$: $$ e_{i,j} \;\longleftrightarrow\; op_{\text{sender}} \;\rightarrow\; op_{\text{receiver}}(c_i) $$ **Step 2 — Action-to-Operation Mapping:** Each action performed during a transition is an operation belonging to *some* class $c_m$: $$ \forall \text{ action } \alpha \in A_i : \exists\, c_m \in C \text{ such that } \alpha \in Ops(c_m) $$ **Step 3 — Script Construction (Iterative Event Chain):** A script $\sigma$ is a sequence of events: $$ \sigma = (e_1, e_2, e_3, \ldots, e_t) $$ such that for each event $e_r$ with source object $obj_s$ and target object $obj_t$: $$ \delta_{\text{class}(obj_t)}\big(\text{state}(obj_t,\, t_{r-1}),\, e_r\big) = \text{state}(obj_t,\, t_r) $$ and $e_r$ is generated by some action of an object whose state changed at $t_{r-1}$. **Step 4 — Termination:** The script terminates when no more events are generated: $$ \text{Terminate}(\sigma) \iff \forall r,\, \nexists\, e_{r+1} $$ --- ## 3.2 Worked-Out Example: A Telephone Call ### Step 1 — Object Model (Class Diagram Excerpt) | Class | Attributes | Operations | |---|---|---| | `Caller` | phoneNumber | `liftReceiver()`, `dial(number)`, `hangUp()` | | `Callee` | phoneNumber, isAvailable | `answerCall()`, `rejectCall()`, `hangUp()` | | `Call` | callId, duration, status | `connect()`, `disconnect()` | | `Switch` | — | `routeSignal()`, `terminateCall()` | ### Step 2 — Dynamic Model (State Diagram of `Call`) States of `Call`: $S_{Call} = \{\text{Idle}, \text{Dialing}, \text{Ringing}, \text{Connected}, \text{Ended}\}$ Transitions: $$ \begin{aligned} \delta(\text{Idle}, \text{dial}) &= \text{Dialing} \\ \delta(\text{Dialing}, \text{callee-busy}) &= \text{Ended} \\ \delta(\text{Dialing}, \text{callee-free}) &= \text{Ringing} \\ \delta(\text{Ringing}, \text{answer}) &= \text{Connected} \\ \delta(\text{Ringing}, \text{reject}) &= \text{Ended} \\ \delta(\text{Connected}, \text{hangUp}) &= \text{Ended} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step
