> **Engineering Utility:** Operation specifications form the basis of **Design by Contract (DbC)** — a methodology by Bertrand Meyer (creator of Eiffel) widely used in safety-critical systems: avionics (Airbus A380 flight control), banking (account transactions must satisfy pre/post), railway signaling systems. It is also the foundation for **unit testing** frameworks like JUnit, where preconditions become test inputs and postconditions become assertions.

## 2.4 The Precondition / Postcondition Logic Chain

The operation contract can be expressed formally as a Hoare-style logic triple:

$$ \{P\} \;\; \text{operation} \;\; \{Q\} $$

Where:
- **$P$** = Precondition (the predicate assumed true on entry)
- **$Q$** = Postcondition (the predicate guaranteed true on successful exit)

The key inference rules are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{(Strengthening)} \quad & \text{If } \{P'\}\ S\ \{Q\} \text{ and } P \Rightarrow P', \text{ then } \{P\}\ S\ \{Q\} \\
\textbf{(Weakening)} \quad & \text{If } \{P\}\ S\ \{Q'\} \text{ and } Q \Rightarrow Q', \text{ then } \{P\}\ S\ \{Q\} \\
\textbf{(Composition)} \quad & \text{If } \{P\}\ S_1\ \{R\} \text{ and } \{R\}\ S_2\ \{Q\}, \text{ then } \{P\}\ S_1;S_2\ \{Q\}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** When the examiner asks *"Differentiate strengthening vs weakening of preconditions,"* this is the exact answer expected. Mark allocation: usually 1 mark for each rule with example.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example: Specifying a `debit` Operation on a `BankAccount` Class

We will derive the **complete operation specification** for a banking transaction, step by step. This is a frequently repeated question in KTU university examinations.

### Step 1 — Identify the Class Context

The class is `BankAccount` with the following private attributes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{accountNumber} & : \text{String} \\
\text{balance} & : \text{Real} \\
\text{owner} & : \text{String}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — State the Operation Name and Visibility

The operation we want to specify is **debit money from the account**.

- **Name:** `debit`
- **Visibility:** `public` (clients must call it from outside the class)
- **Scope:** Instance-level (each account is debited individually)
- **Class-scope annotation:** *Not underlined* (since it is instance-level)

Therefore, the bare signature is:

$$+\,\text{debit}(\text{amount} : \text{Real}) : \text{Boolean}$$

### Step 3 — Define the Precondition

A *debit* must not be allowed if there are insufficient funds, or if the amount is non-positive. The **caller's responsibility** is to ensure these hold:

$$
\text{Precondition} : \;\; \text{amount} > 0 \;\; \wedge \;\; \text{balance} \geq \text{amount}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In UML, preconditions are conventionally enclosed in curly braces and prefixed by the keyword `pre:` immediately following the signature line.

### Step 4 — Define the Postcondition

A *successful* debit must decrease the balance by exactly the amount and return `true`; a *failed* debit must leave balance unchanged and return `false`. The classic convention uses the **prime notation** (`'`) to denote "value after the operation":

$$
\text{Postcondition} : \;\; (\text{result} = \text{true} \wedge \text{balance}' = \text{balance} - \text{amount}) \;\; \vee \;\; (\text{result} = \text{false} \wedge \text{balance}' = \text{balance})
$$

### Step 5 — Specify Exceptions

The operation may also raise an exception in some design philosophies (Eiffel style) instead of returning a Boolean:

$$+\,\text{debit}(\text{amount} : \text{Real}) \;\; \textbf{raises} \;\; \text{InsufficientFundsException}$$

### Step 6 — Describe the Operation Body (Algorithmic Specification)

The KTU examiner typically awards 2 marks for a structured-English body. The OMT-style description is:

> **Operation Body — `debit(amount : Real) : Boolean`**
> 1. **Begin**
> 2.   *if* `amount > 0` **and** `balance ≥ amount` **then**
> 3.     `balance ← balance − amount`
> 4.     `return true`
> 5.   *else*
> 6.     `return false`
> 7.   *end if*
> 8. **End**

### Step 7 — Final Composite Specification (Full OMT Form)

The final, publication-grade OMT specification as expected by a KTU board examiner is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text{Class: BankAccount} \\
& \text{Operation: debit} \\
& \quad \text{Visibility:} \; + \text{ (public)} \\
& \quad \text{Scope:} \; \text{instance} \\
& \quad \text{Signature:} \; + \text{debit(amount : Real) : Boolean} \\
& \quad \text{Precondition:} \; \text{amount} > 0 \wedge \text{balance} \geq \text{amount} \\
& \quad \text{Postcondition:} \; (\text{result} = \text{true} \wedge \text{balance}' = \text{balance} - \text{amount}) \\
& \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \vee \; (\text{result} = \text{false} \wedge \text{balance}' = \text{balance}) \\
& \quad \text{Operation Body:} \; \text{(structured English as in Step 6)}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Operation vs. Method vs. Signature

The KTU 2024 examiner frequently tests this distinction using a 2-mark question. The rigorous derivation is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Operation} & = \text{declared behavior on a class (interface contract)} \\
\textbf{Method} & = \text{the concrete algorithmic implementation of an operation} \\
\textbf{Signature} & = \text{the syntactic name + parameters + return type of an operation} \\
\therefore \quad \textbf{One Operation} & \longmapsto \textbf{Many Methods (polymorphism)} \\
\therefore \quad \textbf{One Method} & \longmapsto \textbf{One Operation (single contract)} \\
\therefore \quad \textbf{Signature} & \subset \textbf{Operation} \subset \textbf{Class Behaviour}
\end{aligned}
$$

| Property | Operation | Method | Signature |
|:---------|:----------|:-------|:----------|
| Defined at | Design time | Coding time | Both |
| Contains code? | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| Polymorphic? | ✅ Yes (one op, many methods) | ✅ Yes (per subclass) | ❌ No (must be unique) |
| Part of UML class diagram? | ✅ Yes (3rd compartment) | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (within compartment) |
| KTUs reward in marks | 2 | 1 | 1 |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Whenever a question says *"specify the operation,"* the expected answer must include **signature + preconditions + postconditions + body**. A signature alone earns only 2 of the 7 marks.

## 3.3 Worked Example: Specifying Operations for a Library Management System (Full OMT Form)

Suppose the question asks: *"Specify the operations of the `Book` class for a library system, including a `checkOut(member : Member)` operation."*

**Step A — Identify all required operations on `Book`:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{addCopy} & : \text{increases total copies count} \\
\text{checkOut} & : \text{marks the book as borrowed by a member} \\
\text{returnBook} & : \text{marks the book as available} \\
\text{getStatus} & : \text{returns current availability}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step B — Signature for `checkOut`:**

$$+\,\text{checkOut}(\text{m} : \text{Member}) : \text{Boolean}$$

**Step C — Pre/Postconditions:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Pre} : & \; \text{availableCopies} \geq 1 \wedge \text{m.isActive} = \text{true} \\
\text{Post} : & \; \text{availableCopies}' = \text{availableCopies} - 1 \wedge \text{borrowedBy} = \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step D — Body in Structured English:**

> 1. If `availableCopies < 1`, return `false`.
> 2. If `not m.isActive`, return `false`.
> 3. Decrement `availableCopies` by 1.
> 4. Set `borrowedBy ← m`.
> 5. Record `dueDate ← today + 14 days`.
> 6. Return `true`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern Alert:** A 14-mark question on specifying operations usually carries:
> - 3 marks for the signature
> - 4 marks for correct pre/postconditions
> - 4 marks for the structured-English body
> - 3 marks for correctly drawing the class diagram with visibility markers
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Class Diagram Showing Fully Specified Operations

The following Mermaid `classDiagram` renders a UML 2.x class diagram of a `BankAccount` class with its complete operation specifications. This is the diagram a student is expected to draw in a KTU 14-mark question.

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class BankAccount {
        - accountNumber : String
        - balance : Real
        - owner : String
        + BankAccount(num : String, bal : Real, o : String)
        + debit(amount : Real) : Boolean
        + credit(amount : Real) : void
        + getBalance() : Real
        + getStatement() : String
    }

    class SavingsAccount {
        - interestRate : Real
        + calculateInterest() : Real
    }

    class CurrentAccount {
        - overdraftLimit : Real
        + debit(amount : Real) : Boolean
    }

    BankAccount <|-- SavingsAccount
    BankAccount <|-- CurrentAccount

    note for BankAccount "pre (debit): amount > 0 AND balance >= amount\npost (debit): (result=true AND balance'=balance-amount)\n       OR (result=false AND balance'=balance)"
    note for SavingsAccount "pre (calcInterest): balance >= 0\npost: interest' = balance * interestRate / 100"
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safeguard Applied:** All node IDs are purely alphanumeric (`BankAccount`, `SavingsAccount`, `CurrentAccount`). The `note for` syntax is used for the precondition/postcondition annotation block, which is rendered as a separate attached note — this is the standard UML way to specify pre/postconditions next to an operation in a class diagram.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Operation Specification

When a physical drawing is infeasible or the question is conceptual, the KTU examiner accepts a **block diagram** of the operation specification pipeline. The following Mermaid `flowchart` maps the entire specification flow:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Class Definition] --> B[Identify Required Behavior]
    B --> C[Choose Operation Name Verb-Noun]
    C --> D[Determine Visibility Scope]
    D --> E[Define Parameter List with Types]
    E --> F[Define Return Type]
    F --> G[Specify Preconditions]
    G --> H[Specify Postconditions with Prime Notation]
    H --> I[Document Exception Conditions]
    I --> J[Write Structured English Body]
    J --> K[Validate Against Use Cases]
    K --> L{Final OMT Specification}

    L --> M[Class Diagram Compartment 3]
    L --> N[Data Dictionary Entry]
    L --> O[Contract Document Design by Contract]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b,color:#000
    style L fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    style M fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
    style N fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
    style O fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Sequence Diagram: How a Specified Operation is Invoked at Runtime

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Client
    participant Account as BankAccount Object
    Client->>Account: debit(amount=1000)
    Note over Account: Check pre: amount > 0 AND balance >= 1000
    Account->>Account: balance := balance - 1000
    Note over Account: Post: balance' = old_balance - 1000
    Account-->>Client: return true
    Client->>Account: getBalance()
    Account-->>Client: return new_balance
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The note above `Account` (`Check pre: ...`) and the note after the action (`Post: ...`) represent the **contract assertions** the operation guarantees. The dashed return arrow (`-->>`) indicates a reply. This same diagram, drawn on paper in the exam, typically fetches 3 marks as part of a larger design question.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Differentiate between an **Operation** and a **Method** in object-oriented modeling. Why is this distinction important?*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Operation | Method |
|:-------|:----------|:-------|
| Definition | A service that can be requested from an object — a *declaration* of behaviour | The *implementation* (code) that realizes the operation |
| Defined at | Design time (in the class diagram) | Coding time (in the source file) |
| Polymorphism | One operation may have many methods (one per subclass) | Each method implements a specific operation |
| Exists in | Class interface | Class implementation |

The distinction is important because it enables **polymorphism** — the same operation (e.g., `+area() : Real`) can be invoked on different subclasses (`Circle`, `Rectangle`, `Triangle`), and the correct method is dispatched at runtime based on the actual object type. This decouples *what* an object does from *how* it does it, supporting extensibility and substitutability (Liskov Substitution Principle).

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Warning:** Writing *"Operation and method are the same thing"* will fetch **zero marks**. The examiner explicitly looks for the polymorphism justification. Always end your answer with the *why*.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *List and briefly explain the components of an operation specification in OMT.*

**Model Answer:**

The complete OMT operation specification consists of **six components**:

1. **Name** — A unique verb-noun identifier (e.g., `debit`, `calculateArea`).
2. **Argument List** — Typed parameters with direction (`in`, `out`, `inout`).
3. **Return Type** — The data type of the value returned, or `void`.
4. **Visibility** — Public (`+`), private (`−`), protected (`#`), package (`~`).
5. **Precondition** — A predicate that must hold *before* invocation (`requires`).
6. **Postcondition** — A predicate guaranteed *after* successful execution (`ensures`).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often forget the **scope** (instance vs class-level) and the **exception specification**. A full 3-mark answer should also mention *exception conditions* and *operation body / semantic description* in structured English.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks with two sub-parts of 7 marks each, and the student must answer *one* of the two provided choices. Below are **two fully independent alternatives**, exactly as a KTU question paper presents them.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]*

Consider the `Library` information system of a college. A `Book` class has the following attributes: `title : String`, `author : String`, `ISBN : String`, `availableCopies : Integer`, `borrowedBy : Member`, `dueDate : Date`.

**(a)** Specify the complete OMT operation specification for the `checkOut(member : Member) : Boolean` operation of the `Book` class, including signature, preconditions, postconditions, and a structured-English operation body. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Draw the UML 2.x class diagram for `Book` and `Member` showing all attributes, operations with visibility, and the relationship between them. Annotate the pre/postcondition of `checkOut` as a UML note attached to the class. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Operation Specification for `checkOut` (7 marks):**

- **Name:** `checkOut` (1 mark)
- **Signature:** `+ checkOut(m : Member) : Boolean` (1 mark)
- **Visibility:** Public (1 mark)
- **Preconditions:** `availableCopies >= 1 ∧ m.isActive = true` (1 mark)
- **Postconditions:** `availableCopies' = availableCopies − 1 ∧ borrowedBy' = m ∧ dueDate' = today + 14` (1 mark)
- **Structured-English Body:** (2 marks)

> 1. If `availableCopies < 1`, return `false`.
> 2. If `not m.isActive`, return `false`.
> 3. Decrement `availableCopies` by 1.
> 4. Set `borrowedBy ← m`.
> 5. Set `dueDate ← today + 14 days`.
> 6. Return `true`.

**(b) Class Diagram (7 marks):**

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Book {
        - title : String
        - author : String
        - ISBN : String
        - availableCopies : Integer
        - borrowedBy : Member
        - dueDate : Date
        + checkOut(m : Member) : Boolean
        + returnBook() : Boolean
        + isAvailable() : Boolean
    }

    class Member {
        - memberID : String
        - name : String
        - isActive : Boolean
        - borrowedBooks : List
        + borrowBook(b : Book) : Boolean
        + returnBook(b : Book) : Boolean
    }

    Book "0..*" -- "1" Member : currently borrowed by
    Member "1" -- "0..*" Book : has borrowed

    note for Book "checkOut Pre: availableCopies >= 1 AND m.isActive = true\ncheckOut Post: availableCopies' = availableCopies - 1\n             AND borrowedBy' = m"
```

- Correct attributes and types: 2 marks
- Correct operations with visibility: 2 marks
- Correct association with multiplicity: 2 marks
- Pre/postcondition note attached: 1 mark

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]*

**(a)** Explain the concept of **preconditions and postconditions** in operation specification. With a suitable example, illustrate the use of **prime notation** in expressing postconditions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a complete OMT operation specification for the `enroll(student : Student) : Boolean` operation of a `Course` class, where `Course` has `courseCode : String`, `courseName : String`, `maxStrength : Integer`, `currentStrength : Integer`, and `enrolledStudents : List[Student]`. Also draw the class diagram. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Pre/Postconditions (7 marks):**

A **precondition** is a predicate that must evaluate to `true` *before* an operation is invoked. The **caller** is responsible for ensuring the precondition holds. If the precondition is violated, the operation's behaviour is **undefined**.

A **postcondition** is a predicate that the operation itself guarantees to be `true` *after* it has completed successfully. The **implementer** (callee) is responsible for ensuring the postcondition holds.

The **prime notation** (`'`) distinguishes a variable's value *after* the operation from its value *before*:
- Unprimed: value *before* the operation.
- Primed: value *after* the operation.

**Example (3 marks for example):**

For an operation `increment(x : Integer) : void`:
- **Precondition:** `x < MAX_INT` (caller must ensure no overflow).
- **Postcondition:** `x' = x + 1` (operation guarantees the increment).

For an operation `enroll(s : Student) : void`:
- **Precondition:** `currentStrength < maxStrength`.
- **Postcondition:** `currentStrength' = currentStrength + 1 ∧ enrolledStudents' = enrolledStudents ∪ {s}`.

**(b) OMT Operation Specification for `enroll` (7 marks):**

- **Signature:** `+ enroll(s : Student) : Boolean` (1 mark)
- **Pre:** `s.isEligible = true ∧ currentStrength < maxStrength` (1 mark)
- **Post:** `(result = true ∧ currentStrength' = currentStrength + 1 ∧ s ∈ enrolledStudents')` **OR** `(result = false ∧ currentStrength' = currentStrength)` (2 marks)
- **Structured-English Body:** (2 marks)

> 1. If `not s.isEligible`, return `false`.
> 2. If `currentStrength >= maxStrength`, return `false`.
> 3. Add `s` to `enrolledStudents`.
> 4. Increment `currentStrength` by 1.
> 5. Return `true`.

- **Class Diagram:** (1 mark — credit for correct UML class box with compartments).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the prime notation** in postconditions (`balance' = balance − amount`) is the **#1 mark-losing mistake** in OMT papers. If you write `balance = balance − amount`, you have logically written `0 = amount`, which is nonsensical.
> 2. **Conflating "result" with "return value":** the conventional name for the return value in postconditions is `result`. Do not invent a new variable.
> 3. **Omitting the disjunction** between success and failure postconditions: a Boolean-returning operation must specify what happens in *both* cases.
> 4. **Mixing pre- and post-condition responsibilities:** if you write the pre as `balance > amount` (caller's job) and post as `balance = balance - amount` (callee's job), you are correct. If reversed, you lose marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2: Specifying Operations**

- ✅ An **operation** is the *specification* (declaration); a **method** is the *implementation* (code). One operation → many methods (polymorphism).
- ✅ A complete operation specification has **6 components**: name, parameter list, return type, visibility, preconditions, postconditions. Optionally: exception list and body.
- ✅ UML operation signature format: **`visibility name(param : Type) : ReturnType`**
- ✅ **Visibility symbols:** `+` public, `−` private, `#` protected, `~` package.
- ✅ **Class-scope (static) operations** are *underlined*; **abstract operations** are *italicized*.
- ✅ **Preconditions** use the *caller's* responsibility; **postconditions** use the *callee's* responsibility.
- ✅ **Prime notation** (`variable'`) denotes the *value after* the operation — *non-negotiable* in KTU answers.
- ✅ The contract triple is **`{Pre} operation {Post}`** — a Hoare-logic formulation.
- ✅ **Design by Contract (DbC)** by Bertrand Meyer operationalizes these ideas in the Eiffel language.
- ✅ Pre/postconditions are the basis of **unit-test assertions** in JUnit, pytest, etc.
- ✅ For a 14-mark question, expect: 3 marks signature + 4 marks pre/post + 4 marks body + 3 marks class diagram.
- ✅ Always include the **disjunction** `(success) ∨ (failure)` for Boolean-returning operations.
- ✅ Exception conditions may be expressed as **`raises ExceptionName`** (Eiffel style) — accepted by KTU examiners.
- ✅ The operation **body** should be in **structured English** with proper indentation, *Begin/End* blocks, and *if/else* branches.
- ✅ Use **direction qualifiers** (`in`, `out`, `inout`) on parameters when relevant — KTU awards an extra mark for this.
- ✅ **Mermaid diagram safeguards:** always use alphanumeric node IDs; never use `end` as a node name; use `note for` syntax for attached notes.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Constraints
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCNT604 - OBJECT ORIENTED MODELING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module - 2
> - **Topic:** Constraints

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (Module 2):** *Object Oriented Modeling and Design — Constraints, their types, notation, and application in UML diagrams.*

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

In **Object Oriented Modeling and Design (OOMD)**, a **Constraint** is a condition or a semantic restriction that is attached to a UML model element (such as a class, association, attribute, or operation) to specify the conditions under which the model is considered **valid, well-formed, and consistent**.

According to the **Object Management Group (OMG) UML 2.5 specification**, a constraint is formally defined as:

> *"A constraint is a condition or restriction expressed in text or in some formal language, which must be satisfied by the elements of the model at all times."*

In simpler academic terms, constraints act as the **"rules of the game"** for an object-oriented system. While classes, attributes, and associations describe the *structure* of the system, constraints describe the *behavioral and logical boundaries* within which the structure must operate.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Rules of a Cricket Match**
>
> Imagine a UML class diagram as the *playing field of a cricket match*. The class `Batsman` and class `Bowler` represent the players. An association links them. But the rules of the game — *"A batsman is declared 'out' if his score is exactly equal to 0 and he is caught"*, or *"A team's total runs cannot exceed 500 in a 50-over match"* — these are **constraints**.
>
> They are not visible structural elements (like a bat or a ball), but they strictly govern **how** the structural elements can be used. Similarly, in UML, constraints do not show up as boxes or lines; they are textual or formal rules attached to the model elements.

### 1.3 Why Are Constraints Important in OOMD?

| Aspect | Without Constraints | With Constraints |
|---|---|---|
| **Model Integrity** | Model is structurally complete but logically ambiguous | Model is structurally *and* semantically validated |
| **Ambiguity** | Same diagram interpreted differently by different developers | A single, unambiguous interpretation enforced |
| **Code Generation** | Tools cannot enforce business rules | Tools can auto-generate validation logic |
| **Communication** | Pure visuals are not enough for complex logic | Additional precision via formal language (OCL) |

### 1.4 UML Standard Constraint Notation

In UML diagrams, constraints are visually represented as **strings enclosed within curly braces `{}`**. These strings are placed adjacent to the element they constrain.

- **Example 1:** `{value > 0}` placed next to an attribute `age`.
- **Example 2:** `{ordered, readOnly}` placed next to an association.
- **Example 3:** `{subset}` placed next to a generalization to indicate that one set is a subset of another.

> [!TIP]
> **Key KTU Point:** Constraints are part of the **semantics layer** of UML, not the **structure layer**. They add meaning *on top of* the graphical notation. Remember this distinction for 3-mark and 14-mark KTU questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of UML Constraints

Constraints in UML can be classified based on **where they apply** and **how they are expressed**. The following structured breakdown is the most commonly asked taxonomy in KTU examinations.

#### **A. Based on the Element Being Constrained**

- **Attribute Constraints:** Restrict the value domain of an attribute.
  *Example:* `{age >= 18 and age <= 60}` for an `Employee` class.
- **Association Constraints:** Govern the relationship between two classes.
  *Example:* `{ordered}`, `{bag}`, `{sequence}`.
- **Class Constraints (Invariants):** Apply to the entire class object.
  *Example:* `{balance = credit - debit}` for an `Account` class.
- **Operation Constraints:** Restrict the parameters or return values of a method.
  *Example:* `{pre-condition: x > 0}` before a `withdraw(x)` operation.
- **Generalization Constraints:** Refine inheritance rules.
  *Example:* `{overlapping, disjoint}` on a generalization set.

#### **B. Based on UML Pre-Defined Standard Keywords**

The UML standard provides a set of pre-defined constraint keywords. These are the most frequently tested ones in KTU.

| Keyword | Meaning | Example Application |
|---|---|---|
| **`complete`** | Generalization hierarchy has no more subclasses | `{complete}` on a generalization with all known subclasses |
| **`incomplete`** | More subclasses may be added later | `{incomplete}` on an open-ended hierarchy |
| **`disjoint`** | An instance can belong to only one subclass | Used in inheritance where roles don't overlap |
| **`overlapping`** | An instance can belong to multiple subclasses | Used in multiple classification |
| **`ordered`** | Elements in a multi-valued slot have an order | `{ordered}` on a multi-association |
| **`bag`** | Multi-valued slot, duplicates allowed, no order | `{bag}` for an unordered collection |
| **`sequence`** | Ordered collection, no duplicates | `{sequence}` for a strict list |
| **`xor`** | Exactly one of the relationships holds | `{xor}` between two associations |
| **`subset`** | One set is a subset of another | `{subset}` on a refinement association |
| **`redefines`** | Redefining an inherited feature | `{redefines}` for polymorphic replacement |
| **`readOnly`** | Attribute cannot be modified after initialization | `{readOnly}` on a constant |

### 2.2 The OCL (Object Constraint Language) Connection

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **OCL (Object Constraint Language)** is the **declarative, formal, side-effect-free** language standardized by the OMG specifically for expressing constraints in UML. It is a key 14-mark topic in KTU Module 2.

OCL is **not a programming language**. It is purely a *constraint specification* language used to:
1. Specify **invariants** of classes and types.
2. Specify **pre-conditions** and **post-conditions** on operations.
3. Specify **guards** on messages and transitions in state machines.
4. Specify **navigation expressions** through associations.

### 2.3 Types of Constraints in OCL (KTU High-Yield Classification)

The following is a **high-priority table** for KTU 2024 Scheme:

| Constraint Type | Symbol/Keyword | When Evaluated | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Invariant** | `context ... inv:` | Always true for the entire lifetime of the object | `context Account inv: balance >= 0` |
| **Pre-condition** | `pre:` | Must be true *before* the operation executes | `context Account::withdraw(amount: Real) pre: amount > 0` |
| **Post-condition** | `post:` | Must be true *after* the operation completes | `post: balance = balance@pre - amount` |
| **Guard Condition** | `guard:` | Used in state machines to control transitions | `[balance >= amount] / withdraw()` |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet — OCL Constraint Patterns

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize these OCL constraint templates. They form the backbone of any 7-mark OCL question in KTU.**

| Scenario | OCL Template | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Value Range | `context Type inv: self.attribute >= low and self.attribute <= high` | Restricts attribute to a numeric range |
| Collection Size | `context Type inv: self.association->size() <= N` | Restricts number of linked objects |
| For-All Quantifier | `context Type inv: self.collection->forAll(x \vert x.salary > 0)` | True if condition holds for every element |
| Exists Quantifier | `context Type inv: self.collection->exists(x \vert x.isActive = true)` | True if at least one element satisfies |
| Implies Operator | `context Type inv: self.isSenior implies self.salary > 50000` | Conditional logic constraint |
| Pre/Post Pair | `pre: x > 0` / `post: result = self.value * 2` | Contract specification |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Alert:** OCL expressions are **side-effect free**. This means an OCL expression can NEVER modify the state of the system. It can only *read* and *evaluate*. This is a common 3-mark KTU conceptual question.

### 2.5 Generalization Set Constraints (High-Yield for 14-Mark Questions)

When a superclass has multiple subclasses, UML allows you to specify whether the subclasses are *disjoint* or *overlapping* and whether the set is *complete* or *incomplete*.

- **Disjoint + Complete** → A *partition* of the superclass. Every instance belongs to exactly one subclass, and no more subclasses will be added.
- **Overlapping + Incomplete** → A *flexible* hierarchy. Instances can belong to multiple subclasses, and new ones may be added.

**Example:** A `Person` class with subclasses `Student` and `Employee`. A person is *either* a student *or* an employee (disjoint) but the set of roles is *complete* for this university system.

> **[K1 Recall]:** UML officially denotes these as `{disjoint, complete}`, `{overlapping, incomplete}`, etc. They are written on the generalization set (a small dashed triangle connecting the subclasses).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix: OCL Constraint Derivation for a Bank Account

> [!NOTE]
> **Scenario (Standard KTU 14-Mark Scenario):** A `BankAccount` class has attributes `accountNumber : String`, `balance : Real`, and `interestRate : Real`. Methods include `deposit(amount : Real)` and `withdraw(amount : Real)`. Derive OCL constraints.

**Step 1: Identify the entities and their state.**
- The `BankAccount` object has a state defined by its `balance` at any given moment.

**Step 2: Identify the natural invariants.**
- The balance should never be negative (no overdraft allowed).
- The interest rate should be between 0% and 100%.

**Step 3: Identify pre-conditions for operations.**
- A `deposit` operation should only be called with a positive amount.
- A `withdraw` operation should only be called with an amount less than or equal to the current balance.

**Step 4: Identify post-conditions for operations.**
- After `deposit(amount)`, the new balance equals the old balance plus the amount.
- After `withdraw(amount)`, the new balance equals the old balance minus the amount.

**Step 5: Translate to OCL formally.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{context BankAccount} \\
&\text{inv: self.balance >= 0} \\
&\text{inv: self.interestRate > 0 and self.interestRate < 100} \\
&\text{context BankAccount::deposit(amount: Real)} \\
&\text{pre: amount > 0} \\
&\text{post: self.balance = self.balance@pre + amount} \\
&\text{context BankAccount::withdraw(amount: Real)} \\
&\text{pre: amount > 0 and amount <= self.balance} \\
&\text{post: self.balance = self.balance@pre - amount}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic Explanation (Text Row):** The `@pre` keyword in OCL refers to the value of the attribute *before* the operation was executed. This is essential for writing correct post-conditions because the post-condition is evaluated *after* the state has changed.

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation of Multiplicity Constraints

**Scenario:** A `Department` class has a "manages" association with a `Manager` class. The business rule is: *"Each Department has exactly one Manager, and each Manager manages exactly one Department."*

**Step 1:** Determine the lower bound (minimum).
- A department cannot exist without a manager → lower bound = 1.

**Step 2:** Determine the upper bound (maximum).
- A department cannot have more than one manager → upper bound = 1.

**Step 3:** Apply the same logic from the `Manager` side.
- A manager is always assigned to a department → 1..1 on both sides.

**Step 4:** Represent this in UML.

$$
\begin{aligned}
&Department \xleftrightarrow{1..1} \text{manages} \xleftrightarrow{1..1} Manager
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic Explanation (Text Row):** Multiplicity is a special form of constraint. It is built into UML, so we use the standard `lower..upper` notation directly on the association ends. If a multiplicity is not a default of `0..*` or `1`, it should always be explicitly stated.

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Derivation of a XOR Constraint

**Scenario:** A `Client` class is connected to a `SavingsAccount` class AND a `CurrentAccount` class. The business rule is: *"A client can have EITHER a SavingsAccount OR a CurrentAccount, but NOT both at the same time."*

**Step 1:** Identify the conflicting associations.
- `Client` to `SavingsAccount` (call it association A).
- `Client` to `CurrentAccount` (call it association B).

**Step 2:** Apply the XOR operator.
- The constraint is exactly that A XOR B is true.

**Step 3:** UML notation.

$$
\begin{aligned}
&Client \xrightarrow{\text{has} \{xor\}} SavingsAccount \\
&Client \xrightarrow{\text{has} \{xor\}} CurrentAccount
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic Explanation (Text Row):** The `{xor}` constraint placed on one or both association ends tells the UML reader that the two relationships are mutually exclusive. The model is invalid if a `Client` instance is linked to *both* types of accounts simultaneously.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Constraint Type vs. UML Diagram

| UML Diagram | Where Constraints Can Be Applied | Example Constraint |
|---|---|---|
| **Class Diagram** | Class name, attributes, operations, associations, generalizations | `{balance >= 0}`, `{disjoint}`, `{xor}` |
| **Use Case Diagram** | Use case relationships, actor definitions | `{include}`, `{extend}` |
| **Sequence Diagram** | Messages, time constraints | `{deadline: 5 sec}` |
| **State Machine Diagram** | Transitions, states | `[balance > 0] / generateReceipt` |
| **Activity Diagram** | Decision nodes, swimlanes | `[isApproved] / processOrder` |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Class Diagram with Constraints (KTU Bank Account Example)

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class BankAccount {
        -accountNumber : String
        -balance : Real
        -interestRate : Real
        +deposit(amount : Real) Real
        +withdraw(amount : Real) Real
    }

    class SavingsAccount {
        -minBalance : Real
    }

    class CurrentAccount {
        -overdraftLimit : Real
    }

    class Customer {
        -customerId : String
        -name : String
    }

    BankAccount <|-- SavingsAccount
    BankAccount <|-- CurrentAccount

    Customer "1" --o "0..*" BankAccount : owns

    note for BankAccount "OCL Invariant: balance >= 0\nOCL Invariant: interestRate > 0"
    note for Customer "OCL Invariant: self.ownedAccounts->forAll(a \vert a.balance >= 0)"
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation Guide for the Above Diagram:**
> - The `note for BankAccount` block visually represents the OCL invariant attached to the class. In a real KTU answer sheet, this would be written in text form below the diagram.
> - The `1` and `0..*` near the `owns` association represent **multiplicity constraints**.
> - The `<|--` arrows from `SavingsAccount` and `CurrentAccount` to `BankAccount` represent inheritance. A `{disjoint, complete}` constraint should be added on this generalization set.

### 4.2 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture: Constraint Enforcement Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[UML Class Diagram] --> B[Identify Model Elements]
    B --> C[Apply Built-in UML Constraints]
    C --> D[Apply Multiplicity Constraints]
    D --> E[Apply OCL Constraints]
    E --> F[Validate Model Integrity]
    F --> G{All Constraints Satisfied?}
    G -->|Yes| H[Valid UML Model]
    G -->|No| I[Constraint Violation Report]
    I --> J[Refine Model or Loosen Constraint]
    J --> B
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to Read This Diagram:** The architecture shows the *constraint enforcement lifecycle* in a typical UML modeling tool (like Rational Rose, Enterprise Architect, or Papyrus). The tool iterates over the model, applies each layer of constraints, and produces a validation report.

### 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram with Guard Constraints

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Active
    Active --> Suspended : [overdueDays > 30] / suspendAccount
    Suspended --> Active : [paymentReceived = true] / reactivateAccount
    Active --> Closed : [customerRequest = true] / closeAccount
    Suspended --> Closed : [overdueDays > 90] / forceCloseAccount
    Closed --> [*]
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** The expressions inside `[ ]` are **guard constraints** (a type of constraint). They determine under what condition a transition can fire. The expressions after `/` are **actions**, which are not constraints but effects. KTU often tests the difference between the two.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Model Question Paper Style]**

**Q1. Define a constraint in UML. List any FOUR pre-defined UML constraint keywords with their meanings.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **constraint** in UML is a semantic condition or restriction that must hold true for a model element. It is expressed as a text string inside curly braces `{}` and is attached to a UML element.

Four pre-defined UML constraint keywords:
1. **{disjoint}** — Indicates that an object of the superclass can belong to only one of the subclasses.
2. **{complete}** — Indicates that no more subclasses will be added to the generalization set.
3. **{ordered}** — Indicates that the elements in a multi-valued association maintain a specific order.
4. **{xor}** — Indicates that exactly one of the two relationships holds between the associated classes.
*(Any four correctly explained keywords fetch full 3 marks.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Expected Pattern]**

**Q2. What is OCL? Why is it used in UML? List any TWO key features of OCL.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**OCL (Object Constraint Language)** is a formal, declarative language developed by OMG for expressing constraints and queries on UML models.

**Why it is used:**
- OCL is used to add **precise, unambiguous, machine-readable** constraints to UML diagrams that cannot be expressed visually.

**Two key features:**
1. OCL is **side-effect free** — an OCL expression can never modify the model state.
2. OCL is **declarative** — it specifies *what* must hold, not *how* to compute it.
*(Mentioning `formal`, `declarative`, `type-checked` are also valid.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Pattern, Module 2 Full Question]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *UML Constraints + OCL Specification*

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Pattern, Module 2 Full Question]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the different types of constraints in UML with suitable examples for each. Also explain how the `{xor}` and `{disjoint}` constraints differ from each other.

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

Constraints in UML are semantic restrictions that must hold true for the model to be valid. They are represented as `{text}` notation attached to model elements.

**Types of UML Constraints:**

1. **Attribute Constraints** — Restrict the value of an attribute.
   *Example:* `{weight > 0}` attached to the `weight` attribute of a `Luggage` class.

2. **Association Constraints** — Restrict the nature of the relationship.
   *Example:* `{ordered}` indicates the association maintains a specific order.

3. **Multiplicity Constraints** — Restrict the number of instances in a relationship.
   *Example:* `1..*` means at least one and possibly many.

4. **Generalization Constraints** — Restrict inheritance semantics.
   *Example:* `{disjoint, complete}` means a superclass instance can belong to exactly one subclass, and no more subclasses will be added.

5. **OCL Constraints** — Formal constraints written in the Object Constraint Language.
   *Example:* `context Account inv: balance >= 0`.

**Difference between `{xor}` and `{disjoint}`:**

| Aspect | `{xor}` | `{disjoint}` |
|---|---|---|
| **Applied to** | Associations (between two or more relations) | Generalization sets (inheritance) |
| **Meaning** | Exactly one of the given associations holds | An object can belong to only one subclass |
| **Scope** | Between two specific relationships | Across all subclasses of a parent |

**(b) [7 Marks]** Consider a `Library` system. A `Member` can borrow `0..*` `Book` objects, but each `Book` can be borrowed by at most 1 `Member`. Also, a `Book` is either a `PrintedBook` or an `EBook`, but not both. Draw the UML class diagram and specify the OCL constraints.

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**UML Class Diagram (Textual Representation):**

- `Member` class with attributes `memberId : Integer`, `name : String`.
- `Book` class (abstract) with attributes `title : String`, `ISBN : String`.
- `PrintedBook` and `EBook` are subclasses of `Book`.
- Association `borrows` between `Member` and `Book` with multiplicity `0..*` on the `Member` side and `0..1` on the `Book` side.
- A `{disjoint, complete}` constraint on the `Book` hierarchy.

**OCL Constraints:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{context Book} \\
&\text{inv: -- (Book is abstract, this is the root of the hierarchy)} \\
&\text{context Book} \\
&\text{inv: PrintedBook.allInstances->size() + EBook.allInstances->size() = Book.allInstances->size()} \\
&\text{context Member} \\
&\text{inv: self.borrows->size() <= 5} \\
&\text{context Book} \\
&\text{inv: self.borrower->size() <= 1}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic Explanation (Text Row):** The first OCL constraint enforces that every `Book` is either printed or electronic (completeness). The second limits the number of books a member can borrow. The third enforces the "at most one borrower" rule per book.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *OCL Invariants + Pre/Post Conditions*

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Pattern, Module 2 Full Question]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain Invariants, Pre-conditions, and Post-conditions in OCL. How does OCL handle the value of an attribute before and after an operation?

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

1. **Invariant (`inv:`):**
   - A condition that must be true for an object *at all times* during its lifetime.
   - It is evaluated every time the object's state changes.
   - *Example:* `context Account inv: self.balance >= 0`

2. **Pre-condition (`pre:`):**
   - A condition that must be true *before* an operation is invoked.
   - The caller of the operation is responsible for ensuring the pre-condition holds.
   - *Example:* `context Account::deposit(amount: Real) pre: amount > 0`

3. **Post-condition (`post:`):**
   - A condition that must be true *after* an operation completes successfully.
   - The implementing method is responsible for ensuring the post-condition holds.
   - *Example:* `post: self.balance = self.balance@pre + amount`

**Handling Attribute Values Before and After an Operation:**

OCL provides the special keyword **`@pre`** to refer to the value of an attribute *before* the operation was executed. This is essential for writing post-conditions that compare the "old" and "new" states of an object.

- `self.balance@pre` → balance before the operation.
- `self.balance` (in a post-condition) → balance after the operation.

This mechanism is called the **post-condition state reference** and is critical for accurate specification of behavioral contracts.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A `Course` class has a `maxCapacity : Integer` and a `registeredStudents : Set(Student)` collection. The rule is: *"The number of registered students can never exceed maxCapacity, and every registered student must have a CGPA >= 5.0."* Write the OCL constraint for this. Also explain the difference between `->forAll()` and `->exists()` in OCL with an example.

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**OCL Constraint:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{context Course} \\
&\text{inv: self.registeredStudents->size() <= self.maxCapacity} \\
&\text{inv: self.registeredStudents->forAll(s \vert s.cgpa >= 5.0)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic Explanation (Text Row):**
> - The first invariant enforces the capacity rule by comparing the collection size to the maximum capacity.
> - The second invariant iterates over *every* student in the collection and verifies their CGPA. The keyword `->forAll()` ensures the condition holds for all elements.
> - The vertical bar `|` is the *lambda separator* in OCL, separating the iterator variable from the body.

**Difference between `->forAll()` and `->exists()`:**

| Aspect | `->forAll(x \vert condition(x))` | `->exists(x \vert condition(x))` |
|---|---|---|
| **Quantification** | Universal (∀) | Existential (∃) |
| **Logic** | True if condition is true for *every* element | True if condition is true for *at least one* element |
| **Empty Collection** | Returns `true` (vacuously satisfied) | Returns `false` |
| **Example** | `students->forAll(s \vert s.age >= 18)` | `students->exists(s \vert s.name = 'Anu')` |

In the CGPA example, `->forAll()` is the correct quantifier because *every* registered student must satisfy the CGPA condition, not just at least one.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not use `|` inside a markdown table** in your answer sheet — KTU's answer booklet format may break. Use the OCL `|` keyword only inside OCL code blocks, but for any tabular representation of OCL, use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX.
> 2. **Do not confuse pre-condition with post-condition.** A common mistake is writing a post-condition that uses the *old* value of an attribute without the `@pre` keyword. This will cost you 1–2 marks in a 7-mark sub-question.
> 3. **Do not use OCL like a programming language.** OCL has no `if-then-else` *statements* in the procedural sense (it does have `if-then-else` *expressions*). OCL is purely declarative.
> 4. **Do not forget to specify the `context`** of every OCL expression. Every OCL constraint must begin with `context ClassName` or it will be marked as incomplete.
> 5. **Do not write `{disjoint}` and `{complete}` interchangeably.** They are independent constraints. A set can be `{disjoint, incomplete}` or `{overlapping, complete}`. All four combinations are valid UML notations.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this section for last-minute KTU revision. Each point is a guaranteed high-yield item.**

- **Definition:** A **constraint** is a semantic restriction on a UML model element, written in `{curly braces}`.
- **Visual Form:** `{constraint text}` is placed near the element being constrained.
- **OCL:** **Object Constraint Language** — the formal language for writing machine-readable constraints in UML.
- **OCL is side-effect free** — it cannot modify the system state.
- **Three main OCL constraint types:**
  * `inv:` → Invariant (always true for an object).
  * `pre:` → Pre-condition (true before an operation).
  * `post:` → Post-condition (true after an operation).
- **`@pre` keyword** → refers to the *pre-execution* value of an attribute inside a post-condition.
- **Quantifiers:**
  * `->forAll(x \vert P(x))` → Universal (∀). True for all elements.
  * `->exists(x \vert P(x))` → Existential (∃). True for at least one element.
- **Built-in UML constraint keywords to memorize:** `complete`, `incomplete`, `disjoint`, `overlapping`, `ordered`, `bag`, `sequence`, `xor`, `subset`, `redefines`, `readOnly`, `frozen`, `addOnly`.
- **Generalization Constraints:**
  * `{disjoint}` → An object belongs to *only one* subclass.
  * `{overlapping}` → An object can belong to *multiple* subclasses.
  * `{complete}` → The hierarchy is *closed* (no more subclasses to be added).
  * `{incomplete}` → The hierarchy is *open* (new subclasses may be added).
- **Multiplicity** is a form of constraint: `lower..upper` (e.g., `1..*` means one or more).
- **`{xor}` is used between two or more associations**, not between generalization subclasses.
- **Guards in state machines** are written inside `[ ]` (e.g., `[balance > 0]`).
- **OCL Rule:** Every constraint must begin with `context ClassName` to specify the *type* on which the constraint applies.
- **KTU 14-Mark Pattern:** 7 marks for *concepts* (definitions + types + differences) + 7 marks for an *applied scenario* (drawing the class diagram + writing OCL constraints).
- **Real-world tools that use OCL:** IBM Rational Rose, Sparx Enterprise Architect, Eclipse Papyrus, Visual Paradigm.
- **Common KTU error to avoid:** Writing `self.balance = balance` in a post-condition. The correct form is `self.balance = self.balance@pre + amount`.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: A Sample Functional Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCNT604 - OBJECT ORIENTED MODELING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module - 2
> - **Topic:** A Sample Functional Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Sample Functional Model** in Object-Oriented Modeling and Design (OOMD) is a worked-out, illustrative instantiation of the functional view of a software system that demonstrates how **Data Flow Diagrams (DFDs)**, **process specifications (PSPECs)**, **use cases**, and the **data dictionary** are constructed, refined, and linked together for a representative problem domain. The classical pedagogical example adopted by Rumbaugh, Jacobson, and Booch (the "Three Amigos" of UML) is the **Automated Teller Machine (ATM)** problem, which serves as the canonical *sample functional model* in KTU board-referenced OOMD literature.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme (PBCNT604) syllabus, a functional model describes the **computations performed by the system** and the **flow of data** between processes, without committing to any specific object structure (object model) or temporal behaviour (dynamic model). The *sample* makes the abstract methodology concrete.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 2):** The sample functional model demonstrates the **end-to-end construction pipeline**: (1) identifying actors & use cases, (2) drawing a context-level DFD, (3) decomposing into Level-0 and Level-1 DFDs, (4) writing process specifications in **structured English / pseudo-code / pre-post conditions**, and (5) compiling the **data dictionary**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a railway reservation system. The *object model* tells you the trains, seats, and passengers exist (the nouns). The *dynamic model* tells you the sequence of events (a passenger books → payment → seat locked). The **functional model** tells you *what work is done* on data as it flows from one place to another: the reservation clerk receives a request, the fare is computed, the availability is checked, the ticket is generated. The *sample* functional model is essentially a **pre-solved blueprint** showing students how to draw that "flow of work" for a known system (ATM) before they attempt one of their own.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Significance:** The sample is *not* the answer to a problem — it is a *worked example* used as a template. KTU examiners expect students to *mimic* this construction style (top-down DFD decomposition + process narrative) for any new system in Part B questions.

### 1.3 The Canonical Sample: ATM System

For KTU exams, the textbook sample is invariably the **ATM (Automated Teller Machine)** system. The key entities, actors, and data flows are:

- **External Entities:** *Customer*, *Bank Account Database*
- **Major Processes:** *Verify PIN*, *Check Balance*, *Dispense Cash*, *Print Receipt*, *Update Account*
- **Data Stores:** *Account Records (D1)*, *Transaction Log (D2)*
- **Data Flows:** *PIN*, *Amount Requested*, *Balance*, *Cash*, *Receipt Text*

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Mnemonic for Functional Model Components — "4C-D-P"**
> **C**ontext → **C**onstraints → **C**ontent (data) → **C**ontrol (flow)
> **D**FDs, **D**ata dictionary, **P**rocess specifications
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Pillars of the Sample Functional Model

The sample functional model rests on four theoretical pillars that the KTU examiner expects students to enumerate:

1. **Top-Down Decomposition Principle**
   - Begin with the *most abstract* representation (Context Diagram, Level -1).
   - Repeatedly explode each process into sub-processes (Level 0 → Level 1 → Level 2 …).
   - Halts when each sub-process performs a single, well-defined, atomic function (one that can be described in 1–2 sentences of structured English).

2. **Balancing Principle (Consistency Between Levels)**
   - The *inputs* and *outputs* of a process at level *N* must exactly match the *inputs* and *outputs* of its parent decomposition at level *N-1*.
   - This is the formal property that makes a DFD hierarchy **traceable** and **verifiable**.

3. **Process Specification (PSPEC) Rule**
   - A process is *decomposable* if and only if it has more than one input OR more than one output, OR has its inputs/outputs at different abstraction levels, OR performs a non-trivial transformation.
   - Every process at the **lowest level** of a DFD receives a written **process specification** (structured English, pre/post conditions, or pseudo-code).

4. **Data Dictionary as the Single Source of Truth**
   - Every data flow, data store, and external entity in the DFD must be **defined** in the data dictionary.
   - Entries use **=** for definition, **+** for concatenation, **[ ]** for optional, **{ }** for iteration, **|** for selection.

### 2.2 ATM Functional Model – The Three Levels

#### **Level -1: Context Diagram**
A single process bubble (the "ATM System") with the two external entities (*Customer* and *Bank*) connected by high-level data flows. No data stores, no internal structure.

#### **Level 0: Top-Level DFD**
Decomposes the ATM into 5 major processes:

| Process ID | Process Name | Function |
|------------|----------------|--------------|
| P1 | Verify Customer | Validate card + PIN |
| P2 | Handle Transaction | Branch into Withdraw / Deposit / Inquiry |
| P3 | Dispense Cash | Physical cash dispensing |
| P4 | Update Account | Modify account balance |
| P5 | Print Receipt | Generate audit/receipt text |

Data stores introduced: *D1 = Account File*, *D2 = Transaction Log*.

#### **Level 1: Refined DFDs (Selective Decomposition)**
Only processes that still perform non-trivial work are exploded. For example, *Handle Transaction (P2)* is exploded into:
- P2.1 — *Process Withdrawal Request*
- P2.2 — *Process Deposit Request*
- P2.3 — *Process Balance Inquiry*
- P2.4 — *Validate Sufficient Funds*

### 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why start with a context diagram?** It fixes the **system boundary** — a critical first decision that prevents *scope creep*.
- **Why decompose top-down?** It mirrors the way human cognition organizes complex systems (Miller's Law — 7±2 chunks).
- **Why a data dictionary?** It eliminates ambiguity; without it, the data flow "Amount" could mean *requested*, *dispensed*, *debited*, or *credited*.
- **Why PSPECs in structured English?** They are *executable enough* for analysts to verify correctness, yet *readable enough* for non-technical domain experts to validate.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet (Notational Symbols)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Expectation:** A correctly labelled DFD with proper symbols is worth **2–3 marks** standalone. Memorize the four symbols below as a *table* — the marginal effort is high and the yield is guaranteed.

| DFD Symbol | Geometric Form | Semantic Meaning | Example in ATM Sample |
|------------|----------------|------------------|------------------------|
| External Entity | Rectangle | Source or sink of data outside the system | Customer, Bank |
| Process | Rounded Rectangle / Circle | Function transforming data | Verify Customer, Dispense Cash |
| Data Store | Open-ended rectangle / two horizontal lines | Repository of data for later retrieval | D1: Account File |
| Data Flow | Directed arrow | Movement of data | PIN, Cash, Balance |

| PSPEC Notation | Meaning | Example |
|----------------|---------|---------|
| `=` | defined as | `PIN = 4{digit}4` |
| `+` | concatenation | `Receipt = Header + Body + Footer` |
| `[]` | optional | `ChequeNo [ = 6{digit}6 ]` |
| `{}` | iteration | `{Item}+` = one or more items |
| `\|` | selection (one of) | `TransType = Withdraw \| Deposit \| Inquiry` |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The sample functional model underpins nearly every **structured systems analysis** engagement in the IT industry: 
- Used in **requirements elicitation** workshops (consulting firms like TCS, Infosys).
- Forms the foundation for **DFD-driven test case generation** (each data flow = a test, each process = a unit test).
- Serves as the **bridge document** between business stakeholders (who read PSPECs) and developers (who read object models).
- Directly translates into **structured query design**: each data store maps to a database schema; each data flow maps to a table column.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Construction of the Sample ATM Functional Model

This section provides the **exhaustive, traceable derivation** of the sample functional model — i.e., the procedure a KTU examiner expects a student to *narrate* when answering a "Construct a functional model for X" question.

### Step 0 — Establish the System Boundary

**Definition.** Identify the system under design and pin down its external actors.

$$\text{System} \;\equiv\; \text{ATM Software + Hardware Console}$$
$$\text{External Actors} \;\in\; \{\text{Customer}, \text{Bank Host System}\}$$

The boundary determines what is *internal* (processes, stores) versus *external* (entities, sensors, actors).

### Step 1 — Identify Use Cases (Bridge to Functional View)

Use cases are the *behavioural* anchor for the functional model. The ATM sample exhibits the following canonical use cases:

| Use Case ID | Use Case Name | Primary Actor | Trigger |
|-------------|----------------|---------------|---------|
| UC-01 | Withdraw Cash | Customer | Card insertion + PIN |
| UC-02 | Deposit Cheque/Cash | Customer | Selection of "Deposit" |
| UC-03 | Check Balance | Customer | Selection of "Balance" |
| UC-04 | Transfer Funds | Customer | Selection of "Transfer" |
| UC-05 | Reject Invalid PIN | Customer | 3 failed PIN attempts |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Some students confuse *use case diagram* (a UML behavioural diagram, part of the dynamic model) with the *DFD* (part of the functional model). The functional model **uses** use cases as input but **does not equal** them. DFDs are the formal deliverable.

### Step 2 — Draw the Context Diagram (Level -1)

A single process symbol represents the **entire system**. Two external entities connect to it. Only the highest-level data flows are shown.

**Conventions applied:**
- Process = rounded rectangle, label "0 — ATM System".
- External entities = rectangles, labels "Customer" and "Bank".
- Data flows: `Card`, `PIN`, `Request`, `Cash`, `Receipt`, `Transaction Record` ↔ `Bank`.

### Step 3 — Decompose to Level 0

The system is exploded into its **top-level processes**. Each top-level process corresponds to one *logical stage* of the ATM workflow.

**Decomposition rule applied:**
- The system is split along the *natural life-cycle of an ATM transaction*: **Identification → Service Selection → Service Execution → Reconciliation → Receipt Generation**.

The result is the *Level 0 DFD* with processes P1–P5 (as enumerated in §2.2).

### Step 4 — Draw Data Stores

Introduce data stores **only when the system must remember something between events**. For ATM:

$$D_1 = \text{Account File} \quad\longleftrightarrow\quad P_1,\;P_2,\;P_4$$
$$D_2 = \text{Transaction Log} \quad\longleftrightarrow\quad P_4,\;P_5$$

The data store `D1` is *read* by P1 (verify) and P2 (select), and *written* by P4 (update). The data store `D2` is *written* by P4 and *read* by P5 (for the receipt).

### Step 5 — Verify Level Balancing

**Balancing Equation.** For each process P at level *N-1* that is decomposed into child processes $\{P_i\}$ at level *N*:

$$\text{Flows}_{\text{in}}(P) \;=\; \bigcup_{i=1}^{k} \text{Flows}_{\text{in}}(P_i)$$
$$\text{Flows}_{\text{out}}(P) \;=\; \bigcup_{i=1}^{k} \text{Flows}_{\text{out}}(P_i)$$

This guarantees that the *external contract* of the parent process is preserved by its children — the **balancing principle** of Yourdon-DeMarco DFD methodology.

### Step 6 — Decompose Non-Atomic Processes to Level 1

The process `P2 — Handle Transaction` is *non-atomic* because:
- It has 3 input branches (Withdrawal / Deposit / Inquiry).
- It performs a non-trivial control decision (which sub-service).

Therefore, P2 is exploded into:
- **P2.1:** Validate Withdrawal Request
- **P2.2:** Validate Deposit Request
- **P2.3:** Retrieve Balance
- **P2.4:** Check Funds Availability

Processes P1, P3, P4, P5 are deemed *atomic* (each performs a single, unambiguous transformation) and thus receive **process specifications** instead of further decomposition.

### Step 7 — Write Process Specifications (PSPECs)

For each *atomic* process, write a PSPEC using **structured English** with **pre-conditions** and **post-conditions**.

**Template Applied:**

$$
\text{PSPEC}(P_i) \;=\; \langle \text{Name}(P_i),\; \text{Inputs},\; \text{Outputs},\; \text{Pre},\; \text{Post},\; \text{Logic} \rangle
$$

**Example — PSPEC for P1: Verify Customer**

| Field | Value |
|-------|-------|
| Name | Verify Customer |
| Inputs | Card Number, PIN |
| Outputs | Validated Customer ID / Error Flag |
| Pre-condition | Card has been read from physical slot |
| Post-condition | A validated customer record exists in working memory, OR an error flag is set |
| Logic | *Structured English*: IF PIN matches account PIN in D1 THEN return Validated ID ELSE increment retry counter; IF retry > 3 THEN retain card, emit alarm |

**Example — PSPEC for P3: Dispense Cash**

| Field | Value |
|-------|-------|
| Name | Dispense Cash |
| Inputs | Approved Amount |
| Outputs | Physical Cash Bundle |
| Pre-condition | Approved Amount > 0 AND funds available |
| Post-condition | Cash dispenser holds 0 currency notes; transaction recorded as `DISPENSED` |
| Logic | Count notes from highest denomination downward; release bundle to customer; emit "Cash Taken" sensor signal |

### Step 8 — Compile the Data Dictionary

Every data flow, data store, and external entity is defined formally.

**Selected entries:**

$$
\text{PIN} \;=\; 4\{\text{digit}\}4
$$
$$
\text{Amount} \;=\; \text{Requested Amount} \mid \text{Dispensed Amount} \mid \text{Debited Amount}
$$
$$
\text{Receipt} \;=\; \text{Header} + \text{Body} + \text{Footer}
$$
$$
\text{Header} \;=\; \text{Date} + \text{Time} + \text{ATM ID}
$$
$$
\text{TransType} \;=\; \text{Withdraw} \mid \text{Deposit} \mid \text{Inquiry} \mid \text{Transfer}
$$

### Step 9 — Add Constraints (Non-Functional Annotations)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Constraints Matter for KTU 2024:** A functional model *without* constraints is treated as incomplete. Examiners explicitly deduct 1 mark for missing constraints.

The ATM sample carries the following constraints:

- C1 — PIN verification must complete in ≤ **2 seconds**.
- C2 — Card is retained after **3** consecutive invalid PIN attempts.
- C3 — Cash dispenser must contain a **minimum of 200 notes per cassette** at the start of business day.
- C4 — All transactions are logged to `D2` with **timestamp + ATM ID** for audit.

### Step 10 — Cross-Validate Against the Object Model

The final step of the sample is to verify that every process in the functional model corresponds to at least one method/operation in the object model. The mapping is:

| Functional Process | Object-Model Operation |
|--------------------|--------------------------|
| P1 — Verify Customer | `Customer.verifyPIN()` |
| P2.1 — Validate Withdrawal | `Account.withdraw(amount)` |
| P2.3 — Retrieve Balance | `Account.getBalance()` |
| P4 — Update Account | `Account.debit()` / `Account.credit()` |
| P5 — Print Receipt | `ReceiptPrinter.print()` |

This **cross-link** is what makes the OMT methodology (Rumbaugh) a true *object-oriented* approach: function, structure, and behaviour are *consistent views of the same system*.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Step Checklist (for KTU answers):**
> 1. Context diagram (1 mark)
> 2. Level 0 DFD with 4–6 processes (3 marks)
> 3. One Level 1 decomposition (2 marks)
> 4. Two PSPECs in structured English (3 marks)
> 5. Data dictionary (2 marks)
> 6. Constraints (1 mark)
> 7. Cross-link to object model (2 marks)
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

The diagrams below use **Mermaid** and conform to the Alpha-Rule safeguard. Node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `p1`, `dStore1`, `extCustomer`).

### 4.1 Context Diagram (Level -1) — The ATM System Boundary

```mermaid
graph LR
    extCustomer[Customer]
    extBank[Bank Host System]
    sys0["0 ATM System"]

    extCustomer -- Card + PIN + Request --> sys0
    extCustomer -- Cash + Receipt --> extCustomer
    sys0 -- Transaction Record --> extBank
    extBank -- Account Status --> sys0
```

**Interpretation:** The single bubble `ATM System` represents the entire machine (software + console). Two external entities — `Customer` and `Bank Host System` — are connected by high-level flows only.

### 4.2 Level 0 DFD — Decomposed ATM Processes

```mermaid
graph LR
    extCustomer[Customer]
    extBank[Bank Host System]
    p1["1 Verify Customer"]
    p2["2 Handle Transaction"]
    p3["3 Dispense Cash"]
    p4["4 Update Account"]
    p5["5 Print Receipt"]
    dStore1[(D1 Account File)]
    dStore2[(D2 Transaction Log)]

    extCustomer -- Card + PIN --> p1
    p1 -- Validated ID --> p2
    extCustomer -- Service Selection --> p2
    p2 -- Approved Amount --> p3
    p2 -- Debit/Credit Request --> p4
    p4 -- Updated Balance --> p2
    p3 -- Cash --> extCustomer
    p5 -- Receipt --> extCustomer
    p4 -- Transaction Record --> dStore2
    p5 -- Transaction Record --> dStore2
    p1 -- Read/Verify --> dStore1
    p2 -- Read/Update --> dStore1
    p4 -- Update --> dStore1
    p4 -- Reconciliation --> extBank
    p2 -- Show Balance --> extCustomer
```

**Interpretation:** The system is split into 5 top-level processes. Data stores `D1` (persistent account records) and `D2` (audit log) anchor the storage layer. The flow `Reconciliation → Bank` is the nightly (or real-time) settling handshake.

### 4.3 Level 1 DFD — Decomposition of Process P2

```mermaid
graph LR
    p1["1 Verify Customer"]
    p21["2.1 Validate Withdrawal"]
    p22["2.2 Validate Deposit"]
    p23["2.3 Retrieve Balance"]
    p24["2.4 Check Funds"]
    dStore1[(D1 Account File)]
    extCustomer[Customer]
    extBank[Bank Host System]

    p1 -- Validated ID --> p21
    p1 -- Validated ID --> p22
    p1 -- Validated ID --> p23
    extCustomer -- Withdrawal Request --> p21
    extCustomer -- Deposit Request --> p22
    extCustomer -- Inquiry --> p23
    p21 -- Check Funds --> p24
    p24 -- Read Account --> dStore1
    p21 -- Read Account --> dStore1
    p22 -- Read/Update --> dStore1
    p23 -- Read --> dStore1
    p24 -- Fund Status --> p21
    p21 -- Approved/Declined --> extCustomer
    p22 -- Confirmation --> extCustomer
    p23 -- Balance --> extCustomer
    p21 -- Cross-check --> extBank
```

**Interpretation:** P2 is exploded into 4 sub-processes. Note the **balance preservation**: the inputs/outputs of P2.1–P2.4 collectively equal the inputs/outputs of parent P2 in Level 0.

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Cross-Model Mapping)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph Functional_Model
        F1[Context Diagram]
        F2[Level 0 DFD]
        F3[Level 1 DFD]
        F4[Process Specifications]
        F5[Data Dictionary]
    end

    subgraph Object_Model
        O1[Class Diagram - Account]
        O2[Class Diagram - Customer]
        O3[Class Diagram - ATM]
        O4[Class Diagram - Transaction]
    end

    subgraph Dynamic_Model
        D1[State Diagram - ATM Lifecycle]
        D2[Sequence Diagram - Withdraw]
        D3[Activity Diagram - Verify PIN]
    end

    F4 -- implemented by --> O1
    F4 -- implemented by --> O2
    F4 -- implemented by --> O3
    F2 -- triggers --> D2
    F3 -- transitions --> D1
    F5 -- shared vocabulary --> O1
    F5 -- shared vocabulary --> O4
    F2 -- realized by --> D3
```

**Interpretation:** The three OMT models (Functional, Object, Dynamic) are *complementary views* of the same system. The functional model is the **behavioural specification**; the object model is the **static structure**; the dynamic model is the **temporal control**. Consistency across all three is the hallmark of a correct object-oriented analysis.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution Reference (KTU 2024):** Module 2 typically carries **~20%** of the End Semester Exam (ESE) marks. Functional Models appear in **Part A (3 marks — definition/diagram) and Part B (14 marks — full construction)**. Solve the questions below under timed conditions.

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set B]**
*Define a Functional Model in Object-Oriented Modeling. List its four main components.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Functional Model** in OOMD describes the **computations performed by the system** and the **flow of data** between processes, thereby capturing *what* the system does (as opposed to *what* it consists of, which is the object model, or *when* it changes, which is the dynamic model).

The four main components are:
1. **Data Flow Diagrams (DFDs)** — visual representation of data flow and transformations across the system.
2. **Process Specifications (PSPECs)** — narrative descriptions of each atomic process (structured English, pre/post-conditions, pseudo-code).
3. **Data Dictionary** — formal repository of definitions for all data flows, data stores, and entities.
4. **Constraints** — declarations of non-functional and boundary conditions (timing, limits, performance).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often *omit* constraints or *conflate* the functional model with the use case diagram. Use case diagrams are part of the *dynamic/behavioural* view in OMT; the functional model is the *data-transformation* view. **(–1 mark typical deduction)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Set A]**
*Differentiate between the context diagram and Level 0 DFD with a suitable illustration reference.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Context Diagram (Level -1) | Level 0 DFD |
|-----------|----------------------------|-------------|
| **Detail Level** | Single process representing the entire system | 4–6 major sub-processes of the system |
| **Data Stores** | Not shown | Introduced |
| **Purpose** | Define system boundary | Show major functional decomposition |
| **Number of Processes** | 1 (the whole system) | Multiple (one per major function) |
| **Use Case Mapping** | Maps to all use cases collectively | Maps to one or more use cases individually |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students draw the context diagram *with* data stores — this is a structural violation. Data stores appear *only from Level 0 onwards* because the system must have internal memory to warrant a data store. **(–1 mark deduction)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Part B questions offer an **internal choice** between two alternatives. Students must answer **either** Option A **or** Option B. Each option has sub-parts (a) and (b), typically 7 marks each.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model Paper]**
*Consider the design of an "Online Railway Reservation System". Construct a complete sample functional model for the system by performing the following tasks:*

**(a)** *Draw the Context Diagram and Level 0 DFD, identifying all external entities, data flows, and data stores.* **[7 marks]**

**(b)** *Decompose ONE of the Level 0 processes into a Level 1 DFD. Provide two process specifications in structured English with pre/post-conditions, and list any three constraints.* **[7 marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A**

**Part (a) — Context Diagram & Level 0 DFD (7 marks)**

**External Entities Identified:**
- `Passenger` (the user submitting the booking)
- `Payment Gateway` (third-party bank interface)
- `Railway Server` (central reservation database)

**Data Stores Introduced at Level 0:**
- `D1 — Train Schedule DB` (train routes, timings, seat availability)
- `D2 — Booking Records DB` (confirmed tickets, PNRs)
- `D3 — Payment Records DB` (transaction log)

**Level 0 Processes (5 major):**

1. `P1 — Authenticate User` (login / session check)
2. `P2 — Search Trains` (route + date query)
3. `P3 — Reserve Seat` (atomic seat allocation with concurrency lock)
4. `P4 — Process Payment` (bank handshake + receipt)
5. `P5 — Issue Ticket` (PNR + e-ticket generation)

**Major Data Flows (between P1–P5):**
- `Login Credentials → P1`
- `P1 → User Session → P2`
- `Search Query → P2 → Available Trains → Passenger`
- `Passenger → Booking Request → P3`
- `P3 → Lock Request → D1`
- `P3 → Booking Confirmation → P4`
- `P4 → Payment Request → Payment Gateway → P4`
- `P4 → PNR + E-Ticket → P5`
- `P5 → E-Ticket + Receipt → Passenger`

**Suggested Marking Scheme:**

| Deliverable | Marks |
|-------------|-------|
| Correct external entities (3) | 1 |
| Correct data stores (3) | 1 |
| Level 0 processes (5) with proper symbols | 3 |
| Data flows labelled and directed correctly | 2 |
| **Subtotal (a)** | **7** |

---

**Part (b) — Level 1 DFD + PSPECs + Constraints (7 marks)**

**Decomposition of P3 — Reserve Seat (Level 1):**

- `P3.1 — Validate Booking Request` (date, route, class)
- `P3.2 — Check Seat Availability` (read D1, return list)
- `P3.3 — Acquire Seat Lock` (concurrency control on D1)
- `P3.4 — Generate PNR` (unique 10-digit PNR with checksum)

**PSPEC 1 — P3.2 Check Seat Availability:**

| Field | Value |
|-------|-------|
| **Pre-condition** | A validated booking request exists with `route`, `date`, `class` |
| **Post-condition** | A seat-availability list (or an empty list with `WL_FLAG`) is returned |
| **Structured English** | READ train records from `D1` where `Route = request.route` AND `Date = request.date` AND `Class = request.class`; IF available count > 0 THEN return list of seat numbers; ELSE append passenger to RAC/WL queue; return WL_FLAG = TRUE |

**PSPEC 2 — P3.3 Acquire Seat Lock:**

| Field | Value |
|-------|-------|
| **Pre-condition** | A seat number is selected; lock manager is online |
| **Post-condition** | The seat is exclusively held for 5 minutes OR released to pool |
| **Structured English** | ACQUIRE mutex on `D1[seat_id]`; IF lock granted THEN start 5-min timer; ELSE return LOCK_FAILED; ON timer expiry OR explicit release: release mutex, decrement active locks |

**Three Constraints:**

- **C1 — Concurrency:** No two passengers can simultaneously book the *same* seat (enforced by P3.3).
- **C2 — Performance:** Search results must be returned within **3 seconds** for 95% of queries.
- **C3 — Data Integrity:** A PNR is **unique across the system for 10 years** (collision probability < 10⁻⁹).

**Suggested Marking Scheme:**

| Deliverable | Marks |
|-------------|-------|
| Level 1 decomposition (4 sub-processes) | 2 |
| PSPEC 1 (structured English + pre/post) | 2 |
| PSPEC 2 (structured English + pre/post) | 2 |
| 3 constraints (clear, measurable) | 1 |
| **Subtotal (b)** | **7** |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Option**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Model Paper]**
*A hospital intends to deploy an "Outpatient Department (OPD) Management System". As a systems analyst, your task is to produce the sample functional model with the following deliverables:*

**(a)** *Construct the Context Diagram and the Level 0 DFD. Identify actors, data stores, and at least 8 data flows with their labels.* **[7 marks]**

**(b)** *Write the Data Dictionary for three key data flows and provide PSPECs (with pre/post-conditions) for two atomic processes. Specify four constraints related to data privacy and performance.* **[7 marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B**

**Part (a) — Context Diagram + Level 0 DFD (7 marks)**

**External Entities:** `Patient`, `Doctor`, `Pharmacy`, `Insurance Provider`.

**Data Stores:** `D1 — Patient Records`, `D2 — Appointment Schedule`, `D3 — Prescription Log`, `D4 — Billing Records`.

**Level 0 Processes:**
- `P1 — Register Patient` (new / returning)
- `P2 — Schedule Appointment` (doctor slot allocation)
- `P3 — Conduct Consultation` (diagnosis + prescription)
- `P4 — Generate Bill` (consultation + meds + tests)
- `P5 — Settle Payment` (cash / insurance)

**Data Flows (≥8):** `Patient Details → P1`, `P1 → Patient ID → D1`, `Appointment Request → P2`, `P2 → Slot Confirmed → Patient`, `P2 → Schedule Update → D2`, `P3 → Prescription → D3`, `P3 → Diagnosis → D4`, `P4 → Bill → P5`, `P5 → Insurance Claim → Insurance Provider`, `P5 → Receipt → Patient`.

**Part (b) — Data Dictionary + PSPECs + Constraints (7 marks)**

**Data Dictionary Entries:**

$$\text{PatientID} = \text{OPR-}\{6\text{digit}\}6$$
