$$\text{AppointmentSlot} = \text{Date} + \text{Time} + \text{DoctorID}$$
$$\text{Prescription} = \text{Header} + \{\text{Medication}\} + \text{DoctorSignature}$$

**PSPEC 1 — P1 Register Patient:**

| Field | Value |
|-------|-------|
| Pre | New patient walks in OR returning patient provides ID |
| Post | A unique PatientID is created/retrieved and stored in `D1` |
| Logic | IF patient not in `D1` THEN collect demographics; generate `PatientID`; store in `D1`; ELSE retrieve existing record |

**PSPEC 2 — P2 Schedule Appointment:**

| Field | Value |
|-------|-------|
| Pre | Valid PatientID and a doctor specialty chosen |
| Post | A unique AppointmentSlot is reserved in `D2` |
| Logic | READ doctor schedule from `D2`; FIND earliest free slot matching specialty; IF available THEN write slot to `D2`; ELSE return WL position |

**Four Constraints:**

- **C1 — Privacy:** Patient records must be **AES-256 encrypted at rest** and **TLS 1.3 in transit**.
- **C2 — Access Control:** Only the assigned doctor and the patient (read-only) may view `D1` records (RBAC enforcement).
- **C3 — Performance:** Appointment scheduling must complete in **≤ 1 second** for 99% of requests.
- **C4 — Compliance:** System must log **all PHI access** in an immutable audit trail (HIPAA / DISHA Act compliance).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. **Diagrams without symbols:** Examiners deduct **1 mark** if you draw a flowchart instead of a DFD (no decision diamonds, no proper process bubbles).
> 2. **Vague constraints:** "System should be fast" → 0 marks. "Search ≤ 3 sec for 95% of queries" → full credit. *Always quantify*.
> 3. **Missing data dictionary:** The data dictionary is a **mandatory deliverable**. Omitting it costs 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Unbalanced DFDs:** If your Level 1 inputs/outputs do not match the parent process, the KTU examiner will mark the entire decomposition 0.
> 5. **Confusing DFD with Control Flow:** DFDs show *data*, not *control*. Do not draw "yes/no" branches as if it were a flowchart.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Bullet Points (High-Density, Board-Exam Focused):**

- ✅ A **Functional Model** captures *what the system does* in terms of data transformations and flow.
- ✅ Its **four pillars** are: **DFDs, PSPECs, Data Dictionary, Constraints**.
- ✅ The **canonical sample** (used in textbooks and KTU papers) is the **ATM System**.
- ✅ DFDs follow a **top-down hierarchy**: Context (Level -1) → Level 0 → Level 1 → … until atomic.
- ✅ The **balancing principle** is non-negotiable: child inputs/outputs must equal parent inputs/outputs.
- ✅ **Four DFD symbols**: Rectangle (entity), Rounded Rectangle (process), Open-ended Rectangle (data store), Arrow (data flow).
- ✅ **Data Dictionary operators**: `=` (is), `+` (concatenation), `[]` (optional), `{}` (iteration), `|` (selection).
- ✅ **PSPECs** are written only for **atomic** processes, in structured English, with explicit pre/post-conditions.
- ✅ **Constraints** must be **quantified** (e.g., "3 seconds", "3 retries", "AES-256"). Vague constraints score zero.
- ✅ The functional model **cross-links** with the object model (each process ↔ an object method) and the dynamic model (each DFD ↔ a sequence diagram).
- ✅ In OOMD, the functional model is **complementary** to the object model — not a replacement. Function, structure, and behaviour are three views of the *same* system.
- ✅ For KTU 2024, expect at least one **full construction (14 marks)** question on a sample functional model in every End Semester Exam paper. Practice with: ATM, Railway Reservation, Hospital OPD, Library Management, E-Commerce Checkout.
- ✅ Memorize the **4C-D-P mnemonic**: Context, Constraints, Content, Control, DFDs, Data dictionary, Process specs.
- ✅ Always **label every arrow** in a DFD. An unlabelled arrow is a guaranteed 0.5-mark deduction.
- ✅ The **last step** of building the sample functional model is *cross-validation against the object model* — this is the OMT hallmark.

---

**End of KTU-Premier-Engine V10 Notes for "A Sample Functional Model" — Module 2, PBCNT604.**
*Prepared in alignment with KTU 2024 Scheme, OBE-NEP 2020 outcomes, and Revised Bloom's Taxonomy (Levels L1–L5).*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Jacobson Methodology: Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCNT604 - OBJECT ORIENTED MODELING AND DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Jacobson Methodology: Architecture # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Jacobson Methodology** (commercially known as **Object-Oriented Software Engineering — Objectory**) is a use-case driven, architecture-centric, and iterative object-oriented software engineering process introduced by **Ivar Jacobson** in the 1990s. Within this methodology, the term **Architecture** refers to the structured decomposition of the system into a hierarchy of **Blocks** (logical building units) and the layered organization of the system across **four abstract levels of refinement**: the *Analysis* level, the *Design* level, the *Implementation* level, and the *Installation* level. A **Block** is formally defined as a cohesive package of cooperating classes, interfaces, and mechanisms that together provide a well-defined set of services. Blocks at higher levels decompose into smaller blocks at lower levels, creating a **recursive, layered, and traceable** architecture. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Students must be able to (i) describe the four architectural levels, (ii) identify and explain the role of *Blocks*, *Subsystems*, *Interfaces*, and *Mechanisms*, and (iii) relate the architecture to the *Objectory process* and *Use-Case driven development*. ## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The "Building Plan" Analogy Think of building a **multi-storey apartment complex**: | Engineering Concept | Apartment Analogy | |---|---| | **System Block** | The entire building | | **Subsystem Block** | Each tower / wing of the building | | **Block** | Each floor or functional unit (lobby, parking, flats) | | **Interface** | The doors, gates, corridors connecting units | | **Analysis Model** | The architect's *concept* — what the building must do (shelter, accessibility, security) | | **Design Model** | The *blueprint* — exact room layout, plumbing paths, structural drawings | | **Implementation Model** | The *actual construction* — concrete poured, bricks laid, wires installed | | **Installation Model** | The *delivery* — furniture moved in, lifts commissioned, residents occupying | Just as the architect first plans at a high level (concept), then refines to a detailed blueprint, then physically constructs, and finally installs the building, Jacobson's architecture captures the system at **four progressively concrete levels**, each level traced consistently back to the **Use Cases** that drive the development. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** The most distinctive feature of Jacobson's architecture is that *the same block can be viewed at four different levels* — the **same entity** is examined for *what it must do* (analysis), *how it is structured* (design), *how it is coded* (implementation), and *how it is deployed* (installation). This is what gives Objectory its powerful **traceability** property. ## 1.3 Standard Terminology (Bold Constants & Metrics) - **Block** — basic architectural unit encapsulating classes and interfaces. - **Subsystem** — a block that can be independently developed and integrated. - **Interface** — a named set of operations defining a contract between blocks. - **Mechanism** — a reusable solution pattern addressing a cross-cutting concern. - **Use Case** — driver of all development activities. - **Traceability** — ability to follow a requirement from use case down to code. - **Five Models of Objectory:** Requirements, Analysis, Design, Implementation, Test. - **Four Architectural Levels:** Analysis → Design → Implementation → Installation. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 The Four Architectural Levels of Jacobson The system is described **recursively** at each of the following four levels. Every block appearing in the system model has a corresponding view at each level. ### Level 1 — Analysis Level (Problem-Domain Objects) - Captures **what** the system must do, **not how**. - Objects represent real-world entities from the problem domain. - Emphasizes vocabulary of the user / customer. - Independent of programming language, databases, or hardware. - **Purpose:** establish a common understanding between customer and developer. ### Level 2 — Design Level (System Architecture Objects) - Captures **how** the analysis objects are structured to fulfill use cases. - Introduces the *block structure* — classes are grouped into blocks. - Defines **interfaces** (provided and required) for each block. - Introduces **mechanisms** for handling cross-cutting concerns (persistence, communication, user-interface). - **Purpose:** produce an implementation-independent blueprint of the system. ### Level 3 — Implementation Level - The design blocks are mapped to actual programming constructs. - Classes, components, and source files are produced. - Implementation language, libraries, and frameworks are chosen. - Components are packaged into deployable units. - **Purpose:** produce executable artifacts. ### Level 4 — Installation Level (Physical / Deployment Level) - Describes how implementation units are physically deployed. - Identifies nodes, processes, and network connections. - Maps blocks onto hardware, operating systems, and middleware. - **Purpose:** define the runtime topology of the system. ## 2.2 Properties of a Good Jacobson Architecture A block-oriented architecture in Objectory is judged by these structural qualities: 1. **High Cohesion** — every class inside a block should serve a single, unified purpose. 2. **Low Coupling** — blocks should communicate *only* through well-defined interfaces. 3. **Encapsulation of Mechanisms** — mechanisms (persistence, distribution, fault-tolerance) are treated as reusable architectural patterns, not scattered across classes. 4. **Traceability** — every design element must be traceable to a use case. 5. **Open for Extension, Closed for Modification** — the block structure must support reuse and variation. ## 2.3 Interfaces — The Heart of the Architecture Interfaces are the **glue** of the architecture. A block exposes two kinds of interfaces: - **Provided Interface** — services the block offers to other blocks (denoted as a *lollipop* in UML). - **Required Interface** — services the block needs from other blocks (denoted as a *socket* in UML). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Point:** If a question asks *"What is the role of interfaces in Jacobson's architecture?"*, the canonical answer is: *Interfaces decouple the provider of a service from its consumer, enabling blocks to be developed, replaced, and reused independently, and they make the architecture resilient to change.* ## 2.4 Mechanisms A **mechanism** is a standard, reusable solution to a recurring design problem that cuts across multiple blocks. Jacobson groups them into: - **Basic Mechanisms** — encapsulation, polymorphism, persistence. - **Specialized Mechanisms** — error handling, inter-process communication, distribution, transaction management, security. Mechanisms are documented separately and applied uniformly throughout the system, ensuring consistent handling of cross-cutting concerns. ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Quick-Reference Table | Element | Symbol / Notation | Definition | Architectural Level | |---|---|---|---| | Block | Rectangle (UML) | Cohesive package of classes & interfaces | All four | | Provided Interface | Lollipop (lollipop) | Services offered outward | Design | | Required Interface | Socket (open socket) | Services consumed from other blocks | Design | | Subsystem | Block with the word `subsystem` | Independently developable block | Design | | Actor | Stick figure (UML) | External entity interacting with system | Requirements / Analysis | | Use Case | Ellipse (UML) | Functional requirement from actor's view | Requirements / Analysis | | Mechanism | Documented pattern | Reusable cross-cutting solution | Design | | Analysis Object | Domain term | Real-world entity, no implementation detail | Analysis | | Implementation Object | Code-level class | Concrete class in chosen language | Implementation | | Installation Object | Node / artifact | Deployed component on hardware | Installation | > [!NOTE] > **Exam Tip:** When asked to "explain the Jacobson architecture", always list *all four levels* with at least *one sentence each* and emphasise that the **same block is traceable across all four levels**. Marks are frequently lost when students describe only two or three levels. ## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering Jacobson's architecture directly inspired several industry practices: - **Rational Unified Process (RUP)** — Jacobson co-developed RUP with Rumbaugh and Booch, embedding his architecture into it. - **Component-Based Development (CBD)** — block = component, interfaces = contracts. - **Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA) & Microservices** — provided/required interfaces map directly to *published APIs* and *consumed APIs*. - **AUTOSAR** (in automotive embedded systems) — uses the layered block-with-interfaces concept. - **Android & iOS modular apps** — each feature module is a "block" exposing interfaces to others. # 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough of Jacobson's Architecture This section walks through the **logical derivation** of the architecture from the requirements specification, step by step, exactly as a student would reproduce it in a 14-mark KTU answer. ## 3.1 Starting Point: The Use-Case Model Jacobson begins architecture design **only after** the use-case model is mature. Let the use-case set be: $$U = \{u_1, u_2, u_3, \dots, u_n\}$$ Each use case $u_i$ describes a sequence of actions yielding an *observable result* of value to an *actor* $a_i$. ## 3.2 Step 1 — Identify Analysis Objects from Use Cases For each use case $u_i$, nouns in its description are candidates for **analysis objects**. Let the resulting set of candidate analysis objects be: $$A = \{a_1, a_2, a_3, \dots, a_m\}$$ These objects live **only in the analysis model** and contain *responsibilities* (operations they must perform) without implementation details. ## 3.3 Step 2 — Group Analysis Objects into Blocks Apply the **Common Closure Principle** (classes that change together belong together) and **Reuse-Release Equivalence Principle** to partition the analysis objects $A$ into $k$ disjoint blocks: $$B_{\text{design}} = \{B_1, B_2, B_3, \dots, B_k\}$$ such that: $$B_1 \cup B_2 \cup B_3 \cup \dots \cup B_k = A$$ and $$B_i \cap B_j = \emptyset \;\;\; \forall \; i \neq j$$ This means **every analysis object belongs to exactly one design block**, and the union of all blocks covers the entire analysis model with no overlap. ## 3.4 Step 3 — Define Interfaces for Each Block For every block $B_i$, define: - $P(B_i)$ — the **provided interface** (services $B_i$ offers to others). - $R(B_i)$ — the **required interface** (services $B_i$ needs). A block $B_i$ can communicate with another block $B_j$ **only if** $P(B_j) \supseteq R(B_i)$, i.e.: $$B_i \rightarrow B_j \;\;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\;\; R(B_i) \subseteq P(B_j)$$ This is the **Interface Contract Rule** — the foundation of low coupling. ## 3.5 Step 4 — Apply Mechanisms For each cross-cutting concern $c \in C$ (e.g. *persistence*, *security*, *distribution*), select a **mechanism** $M_c$ and apply it to all blocks requiring it. The mechanism is documented in a *separate architectural document* and referenced from each block that uses it. ## 3.6 Step 5 — Recursive Decomposition (Sub-Blocks) If any block $B_i$ is too large or too complex, decompose it into sub-blocks: $$B_i = \{b_{i,1}, b_{i,2}, \dots, b_{i,p}\}$$ The decomposition is **recursive** and continues until each leaf block contains a small, manageable number of closely related classes (typically 5–20). ## 3.7 Step 6 — Map to Implementation and Installation Levels For each leaf block $B_{\text{leaf}}$, choose the implementation unit: $$B_{\text{leaf}} \mapsto \text{Component} \; c_i \;\;\; \text{(source file, library, .jar, .dll, etc.)}$$ Then map each component to a **deployment node** in the installation model: $$c_i \mapsto N_j \;\;\; \text{(physical node, server, container, etc.)}$$ ## 3.8 Worked Mini-Example — ATM System **Use cases:** Withdraw Cash, Check Balance, Deposit Cash. **Step 1 — Analysis objects identified:** - `Account`, `Card`, `CashDispenser`, `ATMController`, `ReceiptPrinter`, `TransactionLog`. **Step 2 — Grouping into design blocks:** | Block | Contained Objects | Responsibility | |---|---|---| | `UserInterface` | Card, ReceiptPrinter | Interaction with customer | | `Domain` | Account, TransactionLog | Business rules | | `Hardware` | CashDispenser | Physical money handling | | `Controller` | ATMController | Coordination of use cases | **Step 3 — Interfaces:** | Block | Provided Interface | Required Interface | |---|---|---| | `UserInterface` | `readCard()`, `printReceipt()` | `getBalance()`, `debitAccount()` | | `Domain` | `getBalance()`, `debitAccount()` | `logTransaction()` | | `Hardware` | `dispenseCash()` | — | | `Controller` | `startSession()`, `endSession()` | All above | **Step 4 — Mechanism applied:** `Persistence Mechanism` applied to `Account` (in `Domain`) and `TransactionLog`. **Step 5 — Implementation mapping:** Each block → a separate package and compiled to a `.jar`. **Step 6 — Installation mapping:** `Hardware` block runs on the ATM's embedded node; `Controller` and `Domain` run on the bank's application server. > [!NOTE] > **The same set of four blocks** is described at all four architectural levels — analysis (what they must do), design (interfaces & mechanisms), implementation (Java/Python classes), and installation (which node they run on). This is the **traceability property** unique to Jacobson's architecture. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Jacobson's Four-Level Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB L1["LEVEL 1 : ANALYSIS
Problem Domain Objects
Captures WHAT the system does"] L2["LEVEL 2 : DESIGN
System Architecture Objects
Captures HOW it is structured"] L3["LEVEL 3 : IMPLEMENTATION
Implementation Objects
Source code, components"] L4["LEVEL 4 : INSTALLATION
Installation Objects
Deployment on nodes"] L1 ==> L2 L2 ==> L3 L3 ==> L4 UC["USE CASES
(Driver of all levels)"] -. drives .-> L1 UC -. traces to .-> L2 UC -. traces to .-> L3 UC -. traces to .-> L4 classDef levelStyle fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef ucStyle fill:#8b0000,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff class L1,L2,L3,L4 levelStyle class UC ucStyle ``` ## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Block Hierarchy with Interfaces ```mermaid flowchart TB SysBlock["SYSTEM BLOCK
(Root)"] subgraph SubA ["SUBSYSTEM A : UserInterface"] A1["Class A1"] A2["Class A2"] end subgraph SubB ["SUBSYSTEM B : Domain"] B1["Class B1"] B2["Class B2"] end subgraph SubC ["SUBSYSTEM C : Hardware"] C1["Class C1"] end SysBlock --> SubA SysBlock --> SubB SysBlock --> SubC PI_A(["Provided Interface UI"]) --- A1 PI_B(["Provided Interface DOM"]) --- B1 PI_C(["Provided Interface HW"]) --- C1 A2 -. requires .-> PI_B B2 -. requires .-> PI_C A1 -. requires .-> PI_B classDef sysStyle fill:#0b5394,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef ifaceStyle fill:#cc0000,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef classStyle fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#274e13,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000 class SysBlock sysStyle class PI_A,PI_B,PI_C ifaceStyle class A1,A2,B1,B2,C1 classStyle ``` ## 4.3 Diagram 3 — The Objectory Process & Five Models ```mermaid flowchart LR REQ["REQUIREMENTS MODEL
Use Cases & Actors"] ANA["ANALYSIS MODEL
Problem Domain Objects"] DES["DESIGN MODEL
System Architecture, Blocks & Interfaces"] IMP["IMPLEMENTATION MODEL
Source Code & Components"] TST["TEST MODEL
Test Cases & Procedures"] REQ ==> ANA ==> DES ==> IMP ==> TST TST -. feedback .-> REQ MAINT["MAINTENANCE PHASE
(Iterative cycles)"] -.-> REQ classDef modelStyle fill:#274e13,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff class REQ,ANA,DES,IMP,TST,MAINT modelStyle ``` ## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Block Composition View (One Block at All Four Levels) ```mermaid flowchart TB BLK["BLOCK B1 : Persistence"] subgraph LV1 ["ANALYSIS LEVEL"] A1["Persistent Object
(domain term only)"] end subgraph LV2 ["DESIGN LEVEL"] A2["Class with Persistence Interface
+ Mechanism applied"] end subgraph LV3 ["IMPLEMENTATION LEVEL"] A3["Java/Python Class
(code)"] end subgraph LV4 ["INSTALLATION LEVEL"] A4["Deployed on Node N1
(server/container)"] end BLK --> LV1 BLK --> LV2 BLK --> LV3 BLK --> LV4 classDef blkStyle fill:#1c4587,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef lvlStyle fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#7f6000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000 class BLK blkStyle class A1,A2,A3,A4 lvlStyle ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading Tip for the Diagrams:** > - *Diagram 1* summarises the four architectural levels and how use cases drive each. > - *Diagram 2* illustrates the **interface contract** — blocks communicate *only* through their lollipop (provided) and socket (required) ends. > - *Diagram 3* places the architecture inside the broader Objectory process of five models. > - *Diagram 4* is the single most-asked visual in KTU papers — the same block seen at four levels. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **Define a *Block* in Jacobson's Objectory methodology. List any two properties of a well-formed block.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **Block** is a cohesive collection of cooperating classes, interfaces, and mechanisms that together provide a well-defined set of services. It is the basic architectural unit in Jacobson's Objectory methodology. Two properties of a well-formed block: 1. **High cohesion** — all classes inside the block contribute to a single, unified purpose. 2. **Low coupling** — the block communicates with other blocks *only* through well-defined interfaces (provided and required). *(Allocate ~1 mark for the definition and 1 mark each for the properties.)* --- ### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **What is the role of *Mechanisms* in Jacobson's architecture? Give one example.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **mechanism** is a reusable, standard solution to a recurring design problem that cuts across multiple blocks in the system. Mechanisms encapsulate cross-cutting concerns so that they are handled uniformly and can be changed in one place. *Example:* A **Persistence Mechanism** defines how objects are stored and retrieved from a database; it is applied uniformly to every block that needs persistence, ensuring consistent handling of data storage across the system. *(Allocate ~2 marks for the role and 1 mark for the example.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice) ### Question A. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **(a)** Explain in detail the **four architectural levels** of Jacobson's Objectory methodology, with a neat diagram. **(7 marks)** **(b)** What is a **Subsystem**? Explain the concept of **provided and required interfaces** with an example. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Answer for Question A(a) — 7 Marks The Jacobson methodology describes the system at **four progressive levels of abstraction**. The same block can be viewed at all four levels, providing strong *traceability* from requirements to deployment. 1. **Analysis Level (Problem-Domain Objects)** — describes *what* the system must do using the vocabulary of the problem domain. Objects are real-world entities, free of any programming or hardware detail. Purpose: build a shared understanding between customer and developer. 2. **Design Level (System Architecture Objects)** — describes *how* the analysis objects are organised. Classes are grouped into **blocks**; each block exposes **interfaces** and uses internal **mechanisms** (persistence, security, error handling, distribution). Purpose: produce an implementation-independent blueprint. 3. **Implementation Level (Implementation Objects)** — design blocks are mapped to actual source files, components, libraries and executables in a chosen programming language and framework. Purpose: produce runnable code. 4. **Installation Level (Installation Objects)** — implementation units are deployed onto physical or virtual **nodes** (servers, containers, devices). The runtime topology and communication paths are defined. Purpose: deliver a working, deployed system. *(Award ~1.5 marks per level + 0.5 mark for traceability comment + 0.5 mark for the diagram.)* **Diagram (already covered in SECTION 4 — Diagram 1).** --- #### Model Answer for Question A(b) — 7 Marks A **Subsystem** is a block that can be developed, tested, and integrated **independently** of the rest of the system. It has clearly defined interfaces, encapsulates its internal classes, and can be replaced or reused without affecting other subsystems. **Interfaces** are the contract through which a subsystem (or block) interacts with its environment. Jacobson distinguishes two kinds: - **Provided Interface** — the set of services that the block *offers* to other blocks. In UML it is shown as a *lollipop* symbol. - **Required Interface** — the set of services that the block *needs* from other blocks in order to perform its responsibilities. In UML it is shown as an *socket* (open half-circle) symbol. **Example (ATM system):** - The `Domain` subsystem **provides** the interface `debitAccount()` and `getBalance()`. - The `UserInterface` subsystem **requires** `getBalance()` to display the balance to the customer. - The `Hardware` subsystem **provides** the interface `dispenseCash()`. - The `Domain` subsystem **requires** `dispenseCash()` when a withdrawal is approved. The contract rule is: *A block $B_i$ can depend on a block $B_j$ only if the required interface of $B_i$ is a subset of the provided interface of $B_j$.* This makes subsystems **loosely coupled** and **independently replaceable**. *(Award ~2 marks for subsystem definition, ~3 marks for interfaces with UML notation, ~2 marks for the example.)* --- ### Question B. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] *(Alternative Choice)* **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **block hierarchy and architectural decomposition** in Jacobson's Objectory methodology. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain how **use cases drive the architecture** in Jacobson's methodology. List the **five models** of Objectory and state the purpose of each. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Answer for Question B(a) — 7 Marks The architecture of a system in Jacobson's Objectory is organised as a **hierarchical tree of blocks**, beginning from the *system block* (root) and recursively decomposed into sub-blocks until each leaf block contains a small, cohesive set of classes. **Hierarchy (top to bottom):** - **System Block** — represents the entire application. - **Subsystem Blocks** — major independent parts of the system. - **Blocks** — cohesive units within a subsystem. - **Classes** — the actual object-oriented constructs inside each block. **Decomposition principles:** - *Common-Closure Principle* — classes that change for the same reason are placed in the same block. - *Reuse-Release Equivalence Principle* — classes released together are grouped together. - *Stable-Dependencies Principle* — a block depends only on blocks that are more stable than itself. **Communication rule:** Every block exposes a **provided interface** and consumes a **required interface**. Inter-block communication happens *only* through these interfaces, never through direct class access. This keeps the architecture **modular, testable, and reusable**. *(Refer to the diagram in SECTION 4 — Diagram 2 for the visual hierarchy.)* *(Award ~2 marks for hierarchy description, ~3 marks for decomposition principles, ~2 marks for communication rule and interface contract.)* --- #### Model Answer for Question B(b) — 7 Marks In Jacobson's methodology, **use cases are the primary driver of every development activity** — from requirements capture to testing. The architecture is not designed in isolation; it is *derived* from the use cases. **How use cases drive the architecture:** 1. Use cases are first written in the **Requirements Model** from the actor's point of view. 2. Each use case is **analysed** to identify the analysis (problem-domain) objects that participate in it. 3. These analysis objects are **grouped into design blocks** based on cohesion, reuse, and stability. 4. The design blocks are then **implemented** and **deployed** — both steps still trace back to the originating use case. 5. **Test cases** are derived directly from the use cases, ensuring full functional coverage. **The Five Models of Objectory:** | # | Model | Purpose | |---|---|---| | 1 | **Requirements Model** | Captures use cases, actors, and the system's external view. | | 2 | **Analysis Model** | Captures the system's structure as problem-domain objects. | | 3 | **Design Model** | Defines the block architecture, interfaces, and mechanisms. | | 4 | **Implementation Model** | Realises the design as source code and components. | | 5 | **Test Model** | Verifies that each use case is correctly realised by the implementation. | *(Award ~3 marks for the "how" explanation, ~3 marks for the five models in a table, ~1 mark for the conclusion that use cases provide end-to-end traceability.)* --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common places where students lose marks:** > 1. **Listing only 2 or 3 levels** instead of all four (Analysis, Design, Implementation, Installation). Always list all four — this alone is worth ~1.5 marks. > 2. **Confusing "Design Model" with "Design Level".** The Design Model is one of the five Objectory models; the Design *Level* is one of the four architectural levels describing the *same* block. Don't interchange the two. > 3. **Forgetting mechanisms** when explaining the Design Level. Mechanisms (persistence, security, distribution) are a *required* part of the answer and are often missed. > 4. **No diagram.** A 14-mark answer without a labelled architectural diagram typically loses 1–2 marks. > 5. **Mixing up provided/required interface notation.** Lollipop = provided; socket = required. Reversing these is a common slip-up. > 6. **Skipping traceability.** Always close the answer with the line: *"The same block is traceable across all four levels, ensuring consistency from requirements to deployment."* --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **Objectory** = Jacobson Methodology; **use-case driven**, **architecture-centric**, **iterative**. - The architecture has **four levels**: Analysis, Design, Implementation, Installation. - The **same block** is described at **all four levels** — this is the **traceability principle**. - **Block** = cohesive unit of classes + interfaces + mechanisms. - **Subsystem** = independently developable block. - **Provided interface** (lollipop) = services a block offers. - **Required interface** (socket) = services a block needs. - **Mechanisms** handle cross-cutting concerns (persistence, security, error handling, distribution, communication). - **Five Objectory Models**: Requirements, Analysis, Design, Implementation, Test. - **Use cases** drive every model and every level. - **Block hierarchy**: System → Subsystems → Blocks → Classes. - **Communication rule**: $B_i \rightarrow B_j \iff R(B_i) \subseteq P(B_j)$. - **Cohesion within block, coupling between blocks only via interfaces** — the golden rule. - Architecture decomposition principles: *Common-Closure*, *Reuse-Release*, *Stable-Dependencies*. - Real-world impact: RUP, CBD, SOA, microservices, AUTOSAR all inherit these ideas. - Always include a **diagram** in 14-mark answers — at least one block-level hierarchy or four-level architecture diagram. - The **last line** of every architecture answer should emphasise **traceability** from use case to installation.

topics.title: Jacobson Methodology: Actors and Use-Cases
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCNT604 - OBJECT ORIENTED MODELING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module - 2
> - **Topic:** Jacobson Methodology: Actors and Use-Cases

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Jacobson Methodology**, formally known as **Object-Oriented Software Engineering (OOSE)**, is a use-case driven, architecture-centric, and iterative–incremental software development method introduced by **Ivar Jacobson** in 1992. It is one of the precursor object-oriented methodologies that heavily influenced the **Unified Modeling Language (UML)** and the **Unified Process (UP)**.

In the Jacobson approach, the entire software development lifecycle is organized around three principal models:

1. **The Use-Case Model** – Captures *what* the system must do from the user's perspective.
2. **The Domain Object Model** – Captures *what* objects exist in the problem domain.
3. **The Implementation/Architectural Model** – Captures *how* the system is built.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 2 of PBCNT604 explicitly mandates that students be able to "explain the Jacobson methodology with focus on actors and use-cases." Questions frequently (≈ 70% probability) test the distinction between *actor* types and the *structure of a use-case document*.

Within this methodology, two concepts form its very backbone: **Actors** and **Use-Cases**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are designing a **banking system**. The bank has:

- **Customers** who deposit money and withdraw cash.
- **Tellers** who process these transactions.
- **The ATM machine** that interacts with the customer's debit card.

If you were to describe the system to a non-technical stakeholder, you would not start with classes, methods, or database tables. You would say:

> *"The **Customer** can **Withdraw Cash** from the ATM. The **Teller** can **Open a New Account** for the customer."*

In this everyday description:

- The nouns referring to *people* or *external systems* (Customer, Teller, ATM-network) are **Actors**.
- The phrases describing *what they want to achieve* (Withdraw Cash, Open a New Account) are **Use-Cases**.

Jacobson's insight was revolutionary: **describe the system in plain English first, then map that description to objects and classes later.** The use-case acts as the "story" that ties user goals to system behavior.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Actor:** An actor is an external entity (a person, another system, or a device) that interacts with the system under consideration to achieve a goal. Actors are always *outside* the system boundary.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Use-Case:** A use-case is a sequence of actions performed by the system that yields an *observable result of value* to a particular actor. It is a unit of functionality the system exposes.

### 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | One-line Meaning |
|---|---|
| Actor | External entity interacting with the system |
| Use-Case | A piece of system functionality valuable to an actor |
| System Boundary | The dividing line between the system and its environment |
| Scenario | A specific path (instance) through a use-case |
| Use-Case Diagram | A UML diagram showing actors, use-cases, and their relationships |
| Use-Case Realization | The internal objects and collaborations that fulfill a use-case |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Use-Case Model — The Heart of OOSE

Jacobson considered the **use-case model** to be the single most important artifact of requirements. It is composed of:

1. **Actors** (external roles)
2. **Use-Cases** (system functions)
3. **System Boundary** (scope of the system)
4. **Relationships** (association, generalization, include, extend)

The use-case model is depicted using the **UML Use-Case Diagram**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Use-Case" and not "Function" or "Feature"?** A use-case explicitly captures an *interaction* between an actor and the system, the *value delivered*, and the *context* (pre/post conditions). A function alone is internal and value-neutral.

### 2.2 Actors — Deep Structural Analysis

#### 2.2.1 Definition & Scope

An **actor** is *not* a concrete user (e.g., "John"); it is a **role** that an external entity plays when interacting with the system. One person can play multiple actor roles, and a single role can be played by multiple people.

Examples:

- A single bank employee may play *Teller* in one use-case and *Loan Officer* in another.
- A single hardware device may play the role of *Card Reader* in *Withdraw Cash* and the role of *Receipt Printer* in *Print Statement*.

#### 2.2.2 Categories of Actors (KTU High-Yield)

Jacobson classifies actors into **three primary categories**:

1. **Primary Actors** — Actors who initiate interaction with the system to achieve a goal. The system serves them.
   - *Example:* Customer using an ATM to withdraw cash.
2. **Supporting Actors (Secondary Actors)** — Actors that provide a service to the system. The system uses them.
   - *Example:* An external credit-card authorization service that the ATM contacts.
3. **Offstage Actors** — Actors that have an interest in the use-case outcome but do not directly interact with the system.
   - *Example:* A government tax authority that must receive annual reports generated by the system.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick — PSO:** Primary → Serves (system serves them), Supporting → Served (system uses them), Offstage → Observer (interest only).

#### 2.2.3 Identifying Actors — A Stepwise Method

| Step | Action |
|---|---|
| 1 | Identify the **system boundary** — what is "in" and what is "out" |
| 2 | List the **primary user roles** who need the system to perform tasks for them |
| 3 | Identify **other systems** that must interact with the current system |
| 4 | Identify **hardware devices** that stimulate the system (sensors, printers) |
| 5 | Identify **time triggers** (e.g., a scheduler that fires monthly) — modeled as a "Clock" actor |
| 6 | Group roles; do not duplicate. One role = one actor |

#### 2.2.4 Notation in UML

In UML, an actor is drawn as a **stick figure** with the name below it, or as a **class rectangle with the stereotype `<<actor>>`**.

```
  (Customer)
     |
     |
  [Use-Case]   (line = Association)
```

#### 2.2.5 Generalization Among Actors

Actors can have a **generalization relationship** (similar to class inheritance). The child actor inherits the use-case associations of the parent.

Example: A `PremiumCustomer` actor is a specialization of `Customer`; it inherits all customer use-cases and may have additional ones.

### 2.3 Use-Cases — Deep Structural Analysis

#### 2.3.1 Anatomy of a Use-Case

A use-case is more than just a bubble in a diagram. It has a **structured textual description** (Jacobson's template) containing the following fields:

| Field | Purpose | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Name | A verb-noun phrase | "Withdraw Cash" |
| Actors | Primary + Supporting | Customer, BankServer |
| Preconditions | What must be true before | "Account is active and has balance" |
| Postconditions | What is true after success | "Account debited, cash dispensed" |
| Trigger | What starts the use-case | "Customer inserts card and selects Withdraw" |
| Main Flow | The happy path, step-by-step | Steps 1, 2, 3 ... |
| Alternative Flows | Exception/variant paths | Insufficient funds, invalid PIN |
| Special Requirements | Non-functional constraints | "Must complete within 5 seconds" |

#### 2.3.2 Naming Conventions

- Use **active voice**, **verb + noun** form.
- Begin with a strong verb: *Withdraw*, *Register*, *Generate*, *Calculate*.
- Avoid technical jargon: "Print Invoice" is better than "Execute the PrintInvoiceServlet".

#### 2.3.3 Levels of Use-Cases (Cockburn's Extension Adopted by Jacobson)

Jacobson later accepted a three-level hierarchy, often tested in KTU exams:

| Level | Granularity | Purpose | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Summary** (Cloud) | One-liner | Summary of system value | "Manage Accounts" |
| **User-Goal** (Sea) | Single user goal | The typical use-case | "Withdraw Cash" |
| **Sub-function** (Fish) | Sub-step of a goal | Helper functionality | "Validate PIN" |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Most exam questions target the **User-Goal level** because that is where the system delivers business value. Sub-function use-cases are often factored out using `<<include>>`.

#### 2.3.4 Use-Case Relationships

Jacobson formalized three core relationships between use-cases:

##### (a) Association
A simple line between an actor and a use-case indicating participation.

##### (b) `<<include>>` — Mandatory Reuse
Used when a use-case *always* requires another use-case to fulfill its task. The included use-case is **mandatory**.

- **Keyword:** include
- **Direction:** From base to supplier
- **Example:** "Withdraw Cash" `<<include>>` "Validate PIN"

##### (c) `<<extend>>` — Optional/Exceptional Behavior
Used when one use-case *optionally* adds behavior to another. The extending use-case is triggered under specific conditions.

- **Keyword:** extend
- **Direction:** From extender to base
- **Example:** "Withdraw Cash" `<<extend>>` "Help" (only when user presses Help)

##### (d) Generalization
A use-case can inherit behavior from a more general use-case (similar to class inheritance).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse `<<include>>` and `<<extend>>`. The direction of the arrow is critical. For `<<include>>`, the arrow points **FROM the base TO the included**. For `<<extend>>`, the arrow points **FROM the extending TO the base**.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

Although use-cases are qualitative, the following tabulated metrics and ratios are commonly asked in **3-mark short-answer questions**.

| Concept | Symbolic Rule | Unit | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| Total Use-Cases (UC) | $N_{UC} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} UC_{i}$ | count | Size of functional scope |
| Actor-UseCase Links | $L = \sum_{a \in Actors} \text{deg}(a)$ | count | Coupling measure |
| Avg Use-Cases per Actor | $\bar{UC}_{A} = \dfrac{N_{UC}}{N_{A}}$ | count | Fan-out indicator |
| Reuse Factor via Include | $R_{\text{inc}} = \dfrac{N_{\text{include edges}}}{N_{UC}}$ | ratio | Internal reuse density |
| Extension Factor | $R_{\text{ext}} = \dfrac{N_{\text{extend edges}}}{N_{UC}}$ | ratio | Variability density |
| Use-Case Complexity Index (heuristic) | $C_{uc} = w_1 \cdot \text{Steps} + w_2 \cdot \text{AltFlows} + w_3 \cdot \text{Constraints}$ | score | Effort estimation input |

Where:
- $N_{A}$ = number of actors
- $N_{UC}$ = number of use-cases
- $w_1, w_2, w_3$ are weighting constants (often $w_1 = 1$, $w_2 = 2$, $w_3 = 3$)
- $\text{deg}(a)$ = number of use-cases associated with actor $a$

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The actor-use-case framework is not academic theory; it directly drives:

- **Requirements Traceability:** Each requirement maps to one or more use-cases.
- **Test Case Generation:** Each use-case flow produces at least one test case.
- **Project Estimation:** Use-case points (analogous to function points) are used for effort estimation.
- **Communication:** Non-technical stakeholders understand use-case diagrams without UML training.
- **Architecture Inference:** A use-case realization is the input to class and sequence diagrams.

In industry, use-case driven development forms the basis of **Agile user stories**, with use-cases often being the formal parent of story-mapping activities.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Actor Categories to Use-Case Levels

The following matrix is a high-yield analytical tool for KTU answer-writing. It links **actor type** to the **use-case level** that most commonly results.

| Actor Category \ Use-Case Level | Summary (Cloud) | User-Goal (Sea) | Sub-function (Fish) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Primary Actor | Defines mission of the system | Most common pairing | Rarely justified |
| Supporting Actor | Generally not targeted | Provides external service | Common (e.g., "Send SMS") |
| Offstage Actor | May trigger periodic reporting | Period-driven use-case | Notification subtasks |

> **Reading the matrix:** A *Primary Actor* typically participates in a *User-Goal* use-case. A *Supporting Actor* usually appears in a *Sub-function* use-case (helper functionality). An *Offstage Actor* is often associated with a *Summary-level* use-case like "Generate Annual Report".

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Procedure — Building a Use-Case Model for a Library System

The following exhaustive procedure is the type of derivation KTU expects in 7- or 14-mark answers.

**Step 1 — Identify the System Boundary.**
Decide what is "in" the system and what is "out". For a Library Management System, the system *includes* the catalog, the circulation desk, and the borrower database. It *excludes* the borrower as a person (he/she is an actor) and the external ISBN service.

**Step 2 — Identify Primary Actors.**
A primary actor is one whose goal the system exists to satisfy.

- *Borrower* — wants to borrow and return books.
- *Librarian* — wants to manage the catalog and members.

**Step 3 — Identify Supporting Actors.**
These are external services the system depends on.

- *ISBN Lookup Service* — used during book registration.
- *Payment Gateway* — used for fine payment.

**Step 4 — Identify Offstage Actors.**
- *Library Director* — interested in monthly reports but does not interact with the system directly.

**Step 5 — Enumerate Use-Cases (User-Goal level).**

- Issue Book
- Return Book
- Register New Member
- Add New Book
- Search Catalog
- Pay Fine

**Step 6 — Identify Sub-Function Use-Cases (Fish level).**

- Validate Member
- Calculate Fine
- Notify Overdue (requires Supporting Actor: SMS Gateway)

**Step 7 — Establish Relationships.**

- *Issue Book* `<<include>>` *Validate Member* (mandatory)
- *Return Book* `<<include>>` *Calculate Fine* (mandatory if overdue)
- *Pay Fine* `<<include>>` *Process Payment* (which is a helper)
- *Issue Book* `<<extend>>` *Notify Overdue* (extension added if book is not returned on time)

**Step 8 — Apply Generalization.**

- *Premium Member* and *Regular Member* both generalize from *Member*; both inherit "Issue Book" and "Return Book", but only *Premium Member* can "Reserve Book".

**Step 9 — Validate.**

- Each use-case delivers observable value to an actor.
- Each use-case has at least one primary actor.
- No use-case is purely internal to the system.

**Step 10 — Document.**

For the use-case *Issue Book*, the use-case specification would be:

- **Name:** Issue Book
- **Primary Actor:** Borrower
- **Supporting Actors:** ISBN Lookup Service
- **Precondition:** Borrower is a registered member; book is available.
- **Postcondition:** Book is issued; loan record is created.
- **Trigger:** Borrower presents book and membership card at the desk.
- **Main Flow:**
  1. Borrower approaches the librarian with a book.
  2. Librarian scans the membership card.
  3. System validates the member (via `<<include>>` Validate Member).
  4. Librarian scans the book barcode.
  5. System records the loan.
  6. System prints a due-date slip.
  7. Librarian hands over the book.
- **Alternative Flow A — Book Unavailable:**
  - At step 5, system reports the book is reserved/issued.
  - Use-case terminates unsuccessfully.
- **Alternative Flow B — Member Has Outstanding Fines:**
  - At step 3, the `Validate Member` sub-use-case returns "blocked".
  - Use-case terminates; borrower is informed.

### 3.3 Quantitative Worked Example — Use-Case Metrics

Given the Library System above, suppose:

- $N_{UC} = 6$ (user-goal use-cases)
- $N_{A} = 4$ (Borrower, Librarian, ISBN Service, Payment Gateway)
- Total include-edges = 4
- Total extend-edges = 2
- For a single use-case "Issue Book": Steps = 7, AltFlows = 2, Constraints = 3, with $w_1 = 1$, $w_2 = 2$, $w_3 = 3$.

Compute the use-case complexity index for *Issue Book*:

$$
C_{uc} = w_1 \cdot \text{Steps} + w_2 \cdot \text{AltFlows} + w_3 \cdot \text{Constraints}
$$

$$
C_{uc} = 1 \cdot 7 + 2 \cdot 2 + 3 \cdot 3
$$

$$
C_{uc} = 7 + 4 + 9
$$

$$
\boxed{C_{uc} = 20}
$$

Compute the average use-cases per actor:

$$
\bar{UC}_{A} = \dfrac{N_{UC}}{N_{A}} = \dfrac{6}{4} = 1.5
$$

Compute the include-reuse density:

$$
R_{\text{inc}} = \dfrac{N_{\text{include edges}}}{N_{UC}} = \dfrac{4}{6} \approx 0.667
$$

Compute the extension density:

$$
R_{\text{ext}} = \dfrac{N_{\text{extend edges}}}{N_{UC}} = \dfrac{2}{6} \approx 0.333
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A reuse density of ~0.67 means about two-thirds of the user-goal use-cases reuse at least one sub-function. This is a healthy modularization indicator, often used in design reviews.

### 3.4 Use-Case Realization — Deriving Objects from Use-Cases

Jacobson's second key insight is that **each use-case must be "realized"** — i.e., mapped onto collaborating objects. The procedure is:

$$
\text{Use-Case} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Realization Diagram} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Object Diagram / Sequence Diagram}
$$

In a sequence diagram, each step in the use-case's main flow becomes a message between objects. The actors trigger the use-case; the boundary objects (UI) receive input; the entity objects (domain) hold data; the control objects orchestrate flow.

This mapping is the **bridge** between requirements and design in Jacobson's methodology, and is the conceptual predecessor to today's **BCE (Boundary-Control-Entity) pattern** used in software architecture.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Jacobson's OOSE Big Picture — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    reqA[Requirements Capture] --> ucm[Use-Case Model]
    dom[Domain Object Model] --> arch[Implementation Model]
    ucm --> arch
    arch --> anlys[Analysis]
    anlys --> constr[Construction]
    constr --> test[Test]
    test --> impl[Implemented System]
    impl --> mnt[Maintenance]
    mnt --> reqA
    ucm -.realizes.-> anlys
    dom -.refines.-> anlys
    arch -.guides.-> constr
    styleucm[Use-Case Model]
    styledom[Domain Object Model]
    stylearch[Implementation Model]
    styleanlys[Analysis]
    styleconstr[Construction]
    styletest[Test]
    styleimpl[Implemented System]
    stylemnt[Maintenance]
    stylerea[Requirements Capture]
```

> The diagram illustrates Jacobson's iterative loop: requirements feed the use-case model, which together with the domain and implementation models drives analysis, construction, testing, and the final implemented system. Maintenance loops back to fresh requirements capture.

    director[Library Director]

    borrower --- uc1
    borrower --- uc2
    borrower --- uc5
    borrower --- uc6
    librarian --- uc3
    librarian --- uc4
    librarian --- uc5
    librarian --- uc11
    director --- uc11
    isbn --- uc4
    paygw --- uc9
    uc1 -. include .-> uc7
    uc2 -. include .-> uc7
    uc2 -. include .-> uc8
    uc6 -. include .-> uc9
    uc2 -. extend .-> uc10

```

> In this diagram, the actors are the stick-figures, the use-cases are the ellipses, solid lines are associations, and the dashed arrows with stereotypes represent `<<include>>` and `<<extend>>` relationships.

### 4.3 Actor Generalization Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    member[Member]
    reg[Regular Member]
    prem[Premium Member]
    member --- reg
    member --- prem
    prem -. inherits .-> ucr1((Reserve Book))
    reg -. inherits .-> ucr2((Issue Book))
    prem -. inherits .-> ucr2
    stylemember[Member]
    stylereg[Regular Member]
    styleprem[Premium Member]
    styleucr1[Reserve Book]
    styleucr2[Issue Book]
```

> The diagram shows that *Regular Member* and *Premium Member* are specializations of *Member*, and they inherit the *Issue Book* use-case, but only *Premium Member* enjoys *Reserve Book*.

### 4.4 Lifecycle of a Use-Case in the Jacobson Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    id1[Identification] --> id2[Drafting]
    id2 --> id3[Review with Stakeholders]
    id3 --> id4[Refinement]
    id4 --> id5[Realization]
    id5 --> id6[Implementation]
    id6 --> id7[Test]
    id7 --> id8[Deployed and Maintained]
    styleid1[Identification]
    styleid2[Drafting]
    styleid3[Review with Stakeholders]
    styleid4[Refinement]
    styleid5[Realization]
    styleid6[Implementation]
    styleid7[Test]
    styleid8[Deployed and Maintained]
```

> This flow shows how a single use-case evolves from initial identification through to deployment and maintenance, and matches Jacobson's iterative philosophy.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`** Differentiate between **primary** and **supporting actors** with an example.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **primary actor** is an external entity whose goal the system exists to satisfy. The system serves this actor. *Example:* A *Customer* using an ATM to withdraw cash is a primary actor because the ATM is built primarily to serve the customer.

A **supporting actor** is an external entity that the system itself uses to fulfill its purpose; the system serves *from* this actor. *Example:* The *Bank's central server* that the ATM contacts to authorize a transaction is a supporting actor.

| Aspect | Primary Actor | Supporting Actor |
|---|---|---|
| Direction of service | System → Actor | Actor → System |
| Example in ATM | Customer | Bank Authorization Server |
| Initiates use-case? | Yes | No (reactive) |

*(Award full 3 marks only if the example and one distinguishing line are present.)*

---

#### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`** Explain the difference between `<<include>>` and `<<extend>>` relationships in a use-case diagram.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

`<<include>>` represents **mandatory reuse** — the base use-case *always* requires the included use-case. The arrow points from the *base* to the *included*. *Example:* "Withdraw Cash" `<<include>>` "Validate PIN".

`<<extend>>` represents **optional or exceptional behavior** — the extending use-case adds behavior to the base under specific conditions. The arrow points from the *extending* to the *base*. *Example:* "Withdraw Cash" `<<extend>>` "Print Help" (only if user presses the Help button).

| Aspect | `<<include>>` | `<<extend>>` |
|---|---|---|
| Mandatory? | Yes | No |
| Arrow direction | Base → Supplier | Extender → Base |
| Typical use | Common sub-function | Variant / exceptional flow |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Jacobson Methodology (OOSE)** in detail. Discuss the role of *actors* and *use-cases* with a real-world banking example.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Construct a **UML Use-Case Diagram** for a *Hospital Management System* with at least **4 actors** and **6 use-cases**, showing `<<include>>` and `<<extend>>` relationships. Justify each relationship with a one-line rationale.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Jacobson Methodology — 7 Marks**

The **Jacobson Methodology (Object-Oriented Software Engineering, OOSE)** is a use-case driven, architecture-centric, iterative–incremental software engineering method proposed by **Ivar Jacobson** in 1992. The methodology organizes the entire development lifecycle around three models that evolve in parallel:

1. **Use-Case Model** — describes the *functional requirements* of the system from the perspective of external actors. It captures the *who* (actors) and *what* (use-cases) of the system.
2. **Domain Object Model** — describes the *objects*, *classes*, and their *relationships* in the problem domain.
3. **Implementation/Architectural Model** — describes the *physical and logical architecture* — components, processes, and their deployment.

The methodology proceeds in **phases**: Requirements, Analysis, Construction, and Testing, with each phase iterating over the three models. Use-cases act as the *thread of continuity* — every analysis class, every test case, every code module traces back to a use-case.

**Banking Example — 5 sub-points (1 mark each):**

- **Primary Actor:** *Customer* — wants to withdraw money and check balance.
- **Supporting Actor:** *Bank Server* — authorizes transactions.
- **Offstage Actor:** *Tax Authority* — interested in yearly interest reports.
- **Use-Case 1 — "Withdraw Cash":** Triggered by Customer, `<<include>>` "Authenticate User".
- **Use-Case 2 — "Check Balance":** Returns current balance to Customer.
- **Use-Case 3 — "Generate Annual Interest Report":** Generated periodically, Offstage Actor Tax Authority receives it.

*(Allocate marks: 3 marks for methodology, 4 marks for banking example with at least 2 actors and 2 use-cases.)*

**(b) Use-Case Diagram for Hospital Management System — 7 Marks**

**Actors (4):**

1. *Patient* (primary)
2. *Doctor* (primary)
3. *Receptionist* (primary)
4. *Insurance System* (supporting)

**Use-Cases (6):**

1. *Book Appointment*
2. *Cancel Appointment*
3. *View Medical History*
4. *Update Treatment Record*
5. *Generate Bill*
6. *Verify Insurance*

**Diagram (textual representation; equivalent to UML):**

```
   Patient          Doctor         Receptionist       Insurance System
     |                |                |                    |
     |---Book Appt----|                |                    |
     |                |---View Hist----|                    |
     |                |---Update Treat-|                    |
     |                |                |---Gen Bill---------|
     |---Cancel Appt->|                |                    |
     |                |                |---Verify Ins-------|
     |                |                |                    |
     |---Book Appt---include--->---Validate Patient       |
     |---Gen Bill----include--->---Verify Insurance        |
     |---Cancel Apt--extend---->---Notify Waitlist         |
```

**Justifications (1 mark each for valid justifications of include/extend, capped at 3 marks):**

- `Book Appointment` `<<include>>` `Validate Patient` — every appointment booking *must* validate the patient, hence mandatory inclusion.
- `Generate Bill` `<<include>>` `Verify Insurance` — billing *always* requires insurance verification, hence mandatory.
- `Cancel Appointment` `<<extend>>` `Notify Waitlist` — waitlist notification is *optional*, executed only if there is a waiting patient. Hence optional extension.

*(Allocate 3 marks for diagram correctness, 1 mark each for three justified relationships.)*

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — **Alternative Choice**
**`[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the **anatomy of a use-case specification** in Jacobson's methodology. List all fields and explain each with a one-line example.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For a *University Course Registration System*, identify **3 actors** (one of each type — primary, supporting, offstage) and **5 use-cases**. Build a use-case diagram and compute the following metrics:
   (i) Average use-cases per actor
   (ii) Include-reuse density
   (iii) Use-case complexity index for the use-case *"Register for Course"* if it has 8 main-flow steps, 3 alternative flows, and 4 special requirements. Use $w_1 = 1$, $w_2 = 2$, $w_3 = 3$.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Anatomy of a Use-Case Specification — 7 Marks**

A **use-case specification** in Jacobson's methodology is a structured text document that fully describes a single use-case. It is the *primary deliverable* of the requirements phase.

**Fields of a Use-Case Specification (7 fields × 1 mark each, capped at 7):**

| Field | Definition | Example for *Withdraw Cash* |
|---|---|---|
| **Name** | Verb–noun phrase | "Withdraw Cash" |
| **Actors** | Primary and supporting participants | Customer, Bank Server |
| **Preconditions** | State that must hold before | "Account active with balance > 0" |
| **Postconditions** | State that holds after success | "Cash dispensed, account debited" |
| **Trigger** | Event that starts the use-case | "Customer inserts card" |
| **Main Flow** | Numbered steps of the happy path | Step 1: Insert card ... Step 6: Take cash |
| **Alternative Flows** | Exception/variant paths | "Invalid PIN", "Insufficient funds" |

(One additional sub-point of 1 mark): The use-case specification is paired with a **use-case diagram** entry; together they form the complete use-case model artifact.

**(b) University Course Registration System — 7 Marks**

**Actors:**

1. *Student* (Primary) — wants to register for courses.
2. *Payment Gateway* (Supporting) — system uses it to collect fees.
3. *Accreditation Body* (Offstage) — receives semester reports.

**Use-Cases (5):**

1. *Register for Course*
2. *Drop Course*
3. *View Transcript*
4. *Pay Tuition*
5. *Generate Semester Report*

**Use-Case Diagram (textual):**

```
  Student --------Register for Course
     |                  |
     |                  |---include---> Validate Prerequisite
  Student ----Drop Course
     |                  |
     |                  |---include---> Calculate Refund
  Student ----View Transcript
  Student ----Pay Tuition
                     |
                     |---include---> Process Payment (Payment Gateway)
  Accreditation Body ----Generate Semester Report
```

**Metrics (5 marks):**

- Number of actors $N_{A} = 3$
- Number of use-cases $N_{UC} = 5$
- Number of include-edges = 3

**(i) Average use-cases per actor (1 mark):**

$$
\bar{UC}_{A} = \dfrac{N_{UC}}{N_{A}} = \dfrac{5}{3} \approx 1.67
$$

**(ii) Include-reuse density (1 mark):**

$$
R_{\text{inc}} = \dfrac{N_{\text{include edges}}}{N_{UC}} = \dfrac{3}{5} = 0.6
$$

**(iii) Use-case complexity for "Register for Course" (3 marks):**

$$
C_{uc} = w_1 \cdot \text{Steps} + w_2 \cdot \text{AltFlows} + w_3 \cdot \text{Constraints}
$$

$$
C_{uc} = 1 \cdot 8 + 2 \cdot 3 + 3 \cdot 4
$$

$$
C_{uc} = 8 + 6 + 12
$$

$$
\boxed{C_{uc} = 26}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing `<<include>>` and `<<extend>>` arrow direction:** Most students lose 1–2 marks per question by drawing the arrow the wrong way. Remember: *include = base needs the helper; extend = helper adds to base optionally*.
> 2. **Treating use-cases as functions:** A use-case *always* has an actor and delivers *observable value*. A pure internal calculation (e.g., "Compute SHA-256") is **not** a use-case at the user-goal level.
> 3. **Forgetting offstage actors:** Examiners often award a 1-mark bonus for correctly identifying an offstage actor, but most students miss them.
> 4. **Listing classes as actors:** Actors are *external* to the system. The database server, while external, is usually **not** an actor unless it initiates or receives information. A class within the system is never an actor.
> 5. **In metric problems, not stating units or assumptions:** Always mention that $w_1, w_2, w_3$ are weighting constants and present final answers with units (count or score).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Jacobson Methodology (OOSE)** is *use-case driven*, *architecture-centric*, and *iterative–incremental*.
- The **Use-Case Model** is the central artifact; it contains *actors*, *use-cases*, and their *relationships*.
- **Actor** = external role (not a concrete user). **Use-Case** = a unit of system functionality delivering *observable value* to an actor.
- **Three actor types:** Primary (served by system), Supporting (serves the system), Offstage (interested but non-interacting).
- **Three use-case relationships:** Association, `<<include>>` (mandatory reuse), `<<extend>>` (optional/exceptional). **Generalization** is a fourth.
- **Use-Case Specification** has at least seven fields: Name, Actors, Pre/Postconditions, Trigger, Main Flow, Alternative Flows, Special Requirements.
- **Three use-case levels:** Summary (cloud), User-Goal (sea), Sub-function (fish). User-Goal is the most common target.
- **Use-Case Realization** is the bridge from requirements to design — each use-case is mapped to collaborating objects in sequence/collaboration diagrams.
- **Key metrics:** $\bar{UC}_{A} = N_{UC} / N_{A}$, $R_{\text{inc}}$, $R_{\text{ext}}$, and the complexity index $C_{uc} = w_1 \cdot \text{Steps} + w_2 \cdot \text{AltFlows} + w_3 \cdot \text{Constraints}$.
- **For KTU exams:** Always draw a neat use-case diagram, label all relationships, and provide at least one example use-case specification in long-answer questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analysis: Object Modeling - Identifying Object Classes, Preparing a Data Dictionary, Identifying Associations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCNT604 - OBJECT ORIENTED MODELING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module - 2
> - **Topic:** Analysis: Object Modeling - Identifying Object Classes, Preparing a Data Dictionary, Identifying Associations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Object Modeling (OM)?

**Object Modeling** is the first and most foundational phase of the **Object Modeling Technique (OMT)**, proposed by James Rumbaugh. It is the process of identifying the important **objects (classes)** in the application domain and capturing the **static, structural relationships** between them, completely independent of any procedural or temporal behavior. The output is a **Class Diagram** (a.k.a. Object Diagram in OMT terminology) that depicts the **static data model** of the system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping (Module 2):**
> *Analysis: Object Modeling* — Identifying Object Classes, Preparing a Data Dictionary, Identifying Associations. This topic is **high-weightage** in Part B questions (14-mark problems) and frequently appears as a Part A (3-mark) definition question.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Blueprints of a House"

Imagine you are an architect designing a house. Before you think about *how* the family will move through it, *when* lights will turn on, or *what happens* during a fire, you must first list **what physical things exist** (rooms, doors, windows, walls, pipes) and **how they are connected** (a door *connects* two rooms; a window is *part-of* a wall).

| Real-World Analogy | Object Modeling Equivalent |
|---|---|
| A house | The **System** being built |
| Rooms, doors, windows | **Object Classes** |
| The wall between two rooms | An **Association** |
| The blueprint document | The **Class Diagram** + **Data Dictionary** |
| A label on a pipe (e.g., "1-inch copper") | An **Attribute definition** in Data Dictionary |

In short: **Object Modeling is the act of building a *structural blueprint* of a software system before worrying about its dynamic behaviour or implementation.**

---

## 1.2 The Three Pillars of Object Modeling

The KTU syllabus divides this topic into **three sequential sub-tasks**, which together form the Analysis phase:

### Pillar 1 — Identifying Object Classes
The act of *discovering* which real-world entities become software classes. We use the **Noun Phrase Approach** on the problem statement, supplemented by **Classical Categories** (tangible things, events, roles, locations) and a **Common Object List** (Specification, Description, etc.).

### Pillar 2 — Preparing a Data Dictionary
A **central, formal repository** describing every class, attribute, relationship, and operation in precise, unambiguous language. It is the *glossary of the project* that eliminates ambiguity between developers, analysts, and clients.

### Pillar 3 — Identifying Associations
The act of discovering the **semantic relationships** that exist between the discovered object classes (e.g., *owns*, *works-for*, *contains*, *references*). Associations answer the question: *"How do objects of different classes talk to each other?"*

> [!NOTE]
> **Order of execution is critical**: Classes are identified **first** (you cannot have relationships between nothing), then the **Data Dictionary** is drafted (to fix terminology), and finally **Associations** are discovered and added. The Data Dictionary is updated continuously throughout the project.

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Formal Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Object Class** | A set of objects that share a common structure and behaviour | `Student`, `Course`, `BankAccount` |
| **Instance** | A specific, concrete object that belongs to a class | `student101`, `account_ABC` |
| **Attribute** | A named property of a class that holds a value | `name`, `balance`, `rollNo` |
| **Association** | A semantic relationship between two (or more) classes | `Student` *enrolls in* `Course` |
| **Aggregation** | A stronger "whole–part" association (a *is-part-of* b) | `Department` **1** — **\*** `Faculty` |
| **Multiplicity** | How many objects participate on each end of an association | `1`, `*`, `0..1`, `1..*` |
| **Role** | The name given to an end of an association | `employer` and `employee` on `Person`–`Company` |
| **Data Dictionary** | Formal documentation of all model elements | "A `Student` has exactly one `name`…" |

> [!TIP]
> **Why is this module so heavily tested at KTU?**
> Because it is the **gateway skill** for all subsequent OMT diagrams (Dynamic Model, Functional Model). If classes and associations are wrong at this stage, the entire object-oriented design collapses downstream. Examiners therefore test it rigorously for *correctness of noun extraction, association filtering, and multiplicity assignment*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sub-Task 1: Identifying Object Classes (Detailed Methodology)

Object class identification is **the most heuristic-driven activity** in the entire OMT methodology. There is no single deterministic algorithm; instead, Rumbaugh prescribes a **multi-source, filter-based** approach.

### Step 1A — Reuse Existing Classes

Before discovering anything new, **scan libraries, previous projects, domain frameworks, and design patterns**. Object-orientation is fundamentally about reuse. If a `Customer` class already exists in a `Library` of reusable classes, you should import it rather than reinvent it.

### Step 1B — Identify Candidate Classes (3 Source Techniques)

#### Technique 1: Classical Categories
Rumbaugh's classical object categories (sometimes called *Coad & Yourdon's categories*):

| Category | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Tangible things** (objects) | Easily perceivable real-world items | `Book`, `Car`, `Cashier` |
| **Roles** | People playing a part in the system | `Customer`, `Pilot`, `Doctor` |
| **Events** | Things that happen, often with a timestamp | `Sale`, `Flight`, `Meeting` |
| **Locations** | Physical or conceptual places | `Warehouse`, `Branch`, `ServerRoom` |
| **Organizational units** | Collections of people/resources | `Department`, `Branch`, `Team` |

#### Technique 2: Noun Phrase Approach (Most KTU-Favoured)
Read the **problem statement / requirements document** and list **every noun (or noun phrase)** as a potential class candidate. This is the method that appears in **almost every KTU exam**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Method in Detail:**
> 1. Read the problem statement **twice** — once for gist, once for nouns.
> 2. Underline every noun.
> 3. Write each unique noun in a list — these are **candidates**.
> 4. **Do not** list pronouns (he, she, it) or generic nouns ("the system", "the user").

#### Technique 3: Common Object List
Rumbaugh's well-known list of objects that appear in *almost every* information system:

`Specification`, `Description`, `Instance`, `Document`, `Reference`, `Item`, `Place`, `Container`, `Field`, `Log`, `Notification`, `Profile`, `Plan`, `Rule`, `Transaction`, `History`, `Record`, `Report`, `Schedule`, `Form`, `List`, `Group`, `Member`, `Note`, `Memo`.

### Step 1C — Apply Selection Criteria (Filtering)

A noun is a *good* class if it meets **all** of these criteria:

1. **It contains enough information to remember** (i.e., it has *retained information* worth storing across time).
2. **It provides a needed service** (other classes will call its operations).
3. **It has multiple attributes** (one-attribute classes are usually red flags).
4. **It has multiple instances** within the problem domain.
5. **It has a clear scope** — both the developer and the client can agree on its meaning.

### Step 1D — Eliminate Non-Classes (Common Filtering Errors)

| Redundant / Wrong Candidate | Reason for Elimination | Action |
|---|---|---|
| Synonym of another class | "Vehicle" and "Car" — Car is a subclass | Keep the more specific one |
| Outside the system scope | "Government" in a bank ATM problem | Delete |
| A *property*, not an object | "Age" is a property of Person | Make it an **attribute** |
| An *operation*, not an object | "Payment" is a verb / event | Reframe carefully (often a class) |
| Role of a person | "Customer" is a *role* played by Person | Usually **kept** as a class |
| Implementation leak | "ArrayList", "Database" | Remove — these are not domain objects |

### Step 1E — Naming Conventions for Classes

KTU expects students to follow the **OMT standard**:
- **Capitalised** (`Student`, not `student`).
- **Singular** noun (`Course`, not `Courses`).
- **Specific** (`SavingsAccount` is better than `Account` if both exist).
- **Domain-accurate** — no implementation words like "Manager", "Handler" unless they belong to the domain.
- **No abbreviation** unless industry-standard (`URL`, `ATM` are fine).

### Step 1F — Adding Attributes (Light Form)

After filtering, each surviving class is augmented with essential attributes. KTU questions often ask to "list the attributes of each class" — these are typically:
- **Required** attributes mentioned in the problem.
- **Identifier** attribute (`id`, `rollNo`, `accountNumber`).
- **Foreign-key-like** references that will later become associations.

---

## 2.2 Sub-Task 2: Preparing a Data Dictionary

The **Data Dictionary (DD)** is a *centrally maintained, textual* document that records **every modelling decision** taken in the project. It is the single source of truth that prevents misinterpretation.

### 2.2.1 Why is a Data Dictionary Mandatory?

> [!NOTE]
> *In real OMT projects, the data dictionary is the contract between analyst, designer, and programmer. A class diagram without a data dictionary is legally non-binding.*

### 2.2.2 What Goes Inside a Data Dictionary Entry?

For **every** element in the class diagram, the DD must record:

| Model Element | Information Recorded |
|---|---|
| **Class** | Meaning, purpose, scope notes, assumptions, owner |
| **Attribute** | Name, type, units, allowed range, default, NULL policy |
| **Association** | Meaning, participating classes, multiplicity, constraints |
| **Operation** | Signature, preconditions, postconditions, semantics |
| **System** | Top-level description of the system being modelled |

### 2.2.3 Standard Data Dictionary Format (KTU Convention)

```
CLASS <ClassName>
   MEANING : <human-readable definition>
   ATTRIBUTES :
      <attrName> : <Type>     [range/default/constraints]
      <attrName> : <Type>     [...]
   OPERATIONS :
      <opName>(<params>) : <returnType>   [purpose]
   ASSOCIATIONS :
      <roleName> of <OtherClass>  [multiplicity]
   CONSTRAINTS : <textual rules not expressible on the diagram>
```

### 2.2.4 Example Data Dictionary Entry

```
CLASS Order
   MEANING : A customer's request to purchase one or more items.
   ATTRIBUTES :
      orderId     : Integer    [unique, 6-digit, auto-generated]
      orderDate   : Date       [default = system date]
      totalAmount : Currency   [>= 0, derived]
      status      : Enum       [one of {Placed, Shipped, Delivered, Cancelled}]
   OPERATIONS :
      calculateTotal() : Currency
      cancel() : void
   ASSOCIATIONS :
      placedBy      of Customer     [exactly 1]
      contains      of OrderItem    [1..*]
      processedBy   of Employee     [0..1]
   CONSTRAINTS :
      An Order must contain at least 1 OrderItem.
      A cancelled Order cannot be shipped.
```

---

## 2.3 Sub-Task 3: Identifying Associations

### 2.3.1 What is an Association?

An **association** is a *semantic relationship* between two or more object classes that describes a *meaningful connection* between their instances. It is **drawn as a line** between the class boxes in a class diagram.

Formally:

$$ A \subseteq C_1 \times C_2 \times \dots \times C_n $$

For a binary association between classes $C_1$ and $C_2$, the set of links is a subset of the Cartesian product of their instance sets. For ternary associations, three classes are involved simultaneously.

### 2.3.2 Steps to Identify Associations

#### Step 3A — Identify Preliminary Associations
Use the **verb phrase approach** on the problem statement. Read between the noun-phrases identified earlier. Common verb-phrase associations include:

| Verb Phrase | Association Name |
|---|---|
| owns, has, contains | possession |
| works for, employs, reports to | management |
| talks to, communicates with | communication |
| is a kind of | **generalization** (not a plain association) |
| is part of, consists of | **aggregation** |
| contains, includes | containment |
| refers to, points to | reference |

#### Step 3B — Filter Associations (Rumbaugh's Filter)
A candidate association is *kept* if **all** of these hold:

1. It is **knowledge-level** — knowledge that must be preserved across time, not a transient computation.
2. It is **not derived** — the relationship itself is not already a mathematical derivation of another association.
3. It is **not implementation noise** — "calls", "uses" (procedural) are not associations; they are messages.

Eliminate if it represents:
- An *operation* between classes (e.g., "transfers money to") — only keep if *both* classes need to remember it.
- A *ternary* association that should be split into **binary** associations (Rumbaugh prefers binary).
- An *implementation* dependency (e.g., "uses ArrayList").

#### Step 3C — Document Each Association
For each surviving association, the Data Dictionary must record:
- **Name** (verb phrase, often a noun: `enrolment`, `ownership`).
- **Roles** at each end (the meaning of the participation).
- **Multiplicity** (see below).
- **Path** (binary vs ternary).

### 2.3.3 Multiplicity — The Most Tested Property

**Multiplicity** states *how many* instances of one class can participate in the association with *one* instance of the other class.

| Notation | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `1` | Exactly one |
| `*` or `0..*` | Zero or more |
| `1..*` | One or more |
| `0..1` | Optional (zero or one) |
| `m..n` | Between m and n inclusive |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Association vs. Aggregation vs. Composition**
> - **Association** (`—`): generic "knows about" or "uses".
> - **Aggregation** (`◇—`): weaker whole–part; parts can exist without the whole. Example: `Department ◇— Employee`.
> - **Composition** (`◆—`): stronger whole–part; parts die with the whole. Example: `Window ◆— Frame`.
