### 2.3.4 Roles

Each **end of an association** is called a *role* (or *participant*). Roles:
- Are especially useful when the **same class appears twice** in an association (e.g., `Person — "employer" — Company — "employee" — Person`).
- Have a **name** shown at their end of the line.

### 2.3.5 Ternary Associations (Rare but Important)

A *ternary* association involves **three classes simultaneously**. Example: a `Customer` buys a `Product` at a `Store` on a `Date`. Rumbaugh discourages ternary associations because they are conceptually difficult and require diamond-shaped lines on the diagram. They can usually be **decomposed into binary associations plus a link class**.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formal Expression / Rule | KTU Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Object class filter | "Retained info" ∧ "Provides service" ∧ "Has attributes" ∧ "Has instances" | Use in filtering nouns |
| 2 | Association set | $A \subseteq C_1 \times C_2$ (binary) | Theoretical definition |
| 3 | Multiplicity range | $0..*, 0..1, 1..*, m..n$ | Always mark on every end |
| 4 | Aggregation | "is-part-of" with shared lifecycle independence | $D \;\lozenge\!-\!-\; E$ |
| 5 | Composition | "is-part-of" with shared lifecycle dependence | $D \;\blacklozenge\!-\!-\; E$ |
| 6 | Generalization | "is-a-kind-of" (NOT an association; IS inheritance) | Triangle arrow |
| 7 | Data dictionary entry template | See Section 2.2.3 | Must include 5 fields per element |
| 8 | Noun Phrase Rule | Underline every noun in problem statement | Step 1B in 90% of KTU questions |
| 9 | Verb Phrase Rule | Identify verb-phrases between candidate classes | Step 3A in association discovery |
| 10 | Ternary Decomposition | Convert $A(C_1, C_2, C_3)$ into a link class | Avoids diamond diagrams |
| 11 | Role Naming | Required when a class appears twice | Example: employer/employee |
| 12 | Naming Convention | Singular, Capitalised, no implementation terms | Mandatory in all class diagrams |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:**
> Object Modeling (especially class diagrams) is the **backbone of every UML-based software engineering project** — from enterprise Java/Spring apps to embedded systems in automotive, aerospace, and medical device industries. Tools like *Enterprise Architect*, *Rational Rose*, *StarUML*, and *MagicDraw* read these class diagrams to auto-generate code skeletons, database schemas, and even entire API documentation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Example & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Canonical Worked Example: A Library Management System

> **Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
> *"A library lends books to registered members. Each book has a unique ISBN, a title, an author, and a category. Each member has a membership ID, name, address, and phone. A member can issue up to 3 books at a time, and the issue is recorded with a date. The library has multiple branches, and each branch keeps track of its own stock."*

We will walk through **all three sub-tasks** of Object Modeling for this problem.

---

### STEP 1 — Identify Object Classes

#### 1A — Noun Phrase Extraction
We read the problem statement and extract every unique noun:

`library`, `book`, `member`, `ISBN`, `title`, `author`, `category`, `membership ID`, `name`, `address`, `phone`, `issue`, `date`, `branch`, `stock`

#### 1B — Apply Classical Category Mapping

| Extracted Noun | Category | Decision |
|---|---|---|
| library | organizational unit / location | **Keep as class** `Library` |
| book | tangible thing | **Keep as class** `Book` |
| member | role | **Keep as class** `Member` |
| ISBN | attribute of book | Make an **attribute** |
| title | attribute of book | Make an **attribute** |
| author | attribute of book (or class?) | Make an **attribute** if simple string; make a class if multiple authors needed |
| category | attribute of book | Make an **attribute** (or enum) |
| membership ID | attribute of member | Make an **attribute** |
| name | attribute of member | Make an **attribute** |
| address | attribute of member | Make an **attribute** |
| phone | attribute of member | Make an **attribute** |
| issue | event | **Keep as class** `Issue` |
| date | attribute of issue | Make an **attribute** of `Issue` |
| branch | organizational unit | **Keep as class** `Branch` |
| stock | attribute of branch / relationship | Becomes an **association** between `Branch` and `Book` |

#### 1C — Apply Common Object List Check
The noun `record` (mentioned as "the issue is recorded") — yes, `Issue` captures this. No additional common objects apply.

#### 1D — Final List of Classes (after filtering)

$$
\boxed{\text{Classes} = \{\, \text{Library}, \text{Branch}, \text{Book}, \text{Member}, \text{Issue}, \text{Author} \,\}}
$$

> We promoted `Author` to a class because the problem says "an author" (singular, but a book can have multiple authors in real libraries — KTU examiners love this test). If the problem had said "the author name is a string", we would have kept it as an attribute.

#### 1E — Add Attributes

| Class | Attributes |
|---|---|
| `Library` | `libraryId`, `name`, `address` |
| `Branch` | `branchId`, `branchName`, `location` |
| `Book` | `ISBN`, `title`, `category`, `price` |
| `Member` | `memberId`, `name`, `address`, `phone` |
| `Issue` | `issueId`, `issueDate`, `returnDate` |
| `Author` | `authorId`, `authorName` |

---

### STEP 2 — Prepare the Data Dictionary

We now formalise the model. Below is a **complete Data Dictionary entry** for `Book` and `Member`. (In a real KTU answer, you would write one entry per class.)

```
CLASS Book
   MEANING : A physical or digital item available for lending in a library branch.
   ATTRIBUTES :
      ISBN    : String  [13-character unique identifier, mandatory]
      title   : String  [max 200 characters, mandatory]
      category: Enum    [one of {Fiction, Non-Fiction, Reference, Magazine}]
      price   : Currency[>= 0, default 0.00]
   OPERATIONS :
      checkAvailability(branchId) : Boolean
      getAuthor() : String
   ASSOCIATIONS :
      writtenBy   of Author   [1..*]   // a book has 1 or more authors
      heldBy      of Branch   [1..*]   // a book is stocked in 1 or more branches
      issuedUnder of Issue    [0..*]   // a book may be issued 0 or more times
   CONSTRAINTS :
      ISBN must be unique across the entire library system.
      A book must be stocked in at least 1 branch.

CLASS Member
   MEANING : A person registered with the library who can issue books.
   ATTRIBUTES :
      memberId : Integer [unique, 5-digit]
      name     : String  [max 100 characters]
      address  : String  [max 250 characters]
      phone    : String  [10-digit Indian mobile format]
   OPERATIONS :
      issueBook(bookISBN) : Issue
      returnBook(issueId) : void
      getIssueHistory()   : List<Issue>
   ASSOCIATIONS :
      memberOf   of Library     [exactly 1]
      borrowsVia of Issue       [0..*]
   CONSTRAINTS :
      A member can have at most 3 active issues at any time.
      phone must be unique across the system.
```

---

### STEP 3 — Identify Associations

#### 3A — Verb Phrase Extraction
We read between the nouns: *lends books to*, *has a*, *is registered with*, *is recorded with*, *keeps track of its own stock*, *has multiple branches*.

#### 3B — Filtered Associations (with multiplicity and roles)

| # | Association | Classes | Multiplicity | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Library *owns* Branch | Library — Branch | `1` — `*` | Each branch belongs to exactly 1 library |
| 2 | Branch *stocks* Book | Branch — Book | `*` — `*` | A branch stocks many books; a book may be in many branches |
| 3 | Book *is written by* Author | Book — Author | `*` — `1..*` | A book has at least 1 author; an author writes many books |
| 4 | Member *issues* Book (via Issue) | Member — Issue — Book | Ternary, decomposed | Issue is the link class |
| 5 | Library *registers* Member | Library — Member | `1` — `*` | Each member belongs to one library |

#### 3C — Ternary Decomposition of "Issue"
A `Member` issues a `Book` on a `Date` is a **ternary** relationship. We model this with an **association class** `Issue`:

$$
\text{Issue} = \text{link\_class}(\text{Member}, \text{Book}, \text{Date})
$$

Decomposed into **two binary** associations:
- `Member — Issue` (multiplicity `1 — *`)
- `Book — Issue` (multiplicity `1 — *`)

The `issueDate` becomes an attribute of `Issue`.

> [!TIP]
> **Why decompose?** Ternary associations (diamond shapes) are notoriously hard to maintain and reason about. Splitting them into a link class gives you a clean binary structure with one extra class for attributes (e.g., `issueDate`, `returnDate`).

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: How to Choose Between Aggregation, Composition, and Plain Association

This is a **frequently asked 7-mark sub-question** in KTU. Use this decision matrix:

| Criterion | Plain Association | Aggregation (◇) | Composition (◆) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Lifecycle dependence | Independent | Independent | Part dies with whole |
| Example | `Teacher — Student` (mutual references) | `Department ◇— Faculty` | `Window ◆— Pane` |
| Multiplicity typical | `* — *` or `1 — *` | `1 — *` | `1 — *` |
| Sharing allowed | Yes | Yes (Faculty can be in 2 depts) | No (Pane is in only 1 Window) |
| Strength of "part-of" | None | Weak | Strong |

**Application rule (decision tree):**

$$
\text{Is the part a "logical or physical component" of the whole?}
\begin{cases}
\text{No} \to \text{Plain Association} \\
\text{Yes} \to \text{Does the part outlive the whole?} \begin{cases}
\text{Yes} \to \text{Aggregation} \\
\text{No} \to \text{Composition}
\end{cases}
\end{cases}
$$

---

## 3.3 Mathematical Foundation — Multiplicity Combinations

For a binary association $A$ between $C_1$ (left) and $C_2$ (right) with multiplicities $m_1$ and $m_2$:

$$ |A| = \prod_{i=1}^{2} \text{instances}(C_i) \quad \text{subject to } \text{link}(C_1, C_2) \leq m_1 \times m_2 $$

For example, if `Student` has `*` multiplicity with `Course` and `Course` has `*` multiplicity with `Student`:

$$ A_{\text{enrolment}} \subseteq \text{Student} \times \text{Course}, \quad |A_{\text{enrolment}}| \leq |\text{Student}| \times |\text{Course}| $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Common KTU trap:** Students often confuse the *direction of multiplicity*. The number at **end A** of the line refers to *instances of A* per *one instance of B*. Always read: *"For each B, how many A?"*
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Object Modeling Pipeline (Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid **flow diagram** captures the entire object modeling workflow as prescribed in the KTU Module-2 syllabus. Notice the **feedback loops** — in practice, the Data Dictionary is updated *continuously* throughout the project, not as a one-shot step.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Problem Statement] --> B[Step 1A: Reuse Existing Classes]
    B --> C[Step 1B: Identify Candidate Classes<br/>Noun Phrase + Classical + Common List]
    C --> D[Step 1C: Apply Selection Criteria<br/>Retained info, Service, Multi-attr, Multi-instance]
    D --> E[Step 1D: Filter Redundant / Wrong Candidates]
    E --> F[Step 1E: Apply Naming Conventions]
    F --> G[Step 1F: Add Attributes]
    G --> H[Preliminary Class List]
    H --> I[Step 2: Prepare Data Dictionary<br/>Class, Attribute, Operation, Association, Constraint]
    I --> J[Step 3A: Identify Preliminary Associations<br/>Verb Phrase Analysis]
    J --> K[Step 3B: Filter Associations<br/>Remove Implementation / Derived / Procedural]
    K --> L[Step 3C: Assign Multiplicity and Roles]
    L --> M[Step 3D: Decide Aggregation vs Composition]
    M --> N[Final Class Diagram + Data Dictionary]
    I -.Continuous Update.-> N
    N --> O[Object Model Complete]
```

---

## 4.2 Class Diagram for the Library Management System (Worked Example)

The following Mermaid `classDiagram` block renders the complete object model derived in Section 3. Every class, attribute, multiplicity, aggregation, and association name is preserved **exactly** as documented in the Data Dictionary.

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Library {
        +String libraryId
        +String name
        +String address
    }
    class Branch {
        +String branchId
        +String branchName
        +String location
    }
    class Book {
        +String ISBN
        +String title
        +String category
        +double price
    }
    class Member {
        +int memberId
        +String name
        +String address
        +String phone
    }
    class Author {
        +int authorId
        +String authorName
    }
    class Issue {
        +int issueId
        +Date issueDate
        +Date returnDate
    }
    Library "1" o-- "*" Branch : contains
    Branch "*" -- "*" Book : stocks
    Book "*" -- "1..*" Author : writtenBy
    Library "1" -- "*" Member : registers
    Member "1" -- "*" Issue : raises
    Book "1" -- "*" Issue : listedIn
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- `Library "1" o-- "*" Branch` — one library aggregates many branches (an open diamond = **aggregation**; branches can exist without a library temporarily, e.g., during setup).
- `Branch "*" -- "*" Book` — many-to-many stocking (a plain association).
- `Book "*" -- "1..*" Author` — a book has 1 or more authors; an author has many books.
- `Member "1" -- "*" Issue` and `Book "1" -- "*" Issue` — the ternary "issue" relationship is decomposed into two binary associations with `Issue` as the **link class**.

---

## 4.3 Decision Flowchart: Should this Noun Become a Class?

This is the single most **valuable mental model** for KTU Part-B questions. Use it to defend your class list in front of the examiner.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    N[Noun from Problem Statement] --> Q1{Is it a pronoun or generic?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> R1[Discard: he / she / the system]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Does it have retained information?}
    Q2 -- No --> R2[Discard or make it a constant]
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{Is it actually a property of another noun?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> R3[Make it an attribute of that class]
    Q3 -- No --> Q4{Does it have multiple attributes itself?}
    Q4 -- No --> R4[Question - may be redundant]
    Q4 -- Yes --> Q5{Is it a synonym of an existing candidate?}
    Q5 -- Yes --> R5[Keep the more specific one]
    Q5 -- No --> Q6{Is it within the system scope?}
    Q6 -- No --> R6[Discard: outside scope]
    Q6 -- Yes --> KEEP[Keep as Object Class]
    KEEP --> ATTR[Add at least 2 attributes and naming convention]
```

---

## 4.4 Decision Flowchart: Choosing Association Strength

Use this diagram to decide between plain association, aggregation, and composition in your class diagram.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Two Candidate Classes X and Y] --> Q1{Is there a meaningful relationship?}
    Q1 -- No --> NA[No association]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Is it a kind-of relationship?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> GEN[Generalization: triangle arrow]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Is X a logical or physical part of Y?}
    Q3 -- No --> A1[Plain Association: line]
    Q3 -- Yes --> Q4{Can the part exist without the whole?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> AGG[Aggregation: open diamond]
    Q4 -- No --> COM[Composition: filled diamond]
```

---

## 4.5 Data Dictionary Structure (Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DD[Data Dictionary] --> C1[Class Entries]
    DD --> C2[Attribute Entries]
    DD --> C3[Operation Entries]
    DD --> C4[Association Entries]
    DD --> C5[Constraint Entries]
    C1 --> M1[Meaning / Purpose]
    C1 --> M2[Scope Notes]
    C1 --> M3[Assumptions]
    C2 --> M4[Name / Type / Range / Default]
    C2 --> M5[NULL Policy]
    C3 --> M6[Signature / Pre / Post]
    C4 --> M7[Roles / Multiplicity / Path]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define an Object Class. Differentiate between an object class and an object instance with a suitable example.
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model QP]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **Object Class** is a blueprint or template that describes a set of objects sharing common attributes, operations, semantics, and relationships. An **Object Instance** is a specific, concrete object that exists in memory and belongs to a particular class.

- *Class:* `Student` is a template (id, name, cgpa, issue()).
- *Instance:* `student101` (id=101, name="Asha", cgpa=8.4) is one specific object of `Student`.

A class is a *type*; an instance is a *value* of that type. The class exists once in the design; multiple instances of it may exist at runtime.

---

### Q2. List any **three classical object categories** given by Rumbaugh for identifying candidate object classes. Give one example for each.
`[KTU University Exam – July 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

According to Rumbaugh, the classical object categories for identifying candidate classes are:

1. **Tangible things (objects)** — physical, real-world items. *Example:* `Book`, `Car`.
2. **Events** — things that happen, often with a time/place. *Example:* `Sale`, `Flight`, `Meeting`.
3. **Roles** — played by people within the system. *Example:* `Customer`, `Payer`, `Borrower`.
4. **Locations** — physical or conceptual places. *Example:* `Warehouse`, `Branch`.
5. **Organizational units** — collections of people/resources. *Example:* `Department`, `Team`.

(Any three with correct examples score 3 marks. KTU specifically expects examples.)

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks, split into two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Internal choice is mandatory.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — SET 1
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**A.** *(7 marks)* Consider the following problem statement for a **Hospital Management System**:

> *"A hospital has multiple departments. Each department has a unique department ID and a name. Doctors are assigned to departments, and each doctor has a doctor ID, name, specialization, and phone. Patients register at the hospital, get a patient ID, and are admitted to specific rooms. A room has a room number, type, and charges. A patient is treated by one or more doctors, and each treatment is recorded with a date."*

**Identify all the object classes** for the above system. Justify why you **included** or **excluded** at least 4 candidate nouns. List the **attributes** for each class.

**B.** *(7 marks)* For the object classes identified in part (A), **prepare a complete Data Dictionary entry** for the `Doctor` and `Patient` classes. Also state the **selection criteria** used to filter candidate classes.

#### MODEL ANSWER — A(1) Object Class Identification

**Step 1 — Noun Phrase Extraction:**
`hospital`, `department`, `department ID`, `name`, `doctor`, `doctor ID`, `specialization`, `phone`, `patient`, `patient ID`, `room`, `room number`, `type`, `charges`, `treatment`, `date`.

**Step 2 — Apply Classical Category Mapping:**

| Noun | Category | Decision |
|---|---|---|
| hospital | organizational unit | **Keep as class** `Hospital` |
| department | organizational unit | **Keep as class** `Department` |
| doctor | role | **Keep as class** `Doctor` |
| patient | role | **Keep as class** `Patient` |
| room | tangible thing / location | **Keep as class** `Room` |
| treatment | event | **Keep as class** `Treatment` |
| department ID | attribute | Attribute of `Department` |
| doctor ID | attribute | Attribute of `Doctor` |
| name | attribute | Attribute of `Doctor` & `Department` |
| specialization | attribute | Attribute of `Doctor` |
| phone | attribute | Attribute of `Doctor` |
| patient ID | attribute | Attribute of `Patient` |
| room number | attribute | Attribute of `Room` |
| type | attribute | Attribute of `Room` |
| charges | attribute | Attribute of `Room` |
| date | attribute | Attribute of `Treatment` |

**Step 3 — Final Classes:**
$$
\text{Classes} = \{\,\text{Hospital}, \text{Department}, \text{Doctor}, \text{Patient}, \text{Room}, \text{Treatment}\,\}
$$

**Step 4 — Justification for Inclusion/Exclusion (4 candidates minimum required):**

| Candidate | Decision | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| `doctor` | **Included as class** | Has multiple attributes (id, name, specialization, phone), multiple instances, retained info across admissions |
| `specialization` | **Excluded; made attribute of Doctor** | A simple property, no independent lifecycle — is a *property*, not a *thing* |
| `room` | **Included as class** | Has attributes (roomNo, type, charges) and is referenced by `Patient` admission — passes filter |
| `treatment` | **Included as class** | Is an *event* with a date and links Doctor↔Patient; has retained info |
| `charges` | **Excluded; attribute of Room** | A numeric property of a room, not a standalone entity |

**Step 5 — Attributes per Class:**

| Class | Attributes |
|---|---|
| `Hospital` | `hospitalId`, `name`, `address` |
| `Department` | `deptId`, `deptName` |
| `Doctor` | `doctorId`, `name`, `specialization`, `phone` |
| `Patient` | `patientId`, `name`, `age`, `gender`, `phone` |
| `Room` | `roomNo`, `type`, `charges` |
| `Treatment` | `treatmentId`, `date`, `diagnosis` |

**(7 marks breakdown: 1 for noun list, 2 for category mapping + filtering, 2 for inclusion/exclusion justification, 2 for attribute listing.)**

---

#### MODEL ANSWER — A(2) Data Dictionary for Doctor and Patient

**Selection Criteria Used (Rumbaugh's filter):**
1. The class contains **retained information** (worth remembering across time).
2. The class **provides a needed service** (has methods other classes invoke).
3. The class has **multiple attributes** (not just one).
4. The class has **multiple instances** in the system.
5. The class has a **clear scope** (developer and client agree on its meaning).

**Data Dictionary — `Doctor`:**

```
CLASS Doctor
   MEANING : A licensed medical professional working in a hospital department.
   ATTRIBUTES :
      doctorId      : Integer   [unique, 5-digit, auto-generated]
      name          : String    [max 100 chars, mandatory]
      specialization: Enum      [one of {Cardiology, Neurology, Ortho, General}]
      phone         : String    [10-digit mobile format, unique]
   OPERATIONS :
      prescribe(patientId, medicine) : void
      viewSchedule(date) : List<Appointment>
      addSpecialization(newSpec) : void
   ASSOCIATIONS :
      belongsTo   of Department    [exactly 1]
      performs    of Treatment     [0..*]
   CONSTRAINTS :
      doctorId is system-generated and immutable.
      A doctor must belong to at least one department.
```

**Data Dictionary — `Patient`:**

```
CLASS Patient
   MEANING : A person registered with the hospital for treatment.
   ATTRIBUTES :
      patientId  : Integer   [unique, 6-digit]
      name       : String    [max 100 chars]
      age        : Integer   [range 0..150]
      gender     : Enum      [one of {Male, Female, Other}]
      phone      : String    [10-digit, unique]
   OPERATIONS :
      register()   : void
      bookRoom(roomNo) : void
      viewTreatments() : List<Treatment>
   ASSOCIATIONS :
      admittedTo  of Room         [0..1]
      receives    of Treatment    [0..*]
   CONSTRAINTS :
      A patient can be admitted to at most 1 room at a time.
      phone must be unique across the hospital.
```

**(7 marks breakdown: 2 for selection criteria, 2 for Doctor entry, 2 for Patient entry, 1 for naming/multiline structure.)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — SET 2 (Alternative Choice)
`[KTU University Exam – July 2023, Adapted]`

**A.** *(7 marks)* Consider a **University Course Registration System** with the following requirements:

> *"The university offers courses. Each course has a course code, name, credits, and is taught by exactly one faculty member. Faculty members have faculty ID, name, department, and email. Students enroll in courses, and an enrolment has an enrolment date and a grade. A student can enrol in multiple courses."*

**Identify all object classes and all associations** between them. For each association, give a **suitable name, roles, and multiplicity**. Use a **class diagram** to illustrate.

**B.** *(7 marks)* Write the **complete Data Dictionary** for the `Course` and `Enrolment` classes. Explain the **difference between association, aggregation, and composition** with examples from this system.

#### MODEL ANSWER — B(1) Classes & Associations

**Step 1 — Classes Identified:**

$$
\text{Classes} = \{\,\text{University}, \text{Course}, \text{Faculty}, \text{Student}, \text{Enrolment}\,\}
$$

**Step 2 — Associations with Multiplicity:**

| # | Association | End 1 (role, mult.) | End 2 (role, mult.) | Type |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | University *offers* Course | `University` (1) | `Course` (offeredBy, *) | Plain |
| 2 | Faculty *teaches* Course | `Faculty` (taughtBy, 1) | `Course` (taughtTo, *) | Plain |
| 3 | Student *enrols in* Course (via Enrolment) | decomposed → two binary | | Link class |
| 4 | Student *creates* Enrolment | `Student` (raisedBy, 1) | `Enrolment` (for, *) | Plain |
| 5 | Enrolment *references* Course | `Enrolment` (refersTo, 1) | `Course` (referencedBy, *) | Plain |

**Step 3 — Class Diagram:**

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class University {
        +String uniId
        +String name
    }
    class Course {
        +String courseCode
        +String name
        +int credits
    }
    class Faculty {
        +int facultyId
        +String name
        +String department
        +String email
    }
    class Student {
        +int rollNo
        +String name
        +String program
    }
    class Enrolment {
        +int enrolmentId
        +Date enrolmentDate
        +char grade
    }
    University "1" -- "*" Course : offers
    Faculty "1" -- "*" Course : teaches
    Student "1" -- "*" Enrolment : raises
    Course "1" -- "*" Enrolment : isFor
```

**(7 marks breakdown: 2 for class list with justification, 3 for association table with multiplicity, 2 for class diagram.)**

---

#### MODEL ANSWER — B(2) Data Dictionary + Association Types

**Data Dictionary — `Course`:**

```
CLASS Course
   MEANING : A unit of study offered by the university, taught by one faculty.
   ATTRIBUTES :
      courseCode : String  [6-character unique code, e.g., "CS304"]
      name       : String  [max 100 chars]
      credits    : Integer [range 1..6, default 4]
   OPERATIONS :
      getSyllabus() : String
      assignGrade(studentId, grade) : void
   ASSOCIATIONS :
      offeredBy  of University    [exactly 1]
      taughtBy   of Faculty       [exactly 1]
      enrolledIn of Enrolment     [0..*]
   CONSTRAINTS :
      credits must be in {1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6}.
      A course must be taught by exactly 1 faculty in a given semester.
```

**Data Dictionary — `Enrolment`:**

```
CLASS Enrolment
   MEANING : A record of a student enrolling in a course, with a date and grade.
   ATTRIBUTES :
      enrolmentId   : Integer [unique, 8-digit]
      enrolmentDate : Date    [default = system date]
      grade         : Char    [one of {S, A, B, C, D, E, F, NULL}]
   OPERATIONS :
      assignGrade(g : Char) : void
      withdraw() : void
   ASSOCIATIONS :
      forStudent  of Student    [exactly 1]
      forCourse   of Course     [exactly 1]
   CONSTRAINTS :
      A student cannot have two enrolments for the same course in one semester.
      grade is NULL until the course is completed.
```

**Difference between Association, Aggregation, and Composition:**

| Property | Association | Aggregation (◇) | Composition (◆) |
|---|---|---|---|
| UML Symbol | Plain line `—` | Open diamond `◇—` | Filled diamond `◆—` |
| Strength | "knows-about" | "weak part-of" | "strong part-of" |
| Lifecycle | Independent classes | Part can outlive whole | Part dies with whole |
| Sharing | Allowed | Allowed (e.g., Faculty in 2 Depts) | Not allowed (exclusive part) |
| **Example in this system** | `Faculty — teaches — Course` | `University ◇— Course` (course can be transferred between universities) | `Enrolment ◆— GradeRecord` (a grade record cannot exist without its enrolment) |

> In our system: **`University ◇— Course`** is aggregation because a course might survive a university restructuring; **`Enrolment ◆— GradeRecord`** would be composition because once an enrolment is deleted, the grade entry has no meaning.

**(7 marks breakdown: 2 for Course DD, 2 for Enrolment DD, 3 for association-vs-aggregation-vs-composition table with example.)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically LOSE MARKS in this topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "is-a" with "is-part-of"** — `Student — Course` is NOT a part-of; it is an enrolment. Students often wrongly aggregate `Course` into `Student` (or vice-versa). Aggregation = `Course ◆— Module` (a Module is a part of Course).
> 2. **Reversing multiplicity direction** — The number at end A refers to instances of A. Students write `Student 1 — * Course` thinking "a student takes many courses" but forget to confirm that the inverse is also true in the problem (i.e., one course can have many students).
> 3. **Skipping the Data Dictionary** — KTU examiners will deduct up to 2 marks if you draw the class diagram but provide NO data dictionary entry. The DD is **mandatory** in 14-mark questions.
> 4. **Promoting every noun to a class** — `name`, `age`, `date` are **attributes**, not classes. Examiners will mark this as a fundamental error.
> 5. **Missing the Noun Phrase step** — Even if your final class list is correct, you **must show the noun extraction step** for full marks. Examiners award 2 marks purely for the explicit noun listing.
> 6. **Forgetting role names** when a class appears twice in an association (e.g., the `Person`—`Person` "is-married-to" relationship needs both `spouse` and `spouse` roles).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute revision before KTU exam — print this section!**

### A. Identifying Object Classes
- ✅ Always start with the **Noun Phrase Approach** (underline every noun in the problem statement).
- ✅ Supplement with **Classical Categories** (Tangible, Event, Role, Location, Organizational Unit) and **Common Object List** (Specification, Description, Item, etc.).
- ✅ Apply Rumbaugh's **5-point filter**: retained info, provides service, multiple attributes, multiple instances, clear scope.
- ✅ Use **singular, capitalised, domain-specific** names.
- ✅ Promote properties to attributes unless they have their own independent lifecycle.
- ✅ **Always show the noun extraction** in your answer for full marks.

### B. Preparing a Data Dictionary
- ✅ One entry per **class, attribute, operation, association, and constraint**.
- ✅ Standard format: `MEANING`, `ATTRIBUTES`, `OPERATIONS`, `ASSOCIATIONS`, `CONSTRAINTS`.
- ✅ Mention **types, ranges, defaults, NULL policy** for every attribute.
- ✅ Update the Data Dictionary **continuously** — it is a living document.

### C. Identifying Associations
- ✅ Use the **Verb Phrase Approach** on the problem statement.
- ✅ Filter out implementation/derived/procedural relationships.
- ✅ Always specify **multiplicity** at both ends (`1`, `*`, `0..1`, `1..*`).
- ✅ Add **role names** when a class appears twice or when the role is non-obvious.
- ✅ Choose between **association, aggregation, composition** based on lifecycle dependence.
- ✅ Decompose **ternary associations** into binary + link class.
- ✅ Draw a **class diagram** with multiplicities and role names; an answer without a diagram loses 2-3 marks.

### D. Common KTU Question Patterns
- "Identify the **object classes** from the given problem statement" → Noun phrase + filtering (7 marks).
- "Prepare a **Data Dictionary** for the system" → Show DD entries for 2-3 classes (7 marks).
- "Identify **associations** with multiplicity" → Verb phrases + class diagram (7-14 marks).
- "Differentiate between **aggregation and composition**" → Definition + example + symbol (3-7 marks).
- "Explain the **naming conventions** for object classes" → Capitalised, singular, domain-specific (3 marks).

### E. One-Line Memory Aids
- **"Nouns become classes, verbs become associations."**
- **"Attributes describe; classes contain."**
- **"Multiplicity reads right-to-left at the right end, left-to-right at the left end."**
- **"Aggregation = weak part-of; Composition = strong part-of."**
- **"Data Dictionary is the contract; class diagram is the picture."**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analysis: Dynamic Modeling - Preparing a Scenario, Interface Format, Identifying Events, Building a State Diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCNT604 - OBJECT ORIENTED MODELING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Analysis: Dynamic Modeling - Preparing a Scenario, Interface Format, Identifying Events, Building a State Diagram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Dynamic Modeling — The Formal Definition

In the **Object Modeling Technique (OMT)** framework adopted in OO Analysis & Design, **Dynamic Modeling** is the process of representing the temporal and behavioral aspects of a system through state diagrams, scenarios, and event traces. Unlike the **Object Model** (which captures *what* exists) and the **Functional Model** (which captures *what happens*), the dynamic model explicitly answers the question: **"How does the system behave over time, and how do its objects change state?"**

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — PBCNT604, Module 2):**
> Dynamic modeling focuses on the **control and time-dependent behavior** of objects. Its three primary artifacts are: (1) **Scenarios** (interaction narratives), (2) **Event Traces** (timelines), and (3) **State Diagrams** (state-transition graphs).

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Film vs. Photograph" View

Imagine a software system as a **city**:
- The **Object Model** is the **architectural map** (buildings, roads, parks) — *static structure*.
- The **Functional Model** is the **rule book** (zoning laws, water flow equations) — *transformations and constraints*.
- The **Dynamic Model** is the **traffic CCTV footage** — it shows how cars, pedestrians, and traffic lights *change their states* from second to second: red→green, parked→moving, closed→open.

Dynamic modeling is essentially the **"movie"** that complements the **"photographs"** produced by the other two models.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Pillars of OO Modeling (Rumbaugh et al.):**
> 1. **Object Model** — *static*, structural (UML Class Diagram).
> 2. **Dynamic Model** — *temporal*, behavioral (UML State/Interaction Diagrams).
> 3. **Functional Model** — *transformational*, data-flow (UML Use-Case / DFD).
> All three must be **consistent** — dynamic events must correspond to operations in the object model, and data flows in the functional model must touch real attributes.

## Core Vocabulary for This Topic

| Term | One-Line Definition |
|---|---|
| **Scenario** | A trace of the interaction between objects over time, usually drawn as an object/sequence diagram for *one* execution path. |
| **Event** | An instantaneous occurrence (e.g., button press, timer expiry) that triggers a change of state. |
| **State** | A condition of an object during which it satisfies some condition, performs an activity, or waits for an event. |
| **Interface Format** | A *signature* (template) that defines the name, parameters, and pre/post-conditions of an event/message exchanged between an object and its environment. |
| **State Diagram** | A directed graph whose nodes are states and whose edges are transitions labelled `event [guard] / action`. |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 The 4-Stage Pipeline of Dynamic Modeling

Dynamic modeling in KTU's OOAD syllabus is taught as a four-step pipeline. The student is expected to remember the *order* and the *artefact* produced at each stage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pipeline (in syllabus order):**
> 1. **Prepare a Scenario** → Narrative + sequence-like interaction sketch.
> 2. **Define an Interface Format** → Formalize the messages exchanged.
> 3. **Identify Events** → Extract atomic, instantaneous occurrences.
> 4. **Build a State Diagram** → State-transition graph for *each* class of interest.

### Stage 1 — Preparing a Scenario

A scenario is a *time-ordered* story of object interaction. To prepare one, follow these structured steps:

1. **Pick one normal-flow use case** (e.g., "Customer withdraws cash from ATM").
2. **Identify the participating objects** from the object model (e.g., `:Customer`, `:ATM`, `:BankServer`, `:CashDispenser`).
3. **Walk through the use case, step by step**, marking each moment when an object sends a message to another.
4. **Mark time on the vertical axis** (top = earlier, bottom = later) and **objects on the horizontal axis** — this is essentially a **sequence diagram** in modern UML.
5. **Note the start state and end state** of each object.

### Stage 2 — Interface Format

The **Interface Format** is a *contract* — a template describing the external view of an operation. It typically contains:

- **Name** of the event/operation.
- **Parameter List** with types.
- **Return Type** (if any).
- **Guard / Precondition** (optional boolean condition).
- **Postcondition** (state-change description).
- **Exceptions** raised.

A common notation used in OMT is:

```
eventName ( param1 : Type1, param2 : Type2 ) : ReturnType
precondition:  P
postcondition: Q
```

### Stage 3 — Identifying Events

Events are the *atoms* of dynamic behavior. To identify them rigorously:

- Look at **every message in the scenario** — each one is (or maps to) an event.
- Separate **external events** (from outside the system: user click, sensor trigger) from **internal events** (within the system: completion of a calculation, expiry of a timer).
- Identify **conditional events** (an event that fires only when a guard is true).
- Discard pseudo-events (repeated polling, internal time steps that do not change state).

> [!NOTE]
> **Classification of Events (high-yield for KTU):**
>
> | Category | Example | Source |
> |---|---|---|
> | External, asynchronous | User presses "Cancel" | User |
> | External, synchronous | Query from another system | Network |
> | Internal, temporal | Timeout of 30 s | System clock |
> | Internal, conditional | `balance < amount` | System logic |

### Stage 4 — Building a State Diagram

A state diagram is built **per class of interest** (one diagram per class — *not* one per system). The construction algorithm is:

1. **Start state** → solid filled circle.
2. For each event identified in Stage 3, decide which states it can occur *in* and what state it transitions *to*.
3. Draw a directed edge from the source state to the target state, labelled `event [guard] / action`.
4. Apply **no-event (after/do) actions** for activities that happen while the object is *inside* a state.
5. Add **termination / final state** → bullseye circle.
6. Validate by replaying the scenario and walking the path on the diagram.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Notation Sheet

Since dynamic modeling is descriptive rather than numerical, the "formula" sheet here is a **notation reference** for diagrams. Master this table — KTU paper-setters love to test it.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **State-Diagram Notation (Rumbaugh OMT / UML-equivalent):**
>
> | Symbol | Meaning |
> |---|---|
> | Solid black dot | Start (initial) state |
> | Bullseye (concentric circles) | End (final) state |
> | Rounded rectangle | State (with optional `do:` or `after:` actions) |
> | Labelled arrow `e` | Transition triggered by event `e` |
> | `e [g] / a` | Transition on event `e` if guard `g` holds, performing action `a` |
> | Dashed arrow | Internal transition (no state change) |

**State Equation (conceptual):**

$$
\text{NextState} \;=\; f\!\left(\text{CurrentState},\; \text{Event},\; \text{Guard}\right)
$$

> The function $f$ maps a triple to either the same state (no change) or a new state. The guard is a boolean predicate; if it evaluates to **false**, the transition is **not** taken.

### Real-World Engineering Utility

Dynamic modeling is used heavily in:

- **Embedded control systems** (washing-machine controller, elevator logic, anti-lock braking).
- **Real-time telecom protocol stacks** (call state machines in VoIP).
- **UI workflow engines** (e-commerce checkout state machines).
- **Compiler theory** (lexer's DFA = a state diagram).
- **VLSI verification** (FSM extraction from RTL, model checking).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Construction Walkthrough

This section shows the **complete, exhaustive** construction of a state diagram for a classic KTU textbook example: the **microwave oven controller**. Every decision is justified; no step is skipped.

## 3.1 Stage 1 — Preparing a Scenario

**Use case picked:** "User heats food for 30 seconds."

**Objects identified from the object model:**

- `:User` (actor)
- `:DoorSensor`
- `:MicrowaveController` (the class of interest)
- `:Magnetron` (the heater hardware)
- `:Timer`

**Step-by-step scenario narrative (time flows top → bottom):**

1. `t0` — User opens the door → `DoorSensor.doorOpen() = true`.
2. `t1` — User places food inside, closes the door → `DoorSensor.doorOpen() = false`.
3. `t2` — User presses the `Start` button.
4. `t3` — `MicrowaveController` sends `energize()` to `Magnetron`.
5. `t4` — `MicrowaveController` sends `start(30s)` to `Timer`.
6. `t5` — `Timer` counts down to 0.
7. `t6` — `Timer` fires `timeout` event to `MicrowaveController`.
8. `t7` — `MicrowaveController` sends `de-energize()` to `Magnetron`.
9. `t8` — System is back to *idle*, ready for the next cycle.

> [!NOTE]
> The output of Stage 1 is a **scenario diagram** (sequence-like). Notice that this single normal scenario does **not** yet cover error paths like *"user opens door during cooking"* — those would be **alternative scenarios** that must be analyzed later.

## 3.2 Stage 2 — Defining the Interface Format

From the scenario, we extract the messages and write their **interface formats**. The class of interest is `MicrowaveController`; the messages it exchanges with the outside world are formalized below.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{startCooking} (\, \text{duration : Integer}\,) & : \text{Void} \\
\text{precondition} &: \text{doorIsClosed} \;\land\; \text{idle} \\
\text{postcondition} &: \text{cooking} \;=\; \text{true} \;\land\; \text{remaining} \;=\; \text{duration}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{doorOpened} (\,)\, & : \text{Void} \\
\text{precondition} &: \text{none} \\
\text{postcondition} &: \text{cooking} \;=\; \text{false} \;\land\; \text{magnetronOff}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{timeout} (\,)\, & : \text{Void} \\
\text{precondition} &: \text{cooking} \;=\; \text{true} \\
\text{postcondition} &: \text{cooking} \;=\; \text{false}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why three formats and not one?** Each event originates from a *different* source (`User`, `DoorSensor`, `Timer`) and so its contract — the legal *before* and *after* — differs. KTU examiners award marks for the **completeness** of the interface format (pre, post, exceptions).

## 3.3 Stage 3 — Identifying Events

From the interface formats above, the *atomic* events are extracted:

- `startCooking` (external, asynchronous, from User)
- `doorOpened` (external, asynchronous, from DoorSensor)
- `doorClosed` (external, asynchronous, from DoorSensor)
- `timeout` (internal, temporal, from Timer)

> **Decision rule used:** *"If removing this event from the scenario makes the system miss a state change, it is a genuine event."* Polling and intermediate computations are **not** events.

## 3.4 Stage 4 — Building the State Diagram

We now enumerate the **states** of `MicrowaveController` by asking, *"Between which events does the object dwell in a recognizably distinct condition?"* The answer yields **three** states.

**State table (constructed from event analysis):**

| State | Object Condition | Activity |
|---|---|---|
| `Idle` | door closed, magnetron off, no timer running | Waiting for user input |
| `Cooking` | door closed, magnetron on, timer running | Heating food |
| `Paused` | door open, magnetron off, timer paused | Suspended |

**Transition table:**

| From | Event | Guard | To | Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `Idle` | `startCooking` | door closed | `Cooking` | `magnetron.on()`; `timer.start(d)` |
| `Cooking` | `timeout` | — | `Idle` | `magnetron.off()` |
| `Cooking` | `doorOpened` | — | `Paused` | `magnetron.off()`; `timer.pause()` |
| `Paused` | `doorClosed` | — | `Cooking` | `magnetron.on()`; `timer.resume()` |
| `Paused` | `cancel` | — | `Idle` | `timer.reset()` |

**Conceptual state equation applied to the first row:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NextState} &= f\!\left(\text{Idle},\; \text{startCooking},\; \text{doorIsClosed}\right) \\
&= \text{Cooking} \\
\text{with side effect} &: \text{magnetron.energize()} \;\land\; \text{timer.start}(d)
\end{aligned}
$$

This equation captures both the **state change** and the **actions** triggered by the event.

> [!NOTE]
> **Self-check for the student:**
> Walk through the scenario of §3.1 on the state diagram. The trace `Idle → Cooking → Idle` (on `startCooking`, then `timeout`) should match steps `t2` → `t6` → `t8`. If the diagram cannot replay the scenario, the diagram is **incomplete** — go back to §3.3 and add the missing event.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The 4-Stage Dynamic Modeling Pipeline (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start: Use Case]) --> stepA1[Identify participating objects]
    stepA1 --> stepA2[Walk through normal flow]
    stepA2 --> stepA3[Mark time axis and message order]
    stepA3 --> scenOut[/"Scenario Diagram"/]

    scenOut --> stepB1[Extract every message in scenario]
    stepB1 --> stepB2[Write name, params, return type]
    stepB2 --> stepB3[Add pre and post conditions]
    stepB3 --> ifOut[/"Interface Format Catalog"/]

    ifOut --> stepC1[Mark each message as event]
    stepC1 --> stepC2[Classify: external vs internal]
    stepC2 --> stepC3[Filter out non-events polling]
    stepC3 --> evOut[/"Event List"/]

    evOut --> stepD1[Enumerate states of the class]
    stepD1 --> stepD2[For each state, list relevant events]
    stepD2 --> stepD3[Draw transitions with event guard and action]
    stepD3 --> stepD4[Add start and final states]
    stepD4 --> sdOut[/"State Diagram"/]
    sdOut --> stopA([End: State Diagram Ready])
```

## 4.2 Microwave Controller State Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Cooking : startCooking [doorClosed] / magnetron.on, timer.start
    Cooking --> Cooking : tick / timer.decrement
    Cooking --> Paused : doorOpened / magnetron.off, timer.pause
    Cooking --> Idle : timeout / magnetron.off, timer.reset
    Paused --> Cooking : doorClosed / magnetron.on, timer.resume
    Paused --> Idle : cancel / timer.reset
    Idle --> Idle : doorOpened / ignore
    Cooking --> [*] : powerFail / safeShutdown
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram (KTU-expected answer style):**
> - The filled dot `[ * ]` is the **start state**.
> - `tick / timer.decrement` is an **internal transition** (dashed in OMT, plain arrow in Mermaid) — the object remains in `Cooking` but a `do`-action is performed.
> - `powerFail / safeShutdown` leads to the **final state** — system halts safely.

## 4.3 Object ↔ Dynamic Model Linkage (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph OM[Object Model]
        classA[Class: MicrowaveController]
        attrA1[Attribute: doorClosed Boolean]
        attrA2[Attribute: remainingTime Integer]
        opA1[Operation: startCooking]
        opA2[Operation: doorOpened]
    end

    subgraph DM[Dynamic Model]
        stateA1[State: Idle]
        stateA2[State: Cooking]
        stateA3[State: Paused]
        trA1[Transition: startCooking]
        trA2[Transition: doorOpened]
    end

    subgraph FM[Functional Model]
        fnA1[Function: computeRemainingTime]
        fnA2[Function: magnetronControl]
    end

    opA1 -. maps to .-> trA1
    opA2 -. maps to .-> trA2
    attrA1 -. read by guard .-> trA1
    attrA2 -. written by .-> fnA1
    fnA2 -. invoked during .-> stateA2
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The three boxes above must remain **mutually consistent**. If the Object Model lists an operation `startCooking`, the Dynamic Model must have a corresponding event of the same name, and the Functional Model must have a function that computes the side-effect. KTU paper questions often ask you to *verify* this consistency.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **One state diagram per class** — students often draw a single "system" state diagram. This loses **2–3 marks** instantly. The KTU answer key awards marks *per class of interest*.
> 2. **Forgetting the start state (solid dot)** and the **final state (bullseye)** loses **1 mark** each.
> 3. **Guards without square brackets** (`event guard / action` instead of `event [guard] / action`) is a common slip. Always wrap the guard in `[ ]`.
> 4. **Conflating scenarios with state diagrams** — a scenario is a *single execution trace*; a state diagram covers *all* possible traces. Don't draw a state diagram when the question asks for a scenario.
> 5. **Missing pre/post-conditions** in the interface format — this is a 2-mark deduction per format.

---

## Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Q1. Define the term *event* as used in dynamic modeling. Differentiate between *external* and *internal* events with one example each. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **event** is an instantaneous occurrence at a point in time that can trigger a change of state in an object or a message sent from one object to another. Events have **no duration** — they are atomic in time.

- **External event** — originates *outside* the system boundary. **Example:** User pressing a button on an ATM (source = human actor).
- **Internal event** — originates *inside* the system, often from a timer, a computation, or another object. **Example:** A 30-second timer firing a `timeout` event in a microwave controller.

*(Award 1 mark for the definition, 1 mark for external + example, 1 mark for internal + example.)*

### Q2. What is an *interface format*? List any **four** components that a well-formed interface format should specify. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **interface format** is a formal template that describes the *signature* of an event or message — what data it carries, what preconditions must hold before it fires, and what postconditions describe its effect.

Four components:
1. **Name** of the event/operation.
2. **Parameter list** with types.
3. **Precondition** (boolean guard that must be true for the event to be valid).
4. **Postcondition** (state change or effect produced after the event is processed).

*(1 mark for the definition, 2 marks for four valid components — ½ mark each.)*

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module-2 Internal Choice)

### Question A — Dynamic Modeling of an Elevator Controller

**(a)** Prepare a normal scenario for the use case *"A passenger requests the elevator at the 5th floor and rides down to the ground floor"*. List the participating objects and show the time-ordered messages. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Identify the events from the scenario, write the **interface format** for the `requestFloor()` event, and draw the **state diagram** for the `ElevatorController` class. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Participating objects (1 mark):** `:Passenger` (actor), `:FloorButton` (5th-floor button), `:ElevatorController`, `:Motor`, `:Door`.

**Time-ordered scenario (sequence-style, 6 marks):**

| Time | Object | Action / Message |
|---|---|---|
| `t0` | `:Passenger` | presses button on 5th floor |
| `t1` | `:FloorButton` | `requestFloor(5) → ElevatorController` |
| `t2` | `:ElevatorController` | decides direction; sends `moveUp()` to `:Motor` |
| `t3` | `:Motor` | moves car to floor 5; fires `arrivedAt(5)` |
| `t4` | `:ElevatorController` | sends `open()` to `:Door` |
| `t5` | `:Door` | `doorOpened` event fires |
| `t6` | `:Passenger` | enters; presses `0` (ground) on cabin panel |
| `t7` | `:ElevatorController` | sends `moveDown()` to `:Motor` |
| `t8` | `:Motor` | moves to ground; fires `arrivedAt(0)` |
| `t9` | `:ElevatorController` | sends `open()` to `:Door` |
| `t10` | `:Passenger` | exits |

**Start/end state of `ElevatorController`:** starts in `IdleAtGround`, ends in `IdleAtGround` after the trip.

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Events identified (2 marks):**

- `requestFloor(n)` — external, asynchronous (from button).
- `arrivedAt(n)` — internal, synchronous (from motor sensor).
- `doorOpened`, `doorClosed` — external, asynchronous (from door sensor).
- `cabinRequest(n)` — external, asynchronous (from cabin panel).

**Interface format for `requestFloor()` (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{requestFloor} (\, \text{floor : Integer}\,) & : \text{Void} \\
\text{precondition} &: 0 \;\leq\; \text{floor} \;\leq\; \text{maxFloor} \\
\text{postcondition} &: \text{requestedFloor} \;=\; \text{floor}
\end{aligned}
$$

**State diagram for `ElevatorController` (3 marks):**

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> MovingUp : requestFloor(n) [n > currentFloor] / motor.up
    Idle --> MovingDown : requestFloor(n) [n < currentFloor] / motor.down
    MovingUp --> DoorOpen : arrivedAt(n) / motor.stop, door.open
    MovingDown --> DoorOpen : arrivedAt(n) / motor.stop, door.open
    DoorOpen --> Idle : doorClosed after 5s / timer.reset
    Idle --> [*] : powerFail / safeStop
```

> **Mark allocation (b):** Events 2 + Interface format 2 + State diagram 3 = **7 marks**.

---

### Question B — Dynamic Modeling of a Washing Machine

**(a)** Explain with a neat diagram the **four stages** of dynamic modeling in the order prescribed by the OMT methodology. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** For a washing machine controller, identify the **states**, the **events**, and draw the **state diagram**. Write the interface format for the `startWash()` event. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 4 Stages of Dynamic Modeling

1. **Prepare a Scenario (2 marks)** — Choose a normal use case, identify objects from the object model, walk through the interaction in time order, and produce a sequence-style scenario.
2. **Interface Format (1 mark)** — Formalize the messages in the scenario as contracts (name, parameters, preconditions, postconditions).
3. **Identify Events (2 marks)** — Extract every atomic, instantaneous occurrence; classify as external/internal, synchronous/asynchronous; reject pseudo-events.
4. **Build a State Diagram (2 marks)** — One state diagram **per class of interest**, showing states as nodes and transitions as labelled directed edges.

(Any flowchart-style or block-diagram presentation using the 4-stage pipeline shown in §4.1 earns full marks.)

#### Model Solution for (b) — Washing Machine

**Events identified (1 mark):** `startWash`, `doorClosed`, `doorOpened`, `fillComplete`, `drainComplete`, `spinComplete`, `cancel`.

**States (1 mark):** `Idle`, `Filling`, `Washing`, `Draining`, `Spinning`, `Paused`.

**Interface format for `startWash()` (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{startWash} (\, \text{program : WashProgram}\,) & : \text{Void} \\
\text{precondition} &: \text{doorClosed} \;\land\; \text{state} \;=\; \text{Idle} \\
\text{postcondition} &: \text{state} \;=\; \text{Filling} \;\land\; \text{valve.open}
\end{aligned}
$$

**State diagram (3 marks):**

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Filling : startWash(prog) [doorClosed] / valve.open
    Filling --> Washing : fillComplete / motor.on
    Washing --> Draining : washTimerDone / motor.off, pump.on
    Draining --> Spinning : drainComplete / pump.off, motor.on high
    Spinning --> Idle : spinComplete / motor.off, buzzer.beep
    Washing --> Paused : doorOpened / motor.off, pump.off
    Paused --> Idle : cancel / reset
    Draining --> Paused : doorOpened / pump.off
    Spinning --> Paused : doorOpened / motor.off
    Idle --> [*] : powerFail
```

> **Mark allocation (b):** Events 1 + States 1 + Interface 2 + State diagram 3 = **7 marks**.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Dynamic modeling** describes *time-varying* behavior; it complements (does *not* replace) the object and functional models.
- The **four stages** in order are: **Scenario → Interface Format → Events → State Diagram**.
- A **scenario** is one execution trace; a **state diagram** covers **all** possible traces of a class.
- An **event** is *atomic in time* and may be *external* (from outside) or *internal* (from within).
- An **interface format** must include: name, parameters, return type, precondition, postcondition.
- A **state** is a recognizable *between-events* condition of an object.
- A **transition** has the form `event [guard] / action` — guard is boolean; action is executed only if the transition fires.
- **One state diagram per class** of interest — not one per system.
- **Always include the start state (solid dot) and final state (bullseye)** if applicable.
- **Consistency check:** every event in the dynamic model must map to an operation in the object model and to a flow in the functional model.
- **Common KTU verbs:** *"prepare a scenario"*, *"define an interface format"*, *"identify events"*, *"build a state diagram"* — answer exactly what is asked; do not substitute a sequence diagram for a state diagram.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analysis: Functional Modeling - Identifying Input and Output Values, Building Data Flow Diagrams, Describing Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCNT604 - OBJECT ORIENTED MODELING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Analysis: Functional Modeling - Identifying Input and Output Values, Building Data Flow Diagrams, Describing Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Functional Modeling** is a core analysis activity within the Object-Oriented (OO) software engineering process (specifically the Booch/Rumbaugh/Jacobson unified tradition adopted in KTU's OOMD curriculum) that captures the **dynamic, transformational behaviour** of a system — *what information the system consumes, what it produces, how it transforms one into the other, and where it stores intermediate data* — independently of *when* (control flow) or *who* (object responsibility).

In formal KTU/OOMD terms (Rumbaugh et al., *Object-Oriented Modeling and Design*, adopted by KTU):

> **Functional Model** = a collection of **Data Flow Diagrams (DFDs)** that describe the **meaningful transformations of values** within a system, plus a set of **function descriptions (PSPECs)** that specify the processing logic for each transformation.

It complements the **Object Model** (static structure) and the **Dynamic Model** (state/event behaviour), forming the three pillars of OMT (Object Modeling Technique).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PBCNT604, Module 2):**
> Functional Modeling explicitly covers three deliverables:
> 1. Identifying **Input and Output Values**
> 2. Building **Data Flow Diagrams (DFDs)** at multiple levels
> 3. **Describing Functions** (process specifications / PSPECs)

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Bakery Factory"

Imagine a **bread factory**:

| Bakery Concept | Functional Modeling Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Raw materials entering (flour, water, yeast) | **Input Values** |
| The mixing & baking rooms | **Processes (Functions)** |
| Intermediate ingredient bins | **Data Stores** |
| Finished loaves leaving the factory | **Output Values** |
| Customers placing orders | **External Entities** |
| Recipe cards hanging on each room door | **Function Descriptions (PSPECs)** |

The factory manager doesn't care *who* operates the mixer (that's an object) or *when* the oven switches on (that's control flow). The manager only cares about: *what goes in → what transformations happen → what comes out*. **That is exactly what functional modeling captures.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Definition for First-Time Learners:**
> Functional Modeling is the process of drawing a "value-flow map" of a software system. You list what data enters, list what data leaves, draw arrows showing how the data flows through processes, and write a short recipe (PSPEC) explaining what each process does to the data.

---

## 1.3 The Three Deliverables — Quick Glossary

| Term | One-Line Definition |
|---|---|
| **Input Value** | A piece of data supplied *to* a process from an external entity or another process |
| **Output Value** | A piece of data produced *by* a process and consumed by an external entity, store, or another process |
| **Data Flow Diagram (DFD)** | A directed graph showing processes, data flows, data stores, and external entities |
| **Context Diagram (Level 0 DFD)** | A single-process view of the entire system as one black box |
| **Level 0 / Level 1 DFD** | Successive decompositions of the context diagram into sub-processes |
| **Function Description / PSPEC** | A textual or tabular specification of what a single process does |
| **Balancing** | The property that inputs/outputs of a parent DFD match inputs/outputs of its child DFD |
| **External Entity (Terminator)** | Anything *outside* the system boundary that supplies or receives data |
| **Data Store** | A repository of data for later retrieval (e.g., a database table) |

> [!TIP]
> **Why Functional Modeling matters in OOMD:**
> Even though OO design emphasizes objects, the *requirements phase* often captures "what the system must do" most naturally as data transformations. DFDs serve as a **bridge** between user requirements and the eventual object/operation discovery in the object model.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

Although functional modeling is not a physics topic, KTU examiners expect familiarity with these **standard quality metrics** for DFDs:

- **DFD Level Count:** typically **3–4** levels in practice (Context → Level 0 → Level 1 → Level 2)
- **Process Granularity Rule:** Level *n* DFD should have **3–7 processes** (Rumbaugh's rule of thumb)
- **Balancing Rule:** **100%** of I/O flows must match between parent and child DFDs
- **Naming Convention:** Processes use **strong verbs** (`Verify_Password`); Data flows use **noun phrases** (`User_Credentials`); Data stores use **plural nouns** (`Customers`)
- **Notation:** KTU follows the **Gane-Sarson / Yourdon-DeMarco** symbol set
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Functional Modeling Pipeline (Three Phases)

Rumbaugh's OMT methodology (which KTU's PBCNT604 module follows) prescribes **three sequential activities** for functional modeling. These are exactly the three sub-topics in your syllabus title.

### Phase A — Identifying Input and Output Values

**Purpose:** Determine, *per use case*, what data the system must accept and what data it must produce.

**Step-by-step logic:**
1. Review every **use case** identified in Module 1 (requirements phase).
2. For each use case scenario, ask:
   - *"What information must the actor supply before the system can act?"* → **Inputs**
   - *"What information must the system return to the actor or persist?"* → **Outputs**
3. Classify each value as:
   - **Persistent** (goes into a data store) vs **Transient** (used and discarded)
   - **Simple** (single attribute) vs **Composite** (multiple attributes grouped)
4. Assign a **unique, descriptive name** to each value (used later in DFD labels).
5. Cross-check against the **Object Model** (Module 2's companion deliverable) — inputs/outputs typically correspond to attributes of objects.

**KTU "Why it matters":** Misidentifying I/O is the #1 reason for incomplete DFDs. Every arrow in the DFD must trace back to a documented I/O value.

---

### Phase B — Building Data Flow Diagrams

**Purpose:** Graphically represent the flow of identified values through processes, stores, and external entities.

**The four DFD primitives (Yourdon-DeMarco notation — KTU standard):**

| Symbol | Name | Meaning | Notation |
|---|---|---|---|
| ⬜ (square) | **External Entity** (Terminator / Actor) | Source or sink of data, *outside* the system boundary | Label with role name |
| ⭕ (circle) / rounded rect | **Process** | A function that transforms inputs into outputs | Label with `Verb_Noun` + unique ID (`1`, `2.1`, etc.) |
| ➡️ (arrow) | **Data Flow** | Movement of a data value/structure | Label with noun phrase |
| ▭ (open rect / parallel lines) | **Data Store** | A passive repository of data | Label with plural noun + ID (`D1`, `D2`) |

**The four-level hierarchy of DFDs:**

**Level 0 — Context Diagram**
- The entire system is **one single process** (a black box).
- All external entities (actors) are drawn around it.
- All input/output arrows cross the system boundary.
- *No data stores* appear in a context diagram.

**Level 1 — System Decomposition**
- The single process is exploded into **3–7 major sub-processes**.
- Data flows are re-routed between the sub-processes, possibly via data stores.
- This is the most common DFD level examined in KTU papers.

**Level 2 (and beyond) — Function Decomposition**
- A single Level-1 process is selected and decomposed further.
- Stops when each process is **atomic** (a single well-defined transformation).

**Level *n* ↔ Level *n+1* Relationship:**
- The child diagram **must be balanced** with its parent — meaning the *net* I/O of the child equals the *net* I/O of the parent process node.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Pearl:**
> A process at any level is **never** allowed to act as both source and sink of a data flow to itself (the "self-loop"). This is a frequently asked conceptual question.

---

### Phase C — Describing Functions (Process Specifications / PSPECs)

**Purpose:** Document *what* each atomic process does, since a DFD alone shows structure, not behaviour.

**PSPEC Formats (any of these may appear in KTU questions):**

**Format 1 — Input–Process–Output (IPO) Table**

| Section | Content |
|---|---|
| **Process ID & Name** | `3.2 Calculate_Fine` |
| **Inputs** | `Return_Date, Due_Date` |
| **Outputs** | `Fine_Amount` |
| **Processing Logic** | Days_Overdue = Return_Date − Due_Date; Fine = Days_Overdue × Rate |
| **Pre-conditions** | A loan record exists |
| **Post-conditions** | Fine_Amount ≥ 0; loan record is updated |

**Format 2 — Pseudocode (Structured English)**

```
PROCESS: Calculate_Fine
INPUT:  Return_Date, Due_Date
OUTPUT: Fine_Amount
BEGIN
    Days_Overdue ← Return_Date − Due_Date
    IF Days_Overdue > 0 THEN
        Fine_Amount ← Days_Overdue × DAILY_FINE_RATE
    ELSE
        Fine_Amount ← 0
    END IF
END
```

**Format 3 — Decision Table / Decision Tree** — used when PSPEC has many branches.

**Format 4 — Mathematical / Equation Form** — used for computational processes.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet & Reference Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The "formulas" below are **structural rules / quality metrics** that KTU examiners expect verbatim in design questions.

| Rule / Metric | Formula or Threshold | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Process Granularity (Gane-Sarson)** | $3 \leq P_n \leq 7$ where $P_n$ = processes at level $n$ | Controls DFD readability |
| **DFD Balance Check** | $\sum \text{Inputs}_{\text{parent}} = \sum \text{Inputs}_{\text{child}}$ and same for outputs | Ensures no information loss across levels |
| **Minimum Fan-In (Cohesion)** | $\text{Fan-In} \geq 1$ for every process | A process must have at least one input |
| **Minimum Fan-Out (Coupling)** | $\text{Fan-Out} \geq 1$ for every process | A process must produce at least one output (unless sink-only process) |
| **No Self-Loop Rule** | $\nexists f : P \rightarrow P$ | DFD must be acyclic in self-edges |
| **Max DFD Depth** | Depth $\leq 4$ levels (Context + 3) | Prevents unbounded decomposition |
| **Cyclomatic Complexity of DFD** | $V(G) = E - N + 2P$ where $E$ = edges, $N$ = nodes, $P$ = connected components | Optional KTU advanced question |
| **Naming Length Heuristic** | $\vert \text{Label} \vert \leq 30$ characters | Diagram readability |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Utility of Functional Modeling

- **Requirements Engineering:** DFDs are ISO/IEEE 830-compliant artefacts for specifying transformations unambiguously.
- **Legacy System Reverse Engineering:** DFDs can be re-extracted from COBOL/mainframe code via static analysis.
- **Banking & ERP Systems:** Transaction processing is naturally a data-transformation pipeline — perfect DFD fit.
- **ETL (Extract-Transform-Load) Pipelines:** Modern data engineering reuses DFD concepts under the name "data lineage diagrams."
- **Safety-Critical Systems (DO-178C, ISO 26262):** Functional decomposition via DFDs precedes FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis).

---

## 2.4 Common Pitfalls in KTU Answers

| Pitfall | Correct Practice |
|---|---|
| Drawing **control flows** (decision diamonds) inside a DFD | DFDs show **data**, not control — use a separate STD (State Transition Diagram) for control |
| Using **vague process names** like "Process Data" | Use strong verbs: `Validate_Customer`, `Calculate_Tax` |
| Forgetting to **balance** Level 0 and Level 1 | List flows in/out of parent process, then verify child flows match |
| Including **UI labels** in DFDs | DFDs are technology-agnostic — "Click Submit" is *not* a valid data flow |
| Drawing a data flow **directly** between two data stores | DFDs require a **process** to mediate any data-store interaction |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Procedure — Building a Complete Functional Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Running Example used throughout Section 3:** *Library Management System (LMS)*
> This will be the basis for the entire derivation procedure. KTU frequently uses such relatable systems.

---

## 3.1 Step 1 — Identify Input and Output Values

### Step 1.1: Enumerate Use Cases (from Module 1)

For the LMS we assume these use cases exist:
- **UC-01:** Issue Book to Member
- **UC-02:** Return Book
- **UC-03:** Register New Member
- **UC-04:** Search Catalogue
- **UC-05:** Generate Overdue Report

### Step 1.2: Extract I/O per Use Case

For **UC-01 (Issue Book)**, the analysis yields:

| Type | Value Name | Source / Sink | Composite? | Persistent? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Input | `Member_ID` | External Entity → System | Simple (string) | No |
| Input | `Book_ISBN` | External Entity → System | Simple (string) | No |
| Input | `Member_Record` | Data Store `D2:Members` | Composite | Yes |
| Input | `Book_Record` | Data Store `D1:Books` | Composite | Yes |
| Output | `Issue_Receipt` | System → External Entity | Composite (Date, Book title) | Transient |
| Output | `Loan_Record` | System → Data Store `D3:Loans` | Composite | Yes |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Insight — Composite vs Simple Values:**
> KTU asks: *"Is the value a single attribute or a group?"* If it is a group, you must decompose it further in the data dictionary, but on the DFD arrow you still label it with one composite name.

### Step 1.3: Document in Data Dictionary (best practice)

$$
\text{Member\_Record} = \{\text{Member\_ID}, \text{Name}, \text{Phone}, \text{Membership\_Status}\}
$$

$$
\text{Book\_Record} = \{\text{ISBN}, \text{Title}, \text{Author}, \text{Availability\_Flag}\}
$$

$$
\text{Loan\_Record} = \{\text{Loan\_ID}, \text{Member\_ID}, \text{ISBN}, \text{Issue\_Date}, \text{Due\_Date}\}
$$

---

## 3.2 Step 2 — Build the Data Flow Diagram

### Step 2.1: Draw the Context Diagram (Level 0)

**Procedure:**
1. Draw **one central process** labelled `0 : Library Management System`.
2. Draw a system boundary (dashed rectangle) around it.
3. Identify external entities: `Librarian`, `Member`.
4. Connect each entity to the central process with one **input arrow** and one **output arrow**.

**Mermaid representation (Block Architecture Style, since Mermaid does not have a native DFD shape set):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L["Librarian (EE)"]
    M["Member (EE)"]
    SYS(("0 : Library Management System"))
    L -- "Member Details, Book Request" --> SYS
    SYS -- "Issue Receipt, Status" --> L
    M -- "Search Query, Return Book" --> SYS
    SYS -- "Catalogue Result, Fine Notice" --> M
```

### Step 2.2: Identify the Major Sub-Processes (Level 1 Decomposition)

Apply Rumbaugh's rule $3 \leq P_1 \leq 7$. For LMS we choose **4 processes**:

| Process ID | Process Name | Inputs | Outputs |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `Register_Member` | Member_Details | Confirmation, Member_Record |
| 2 | `Issue_Book` | Member_ID, Book_ISBN | Issue_Receipt, updated Loan_Record |
| 3 | `Return_Book` | Book_ISBN, Return_Date | Return_Receipt, Fine_Notice (if any) |
| 4 | `Search_Catalogue` | Search_Query | Catalogue_Result |

### Step 2.3: Draw the Level 1 DFD

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L["Librarian (EE)"]
    M["Member (EE)"]
    D1[("D1 : Books")]
    D2[("D2 : Members")]
    D3[("D3 : Loans")]
    P1(("1 : Register_Member"))
    P2(("2 : Issue_Book"))
    P3(("3 : Return_Book"))
    P4(("4 : Search_Catalogue"))
    L -- "Member_Details" --> P1
    P1 -- "Confirmation" --> L
    P1 -- "Member_Record" --> D2
    D2 -- "Member_Record" --> P2
    L -- "Issue_Request\nMember_ID, Book_ISBN" --> P2
    P2 -- "Issue_Receipt" --> L
    P2 -- "Loan_Record" --> D3
    D3 -- "Loan_Record" --> P3
    M -- "Return_Info\nBook_ISBN, Return_Date" --> P3
    P3 -- "Return_Receipt\nFine_Notice" --> M
    D1 -- "Book_Record" --> P2
    D1 -- "Book_Record" --> P3
    D1 -- "Book_Record" --> P4
    M -- "Search_Query" --> P4
    P4 -- "Catalogue_Result" --> M
```

### Step 2.4: Balance the DFD

**Balancing Derivation:**

Let $\text{IO}_P$ = set of input/output flows at parent process, and $\text{IO}_C$ = net I/O at child level.

$$
\text{Balanced} \iff \text{IO}_P = \text{IO}_C
$$

For the central process `0` in our context diagram:

$$
\text{IO}_0 = \{\text{Member\_Details}, \text{Search\_Query}, \text{Return\_Book}, \text{Confirmation}, \text{Status}, \text{Catalogue\_Result}, \text{Fine\_Notice}\}
$$

For the Level 1 DFD's net input from external entities:

$$
\text{IO}_{L1} = \{\text{Member\_Details}, \text{Search\_Query}, \text{Return\_Info}\}
$$

$$
\text{IO}_{L1,\text{out}} = \{\text{Confirmation}, \text{Catalogue\_Result}, \text{Return\_Receipt}, \text{Fine\_Notice}, \text{Issue\_Receipt}\}
$$

Both must reconcile — internal flows (Book_Record, Loan_Record) are invisible to the boundary and don't affect balance.

### Step 2.5: Decompose a Complex Process to Level 2 (e.g., Process 2: Issue_Book)

`Issue_Book` itself has sub-steps:

| Sub-Process ID | Name | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 2.1 | `Verify_Member` | Check membership validity |
| 2.2 | `Check_Book_Availability` | Verify book is not on loan |
| 2.3 | `Create_Loan_Record` | Generate and persist loan |
| 2.4 | `Generate_Receipt` | Print/display issue receipt |

**Balancing check for Process 2 ↔ Level 2:**

$$
\text{IO}_2 = \{\text{Member\_ID}, \text{Book\_ISBN}, \text{Member\_Record}, \text{Book\_Record}, \text{Issue\_Receipt}, \text{Loan\_Record}\}
$$

This must equal the union of flows entering and exiting sub-processes 2.1–2.4 combined.

---

## 3.3 Step 3 — Describe Each Function (PSPEC)

For each atomic process in the deepest DFD level, write a PSPEC. Below is the PSPEC for `2.2 Check_Book_Availability`:

> **Process ID:** `2.2`
> **Process Name:** `Check_Book_Availability`
>
> **Inputs:** `Book_Record` (from D1)
> **Outputs:** `Availability_Flag` (Boolean), updated `Book_Record` (to D1)
> **Pre-conditions:** A `Book_Record` exists in D1 for the given ISBN
> **Post-conditions:** Either `Availability_Flag = TRUE` and `Book_Record.Availability = "Reserved"`, OR `Availability_Flag = FALSE`
>
> **Processing Logic (Structured English):**
>
> ```
> PROCESS: Check_Book_Availability
> INPUT:  Book_ISBN
> OUTPUT: Availability_Flag, Book_Record
> BEGIN
>     Book_Record ← Lookup(D1, Book_ISBN)
>     IF Book_Record = NULL THEN
>         Availability_Flag ← FALSE
>         RETURN
>     END IF
>     IF Book_Record.Availability = "Available" THEN
>         Availability_Flag ← TRUE
>         Book_Record.Availability ← "Reserved"
>         Update(D1, Book_Record)
>     ELSE
>         Availability_Flag ← FALSE
>     END IF
> END
> ```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Pattern — When to use which PSPEC format:**
> - **IPO Table** → for simple, computational processes (calculations, validations).
> - **Pseudocode / Structured English** → for processes with sequential logic and conditionals.
> - **Decision Table** → for processes with multiple independent conditions (e.g., discount calculation).
> - **Pre/Post-condition form** → for use-case-driven processes (Booch notation).

---

## 3.4 Step 4 — Cross-Check with Object Model (KTU Integration Point)

In OOMD, functional modelling is *not* done in isolation. For every PSPEC, ask:

$$
\text{Which object class is responsible for this transformation?}
$$

For `2.2 Check_Book_Availability`, the answer is `Catalog` or `Book` class — this becomes an **operation** in the object model. KTU questions often ask this traceability.

---

## 3.5 Step 5 — Validation Checklist (Self-Audit Before Submission)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Self-Audit Checklist (use this before submitting any DFD in an exam):**
>
> 1. ✅ Every process name is a strong verb phrase.
> 2. ✅ Every data flow is a noun phrase.
> 3. ✅ Every external entity is *outside* the boundary.
> 4. ✅ Every data store has a unique ID (`D1`, `D2`, …).
> 5. ✅ No data store-to-data store flow exists without an intervening process.
> 6. ✅ No process is a "black hole" (input without output) — except pure source-like sinks.
> 7. ✅ No process is a "miracle" (output without input).
> 8. ✅ Level *n* and Level *n+1* are **balanced**.
> 9. ✅ Granularity: 3–7 processes per diagram.
> 10. ✅ Every atomic process has a PSPEC.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
```

```mermaid
    L2A["Level 2 for Process 2<br/>2.1 Verify_Member<br/>2.2 Check_Book_Availability<br/>2.3 Create_Loan_Record<br/>2.4 Generate_Receipt"]

```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Process 2 : Issue_Book)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN_M["Member_ID"] --> S1
    IN_B["Book_ISBN"] --> S2
    S1["2.1 Verify_Member<br/>(reads D2:Members)"]
    S2["2.2 Check_Book_Availability<br/>(reads/updates D1:Books)"]
    S1 -- "Member_Verified?" --> S2
    S2 -- "Available?" --> S3
    S3["2.3 Create_Loan_Record<br/>(writes D3:Loans)"]
    S3 --> S4

    S4 -- "Issue_Receipt" --> OUT["To Librarian (EE)"]
    S3 -- "Loan_Record" --> D3["D3 : Loans"]
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PSPEC["PSPEC : Check_Book_Availability<br/>(Process 2.2)"]
    OBJ["Object Model<br/>Class: Catalog<br/>Operation: checkAvailability(ISBN): Boolean"]
    DYN["Dynamic Model<br/>State: Book_Available → Book_On_Loan"]
    PSPEC --> OBJ
    PSPEC --> DYN
```
