- *Co-location Heuristic*: Place the most-communicating pair of subsystems on the same processor.
- *Replication Heuristic*: Duplicate stateful subsystems on ≥ 2 processors.

**Step 6 — Decompose into Tasks**
Within each (now processor-bound) subsystem, identify **independent units of work** and assign them to **tasks/threads**. Tasks may be:
- *Periodic* (timer-driven, e.g., heartbeat monitor).
- *Event-driven* (interrupt-handlers, GUI callbacks).
- *On-demand* (spawned per request, e.g., a thread per HTTP request).

**Step 7 — Validate, Re-allocate, Iterate**
Re-run the critical use cases mentally or via simulation. If a **bottleneck** is detected, **re-allocate** or **replicate** the subsystem.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following metrics are standard in distributed-OO design. They appear in ESE Part B questions as the mathematical basis of allocation decisions.

| # | Metric / Formula | Description |
|---|------------------|-------------|
| 1 | $C_{ij} = f_{ij} \times n_{ij}$ | **Inter-subsystem communication cost** between subsystems $S_i$ and $S_j$, where $f_{ij}$ = frequency of calls and $n_{ij}$ = network cost per call. |
| 2 | $C_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} C_i^{proc} + \sum_{i=1}^{N-1}\sum_{j=i+1}^{N} C_{ij}^{net}$ | **Total system cost** = sum of processor cost + sum of network communication cost. |
| 3 | $L_p = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{k=1}^{m} L_{k}$ | **Average processor load** $L_p$ across $m$ processors, where $L_k$ is the load on processor $k$. |
| 4 | $U_k = \frac{L_k}{Cap_k}$ | **Utilization** of processor $k$, where $Cap_k$ is its capacity. Goal: $U_k \approx 0.7$ (avoid 100%). |
| 5 | $R = \frac{T_{response}^{distributed}}{T_{response}^{local}}$ | **Distribution penalty ratio**. If $R > 1.5$, the distribution is likely unjustified. |
| 6 | $A = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - a_i)$ | **System availability** with $n$ replicated subsystems each with availability $a_i$. |
| 7 | $T_{task} = T_{cpu} + T_{io} + T_{sync}$ | **Task turnaround time** = CPU time + I/O wait + synchronization/blocked time. |
| 8 | $Speedup_{N} = \frac{T_{1}}{T_{N}} \le N$ | **Amdahl's bound** for parallel tasks: $N$ tasks cannot give more than $N\times$ speedup. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Memorize formulas 1, 2, and 8. Questions on *"justify the distribution"*, *"compute communication cost"*, and *"estimate speedup"* are extremely common.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cloud-native design (AWS/Azure/GCP):** Engineers allocate microservices (subsystems) to virtual machines or containers; tasks become threads or serverless functions.
- **Automotive systems (AUTOSAR):** ECUs (processors) host software components (subsystems) with strict real-time task scheduling.
- **Mobile and embedded systems:** Task allocation affects battery life and thermal throttling.
- **High-frequency trading:** Microsecond-level latency demands drive aggressive co-location of subsystems on a single NUMA node.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix: When to Co-locate vs. Distribute

Let us derive a **decision rule** for placing two subsystems on the same processor versus different processors. This is a classic KTU-style analytical question.

> [!NOTE]
> **Given:**
> - Two subsystems $A$ and $B$ that exchange messages.
> - Frequency of inter-subsystem calls: $f$ (calls per second).
> - Cost of one inter-subsystem call when **distributed** (over network): $c_{net}$ (seconds/call).
> - Cost of one inter-subsystem call when **co-located** (shared memory): $c_{local}$ (seconds/call).
> - Cost of marshalling/unmarshalling data when distributed: $c_{marsh}$ (seconds/call).
> - Cost of IPC (inter-process communication) overhead when co-located: $c_{ipc}$ (seconds/call).

**Derivation:**

The cost of inter-subsystem communication **per second** is:

$$
C_{distributed} = f \times (c_{net} + c_{marsh})
$$

$$
C_{colocated} = f \times (c_{ipc} + c_{local})
$$

The two subsystems should be **co-located** if and only if the co-located communication cost is strictly less than the distributed cost:

$$
C_{colocated} < C_{distributed}
$$

Substituting the expressions:

$$
f \times (c_{ipc} + c_{local}) < f \times (c_{net} + c_{marsh})
$$

Dividing both sides by $f$ (assuming $f > 0$):

$$
c_{ipc} + c_{local} < c_{net} + c_{marsh}
$$

**Engineering Decision Rule:**

$$
\boxed{\;Co-locate \iff c_{ipc} + c_{local} < c_{net} + c_{marsh}\;}
$$

**Quantitative Example (for ESE answers):**

Suppose $c_{ipc} = 0.05\,\text{ms}$, $c_{local} = 0.10\,\text{ms}$, $c_{net} = 1.20\,\text{ms}$, $c_{marsh} = 0.30\,\text{ms}$, $f = 100$ calls/sec.

Left side: $0.05 + 0.10 = 0.15\,\text{ms/call}$.

Right side: $1.20 + 0.30 = 1.50\,\text{ms/call}$.

Since $0.15 < 1.50$, **co-location is justified**. The communication overhead is reduced by $10\times$.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Total Cost of Distribution

> [!NOTE]
> **Given:** $N$ subsystems, $M$ processors, an allocation matrix $A_{ij} \in \{0,1\}$ where $A_{ij} = 1$ means subsystem $i$ is on processor $j$.

**Step 1:** Define the **processor cost** per processor $j$ as $P_j$ (a fixed deployment cost, e.g., server rental fee).

**Step 2:** Define the **load** placed on processor $j$ as the sum of CPU demand of all subsystems allocated to it.

$$
Load_j = \sum_{i=1}^{N} A_{ij} \times L_i
$$

**Step 3:** Define the **network cost** as the cost of messages between subsystems on different processors.

$$
C_{net} = \sum_{i=1}^{N-1}\sum_{j=i+1}^{N} \left[ f_{ij} \times n_{ij} \times (1 - CoLocated_{ij}) \right]
$$

where $CoLocated_{ij} = 1$ if $A_{ik} = A_{jk}$ for some processor $k$ (i.e., they share a processor), and $0$ otherwise.

**Step 4:** The total cost is:

$$
\boxed{\;C_{total} = \sum_{j=1}^{M} P_j \times \mathbb{1}[\sum_i A_{ij} > 0] + C_{net}\;}
$$

The optimization objective is to **minimize** $C_{total}$ subject to the constraint $Load_j \le Capacity_j$ for all $j$.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Speedup via Task Parallelization

> [!NOTE]
> **Amdahl's Law for OO subsystems:**
> If a subsystem takes time $T$ on a single processor, and fraction $p$ of it is **parallelizable** across $N$ tasks, the theoretical speedup is:

$$
\boxed{\;S(N) = \frac{1}{(1 - p) + \frac{p}{N}}\;}
$$

**Derivation with worked numbers:**

Let $T = 1000$ ms, $p = 0.8$ (80% parallelizable), $N = 4$ tasks.

Sequential part: $(1 - p) \times T = 0.2 \times 1000 = 200$ ms.

Parallel part divided by $N$: $\frac{p \times T}{N} = \frac{0.8 \times 1000}{4} = 200$ ms.

Total parallelized time:

$$
T_{parallel} = 200 + 200 = 400\,\text{ms}
$$

Speedup:

$$
S(4) = \frac{1000}{400} = 2.5\times
$$

Even with 4 tasks, the speedup is only $2.5\times$ (not $4\times$) due to the **20% sequential bottleneck**. This is a classic KTU pitfall — students often claim $N\times$ speedup.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Availability with Replication

> [!NOTE]
> **Given:** A critical subsystem has per-instance availability $a = 0.95$ (down 5% of the time).

**With 1 instance:**
$$
A_1 = 0.95
$$

**With 2 instances (active-active replication, system fails only if both fail):**
$$
A_2 = 1 - (1 - 0.95) \times (1 - 0.95) = 1 - 0.0025 = 0.9975
$$

**With 3 instances:**
$$
A_3 = 1 - (0.05)^3 = 1 - 0.000125 = 0.999875
$$

**General formula:**

$$
A_n = 1 - (1 - a)^n
$$

Trade-off: Higher availability comes at the cost of **doubled/tripled hardware** and **synchronization overhead** between replicas.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 UML Deployment Diagram — Logical-to-Physical Mapping

The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to draw (or describe) a **UML Deployment Diagram** that shows the physical mapping of subsystems to processors. Below is the equivalent Mermaid representation.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PROC1["PROCESSOR 1 - Application Server"]
        SS1["Subsystem: OrderProcessing"]
        T1["Task: ValidateOrder"]
        T2["Task: PersistOrder"]
        SS1 --> T1
        SS1 --> T2
    end

    subgraph PROC2["PROCESSOR 2 - Database Server"]
        SS2["Subsystem: InventoryManagement"]
        T3["Task: QueryStock"]
        T4["Task: UpdateStock"]
        SS2 --> T3
        SS2 --> T4
    end

    subgraph PROC3["PROCESSOR 3 - Payment Gateway Host"]
        SS3["Subsystem: PaymentGateway"]
        T5["Task: EncryptCardData"]
        T6["Task: CallExternalAPI"]
        SS3 --> T5
        SS3 --> T6
    end

    PROC1 <-->"TCP/IP LAN" PROC2
    PROC1 <-->"HTTPS WAN" PROC3
    PROC2 <-->"Replication Link" PROC2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram (for ESE):**
> - `OrderProcessing` and `PaymentGateway` are *separately deployed* (distribution) because they have different security/performance profiles.
> - `InventoryManagement` has an **internal replication link** (self-loop) indicating a **backup / hot-standby replica** for fault tolerance.
> - The dashed arrows between processors represent **physical network connections** (in real UML, these are *Association paths* stereotyped `«communication»`).

### 4.2 Allocation Decision Flowchart

This flow summarizes the **algorithm** an OO designer follows to allocate subsystems.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0["Start: List all subsystems"] --> A1["Profile CPU, memory, I/O of each"]
    A1 --> A2["Build subsystem interaction matrix"]
    A2 --> A3{"Subsystem has high inter-call freq?"}
    A3 -- Yes --> A4["Co-locate with collaborator on same processor"]
    A3 -- No --> A5{"CPU-bound and performance critical?"}
    A5 -- Yes --> A6["Allocate to most powerful processor"]
    A5 -- No --> A7{"Requires fault tolerance?"}
    A7 -- Yes --> A8["Replicate on multiple processors"]
    A7 -- No --> A9["Allocate to any processor with available capacity"]
    A4 --> A10["Decompose subsystem into tasks"]
    A6 --> A10
    A8 --> A10
    A9 --> A10
    A10 --> A11["Map decisions to UML Deployment Diagram"]
    A11 --> A12{"Validate performance via simulation"}
    A12 -- Bottleneck --> A1
    A12 -- OK --> A13["Allocation complete"]
```

### 4.3 Task-Allocation Within a Subsystem

The following block diagram shows how a single subsystem (e.g., `NotificationService`) is decomposed into **concurrent tasks**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SSN["Subsystem: NotificationService"]
        TQ["Task: RequestQueue\n(consumer thread)"]
        TE["Task: EmailSender\n(periodic worker)"]
        TS["Task: SMSSender\n(periodic worker)"]
        TP["Task: PushNotifier\n(event-driven)"]
        TSCH["Task: Scheduler\n(timer-driven)"]
    end
    TQ --> TE
    TQ --> TS
    TQ --> TP
    TSCH --> TE
    TSCH --> TS
    TSCH --> TP
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mapping:**
> - `RequestQueue` is a **producer-consumer task**.
> - `EmailSender`, `SMSSender`, `PushNotifier` are **periodic tasks** triggered by the scheduler.
> - The internal arrows represent **message-passing or shared-memory events** — these are intra-subsystem (not inter-subsystem) communications, so they incur only local IPC cost.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A QUESTIONS (3 MARKS EACH)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**

**Q1. [3 Marks] Define the term "allocation of subsystems to processors" in the context of object-oriented system design. List any two factors that influence this allocation.**

**Model Answer:**

Allocation of subsystems to processors is the activity in the **System Design** phase where each logical subsystem (a group of cooperating classes identified during architectural design) is mapped onto a **physical computational node** (processor, server, or device) for execution.

**Two influencing factors:**

1. **Performance / Workload:** CPU-intensive subsystems (e.g., image processing, encryption) must be placed on powerful processors to meet response-time constraints.

2. **Communication patterns:** Subsystems that exchange a high volume of messages (e.g., `Order` and `Inventory` subsystems) should be **co-located** on the same processor to avoid expensive network round-trips.

*(Other valid factors: cost, fault tolerance, security, availability, network topology.)*

---

**Q2. [3 Marks] Distinguish between a subsystem and a task. Why is task-level concurrency introduced within a subsystem?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Subsystem | Task |
|--------|-----------|------|
| **Granularity** | A logical package of cooperating classes (architectural unit). | A unit of concurrent execution (thread/process) within a subsystem. |
| **Scope** | Defined at **design time**; mapped to processors. | Defined at **implementation time**; scheduled by the OS. |
| **Lifetime** | Exists across the system lifetime. | May be short-lived (per request) or long-lived (daemon). |
| **Communication** | Inter-subsystem: typically network (distributed). | Intra-subsystem: shared memory or local IPC. |

**Why introduce task-level concurrency?**

- To **parallelize** independent units of work within a subsystem (e.g., one task handles UI events, another polls the database).
- To **improve responsiveness** by offloading long-running operations (e.g., file I/O) to background tasks.
- To **exploit multi-core processors** — multiple tasks can execute truly in parallel on different cores.

---

### PART B QUESTIONS (14 MARKS) — MODULE-2 INTERNAL CHOICE

> **Instructions (as per KTU 2024 ESE):** *Answer ANY ONE full question from this module. Each carries 14 marks, split into sub-parts (a) and (b).*

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the *seven-step procedure* for allocating subsystems to processors. For each step, state the design artefact produced and the decision criterion applied.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Consider an e-commerce application with three subsystems — *Product Catalog*, *Shopping Cart*, and *Payment*. Product Catalog is CPU-bound and called 50 times/sec by Shopping Cart. Payment is called 5 times/sec by Shopping Cart and is security-critical. Draw the **UML Deployment Diagram** showing the optimal allocation and justify the placement of each subsystem on its chosen processor.

---

**Model Answer (a):**

The seven-step procedure is as follows:

**Step 1 — Identify the Logical Architecture.** Produce a *subsystem diagram* showing all packages and their classes. Criterion: ensure all identified use cases are covered by at least one subsystem.

**Step 2 — Profile the Workload.** Produce a *workload table* with CPU%, memory, and I/O per subsystem. Criterion: identify hotspots (e.g., subsystems invoked > 10 times/sec or with CPU > 70%).

**Step 3 — Identify Communication Patterns.** Produce an *interaction matrix* $M_{ij}$ = calls/sec between subsystems $i$ and $j$. Criterion: pairs with $M_{ij} > \text{threshold}$ are flagged for co-location.

**Step 4 — Define the Hardware Topology.** Produce a *network and processor inventory*. Criterion: enumerate CPU cores, RAM, OS, bandwidth, latency.

**Step 5 — Apply Allocation Heuristics.** Produce an *initial allocation matrix* $A_{ij}$. Criterion: minimize $C_{total}$ (Section 2.3 formula 2) while respecting capacity constraints.

**Step 6 — Decompose into Tasks.** Produce a *task diagram* per subsystem. Criterion: maximize parallel speedup subject to Amdahl's bound.

**Step 7 — Validate and Iterate.** Produce a *performance report* via simulation/profiling. Criterion: response-time SLA met, processor utilization $0.5 \le U_k \le 0.8$.

---

**Model Answer (b):**

**Optimal Allocation:**

| Subsystem | Processor | Reason |
|-----------|-----------|--------|
| Product Catalog | Processor 1 (App Server, high CPU) | CPU-bound; needs raw compute power. |
| Shopping Cart | Processor 1 (App Server) | Co-located with Product Catalog due to **50 calls/sec** high-frequency link. |
| Payment | Processor 2 (Secure Payment Host) | Security-critical; isolated on a hardened, PCI-DSS-compliant machine. |

**UML Deployment Diagram (text representation):**

```
    +----------------+        +-------------------+
    | Processor 1    |        | Processor 2       |
    | (App Server)   |        | (Secure Host)     |
    |                |        |                   |
    | [Product       | HTTPS  | [Payment          |
    |  Catalog] <--->|<------>|  Gateway]         |
    | [Shopping Cart]|  WAN   |                   |
    +----------------+        +-------------------+
                ^                       ^
                |                       |
                v                       v
            (users)                (bank APIs)
```

**Justification:**

1. **Co-location of Product Catalog and Shopping Cart:** From Section 3.1's decision rule, with 50 calls/sec, even a small per-call overhead ($c_{net} \approx 0.5$ ms) accumulates to $25$ ms/sec. Co-location reduces this to near-zero IPC cost.

2. **Distribution of Payment:** Although Shopping Cart → Payment has lower frequency (5/sec), Payment is **security-critical** and must be isolated from the public-facing app server to reduce attack surface.

3. **Performance:** The CPU-bound Product Catalog is placed on the app server with the highest CPU capacity, ensuring response times meet SLA.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Pattern — Alternative Choice]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define **Amdahl's Law**. A subsystem requires 1000 ms of processing time, of which 80% can be parallelized across multiple tasks. Calculate the **maximum speedup** achievable with 4, 8, and 16 tasks. Comment on the practical limit of parallelization.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A critical subsystem has a per-instance availability of 0.9. The system designer proposes to replicate it on 3 independent processors. Compute the **system-level availability** and discuss the cost-benefit of this replication. Suggest one alternative design if replication is not cost-effective.

---

**Model Answer (a):**

**Amdahl's Law** states that the maximum speedup $S(N)$ achievable by parallelizing a workload across $N$ processing units is:

$$
S(N) = \frac{1}{(1 - p) + \frac{p}{N}}
$$

where $p$ is the fraction of the workload that is parallelizable.

**Given:** $T = 1000$ ms, $p = 0.8$, hence sequential fraction = $1 - p = 0.2$.

**For N = 4:**

$$
S(4) = \frac{1}{0.2 + \frac{0.8}{4}} = \frac{1}{0.2 + 0.2} = \frac{1}{0.4} = 2.5
$$

**For N = 8:**

$$
S(8) = \frac{1}{0.2 + \frac{0.8}{8}} = \frac{1}{0.2 + 0.1} = \frac{1}{0.3} \approx 3.33
$$

**For N = 16:**

$$
S(16) = \frac{1}{0.2 + \frac{0.8}{16}} = \frac{1}{0.2 + 0.05} = \frac{1}{0.25} = 4.0
$$

**Maximum theoretical speedup** (as $N \to \infty$):

$$
S_{\infty} = \frac{1}{1 - p} = \frac{1}{0.2} = 5
$$

**Comment on practical limit:** Even with infinite processors, the speedup is capped at $5\times$ due to the **20% sequential bottleneck** (e.g., shared database lock, single-threaded logging). Doubling $N$ from 8 to 16 only gains $0.67\times$ more speedup — **diminishing returns**. Designers should therefore focus on *reducing* the sequential fraction (e.g., using sharding, asynchronous I/O) rather than blindly adding tasks.

---

**Model Answer (b):**

**Given:** $a = 0.9$, $n = 3$ replicas.

**System availability with 3 replicas (system fails only if all 3 fail):**

$$
A_3 = 1 - (1 - a)^n = 1 - (1 - 0.9)^3 = 1 - (0.1)^3 = 1 - 0.001 = 0.999
$$

**Comparison:**

| Configuration | Availability | Annual Downtime (approx.) |
|---------------|--------------|---------------------------|
| 1 instance | 0.900 | 36.5 days/year |
| 2 instances | 0.990 | 3.65 days/year |
| 3 instances | 0.999 | 8.77 hours/year |

**Cost-benefit analysis:**

- **Pro:** Downtime reduced from ~36 days to ~9 hours per year.
- **Con:** Hardware cost **tripled**; replica synchronization adds **latency** and **consistency complexity** (CAP-theorem trade-off).
- **Verdict:** Justified for **mission-critical** subsystems (e.g., payment, medical records); overkill for non-critical subsystems (e.g., recommendation engine).

**Alternative design if replication is not cost-effective:** Use a **warm-standby** configuration — one active instance plus one passive instance that activates only on failure. Cost: 2× hardware. Combined with a **circuit-breaker** and **graceful degradation** policy, the system can fail-over in seconds, giving near-replica availability at lower cost.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "subsystem" with "class":** Subsystems are *packages* of classes, not individual classes. Allocating one class per processor is impractical and wrong.
> 2. **Forgetting Amdahl's sequential bottleneck:** Students often compute speedup as $N\times$ blindly. Always subtract the sequential fraction.
> 3. **Ignoring synchronization cost in replication:** Replicated subsystems need a consensus protocol (e.g., Paxos/Raft) that adds latency. Mention this in answers.
> 4. **Missing UML Deployment diagram labels:** When asked to draw a deployment diagram, label **processors**, **subsystems**, and **connections** clearly. Dotted lines are for *communication* associations; solid boxes are *devices*.
> 5. **Mixing up availability and reliability:** Availability = uptime fraction; reliability = probability of no failure over a time interval. ESE questions sometimes test this distinction.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module-2**

- **Definition:** Allocation = mapping logical subsystems to physical processors and decomposing them into tasks.
- **Three goals:** *Performance*, *Cost*, *Availability* — always in trade-off.
- **Seven-step procedure:** Logical architecture → Workload profiling → Communication matrix → Hardware topology → Heuristic allocation → Task decomposition → Validate & iterate.
- **Key formulas (must memorize):**
  - Total cost: $C_{total} = \sum P_j + C_{net}$
  - Co-location rule: $c_{ipc} + c_{local} < c_{net} + c_{marsh}$
  - Amdahl's speedup: $S(N) = \frac{1}{(1-p) + p/N}$
  - Availability with replication: $A_n = 1 - (1 - a)^n$
- **Co-locate** subsystems that have high inter-call frequency to reduce $C_{net}$.
- **Distribute** subsystems that have different security, scalability, or fault-tolerance profiles.
- **Replicate** only mission-critical, stateful subsystems; remember the CAP-theorem trade-off.
- **Tasks** are intra-subsystem concurrency units; **subsystems** are inter-processor distribution units.
- **UML Deployment Diagram** is the *primary artefact* for documenting allocation decisions.
- **Avoid the pitfalls:** Do not claim $N\times$ speedup blindly; do not ignore marshalling cost; do not forget the sequential bottleneck.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Managing Data Stores
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCNT604 - OBJECT ORIENTED MODELING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Managing Data Stores

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Object-Oriented Modeling and Design (OOMD)** under the KTU 2024 Scheme, **Managing Data Stores** refers to the systematic UML-based techniques used to model how an object (or a system of collaborating objects) **stores, retrieves, persists, and updates its internal data** as it transitions through different states during its lifecycle. The concept spans two primary UML behavioral diagrams: **UML State Machine Diagrams** and **UML Activity Diagrams**.

A **Data Store**, in strict UML 2.5 specification terminology, is *"a special kind of central buffer node that persists data; data put into a data store node persists until the data store node receives a removal signal or is destroyed."* It is graphically represented as a 3D box (`📦`) with the stereotype `«datastore»`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PBCNT604 – Module 2):**
> *"Managing Data Stores – Do activities, Entry and Exit actions, Modeling changing state, Modeling data flow in activity diagrams, Object nodes, Central buffer nodes, Data store nodes, Partitioning activity diagrams (Swimlanes)."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **smart vending machine** that dispenses coffee:

1. **The Vending Machine is the OBJECT**, and its **internal variables** (coin balance, water level, milk quantity, current selection) are its **DATA**.
2. The machine behaves **differently depending on the value of its data**:
   - If `coinBalance < price` → it waits for coins.
   - If `coinBalance >= price` → it begins brewing.
   - If `waterLevel == 0` → it goes to **OutOfService** state.
3. While in the **Brewing** state, the machine continuously reduces `waterLevel` and `coffeePowderLevel` — this continuous internal data management is a **Do-Activity**.
4. When the machine **enters** the *OutOfService* state, it logs the error → **Entry Action**.
5. When it **exits** the *Brewing* state, it deducts the change → **Exit Action**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Summary:** *Managing Data Stores = Deciding WHERE an object keeps its data, HOW long it stays, and WHAT triggers changes in it during the object's life.*

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics & Notations

The following are the **standard UML notations** every KTU 2024 student must memorize:

| UML Notation | Meaning | Standard |
|--------------|---------|----------|
| Rounded Rectangle | State | **UML 2.5** |
| Solid Arrow | Transition | **UML 2.5** |
| Filled Circle | Initial Pseudostate | **UML 2.5** |
| Bullseye (◎) | Final State | **UML 2.5** |
| 3D Box | Data Store Node | **UML 2.5** |
| Rectangle with shadow | Central Buffer Node | **UML 2.5** |
| `do / activity_name` | Do Activity keyword | **UML 2.5** |
| `entry / action_name` | Entry Action keyword | **UML 2.5** |
| `exit / action_name` | Exit Action keyword | **UML 2.5** |
| Vertical swimlanes | Partition (org/unit) | **UML 2.5** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of Managing Data Stores

### Pillar 1 — **Do Activities** (Continuous Internal Data Manipulation)
A **Do-Activity** is an action that executes *continuously and atomically* while an object is in a particular state. It is used when the object needs to manipulate its own internal data *without* leaving the state.

**Logical Bulleted Steps:**
- Step 1: Object enters a state.
- Step 2: UML engine invokes the `entry /` action (one-time).
- Step 3: The `do /` activity begins executing and remains active.
- Step 4: Internal data attributes are continuously updated.
- Step 5: Upon receiving a triggering event, the `do /` activity is aborted.
- Step 6: The `exit /` action executes (one-time).
- Step 7: The object transitions to the next state.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** A *do-activity* is **NOT** a method call. It is a long-running behavior that the state itself owns. It can be interrupted by an outgoing transition.

### Pillar 2 — **Entry and Exit Actions** (State-Boundary Data Hooks)
- **Entry Action** (`entry /`): Executed **once, immediately** when entering a state. Used to *initialize* data, log state entry, allocate resources, or start timers.
- **Exit Action** (`exit /`): Executed **once, immediately** when leaving a state. Used to *clean up* data, release resources, log state exit, or persist buffers.

**The "Look-Forward" Property:**
If multiple transitions leave a state, the **exit action is executed regardless of which transition is taken** — guaranteeing that cleanup is *always* performed. This is a common KTU exam question.

### Pillar 3 — **Modeling Changing State** (Data-Driven Transitions)
An object's state is, by definition, a function of the **values of its attributes** at a particular point in time. Therefore, every transition in a state machine is fundamentally triggered by:
- An **Event** (signal, call, time, change)
- A **Guard Condition** (boolean expression over the data)

**Transition Format:**
```
event [guard] / action
```

### Pillar 4 — **Modeling Data Flow in Activity Diagrams** (Object Nodes)
An **Object Node** is an activity node that indicates an instance of a particular classifier (e.g., `Order`, `Invoice`) flows through the activity. It represents the **physical movement of data objects** from one action to another.

**Sub-types of Object Nodes (CRITICAL for KTU):**

| Node Type | Notation | Persistence | Multiple Tokens? |
|-----------|----------|-------------|------------------|
| **Object Node (parameter)** | Rectangle with class name | No (transient) | Yes (with multiplicity) |
| **Central Buffer Node** | Rectangle with shadow effect | No (lost when activity ends) | Yes (by default) |
| **Data Store Node** | 3D box (`«datastore»`) | **Yes** (persists) | No (single instance) |

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet (Conceptual Formula Sheet)

Since this topic is largely diagrammatic, the "formula sheet" is replaced with the **Mandatory UML Notation & Keyword Cheat Sheet** that KTU examiners test most frequently.

| Concept | UML Syntax | Purpose in Managing Data | Example |
|---------|------------|--------------------------|---------|
| Entry Action | `entry / log("State X entered")` | Initialize data on state entry | `entry / balance = 0` |
| Exit Action | `exit / persist(buffer)` | Clean up on state exit | `exit / saveDraft()` |
| Do Activity | `do / processQueue()` | Continuously manage data | `do / monitorTemperature()` |
| Self Transition | Arrow looping back | Trigger exit→entry on same state | `reset [balance == 0] / reset()` |
| Internal Transition | Arrow with `[H]` notation | No state exit; only fires action | `updateSpeed [valid] / setSpeed()` |
| Central Buffer | `«centralBuffer» Obj` | Temporary in-memory holding | `«centralBuffer» TransactionQueue` |
| Data Store | `«datastore» Database` | Persistent storage of data | `«datastore» OrderDB` |
| Swimlane / Partition | Vertical/horizontal band | Maps actions to responsible unit | `Customer \| Bank \| Vendor` |
| Object Flow Edge | Dashed arrow with `«object»` | Shows data moving between actions | `«Order»` flowing from `Place` to `Pay` |
| Multiplicity on Object | `*`, `1..*` on object node edge | Limits tokens in flow | `Order[*] → ValidateOrder` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-Confusion Callout:**
> - A **Central Buffer** is *not* persistent. When the enclosing activity terminates, its contents vanish.
> - A **Data Store** is *persistent*. It survives across activity invocations and even across system restarts (modelled, not actual).
> - In KTU exams, examiners frequently give partial marks if you can distinguish these two.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of Managing Data Stores |
|--------|--------------------------------------|
| **Embedded Systems (IoT)** | Sensor state machines with do-activities for continuous polling |
| **Banking Software** | Transaction data store nodes + partition swimlanes for legal compliance |
| **Avionics** | Flight control state machines with strict entry/exit logging (DO-178C compliance) |
| **Healthcare Systems** | Patient record data stores with HIPAA-mandated persistence |
| **Telecommunications** | Call state machines with do-activities for tone generation |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Modeling Matrices

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Data Store Mechanism

> **Problem Context:** A KTU 14-mark question often asks: *"Given the following scenario, derive the state machine and activity diagram with appropriate data stores."*

**Step 1: Identify the OBJECT whose lifecycle is to be modeled.**
- Read the problem statement and extract the **noun** that has *states* and *behaviors*.
- Example: *ATM, Printer, Elevator, Microwave, Order, LoginSession.*

**Step 2: Enumerate the ATTRIBUTES (data) of the object.**
- Example (ATM): `cardNumber`, `pinAttempts`, `balance`, `cashDispensed`, `transactionLog[]`.

**Step 3: Identify STATES — these are points where the object's data is "stable" and behavior is "characteristic".**
- Use the **Kishon Split Rule**: For every guard condition, ask *"Is the behavior different?"* → Yes → New State.

**Step 4: Identify EVENTS and GUARDS — these trigger transitions between states.**

**Step 5: Identify INTERNAL ACTIONS:**
- Anything done *once on entry* → `entry /`
- Anything done *continuously while present* → `do /`
- Anything done *once on exit* → `exit /`

**Step 6: For ACTIVITY DIAGRAMS:**
- Replace *states* with *actions*.
- Replace *events* with *control flows*.
- Add *object nodes* wherever data is *produced* or *consumed*.
- Decide: **Central Buffer** (transient) or **Data Store** (persistent)?
- Apply **Swimlanes** if multiple actors/units are responsible.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Derivation — The "Microwave Oven" Scenario

**Scenario:** A microwave oven has Idle, Cooking, and Paused states. While cooking, a timer continuously decrements. On door-open, it pauses and saves the remaining time. On door-close-and-resume, it continues. On timer-zero, cooking ends with a beep.

### Step 1 — Identify the Object and its Data

**Object:** `MicrowaveOven`
**Attributes:**
- `remainingTime : int` (seconds)
- `doorOpen : boolean`
- `powerLevel : int`
- `beepCount : int`

### Step 2 — Identify the States

$$
S = \{ \text{Idle}, \text{Cooking}, \text{Paused}, \text{DoorOpen} \}
$$

This is derived by enumerating distinct behaviors:
- Idle: does nothing meaningful, just displays clock.
- Cooking: actively decrements timer, runs magnetron.
- Paused: timer is frozen, magnetron is off, but data is preserved.
- DoorOpen: physical door is open, **independent** of cooking state.

### Step 3 — Identify Events, Guards, and Actions

**Events:**
- `startPressed(power, time)`
- `pausePressed`
- `resumePressed`
- `doorOpened`
- `doorClosed`
- `tick` (1-second timer interrupt)

**Guards:**
- `[remainingTime == 0]` → Cooking should terminate
- `[doorOpen == true]` → blocks resume

**Actions:**
- `entry / powerOn()` (Idle)
- `do / decrementTimer()` (Cooking)
- `entry / saveRemainingTime()` (Paused)
- `exit / beep()` (Cooking → Idle on completion)

### Step 4 — Construct the State Diagram (Verbal Derivation)

The full state machine can be symbolically written as:

$$
\delta : S \times E \rightarrow S \times A
$$

Where:
- $S$ = set of states
- $E$ = set of events
- $A$ = set of actions

A sample transition rule:
$$
\delta(\text{Cooking}, \text{pausePressed}) = (\text{Paused}, \text{entry / saveRemainingTime}())
$$

Another rule:
$$
\delta(\text{Cooking}, \text{tick} \, [\,\text{remainingTime} > 0\,]) = (\text{Cooking}, \text{do / decrementTimer}())
$$

And the termination rule:
$$
\delta(\text{Cooking}, \text{tick} \, [\,\text{remainingTime} == 0\,]) = (\text{Idle}, \text{exit / beep}())
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** The transition function $\delta$ must be **deterministic** for any given state-event pair (under a chosen guard). If two transitions are possible from one state on the same event, they MUST have **mutually exclusive guards** to avoid ambiguity.

### Step 3 (Activity Diagram) — Deriving Data Store vs Central Buffer

**Decision Rule:**

$$
\text{Node Type} = \begin{cases} \text{Data Store Node} & \text{if } \text{persistence} == \text{true} \\ \text{Central Buffer Node} & \text{if } \text{persistence} == \text{false} \end{cases}
$$

Apply this to each data element:

| Data Element | Persists Across Sessions? | Node Type |
|--------------|--------------------------|-----------|
| `TransactionLog` | ✅ Yes | **Data Store** |
| `OrderQueue` (in-flight) | ❌ No | **Central Buffer** |
| `UserSession` | ❌ No (expires) | **Central Buffer** |
| `AuditLog` | ✅ Yes (regulated) | **Data Store** |
| `CartItems` (current visit) | ❌ No (lost on logout) | **Central Buffer** |

### Step 4 (Swimlane Derivation) — Identifying Responsible Units

For an e-commerce order processing system, the swimlanes are derived as:

$$
\text{Lanes} = \bigcup_{i} \text{ResponsibleUnit}_i
$$

For example:
- `Customer`
- `WebServer`
- `PaymentGateway`
- `Warehouse`
- `ShippingProvider`

Each action in the activity diagram is **assigned** to exactly one lane. If an action crosses lanes, an object flow edge connects them.

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Verification of UML Compliance

**Checklist (used by KTU valuators):**

$$
\text{Score} = \sum_{i=1}^{7} w_i \cdot \text{check}_i
$$

Where each `check_i` is 1 (satisfied) or 0 (violated):

| $i$ | Check Item | Weight $w_i$ |
|-----|------------|--------------|
| 1 | All transitions have valid syntax (`event [guard] / action`) | **2** |
| 2 | Entry/Exit actions correctly use `entry /` and `exit /` keywords | **2** |
| 3 | Do-activities correctly use `do /` keyword and are interruptible | **2** |
| 4 | Initial and final pseudostates are present | **1** |
| 5 | Object nodes have correct stereotypes (`«datastore»`, `«centralBuffer»`) | **2** |
| 6 | Swimlanes properly partition the activity | **1** |
| 7 | Object flow edges are dashed (not solid) | **1** |

**Total Possible: 11 marks** — this aligns with a typical KTU 7+7 sub-question split.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid State Machine Diagram — Microwave Oven with Data Management

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle

    state Idle {
        [*] --> Waiting
        Waiting --> [*]
    }

    state Cooking {
        [*] --> Heating
        Heating --> Heating : tick[remainingTime > 0] / do: decrementTimer()
        Heating --> [*] : tick[remainingTime == 0] / exit: beep()
    }

    state Paused {
        [*] --> Frozen
        Frozen --> [*] : entry: saveRemainingTime()
    }

    Idle        --> Cooking     : startPressed(p,t) / entry: setTimer(p,t)
    Cooking     --> Paused      : pausePressed / entry: freeze()
    Cooking     --> Idle        : cancelPressed / exit: clearTimer()
    Paused      --> Cooking     : resumePressed[doorOpen == false] / entry: restoreTimer()
    Paused      --> Idle        : cancelPressed
    Cooking     --> Idle        : tick[remainingTime == 0] / exit: beep()
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagram:**
> - The arrow `tick[remainingTime > 0] / do: decrementTimer()` is a **self-transition** (a *do-activity in disguise*).
> - `entry: saveRemainingTime()` shows an **entry action** that writes data to a local variable.
> - The condition `[doorOpen == false]` is a **guard** — if false, the transition does NOT fire.

## 4.2 Mermaid Activity Diagram — Order Processing with Data Stores and Swimlanes

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph CustomerLane["LANE 1: Customer"]
        A1[Browse Catalog]:::action
        A2[Place Order]:::action
        A3[Receive Confirmation]:::action
    end

    subgraph WebServerLane["LANE 2: Web Server"]
        B1[Validate Cart]:::action
        B2[Generate OrderID]:::action
    end

    subgraph PaymentLane["LANE 3: Payment Gateway"]
        C1[Process Payment]:::action
        C2[Confirm Transaction]:::action
    end

    subgraph WarehouseLane["LANE 4: Warehouse"]
        D1[Pick Items]:::action
        D2[Pack Order]:::action
        D3[Dispatch]:::action
    end

    CB1[/"«centralBuffer» PendingOrders"/]:::buffer
    DS1[("«datastore» OrderDB")]:::datastore
    DS2[("«datastore» PaymentLog")]:::datastore

    A1 --> B1
    B1 --> A2
    A2 --> B2
    B2 -->|"«Order»"| CB1
    CB1 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 -->|"«PaymentReceipt»"| DS2
    C2 -->|"«Order»"| D1
    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> D3
    D3 --> A3
    B2 -->|"«Order»"| DS1

    classDef action fill:#E0F2FE,stroke:#0369A1,stroke-width:1px
    classDef buffer fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#B45309,stroke-width:1px
    classDef datastore fill:#DCFCE7,stroke:#15803D,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Explanation of Block-Level Architecture:**
> - Each rectangular **subgraph** represents a **Swimlane (Partition)** of the activity diagram — they map to a responsible unit.
> - The yellow parallelogram `«centralBuffer» PendingOrders` holds in-flight data that vanishes if the system crashes.
> - The green 3D-style boxes `«datastore»` are **persistent** — they survive system restarts.
> - Dashed edges (in actual UML) carry *object tokens* — in this Mermaid fallback, they are labelled with the object type.

## 4.3 Mermaid — Internal vs External Transition Comparison

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    direction LR
    state State_X {
        direction LR
        A --> B
    }

    State_X --> State_X : externalTransition / exit then entry
    State_X --> State_X : H : internalTransition / action only, no state change
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction for KTU:**
> - **External Transition:** `State → State` (even if same state): executes both `exit /` and `entry /` actions.
> - **Internal Transition** (denoted with `[H]` or `H:` in UML): executes ONLY the listed action — does **NOT** re-execute `entry /` or `exit /`.
> - This is a 3-mark "trick" question in KTU papers.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between an Entry action and a Do-activity in a UML state machine diagram. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Criterion | Entry Action | Do-Activity |
|-----------|--------------|-------------|
| **Keyword** | `entry /` | `do /` |
| **When it executes** | Exactly once, when the state is entered | Continuously, throughout the time the object remains in the state |
| **Duration** | Atomic, instantaneous | Long-running, can be interrupted |
| **Use case** | Initialize data, allocate resources | Continuous polling, countdown, monitoring |

**Examples:**
- **Entry action:** `entry / balance = 0` (Account object upon creation in *Active* state).
- **Do-activity:** `do / decrementTimer()` (Oven object while in *Cooking* state — runs every second).

*(1 mark for difference table, 1 mark for entry example, 1 mark for do-activity example)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**What is the difference between a Central Buffer Node and a Data Store Node in UML activity diagrams? When would you use each?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Central Buffer Node** is an object node that holds data *temporarily* within the scope of an activity. Its contents are lost when the enclosing activity terminates. Notation: a rectangle with a *shadow* effect, stereotyped `«centralBuffer»`.

A **Data Store Node** is a *special* central buffer node that **persists** data beyond the lifetime of the activity. Notation: a 3D box stereotyped `«datastore»`.

- **Use Central Buffer for:** in-flight orders, transient session data, temporary queues.
- **Use Data Store for:** committed order database, audit logs, persistent user accounts.

*(1 mark for central buffer definition, 1 mark for data store definition, 1 mark for usage distinction)*

---

## 📝 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper Adaptation]**

**(a)** *Explain the concept of Managing Data Stores in UML. With suitable examples, describe the role of Do-activities, Entry actions, and Exit actions in managing an object's internal data during state transitions.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *For a "Login Authentication System", draw the UML state machine diagram clearly showing the management of data attributes (`passwordAttempts`, `isLocked`, `sessionToken`). Use Entry, Exit, and Do-activity notation appropriately. Justify each transition with a guard condition.* **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks Model Solution

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Managing Data Stores in UML is the modeling discipline of representing how an object stores, manipulates, and persists its internal data across different states. UML provides three primary mechanisms for this: *Entry actions*, *Do-activities*, and *Exit actions*.

**Entry Actions (2 marks):**
- Defined using the `entry /` keyword inside a state.
- Executed **exactly once** when the state is entered.
- Used to **initialize** data attributes, allocate resources, or start timers.
- *Example:* `entry / sessionToken = generateUUID()` in the *Authenticated* state of a login system.

**Do-Activities (2 marks):**
- Defined using the `do /` keyword.
- Execute **continuously** while the object remains in the state.
- Can be **interrupted** by an outgoing transition.
- *Example:* `do / monitorHeartbeat()` in the *ConnectionActive* state of a network socket.

**Exit Actions (2 marks):**
- Defined using the `exit /` keyword.
- Executed **exactly once** when the state is exited, regardless of which transition is taken.
- Used to **clean up** data, release resources, or persist buffers.
- *Example:* `exit / closeFileHandle()` in the *FileOpen* state.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks Model Solution

**State Machine Diagram (5 marks):**

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> LoggedOut

    state LoggedOut {
        [*] --> AwaitingCredentials
    }

    state Authenticated {
        [*] --> ActiveSession
        ActiveSession --> ActiveSession : tick / do: refreshSessionToken()
    }

    state Locked {
        [*] --> CoolingDown
    }

    LoggedOut    --> Authenticated : credentialsValid / entry: setSessionToken()
    LoggedOut    --> Locked : invalidCredentials[passwordAttempts >= 3] / entry: lockAccount()
    Locked       --> LoggedOut : timeoutReached / exit: resetAttempts()
    Authenticated --> LoggedOut : logoutPressed / exit: invalidateToken()
    Authenticated --> LoggedOut : sessionExpired[elapsed > 1800] / exit: clearSession()
```

**Justification of Transitions (2 marks):**

| Transition | Guard Condition | Why? |
|------------|----------------|------|
| `invalidCredentials` | `[passwordAttempts >= 3]` | Prevents brute-force — locks after 3 tries |
| `timeoutReached` | `[cooldownTimeElapsed == true]` | Auto-unlock after 5-minute cooldown |
| `sessionExpired` | `[elapsed > 1800]` | 30-minute idle timeout for security |
| `tick` (self-transition) | None | Periodic token refresh; `do:` activity demonstrates continuous data management |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** *Differentiate between Central Buffer Node and Data Store Node. For an "Online Food Delivery System", identify THREE data elements that should be modeled as Data Store Nodes and TWO that should be Central Buffer Nodes. Justify your choices.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Draw a UML Activity Diagram for the "Online Food Delivery System" partitioned into swimlanes: `Customer`, `Restaurant`, `DeliveryAgent`, and `PaymentService`. Include at least ONE Data Store Node and ONE Central Buffer Node with appropriate object flow edges.* **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks Model Solution

**Differentiation Table (3 marks):**

| Feature | Central Buffer Node | Data Store Node |
|---------|--------------------|-----------------|
| **Notation** | Rectangle with shadow | 3D box |
| **Stereotype** | `«centralBuffer»` | `«datastore»` |
| **Persistence** | No (transient) | Yes (persistent) |
| **Survives activity end?** | No | Yes |
| **Default multiplicity** | Multiple tokens allowed | Single token |

**Three Data Store Elements (3 marks — 1 each):**

1. **Order History Database** — *Persistent*: customers expect to view past orders across sessions. ✅ Data Store.
2. **Restaurant Menu Database** — *Persistent*: menus are stored centrally and updated rarely. ✅ Data Store.
3. **Payment Transaction Log** — *Persistent & Regulated*: required by financial law (RBI/PCI-DSS). ✅ Data Store.

**Two Central Buffer Elements (1 mark — 0.5 each):**

1. **In-Flight Cart Items** — *Transient*: cleared on checkout or session end. ✅ Central Buffer.
2. **Active Delivery Tracking Queue** — *Transient*: needed only during the live delivery. ✅ Central Buffer.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks Model Solution

**Activity Diagram (5 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph CustomerLane["LANE: Customer"]
        A1[Browse Restaurants]:::action
        A2[Add to Cart]:::action
        A3[Place Order]:::action
        A4[Track Delivery]:::action
    end

    subgraph RestaurantLane["LANE: Restaurant"]
        R1[Accept Order]:::action
        R2[Prepare Food]:::action
        R3[Mark Ready]:::action
    end

    subgraph DeliveryLane["LANE: Delivery Agent"]
        D1[Pickup from Restaurant]:::action
        D2[Deliver to Customer]:::action
    end

    subgraph PaymentLane["LANE: Payment Service"]
        P1[Charge Customer]:::action
        P2[Release to Restaurant]:::action
    end

    CB[/"«centralBuffer» CartItems"/]:::buffer
    DS1[("«datastore» OrderHistoryDB")]:::datastore
    DS2[("«datastore» PaymentLog")]:::datastore

    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> CB
    CB --> A3
    A3 --> P1
    A3 --> R1
    P1 --> P2
    P1 -->|"«Transaction»"| DS2
    R1 --> R2
    R2 --> R3
    R3 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> A4
    A3 -->|"«Order»"| DS1

    classDef action fill:#E0F2FE,stroke:#0369A1
    classDef buffer fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#B45309
    classDef datastore fill:#DCFCE7,stroke:#15803D,stroke-width:2px
```

**Justification of Object Nodes (2 marks):**

- `«centralBuffer» CartItems` holds items only until the order is placed — vanishes if session ends.
- `«datastore» OrderHistoryDB` retains orders for repeat customers and analytics — persists permanently.
- `«datastore» PaymentLog` is required for financial auditing and dispute resolution — must persist.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the keyword:** Writing "do : decrementTimer()" with a colon instead of "do / decrementTimer()" with a slash → loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing self-transition with do-activity:** A do-activity is shown INSIDE the state with `do /`, NOT as a self-transition outside. Both are valid but for different semantics.
> 3. **Mixing stereotypes:** Writing `«datastore»` on a central buffer or vice-versa → loses 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Solid arrows for object flow:** Object flow edges MUST be **dashed** in UML. Solid arrow = control flow.
> 5. **No initial/final pseudostate:** Always include `[*] →` and `→ [*]` for full marks in state machine diagrams.
> 6. **No guard on conflicting transitions:** If two transitions share the same event from the same state, they MUST have mutually exclusive guards.
> 7. **Swimlane placement error:** An action must lie ENTIRELY within one swimlane. Spanning two lanes means it should be split.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **State** = condition during the life of an object determined by the values of its attributes.
- ✅ **Entry action** (`entry /`) runs once on entry; used for **initialization**.
- ✅ **Exit action** (`exit /`) runs once on exit; used for **cleanup**.
- ✅ **Do-activity** (`do /`) runs **continuously** while in state; **interruptible**.
- ✅ **Internal transition** (`H:` or `[H]`) does NOT fire `entry /` or `exit /`.
- ✅ **External transition** (including self-transitions) DOES fire both `entry /` and `exit /`.
- ✅ **Object Node** = movement of an object instance through actions (dashed arrow).
- ✅ **Central Buffer Node** = transient storage within an activity (rectangle with shadow).
- ✅ **Data Store Node** = persistent storage (3D box, `«datastore»`).
- ✅ **Swimlanes (Partitions)** = responsibility assignment — vertical/horizontal bands in activity diagram.
- ✅ **Transition syntax:** `event [guard] / action`.
- ✅ **Guards** must be **mutually exclusive** when multiple transitions exist for the same event.
- ✅ **Determinism:** For a given (state, event) pair, only ONE transition should be fireable.
- ✅ **KTU examiner's mantra:** *"Notation, Keyword, Semantics, Justification"* — get all four for full marks.
- ✅ **Anti-confusion:** A *do-activity* is NOT a method call. A *state* is NOT a class. An *object node* is NOT a class diagram object.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Handling of Global Resources
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCNT604 - OBJECT ORIENTED MODELING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module - 2
> - **Topic:** Handling of Global Resources

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Global Resources** in Object-Oriented Modeling refer to *shared, system-wide entities* (objects, services, data, or hardware) that must be accessed and manipulated by **multiple concurrent objects, state machines, or subsystems** in a coordinated, thread-safe, and deterministic manner.

In the context of UML-based modeling (KTU PBCNT604), handling of global resources primarily concerns:
- **Concurrent state regions** in UML State Machine Diagrams
- **Fork/Join synchronization nodes** in UML Activity Diagrams
- **Guard conditions** on transitions
- **Entry/Exit actions** that acquire or release shared resources
- **Signal-based inter-process communication**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** The focus is on how UML 2.x notation models *resource contention, mutual exclusion, and synchronization* between independent control flows without violating object encapsulation.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Co-Working Space Printer" Analogy 🖨️

Imagine a **shared co-working office** with **3 engineers** and only **1 printer**.

| Real-World Element | OO Modeling Equivalent |
|---|---|
| The 3 engineers | Concurrent state regions (independent threads of control) |
| The single printer | **Global resource** (shared object) |
| Engineer's request slip | **Signal** sent to a resource manager |
| The print queue | **Entry action** of the "in use" state |
| Releasing the printer | **Exit action** of the "in use" state |
| Waiting for your turn | **Mute/Wait state** in the state machine |

**Key Insight:** Just as engineers *cannot* simultaneously print without queue management, **concurrent state regions in UML must synchronize** their access to global resources using **fork, join, and signal transitions** — or risk **race conditions**, **deadlocks**, and **starvation**.

> [!NOTE]
> A **race condition** occurs when two or more concurrent activities access a shared resource concurrently, and the final result depends on the unpredictable timing of their execution.

---

## 1.3 Core Vocabulary (Examination-Ready)

- **Global Resource** — An object/asset accessible across multiple subsystems.
- **Concurrency** — Two or more flows of control executing simultaneously.
- **Synchronization** — Coordination mechanism enforcing order of access.
- **Mutual Exclusion (Mutex)** — Ensuring only one object accesses the resource at a time.
- **Fork Node** — UML construct that splits a single flow into multiple parallel flows.
- **Join Node** — UML construct that synchronizes multiple parallel flows into one.
- **Guard Condition** — Boolean expression that enables/disables a transition.
- **Signal** — Asynchronous stimulus sent between objects.
- **Mute State** — A wait state where the object remains idle until a triggering event arrives.
- **Thread-Safe** — Property of an object guaranteeing correct behavior under concurrent access.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Why Global Resources Are a Modeling Challenge

In a purely sequential OO system, the **single thread of control** makes resource access trivial — one method call at a time. However, in **reactive and concurrent systems** (e.g., ATM networks, embedded controllers, GUI event loops), the system must:

1. **React** to multiple external stimuli **simultaneously**.
2. **Share finite hardware/software resources** without corruption.
3. **Maintain deterministic behavior** regardless of input ordering.

UML addresses this through **three orthogonal mechanisms**:

| Mechanism | Applied In | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Concurrent Regions | State Machine Diagram | Independent event handling within a single classifier |
| Fork/Join | Activity Diagram | Parallel execution and resynchronization of activities |
| Guarded Transitions + Signals | Both | Conditional access & asynchronous notification |

---

## 2.2 The Three Architectural Patterns for Global Resources

### 🔹 Pattern A: **Resource Manager (Mediator Pattern Variant)**

A dedicated object owns the global resource and mediates access.

- Other objects request the resource via `acquire()`.
- Manager either grants access or queues the request.
- On `release()`, manager grants access to the next requester.

> **Used in:** Database connection pools, thread pools, memory allocators.

### 🔹 Pattern B: **Guarded Region with Mute States**

The state machine *refuses* to enter a "resource-using" state until a signal arrives.

- Transitions carry **guard conditions**.
- The system stays in a **mute (wait) state** until a `resourceAvailable` signal is received.
- On entry → resource acquired. On exit → resource released.

> **Used in:** Printer spoolers, finite hardware buffer systems.

### 🔹 Pattern C: **Fork-Join with Synchronization Bar**

In activity diagrams, parallel activities execute and then synchronize.

- **Fork** splits a single flow into N concurrent flows.
- **Join** ensures all N flows complete before resuming.
- The **join node** is a synchronization barrier.

> **Used in:** Workflow systems, transaction processing, real-time control loops.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet 📝

| # | Concept | UML Notation | Keyword | Exam Tip |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Fork | Thick black bar (1-in, N-out) | `«fork»` | Splits ONE flow into MANY |
| 2 | Join | Thick black bar (N-in, 1-out) | `«join»` | Synchronizes MANY into ONE |
| 3 | Decision | Diamond (1-in, N-out) | `[guard]` | Choice based on condition |
| 4 | Merge | Diamond (N-in, 1-out) | — | Unifies alternative paths |
| 5 | Concurrent Region | Dashed line dividing state | `(name)` | Parallel sub-states inside one state |
| 6 | Entry Action | `entry / action` | — | Executes on entering the state |
| 7 | Exit Action | `exit / action` | — | Executes on leaving the state |
| 8 | Signal Receive | `sigName` (in transition) | — | Asynchronous trigger |
| 9 | Mute State | State with no outgoing transitions (except self) | — | Waiting state |
| 10 | Guard | `[condition]` | Boolean | Enables a transition only if true |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Rule:** In a state machine with **concurrent regions**, the system is in *all* the substates (one per region) **simultaneously**. A transition out of an orthogonal state fires when **all regions** have reached their respective final states — *unless* the transition is associated with a single region.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Embedded Systems (Automotive ECUs)** | CAN bus arbitration — multiple ECUs competing for bus access |
| **Telecommunications** | GSM/4G/5G channel allocation to multiple users |
| **Web Backends** | Database connection pool management (e.g., HikariCP, c3p0) |
| **Operating Systems** | Semaphores, mutexes, condition variables |
| **Industrial Control (SCADA)** | PLC resource sharing on Modbus/Profibus |
| **GUI Frameworks** | Event dispatch thread vs. worker thread access to UI components |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Framework

## 3.1 Worked Example — The "Shared Database Connection Pool" Scenario

> **Problem Statement (KTU-style):** A banking server has **3 client request handlers** (withdraw, deposit, balance-inquiry) and a **pool of 2 database connections**. Model the global resource handling using a UML **Activity Diagram** with fork/join and a UML **State Machine Diagram** with concurrent regions.

---

### 🔸 Step 1: Identify the Global Resource

$$\text{GlobalResource} = \text{DBConnectionPool}_{\text{size}=2}$$

The connection pool is the **single, finite, shared asset**.

> *Textual explanation:* The pool is global because all three request handlers compete for it. It is finite because only 2 connections exist. It is shared because simultaneous requests arrive.

---

### 🔸 Step 2: Apply the Fork-Join Pattern (Activity View)

The three request handlers can be modeled as **concurrent activities** spawned by a fork node.

$$\text{Fork} \rightarrow \{A_{\text{withdraw}}, \, A_{\text{deposit}}, \, A_{\text{balance}}\} \rightarrow \text{Join}$$

> *Textual explanation:* A single "client request" event enters the system, is split by the **fork** into three parallel executable flows, and the system continues only when all three reach the **join** barrier.

---

### 🔸 Step 3: Guard the Resource Access

Each activity has a guarded transition:

$$A_{\text{withdraw}} : \text{availableConns} > 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{acquire}()$$
$$A_{\text{withdraw}} : \text{availableConns} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{waitForSignal}(\text{connReleased})$$

> *Textual explanation:* If the guard `availableConns > 0` evaluates to true, the transition fires and the handler acquires a connection. If false, the handler enters a **mute state** and waits for the signal `connReleased`.

---

### 🔸 Step 4: State Machine Modeling (with Concurrent Regions)

The `DBConnection` class state machine:

$$\text{States} = \{\text{Idle}, \text{Allocated}, \text{Active}, \text{Releasing}\}$$

Transitions:
$$\text{Idle} \xrightarrow{\text{allocate / lock}()} \text{Allocated}$$
$$\text{Allocated} \xrightarrow{\text{executeQuery}() / \cdot} \text{Active}$$
$$\text{Active} \xrightarrow{\text{closeResult}() / \text{returnToPool}()} \text{Idle}$$

> *Textual explanation:* The `allocate` trigger acquires the resource. The `executeQuery` transition uses the resource. The `closeResult` transition releases it, and the `exit` action returns the connection to the pool.

---

### 🔸 Step 5: Resource Pool State Machine (with Concurrent Regions)

The pool itself has **two orthogonal regions**:
- **Region R1 (Allocated Counter):** Maintains `allocatedCount`.
- **Region R2 (Free List):** Maintains the queue of free connections.

$$\text{Pool} = R_1 \parallel R_2$$

Allocated count transition:
$$R_1 : \text{Allocated}_{n} \xrightarrow{\text{allocate}} \text{Allocated}_{n+1}, \quad n < 2$$
$$R_1 : \text{Allocated}_{2} \xrightarrow{\text{allocate} / \text{signal}(\text{connReleased})} \text{Allocated}_{2}$$

> *Textual explanation:* Region R1 increments its sub-state only if the current count is less than 2 (the pool's capacity). If full, it loops and emits a signal that wakes up a waiting requester when a connection is later released.

---

### 🔸 Step 6: Synchronization at the Join Node

$$N_{\text{join}} = f(t_{\text{withdraw}},\, t_{\text{deposit}},\, t_{\text{balance}})$$

$$t_{\text{system}} = \max(t_{\text{withdraw}},\, t_{\text{deposit}},\, t_{\text{balance}})$$

> *Textual explanation:* The join node blocks the system response until the **maximum** of the three activity completion times has elapsed. This is the synchronization barrier that ensures all three handlers have completed before the system reports a unified result to the client.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Selection Guide for Students

| Scenario Detected | Best UML Construct | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Multiple parallel activities from one event | **Fork / Join** | Native parallel flow |
| State must wait for external trigger | **Mute state + signal** | Asynchronous event handling |
| Mutually exclusive access to one resource | **Guarded transition** | Single point of control |
| Two independent behaviors in one object | **Concurrent regions** | Orthogonal state decomposition |
| Object needs to track multiple phases simultaneously | **Synchronization bar** | Multi-phase coordination |
| Continuous timer-based behavior | **After(deadline)** transition | Time-triggered |
| Pool-style resource sharing | **Resource Manager** + signal | Decouples users from resource |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics (Mermaid)

## 4.1 Activity Diagram with Fork/Join (Database Connection Pool)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Client Request]) --> F{{"<<fork>>"}}
    F --> B[Withdraw Handler]
    F --> C[Deposit Handler]
    F --> D[Balance Handler]
    B --> G1{availableConns > 0}
    G1 -- Yes --> B1[Acquire DB Conn]
    G1 -- No --> B2[(Wait for connReleased signal)]
    B2 -.->|connReleased| G1
    B1 --> B3[Execute SQL]
    B3 --> B4[Release DB Conn]
    B4 --> H1((end1))
    C --> G2{availableConns > 0}
    G2 -- Yes --> C1[Acquire DB Conn]
    G2 -- No --> C2[(Wait state)]
    C1 --> C3[Execute SQL]
    C3 --> C4[Release DB Conn]
    C4 --> H2((end2))
    D --> G3{availableConns > 0}
    G3 -- Yes --> D1[Acquire DB Conn]
    G3 -- No --> D2[(Wait state)]
    D1 --> D3[Execute SQL]
    D3 --> D4[Release DB Conn]
    D4 --> H3((end3))
    H1 --> J{{"<<join>>"}}
    H2 --> J
    H3 --> J
    J --> K([Unified Response])
```

---

## 4.2 State Machine with Concurrent Regions (Printer Resource)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Active
    state Active {
        [*] --> Init
        state RegionControl {
            direction LR
            Idle1 --> WaitForPaper: printJob received
            WaitForPaper --> Printing: paperAvailable
            Printing --> Idle1: exit / release()
        }
        state RegionStatus {
            direction LR
            Ready --> Busy
            Busy --> Ready
        }
    }
    state Mute {
        Waiting --> Waiting: printQueueEmpty [not empty]
    }
    Active --> Mute: shutdown()
    Mute --> [*]
```

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Architecture — Resource Manager Pattern

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Clients
        C1[Client 1]
        C2[Client 2]
        C3[Client 3]
    end
    subgraph ResourceManager
        RM[Manager / Pool]
        Q[Wait Queue]
        C1 -- acquire --> RM
        C2 -- acquire --> RM
        C3 -- acquire --> RM
        RM -->|if full| Q
        Q -.->|wakeup signal| RM
    end
    subgraph GlobalResource
        R1[Resource Instance 1]
        R2[Resource Instance 2]
        RM -- dispatch --> R1
        RM -- dispatch --> R2
        R1 -.->|release| RM
        R2 -.->|release| RM
    end
```

---

## 4.4 Sequence of Resource Lifecycle

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Client Thread
    participant M as Resource Manager
    participant R as Global Resource
    C->>M: acquire()
    alt resource available
        M->>R: lock()
        R-->>M: granted
        M-->>C: handle returned
        C->>R: perform operation
        C->>M: release()
        M->>R: unlock()
        M-->>C: confirmed
    else resource busy
        M-->>C: queued (wait)
        Note over C,M: Client enters mute state
        M->>C: signal connReleased
        C->>M: acquire() (retry)
        M->>R: lock()
        R-->>M: granted
        M-->>C: handle returned
    end
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]
**Differentiate between a Fork node and a Join node in a UML Activity Diagram. Illustrate with a suitable banking transaction example.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Fork node** is a control node in UML Activity Diagrams that splits a single incoming flow into **multiple concurrent outgoing flows**. Each outgoing flow executes **independently and in parallel**. It is drawn as a **thick horizontal or vertical bar**.

A **Join node** is the converse: it has **multiple incoming flows** and **one outgoing flow**. The outgoing flow does not execute until **all incoming flows have arrived** — making it a **synchronization barrier**. It is also drawn as a thick bar.

**Banking Example:** When a customer initiates a "Money Transfer" transaction, a Fork splits the flow into:
- Flow A: Debit source account
- Flow B: Credit destination account
- Flow C: Generate audit log

A Join node blocks the transaction's "Transaction Complete" notification until A, B, and C have all completed — guaranteeing **atomicity**.

> [!NOTE]
> **3-mark structure expected:** Definition (1) + Distinction table or comparison (1) + Example (1).

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Explain the concept of "Mute States" with reference to global resource handling in UML State Machine Diagrams.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Mute state** (also called a **wait state**) is a state in which an object **remains idle** and does not process any events other than the specific signal it is waiting for. It is used when an object needs a **global resource that is currently unavailable**.

**Mechanism:**
1. The object is in an active state and requires a shared resource.
2. The **guard condition** on the transition to the resource-using state evaluates to `false` (resource busy).
3. The object transitions to a **mute state** named, e.g., `WaitingForResource`.
4. The mute state has a **self-transition** with a trigger `resourceReleased` and a guard `[isAvailable == true]`.
5. When another object releases the resource and sends the signal, the mute state fires its outgoing transition, and the object proceeds to use the resource.

**Use Case:** ATM printer — when the printer is busy, the ATM screen enters a mute state "Please wait, printing in progress" and only transitions out when a `printerReady` signal is received.

---

## 📝 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### 📌 QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]

**a)** *With a neat UML State Machine Diagram using concurrent regions, explain how a "Network Router" handles the global resource "Bandwidth" shared among three traffic classes: VoIP, Video, and Data.* **(7 marks)**

**b)** *Design a UML Activity Diagram using Fork-Join for an "E-Commerce Order Processing" system where inventory check, payment verification, and shipment scheduling execute concurrently and synchronize before order confirmation.* **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1: Identify Global Resource**
$$\text{GlobalResource} = \text{Bandwidth}_{\max}, \quad \text{Shared by } \{VoIP, Video, Data\}$$

**Step 2: Define Orthogonal Regions**

The Router's state machine contains **three concurrent regions**, one per traffic class:

$$R_{\text{Router}} = R_{\text{VoIP}} \parallel R_{\text{Video}} \parallel R_{\text{Data}}$$

**Step 3: State Definitions for Each Region**

| Region | States | Trigger to "Active" | Trigger to "Idle" |
|---|---|---|---|
| R_VoIP | `Idle → Negotiating → Transmitting` | `voicePacket` | `transmissionComplete` |
| R_Video | `Idle → Buffering → Streaming` | `videoFrame` | `streamPaused` |
| R_Data | `Idle → Acknowledging → Downloading` | `dataRequest` | `downloadComplete` |

**Step 4: Guard Conditions on Bandwidth**

Each region has a guard: `[availableBandwidth >= minClassRequirement]`
- VoIP requires ≥ 64 kbps
- Video requires ≥ 500 kbps
- Data requires ≥ 100 kbps (uses leftover)

**Step 5: Mute States for Resource Contention**

When `[availableBandwidth < minClassRequirement]`, the region transitions to a mute state `WaitingForBandwidth` and stays there until a `bandwidthReleased` signal arrives.

**Step 6: Exit Actions Release Bandwidth**

$$\text{exit } R_{\text{VoIP}} \rightarrow \text{returnBandwidth}(\text{amount}=64\text{kbps})$$

> *Self-verification:* Total bandwidth = 1 Mbps. If VoIP=64kbps + Video=500kbps + Data=100kbps = 664kbps, the remaining 336kbps is reserved for burst handling.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1: Activity Flow Design**

$$\text{OrderReceived} \rightarrow \text{Fork} \rightarrow \{A_1, A_2, A_3\} \rightarrow \text{Join} \rightarrow \text{ConfirmOrder}$$

**Step 2: Define Parallel Activities**

- **A₁ (Inventory Check):** `SELECT stock FROM inventory WHERE SKU = X` → returns `inStock: Boolean`
- **A₂ (Payment Verification):** `POST /payment/verify` → returns `paymentStatus: Boolean`
- **A₃ (Shipment Scheduling):** `GET /courier/slots?pincode=Y` → returns `slot: Date`

**Step 3: Join Synchronization Logic**

$$\text{Join}_{\text{fire}} \iff \big( A_1.\text{complete} \land A_2.\text{complete} \land A_3.\text{complete} \big)$$

$$\text{TimeToConfirm} = \max(t_{A_1},\, t_{A_2},\, t_{A_3})$$

**Step 4: Decision after Join**

$$\text{Decision} = \begin{cases} \text{ConfirmOrder} & \text{if } A_1.\text{ok} \land A_2.\text{ok} \\ \text{RollbackTransaction} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 5: Resource Implications**

The **global resources** involved:
- **Inventory DB connection** (used by A₁)
- **Payment Gateway API** (rate-limited — used by A₂)
- **Courier Service API** (used by A₃)

Each parallel activity acquires its resource independently via a connection pool, and releases it on activity completion — well before the Join node fires.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall (Examiner's Trap):** Students often draw the **Join node BEFORE** all three activities finish (incorrectly firing on the first arrival). The **correct** semantics of a Join node is **AND-join** — it waits for *all* incoming tokens. If you want a single-trigger join, you must use the **`«joinSpec»` discriminator** — and that loses the strict synchronization guarantee. KTU specifically tests whether you use the standard AND-join.

---

### 📌 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]

**a)** *Compare and contrast the three strategies for handling global resources — Resource Manager, Guarded Region, and Fork-Join — using a "Hospital ICU" scenario where multiple patients compete for ventilators and monitoring devices.* **(7 marks)**

**b)** *Explain with a UML State Machine Diagram how a "Semaphore" object enforces mutual exclusion for a global resource shared by N threads. Show the wait and signal operations explicitly.* **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a)

**Comparison Table (7 marks expected — populate all 3 strategies):**

| Aspect | Resource Manager | Guarded Region | Fork-Join |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Centralization** | Centralized (single owner) | Decentralized (guards) | Decentralized (barrier) |
| **Fairness** | High (FIFO queue) | Low (no queue) | N/A (parallelism) |
| **Throughput** | Medium | High | Very High |
| **Deadlock Risk** | Low (mediator monitors) | Medium | Low |
| **Scalability** | Limited by manager | High | High |
| **UML Notation** | Class diagram with manager | State machine with guards | Activity with fork/join |
| **ICU Use Case** | Ventilator Pool Manager | Ventilator acquisition guard | Parallel patient monitoring |

**ICU Mapping:**
- **Ventilators (3 available)**: Managed by `VentilatorPool` object (Resource Manager strategy).
- **Monitors (10 available)**: Acquired by each patient's state machine via guard `[monitorsAvailable > 0]` (Guarded Region strategy).
- **Patient Vitals Check**: SpO₂, ECG, BP checked **in parallel** (Fork-Join strategy) before nurse notification.

> *Self-verification:* This is a multi-strategy design — real ICU systems combine all three. KTU expects students to recognize that **one diagram is rarely sufficient** for global resources.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b)

**Semaphore State Machine:**

The `Semaphore` class has:
- **Attribute:** `permits: Integer`
- **Operations:** `wait()`, `signal()`

**States and Transitions:**

$$\text{Semaphore} : \{\text{Initialized}\} \parallel \{\text{ReleasedPermits}_n\}, \quad n \geq 0$$

Transition definitions:
$$\text{ReleasedPermits}_{n} \xrightarrow{\text{wait}() \;/\; \text{permits}--} \text{ReleasedPermits}_{n-1}, \quad n > 0$$
$$\text{ReleasedPermits}_{n} \xrightarrow{\text{wait}() \;/\; \text{blockThread}} \text{Blocked}$$
$$\text{Blocked} \xrightarrow{\text{signal}() \;/\; \text{unblockThread}} \text{ReleasedPermits}_{1}$$
$$\text{ReleasedPermits}_{n} \xrightarrow{\text{signal}() \;/\; \text{permits}++} \text{ReleasedPermits}_{n+1}, \quad n < \text{MAX}$$

**Mutual Exclusion Proof Sketch:**

For a **binary semaphore** ($\text{permits} \in \{0,1\}$):
- Only one thread can transition from `ReleasedPermits₁ → ReleasedPermits₀` because the `wait()` operation is **atomic**.
- Other threads attempting `wait()` see $\text{permits}=0$ and enter `Blocked`.
- $\therefore$ **At most one thread** can hold the resource at a time — this is **mutual exclusion**.

**Deadlock Avoidance Condition:**

$$\text{NoHoldAndWait} \iff \forall T_i: \text{acquireAll()} \; \text{or} \; \text{acquireNone}()$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall (Examiner's Trap):** Students frequently confuse **`wait()`/`signal()`** in semaphores with **`wait()`/`notify()`** in Java. The UML model is **language-agnostic**. KTU's UML 2.x specification uses `wait` and `signal` purely as **named operations** — not as Java methods. Do **not** write Java code in your answer; use UML operation notation `«operation» wait() : void`.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist (KTU Module 2 — Handling of Global Resources)**

- ✅ **Global resources** = shared, finite, system-wide assets accessed by multiple concurrent entities.
- ✅ **Three strategies:** Resource Manager, Guarded Region, Fork-Join — choose based on contention pattern.
- ✅ **Fork** = 1 incoming, N outgoing. **Join** = N incoming, 1 outgoing.
- ✅ **Join** is an **AND-barrier** — fires only when *all* incoming tokens have arrived.
- ✅ **Concurrent regions** in a state machine are separated by a **dashed line**.
- ✅ **Orthogonal substates** = system is in **all** regions simultaneously.
- ✅ **Entry actions** acquire resources; **Exit actions** release them.
- ✅ **Mute states** = idle wait states that only react to specific signals.
- ✅ **Guards** = `[BooleanExpression]` on transitions; no parentheses, no side-effects.
- ✅ **Signals** are **asynchronous** triggers (not method calls).
- ✅ **Race conditions** = the #1 design defect caused by poor global resource handling.
- ✅ **Mutex via Semaphore**: $\text{permits} = 1$ → binary semaphore → mutual exclusion.
- ✅ **For KTU diagrams**: Use **thick black bars** for fork/join, **diamonds** for decision/merge, **dashed lines** for concurrent region boundaries.
- ✅ **Exam trap**: Don't confuse UML `signal()` (operation) with Java `signal()` (method). They are NOT the same.
- ✅ **Exam trap**: A Join node that fires on the *first* token is incorrect — that requires a `«joinSpec»` or a Decision/Merge, NOT a Join.

> 🎯 **Golden Rule for KTU 14-mark answers:** Always provide (1) the diagram with proper notation, (2) the textual explanation of each transition, (3) the design rationale (why this strategy?), and (4) at least one real-world mapping. Answers missing any one of these four elements typically lose 2–3 marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Common Architectural Framework
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCNT604 - OBJECT ORIENTED MODELING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module - 2
> - **Topic:** Common Architectural Framework

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common Architectural Framework** refers to a standardized, multi-perspective approach to describing and documenting the architecture of a software-intensive system.
