In the context of Object-Oriented Modeling and Design (OOMD), the most widely accepted common architectural framework is the **4+1 View Model** proposed by **Philippe Kruchten (1995)**, which decomposes software architecture into **five concurrent views**, each addressing a specific stakeholder concern.

The framework provides a **common vocabulary**, **consistent set of viewpoints**, and a **unified method** to bridge the gap between end-users, developers, and system engineers. It is a **view-based architectural description** that captures both **functional** and **non-functional** requirements of a system.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Students must be able to describe the **4+1 View Model**, the **purpose of each view**, the **stakeholders** served, and how the **scenarios (use cases)** tie all views together. This is a **high-weightage topic** in KTU ESE (End Semester Examination).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **designing a new house**. No single drawing tells the complete story:
- The **owner** wants to see the layout of rooms (functional design).
- The **plumber** needs pipe layouts (process/fluid flow).
- The **electrician** needs wiring diagrams (physical infrastructure).
- The **construction company** needs material lists and phasing (development/build).
- The **final walkthrough scenario** ("what happens when we cook dinner?") validates all of these.

A **single blueprint cannot satisfy everyone**. The **Common Architectural Framework (4+1 View Model)** applies the **exact same idea** to software systems — different stakeholders need **different views of the same system**, and all views are unified by a central set of **scenarios (use cases)**.

### 1.3 Why a Common Framework?

| Problem with Ad-hoc Architecture | Solution via Common Framework |
|---|---|
| Ambiguous communication between stakeholders | **Standardized viewpoints** |
| Architecture is "in the head" of one architect | **Documented, multi-view** representation |
| Functional vs non-functional concerns mixed | **Separation of concerns** across views |
| No traceability from requirements to code | **Scenarios (+1 view)** provide the glue |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference:** The 4+1 View Model is also formally aligned with **IEEE Std 1471-2000** (Recommended Practice for Architectural Description), which KTU examiners frequently cite in model answers.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The 4+1 View Model — Detailed Breakdown

The framework organizes architecture into **five concurrent views**, where four are primary and one ("+1") acts as the **integration viewpoint**.

---

#### 2.1.1 Logical View
- **Stakeholder:** End-users, domain experts, analysts, designers.
- **Primary Concern:** **Functional requirements** — *what the system should provide to its users*.
- **Key UML Diagrams Used:** Class Diagram, State Diagram, Object Diagram.
- **Purpose:** Captures the system's **vocabulary** (classes, objects) and the **collaborations** that deliver the required functionality.
- **Analogy:** The **room layout** of a house — bedrooms, kitchen, hall and how they connect.
- **Output:** A logical schema showing the **abstractions** of the system.

#### 2.1.2 Process View
- **Stakeholder:** Integrators, performance engineers.
- **Primary Concern:** **Non-functional requirements** — concurrency, performance, scalability, throughput, fault tolerance.
- **Key UML Diagrams Used:** Activity Diagram, Sequence Diagram (process-level), State Diagram.
- **Purpose:** Captures the **runtime behavior**, **process decomposition**, and **process interactions** (processes = lightweight threads or heavyweight processes).
- **Analogy:** The **HVAC and plumbing flow** of a house — how water/heat is distributed concurrently.
- **Key Properties Addressed:** Deadlock, starvation, race conditions, load balancing.

#### 2.1.3 Physical View (Deployment View)
- **Stakeholder:** System engineers, network engineers, operations team.
- **Primary Concern:** **Mapping of software to hardware** — distribution, communication, installation.
- **Key UML Diagrams Used:** **Deployment Diagram** (primary).
- **Purpose:** Describes the **physical topology** — servers, processors, network links — and the mapping of processes (from Process View) onto nodes.
- **Analogy:** The **foundation plan + utility connections** of a house.
- **Output:** A deployment schema showing hardware nodes and the artifacts running on them.

#### 2.1.4 Development View
- **Stakeholder:** Developers, project managers, configuration managers.
- **Primary Concern:** **Software module organization** — management of the software in development, reuse, team coordination.
- **Key UML Diagrams Used:** **Package Diagram**, Component Diagram.
- **Purpose:** Shows the **static organization** of the software in the development environment (subsystems, libraries, modules, packages).
- **Analogy:** The **construction plan** — how materials are organized on-site, which sub-team builds which wing.
- **Key Properties Addressed:** Reusability, portability, ease of development, code modularity.

#### 2.1.5 Scenarios View (+1 View) — The Integrating View
- **Stakeholder:** All stakeholders, especially end-users.
- **Primary Concern:** **Validation of architecture** — shows how the four views work together to satisfy use cases.
- **Key UML Diagrams Used:** **Use Case Diagram**, Sequence/Collaboration Diagrams.
- **Purpose:** Acts as the **driver** to discover architectural elements; verifies that the architecture supports the key use cases.
- **Analogy:** The **walkthrough scenario** — "what happens when the family sits down for dinner?" — checks that all systems integrate correctly.
- **Why "+1"?** It is **not a separate view in the traditional sense** but a **set of use cases** that bind the four primary views together.

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| View | Stakeholder | UML Artifact | Primary Concern | KTU Buzzword |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Logical** | End-User, Analyst | Class, State, Object | Functional requirements | **Object Model** |
| **Process** | Integrator | Activity, Sequence | Non-functional (runtime) | **Process Model** |
| **Physical** | System Engineer | Deployment | Hardware-to-software mapping | **Deployment Model** |
| **Development** | Developer, PM | Package, Component | Software module organization | **Module/Implementation Model** |
| **Scenarios (+1)** | All | Use Case, Sequence | Integration, validation | **Use-Case Model** |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Common Architectural Framework is used in industries such as:
- **Aerospace (DO-178C compliance):** Mapping avionics software to multiple views for safety certification.
- **Automotive (AUTOSAR):** Multi-view architecture for ECU software.
- **Banking & FinTech:** Separate logical, process, and deployment views to satisfy auditors.
- **Telecommunications (TMF Frameworx):** eTOM uses a 4+1-like multi-view modeling approach.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Recall Trigger:** If a question asks *"Which view addresses the runtime behavior of the system?"* — the answer is **Process View**. *"Which view shows the mapping of software to hardware?"* — the answer is **Physical/Deployment View**. Memorize the stakeholder ↔ view ↔ diagram mapping.

### 2.4 Mapping Views to UML Diagrams (Summary Table)

$$\text{Architecture} = f(\text{Logical}, \text{Process}, \text{Physical}, \text{Development}, \text{Scenarios})$$

Where each $f_i$ corresponds to a different abstraction of the same underlying system $S$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Architectural Model (Conceptual Workflow)

Since this topic is **architectural and design-oriented** (not purely numerical), the "derivation" here is the **logical process of building a complete 4+1 architectural description** from a given set of requirements.

**Step 1: Capture Requirements as Scenarios**
$$\text{Scenarios} = \{\text{Use Case}_1, \text{Use Case}_2, \ldots, \text{Use Case}_n\}$$

These are drawn directly from end-user requirements. Each scenario describes an externally observable interaction with the system.

**Step 2: Develop the Logical View**
From scenarios, identify the **classes**, their **attributes**, **operations**, and **relationships**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Logical View} &= \{C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_k\} \\
\text{where } C_i &= (\text{attributes}_i, \text{operations}_i, \text{relationships}_i)
\end{aligned}
$$

For example, in an **ATM system**, scenarios like "Withdraw Cash" lead to classes: `Account`, `ATM`, `Customer`, `Transaction`, `CashDispenser`.

**Step 3: Develop the Process View**
Decompose the system into **concurrent processes** and define their communication (message-passing or shared memory).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Process View} &= \{P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_m\} \\
\text{Communication} &= \{(P_i, P_j, \text{Message}_{ij})\}
\end{aligned}
$$

For the ATM: `AuthenticationProcess`, `TransactionProcess`, `DatabaseSyncProcess` — all running concurrently, communicating via messages.

**Step 4: Develop the Physical (Deployment) View**
Map each process $P_i$ onto a physical node $N_j$:

$$
\text{map} : P_i \rightarrow N_j
$$

For the ATM: `TransactionProcess` runs on the **ATM's embedded computer**; `DatabaseSyncProcess` runs on the **Bank's main server**.

**Step 5: Develop the Development View**
Organize the software into **modules/packages**, assigning each to a development team:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Development View} &= \{\text{Pkg}_{\text{UI}}, \text{Pkg}_{\text{BusinessLogic}}, \text{Pkg}_{\text{DataAccess}}, \text{Pkg}_{\text{ExternalAPIs}}\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Iterate and Validate**
Use the **scenarios (+1)** to validate that all four views work together:

$$
\text{Validate}(\text{Logical}, \text{Process}, \text{Physical}, \text{Development}) \xrightarrow{\text{Scenarios}} \text{True/False}
$$

If validation fails, **return to the relevant view** and refine.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Stakeholder vs. View Cross-Reference

| Stakeholder Type | Primary View | Secondary View | Concern |
|---|---|---|---|
| **End-User** | Scenarios (+1) | Logical | Functionality, usability |
| **Customer (Buyer)** | Scenarios (+1), Logical | Physical | Cost, features, hardware |
| **System Architect** | All 5 views | — | Overall coherence |
| **Software Developer** | Development | Logical | Code reuse, modules |
| **System Engineer** | Physical (Deployment) | Process | Hardware mapping |
| **Performance Engineer** | Process | Physical | Throughput, latency |
| **Project Manager** | Development | Scenarios (+1) | Team coordination, schedule |
| **Quality / Test Engineer** | Scenarios (+1) | Logical, Process | Test cases, validation |

### 3.3 Architectural Style Selection (Decision Logic)

When applying the 4+1 framework, the architect must select an **architectural style** that satisfies non-functional requirements. The decision logic is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{If } &\text{system requires high availability} \Rightarrow \text{Distributed Architecture} \\
\text{If } &\text{system requires data integrity} \Rightarrow \text{Layered Architecture} \\
\text{If } &\text{system requires high responsiveness} \Rightarrow \text{Event-Driven Architecture} \\
\text{If } &\text{system requires extensibility} \Rightarrow \text{Microservices / Component-Based}
\end{aligned}
$$

This decision is documented in the **Process View** and validated via **scenarios**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The 4+1 View Model — Master Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph S1["Scenarios +1 View"]
        UC1["Use Case Diagram"]
        SQ1["Sequence Diagram"]
    end

    subgraph L1["Logical View"]
        CL1["Class Diagram"]
        ST1["State Diagram"]
    end

    subgraph PR1["Process View"]
        AC1["Activity Diagram"]
        SQ2["Sequence Process Level"]
    end

    subgraph PH1["Physical Deployment View"]
        DP1["Deployment Diagram"]
        ND1["Nodes and Artifacts"]
    end

    subgraph DV1["Development View"]
        PK1["Package Diagram"]
        CP1["Component Diagram"]
    end

    UC1 -. "drives" .-> CL1
    UC1 -. "drives" .-> AC1
    UC1 -. "drives" .-> DP1
    UC1 -. "drives" .-> PK1

    CL1 -. "validated by" .-> SQ1
    AC1 -. "validated by" .-> SQ1
    DP1 -. "validated by" .-> SQ1
    PK1 -. "validated by" .-> SQ1

    CL1 --- AC1
    AC1 --- DP1
    DP1 --- PK1
    PK1 --- CL1
```

### 4.2 Stakeholder-View Mapping Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    EU["End User"] --> SV["Scenarios Plus 1 View"]
    EU --> LV["Logical View"]

    SA["System Architect"] --> LV
    SA --> PV["Process View"]
    SA --> PDV["Physical View"]
    SA --> DV["Development View"]
    SA --> SV

    SD["Software Developer"] --> DV
    SD --> LV

    SE["System Engineer"] --> PDV
    SE --> PV

    PM["Project Manager"] --> DV
    PM --> SV

    PE["Performance Engineer"] --> PV
    PE --> PDV
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture Flow (ATM System Example)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["Customer Inserts Card"] --> A2["Authentication Process"]
    A2 --> A3["Account Validation"]
    A3 --> A4["Transaction Selection"]
    A4 --> A5["Cash Withdrawal Process"]
    A5 --> A6["Database Update Process"]
    A6 --> A7["Cash Dispensing"]
    A7 --> A8["Receipt Printing"]

    A2 -. "Logical View Class" .-> C1["Account Class"]
    A5 -. "Process View Activity" .-> P1["Transaction Process"]
    A6 -. "Physical View Node" .-> N1["Bank Server Node"]
    A4 -. "Development View Package" .-> D1["Business Logic Package"]
```

### 4.4 View-Artifact Matrix Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph V1["Logical View"]
        V1A["Class Diagram"]
        V1B["State Diagram"]
        V1C["Object Diagram"]
    end

    subgraph V2["Process View"]
        V2A["Activity Diagram"]
        V2B["Sequence Diagram"]
    end

    subgraph V3["Physical View"]
        V3A["Deployment Diagram"]
    end

    subgraph V4["Development View"]
        V4A["Package Diagram"]
        V4B["Component Diagram"]
    end

    subgraph V5["Scenarios Plus 1 View"]
        V5A["Use Case Diagram"]
        V5B["Sequence Diagram Collaboration"]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Diagram Strategy:** In the ESE, always **draw the view name at the top, then list the UML diagrams used**. Examiners award **2 marks** for correctly identifying stakeholders and **2 marks** for naming the correct UML diagrams per view.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. Define the Common Architectural Framework. List the five views of the 4+1 View Model.**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The Common Architectural Framework is a standardized, multi-perspective approach to describing software architecture. The 4+1 View Model by Philippe Kruchten (1995) consists of:
1. **Logical View** — functional requirements
2. **Process View** — non-functional / runtime behavior
3. **Physical View** — software-to-hardware mapping
4. **Development View** — software module organization
5. **Scenarios (+1) View** — use cases that integrate all other views
*(1 mark for definition + ½ mark each for the five views)*

#### **Q2. What is the role of the Scenarios (+1) View in the 4+1 Architectural Framework?**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The Scenarios (+1) View serves as the **integrating viewpoint**. It:
- Captures the system's **use cases** from end-user perspective (1 mark).
- **Drives the discovery** of architectural elements in the other four views (1 mark).
- **Validates** that the four views work coherently to deliver the expected functionality (1 mark).
It is called "+1" because it is **not a separate view** but a unifying set of use cases that bind the four primary views.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) Explain the 4+1 View Model of software architecture in detail. Describe the purpose, stakeholders, and UML diagrams used for each view.** `(7 Marks)`

**(b) Consider a "Hospital Management System" as a real-time case study. Apply the 4+1 View Model to describe how each view would be modeled for this system.** `(7 Marks)`

##### **Model Solution for (a):**

The 4+1 View Model was proposed by **Philippe Kruchten in 1995** to provide a standardized way of describing software architecture from multiple stakeholder perspectives.

**View 1 — Logical View:** Captures the **functional requirements**. Stakeholders: end-users, analysts. UML diagrams: **Class Diagram, State Diagram, Object Diagram**. It shows the system's classes, their relationships, and the collaborations delivering functionality. *(1.5 marks)*

**View 2 — Process View:** Captures the **non-functional requirements** (concurrency, performance). Stakeholders: integrators, performance engineers. UML diagrams: **Activity Diagram, Sequence Diagram**. It decomposes the system into processes and describes their interactions. *(1.5 marks)*

**View 3 — Physical (Deployment) View:** Describes the **mapping of software onto hardware**. Stakeholders: system engineers. UML diagram: **Deployment Diagram**. It shows physical nodes, their connections, and the artifacts deployed. *(1.5 marks)*

**View 4 — Development View:** Describes the **static organization of the software** in the development environment. Stakeholders: developers, project managers. UML diagrams: **Package Diagram, Component Diagram**. It shows subsystems, modules, and libraries. *(1.5 marks)*

**View 5 — Scenarios (+1) View:** A set of **use cases** that drive the discovery of architectural elements and validate the four views. UML diagrams: **Use Case Diagram, Sequence/Collaboration Diagram**. *(1 mark)*

##### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Hospital Management System — 4+1 Application:**

**Logical View (1.75 marks):**
Classes: `Patient`, `Doctor`, `Appointment`, `Prescription`, `Billing`, `Ward`, `MedicalRecord`. Relationships: `Patient` books `Appointment` with `Doctor`; `Doctor` issues `Prescription`; `Patient` is admitted to `Ward`. A class diagram is produced.

**Process View (1.75 marks):**
Concurrent processes: `AppointmentScheduler`, `BillingProcessor`, `LabReportGenerator`, `PharmacyService`. They communicate via message passing. An activity diagram shows the workflow from patient registration to discharge.

**Physical View (1.75 marks):**
Nodes: `ReceptionPC`, `DoctorTablet`, `MainHospitalServer`, `PharmacyServer`, `LabServer`. The Deployment Diagram shows artifacts (`AppointmentApp`, `BillingApp`) running on these nodes, connected via LAN.

**Development View (1.75 marks):**
Packages: `com.hms.ui`, `com.hms.bll` (business logic), `com.hms.dal` (data access), `com.hms.api`. A package diagram shows dependencies between packages and assigns them to developer teams.

**Scenarios (+1) View:** Use case *"Patient books an appointment and receives a prescription"* drives the discovery of the `Appointment`, `Prescription` classes and the `AppointmentScheduler` process.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) Discuss the importance of having a Common Architectural Framework. Compare the Logical View and the Process View in terms of their focus, stakeholders, and UML artifacts used.** `(7 Marks)`

**(b) For a real-time "Online Food Delivery System", explain how the 4+1 View Model helps in designing a scalable, reliable architecture. Justify why each view is necessary.** `(7 Marks)`

##### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Importance of a Common Architectural Framework (3.5 Marks):**
1. **Standardization:** Provides a common vocabulary across teams. *(1 mark)*
2. **Stakeholder Communication:** Different stakeholders (users, developers, managers) understand the system from their own perspective. *(1 mark)*
3. **Separation of Concerns:** Functional and non-functional requirements are addressed in separate views. *(0.5 mark)*
4. **Traceability:** Scenarios link requirements to design and implementation. *(0.5 mark)*
5. **Quality Assurance:** Validation through the +1 view ensures architectural integrity. *(0.5 mark)*

**Comparison Table (3.5 Marks):**

| Aspect | Logical View | Process View |
|---|---|---|
| **Focus** | Functional requirements | Non-functional (runtime) |
| **Stakeholder** | End-user, Analyst, Designer | Integrator, Performance Engineer |
| **UML Diagrams** | Class, State, Object | Activity, Sequence |
| **Output** | Object model | Process model |
| **Concerns** | What the system does | How it behaves at runtime |

##### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Online Food Delivery System — Why Each View?**

**Logical View (1.75 marks):**
Classes: `Customer`, `Order`, `Restaurant`, `MenuItem`, `DeliveryAgent`, `Payment`. Necessary to model the **business entities** and their relationships. The system cannot exist without a clear object model.

**Process View (1.75 marks):**
Concurrent processes: `OrderPlacementService`, `PaymentProcessor`, `DeliveryTracker`, `NotificationService`. The system must handle **hundreds of concurrent orders**; the Process View ensures scalability and fault tolerance.

**Physical View (1.75 marks):**
Nodes: `MobileApp` (customer), `WebPortal` (restaurant), `LoadBalancer`, `AppServerCluster`, `DatabaseCluster`, `MapServiceServer`. Necessary to **distribute load** and ensure **high availability**.

**Development View (1.75 marks):**
Packages: `user-service`, `order-service`, `payment-service`, `delivery-service`, `notification-service`. Necessary for **microservices-based modular development** and team coordination.

**Scenarios (+1) View:** *"Customer places an order, pays, and tracks delivery"* — this use case drives and validates the architecture.

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Process View with Physical View:** Students often describe *runtime behavior* under "Deployment" — this is wrong. **Process View = runtime behavior; Physical View = hardware mapping**. (Deducts 2–3 marks)
> 2. **Forgetting to mention the "+1" view is use-case-based:** The Scenarios View is **not a separate architectural dimension** but a set of use cases that **bind** the four views. Examiners expect this clarification. (Deducts 1 mark)
> 3. **Missing stakeholder identification:** Every view **must** have its stakeholders listed. A common KTU sub-question asks *"Who uses this view?"*
> 4. **Incorrect UML diagram mapping:** e.g., writing "Deployment Diagram" under Logical View. Memorize the **View ↔ Diagram** mapping table.
> 5. **Skipping the role of scenarios:** Many students describe the four views but **fail to explain how scenarios (+1) integrate them** — this costs the "synthesis" marks (often 2 marks in a 14-mark question).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ The **4+1 View Model** is the **de-facto common architectural framework** in OOMD — proposed by **Philippe Kruchten (1995)**.
- ✅ **Four primary views** + **one integrating view** (scenarios/use cases) = 5 total.
- ✅ **Logical View** = *what* the system does (functional). **Class Diagram** is its primary artifact.
- ✅ **Process View** = *how* the system behaves at runtime (non-functional: concurrency, performance). **Activity Diagram** is its primary artifact.
- ✅ **Physical (Deployment) View** = *where* the software runs. **Deployment Diagram** is its primary artifact.
- ✅ **Development View** = *how the code is organized* for development. **Package/Component Diagram** is its primary artifact.
- ✅ **Scenarios (+1) View** = *why* the architecture works — use cases drive and validate all four views. **Use Case Diagram** is its primary artifact.
- ✅ **Stakeholders differ per view** — end-users see Logical/Scenarios, developers see Development, system engineers see Physical.
- ✅ The framework enables **separation of concerns**, **stakeholder communication**, and **architectural validation**.
- ✅ Aligned with **IEEE 1471** standard for architectural description.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 Scheme:** Expect 3-mark direct questions and 14-mark case-study/essay questions on this topic.
- ✅ **Key recall trick:** **LPSD + 1** → **L**ogical, **P**rocess, **P**hysical, **D**evelopment, **+1 Scenarios**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Overview of Object Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCNT604 - OBJECT ORIENTED MODELING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module - 4
> - **Topic:** Overview of Object Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Object Design** is the third phase in the Unified Object-Oriented Software Engineering (OOSE) process lifecycle, positioned sequentially after *System Design* and before *Implementation*. It refines the analysis model by adding implementation-level detail, transforming the problem-space objects (domain objects) into concrete solution-space classes complete with fully specified interfaces, internal data structures, and algorithmic strategies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (Bruegge & Dutoit OOSE Framework):**
> *Object Design is the process of adding implementation details to the analysis model — including the selection of algorithms, data structures, software patterns, and the specification of interfaces — without altering the externally observable behavior of the system.*

In the KTU 2024 Scheme, Object Design is examined as a critical bridge activity that ensures the **transition from "what the system does" (Analysis) to "how the system does it" (Implementation)** is structurally sound, reusable, and maintainable.

## 1.2 Position of Object Design in the OOSE Lifecycle

The complete OOSE process follows this mandatory order:

1. **System Analysis** → Defines user requirements and use cases.
2. **System Design** → Decomposes the system into subsystems and defines the high-level architecture.
3. **Object Design** → Adds implementation detail; refines classes; selects algorithms, data structures, and patterns.
4. **Implementation** → Translates the design into executable code.
5. **Testing & Delivery** → Verifies correctness and delivers the product.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Object Design is the *only* phase where both **analysis artifacts** (use cases, class diagrams from domain model) and **system design artifacts** (subsystem architecture) converge into a single, concrete design blueprint.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "Designing the Engine Before Building the Car"

Imagine an automotive company:
- **System Analysis** is the market research — discovering that customers want a fuel-efficient sedan.
- **System Design** is the body frame, chassis layout, and engine compartment blueprint.
- **Object Design** is the engine designer's task: deciding whether to use a turbocharged inline-4, selecting the fuel-injection algorithm, choosing aluminum vs. steel pistons, and specifying the exact interface (bolt pattern, fuel line connector, exhaust flange) so the engine bolts cleanly into the chassis.
- **Implementation** is the factory floor where the engine is physically machined and assembled.

The engine designer (Object Designer) does **not** redefine what a car is (that's Analysis), nor does he/she decide the overall vehicle layout (that's System Design). Instead, he/she takes the existing blueprint and **fully specifies** every internal component, algorithm, and connection point so the engine fits perfectly when manufactured.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook:** Object Design = "Adding the *how* without breaking the *what*."

## 1.4 Primary Goals of Object Design

The four canonical goals of Object Design, as specified in the KTU-syllabus-aligned OOSE framework, are:

| Goal | Definition | Engineering Priority |
|---|---|---|
| **Correctness** | The design must satisfy all functional requirements captured during analysis. | Highest |
| **Robustness** | The design must handle invalid inputs, boundary conditions, and runtime errors gracefully. | High |
| **Extensibility** | New functionality can be added with minimal change to existing classes. | High |
| **Reusability** | Design components (classes, patterns) can be reused across projects. | Medium–High |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **A fifth implicit goal — *Understandability* — is required by KTU examiners** to ensure the design is self-documenting and easy to maintain.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference

## 2.1 The Four Categories of Object Design Decisions

During Object Design, the developer must make decisions in **four orthogonal categories**. Mastering these four categories is essential for scoring full marks in KTU 14-mark questions.

### 2.1.1 Class Selection (Reuse vs. New Class)

The developer decides whether to:
- **Reuse an existing class** from a library or framework.
- **Create a new class** tailored to the current problem.
- **Specialize an existing class** via inheritance or composition.

**Decision Criterion:** Apply the *Rule of Economic Reuse* — reuse when a class provides ≥ **70%** of the required functionality; otherwise, create or specialize.

### 2.1.2 Algorithm Selection

For each operation identified during analysis, select an efficient algorithm. Critical considerations:
- **Time Complexity** (Big-O notation, e.g., $O(n \log n)$ for sorting).
- **Space Complexity** (memory footprint).
- **Numerical Stability** (floating-point error propagation).
- **Boundary Behavior** (handling empty, single-element, or extreme inputs).

### 2.1.3 Data Structure Selection

Choose container types that match access patterns:
- **Sequential access** → Arrays, Linked Lists.
- **Random access by key** → Hash Tables, Hash Maps.
- **Ordered traversal** → Balanced BSTs, Red-Black Trees.
- **LIFO operations** → Stacks.
- **FIFO operations** → Queues, Priority Queues.

### 2.1.4 Protocol Selection

Define the **order of method invocations** for object collaboration. Protocols answer the question: *"In what order must messages be sent to satisfy a use case?"*

---

## 2.2 Object Design Activities (Bruegge-Dutoit OOSE Model)

The Object Design phase is decomposed into **four sequential activities**. KTU frequently asks for a diagrammatic or tabular representation of these.

> [!NOTE]
> **Activity Order (Strictly Sequential):**
> 1. Reuse → Select existing components.
> 2. Interface Specification → Define the full method signature and contracts.
> 3. Object Design Decomposition → Break complex classes into smaller, cohesive ones.
> 4. Design Pattern Application → Apply Gang-of-Four (GoF) patterns to solve recurring problems.

### 2.2.1 Activity 1: Reuse

Reuse is the act of identifying existing software artifacts (classes, frameworks, components) that fulfill part of the new system's requirements. Reuse can occur at multiple levels:

- **Code Reuse** — Function libraries, open-source components.
- **Design Reuse** — Reusable architectural patterns, frameworks.
- **Framework Reuse** — Spring, .NET, Django, etc.

### 2.2.2 Activity 2: Interface Specification

An interface specifies the **complete method signature** — visibility, return type, parameters, exceptions, and pre/post-conditions.

**Interface Specification Template:**

$$
\text{Interface} = \langle V, M, P, E, I_{pre}, I_{post} \rangle
$$

Where:
- $V$ = Visibility (public, private, protected).
- $M$ = Method name and return type.
- $P$ = Parameter list with types.
- $E$ = Exceptions thrown.
- $I_{pre}$ = Preconditions (must be true before invocation).
- $I_{post}$ = Postconditions (guaranteed after successful return).

### 2.2.3 Activity 3: Object Design Decomposition

Decomposition transforms monolithic, complex classes into smaller, **highly cohesive, loosely coupled** classes. The goal is to ensure that each class has a single, well-defined responsibility (adhering to the **Single Responsibility Principle**).

**Decomposition Heuristics:**
- Extract a class whenever its description contains words like "and," "or," or "manages."
- Apply the *Information Expert* GRASP pattern to assign responsibilities to the class holding the necessary data.

### 2.2.4 Activity 4: Design Pattern Application

Design patterns are **proven, reusable solutions** to recurring design problems. The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes three primary pattern categories:

| Pattern Category | Purpose | KTU-Favored Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Creational** | Object creation mechanisms | Singleton, Factory Method, Abstract Factory |
| **Structural** | Class/object composition | Adapter, Composite, Decorator, Facade |
| **Behavioral** | Object collaboration & responsibility | Observer, Strategy, State, Template Method |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula & Reference Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table consolidates all critical formulas, design rules, and reference metrics that are examinable under the "Overview of Object Design" topic in KTU 2024.**

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Notation | Unit / Scope |
|---|---|---|---|
| Object Design Position | $O_{design} = f(Analysis, SystemDesign)$ | Functional dependency | Phase ordering |
| Coupling (ideal) | $C \rightarrow 0$ | Lower is better | Module-level |
| Cohesion (ideal) | $H \rightarrow 1$ | Higher is better | Class-level |
| Reuse Threshold | $R_{threshold} = 0.70$ | Fractional | Decision metric |
| Big-O Selection | $T(n) \le O(n \log n)$ | Sorting benchmark | Algorithm-level |
| Interface Contract | $\langle V, M, P, E, I_{pre}, I_{post} \rangle$ | 6-tuple | Method-level |
| Inheritance Depth | $D_{max} \le 4$ | Avoid deep trees | Class hierarchy |
| Fan-out (method calls) | $F_{out} \le 7 \pm 2$ | Cognitive load | Class-level |
| Liskov Substitution | $\forall x \in T_{base}, x' \in T_{derived} : B(x) \rightarrow B(x')$ | Behavioral subtyping | OOP principle |
| Open/Closed Principle | $E \rightarrow C$ (Extensible but Closed for modification) | SOLID principle | Module-level |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** The Coupling–Cohesion trade-off is universally applied in embedded systems (AUTOSAR architecture), web microservices (API boundary design), and database schema design (normalized vs. denormalized forms). The Object Design phase embodies these same principles at the object level.

## 2.4 Constraints on Object Design

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The following constraints are *non-negotiable* invariants that must be preserved during Object Design. Violating them results in partial-to-full mark deductions.

1. **Preserve Analysis Model Behavior** — The object design must not change the externally observable behavior defined during analysis.
2. **Adhere to System Design Architecture** — Subsystem boundaries and inter-subsystem protocols from System Design must be honored.
3. **Minimize Coupling, Maximize Cohesion** — Always.
4. **Prefer Delegation over Inheritance** (since Bruegge-Dutoit 2nd Edition, this has been emphasized for flexibility).
5. **Document Design Decisions** — Every decision (algorithm, pattern, data structure) must be justified and traceable to a requirement.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations

## 3.1 Derivation: Interface Specification 6-Tuple Construction

> **Problem:** Construct the complete interface specification for a hypothetical `withdraw(amount)` operation of an `Account` class. Express it formally using the 6-tuple.

### Step 1: Identify the Method Name and Return Type

The operation is a withdrawal. The logical return type is a boolean indicating success or failure (or a custom `TransactionResult` enum in robust systems).

Let:
- $M = \text{withdraw} : \mathbb{R}_{+} \rightarrow \mathbb{B}$

### Step 2: Identify Parameter Types

The operation requires a single positive real-valued amount.

$$
P = \langle \text{amount} : \mathbb{R}_{+} \rangle
$$

### Step 3: Identify Visibility

Banking domain conventions require `withdraw` to be **public** (callable by external clients, ATMs, teller systems).

$$
V = \text{public}
$$

### Step 4: Identify Exceptions

The operation may fail under multiple conditions:
- Insufficient funds → `InsufficientFundsException`
- Invalid (non-positive) amount → `InvalidAmountException`
- Account frozen / inactive → `AccountInactiveException`

$$
E = \{\text{InsufficientFundsException}, \text{InvalidAmountException}, \text{AccountInactiveException}\}
$$

### Step 5: Identify Preconditions

Preconditions are conditions that the caller must guarantee *before* invoking the method.

$$
I_{pre} = \begin{cases}
\text{amount} > 0 \\
\text{account.isActive} = \text{true} \\
\text{account.pinVerified} = \text{true}
\end{cases}
$$

### Step 6: Identify Postconditions

Postconditions are conditions guaranteed by the method *after* successful completion.

$$
I_{post} = \begin{cases}
\text{account.balance}_{new} = \text{account.balance}_{old} - \text{amount} \\
\text{transactionLog.append}(\text{withdrawal record}) \\
\text{return value} = \text{true}
\end{cases}
$$

### Step 7: Assemble the 6-Tuple

$$
I_{\text{withdraw}} = \langle \text{public},\ \text{withdraw}(\mathbb{R}_{+}) \rightarrow \mathbb{B},\ \mathbb{R}_{+},\ E,\ I_{pre},\ I_{post} \rangle
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** This 6-tuple serves as a *Design by Contract* (Bertrand Meyer) specification. It allows the implementation team to write code that explicitly asserts preconditions and postconditions, dramatically reducing runtime defects. In Java, this maps to JUnit assertions; in Eiffel, to native contract clauses; in C#, to Code Contracts.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Selecting an Algorithm — Complexity Trade-off Analysis

> **Problem:** A class `SearchEngine` must retrieve records from a database of $n = 10^6$ records. Compare Linear Search, Binary Search, and Hash Table Lookup for this scenario.

### Step 1: Define the Workload

$$
W = 10^6 \text{ records}, \quad f_{query} = 10^3 \text{ queries/sec}
$$

### Step 2: Compute Time Complexity Bounds

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{linear}(n) &= O(n) = 10^6 \text{ operations/query} \\
T_{binary}(n) &= O(\log_2 n) = \log_2(10^6) \approx 20 \text{ operations/query} \\
T_{hash}(n) &= O(1)_{\text{avg}} = 1 \text{ to } 3 \text{ operations/query}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Compute Aggregate Throughput

For a 1-second window:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Linear} &: 10^3 \times 10^6 = 10^9 \text{ operations} \\
\text{Binary} &: 10^3 \times 20 = 2 \times 10^4 \text{ operations} \\
\text{Hash}   &: 10^3 \times 3 = 3 \times 10^3 \text{ operations}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Decision Matrix

| Algorithm | Time Complexity | Space Complexity | Best When | KTU Conclusion |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Linear Search | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ | $n$ is tiny, data unsorted | Reject |
| Binary Search | $O(\log n)$ | $O(1)$ | Data sorted, no updates | Acceptable if static |
| Hash Lookup | $O(1)_{\text{avg}}$ | $O(n)$ | Frequent queries, updates allowed | **Recommended** |

> [!TIP]
> **Step-by-Step Conclusion:** The hash table lookup wins by **5 orders of magnitude** for the linear case. The Object Designer should select a **Hash Map** as the data structure and a **uniform hash function** as the algorithm.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Decomposition — Applying the Single Responsibility Principle

> **Problem:** A monolithic class `CustomerDataHandler` does too much. Decompose it into smaller, cohesive classes.

### Step 1: Identify Current Responsibilities (Smell Test)

A class description containing conjunctions ("and," "or") is a smell.

**Original Description:** *"The CustomerDataHandler **validates** customer data **and persists** it to the database **and generates** monthly reports **and sends** promotional emails."*

### Step 2: Apply the Decomposition Heuristic

For each verb, extract a class:

$$
\text{CustomerDataHandler} \rightarrow
\begin{cases}
\text{CustomerValidator} : \text{validates input data} \\
\text{CustomerRepository} : \text{manages persistence} \\
\text{ReportGenerator}   : \text{produces reports} \\
\text{EmailNotifier}      : \text{sends notifications}
\end{cases}
$$

### Step 3: Verify Coupling and Cohesion

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{new} &\rightarrow 1 \quad \text{(each class has one job)} \\
C_{new} &\rightarrow \text{low} \quad \text{(interaction via interfaces only)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Impact:** This decomposition is the foundation of **Microservices Architecture** in modern cloud-native engineering. Each decomposed class becomes a candidate microservice, communicating via well-defined APIs (the interfaces we specified in §3.1).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: OOSE Lifecycle — Position of Object Design

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["System Analysis"] --> A2["System Design"]
    A2 --> A3["Object Design"]
    A3 --> A4["Implementation"]
    A4 --> A5["Testing and Delivery"]
    A3 -.Feedback.-> A2
    A3 -.Feedback.-> A1
```

**Reading Guide:** The solid arrows represent forward phase progression. The dashed arrows represent *backward traceability* — every object design artifact must be traceable back to a system design or analysis requirement.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: The Four Object Design Activities

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1["Reuse Activity"] --> B2["Interface Specification"]
    B2 --> B3["Object Design Decomposition"]
    B3 --> B4["Design Pattern Application"]
    B1 -.Parallel.- B2
    B2 -.Parallel.- B3
    B3 -.Parallel.- B4
```

**Reading Guide:** In practice, these four activities are **iterative and partially parallel**, not strictly sequential. For instance, decomposition may surface the need for an additional pattern, triggering a return to Step 4.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: The Four Categories of Design Decisions

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    C1["Object Design Phase"]
    C1 --> D1["Category 1: Class Selection"]
    C1 --> D2["Category 2: Algorithm Selection"]
    C1 --> D3["Category 3: Data Structure Selection"]
    C1 --> D4["Category 4: Protocol Selection"]
    D1 --> E1["Reuse vs Create vs Specialize"]
    D2 --> E2["Time Space Stability Tradeoff"]
    D3 --> E3["Match Data Structure to Access Pattern"]
    D4 --> E4["Define Method Invocation Order"]
```

**Reading Guide:** Each design decision category influences the others. A poor class selection may force a poor algorithm choice. The Object Designer must consider all four categories holistically.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Object Design

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    F1["Analysis Model Input: Use Cases and Class Diagram"]
    F2["System Design Input: Subsystem Architecture"]
    F1 --> G1["Object Design Engine"]
    F2 --> G1
    G1 --> G2["Reuse Subsystem"]
    G1 --> G3["Interface Subsystem"]
    G1 --> G4["Decomposition Subsystem"]
    G1 --> G5["Pattern Subsystem"]
    G2 --> H1["Refined Class Library"]
    G3 --> H2["Interface Contracts"]
    G4 --> H3["Decomposed Class Hierarchy"]
    G5 --> H4["Pattern Annotated Design"]
    H1 --> I1["Implementation Phase Input"]
    H2 --> I1
    H3 --> I1
    H4 --> I1
```

**Reading Guide:** This block diagram maps the *data flow* of the Object Design phase. Inputs from upstream phases (Analysis, System Design) feed the central design engine, which dispatches work to four parallel subsystems. Their outputs converge into a unified blueprint that drives the Implementation phase.

---

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram: Design Goal Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    J1["Object Design Goals"]
    J1 --> K1["Correctness"]
    J1 --> K2["Robustness"]
    J1 --> K3["Extensibility"]
    J1 --> K4["Reusability"]
    J1 --> K5["Understandability"]
    K1 --> L1["Satisfy All Use Cases"]
    K2 --> L2["Handle Invalid Input and Errors"]
    K3 --> L3["Support Future Modifications"]
    K4 --> L4["Reuse Across Projects"]
    K5 --> L5["Self Documenting and Readable"]
```

**Reading Guide:** Correctness is non-negotiable. The remaining four goals form a *trade-off quadrilateral* — maximizing one may reduce another, and the Object Designer must balance them based on project priorities.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

### Question 1: Define Object Design. List its four primary goals. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Object Design is the OOSE phase that adds implementation-level details (algorithms, data structures, interfaces, patterns) to the analysis model, transforming problem-domain objects into concrete solution-domain classes.

**Four Primary Goals:**
1. **Correctness** — Satisfies all analysis requirements.
2. **Robustness** — Gracefully handles invalid input and runtime errors.
3. **Extensibility** — Easy to add new functionality.
4. **Reusability** — Components can be reused in future projects.

*(Self-documenting design is an implicit fifth goal.)*

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between Reuse and Design Pattern Application. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Reuse | Design Pattern Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Use existing, pre-built components. | Apply proven, abstract solutions to recurring problems. |
| **Granularity** | Concrete classes / libraries. | Abstract templates / blueprints. |
| **Examples** | Java `ArrayList`, .NET `HttpClient`. | Singleton, Observer, Strategy. |
| **Decision** | Reuse / Create / Specialize. | Choose which pattern fits the design problem. |
| **Output** | A direct class dependency. | A pattern-annotated design structure. |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (Module Internal Choice — 14 Marks)

> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Answer **ONE** full question from the choice. Each question has sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q: Explain the four object design activities in detail. How do they iteratively refine the analysis model into an implementation-ready design? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Object Design follows System Design and precedes Implementation. It consists of four interrelated activities that transform abstract analysis artifacts into concrete implementation specifications.

**Activity 1: Reuse (1.5 marks):**
- Identify existing components (classes, frameworks, libraries) that satisfy identified requirements.
- Reuse can occur at code, design, or framework level.
- Decision rule: Reuse if the existing component provides ≥ 70% of required functionality; otherwise, create or specialize.

**Activity 2: Interface Specification (1.5 marks):**
- Define the complete method signature: visibility, return type, parameters, exceptions, preconditions, and postconditions.
- Represent formally as a 6-tuple $\langle V, M, P, E, I_{pre}, I_{post} \rangle$.
- This enables *Design by Contract* and supports parallel development by decoupling interface from implementation.

**Activity 3: Object Design Decomposition (1.5 marks):**
- Break complex classes into smaller, cohesive units.
- Apply the *Single Responsibility Principle* and *Information Expert* GRASP pattern.
- Improves maintainability, testability, and reusability.

**Activity 4: Design Pattern Application (1.5 marks):**
- Apply Gang-of-Four (GoF) patterns: Creational, Structural, Behavioral.
- Example: Use *Strategy* pattern to encapsulate interchangeable algorithms; use *Observer* to decouple subject from dependents.

**Conclusion (additional credit):**
These four activities are iterative — patterns may trigger further decomposition, which may surface new reuse opportunities. The final output is an *implementation-ready, traceable, and verifiable* design.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q: Illustrate the four categories of design decisions with a banking system example. For each category, justify the selected choice. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`**

**Model Solution:**

**Banking System Context (1 mark):**
Consider a `BankAccount` class with operations for deposit, withdrawal, and statement generation.

| Category | Decision | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Class Selection (2 marks)** | Reuse `BigDecimal` from Java standard library for monetary values. | Avoids floating-point precision errors; provides immutable arithmetic. |
| **2. Algorithm Selection (2 marks)** | Use synchronized locks for concurrent withdrawal safety. | Ensures atomicity in multi-threaded ATM access; prevents race conditions. |
| **3. Data Structure Selection (1 mark)** | Use `LinkedHashMap` to store transaction history (preserves insertion order). | Allows chronological statement generation with $O(1)$ access. |
| **4. Protocol Selection (1 mark)** | Define strict order: `verifyPin() → checkBalance() → withdraw() → logTransaction()`. | Ensures security audit trail and enforces business rules. |

**Summary Conclusion:**
The four decisions together produce a `BankAccount` class that is *correct* (satisfies requirements), *robust* (handles concurrent access), *extensible* (new transaction types can be added), and *reusable* (the class can serve multiple banking products).

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q: What are the constraints that must be preserved during Object Design? Explain each with an example. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Constraints are *invariants* that the Object Design phase must respect. Violating them invalidates the design.

**Constraint 1: Preserve Analysis Model Behavior (2 marks):**
- The external behavior visible to actors (defined in use cases) must not change.
- *Example:* If analysis specifies `withdraw()` returns a `boolean` for success/failure, the object design must not change it to throw an unchecked exception instead — that would alter the observable contract.

**Constraint 2: Adhere to System Design Architecture (2 marks):**
- Subsystem boundaries and inter-subsystem protocols from System Design must be honored.
- *Example:* If System Design places the `Authentication` subsystem in a separate tier, Object Design cannot collapse it into the `Account` class.

**Constraint 3: Minimize Coupling & Maximize Cohesion (1 mark):**
- Classes should communicate through narrow, well-defined interfaces.
- *Example:* Use the *Facade* pattern to provide a single interface to a complex subsystem.

**Constraint 4: Prefer Delegation over Inheritance (1 mark):**
- Composition is more flexible than inheritance for code reuse.
- *Example:* Use a `Logger` member inside `Account` instead of extending a `LoggableAccount` superclass.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q: Construct a complete interface specification for the operation `transferFunds(amount, targetAccount)` of an `Account` class. Use the formal 6-tuple notation. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Visibility (0.5 mark):** $V = \text{public}$

**Step 2: Method and Return (0.5 mark):** $M = \text{transferFunds} : \mathbb{R}_{+} \times \text{Account} \rightarrow \mathbb{B}$

**Step 3: Parameters (0.5 mark):** $P = \langle \text{amount} : \mathbb{R}_{+},\ \text{targetAccount} : \text{Account} \rangle$

**Step 4: Exceptions (1 mark):** $E = \{\text{InsufficientFundsException}, \text{InvalidAccountException}, \text{TransferLimitExceededException}\}$

**Step 5: Preconditions (2 marks):**

$$
I_{pre} = \begin{cases}
\text{amount} > 0 \\
\text{targetAccount} \neq \text{null} \\
\text{targetAccount.id} \neq \text{this.id} \\
\text{this.isActive} = \text{true} \\
\text{targetAccount.isActive} = \text{true}
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 6: Postconditions (2 marks):**

$$
I_{post} = \begin{cases}
\text{this.balance}_{new} = \text{this.balance}_{old} - \text{amount} \\
\text{targetAccount.balance}_{new} = \text{targetAccount.balance}_{old} + \text{amount} \\
\text{transactionLog.append}(\text{transfer record}) \\
\text{return value} = \text{true}
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 7: Final 6-Tuple (0.5 mark):**

$$
I_{\text{transfer}} = \langle \text{public},\ \text{transferFunds}(\mathbb{R}_{+}, \text{Account}) \rightarrow \mathbb{B},\ P,\ E,\ I_{pre},\ I_{post} \rangle
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Object Design Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing Analysis with Object Design** — Students often describe *what* the system does (Analysis) instead of *how* the system does it (Design). Examiners deduct up to **3 marks** for this confusion.
> 2. **Skipping the 6-tuple components** — A partial interface specification missing preconditions or postconditions loses **2 marks** per missing element.
> 3. **Listing design patterns without justifying the choice** — Naming "Singleton" is not enough; you must explain *why* it solves the stated design problem. Unjustified pattern selection loses **2–3 marks**.
> 4. **Violating the System Design boundary** — Placing logic across subsystem boundaries that were defined upstream loses **2 marks**.
> 5. **Omitting the trade-off discussion** — Algorithm and data structure choices must include time/space complexity justification. Choices without trade-off analysis lose **1.5 marks**.
> 6. **Forgetting the iterative nature** — Object Design is not strictly linear. Students who present it as a waterfall lose **1 mark** for missing the iterative refinement aspect.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- **Object Design** is the **third** OOSE phase — it adds *implementation detail* to the *analysis model* without changing observable behavior.
- **Four Goals:** Correctness, Robustness, Extensibility, Reusability (+ implicit Understandability).
- **Four Activities (in order):** Reuse → Interface Specification → Decomposition → Pattern Application. They are *iterative and partially parallel*, not strictly linear.
- **Four Decision Categories:** Class Selection, Algorithm Selection, Data Structure Selection, Protocol Selection.
- **Interface 6-Tuple:** $\langle V, M, P, E, I_{pre}, I_{post} \rangle$ — must include all six elements.
- **Coupling → 0** (low) and **Cohesion → 1** (high) are the twin design imperatives.
- **Reuse Threshold:** Use existing component if it provides ≥ 70% of required functionality.
- **Prefer Delegation over Inheritance** for flexibility and to avoid the fragile base-class problem.
- **Design Patterns:** Be ready to name and justify at least one Creational (Singleton, Factory), one Structural (Facade, Adapter), and one Behavioral (Observer, Strategy) pattern.
- **Constraints are non-negotiable:** Preserve analysis behavior, honor subsystem boundaries, document every decision.
- **Algorithm selection requires Big-O analysis** — always state time and space complexity.
- **Liskov Substitution Principle** must hold for all inheritance hierarchies.
- **Object Design output is the direct input to the Implementation phase** — every design artifact must be implementable in the target language.
- **Traceability is critical** — every design decision must be traceable back to a use case or non-functional requirement.
- **For 14-mark KTU questions:** Always include a diagrammatic representation (Mermaid/UML) to secure the full marks allocated for visual presentation.
- **Memory Hook:** "Analysis says WHAT, System Design says WHERE, Object Design says HOW, Implementation says DONE."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Combining the Three Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCNT604 - OBJECT ORIENTED MODELING AND DESIGN > - **Module:** Module - 4 > - **Topic:** Combining the Three Models ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the **Object Modeling Technique (OMT)** methodology (Rumbaugh et al.), an information system is described using **three orthogonal yet complementary perspectives**: 1. **Object Model** – Captures the *static, structural* view (classes, objects, attributes, operations, relationships like association, aggregation, inheritance). 2. **Dynamic Model** – Captures the *temporal, behavioral* view (states of objects, events that cause state transitions, and the sequence of those events). 3. **Functional Model** – Captures the *transformational, procedural* view (data flow between processes, data stores, external entities, and the constraints governing them). **Combining the Three Models** is the integration phase in which these three independent views are cross-validated, reconciled, and stitched together to yield a *complete, consistent, and unambiguous specification* of the system. The combination ensures that the structure (what the system *is*), the behavior (what the system *does over time*), and the data transformation (how inputs become outputs) are mutually coherent. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Combining the three models is a **mandatory module-end integration topic**. Examiners frequently test the *cross-verification rules* — particularly the mapping between operations, events, and processes. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are designing a **house**: - The **Object Model** is the **architectural blueprint** — walls, rooms, doors, windows (structure). - The **Dynamic Model** is the **electrical wiring and automation plan** — how a light turns ON when you flip a switch, how a door unlocks when a code is entered (state changes over time). - The **Functional Model** is the **plumbing/water-flow diagram** — how water enters, gets heated, flows to taps, and drains out (data transformation). If the plumbing says "hot water enters the kitchen tap" but the blueprint has *no kitchen tap*, the design is **inconsistent**. Combining the three models is the act of laying all three drawings on a table and asking: *Do they all agree with one another?* > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** No single model is sufficient. The Object Model tells you *what exists*, the Dynamic Model tells you *when things happen*, and the Functional Model tells you *how values are computed*. Together, they answer the question: **"What is the system, what does it do, and how does it evolve?"** ### 1.3 The Orthogonality Principle > [!NOTE] > The three models are **orthogonal views** — meaning they describe *independent dimensions* of the system. This is why each can be built semi-independently. However, **orthogonality does NOT mean isolation**; they must converge at integration time to produce a coherent system. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The "Why" Behind Combining the Models In the waterfall order of OMT, the three models are constructed in a logical sequence: 1. **Object Model first** (the skeleton — what objects exist and how they relate). 2. **Dynamic Model next** (life-cycle of *state-bearing* objects). 3. **Functional Model last** (computations that the system performs on data). Once constructed, **the combination step** is needed because: - During individual construction, each model may have been refined in isolation. - Some elements of one model may have been forgotten or duplicated in another. - Inconsistencies must be detected *before* implementation, since they are exponentially costlier to fix later. - The combination also *uncovers missing requirements* — places where the models collectively leave a gap. ### 2.2 Cross-Verification Rules (The Heart of the Topic) > [!IMPORTANT] > The combination is achieved by applying a set of **cross-verification rules**. Every element in one model must have a corresponding element in the other two models (where applicable). The mapping can be tabulated as follows: | Element in **Object Model** | Element in **Dynamic Model** | Element in **Functional Model** | Verification Check | |---|---|---|---| | **Class** | State diagram (if class has interesting states) | DFD (processes operating on instances) | Every state-bearing class has a state diagram | | **Operation of a class** | Action inside a state transition | Process in a DFD | An operation may be either a simple action (in dynamic model) or a complex computation (in functional model) | | **Event in dynamic model** | Triggers an **operation** in object model | May feed a **process** in functional model | Every event should correspond to an operation | | **Attribute** | — | Input/output of a process | Attributes appear as data flowing in DFDs | | **Association** | — | Link between data stores / processes | Associations model data paths | | **Object (instance)** | Current state of object | Data item in DFD | Every object instance is a data token | ### 2.3 The Three Mapping Categories Rumbaugh classifies the mappings between models into **three precise categories**: 1. **Same phenomenon — different models.** Example: The event *"customer places order"* is a *message* in the dynamic model, an *operation trigger* in the object model, and an *input* to a process in the functional model. 2. **Same phenomenon — different level of detail.** Example: A complex operation in the object model (e.g., `computeInvoice()`) is broken into a *series of states* in the dynamic model and a *sub-DFD* in the functional model. 3. **Different phenomena in different models (independent).** Example: A pure data structure (like a linked list) has no dynamic or functional content. Conversely, a *pure computation* (like a sorting algorithm on a transient buffer) may have minimal object-model presence. ### 2.4 Steps in Combining the Three Models The integration is performed in a **systematic, top-down sequence**: **Step 1 — Verify the Object Model.** Ensure every class has well-defined attributes, operations, and relationships. Identify classes with *interesting state* — i.e., classes whose instances change state in response to events. These become candidates for state diagrams. **Step 2 — Verify the Dynamic Model.** For each state-bearing class, build a state diagram. Verify that: - Every state transition is triggered by an event. - Every event corresponds to a valid operation on the class. - Every action in a transition is either a simple operation call or a function that will appear in the functional model. **Step 3 — Verify the Functional Model.** Construct DFDs to model the data flow. Verify that: - Every process corresponds to an operation or set of operations. - Every data store corresponds to a class or attribute. - Inputs and outputs of processes match the parameter signatures of the operations. **Step 4 — Iterate.** Inconsistencies found in one model force revisions in another. Repeat steps 1–3 until convergence. > [!NOTE] > **Convergence criterion:** A combined model is *consistent* if and only if the union of the three models contains no contradiction, no orphan operation, no unreachable state, and no unresolved data token. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility In modern software engineering, the three-model combination is the **philosophical ancestor of Model-Driven Architecture (MDA)**, where: - The **Object Model** ≈ Platform-Independent Model (PIM) structure. - The **Dynamic Model** ≈ Behavioural specifications (state machines, sequence diagrams). - The **Functional Model** ≈ Computational and constraint specifications (OCL, data transformations). The combination principle is also visible in **UML**, which unifies class diagrams, state machine diagrams, and activity/data flow views into a single coherent specification. ### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > Since this is a conceptual design topic, the "formulas" are **mapping rules and consistency checks**, not numerical equations. | # | Mapping Rule (Object → Dynamic → Functional) | Symbol / Notation | Validity Condition | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Operation of Class $C$ → Action in State Diagram of $C$ | $op(C) \equiv act(s_i \to s_j)$ | Action must appear in every transition of $C$ that invokes it | | 2 | Event $E$ → Operation call $op(obj)$ | $E \Rightarrow op(obj)$ | Every event triggers exactly one or more operations | | 3 | Process $P$ in DFD → Operation $op$ of some class | $P \equiv op$ | Process inputs/outputs match operation parameters | | 4 | Data Store $DS$ → Class or Attribute | $DS \equiv C.a$ | Persistent data is stored as class attributes | | 5 | External Entity $EE$ → Actor | $EE \equiv Actor$ | External entity is outside the system boundary | | 6 | State $s$ → Pre-condition / Post-condition | $pre(s) \Rightarrow post(s)$ | Guards must be expressible as constraints in functional model | | 7 | Attribute $a$ of Class $C$ → Data flow token | $a \in tokens(P)$ | Every attribute appears as input or output of at least one process | | 8 | Aggregation $C_1 \diamond C_2$ → Composite data store | $DS_{C_1} \supseteq DS_{C_2}$ | Whole contains parts | ### 2.7 Engineering Domains Where This Is Applied - **Embedded systems** — where state machines govern hardware behavior, object models define component structure, and functional models specify sensor data flow. - **Banking software** — accounts (object), transaction lifecycles (dynamic), interest computation (functional). - **Telecommunications** — call objects, call state machines, data routing DFDs. - **Industrial control** — controllers, sensor events, signal flow. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Detailed Derivation of the Combination Algorithm This section presents the **formal procedure** to combine the three models step-by-step. Treat it as the executable specification that an analyst would follow. --- #### **Phase 1: Preparation** **Step 1.1 — Build the canonical Object Model.** Let the set of classes be denoted as: $$\mathcal{C} = \{C_1, C_2, C_3, \dots, C_n\}$$ Each class $C_i$ has: - Attribute set $\mathcal{A}(C_i) = \{a_1, a_2, \dots, a_{p_i}\}$ - Operation set $\mathcal{O}(C_i) = \{op_1, op_2, \dots, op_{q_i}\}$ - Relationship set $\mathcal{R}(C_i)$ with other classes. **Step 1.2 — Identify state-bearing classes.** A class $C_i$ is *state-bearing* if and only if: $$\exists \; obj \in \text{Instances}(C_i), \; \text{such that} \; \text{State}(obj, t_1) \neq \text{State}(obj, t_2)$$ for some times $t_1 < t_2$. Mark such classes as $C_i^{sb}$. **Step 1.3 — Build state diagrams for state-bearing classes.** For each $C_i^{sb}$, construct a state diagram $\Sigma_i = (S_i, E_i, \delta_i, s_0^i)$ where: - $S_i$ = set of states. - $E_i$ = set of events. - $\delta_i : S_i \times E_i \to S_i$ = transition function. - $s_0^i$ = initial state. **Step 1.4 — Build the DFD hierarchy.** The functional model is a hierarchy of DFDs: $\{F_0, F_1, F_2, \dots\}$ where $F_0$ is the context diagram and $F_k$ for $k \geq 1$ are decomposed levels. Each $F_k$ contains processes $\{P_1^k, P_2^k, \dots\}$, data stores $\{D_1^k, D_2^k, \dots\}$, external entities, and data flows. --- #### **Phase 2: Cross-Verification** **Step 2.1 — Operation–Action Mapping.** For every operation $op \in \mathcal{O}(C_i)$, check whether it appears as an *action* in some state transition of $\Sigma_i$ (if $C_i$ is state-bearing), or whether it appears as a *process* in the functional model (if $op$ is a computation). Formally, the mapping operator $\mathcal{M}_{OD} : \mathcal{O} \to \text{Actions}$ is defined as: $$\mathcal{M}_{OD}(op) = \begin{cases} \text{act}(s_p \xrightarrow{E} s_q) \in \Sigma_i & \text{if } op \text{ is invoked during state change} \\ \emptyset & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$ **Step 2.2 — Event–Operation Mapping.** For every event $E \in E_i$, verify that $E$ triggers some $op \in \mathcal{O}(C_i)$: $$\forall E \in E_i, \; \exists \; op \in \mathcal{O}(C_i) \; : \; E \to op$$ **Step 2.3 — Process–Operation Mapping.** For every process $P$ in a DFD, verify that $P$ corresponds to an operation: $$\forall P \in F_k, \; \exists \; op \in \mathcal{O}(C_i) \; : \; P \equiv op$$ **Step 2.4 — Data Store–Object Mapping.** For every data store $D$ in a DFD, verify: $$\forall D, \; \exists \; C_i \in \mathcal{C} \;\text{or}\; a \in \mathcal{A}(C_i) \; : \; D \equiv C_i \;\text{or}\; D \equiv a$$ --- #### **Phase 3: Consistency Resolution** If any of the above mappings fail, the analyst must **resolve inconsistencies** by one of these actions: - **Add** a missing element in the deficient model. - **Delete** an extraneous element. - **Modify** an element to match the others. The cycle of *Build → Verify → Resolve* repeats until all mappings are satisfied. Formally, the consistency condition is: $$\bigwedge_{i=1}^{n} \Big[ \mathcal{M}_{OD}(\mathcal{O}(C_i)) = \text{Valid} \;\wedge\; \mathcal{M}_{EO}(E_i) = \text{Valid} \;\wedge\; \mathcal{M}_{PO}(F_k) = \text{Valid} \;\wedge\; \mathcal{M}_{DS}(\text{DFD}) = \text{Valid} \Big]$$ This conjunction must evaluate to **TRUE** for the combined model to be declared *consistent*. --- ### 3.2 Worked Example: Automated Teller Machine (ATM) **Object Model excerpt:** $$\mathcal{C} = \{\text{ATM, Customer, Card, Account, Transaction}\}$$ $$\mathcal{O}(\text{Account}) = \{\text{debit}() ,\; \text{credit}() ,\; \text{getBalance}()\}$$ **Dynamic Model (Account state diagram):** States: $S = \{\text{Active, Overdrawn, Frozen, Closed}\}$ Events: $E = \{\text{debit, credit, lock, unlock, close}\}$ Transition: $\delta(\text{Active}, \text{debit}) = \text{Overdrawn}$ with action $\text{updateBalance}()$ **Functional Model (DFD excerpt):** - Process: `compute_interest()` corresponds to operation $\text{Account.accrueInterest}()$. - Data store: `Account_DB` corresponds to class $\text{Account}$. - Input: `transaction_amount` corresponds to event $\text{debit}$ or $\text{credit}$. **Cross-Verification Result:** | Object Model Element | Dynamic Model Counterpart | Functional Model Counterpart | Consistent? | |---|---|---|---| | `Account.debit()` | action in Active → Overdrawn | process `debit_account` | ✅ | | Event `lock` | transition Overdrawn → Frozen | input to `freeze_account` | ✅ | | `Transaction` class | no state diagram (not state-bearing) | DFD token | ✅ | | Attribute `balance` | — | data store `Account_DB.balance` | ✅ | > [!NOTE] > **Conclusion:** All four cross-checks pass. The combined model is consistent. Had the operation `freeze()` not existed in the class `Account` while a `lock` event existed in the state diagram, the models would be inconsistent, and the analyst would have to add the missing operation. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Pitfalls and Resolutions | # | Common Pitfall | Symptom | Resolution | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Orphan operation** | Operation in object model not triggered by any event | Add the missing event in dynamic model | | 2 | **Dangling event** | Event in dynamic model triggers no operation | Add the missing operation in object model | | 3 | **Unmapped process** | DFD process corresponds to no class operation | Add the operation or remove the process | | 4 | **Unreferenced attribute** | Attribute never appears in any DFD flow | Either delete it or add a process that uses it | | 5 | **State explosion** | Object modified to have too many states | Decompose into smaller classes (Refactoring) | | 6 | **Static class confused with state-bearing** | DFD only; no state diagram drawn wrongly | Determine if instances truly change state | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: The Three-Model Integration Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD OM[Object Model
Classes, Attributes, Operations, Relationships] DM[Dynamic Model
State Diagrams, Events, Transitions] FM[Functional Model
DFD, Processes, Data Stores, Flows] OM -->|operations trigger| DM DM -->|events invoke| OM OM -->|attributes and data tokens| FM FM -->|processes implement| OM DM -->|state changes drive| FM FM -->|data flow constraints| DM VERIFY{Cross Verification
Rules Apply} OM --> VERIFY DM --> VERIFY FM --> VERIFY VERIFY -->|Consistent| CM[Combined Model
Coherent System Spec] VERIFY -->|Inconsistent| FIX[Iterate and Fix] FIX --> OM FIX --> DM FIX --> FM CM --> IMPL[Implementation Phase] ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Combination Algorithm Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Three Models Built] --> B[List all classes C in Object Model] B --> C{Is C state-bearing?} C -->|Yes| D[Build state diagram for C] C -->|No| E[Skip to next class] D --> F[List all operations of C] E --> F F --> G{For each operation op: does it appear as action in dynamic model OR as process in functional model?} G -->|Yes| H[Mark op as Verified] G -->|No| I[Add missing mapping - orphan operation detected] I --> J[Update respective model] J --> K{All operations verified?} H --> K K -->|No| F K -->|Yes| L[List all events E in Dynamic Model] L --> M{For each event E: does it correspond to a valid operation in object model?} M -->|Yes| N[Mark event as Verified] M -->|No| O[Add missing operation - dangling event detected] O --> P[Update Object Model] P --> Q{All events verified?} N --> Q Q -->|No| L Q -->|Yes| R[List all processes in Functional Model] R --> S{For each process: does it correspond to an operation?} S -->|Yes| T[Mark process as Verified] S -->|No| U[Add missing operation - unmapped process detected] U --> V[Update Object Model] V --> W{All processes verified?} T --> W W -->|No| R W -->|Yes| X[Combined Model is CONSISTENT] ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: ATM Example — Combination View ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph OBJ[Object Model] ATM1[ATM] CUST1[Customer] ACCT1[Account] TXN1[Transaction] ATM1 -->|serves| CUST1 CUST1 -->|owns| ACCT1 ACCT1 -->|records| TXN1 end subgraph DYN[Dynamic Model - Account] S1((Active)) S2((Overdrawn)) S3((Frozen)) S1 -->|debit event| S2 S2 -->|credit event| S1 S2 -->|lock event| S3 S3 -->|unlock event| S2 end subgraph FUN[Functional Model - DFD] PROC1[debit_account] PROC2[credit_account] PROC3[compute_interest] DS1[(Account_DB)] PROC1 --> DS1 DS1 --> PROC2 DS1 --> PROC3 end ACCT1 -.operations.-> S1 S1 -.debit event.-> PROC1 PROC1 -.updates.-> ACCT1 DS1 -.persists.-> ACCT1 ``` ### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Mapping Categories Visualization ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph CAT1[Category 1 - Same Phenomenon Different Models] A1[Object Model: Operation debit] A2[Dynamic Model: Action in state transition] A3[Functional Model: Process in DFD] A1 --- A2 A2 --- A3 end subgraph CAT2[Category 2 - Same Phenomenon Different Detail] B1[Object Model: Operation computeInvoice] B2[Dynamic Model: Series of states] B3[Functional Model: Sub-DFD] B1 --- B2 B2 --- B3 end subgraph CAT3[Category 3 - Independent Phenomena] C1[Object Model: Class LinkedList] C2[Dynamic Model: none] C3[Functional Model: Sort routine] C1 --- C3 end ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam - Model Question, Module 4]** **Q1. [3 Marks]** **What is meant by "Combining the Three Models" in object-oriented modeling? Why is this step necessary after building the Object, Dynamic, and Functional models independently?** **Model Answer:** Combining the three models is the integration phase in OMT where the Object Model (static structure), Dynamic Model (temporal behavior), and Functional Model (data transformation) are cross-verified and reconciled to form a single, consistent system specification. This step is necessary because: 1. The three models are built semi-independently and may contain inconsistencies such as orphan operations, dangling events, or unmapped processes. 2. Cross-verification ensures that every operation in the Object Model corresponds to an action in the Dynamic Model or a process in the Functional Model, and vice versa. 3. It uncovers missing requirements and guarantees completeness. 4. The combined model provides a complete, unambiguous view needed before implementation. The combination yields a *coherent specification* where structure, behavior, and computation are mutually consistent. --- **Q2. [3 Marks]** **List any three cross-verification rules used while combining the three models. Give a one-line example for each.** **Model Answer:** | # | Rule | Example | |---|---|---| | 1 | Every event in the Dynamic Model must correspond to an operation in the Object Model | Event `debit` triggers `Account.debit()` | | 2 | Every process in the Functional Model must correspond to an operation in the Object Model | Process `compute_interest` maps to `Account.accrueInterest()` | | 3 | Every data store in the DFD must correspond to a class or attribute in the Object Model | Data store `Customer_DB` maps to class `Customer` | | 4 | Every state-bearing class in the Object Model must have a state diagram in the Dynamic Model | Class `Account` has states Active, Overdrawn, Frozen | | 5 | Every attribute of a class must appear as data flow in some DFD process | Attribute `balance` appears in `debit_account` process flow | --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam - Model Question, Module 4, ESE Pattern]** --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the three categories of mapping between the Object, Dynamic, and Functional models as described in OMT. Illustrate each category with a suitable example. **(b) [7 Marks]** For a Library Management System, the Object Model includes classes `Book`, `Member`, and `Loan`. A `Book` can be in states: `Available`, `Issued`, `Reserved`, and `Lost`. The events are: `issue`, `return`, `reserve`, `markLost`. Draw the state diagram for `Book` and verify its consistency with a sample Functional Model DFD showing processes `issue_book`, `return_book`, `mark_lost`, and data store `Book_DB`. List the cross-checks performed. --- **Model Answer for Q.A(a):** The three categories of mapping, as described by Rumbaugh in OMT, are: **Category 1: Same phenomenon — different models.** The same real-world event appears in all three models, but in different forms. *Example:* When a customer places an order: - Object Model: Operation `Order.place()`. - Dynamic Model: Event `placeOrder` causing a state transition in `Order`. - Functional Model: Process `place_order` in a DFD with input `order_details` and output `confirmation`. **Category 2: Same phenomenon — different level of detail.** A complex concept is shown at different granularities in each model. *Example:* The operation `computeInvoice()` in the Object Model: - In the Dynamic Model, it appears as a *sequence of states* (calculating tax → applying discount → finalizing). - In the Functional Model, it is decomposed into a *sub-DFD* containing processes for tax computation, discount application, and total calculation. **Category 3: Different phenomena in different models (independent views).** Some elements exist in only one or two models, not all three. *Example:* A pure data structure class like `LinkedList` has presence only in the Object Model (no state changes, no functional transformations). Conversely, a sorting algorithm may exist in the Functional Model without a corresponding state diagram.
