These categories help the analyst identify *what to combine* and *what to leave independent* during integration. --- **Model Answer for Q.A(b):** **State Diagram for `Book`:** States: $S_{\text{Book}} = \{\text{Available, Issued, Reserved, Lost}\}$ Events: $E_{\text{Book}} = \{\text{issue, return, reserve, markLost}\}$ | Current State | Event | Next State | Action | |---|---|---|---| | Available | issue | Issued | setIssueDate() | | Available | reserve | Reserved | setReservation() | | Issued | return | Available | clearIssueDate() | | Reserved | issue | Issued | setIssueDate() | | Issued | markLost | Lost | recordPenalty() | | Available | markLost | Lost | recordPenalty() | **DFD (Level 1) excerpt:** - Processes: `issue_book`, `return_book`, `mark_lost`. - Data Store: `Book_DB` (maps to class `Book`). - Inputs: `member_id`, `book_id`, `event_type`. - Outputs: `book_status`, `due_date`, `penalty_amount`. **Cross-Verification Performed:** | Object Model Element | Dynamic Model Counterpart | Functional Model Counterpart | Result | |---|---|---|---| | Class `Book` | State diagram exists | Data store `Book_DB` | ✅ | | Operation `Book.issue()` | Action in Available→Issued | Process `issue_book` | ✅ | | Event `markLost` | Transition to Lost | Process `mark_lost` | ✅ | | Attribute `dueDate` | — | Output of `issue_book` | ✅ | | Attribute `status` | Represented by current state | Input to all processes | ✅ | | Class `Loan` | No state diagram (not state-bearing) | DFD token for loan records | ✅ | The combined model is **consistent**. All operations, events, and processes are mapped, and all attributes flow through the DFD. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** **(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the systematic procedure to combine the three models. List at least four specific cross-verification rules with their formal mapping notation. **(b) [7 Marks]** Consider an Online Shopping Cart System. The `Order` class has operations `addItem`, `removeItem`, `confirmOrder`, and `cancelOrder`. Events are `itemAdded`, `itemRemoved`, `orderConfirmed`, `orderCancelled`. States are `Empty`, `Filling`, `Confirmed`, `Cancelled`. Construct the state diagram and verify it against the Object and Functional models. Identify and fix any inconsistencies. --- **Model Answer for Q.B(a):** The systematic procedure to combine the three models comprises three phases: **Phase 1 — Preparation:** 1. Build the complete Object Model: identify all classes, attributes, operations, and relationships. 2. Identify state-bearing classes $C_i^{sb}$ and build their state diagrams $\Sigma_i$. 3. Build the DFD hierarchy of the Functional Model. **Phase 2 — Cross-Verification Rules:** | Rule | Formal Mapping | Description | |---|---|---| | Rule 1 | $\forall op \in \mathcal{O}(C_i), \; \mathcal{M}_{OD}(op) \neq \emptyset$ | Every operation must appear as an action or process | | Rule 2 | $\forall E \in E_i, \; \exists op \in \mathcal{O}(C_i) : E \to op$ | Every event must trigger an operation | | Rule 3 | $\forall P \in F_k, \; \exists op : P \equiv op$ | Every DFD process must correspond to an operation | | Rule 4 | $\forall D \in \text{DFD}, \; \exists C_i \text{ or } a \in \mathcal{A}(C_i) : D \equiv C_i \text{ or } D \equiv a$ | Every data store must map to a class or attribute | | Rule 5 | $\forall a \in \mathcal{A}(C_i), \; a \in \text{tokens}(P) \text{ for some } P$ | Every attribute must flow through a DFD process | **Phase 3 — Consistency Resolution:** Iterate Build → Verify → Fix until: $$\bigwedge_{i=1}^{n} [\text{Rule}_i \text{ satisfied}] = \text{TRUE}$$ --- **Model Answer for Q.B(b):** **State Diagram for `Order`:** States: $S = \{\text{Empty, Filling, Confirmed, Cancelled}\}$ Events: $E = \{\text{itemAdded, itemRemoved, orderConfirmed, orderCancelled}\}$ | Current State | Event | Next State | Action | |---|---|---|---| | Empty | itemAdded | Filling | addItem() | | Filling | itemAdded | Filling | addItem() | | Filling | itemRemoved | Filling or Empty | removeItem() | | Filling | orderConfirmed | Confirmed | confirmOrder() | | Filling | orderCancelled | Cancelled | cancelOrder() | | Confirmed | — | — | (terminal) | | Cancelled | — | — | (terminal) | **Verification Against Object Model:** - Operations present: `addItem`, `removeItem`, `confirmOrder`, `cancelOrder` — all four appear as actions in the state diagram. ✅ - Events present: all four events trigger valid operations. ✅ **Verification Against Functional Model:** Suppose the DFD has: - Process `add_item_to_cart` ↔ `Order.addItem()` ✅ - Process `remove_item_from_cart` ↔ `Order.removeItem()` ✅ - Process `confirm_order_processing` ↔ `Order.confirmOrder()` ✅ - Process `cancel_order_processing` ↔ `Order.cancelOrder()` ✅ - Data store `Order_DB` ↔ class `Order` ✅ **Inconsistency Detected and Fixed:** - *Initial inconsistency:* The operation `Order.applyDiscount()` exists in the Object Model but is not invoked by any event in the Dynamic Model, nor represented as a process in the Functional Model. - *Fix:* Add an event `discountApplied` in the Dynamic Model that triggers a transition within `Confirmed` state (e.g., showing the discount on the invoice) and a process `apply_discount` in the Functional Model that uses the `discountRate` attribute. After the fix, the combined model is fully consistent. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in "Combining the Three Models" Questions:** > > 1. **Writing only the definitions** without listing the cross-verification rules — examiners award marks specifically for the *mapping rules*, not just the abstract concept. Always state the rules explicitly. > 2. **Confusing operations with processes.** A process in the DFD is **not** the same as an operation in the object model unless explicitly mapped. Many students treat them as identical and lose marks. > 3. **Forgetting state-bearing vs non-state-bearing classes.** Not every class needs a state diagram. Mark explicitly which classes are state-bearing. > 4. **Skipping the iteration step.** Examiners expect you to mention that combining is *iterative* — Build → Verify → Fix → Repeat. A single pass is incorrect. > 5. **Not providing examples in Part B answers.** Even for 14-mark theory questions, KTU examiners expect at least one worked example with a class and its state diagram to substantiate the answer. > 6. **Mixing up the three categories of mapping.** Many students write only Category 1 and forget Categories 2 and 3, losing 2–3 marks. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Combining the Three Models** - ⭐ **Three Models:** Object (structure), Dynamic (behavior over time), Functional (data transformation). - ⭐ **Orthogonal but Complementary:** Built semi-independently, integrated at the end. - ⭐ **Combination = Cross-Verification:** Apply mapping rules to ensure consistency. - ⭐ **Five Key Mapping Rules:** Operation↔Action, Event↔Operation, Process↔Operation, DataStore↔Class/Attribute, Attribute↔DataFlow. - ⭐ **Three Mapping Categories (Rumbaugh):** Same phenomenon–different models, Same phenomenon–different detail, Independent phenomena. - ⭐ **State-Bearing Classes:** Only classes whose instances change state need state diagrams. - ⭐ **Iteration:** Build → Verify → Fix → Repeat until all rules pass. - ⭐ **Consistency Criterion:** $\bigwedge_{i=1}^{n} [\text{Rule}_i \text{ satisfied}] = \text{TRUE}$. - ⭐ **Modern Relevance:** Predecessor of UML's unified modelling; principle still used in MDA. - ⭐ **Real-world examples to remember:** ATM (Account states), Library (Book states), Shopping Cart (Order states). - ⭐ **Common pitfalls to avoid:** Orphan operations, dangling events, unmapped processes, unreferenced attributes. - ⭐ **Always provide examples in 14-mark answers** — KTU examiners reward practical illustration.

topics.title: Designing Algorithms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCNT604 - OBJECT ORIENTED MODELING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module - 4
> - **Topic:** Designing Algorithms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Algorithm Design** is the systematic, specification-driven engineering process of defining a finite, ordered sequence of unambiguous, executable computational steps that transform a well-defined input state into a desired output state, while satisfying correctness, termination, and efficiency constraints enforced within an Object-Oriented (OO) modeling framework.

In the context of **Object Oriented Modeling and Design (OOMD)**, algorithm design is *not* the writing of raw procedural code; it is the **modeling** of behavior on top of existing **UML class diagrams, sequence diagrams, and state machines**. Each algorithm is attached as an **operation/method** to a class, and the algorithm's correctness is judged against the **collaboration contracts** defined between cooperating objects (Larman’s GRASP patterns + GoF patterns).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen Recipe" Model

Imagine you walk into a professional kitchen to bake a cake. The kitchen is your **class** (with tools = *attributes* and cooks = *methods*). The recipe card on the wall is your **algorithm**.

| Kitchen Analogy | Object-Oriented Counterpart |
|---|---|
| Recipe card pinned on wall | **Operation** inside a UML class |
| List of ingredients | **Pre-conditions** of an operation |
| Step-by-step instructions | **Algorithm body** (sequence/loop/branch) |
| Final cake on the tray | **Post-conditions** of an operation |
| Multiple cooks sharing tasks | **Collaborations** between objects |
| Choosing the best knife for the job | **Design Pattern selection** (Strategy, Template, etc.) |
| Time taken to bake | **Time Complexity** $T(n)$ |
| Gas/electricity consumed | **Space Complexity** $S(n)$ |

Just as a head chef wouldn't pick a recipe without considering the available cooks, ingredients, and oven, an OOD designer doesn't pick an algorithm without first analyzing the **collaborating objects**, their **responsibilities**, and the **state invariants** the algorithm must preserve.

### 1.3 Key Properties an Algorithm MUST Satisfy (KTU Board-Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> A correct algorithm in OOMD must satisfy all five **FInite Properties (FIPP)**:

1. **Finiteness** – Must terminate after a bounded number of steps (no infinite loops).
2. **Input** – Zero or more well-typed parameters, conforming to the operation's signature.
3. **Output** – At least one well-formed result satisfying post-conditions.
4. **Precision (Definiteness)** – Every instruction is unambiguous to the executing object.
5. **Effectiveness** – Each step is basic enough to be carried out exactly and in finite time.

Additional OO-specific requirement:
6. **Contract Fidelity** – The algorithm must satisfy **pre-conditions**, **post-conditions**, and **invariants** declared in the class diagram (the **Operation Contract** in Design by Contract, Meyer).

### 1.4 Why Object-Oriented Algorithm Design Matters

- **Encapsulation of Behavior**: Algorithms become reusable operations inside classes.
- **Polymorphism**: The *same* operation can execute *different* algorithms at runtime (Strategy Pattern).
- **Substitutability (Liskov)**: A subclass algorithm must honor the parent’s contract.
- **State-Dependent Logic**: Algorithms can read and mutate private object state safely.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Algorithm Design Pipeline (Step-by-Step Logic)

The KTU 2024 OOMD syllabus expects students to follow a **seven-stage pipeline** when designing algorithms within an OO model:

**Step 1 — Requirement Elicitation from Use Cases**
- Trace each use-case flow into specific system operations.
- Example: "Withdraw Cash" → operation `withdraw(amount)` on class `ATM`.

**Step 2 — Contract Specification**
- Define **pre-conditions**, **post-conditions**, and **invariants** for each operation.
- This is the formal specification that the algorithm must satisfy.

**Step 3 — Algorithm Strategy Selection**
- Choose from: **Iteration, Recursion, Divide & Conquer, Greedy, Dynamic Programming, Backtracking, Branch & Bound**.

**Step 4 — Pseudo-code Construction**
- Write language-agnostic pseudo-code; do not jump to code yet.
- KTU board exams typically award 2 marks for clean pseudo-code.

**Step 5 — Complexity Analysis**
- Compute $T(n)$ (time) and $S(n)$ (space) using **Asymptotic Notation**.

**Step 6 — Mapping to Collaborating Objects**
- Identify which classes participate (Controller, Information Expert, Creator — GRASP).
- Replace local blocks with **messages** sent to other objects.

**Step 7 — Pattern Application**
- Wrap the algorithm in a suitable GoF pattern (Template, Strategy, State, Command).

### 2.2 Asymptotic Notation — The Mathematical Backbone

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Asymptotic notation expresses the **growth rate** of $T(n)$ as $n \to \infty$. KTU examiners frequently ask students to formally define Big-O, Big-Theta, and Big-Omega and apply them to small loops.

**2.2.1 Big-O Notation — Upper Bound**

$$f(n) = O(g(n)) \iff \exists \, c > 0, \, n_0 > 0 \text{ such that } 0 \le f(n) \le c \cdot g(n) \;\; \forall \, n \ge n_0$$

Intuition: *"f grows **no faster** than g."* — Used to express **worst-case** runtime.

**2.2.2 Big-Omega Notation — Lower Bound**

$$f(n) = \Omega(g(n)) \iff \exists \, c > 0, \, n_0 > 0 \text{ such that } 0 \le c \cdot g(n) \le f(n) \;\; \forall \, n \ge n_0$$

Intuition: *"f grows **at least** as fast as g."* — Used for **best-case** statements.

**2.2.3 Big-Theta Notation — Tight Bound**

$$f(n) = \Theta(g(n)) \iff f(n) = O(g(n)) \; \text{and} \; f(n) = \Omega(g(n))$$

Intuition: *"f grows **at the same rate** as g."*

**2.2.4 Little-o and Little-omega**
- $f(n) = o(g(n))$ means $f$ grows **strictly slower** than $g$.
- $f(n) = \omega(g(n))$ means $f$ grows **strictly faster** than $g$.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Reference Table

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates every formula, recurrence, and growth-rate reference required for KTU 2024 OOMD Module 4 problems. Use this as your last-minute revision anchor.

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Meaning / Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| Big-O definition | $f(n) \le c \cdot g(n)$ | Worst-case upper bound |
| Big-Omega definition | $f(n) \ge c \cdot g(n)$ | Best-case lower bound |
| Big-Theta definition | $f(n) = O(g(n)) \wedge f(n) = \Omega(g(n))$ | Tight asymptotic bound |
| Master Theorem | $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$ | Divide & conquer recurrences |
| Master Case 1 | $f(n) = O(n^{\log_b a - \epsilon})$ | $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a})$ |
| Master Case 2 | $f(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \log^k n)$ | $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \log^{k+1} n)$ |
| Master Case 3 | $f(n) = \Omega(n^{\log_b a + \epsilon})$ | $T(n) = \Theta(f(n))$ |
| Linear search $T(n)$ | $T(n) = T(n-1) + c$ | Yields $\Theta(n)$ |
| Binary search $T(n)$ | $T(n) = T(n/2) + c$ | Yields $\Theta(\log n)$ |
| Bubble / Insertion / Selection sort | $\frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ comparisons | $\Theta(n^2)$ |
| Merge sort recurrence | $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$ | $\Theta(n \log n)$ |
| Quick sort avg | $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$ | $\Theta(n \log n)$ |
| Quick sort worst | $T(n) = T(n-1) + n$ | $\Theta(n^2)$ |
| Fibonacci (naive) | $T(n) = T(n-1) + T(n-2)$ | $\Theta(\phi^n)$, $\phi = 1.618$ |
| Fibonacci (DP) | $T(n) = T(n-1) + c$ | $\Theta(n)$ |
| Space for recursion depth $d$ | $S(n) = O(d)$ | Call-stack usage |
| Geometric series | $\sum_{i=0}^{k} r^i = \frac{r^{k+1}-1}{r-1}$ | Used in nested loop sums |
| Arithmetic series | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} i = \frac{n(n+1)}{2}$ | Linear search comparisons |
| Stirling's approx | $n! \approx \sqrt{2\pi n} \left(\frac{n}{e}\right)^n$ | Factorial growth |

### 2.4 Algorithm Design Strategies — Deep Dive

**2.4.1 Brute Force**
- Try every possibility. Simple, but usually $O(n!)$ or $O(2^n)$.
- OO placement: Used in early prototyping classes (e.g., `BruteForceLogin` subclass of `AuthStrategy`).

**2.4.2 Divide and Conquer (D&C)**
- Split → Solve subproblems → Combine. Three canonical examples: **Merge Sort, Quick Sort, Binary Search**.
- Always analyzed via the **Master Theorem**.

**2.4.3 Greedy Algorithms**
- At each step, pick the *locally optimal* choice, hoping for global optimum.
- Classic OO scenarios: Coin change (canonical denominations), Dijkstra, Kruskal, Prim.
- Must prove **Greedy-Choice Property** + **Optimal Substructure**.

**2.4.4 Dynamic Programming (DP)**
- Solve once, store, reuse. Trades **space for time**.
- Two flavors: **Top-Down (memoization)** vs **Bottom-Up (tabulation)**.
- Canonical examples: Fibonacci, Matrix Chain Multiplication, 0/1 Knapsack, Floyd–Warshall.

**2.4.5 Backtracking**
- DFS with **pruning** of infeasible branches. Used in N-Queens, Sudoku, Graph Coloring.
- Worst case still exponential, but vastly smaller in practice.

**2.4.6 Branch and Bound**
- Backtracking + lower-bound cost estimates to prune search tree (e.g., TSP with $O(n^2 \cdot 2^n)$).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Compiler Design**: Lexical analyzers use **DFA-based pattern matching** (linear algorithms). Parsers use **LL/LR algorithms** (linear in input size).
- **Database Indexing**: B-Tree search is $\Theta(\log n)$. Hash index lookup is $\Theta(1)$ average.
- **Network Routing**: Dijkstra and Bellman–Ford are foundational to OSPF and RIP protocols.
- **Machine Learning Pipelines**: Gradient Descent is iterative; backpropagation is DP-based.
- **Graphics Rendering**: Painter's algorithm (back-to-front sort) — uses **topological sort**, $\Theta(V+E)$.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Time Complexity of Insertion Sort

Consider the Insertion Sort algorithm operating on an array $A[1 \ldots n]$.

**Pseudo-code:**
```
for i ← 2 to n do
    key ← A[i]
    j ← i − 1
    while j > 0 and A[j] > key do
        A[j+1] ← A[j]
        j ← j − 1
    end while
    A[j+1] ← key
end for
```

**Step-by-step complexity build-up:**

For each outer-loop iteration $i$, the inner `while` loop executes at most $(i-1)$ comparisons in the worst case. Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{worst}}(n) &= \sum_{i=2}^{n}(i-1) + (n-1) \text{ assignments} \\
&= \sum_{i=2}^{n}(i-1) + (n-1) \\
&= \sum_{k=1}^{n-1}k + (n-1) \\
&= \frac{(n-1)n}{2} + (n-1) \\
&= \frac{n^2 - n}{2} + n - 1 \\
&= \frac{n^2}{2} + \frac{n}{2} - 1 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Taking the dominant term as $n \to \infty$:

$$
T_{\text{worst}}(n) = \Theta(n^2)
$$

**Best case** (already sorted array): the inner `while` loop fails on first comparison for every $i$. So:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{best}}(n) &= \sum_{i=2}^{n} 1 = n-1 = \Theta(n)
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore Insertion Sort exhibits the classic **$O(n^2)$ worst-case, $\Theta(n)$ best-case** asymmetry.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Solving the Merge Sort Recurrence via Master Theorem

Merge Sort splits the array into two halves of size $n/2$, recursively sorts each, then merges them in $\Theta(n)$ time. The recurrence is:

$$
T(n) = 2T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + n
$$

**Step 1: Identify parameters.**
- $a = 2$ (number of subproblems)
- $b = 2$ (size-reduction factor)
- $f(n) = n$ (cost of combine step)

**Step 2: Compute critical exponent.**

$$
n^{\log_b a} = n^{\log_2 2} = n^1 = n
$$

**Step 3: Compare $f(n)$ with $n^{\log_b a}$.**

Here $f(n) = n$ and $n^{\log_b a} = n$. They grow at the **same rate**, so we are exactly in **Master Theorem Case 2** with $k = 0$.

**Step 4: Apply Case 2 result.**

$$
T(n) = \Theta\!\left(n^{\log_b a} \log^{k+1} n\right) = \Theta\!\left(n^1 \log^1 n\right) = \Theta(n \log n)
$$

**Conclusion:** Merge Sort runs in $\Theta(n \log n)$ time in all three cases (best, average, worst). This is why it is the preferred comparison-based sort in production OOD frameworks (e.g., Java's `Arrays.sort` for objects uses TimSort, a hybrid of merge + insertion).

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Fibonacci — Naive Recursion vs Dynamic Programming

**Naive recursive Fibonacci:**
```
fib(n):
    if n ≤ 1: return n
    return fib(n−1) + fib(n−2)
```

**Recurrence:** $T(n) = T(n-1) + T(n-2) + \Theta(1)$

**Solution using characteristic equation:**

$$
x^2 = x + 1 \;\Longrightarrow\; x^2 - x - 1 = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; x = \frac{1 \pm \sqrt{5}}{2}
$$

The dominant root is the **Golden Ratio**:

$$
\phi = \frac{1 + \sqrt{5}}{2} \approx 1.618
$$

Therefore:

$$
T(n) = \Theta(\phi^n) \approx \Theta(1.618^n)
$$

**Exponential blow-up:** For $n = 50$, $T(50) \approx 2.8 \times 10^{10}$ operations — catastrophic.

**DP solution (bottom-up):**
```
fib(n):
    prev ← 0; curr ← 1
    for i ← 2 to n:
        next ← prev + curr
        prev ← curr
        curr ← next
    return curr
```

**New recurrence:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= T(n-1) + c \\
T(n) &= \Theta(n)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Speedup factor:**

$$
\frac{\phi^n}{n} \to \infty \text{ as } n \to \infty
$$

For $n = 50$: DP takes **50 steps** vs naive's **$2.8 \times 10^{10}$** — a **$5.6 \times 10^8$** speedup.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Strategy

> [!NOTE]
> The following matrix maps each algorithmic strategy to its **OO design pattern**, **typical use case**, and **complexity class**. KTU examiners love cross-questions like "Which design pattern would you choose for swapping greedy vs DP variants at runtime?" — Answer: **Strategy Pattern**.

| Strategy | Best For | Typical Complexity | Preferred OO Pattern | Key Risk |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Brute Force | Tiny inputs, prototyping | $O(n!)$ or $O(2^n)$ | None (raw method) | Combinatorial explosion |
| Divide & Conquer | Sortable/searchable structures | $O(n \log n)$ | Template Method | Recursion depth / stack overflow |
| Greedy | Optimization w/ matroid structure | $O(n \log n)$ to $O(n^2)$ | Strategy | Locally optimal ≠ globally optimal |
| Dynamic Programming | Overlapping subproblems | $O(n)$ to $O(n^3)$ | Memento (caching) | High memory footprint |
| Backtracking | Constraint satisfaction | Exponential worst | Recursion + pruning | State explosion |
| Branch & Bound | Combinatorial optimization | Exponential worst, much better avg | Composite + Visitor | Lower bound quality |
| Iterative Improvement | Heuristic search | Variable | State | May converge to local optimum |

### 3.5 Worked Example — Binary Search Within a Sorted Collection

Consider the operation `boolean contains(Item key)` on class `SortedCatalog`.

**Algorithm (Iterative):**
```
low ← 1; high ← n
while low ≤ high do
    mid ← (low + high) / 2
    if catalog[mid] = key: return true
    else if catalog[mid] < key: low ← mid + 1
    else: high ← mid − 1
end while
return false
```

**Recurrence Analysis:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= T\!\left(\left\lceil \frac{n}{2} \right\rceil\right) + c \\
T(1) &= c_0 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Unrolling the recurrence:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + c \\
&= T\!\left(\frac{n}{4}\right) + 2c \\
&= T\!\left(\frac{n}{8}\right) + 3c \\
&\;\;\vdots \\
&= T\!\left(\frac{n}{2^k}\right) + kc \\
\end{aligned}
$$

The recursion bottoms out when $\frac{n}{2^k} = 1$, i.e., $k = \log_2 n$. Substituting:

$$
T(n) = T(1) + c \log_2 n = \Theta(\log n)
$$

This is why binary search is the canonical **$O(\log n)$** example in every KTU paper.

### 3.6 Exhaustive Iteration Bound — Double Loop

For a nested loop of the form `for i = 1..n: for j = i..n:`, the number of iterations is the upper triangular sum:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=i}^{n} 1 &= \sum_{i=1}^{n}(n - i + 1) \\
&= \sum_{k=1}^{n} k \quad (\text{substituting } k = n-i+1) \\
&= \frac{n(n+1)}{2} \\
&= \Theta(n^2) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Algorithm Design Workflow in an OO Context

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Use Case Description"] --> B["Identify System Operations"]
    B --> C["Write Operation Contracts"]
    C --> D{"Choose Algorithm Strategy"}
    D --> E1["Brute Force"]
    D --> E2["Divide and Conquer"]
    D --> E3["Greedy"]
    D --> E4["Dynamic Programming"]
    D --> E5["Backtracking"]
    E1 --> F["Draft Pseudo-code"]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    E5 --> F
    F --> G["Analyze Time and Space"]
    G --> H["Assign to Collaborating Classes"]
    H --> I{"Apply Design Pattern?"}
    I --> J1["Strategy Pattern"]
    I --> J2["Template Method Pattern"]
    I --> J3["State Pattern"]
    I --> J4["Command Pattern"]
    J1 --> K["Final Sequence Diagram"]
    J2 --> K
    J3 --> K
    J4 --> K
    K --> L["Implementation and Unit Tests"]
```

### 4.2 Divide and Conquer Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P["Problem of size n"] --> Q["Base Case n is small?"]
    Q -- Yes --> R["Solve directly and return"]
    Q -- No --> S1["Divide into a subproblems of size n/b"]
    S1 --> R1["Recursively solve subproblem 1"]
    S1 --> R2["Recursively solve subproblem 2"]
    S1 --> R3["Recursively solve subproblem k"]
    R1 --> C["Combine sub-results"]
    R2 --> C
    R3 --> C
    C --> O["Final solution"]
    R --> O
```

### 4.3 Dynamic Programming — Top-Down vs Bottom-Up

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TOPDOWN["TOP-DOWN MEMOIZATION"]
        A1["fib n"] --> A2["fib n-1"]
        A1 --> A3["fib n-2"]
        A2 --> A4["fib n-2 stored"]
        A4 --> A5["return cached value"]
        A3 --> A6["fib n-3"]
        A3 --> A7["fib n-4"]
    end
    subgraph BOTTOMUP["BOTTOM-UP TABULATION"]
        B1["table 0 = 0"] --> B2["table 1 = 1"]
        B2 --> B3["for i = 2 to n"]
        B3 --> B4["table i = table i-1 + table i-2"]
        B4 --> B5["return table n"]
    end
```

### 4.4 Strategy Pattern Applied to Algorithm Selection

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Context {
        -strategy AlgorithmStrategy
        +setStrategy(s AlgorithmStrategy)
        +executeAlgorithm(data)
    }
    class AlgorithmStrategy {
        <<interface>>
        +solve(data)
    }
    class BruteForceStrategy {
        +solve(data)
    }
    class GreedyStrategy {
        +solve(data)
    }
    class DynamicProgrammingStrategy {
        +solve(data)
    }
    Context --> AlgorithmStrategy : delegates
    AlgorithmStrategy <|.. BruteForceStrategy
    AlgorithmStrategy <|.. GreedyStrategy
    AlgorithmStrategy <|.. DynamicProgrammingStrategy
```

### 4.5 Algorithm Complexity Hierarchy (Growth Rate Map)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Constant O(1)"] --> B["Logarithmic O(log n)"]
    B --> C["Linear O(n)"]
    C --> D["Linearithmic O(n log n)"]
    D --> E["Quadratic O(n squared)"]
    E --> F["Cubic O(n cubed)"]
    F --> G["Exponential O(2 to n)"]
    G --> H["Factorial O(n factorial)"]
    style A fill:#d4f4dd
    style B fill:#d4f4dd
    style C fill:#fff4cc
    style D fill:#fff4cc

    style G fill:#ff8080
    style H fill:#ff4d4d
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*Define an algorithm. List and briefly explain any four important characteristics of a good algorithm in the context of object-oriented design.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

An algorithm is a finite, well-defined sequence of unambiguous instructions for solving a class of problems or performing a computation. In OOD, algorithms are encapsulated as **operations** within classes.

**Four key characteristics:**

1. **Finiteness** — The algorithm must terminate after a bounded number of steps. In an OO context, this corresponds to ensuring no operation enters an infinite message loop.

2. **Definiteness (Precision)** — Each step must be precisely defined, mapping to a clear method call or message dispatch between collaborating objects.

3. **Input** — Zero or more well-typed inputs conforming to the operation's signature as declared in the class diagram.

4. **Output** — At least one well-defined output satisfying the operation's post-conditions.

*(Other valid characteristics: Effectiveness, Correctness, Contract Fidelity.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between Big-O, Big-Theta, and Big-Omega asymptotic notations. Provide one example of an algorithm for each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Notation | Meaning | Example Algorithm |
|---|---|---|
| **Big-O** $O(g(n))$ | Asymptotic upper bound — worst case | Linear Search: $O(n)$ |
| **Big-Omega** $\Omega(g(n))$ | Asymptotic lower bound — best case | Linear Search on first element: $\Omega(1)$ |
| **Big-Theta** $\Theta(g(n))$ | Tight bound — both upper and lower | Merge Sort: $\Theta(n \log n)$ in all cases |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**Q3. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the **Divide and Conquer** algorithm design strategy. Apply it to design an algorithm for the **Merge Sort** problem. Write the pseudo-code and derive its time complexity using the Master Theorem. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare **Dynamic Programming** and **Greedy Algorithm** approaches. Illustrate with the **0/1 Knapsack problem**, showing why greedy fails but DP succeeds. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Divide and Conquer — Merge Sort**

**Definition (2 marks):** Divide and Conquer solves a problem by:
1. **Dividing** it into smaller sub-problems of the same type.
2. **Conquering** each sub-problem recursively.
3. **Combining** the solutions of sub-problems to form the solution of the original problem.

**Pseudo-code for Merge Sort (3 marks):**
```
procedure mergeSort(A[1..n])
    if n = 1: return A
    mid ← n / 2
    L ← mergeSort(A[1..mid])
    R ← mergeSort(A[mid+1..n])
    return merge(L, R)
end procedure

procedure merge(L, R)
    result ← empty list
    while L not empty and R not empty:
        if L[0] ≤ R[0]: append L[0] to result; remove L[0]
        else: append R[0] to result; remove R[0]
    append remaining elements of L or R to result
    return result
end procedure
```

**Complexity Derivation (2 marks):**
- Recurrence: $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$
- Master Theorem parameters: $a = 2$, $b = 2$, $f(n) = n$
- $n^{\log_b a} = n^{\log_2 2} = n^1 = n$
- $f(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a})$ — Master Theorem **Case 2** applies with $k = 0$
- Therefore $T(n) = \Theta(n \log n)$ in all three cases.

---

**(b) DP vs Greedy on 0/1 Knapsack (7 marks)**

**Greedy Approach (3 marks):**
The greedy strategy picks items in order of **maximum value-to-weight ratio**. While this gives an *intuitively* good result, it **fails** for 0/1 Knapsack because:
- Items are **indivisible** — you cannot take a fraction.
- The locally optimal ratio item may consume capacity that a slightly worse-ratio item would use more efficiently.

**Counter-example:** Capacity $W = 10$.
- Item A: weight 6, value 8, ratio 1.33
- Item B: weight 5, value 5, ratio 1.00
- Item C: weight 5, value 5, ratio 1.00

Greedy picks A first (ratio 1.33), then fits one of B or C → total value **13**.
Optimal: B + C → total value **10**? Actually **13+? wait:** the optimal here is also 13. Better counter-example:

**Cleaner Counter-example:** $W = 50$, three items:
- A: weight 30, value 30, ratio 1.0
- B: weight 20, value 20, ratio 1.0
- C: weight 40, value 40, ratio 1.0

Use a non-uniform ratio example:

- $W = 10$
- A: weight 6, value 7, ratio 1.17
- B: weight 5, value 5, ratio 1.00
- C: weight 5, value 5, ratio 1.00

Greedy picks A → only fits one of B/C → value = 12.
Optimal picks B + C → value = 10? Still 12. Let us fix:

- A: weight 4, value 4, ratio 1.00
- B: weight 5, value 6, ratio 1.20
- C: weight 6, value 7, ratio 1.17
- $W = 10$

Greedy picks B (ratio 1.20), then C (6 ≤ 10-5=5? NO). So Greedy picks only B → value 6.
Greedy picks B + nothing else? After B (weight 5), remaining capacity 5 — cannot pick C. Picks A → B+A: value 10.
Optimal: C + A: weight 4+6=10, value 7+4 = **11**. Greedy failed.

**DP Solution (4 marks):**
Let $V[i][w]$ = maximum value using first $i$ items with capacity $w$.

**Recurrence:**
$$
V[i][w] = \begin{cases}
0 & \text{if } i = 0 \text{ or } w = 0 \\
V[i-1][w] & \text{if } w_i > w \\
\max(V[i-1][w], \; v_i + V[i-1][w-w_i]) & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

DP fills an $(n+1) \times (W+1)$ table bottom-up, yielding $O(nW)$ time and $O(nW)$ space (reducible to $O(W)$).

**DP guarantees the optimal solution** because it explores *all* valid combinations, eliminating the local-optimum pitfall of greedy.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2

**Q4. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** What is **recursion**? Explain its advantages and disadvantages. Write a recursive algorithm to compute the $n$-th Fibonacci number and analyze its time complexity. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain how the **Strategy Design Pattern** can be applied to design flexible algorithms in object-oriented systems. Draw the UML class diagram and describe how it satisfies the **Open/Closed Principle**. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Recursion (7 marks)**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Recursion is an algorithm design technique where a function **calls itself** with smaller sub-problems until a **base case** is reached. Each call creates a new stack frame containing local variables and the return address.

**Advantages (1.5 marks):**
- Naturally expresses problems with recursive structure (trees, fractals, DFS).
- Reduces complex problems to elegant 3–5 line solutions.
- Naturally fits divide-and-conquer paradigm.

**Disadvantages (1.5 marks):**
- Memory overhead due to call-stack growth: $S(n) = O(n)$ for depth-`n` recursion.
- Risk of **stack overflow** for large `n`.
- Function call overhead (slower than iteration).
- May compute the same sub-problem repeatedly (e.g., naive Fibonacci).

**Recursive Fibonacci Algorithm (1 mark):**
```
fib(n):
    if n ≤ 1: return n
    return fib(n−1) + fib(n−2)
```

**Time Complexity Derivation (1 mark):**

$$
T(n) = T(n-1) + T(n-2) + c
$$

Using the characteristic equation $x^2 - x - 1 = 0$, the dominant root is $\phi = \frac{1+\sqrt{5}}{2}$.

$$
T(n) = \Theta(\phi^n) \approx \Theta(1.618^n)
$$

For $n = 50$, this requires approximately $2.8 \times 10^{10}$ calls — proving the inefficiency.

---

**(b) Strategy Design Pattern (7 marks)**

**Concept (2 marks):**
The **Strategy Pattern** defines a family of algorithms, encapsulates each one, and makes them **interchangeable** at runtime. It allows the algorithm's behavior to vary independently of the clients that use it.

**UML Class Diagram Description (2 marks):**

| Class / Interface | Role |
|---|---|
| `Context` | Maintains a reference to a `Strategy` object. Delegates the work to the linked strategy. |
| `ISortStrategy` (interface) | Declares the algorithm signature: `void sort(int[] data)` |
| `BubbleSortStrategy` | Concrete implementation: Bubble Sort |
| `QuickSortStrategy` | Concrete implementation: Quick Sort |
| `MergeSortStrategy` | Concrete implementation: Merge Sort |

```
Context --> ISortStrategy
ISortStrategy <|.. BubbleSortStrategy
ISortStrategy <|.. QuickSortStrategy
ISortStrategy <|.. MergeSortStrategy
```

**Open/Closed Principle Alignment (3 marks):**
- **Open for extension**: New sorting algorithms (e.g., `HeapSortStrategy`) can be added by simply creating a new class that implements `ISortStrategy`.
- **Closed for modification**: The `Context` class and existing strategy implementations remain untouched.
- This decouples algorithm selection from algorithm execution, complying with **Dependency Inversion** as well.

**Real-world OO Example:** Java's `Collections.sort(List, Comparator)` — the `Comparator` argument is essentially a runtime-injected strategy.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Module 4 — Designing Algorithms:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Big-O with worst-case in all contexts** — Big-O is an *upper bound*, not exclusively a worst-case statement. Use Big-Theta when best = worst = average.
> 2. **Skipping the base case in recursion analysis** — Every recursion complexity derivation must state the **base condition** explicitly (1 mark lost otherwise).
> 3. **Forgetting to mention space complexity** — KTU awards 1 mark for $S(n)$ even when the question emphasizes $T(n)$.
> 4. **Pseudo-code that mixes paradigms** — Don't write Java syntax with informal English. Stay language-neutral OR commit fully to one language.
> 5. **Master Theorem Case confusion** — Case 1 vs Case 2 is decided by comparing $f(n)$ with $n^{\log_b a}$. Students often skip the comparison step.
> 6. **No UML diagram for design-pattern questions** — A text-only answer to a Strategy/Template question forfeits 2–3 marks.
> 7. **Not stating assumptions** in greedy proofs — Always declare: *"Assume the greedy-choice property holds, then we prove by exchange argument..."*

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 60-Second Revision Sheet — Module 4: Designing Algorithms**

- [ ] An algorithm must satisfy **Finiteness, Input, Output, Definiteness, Effectiveness** — and in OOD, also **Contract Fidelity**.
- [ ] **Big-O** = upper bound (worst case), **Big-Omega** = lower bound (best case), **Big-Theta** = tight bound.
- [ ] Complexity hierarchy (fastest → slowest): $O(1) < O(\log n) < O(n) < O(n \log n) < O(n^2) < O(n^3) < O(2^n) < O(n!)$.
- [ ] **Master Theorem**: $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$. Critical exponent = $n^{\log_b a}$.
  - Case 1: $f(n)$ polynomially smaller → $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a})$
  - Case 2: $f(n)$ matches → $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \log^{k+1} n)$
  - Case 3: $f(n)$ polynomially larger → $T(n) = \Theta(f(n))$
- [ ] **Divide & Conquer**: Recurrence $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$. Examples: Merge Sort, Quick Sort, Binary Search, Strassen.
- [ ] **Greedy** = locally optimal choice. Works for: Dijkstra, Kruskal, Prim, Huffman. Fails for 0/1 Knapsack, TSP.
- [ ] **Dynamic Programming** = overlapping subproblems + optimal substructure. Use when subproblems repeat. Trade space for time.
- [ ] **Backtracking** = DFS + pruning. Used for N-Queens, Sudoku, mazes, graph coloring.
- [ ] **Naive Fibonacci** = $\Theta(\phi^n)$, **DP Fibonacci** = $\Theta(n)$. Speedup factor grows exponentially.
- [ ] **Insertion Sort**: best $\Theta(n)$, worst $\Theta(n^2)$, average $\Theta(n^2)$, in-place, stable.
- [ ] **Merge Sort**: all cases $\Theta(n \log n)$, requires $O(n)$ auxiliary space, stable.
- [ ] **Quick Sort**: best/avg $\Theta(n \log n)$, worst $\Theta(n^2)$, in-place, **not** stable.
- [ ] **Binary Search**: $\Theta(\log n)$, requires sorted array.
- [ ] **OO Patterns for Algorithms**: Strategy (interchangeable algorithms), Template (algorithm skeleton), State (state-dependent behavior), Command (encapsulate requests).
- [ ] **Open/Closed Principle**: Open for extension, closed for modification — perfectly embodied by Strategy.
- [ ] **Space complexity of recursion**: $S(n) = O(\text{depth of call tree})$.
- [ ] **Sum identities**: $\sum_{i=1}^n i = \frac{n(n+1)}{2}$, $\sum_{i=0}^{k} 2^i = 2^{k+1}-1$.
- [ ] Always end your complexity answer with the **asymptotic class** (Big-O/Theta) — never leave it as a polynomial.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design Optimization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCNT604 - OBJECT ORIENTED MODELING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Design Optimization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Optimization** in Object-Oriented Modeling and Design is the systematic process of refining an OO system's structure—its classes, objects, relationships, responsibilities, and collaborations—to achieve an optimal balance among competing quality attributes such as **performance**, **maintainability**, **reusability**, **extensibility**, and **reliability**, while satisfying the functional requirements specified in the use-case and analysis models.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context (PBCNT604), Design Optimization is treated as the **post-analysis refinement phase** that sits between the logical OO analysis model and the physical implementation. It applies a set of proven **heuristics**, **design metrics**, and **refactoring transformations** to the preliminary class diagram, use-case realizations, and interaction diagrams produced during analysis.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping (Module 4):**
> Design Optimization covers identification of suboptimal design elements (excessive coupling, weak cohesion, redundant inheritance, duplicated behavior), application of optimization heuristics (GRASP), reallocation of responsibilities, and the use of OO metrics (Chidamber–Kemerer suite) to quantitatively validate the improvements.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Workshop Tune-Up" Metaphor

Imagine you have just **assembled a car from spare parts** (this is your OO analysis model — the parts exist, they connect, the engine starts). But the car is jerky, the fuel efficiency is poor, and certain bolts are loose. **Design Optimization is the "tune-up" stage**:

- A mechanic inspects every joint and connection (**coupling analysis**).
- The mechanic checks whether similar bolts are doing the same job (**duplicated code / reusability**).
- The mechanic consolidates related parts into a single well-oiled unit (**cohesion improvement**).
- The mechanic removes parts that add weight without adding value (**dead code / speculative generality**).
- After the tune-up, the car runs smoother, uses less fuel, and is easier to repair later.

Similarly, in OO Design Optimization, we look at the design from a **quality perspective**, not a **functional perspective** — the system already *works*, but now we ask: *"How well does it work, and how cheaply can we change it tomorrow?"*

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of OO Design Optimization

| Pillar | Core Question Addressed | Typical KTU Keyword |
|---|---|---|
| **Responsibility Assignment** | *Who does what?* | GRASP patterns |
| **Structural Refinement** | *How are classes connected?* | Coupling & Cohesion |
| **Quantitative Validation** | *Is it provably better?* | CK Metrics |

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Insight for KTU Exams:** Whenever a question asks "How do you optimize an OO design?", your answer should triangulate these **three pillars**. Examiners reward answers that show *both* qualitative heuristics *and* quantitative metrics.

## 1.4 Why Design Optimization Matters — The Cost Curve

```
Cost of Fixing a Defect vs. Phase of Discovery
(Rising Exponentially as Project Progresses)
   │
   │                                              *
   │                                         *
Cost│                                    *
   │                              *
   │                        *
   │                  *
   │            *
   │      *
   │ *
   └─────────────────────────────────────────────────►
     Requirements  Design   Coding  Testing  Production
              (Cheapest)              (Most Expensive)
```

A design flaw discovered during the **analysis/design phase** is **10×–100× cheaper** to fix than the same flaw found in production. Design Optimization is therefore a **risk-mitigation investment**, not an academic luxury.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Optimization Process — A 5-Stage Pipeline

The optimization of an OO design is not a single action; it is a **pipeline** of iterative refinement steps:

1. **Baseline Measurement** — Capture the current values of OO metrics (WMC, CBO, DIT, NOC, RFC, LCOM) for every class.
2. **Smell Identification** — Detect "design smells" such as *Refused Bequest*, *Speculative Generality*, *Inappropriate Intimacy*, *Feature Envy*, and *God Class*.
3. **Heuristic Application** — Apply GRASP (General Responsibility Assignment Software Patterns) and GoF (Gang of Four) patterns to redistribute responsibilities.
4. **Refactoring Execution** — Apply mechanical transformations (*Extract Class*, *Move Method*, *Pull Up Field*, *Replace Inheritance with Delegation*, etc.).
5. **Re-Measurement & Validation** — Recompute metrics; confirm improvement; iterate if necessary.

> [!NOTE]
> **GRASP Recap (KTU-Favorite):**
> - **Information Expert** — Assign responsibility to the class with the information needed to fulfil it.
> - **Creator** — Assign class B the responsibility to create A if B aggregates, contains, or closely uses A.
> - **Low Coupling** — Minimize dependencies between classes.
> - **High Cohesion** — Keep responsibilities of a single class focused.
> - **Controller** — Route system events to a non-UI class.
> - **Polymorphism** — Use polymorphic operations to handle type-based variation.
> - **Pure Fabrication** — Invent a class to achieve low coupling/high cohesion.
> - **Indirection** — Introduce an intermediate object to decouple two classes.
> - **Protected Variations** — Identify points of predicted instability and protect them with a stable interface.

## 2.2 The Chidamber–Kemerer (CK) Metric Suite — Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **CK Metrics** were proposed by S. R. Chidamber and C. F. Kemerer in 1994 and form the **gold-standard quantitative toolkit** for OO design optimization. KTU examinations frequently test these formulas directly.

| Metric | Full Name | Formula (LaTeX) | Interpretation | Optimization Goal |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **WMC** | Weighted Methods per Class | $\text{WMC} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i$ where $c_i$ = complexity of method $i$, $n$ = total methods | Measures class complexity | $\downarrow$ Lower is better |
| **DIT** | Depth of Inheritance Tree | Length of longest path from class to root | Measures inheritance depth | $\downarrow$ Avoid very deep trees |
| **NOC** | Number of Children | Count of immediate subclasses | Measures inheritance breadth | $\downarrow$ Lower = less reuse pressure |
| **CBO** | Coupling Between Objects | Count of classes coupled to a given class (uses / used-by) | Measures inter-class dependency | $\downarrow$ Lower is better |
| **RFC** | Response For a Class | $\text{RFC} = \vert RS \vert$ where $RS$ = set of methods that can be invoked in response to a message | Measures potential communication complexity | $\downarrow$ Lower is better |
| **LCOM** | Lack of Cohesion of Methods | $\text{LCOM} = P - Q$ if $(P-Q)>0$, else $0$; where $P$ = method pairs not sharing any instance variable, $Q$ = pairs sharing at least one | Measures internal disjointedness | $\downarrow$ Lower = more cohesive |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid — "WDRNCL":** *W*MC, *D*IT, *R*FC, *N*OC, *C*BO, *L*COM. Memorize this order for rapid recall in the exam hall.

## 2.3 Coupling and Cohesion — Qualitative Metrics

Beyond CK, two qualitative forces govern every OO design:

- **Coupling ($\kappa$):** The degree of interdependence between modules. Range: *Content* < *Common* < *External* < *Control* < *Stamp* < *Data* < *Message*.
- **Cohesion ($\chi$):** The degree to which elements of a module belong together. Range: *Coincidental* < *Logical* < *Temporal* < *Procedural* < *Communicational* < *Sequential* < *Functional*.

The optimization goal is **minimize coupling, maximize cohesion** (often abbreviated as the **"Low-High" rule**).

$$\text{Design Quality Index (DQI)} \propto \frac{\chi_{\text{cohesion}}}{\kappa_{\text{coupling}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** In large-scale distributed systems (e.g., microservices), CBO and RFC are the two most actionable CK metrics because they directly predict the **blast radius** of a change — high CBO/RFC means a single class modification can ripple across the system.

## 2.4 Design Smells & Their Refactoring Remedies

| Design Smell | Symptom | Refactoring Cure |
|---|---|---|
| **God Class / Large Class** | One class has too many responsibilities (high WMC, LCOM) | *Extract Class*, *Extract Superclass* |
| **Feature Envy** | A method uses more features of another class than its own | *Move Method* |
| **Inappropriate Intimacy** | Classes know too much about each other's private parts | *Move Method*, *Extract Class*, *Change Bidirectional to Unidirectional* |
| **Refused Bequest** | Subclass inherits but does not need/use parent features | *Replace Inheritance with Delegation* |
| **Speculative Generality** | "Just-in-case" code never used | *Collapse Hierarchy*, *Remove Parameter*, *Rename Method* |
| **Duplicated Code** | Same logic in multiple places | *Extract Method*, *Form Template Method* |
| **Long Method** | Method does too much (high cyclomatic complexity) | *Extract Method*, *Replace Method with Method Object* |
| **Shotgun Surgery** | A single change requires many small edits across classes | *Move Method*, *Inline Class* |

## 2.5 Cyclomatic Complexity (McCabe's Metric) — For Method-Level Optimization

Although not strictly an OO metric, McCabe's **Cyclomatic Complexity** is essential for method-level optimization:

$$V(G) = E - N + 2P$$

Where:
- $E$ = number of edges in the control flow graph
- $N$ = number of nodes in the control flow graph
- $P$ = number of connected components (typically $P = 1$)

Equivalently, for a method:

$$V(G) = \text{Number of decision points} + 1$$

| Cyclomatic Complexity ($V(G)$) | Risk Assessment | Recommended Action |
|---|---|---|
| $1$–$10$ | Simple, low risk | No action |
| $11$–$20$ | Moderate complexity | Refactor recommended |
| $21$–$50$ | High complexity, hard to test | Refactor mandatory |
| $> 50$ | Untestable, error-prone | Decompose module |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students confuse **WMC** (which sums the complexities of all methods of a class) with **McCabe's V(G)** (which is per-method). WMC is *class-level*; V(G) is *method-level*.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — CK Metric Computation

Consider a simplified class `OrderProcessor` with the following structure:

**Class: `OrderProcessor`**
- Instance variables: `customer`, `cart`, `paymentGateway`
- Methods (with cyclomatic complexities):
  - `validateOrder()` → $c_1 = 3$
  - `applyDiscount()` → $c_2 = 5$
  - `chargeCustomer()` → $c_3 = 7$
  - `sendConfirmation()` → $c_4 = 2$
- Coupled to: `Customer`, `Cart`, `PaymentGateway`, `EmailService`, `Logger`, `InventoryManager` (6 classes)
- Methods invocable from `validateOrder()`, `applyDiscount()`, `chargeCustomer()` collectively: 11 (including self + external)
- Inherits from: `BaseProcessor` (Depth of inheritance tree = 2)
- Immediate subclasses: `BulkOrderProcessor`, `SubscriptionOrderProcessor` (NOC = 2)
- Method pairs sharing instance variables: $Q = 4$; method pairs not sharing: $P = 2$

Let us **derive each CK metric** step-by-step.

### Step 1 — Compute WMC

$$\text{WMC} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i = c_1 + c_2 + c_3 + c_4$$

$$\text{WMC} = 3 + 5 + 7 + 2$$

$$\boxed{\text{WMC} = 17}$$

### Step 2 — Compute DIT

The class `OrderProcessor` extends `BaseProcessor`, which extends `Object` (root). The longest path length is:

$$\text{DIT} = \text{length}(\text{OrderProcessor} \rightarrow \text{BaseProcessor} \rightarrow \text{Object}) = 2$$

$$\boxed{\text{DIT} = 2}$$

### Step 3 — Compute NOC

Count immediate subclasses of `OrderProcessor`:

$$\text{NOC} = \vert\{\text{BulkOrderProcessor},\ \text{SubscriptionOrderProcessor}\}\vert$$

$$\boxed{\text{NOC} = 2}$$

### Step 4 — Compute CBO

Count distinct classes that `OrderProcessor` is coupled to (uses *or* is used by):

$$\text{CBO} = \vert\{\text{Customer},\ \text{Cart},\ \text{PaymentGateway},\ \text{EmailService},\ \text{Logger},\ \text{InventoryManager}\}\vert$$

$$\boxed{\text{CBO} = 6}$$

### Step 5 — Compute RFC

RFC is the cardinality of the *Response Set* $RS$, defined as the union of all methods that can be invoked from any method of the class, including the methods of the class itself:

$$\text{RFC} = \vert RS \vert = 11 \quad \text{(as given in the problem)}$$

$$\boxed{\text{RFC} = 11}$$

### Step 6 — Compute LCOM

$$\text{LCOM} = P - Q \quad \text{only if } (P - Q) > 0,\ \text{else}\ 0$$

$$\text{LCOM} = P - Q = 2 - 4 = -2$$

Since $(P - Q) < 0$, by the standard Chidamber–Kemerer definition:

$$\boxed{\text{LCOM} = 0}$$

This indicates the class is **cohesive** — its methods share instance variables well.

## 3.2 Optimization Decision Matrix

After computing the metrics, the next step is to decide **where to refactor**. The following decision matrix maps high metric values to specific refactorings.

| Metric | Computed Value | Threshold | Decision | Refactoring to Apply |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **WMC** | $17$ | $>15$ | ⚠ Refactor | *Extract Class* — split into `OrderValidator`, `DiscountEngine` |
| **DIT** | $2$ | $\le 3$ | ✅ OK | None |
| **NOC** | $2$ | $\le 5$ | ✅ OK | None |
| **CBO** | $6$ | $>5$ | ⚠ Refactor | *Introduce Facade* — decouple `OrderProcessor` from `Logger` and `InventoryManager` |
| **RFC** | $11$ | $>10$ | ⚠ Refactor | *Move Method* — relocate some method calls to collaborator classes |
| **LCOM** | $0$ | $=0$ | ✅ OK | None |

## 3.3 Analytical Proof — Why "Low Coupling, High Cohesion" Optimizes Maintainability

The **Maintainability Index (MI)** (Halstead / Coleman formula, simplified):

$$\text{MI} = 171 - 5.2 \cdot \ln(V) - 0.23 \cdot \text{CBO} - 16.2 \cdot \ln(\text{LOC}) + 50 \cdot \sin\left(\sqrt{2.46 \cdot \chi}\right)$$

Where:
- $V$ = Halstead volume
- $\text{CBO}$ = Coupling Between Objects
- $\text{LOC}$ = Lines of Code
- $\chi$ = average cohesion (normalized to $[0,1]$)

> [!TIP]
> The sign of the CBO coefficient is **negative** ($\mathbf{-0.23}$), meaning *as coupling rises, maintainability falls*. Conversely, the cohesion term is *positive* ($\mathbf{+50 \cdot \sin(\cdots)}$), meaning *as cohesion rises, maintainability rises*. This is the formal algebraic justification of the heuristic rule.

Let us evaluate for two hypothetical designs:

**Design A:** $\text{CBO}_A = 8$, $\chi_A = 0.4$ (low cohesion)
**Design B:** $\text{CBO}_B = 3$, $\chi_B = 0.85$ (high cohesion)

Assume identical $V$ and $\text{LOC}$.

$$\Delta \text{MI} = \text{MI}_B - \text{MI}_A = -0.23 \cdot (3 - 8) + 50 \cdot \left[\sin(\sqrt{2.46 \cdot 0.85}) - \sin(\sqrt{2.46 \cdot 0.4})\right]$$

$$\Delta \text{MI} = -0.23 \cdot (-5) + 50 \cdot \left[\sin(1.446) - \sin(0.992)\right]$$

$$\Delta \text{MI} = 1.15 + 50 \cdot [0.9925 - 0.8364]$$

$$\Delta \text{MI} = 1.15 + 50 \cdot 0.1561$$

$$\Delta \text{MI} = 1.15 + 7.805$$

$$\boxed{\Delta \text{MI} \approx +8.96}$$

Design B is **approximately 9 points more maintainable** than Design A, purely through coupling reduction and cohesion enhancement.

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Refactoring Walkthrough — From Smell to Solution

**Initial Smell:** `OrderProcessor` is a God Class with WMC = 17.

**Step 1 — Identify Method Clusters by Concern**

- *Validation concern:* `validateOrder()`
- *Pricing concern:* `applyDiscount()`
- *Payment concern:* `chargeCustomer()`
- *Notification concern:* `sendConfirmation()`

**Step 2 — Apply *Extract Class* for the Largest Cluster**

Move `validateOrder()` and its supporting instance variable `cart` into a new class `OrderValidator`.

$$\text{New WMC}_{\text{OrderProcessor}} = 17 - 3 = 14$$

**Step 3 — Repeat for Pricing**

Move `applyDiscount()` into `DiscountEngine`.

$$\text{New WMC}_{\text{OrderProcessor}} = 14 - 5 = 9$$

**Step 4 — Re-measure CBO**

`OrderProcessor` now interacts via `OrderValidator` and `DiscountEngine` instead of directly with `Cart` and `PromotionRules`. CBO drops from 6 to 4.

**Step 5 — Verify LCOM Improvement**

The remaining methods in `OrderProcessor` (`chargeCustomer()`, `sendConfirmation()`) now share only `paymentGateway`, raising internal coherence — LCOM remains at 0, but conceptual cohesion improves.

$$\boxed{\text{Final WMC} = 9,\quad \text{Final CBO} = 4 \quad \Rightarrow \text{Optimization successful.}}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Model)]**
**Define Design Optimization in the context of Object-Oriented Modeling and Design. List any three optimization heuristics.**

**Model Answer:**
Design Optimization is the systematic refinement of an OO design to improve quality attributes such as maintainability, reusability, performance, and extensibility while preserving the functional behavior captured in the use-case and analysis models. It involves identifying design smells, applying heuristics, and validating improvements via OO metrics.

**Three Optimization Heuristics:**
1. **Minimize coupling** between classes (Low Coupling — GRASP).
2. **Maximize cohesion** within each class (High Cohesion — GRASP).
3. **Eliminate duplicated behavior** by extracting common functionality into reusable abstractions.

*(Award full marks if any two of the three heuristics are correctly named and explained.)*

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model)]**
**What is Coupling Between Objects (CBO)? Why is a high CBO value considered undesirable?**

**Model Answer:**
CBO is the count of classes coupled to a given class, where coupling arises from a method of one class invoking a method or accessing an attribute of another. Formally, $\text{CBO}(C) = \vert \{C' \mid C \text{ uses } C' \text{ or } C' \text{ uses } C\} \vert$.

A **high CBO** is undesirable because:
- Changes in one class ripple to many coupled classes (high **ripple effect**).
- It impedes independent testing and reuse.
- It increases the probability of integration defects.
- It reduces overall **Maintainability Index**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks** split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Examinees answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the **Chidamber–Kemerer (CK) Metric Suite** in detail. Define any **four** metrics with formulas and state the optimization goal for each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **GRASP patterns** used for responsibility assignment in OO design. Explain any **five** patterns with examples. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The **Chidamber–Kemerer (CK) Metric Suite**, proposed in 1994, is a set of six object-oriented metrics designed to quantify the internal structure of an OO design. Each metric captures a distinct quality dimension.

**Metric 1 — Weighted Methods per Class (WMC):**

$$\text{WMC} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i$$

where $c_i$ is the cyclomatic complexity of method $i$ and $n$ is the number of methods in the class. If all methods are assumed equally complex ($c_i = 1$), then $\text{WMC} = n$.

**Optimization goal:** *Minimize WMC*. A high WMC indicates the class does too much — a candidate for *Extract Class* refactoring.

**Metric 2 — Depth of Inheritance Tree (DIT):**

$$\text{DIT} = \text{length of the longest path from the class to the root of the inheritance hierarchy}$$

**Optimization goal:** *Keep DIT moderate (typically $\le 5$)*. Very deep trees make polymorphism hard to reason about; very shallow trees miss reuse opportunities.

**Metric 3 — Coupling Between Objects (CBO):**

$$\text{CBO}(C) = \vert \{C' \mid C \text{ invokes a method of } C' \text{ OR } C' \text{ invokes a method of } C\} \vert$$

**Optimization goal:** *Minimize CBO*. Lower coupling means easier maintenance, testing, and reuse.

**Metric 4 — Lack of Cohesion of Methods (LCOM):**

$$\text{LCOM} = \begin{cases} P - Q, & \text{if } P - Q > 0 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

where $P$ = number of method pairs that share **no** instance variable, $Q$ = number of method pairs that share **at least one** instance variable.

**Optimization goal:** *Minimize LCOM* (LCOM = 0 is ideal). High LCOM signals that the class should be split.

*(Marking note: Award 7 marks if at least 4 metrics are defined with formula + optimization goal. Deduct 1 mark for missing optimization goal on any metric.)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**GRASP (General Responsibility Assignment Software Patterns)** are nine fundamental patterns for assigning responsibilities to classes and objects in OO design.

**Pattern 1 — Information Expert:**
Assign a responsibility to the class that has the information necessary to fulfil it. *Example:* The `calculateTotal()` responsibility belongs to the `ShoppingCart` class because it has access to all `LineItem` objects.

**Pattern 2 — Creator:**
Assign class B the responsibility to create instances of class A if B contains, aggregates, records, or closely uses A. *Example:* `Order` is a good creator of `OrderLine` because `Order` contains `OrderLine` instances.

**Pattern 3 — Low Coupling:**
Evaluate assignments to keep dependencies low, thereby reducing the impact of changes. *Example:* Use a `PaymentGateway` facade to decouple `OrderProcessor` from multiple third-party APIs.

**Pattern 4 — High Cohesion:**
Keep responsibilities focused so that a class does not become overloaded. *Example:* Split a `Report` God Class into `ReportGenerator`, `ReportFormatter`, and `ReportExporter`.

**Pattern 5 — Controller:**
Delegate system events (from UI layer) to a non-UI controller class that represents the use case or system as a whole. *Example:* A `CheckoutController` handles all click events from the checkout screen instead of the GUI directly manipulating business objects.

*(Award 7 marks for any 5 patterns; deduct partial marks if examples are missing or wrong.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** What are **design smells**? Explain any **four** common design smells with their refactoring remedies. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With suitable formulas, explain **Cyclomatic Complexity** as a method-level optimization metric. Compute the cyclomatic complexity of a method that contains **2 if-statements, 1 while-loop, 1 switch with 4 cases, and 1 for-loop**. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Design Smells** are symptoms of poor OO design — structural indicators that the design violates fundamental OO principles such as encapsulation, cohesion, or low coupling. They are *not* bugs; the code may still work correctly. They are *latent defects* — risk amplifiers for future change.

**Smell 1 — God Class (Large Class):**
A single class accumulates too many responsibilities and instance variables. *Symptom:* High WMC, high LCOM, many methods (> 20). *Refactoring:* **Extract Class** to split by concern.

**Smell 2 — Feature Envy:**
A method accesses more features of another class than of its own. *Symptom:* Long parameter list with objects from a single class; body dominated by foreign-class calls. *Refactoring:* **Move Method** to the class whose features are envied.

**Smell 3 — Refused Bequest:**
A subclass inherits from a parent but does not use or need the inherited behavior. *Symptom:* Empty method overrides, "doesn't apply" stubs. *Refactoring:* **Replace Inheritance with Delegation**.

**Smell 4 — Speculative Generality:**
Code is written "just in case" it might be needed later but is never used. *Symptom:* Abstract classes with a single subclass; unused parameters; "future-proof" hooks. *Refactoring:* **Collapse Hierarchy**, **Remove Parameter**, **Inline Class**.

*(Award 7 marks; each smell carries ~1.75 marks. Deduct 0.5 marks per smell if the refactoring remedy is missing or wrong.)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Cyclomatic Complexity**, introduced by Thomas J. McCabe in 1976, measures the number of linearly independent paths through a program's source code. It quantifies the testing effort and error-proneness of a method.

**Formula 1 — Graph-based:**

$$V(G) = E - N + 2P$$

where $E$ = edges, $N$ = nodes, $P$ = connected components in the control flow graph.

**Formula 2 — Decision-based (practical):**

$$V(G) = \text{Number of decision points} + 1$$

Common decision-point count: each `if`, `case`, `for`, `while`, `do-while`, `&&`, `||`, `?:` adds **1**; each `catch` adds **1**.

**Worked Computation:**

Given method with:
- 2 `if` statements → $+2$
- 1 `while` loop → $+1$
- 1 `switch` with 4 cases → $+4$
- 1 `for` loop → $+1$

Total decision points = $2 + 1 + 4 + 1 = 8$

$$V(G) = 8 + 1$$

$$\boxed{V(G) = 9}$$

**Interpretation:**
- $V(G) = 9$ falls in the **simple-to-moderate** range (1–10).
- The method requires a minimum of **9 independent test cases** for branch coverage.
- **Recommendation:** No mandatory refactoring, but monitor for growth.

*(Award 7 marks: 2 for formula, 3 for step-by-step count, 2 for interpretation.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Confusing WMC with V(G):** WMC is *class-level*; V(G) is *method-level*. Mixing them in a calculation is a **2-mark deduction**.
> 2. **Forgetting the LCOM sign rule:** LCOM is defined as $P - Q$ only when $(P - Q) > 0$. Writing a negative LCOM earns **0 marks** for that sub-part.
> 3. **GRASP without examples:** Naming the pattern alone earns partial credit; a concrete class-level example is **mandatory** for full marks in KTU's Part-B valuation.
> 4. **No metric thresholds:** Simply stating "minimize WMC" without giving a target range or referencing the optimization action loses 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Mixing CK and McCabe:** Keep them in separate paragraphs. Examiners explicitly look for **separate, well-labeled sections**.
> 6. **Skipping the re-measurement step:** The optimization pipeline is incomplete without Stage 5 (Re-Measurement). Always close the loop.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Design Optimization (KTU Module 4)**

- 🔹 **Design Optimization** = refining OO design for *quality*, not *functionality*.
- 🔹 Three pillars: **Responsibility Assignment (GRASP)**, **Structural Refinement (Coupling/Cohesion)**, **Quantitative Validation (CK Metrics)**.
- 🔹 Six **CK Metrics**: WMC, DIT, NOC, CBO, RFC, LCOM — remember the acronym **"WDRNCL"**.
- 🔹 **All CK metrics should be minimized** (with NOC and DIT having practical moderate ranges).
- 🔹 **CBO** = coupled classes count (use + used-by).
- 🔹 **RFC** = response set cardinality.
- 🔹 **LCOM** = $P - Q$ if positive, else $0$.
- 🔹 **WMC** = sum of method cyclomatic complexities.
- 🔹 **McCabe's V(G)** = decision points + 1 (method-level).
- 🔹 **Maintainability Index** is positively affected by cohesion, negatively by coupling.
- 🔹 **Design smells** to know: *God Class, Feature Envy, Refused Bequest, Speculative Generality, Inappropriate Intimacy, Duplicated Code, Long Method, Shotgun Surgery*.
- 🔹 **GRASP patterns** (9 total): *Information Expert, Creator, Low Coupling, High Cohesion, Controller, Polymorphism, Pure Fabrication, Indirection, Protected Variations*.
- 🔹 The **optimization pipeline** has 5 stages: *Measure → Identify → Heuristic → Refactor → Re-validate*.
- 🔹 Always pair a **qualitative heuristic** with a **quantitative metric** when answering KTU long-answer questions.
- 🔹 Final answer should close the loop: *measure → refactor → re-measure → confirm improvement*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Implementation of Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCNT604 - OBJECT ORIENTED MODELING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module - 4
> - **Topic:** Implementation of Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of **Object Oriented Modeling and Design (OOMD)**, **Implementation of Control** refers to the systematic translation of dynamic behavioral models — primarily **UML State Machine Diagrams (Statecharts)** and **Activity Diagrams** — into executable, object-oriented program code. Control, in this sense, is the mechanism that governs:

- **Which object is allowed to perform an action at a given moment** (active state),
- **How the system reacts to internal or external events** (event dispatch),
- **Under what preconditions a transition can occur** (guard evaluation), and
- **What side effects accompany a state change** (action execution).

The KTU 2024 Scheme positions *Implementation of Control* as a bridge between the **dynamic view** of analysis/design (Module 3 — Interaction & State Modeling) and the **physical view** of implementation (Module 5 — Mapping Design to Code).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PBCNT604 – Module 4):**
> The implementation of control deals with realizing the dynamic behavior captured in state machines through well-defined OO constructs such as the **State Pattern (GoF)**, **switch/case dispatch tables**, **event-driven control objects**, and **guard-condition evaluation logic**. The student is expected to *map* UML state diagrams to Java/C++/Python class structures.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Vending Machine" Intuition

Imagine a **coffee vending machine**. From the outside it looks static, but internally it is *always* in one of several well-defined states: `Idle → CoinInserted → BeverageSelected → Dispensing → OutOfOrder`. Each state dictates what buttons respond and what the display shows. The *control logic* is the brain that says:

> *"If I am in 'Idle' and the event 'coinInserted' occurs, transition to 'CoinInserted' and update the display."*

Now think of two extreme ways to build that brain:
- **Naïve way:** Stuff every possible condition into one giant `if-else` ladder inside a single class. Works for 3 states, collapses at 30.
- **Object-Oriented way (State Pattern):** Each state is its **own object** (a `IdleState` class, a `DispensingState` class). The main machine ("Context") simply holds a reference to the *current* state object and delegates the work. Adding a new state means adding a new class — not editing a 500-line `switch` block.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Takeaway:** *Implementation of Control* is the art of converting a *picture* of behavior (statechart) into a *program* of behavior (classes) without losing readability, extensibility, or correctness.

### 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Meaning | Bold Notation |
|---|---|---|
| **State** | A condition of an object during its life | `S = {s₁, s₂, …, sₙ}` |
| **Event** | A notable occurrence that may trigger a transition | `E = {e₁, e₂, …, eₘ}` |
| **Guard** | Boolean condition that must be true for a transition to fire | `g : S × E → {True, False}` |
| **Action** | Atomic computation executed during a transition | `α : T → Operations` |
| **Transition** | A 5-tuple relationship | `T = ⟨S_src, E, g, α, S_target⟩` |
| **Context** | The object whose behavior is state-dependent | `C : has currentState : State` |

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Canonical Implementation Strategies

Object-oriented literature (Gamma et al., *Design Patterns*, 1994; Booch et al., *OOA&D with Applications*) recognizes **three principal strategies** to implement state-based control. The KTU 2024 examiner expects the student to know all three and pick the right one for a given scenario.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strategy Selection Rule of Thumb (KTU-Favorite):**
> - **< 4 states, no inheritance** → *Switch/Case Implementation*
> - **Many states, transitions change often, polymorphism available** → *State Pattern*
> - **State behavior driven by data tables (e.g., protocols, parsers)** → *Table-Driven Implementation*

#### 2.1.1 Strategy 1 — The **Switch/Case (Naïve / Procedural) Implementation**

The entire state machine is encoded inside a single method of the Context class. A loop listens for events, and a `switch` (or chained `if-else`) dispatches them.

**Operational Logic (in plain steps):**

1. Define an `enum` or constant set listing all states: `IDLE, INSERTED, SELECTED, DISPENSING, OUT_OF_ORDER`.
2. Define an `enum` for all events: `COIN, SELECT, DISPENSE, ERROR, REFILL`.
3. Maintain a field `currentState` in the Context.
4. On every event, the `handleEvent(e)` method:
   a. Reads `currentState`.
   b. Switches on it.
   c. For each case, evaluates the guard.
   d. Performs the action.
   e. Updates `currentState` to the target state.

**Pros:** Simple, fast, easy to write initially.  
**Cons:** Violates **Open/Closed Principle** — every new state requires editing the switch.

#### 2.1.2 Strategy 2 — The **State Pattern (GoF Behavioural Pattern)**

The **State Pattern** is the textbook OO answer to dynamic control. It promotes *every state to a full-blown class*, each implementing a common `State` interface.

**Participants (from GoF):**

| Role | Responsibility |
|---|---|
| **Context** | Maintains a reference to a `State` object; forwards requests. |
| **State (interface)** | Declares the event-handling interface for every state. |
| **ConcreteState subclasses** | Implement state-specific behavior; know how to transition. |

**Operational Logic:**

1. Context is initialized with an `initialState` object.
2. An event arrives at Context, e.g., `context.request(event)`.
3. Context delegates: `currentState.handle(event)`.
4. The ConcreteState object performs the action **and** returns the next state.
5. Context updates its pointer: `this.state = nextState`.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the State Pattern is Open/Closed-Compliant:** Adding a new state `MaintenanceState` requires only a new class implementing the `State` interface — *no edits* to Context or to any existing ConcreteState. This is the gold standard KTU answer for "extensibility" questions.

#### 2.1.3 Strategy 3 — The **Table-Driven Implementation**

A **transition table** is used as data, not code. Rows represent (current_state, event) pairs; columns hold the *next state*, the *guard*, and the *action reference*. A generic interpreter scans the table.

**Structure of the Table:**

| Current State | Event | Guard | Action | Next State |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Idle | coinInserted | amount ≥ 0 | updateBalance | Inserted |
| Inserted | beverageSelected | balance ≥ price | deductBalance | Selected |
| Selected | dispense | stock > 0 | pourBeverage | Dispensing |
| Dispensing | done | always | resetBalance | Idle |
| * | error | always | logError | OutOfOrder |

**Pros:** Behaviour is now *data*, easily portable, re-loadable, even editable at runtime.  
**Cons:** Slower (table lookups), less type-safe in statically-typed languages.

### 2.2 The KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following symbols are the *only* ones the KTU 2024 examiner will expect in derivations. Memorize them.

$$
S = \{s_1, s_2, \dots, s_n\} \quad \text{(finite, non-empty set of states)}
$$

$$
E = \{e_1, e_2, \dots, e_m\} \quad \text{(set of events)}
$$

$$
T \subseteq S \times E \times G \times A \quad \text{(transition relation)}
$$

$$
\delta : S \times E \xrightarrow{g} S \quad \text{(next-state function with guard } g \in G \text{)}
$$

$$
\lambda : T \rightarrow A \quad \text{(action-mapping function)}
$$

$$
\sigma_0 \in S \quad \text{(initial state)}
$$

$$
\Sigma = (S,\; E,\; \delta,\; \sigma_0,\; \lambda) \quad \text{(complete state machine tuple)}
$$

| Symbol | Meaning | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|
| $n = \vert S \vert$ | Number of states | Determines `switch` size or state-class count |
| $m = \vert E \vert$ | Number of events | Determines `handleEvent` cases |
| $t = \vert T \vert$ | Number of transitions | Row count in transition table |
| $\delta$ | Determinism indicator | Deterministic ⇒ exactly one next state per (s, e) |

**Real-World Engineering Utility:**

- **Embedded Systems (Automotive ECU):** State Pattern governs ignition states `OFF → ACC → ON → START → RUN`.
- **Network Protocol Stacks (TCP, HTTP):** Table-driven FSMs parse packet headers.
- **Compilers:** Switch/case lexers tokenize input.
- **Robotics:** Hierarchical state machines drive robot *mode* switching (walk → sit → recover).

### 2.3 Determinism, Reachability, and Deadlock — The Three Gotchas

> [!WARNING]
> **The Three Implementation Traps KTU Tests Every Year:**
> 1. **Non-determinism** — Two transitions leave the same state on the same event with no guards. Pick **one** or attach *priorities*.
> 2. **Unreachable states** — A state with no incoming transition (orphan) is dead code. Verify with *reachability analysis* starting from $\sigma_0$.
> 3. **Deadlocks** — A state with no outgoing transition for an event that *can* occur. Provide an *error* or *self-transition* guard.
