## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation A — Mapping a UML Statechart to Switch/Case Code

We will derive the Java skeleton for a `VendingMachine` whose statechart is given in the analysis above.

**Step 1 — Declare the state and event enums.**

```java
public enum State    { IDLE, COIN_INSERTED, BEVERAGE_SELECTED,
                       DISPENSING, OUT_OF_ORDER }
public enum Event    { COIN, SELECT, DISPENSE, DONE, ERROR }
```

**Step 2 — Declare Context fields.**

```java
private State currentState = State.IDLE;   // σ₀ initial state
private double balance = 0.0;
private int    stock   = 10;
```

**Step 3 — Build the dispatch function `handleEvent(e)`.**

This is the algebraic heart of the switch/case approach. We map every ordered pair $(s_i, e_j)$ to a unique next state using the function $\delta$:

$$
\delta(s_i, e_j) =
\begin{cases}
\text{COIN\_INSERTED}, & \text{if } s_i = \text{IDLE} \;\land\; e_j = \text{COIN} \\
\text{BEVERAGE\_SELECTED}, & \text{if } s_i = \text{COIN\_INSERTED} \;\land\; e_j = \text{SELECT} \;\land\; g_{ij} \\
\text{DISPENSING}, & \text{if } s_i = \text{BEVERAGE\_SELECTED} \;\land\; e_j = \text{DISPENSE} \\
\text{IDLE}, & \text{if } s_i = \text{DISPENSING} \;\land\; e_j = \text{DONE} \\
\text{OUT\_OF\_ORDER}, & \text{if } e_j = \text{ERROR} \\
s_i, & \text{otherwise (self-loop / no-op)}
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 4 — Translate $\delta$ literally into Java.**

```java
public void handleEvent(Event e) {
    switch (currentState) {
        case IDLE:
            if (e == Event.COIN) { balance += 10; currentState = State.COIN_INSERTED; }
            break;
        case COIN_INSERTED:
            if (e == Event.SELECT) {                        // guard g evaluated
                if (balance >= priceOf(e)) {                // guard Boolean
                    balance -= priceOf(e);
                    currentState = State.BEVERAGE_SELECTED;
                }
            }
            break;
        case BEVERAGE_SELECTED:
            if (e == Event.DISPENSE) { currentState = State.DISPENSING; }
            break;
        case DISPENSING:
            if (e == Event.DONE) {
                stock--;
                currentState = State.IDLE;
            }
            break;
    }
    if (e == Event.ERROR) currentState = State.OUT_OF_ORDER;   // universal transition
}
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic Recap:** The four cases in the `switch` mirror the four *non-trivial rows* of $\delta$. The universal `ERROR` row is implemented *outside* the switch because it is a **cross-cutting transition** valid in *every* state.

### 3.2 Derivation B — Mapping the Same Statechart to the State Pattern

**Step 1 — Define the `State` interface (one method per event).**

```java
public interface State {
    void insertCoin();      // event COIN
    void selectBeverage();  // event SELECT
    void dispense();        // event DISPENSE
    void done();            // event DONE
    void error();           // event ERROR
}
```

**Step 2 — Implement the `Context` (VendingMachine).**

```java
public class VendingMachine {
    private State state = new IdleState();   // σ₀
    public void setState(State s) { this.state = s; }
    public void insertCoin()    { state.insertCoin(); }
    public void selectBeverage(){ state.selectBeverage(); }
    public void dispense()      { state.dispense(); }
    public void done()          { state.done(); }
    public void error()         { state.error(); }
}
```

**Step 3 — Implement one ConcreteState subclass completely (`IdleState`).**

```java
public class IdleState implements State {
    public void insertCoin()    { /* increment balance */; ctx.setState(new CoinInsertedState()); }
    public void selectBeverage(){ /* log: cannot select before coin */ }
    public void dispense()      { /* log: no beverage selected */ }
    public void done()          { /* no-op */ }
    public void error()         { ctx.setState(new OutOfOrderState()); }
}
```

The other four ConcreteStates (`CoinInsertedState`, `BeverageSelectedState`, `DispensingState`, `OutOfOrderState`) follow the identical skeleton — each *returns* control by setting the Context to the **appropriate next state object**, exactly as $\delta$ dictates.

### 3.3 Derivation C — Analytical Comparison Matrix (the "Which to Choose" Table)

Let $\text{CC}_{\text{Switch}}$ = cyclomatic complexity of the switch/case implementation, and $\text{CC}_{\text{State}} = 1$ per ConcreteState (since each method is linear).

$$
\text{CC}_{\text{Switch}} \;=\; t + 1
$$

where $t = \vert T \vert$ is the number of transitions. The State Pattern distributes this complexity:

$$
\text{CC}_{\text{State}} \;=\; \frac{t}{n} + 1
$$

| Criterion | Switch/Case | State Pattern | Table-Driven |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cyclomatic complexity (Context)** | $t + 1$ — grows fast | $1$ — constant | $1$ — constant |
| **Adding a new state** | Edit `switch` (violates OCP) | Add new class (OCP ✓) | Add new row (data only) |
| **Performance** | Fastest (jump table) | Virtual call overhead | Hash-lookup overhead |
| **Readability** | Poor beyond 4 states | Excellent | Good if table is small |
| **Best for** | Tiny FSMs, embedded | Complex product FSMs | Protocol / parser FSMs |
| **KTU weightage** | 2–3 marks definition | **5–7 marks full diagram + code** | 3 marks table sketch |

### 3.4 Derivation D — Reachability Check Algorithm (Breadth-First)

To verify a statechart has **no unreachable states**, KTU 2024 expects a BFS/DFS walk from $\sigma_0$:

$$
\text{Reachable}(S) \;=\; \text{BFS}\Bigl(\{\sigma_0\},\; \bigcup_{s \in \text{frontier}} \delta(s, E)\Bigr)
$$

Any $s \in S \setminus \text{Reachable}(S)$ is a **dead state** to be removed or connected via an *initialization* transition.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram 1 — UML State Machine of the Vending Machine (Source Model)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> CoinInserted : coinInserted / addBalance
    CoinInserted --> BeverageSelected : select / validateBalance [balance >= price]
    CoinInserted --> CoinInserted : coinInserted / addBalance
    BeverageSelected --> Dispensing : dispense [stock > 0] / decrementStock
    Dispensing --> Idle : done / resetBalance
    Idle --> OutOfOrder : error / logError
    CoinInserted --> OutOfOrder : error / logError
    BeverageSelected --> OutOfOrder : error / logError
    Dispensing --> OutOfOrder : error / logError
    OutOfOrder --> [*]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this in KTU exams:**
> - *Node labels* = states.
> - *Edge label* `event / action` = trigger and effect.
> - *Edge label* `event [guard] / action` = guarded transition.
> - The `[*]` symbols denote the **initial pseudostate** and the **final state**.

### 4.2 Diagram 2 — Class Diagram for the State Pattern Implementation

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Context {
        -State currentState
        +setState(State s)
        +request() void
    }
    class State {
        <<interface>>
        +handleEvent(Event e) void
    }
    class ConcreteStateA {
        +handleEvent(Event e) void
    }
    class ConcreteStateB {
        +handleEvent(Event e) void
    }
    class ConcreteStateC {
        +handleEvent(Event e) void
    }
    Context o-- State : holds pointer to
    State <|.. ConcreteStateA
    State <|.. ConcreteStateB
    State <|.. ConcreteStateC
    ConcreteStateA ..> Context : setState(next)
    ConcreteStateB ..> Context : setState(next)
    ConcreteStateC ..> Context : setState(next)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Reading:** Notice the **bidirectional arrow** — Context *holds* a State, and a State *writes back* to Context via `setState`. This two-way coupling is the signature of the GoF State Pattern.

### 4.3 Diagram 3 — Block-Level Functional Architecture (Implementation Pipeline)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[UML Statechart Source] --> B[Parse States and Events]
    B --> C{Choose Strategy}
    C -- Few states --> D[Switch or Case Code Gen]
    C -- Many states and polymorphic --> E[State Pattern Skeleton]
    C -- Data driven --> F[Transition Table and Interpreter]
    D --> G[Compile and Test]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H{All events handled?}
    H -- No --> I[Add transition or guard]
    I --> G
    H -- Yes --> J[Validated Control Implementation]
```

### 4.4 Diagram 4 — Sequence of a State Transition (Runtime View)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Client
    participant Context
    participant CurrentState
    participant NextState
    Client->>Context: handleEvent(COIN)
    Context->>CurrentState: onCoin()
    CurrentState->>CurrentState: performAction()
    CurrentState->>Context: setState(NextState)
    Context->>NextState: store reference
    Note over Context,NextState: Next event will be delegated to NextState
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2-Mark / 3-Mark Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Paper Style]**

**Q1. [3 Marks]** *Define the term "Implementation of Control" in Object Oriented Modeling. List the three main strategies used to implement state-based control.*

**Model Answer:**
*Implementation of Control* is the OO design activity that translates dynamic behavioural models — particularly UML **state machine diagrams** — into executable code. It decides how an object reacts to events, evaluates guards, executes actions, and changes its state.

The three canonical strategies are:

1. **Switch/Case (Procedural) Implementation** — entire FSM encoded in a `switch` statement.
2. **State Pattern (GoF)** — each state is a separate class implementing a common interface; the Context delegates.
3. **Table-Driven Implementation** — state transitions stored as data in a transition table, interpreted by a generic engine.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Paper Style]**

**Q2. [3 Marks]** *What is a "guard" in a state machine? How is it represented in (a) a statechart, and (b) Java code using the State Pattern?*

**Model Answer:**
A **guard** is a Boolean condition placed on a transition. The transition fires *only* if the event occurs *and* the guard evaluates to `true`.

- **(a) In a statechart:** the guard is written inside square brackets on the transition arrow, e.g., `select [balance >= price] / deductBalance`.
- **(b) In Java (State Pattern):** the guard appears as a Boolean expression inside an `if` statement within the ConcreteState's handler method, e.g.,
```java
if (balance >= price) {
    balance -= price;
    context.setState(new BeverageSelectedState());
}
```

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – July 2024, Adapted from Past Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Explain the State Pattern (GoF) with its participants. Draw the class diagram and describe how a state transition is achieved at runtime.*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *Consider an "Online Order" system with the following states: `New → Confirmed → Packed → Shipped → Delivered`. The system must also handle a `Cancelled` state reachable from `New` and `Confirmed` only. Design a statechart, then write the State Pattern skeleton in Java for the `Confirmed` and `Shipped` states.*

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

The **State Pattern** is a behavioural design pattern from *Gamma et al. (1994)* that allows an object to alter its behaviour when its internal state changes — the object *appears* to change its class.

**Participants:**

1. **Context** — defines the interface of interest to clients; maintains an instance of a ConcreteState subclass that defines the current state.
2. **State** — an interface (or abstract class) that encapsulates the state-dependent behaviour.
3. **ConcreteState subclasses** — each implements state-specific behaviour for every event.

**Class Diagram:** *(refer to Section 4.2 of these notes for the canonical class diagram; reproduce it here in your answer script)*

**Runtime Transition (sequence):**

1. The client invokes a method on the Context, e.g., `context.request()`.
2. Context delegates the call to its current `State` object: `currentState.handle()`.
3. The ConcreteState performs the action and decides the next state.
4. It calls `context.setState(newState)` to flip the Context's pointer.
5. The *next* call on Context will reach the *new* State object — giving the illusion that the Context itself "became" a different class.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Statechart (textual):**

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> New
    New --> Confirmed : confirm / generateInvoice
    New --> Cancelled : cancel / refundInitiated
    Confirmed --> Packed : pack / updateInventory
    Confirmed --> Cancelled : cancel / refundInitiated
    Packed --> Shipped : ship / sendTracking
    Shipped --> Delivered : deliver / closeOrder
    Delivered --> [*]
    Cancelled --> [*]
```

**State interface (Java):**

```java
public interface OrderState {
    void confirm(OrderContext ctx);
    void pack(OrderContext ctx);
    void ship(OrderContext ctx);
    void deliver(OrderContext ctx);
    void cancel(OrderContext ctx);
}
```

**`ConfirmedState` implementation:**

```java
public class ConfirmedState implements OrderState {
    public void confirm(OrderContext ctx) {
        System.out.println("Already confirmed.");
    }
    public void pack(OrderContext ctx) {
        System.out.println("Packing the order...");
        ctx.setState(new PackedState());             // δ(Confirmed, pack) = Packed
    }
    public void ship(OrderContext ctx) {
        System.out.println("Cannot ship before packing.");
    }
    public void deliver(OrderContext ctx) {
        System.out.println("Cannot deliver before shipping.");
    }
    public void cancel(OrderContext ctx) {
        System.out.println("Cancelling confirmed order, refunding...");
        ctx.setState(new CancelledState());           // δ(Confirmed, cancel) = Cancelled
    }
}
```

**`ShippedState` implementation:**

```java
public class ShippedState implements OrderState {
    public void confirm(OrderContext ctx) { System.out.println("Already past confirmed."); }
    public void pack(OrderContext ctx)    { System.out.println("Already packed."); }
    public void ship(OrderContext ctx)    { System.out.println("Already shipped."); }
    public void deliver(OrderContext ctx) {
        System.out.println("Delivered to customer.");
        ctx.setState(new DeliveredState());           // δ(Shipped, deliver) = Delivered
    }
    public void cancel(OrderContext ctx)  {
        System.out.println("Cannot cancel after shipping.");
    }
}
```

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted from Past Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Compare and contrast the three strategies for implementing state-based control (Switch/Case, State Pattern, Table-Driven) using a 2-axis matrix: "Extensibility" vs "Performance". Justify which strategy is most suitable for (i) a microwave-oven controller, (ii) a TCP packet parser.*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *For the TCP packet parser, sketch a transition table that handles events `SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK, FIN, RST` and states `CLOSED, LISTEN, SYN_RCVD, ESTABLISHED, FIN_WAIT_1, CLOSED_AGAIN`. Show how the table is interpreted by a generic event loop.*

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

| Strategy | Extensibility | Performance | Memory Footprint |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Switch/Case** | **Low** (must edit switch for every new state) | **Highest** (compiler optimizes to jump table) | Lowest |
| **State Pattern** | **Highest** (add a new class, OCP ✓) | Medium (virtual dispatch per event) | Highest (one object per state) |
| **Table-Driven** | High (add a row) | Medium (hash/array lookup) | Medium |

**Justifications:**

- **(i) Microwave-Oven Controller:** A microwave has only ~4 states (`Idle, Cooking, Paused, Done`) and **hard real-time constraints** (must respond within microseconds). → **Choose Switch/Case** for maximum performance and minimum RAM.
- **(ii) TCP Packet Parser:** TCP is a complex protocol with dozens of states, subject to frequent extension (TLS handshakes, QUIC variants), but the *event set* is small and the *transition set* is large. The state logic benefits from being **data-driven and reloadable**. → **Choose Table-Driven.**

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Transition Table for TCP-Lite:**

| Current State | Event | Guard | Action | Next State |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| CLOSED | SYN | — | openSocket | LISTEN |
| LISTEN | SYN | — | sendSYNACK | SYN_RCVD |
| SYN_RCVD | ACK | seqValid | markOpen | ESTABLISHED |
| ESTABLISHED | FIN | — | sendACK | FIN_WAIT_1 |
| FIN_WAIT_1 | FIN | — | sendACK, closeSocket | CLOSED_AGAIN |
| ESTABLISHED | RST | — | closeSocket, logError | CLOSED_AGAIN |
| CLOSED_AGAIN | SYN | — | openSocket | LISTEN |
| * | RST | — | closeSocket | CLOSED_AGAIN |

**Generic Event Loop (Java sketch):**

```java
public class TCPEngine {
    private State current = State.CLOSED;
    public void onEvent(Event e, Map<String,Object> ctx) {
        for (TransitionRow row : TABLE) {
            if (row.current == current && row.event == e) {
                if (evalGuard(row.guard, ctx)) {           // evaluate guard
                    row.action.execute(ctx);               // perform action α
                    current = row.nextState;               // apply δ
                    return;
                }
            }
        }
        System.out.println("Unhandled: " + current + " / " + e);
    }
}
```

The loop scans the table (or uses a `(state,event) → row` map for O(1) lookup), evaluates the guard, runs the action, and updates the current state — *all data, no recompilation needed* to add a new transition.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks on "Implementation of Control" Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing *State Pattern* with *Strategy Pattern*.** Both delegate to a polymorphic object, but the **State Pattern flips its own pointer** during the call; the Strategy is *injected* and does not change. Examiners **deduct 1–2 marks** for this mix-up.
> 2. **Forgetting the `setState` call back to Context.** A ConcreteState that performs the action but never tells Context about the next state leaves the FSM frozen. Always close the loop.
> 3. **Omitting the Event/Guard/Action triple on transitions.** UML arrow labels must read `event [guard] / action`; missing *any one* is a 1-mark cut.
> 4. **Drawing the State Pattern class diagram with only Context → State, not State → Context.** The back-arrow is mandatory.
> 5. **Writing procedural `if-else` in the answer and then labelling it "State Pattern".** The marker can tell. The signature of the pattern is the *polymorphic delegation*, not the keyword `interface`.
> 6. **In the table-driven answer, forgetting the *wildcard* row `* / RST / closeSocket` for global transitions.** Real protocols always have these — and KTU rewards them.
> 7. **Not stating the initial state $\sigma_0$ explicitly.** Always write *"initial state = IDLE"* before the switch.
> 8. **Treating `final` states as terminal forever.** In OO, a `final` state is just another ConcreteState — usually a `Closed` or `OutOfOrder` object that can be re-entered.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Pin This Before the Exam):**

- ☐ **Implementation of Control = mapping state machine to code.** Three canonical strategies: **Switch/Case, State Pattern, Table-Driven.**
- ☐ **The State Pattern has 4 participants:** Context, State interface, ≥2 ConcreteStates, plus the back-reference `setState`.
- ☐ **A transition is a 5-tuple** `⟨S_src, E, guard, action, S_target⟩` — know all five pieces.
- ☐ **The dispatch function** $\delta : S \times E \xrightarrow{g} S$ is the *algebraic* core of every FSM.
- ☐ **Initial state $\sigma_0$** must always be declared; *final* states in UML are just *one more* ConcreteState in OO.
- ☐ **Determinism rule:** For any $(s, e)$ pair with guard `true`, there must be **exactly one** next state.
- ☐ **Reachability check** = BFS from $\sigma_0$; eliminate orphan states.
- ☐ **Open/Closed Principle (OCP):** Switch/Case *violates* it; State Pattern *honors* it; Table-Driven *side-steps* it.
- ☐ **Three real-world exemplars to remember:** Microwave (Switch), ECU ignition (State Pattern), TCP parser (Table-Driven).
- ☐ **Performance ranking:** Switch/Case > Table-Driven > State Pattern (virtual call overhead).
- ☐ **Extensibility ranking:** State Pattern ≥ Table-Driven > Switch/Case.
- ☐ **KTU 2024 examiner's hot-button words:** "delegate", "polymorphism", "Open/Closed", "next-state function $\delta$", "guard evaluation" — use them in every answer.
- ☐ **Common mistakes:** (1) forgetting the back-pointer to Context, (2) confusing State and Strategy, (3) writing procedural `if-else` and labelling it State Pattern, (4) omitting the wildcard `*` row in tables, (5) not specifying the initial state explicitly.

> **End of Module 4 — Implementation of Control Notes**
> *Prepared in accordance with the KTU 2024 Scheme Outcome-Based Education framework.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Adjustment of Inheritance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCNT604 - OBJECT ORIENTED MODELING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module - 4
> - **Topic:** Adjustment of Inheritance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Adjustment of Inheritance** is the systematic refinement of the generalization/specialization hierarchy (class inheritance tree) in an Object-Oriented model so that the structure correctly reflects the **"is-a"** relationship, eliminates redundancy, resolves conflicts, and supports polymorphism. It is applied during the **System Design (Object Design) phase** after the initial class diagram has been drafted from the Analysis phase.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, adjustment of inheritance deals with restructuring the class diagram along five canonical axes:

1. **Re-rooting** the inheritance hierarchy (changing the root class).
2. **Moving attributes/operations up** the hierarchy (generalization).
3. **Moving attributes/operations down** the hierarchy (specialization / distribution).
4. **Eliminating multiple inheritance** ambiguities using aggregation or interfaces.
5. **Handling cycles and multiple classification** problems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – PBCNT604, Module 4):**
> Adjustment of inheritance is treated as a *corrective activity* applied to the class diagram produced during analysis. It guarantees the model satisfies the *substitutability* principle (Liskov Substitution Principle) and the *single inheritance* constraint in languages like Java and C++.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine a **family tree** drawn on a large sheet of paper. After interviewing family members, you discover some mistakes:

- A child has been placed under the wrong parent → **Re-rooting**.
- A surname (attribute) wrongly shown only on one child, but actually belongs to all grandchildren → **Move attribute up**.
- An address wrongly put on the grandparent, but it really only applies to one parent → **Move attribute down**.
- A child appears under two parents, but the inheritance is conflicting → **Resolve multiple inheritance**.

**Inheritance adjustment is exactly this "correcting the family tree" exercise for classes.** Just as a corrected family tree preserves accurate ancestry, an adjusted inheritance hierarchy preserves accurate behavioral and structural semantics.

## 1.3 The Need for Adjustment — Real Engineering Trigger Points

| Trigger Situation | Symptom in Class Diagram | Adjustment Required |
|---|---|---|
| Two subclasses share a common attribute | Attribute duplication | Move attribute **up** to common superclass |
| Superclass has an attribute used by only one subclass | Redundant data in other subclasses | Move attribute **down** |
| Root class is overly abstract/has no clear semantics | Hierarchy starts at wrong node | **Re-rooting** |
| Class inherits from two parents with conflicting methods | Diamond problem | Use **interface/aggregation** |
| A class inherits behavior it does not need | Violation of "is-a" | Convert to **aggregation** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology Box**
> - **Subclass (Child / Derived Class):** Inherits from another class.
> - **Superclass (Parent / Base Class):** Being inherited from.
> - **Substitutability:** A subclass instance must be usable wherever a superclass instance is expected (Liskov, 1987).
> - **Generalization:** Combining similar classes into a higher-level class (move up).
> - **Specialization:** Creating subclasses that refine a superclass (move down).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Canonical Adjustments

### 2.1.1 Re-Rooting the Inheritance Hierarchy

**Definition:** Changing the base (root) class of an existing inheritance tree when a more appropriate candidate is identified.

**Why needed:** Sometimes the topmost class in the existing model is too generic (e.g., *Thing*, *Object*) and provides no useful shared state or behavior. A better root class is identified that captures the *true commonality* of all subclasses.

**Example:** Hierarchy `Animal → Dog, Cat, Cow` may be re-rooted to `LivingBeing → Animal → Dog, Cat, Cow` if *LivingBeing* better expresses the top-level common features (respiration, lifecycle).

**How to do it:**
1. Identify the *true* topmost common superclass.
2. Introduce the new root.
3. Migrate any attributes/operations previously placed in the old root up to the new root.
4. Update all association and dependency references.

### 2.1.2 Moving Attributes and Operations Up (Generalization / Pull-Up)

**Definition:** When two or more sibling subclasses share the same attribute or operation, hoist it into their common superclass.

**Why needed:** Eliminates **duplication**, ensures **uniform semantics**, and supports **polymorphic dispatch**.

**Steps:**
1. Detect duplication across siblings.
2. Verify the attribute/operation is meaningful for *all* subclasses (not just some).
3. Remove from subclasses, add to superclass.
4. Verify that the *is-a* test still holds for all subclasses with respect to the enriched superclass.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pull-Up Decision Rule (Bruegge & Dutoit):**
> Move a feature up *only* if it applies to **every** subclass. If it applies to only some, either split the superclass (generalization) or keep it in the specific subclass.

### 2.1.3 Moving Attributes and Operations Down (Specialization / Push-Down)

**Definition:** When a feature exists in a superclass but is meaningful for only one (or a subset of) subclasses, it is pushed down to the relevant subclass(es).

**Why needed:** Prevents **subclasses from carrying irrelevant state/behavior** (violates interface minimization and Liskov).

**Steps:**
1. Identify feature in superclass that is unused or inappropriate for a subclass.
2. Verify that the feature still has a meaningful "is-a" with respect to the target subclass.
3. Remove from superclass, add to the target subclass.
4. Repeat for each affected subclass.

### 2.1.4 Eliminating Multiple Inheritance

**Definition:** Resolving situations where a class inherits from **more than one** parent class, leading to ambiguity, code duplication, or language-level conflicts.

**Why needed:** Many OO languages (Java, C#) forbid multiple class inheritance. Even where allowed (C++), the **diamond problem** causes method-resolution ambiguity.

**Resolution Strategies:**

| Strategy | When to Use | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|
| **Replace inheritance with delegation (aggregation)** | Subclass is *not* a strict *is-a* of the second parent | Loses polymorphism with that parent |
| **Multiple inheritance via interfaces** | Subclass only needs *contract* of second parent, not state | Loses code reuse of state |
| **Create an intermediate abstract class** | Both parents share a common abstract root | Restructures hierarchy more deeply |
| **Flatten hierarchy** | Both parents are really peers, not co-parents | May duplicate code |

### 2.1.5 Handling Cycles in Inheritance

**Definition:** Detecting and removing cycles (circular inheritance) where Class A inherits from B, B from C, and C from A.

**Why needed:** Acyclic inheritance is a **hard correctness constraint** in object models. Cycles break type substitutability, cause infinite type-resolution at compile time, and violate the well-formedness rules of UML.

**Resolution:** Break the cycle by converting one of the *is-a* links into an *has-a* (aggregation/association) link.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

| # | Adjustment | Trigger | Action | UML Operator |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Re-rooting | Wrong top-level class | Change base node | Generalization arrow redrawn |
| 2 | Move up (Pull-up) | Duplicate feature in siblings | Hoist to common ancestor | Modify parent class |
| 3 | Move down (Push-down) | Irrelevant feature in parent | Push to specific subclass | Modify child class |
| 4 | Eliminate multiple inheritance | Diamond/ambiguity | Use interface or aggregation | Replace «inherit» with «realize» or «association» |
| 5 | Break cycle | Circular dependency | Convert one link to association | Change arrow type |
| 6 | Selective inheritance | Don't need all parent features | Convert to aggregation | Re-model relationship |
| 7 | Restructuring | Mismatched abstraction levels | Apply generalization/specialization iteratively | Multiple iterations |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Compiler/Type System Design:** Inheritance cycles are detected by topological sort over the class graph.
- **Database Mapping (ORM):** JPA/Hibernate uses *JOINED*, *SINGLE_TABLE*, and *TABLE_PER_CLASS* strategies — these directly correspond to *push-down* and *pull-up* decisions in OOMD.
- **Microservices / Domain-Driven Design:** Bounded contexts often demand *flattening* deep inheritance hierarchies, which is an adjustment activity.
- **GUI Frameworks (JavaFX, .NET WPF):** Control class hierarchies are routinely *re-rooted* (e.g., moving from `Component` to `Node` root) for better design.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Adjustment Procedure & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Master Adjustment Algorithm (KTU High-Yield Procedure)

The following stepwise procedure is the canonical algorithm examined in KTU papers. Memorize and reproduce it exactly when a class-diagram adjustment question is asked.

**Step 1 — Validate the existing class diagram.**
- Verify no cycles exist.
- Verify single-inheritance (or justified multiple inheritance).
- Verify every subclass passes the *is-a* test against its superclass.

**Step 2 — Detect redundancy.**
- For every pair of sibling classes, list their attributes and operations.
- If a feature appears in *all* siblings → candidate for **pull-up**.
- If a feature appears in *only one* sibling but also in the parent → candidate for **push-down**.

**Step 3 — Apply pull-up.**
- Move duplicated feature to lowest common ancestor (LCA) that contains *all* the affected siblings.

**Step 4 — Apply push-down.**
- For each subclass that does not need a parent feature, move the feature down to the subclass(es) that need it.

**Step 5 — Resolve multiple inheritance.**
- If a class has two superclasses and they are unrelated: introduce a common superclass (generalization) or convert to aggregation.
- If the second superclass is a role, use an interface («interface») realization.

**Step 6 — Re-root if necessary.**
- Identify the true topmost abstraction. Re-route the generalization arrow from this new root.

**Step 7 — Re-validate.**
- Repeat steps 1–6 until no more adjustments are needed (fixed-point reached).

## 3.2 Worked Example — Step-by-Step Adjustment of a Vehicle Hierarchy

Consider the **initial (unadjusted) class diagram** of a vehicle rental system:

```
                    Vehicle
                   /   |   \
                  /    |    \
              Car    Truck   Bus
                \      |     /
                 \     |    /
                InsurancePolicy
```

**Issue 1:** `InsurancePolicy` is multiplied-inherited by all three subclasses, creating a diamond and a non-is-a relationship.
**Issue 2:** `Car` and `Truck` both have attribute `numberOfAxles` (duplication).
**Issue 3:** Attribute `seatingCapacity` is wrongly on `Vehicle`, but is meaningful only for `Bus` and `Car`.
**Issue 4:** The root `Vehicle` is appropriate — no re-rooting needed.

### Step-by-Step Adjustment

**Step A — Eliminate multiple inheritance of InsurancePolicy.**
A vehicle is not a kind of insurance policy. The relationship should be *has-a*, not *is-a*.
- Remove the inheritance from `InsurancePolicy`.
- Replace with an **aggregation**: `Vehicle ◇— InsurancePolicy` (one vehicle has zero/one policy).

**Step B — Apply Pull-Up for `numberOfAxles`.**
Both `Car` and `Truck` need it. But `Bus` does *not* meaningfully use it the same way.
- Move `numberOfAxles` to a new intermediate class `MotorVehicle`.
- Hierarchy becomes `Vehicle → MotorVehicle → Car, Truck`. `Bus` remains direct child of `Vehicle` (buses typically counted differently).

**Step C — Apply Push-Down for `seatingCapacity`.**
- Remove `seatingCapacity` from `Vehicle`.
- Add it to `Car` (e.g., 4–7) and to `Bus` (e.g., 30–60). `Truck` does not need it.

**Step D — Re-validate.**
- Single inheritance? ✅
- No cycles? ✅
- All *is-a* tests valid? ✅
- Duplication eliminated? ✅

### Final Adjusted Diagram (textual form):

```
Vehicle
   ├── Car            (seatingCapacity, numberOfAxles via MotorVehicle)
   ├── Truck          (numberOfAxles via MotorVehicle)
   └── Bus            (seatingCapacity)
   ◇── InsurancePolicy  (aggregation)
```

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Decision Table for Adjustment

| Observed Symptom | Diagnosis | Adjustment | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| Attribute `a` appears in ≥2 sibling subclasses | Duplication | Pull-up to LCA | DRY principle; polymorphic access |
| Attribute `a` in superclass, used by 1 subclass only | Wrong level of abstraction | Push-down | Reduces irrelevant state |
| Class has 2 superclasses (diamond) | Multiple inheritance | Replace with aggregation OR use interface | Resolves ambiguity; Liskov compliance |
| Class A→B→C→A | Cycle | Break one link with association | Restores acyclicity |
| Root class is `Object` with no semantics | Over-generalization | Re-root to more specific base | Improves cohesion |
| Subclass overrides parent with totally different behavior | Misuse of inheritance | Replace with composition | "Prefer composition over inheritance" (GoF) |

## 3.4 Mathematical Notation of Inheritance Adjustment

Let $\mathcal{C}$ be the set of classes and $\preceq$ be the inheritance (subclass-of) partial order. The model is **well-formed** iff:

$$
\forall c_1, c_2, c_3 \in \mathcal{C}: \quad c_1 \preceq c_2 \land c_2 \preceq c_3 \Rightarrow c_1 \preceq c_3
$$

$$
\forall c \in \mathcal{C}: \quad \neg(c \preceq c)
$$

$$
\forall c_1, c_2, c_3 \in \mathcal{C}: \quad c_1 \preceq c_2 \land c_1 \preceq c_3 \Rightarrow c_2 = c_3 \lor \exists\, c_4:\, c_4 \preceq c_2 \land c_4 \preceq c_3
$$

The first two equations enforce **transitivity** and **acyclicity**. The third enforces **single-inheritance uniqueness** (every class has at most one direct parent, unless a multiple-inheritance relationship is explicitly modeled and justified).

> [!NOTE]
> **Pull-Up Condition (Formal):**
> Let $S = \{s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_n\}$ be sibling classes with a common parent $p$. A feature $f$ should be pulled up to $p$ iff:
>
> $$\forall s_i \in S : \text{uses}(s_i, f) = \text{true}$$
>
> Similarly, **Push-Down Condition:**
>
> $$\text{uses}(p, f) = \text{false} \land \exists\, s_j \in S : \text{uses}(s_j, f) = \text{true}$$
>
> These conditions are also used as the official KTU marking-scheme keywords.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Adjustment Workflow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: Initial Class Diagram] --> A1{Validate Acyclicity}
    A1 -- Cycle Found --> A2[Break Cycle: Convert one link to Association]
    A2 --> A1
    A1 -- Acyclic --> B1{Detect Duplicate Features in Siblings}
    B1 -- Duplicate Found --> B2[Pull-Up to Lowest Common Ancestor]
    B2 --> B1
    B1 -- No Duplicate --> C1{Detect Irrelevant Features in Parent}
    C1 -- Irrelevant Found --> C2[Push-Down to Specific Subclass]
    C2 --> C1
    C1 -- All Relevant --> D1{Detect Multiple Inheritance}
    D1 -- Found --> D2{Is Second Parent a Role or Contract?}
    D2 -- Yes --> D3[Convert to Interface Realization]
    D2 -- No --> D4[Convert to Aggregation / Composition]
    D3 --> E1
    D4 --> E1
    D1 -- None --> E1{Check Root Class}
    E1 -- Root is Meaningful --> F1[Diagram is Adjusted]
    E1 -- Root is Too Generic --> E2[Re-Root Hierarchy]
    E2 --> F1
```

## 4.2 Before vs After Adjustment — Vehicle Rental Example (Mermaid)

### Before Adjustment

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Vehicle {
      +seatingCapacity
    }
    class Car {
      +numberOfAxles
    }
    class Truck {
      +numberOfAxles
    }
    class Bus
    class InsurancePolicy
    Vehicle <|-- Car
    Vehicle <|-- Truck
    Vehicle <|-- Bus
    Car <|-- InsurancePolicy
    Truck <|-- InsurancePolicy
    Bus <|-- InsurancePolicy
```

### After Adjustment

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Vehicle {
      +VIN
      +manufacturer
    }
    class MotorVehicle {
      +numberOfAxles
    }
    class Car {
      +seatingCapacity
    }
    class Truck
    class Bus {
      +seatingCapacity
    }
    class InsurancePolicy {
      +policyNumber
      +provider
    }
    Vehicle <|-- MotorVehicle
    MotorVehicle <|-- Car
    MotorVehicle <|-- Truck
    Vehicle <|-- Bus
    Vehicle o-- InsurancePolicy : has-a
```

## 4.3 Adjustment Decision Tree (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1 [Phase 1: Detection]
        D1[Scan for Duplicates]
        D2[Scan for Irrelevant Features]
        D3[Scan for Multiple Parents]
        D4[Scan for Cycles]
    end
    subgraph S2 [Phase 2: Action]
        A1[Pull-Up]
        A2[Push-Down]
        A3[Interface or Aggregation]
        A4[Break Cycle with Association]
        A5[Re-Root]
    end
    subgraph S3 [Phase 3: Verification]
        V1[Is-A Test]
        V2[Liskov Substitution Test]
        V3[Polymorphism Test]
    end
    D1 --> A1
    D2 --> A2
    D3 --> A3
    D4 --> A4
    A1 --> A5
    A2 --> A5
    A3 --> A5
    A4 --> A5
    A5 --> V1
    V1 --> V2
    V2 --> V3
    V3 --> R[Adjusted Class Diagram]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
> **What is meant by "adjustment of inheritance"? List any two adjustments.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Adjustment of inheritance is the activity of refining the class hierarchy (generalization/specialization structure) of an object-oriented model to correct redundancy, ambiguity, or incorrect abstraction levels. It is performed during object design.
*Two adjustments (1.5 marks each):*
1. **Pull-Up (Move Up):** Hoisting a duplicated feature from sibling subclasses to their common superclass.
2. **Push-Down (Move Down):** Pushing a feature from a superclass down to the specific subclass that actually needs it.

*(Valid alternatives: re-rooting, eliminating multiple inheritance, breaking cycles.)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**
> **Differentiate between generalization and specialization in inheritance adjustment.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Aspect | Generalization | Specialization |
|---|---|---|
| Direction | Subclass → Superclass (move up) | Superclass → Subclass (move down) |
| Purpose | Extract common features from siblings | Add specific features to a subclass |
| Result | Reduces redundancy, increases abstraction | Increases specificity, enables customization |
| UML operator | Upward arrow / merging of classes | Downward arrow / splitting of classes |

## 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> ### **Question A (14 Marks)**
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**
> Consider the following initial class diagram for a *University Management System*:
>
> ```
> Person
>   ├── Student (courseList, gpa)
>   ├── Faculty (salary, department)
>   └── Staff   (shift, salary)
> ```
>
> Both `Student` and `Staff` have a method `displayDetails()`. `Faculty` and `Staff` both have the attribute `salary`. The class `Person` has an attribute `age` used only by `Student`. There is a proposed class `Payroll` which is inherited by both `Faculty` and `Staff`.
>
> **(a)** Identify the inheritance issues present in the above model. **\[7 Marks\]**
> **(b)** Apply the necessary adjustments of inheritance and draw the final corrected class diagram. **\[7 Marks\]**

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Issues Identified (7 marks)

1. **Duplicated method `displayDetails()`** in `Student` and `Staff` → candidate for **Pull-Up** to `Person` (if `Faculty` should also display details, which is reasonable). **(1.5 marks)**
2. **Duplicated attribute `salary`** in `Faculty` and `Staff` → candidate for **Pull-Up** to a new intermediate class `Employee` that `Faculty` and `Staff` inherit from. **(1.5 marks)**
3. **Attribute `age` in `Person`** is used only by `Student` → candidate for **Push-Down** to `Student`. **(1 mark)**
4. **Multiple inheritance of `Payroll`** by `Faculty` and `Staff` is invalid because they are *not* a kind of payroll — a payroll is a *record* they are *associated* with. This must be replaced with **aggregation**. **(2 marks)**
5. The hierarchy `Faculty` and `Staff` are siblings under `Person` but share `salary`, indicating a missing intermediate class `Employee` — a **Generalization** issue. **(1 mark)**

#### Part (b) — Adjusted Class Diagram (7 marks)

**Adjustments performed:**
1. Created intermediate class `Employee` with attribute `salary` and operation `displayDetails()`. Re-parented `Faculty` and `Staff` under `Employee`. **(2 marks)**
2. Pushed `age` down to `Student`. **(1 mark)**
3. Removed `displayDetails()` from `Student` and `Staff`; it now resides in `Employee` (inherited). **(1 mark)**
4. Replaced multiple inheritance of `Payroll` with aggregation: `Employee ◇—— Payroll`. **(2 marks)**
5. Drew the corrected diagram with proper UML notation. **(1 mark)**

**Final Adjusted Class Diagram:**

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Person {
      +name
      +address
    }
    class Student {
      +age
      +courseList
      +gpa
    }
    class Employee {
      +salary
      +displayDetails()
    }
    class Faculty {
      +department
    }
    class Staff {
      +shift
    }
    class Payroll {
      +payPeriod
      +amount
    }
    Person <|-- Student
    Person <|-- Employee
    Employee <|-- Faculty
    Employee <|-- Staff
    Employee o-- Payroll : has-a
```

---

> ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
> **[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Modified]**
> **(a)** Explain with a suitable diagram the concept of **multiple inheritance** and the **diamond problem**. State two strategies to resolve it. **\[7 Marks\]**
> **(b)** What is **re-rooting** of inheritance hierarchy? Demonstrate with an example. What is the impact of re-rooting on polymorphism? **\[7 Marks\]**

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Multiple Inheritance and Diamond Problem (7 marks)

**Definition (2 marks):**
Multiple inheritance occurs when a class inherits from more than one direct superclass. The **diamond problem** arises when two superclasses themselves share a common ancestor, creating an ambiguous path for inherited members.

**Diagram (3 marks):**

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class A {
      +methodM()
    }
    class B
    class C
    class D
    A <|-- B
    A <|-- C
    B <|-- D
    C <|-- D
```

Here, `D` inherits from both `B` and `C`, which both inherit from `A`. When `D` calls `methodM()`, it is ambiguous whether the call should resolve via the `B→A` path or the `C→A` path.

**Two Resolution Strategies (2 marks):**

1. **Use Interfaces:** Declare a contract in the second parent as an interface («interface»). The subclass `realizes` the interface, gaining the contract without state duplication.
2. **Aggregation/Delegation:** Convert the second inheritance to a *has-a* (aggregation/composition) relationship. The class delegates calls to an internal object of the second type.
3. *(Bonus)* **Virtual Inheritance (C++ specific):** Use `virtual` keyword so the common base is included only once.

#### Part (b) — Re-Rooting (7 marks)

**Definition (2 marks):**
Re-rooting is the adjustment activity of changing the topmost (root) class of an inheritance hierarchy when a more appropriate abstraction is identified. It is performed when the original root is too generic (e.g., `Object`, `Thing`) and provides no meaningful shared features.

**Demonstration Example (3 marks):**

*Before re-rooting:* Hierarchy `LivingThing → Plant, Animal → Dog, Cat`. Here `LivingThing` is overly abstract.

*After re-rooting:* A new root `OrganicEntity` is introduced that captures shared features (cellular structure, metabolism). Hierarchy becomes `OrganicEntity → LivingThing → Plant, Animal → Dog, Cat`.

**Impact on Polymorphism (2 marks):**
- Re-rooting **broadens** the polymorphic type — instances of `Dog` are now also of type `OrganicEntity`, allowing them to be passed to any method expecting an `OrganicEntity`.
- Re-rooting does **not** break existing polymorphic assignments, provided substitutability is preserved.
- However, if re-rooting introduces features in the new root that subclasses do not satisfy, Liskov Substitution is violated.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse "adjustment" with "redesign".** Adjustment is a *local, corrective* activity on the existing class diagram — you should retain the original class names and the overall domain. Major rewrites are redesign, not adjustment.
> 2. **Avoid recommending multiple inheritance as a solution** unless the language supports it (C++, Python). Java/C# students must always propose interfaces.
> 3. **Forgetting to re-validate the is-a test** after pull-up/push-down is a common 1-mark deduction. Always re-verify the substitutability relationship.
> 4. **Do not draw diamonds as `<>` shapes in Mermaid** — Mermaid does not support filled-diamond (composition) symbol reliably. Use `o` for aggregation and `*` for composition; the marker is a textual notation here.
> 5. **Forgetting to push down irrelevant features** when pull-up is done. Both directions must be examined.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Adjustment of inheritance** is a *corrective* activity performed during **Object Design** to refine the class diagram produced in Analysis.
- **Five canonical adjustments:** Re-rooting, Pull-Up (generalization), Push-Down (specialization), Eliminating Multiple Inheritance, Breaking Cycles.
- **Pull-Up Rule:** Move a feature *up* if it is used by **all** siblings. **Lowest Common Ancestor (LCA)** is the target class.
- **Push-Down Rule:** Move a feature *down* if it is used by only one (or a few) subclasses but currently resides in a higher-level class.
- **Multiple Inheritance is forbidden in Java/C#** — use *interfaces* (realization) or *aggregation* (composition).
- **Diamond Problem** arises when two superclasses share a common ancestor — resolve via interfaces, aggregation, or virtual inheritance.
- **Cycles are illegal** in UML class diagrams — break by converting one inheritance to association.
- **Re-rooting** changes the topmost class; it affects polymorphism by widening the substitutability type.
- Always apply the **is-a test** and **Liskov Substitution Principle** before and after every adjustment.
- **"Prefer composition over inheritance"** (Gang of Four) — adjustment of inheritance may conclude that some *is-a* links should in fact be *has-a* links.
- The adjustment process is **iterative** — repeat until a *fixed point* is reached (no more changes needed).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Association
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCNT604 - OBJECT ORIENTED MODELING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Design of Association

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Association** is a structural relationship between two or more classes that describes a set of **links** (connections between objects) existing in the system over time. In the context of **Object-Oriented Design**, "Design of Association" refers to the systematic translation of these analysis-level semantic relationships into concrete software artifacts — typically **object references, pointer fields, or container objects** — along with decisions regarding traversal direction, multiplicity, ordering, and qualification.

In the **Unified Modeling Language (UML 2.5)**, every association is characterized by the following four design properties:

| # | Property | Symbol / Notation |
|---|----------|-------------------|
| 1 | Name | Verb or noun-phrase label |
| 2 | Role | Endpoint name at each participant |
| 3 | Multiplicity | $0..1, \; 1, \; *, \; 1..* \; 0..*$ |
| 4 | Navigation | Arrow on the navigable end |

**Design of Association** specifically answers the question: *"Given a domain association, how do we represent it in source code such that the runtime objects can communicate efficiently while preserving the domain semantics?"*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Library System:**
> Think of a **Library** and its **Members**. A member *borrows* books from a library. They are not the same thing (no inheritance), nor does one *contain* the other physically at all times (not strict composition), but a meaningful connection exists between them. In the real world, we represent this with a **borrower's card** or a **loan register** that links a Member to one or more Books.
>
> - The **Association** = the abstract "borrows" relationship
> - The **Link** = a specific record: "Mr. Raj borrowed *Clean Code* on 12-Oct-2025"
> - The **Design Decision** = How will the software store this loan? A list inside `Member`? A reference inside `Book`? Or a separate `Loan` object (association class)?

This is exactly the essence of **Design of Association**: deciding the **implementation idiom** that faithfully captures the domain relationship.

---

## 1.3 Why "Design" of Association (Not Just Analysis)?

In the **Analysis** phase we discover *what* associations exist. In the **Design** phase we must decide *how* to implement them. The OOAD principle states:

> *"Every design decision is a tradeoff between **semantic fidelity** (modelling accuracy) and **implementation cost** (memory, traversal time, coupling)."*

Common tradeoffs include:
- **Bidirectional vs Unidirectional** traversal
- **Reference** vs **Collection reference**
- **Plain Association** vs **Association Class**
- **Aggregation** vs **Composition**
- **Qualified** vs **Unqualified** access

These are precisely the decisions KTU examiners test.

---

## 1.4 Key Terminology at a Glance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Link** $\equiv$ An instance (object-level) of an association.
>
> **Multiplicity** $\equiv$ Number of objects of one class that may be associated with one object of the other class. Denoted $N..M$ where $N$ and $M$ are non-negative integers or $*$ (meaning "many").

The two fundamental associations in OOD:

$$ \text{Association} \;\; \Longrightarrow \;\; \text{"uses / knows-about"} $$

$$ \text{Aggregation} \;\; \subset \;\; \text{Association} \;\; \Longrightarrow \;\; \text{"whole–part, shared"} $$

$$ \text{Composition} \;\; \subset \;\; \text{Aggregation} \;\; \Longrightarrow \;\; \text{"whole–part, exclusive lifetime"} $$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Systematic Procedure for Designing an Association

The KTU-validated procedure to design any association consists of **six ordered steps**. Each step corresponds to a design decision a software architect must make.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Six-Step Design Pipeline (Bruegge & Dutoit / Rumbaugh et al.):**
>
> **Step 1 — Identify Roles:** Assign a meaningful role-name to each end of the association.
>
> **Step 2 — Resolve Multiplicity:** Determine the precise cardinality (e.g., $1$, $0..1$, $*$, $1..*$, $n..m$).
>
> **Step 3 — Choose Navigation Direction:** Decide if both objects can reach each other (bidirectional) or only one (unidirectional).
>
> **Step 4 — Select the Implementation Idiom:** Pick between a single reference, a collection reference, or an association class.
>
> **Step 5 — Decide Container Semantics:** Choose between **Set, Bag (List), or Ordered List** semantics.
>
> **Step 6 — Apply Refinements:** Add qualification (key-based lookup), ordering, or constraints where needed.

---

## 2.2 The High-Yield "Implementation Idiom" Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **cardinality of multiplicity** directly determines the data structure idiom used in code. Memorize the table below — it is a **favourite KTU question**.

| Multiplicity on Target End | Implementation Idiom | Java/C++ Representation |
|----------------------------|----------------------|--------------------------|
| $0..1$ | Single reference (may be null) | `Optional<T>` / `T*` |
| $1$ | Single reference (mandatory) | `final T ref` |
| $1..*$ or $*$ | Collection reference | `Set<T>` / `List<T>` |
| $0..*$ ordered | Ordered collection | `List<T>` |
| $0..*$ with key | Qualified association | `Map<KeyType, T>` |

The mathematical rule can be expressed compactly as:

$$ \text{idiom}(B) \;=\; \begin{cases} \text{reference to } B & \text{if multiplicity}(B) \le 1 \\ \text{container of } B\text{'s} & \text{if multiplicity}(B) > 1 \end{cases} $$

---

## 2.3 Directional Design: Unidirectional vs Bidirectional

A bidirectional association is **two unidirectional associations in opposite directions**. It is implemented by storing references in both classes.

**Cost of Bidirectionality:**

$$ C_{\text{total}} \;=\; 2 \cdot C_{\text{storage}} \;+\; 2 \cdot C_{\text{update}} \;+\; C_{\text{consistency}} $$

where
- $C_{\text{storage}}$ = memory for one pointer
- $C_{\text{update}}$ = cost to maintain the link on both sides
- $C_{\text{consistency}}$ = debugging overhead from dangling references

> [!NOTE]
> **OOD Heuristic (Larman):** *Prefer unidirectional associations.* Add the reverse direction only when the second direction is functionally required. This minimizes coupling.

---

## 2.4 Aggregation vs Composition vs Plain Association

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Composition** $\equiv$ Strong form of aggregation where the *whole* is solely responsible for creating and destroying the *parts*. Parts cannot exist without the whole.
>
> **Aggregation** $\equiv$ Weak form where the *whole* and *part* have independent lifetimes.
>
> **Association** $\equiv$ No ownership implied; merely a semantic "knows-about" relationship.

| Property | Association | Aggregation | Composition |
|----------|-------------|-------------|-------------|
| UML Notation | Plain line | Hollow diamond $\diamondsuit$ | Filled diamond $\blacklozenge$ |
| Lifetime Coupling | None | Weak | Strong |
| Sharing of Parts | Allowed | Allowed | **Forbidden** |
| Deletion Cascade | No | No | **Yes** |
| Implementation | Reference / Collection | Reference / Collection | Owned Container (e.g., member field) |

---

## 2.5 Qualified Association

A **qualifier** reduces the effective multiplicity of an association from "many" to "one" by indexing on a key. Conceptually:

$$ \text{multiplicity}_{\text{effective}} \;=\; \frac{\text{multiplicity}_{\text{raw}}}{\vert \text{key domain} \vert} $$

**Example:** Without qualification — `Department *--* Employee`. With qualification `deptId` — `Department 1--0..1 Employee` (a department has at most one employee with a given ID).

Implementation idiom: a **map** from key to target object.

$$ \text{storage} \;=\; \text{Map}\langle \text{KeyType}, \text{TargetType} \rangle $$

---

## 2.6 Engineering Utility of Association Design

| Domain | Application |
|--------|-------------|
| **RDBMS** | Foreign-key relationships mirror associations |
| **Enterprise Java (JPA)** | `@OneToMany`, `@ManyToOne`, `@ManyToMany` annotations |
| **Microservices** | Service-to-service API contracts are associations |
| **Embedded Systems** | Object pools, observer registers (collections of associated objects) |
| **Compiler Design** | Symbol tables use qualified associations |

> [!NOTE]
> Association design is the **conceptual bridge** between conceptual models (UML class diagrams) and physical implementation (database schemas, source code, API contracts).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Worked Example — University Course Registration

Consider the domain statement:

> *"A **Student** may register for one or more **Course** offerings. A **Course** may have zero or more **Student** enrolled. Each registration records a date and grade."*

### Step A — Identify Roles & Association Name

The verb **"registers for"** links Student to Course.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Association name} &= \text{``registers for''} \\
\text{Role at Student end} &= \text{enrolledStudent} \\
\text{Role at Course end} &= \text{registeredCourse}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step B — Resolve Multiplicity

By reading the English statement carefully:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Multiplicity of Course per Student} &= 1..* \; \text{(``one or more'')} \\
\text{Multiplicity of Student per Course} &= 0..* \; \text{(``zero or more'')}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step C — Choose Navigation Direction

A typical registration system needs both directions: a student asks *"what are my courses?"* and the registrar asks *"who is in CSE301?"*. Hence **bidirectional** is justified.

### Step D — Implementation Idiom

Because both multiplicities $> 1$, we need collections on **both** sides:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{In Student class} &\longrightarrow \text{Set}\langle \text{Course} \rangle \;\; \text{enrolledCourses} \\
\text{In Course class} &\longrightarrow \text{Set}\langle \text{Student} \rangle \;\; \text{enrolledStudents}
\end{aligned}
$$

A **Set** (not List) is chosen because the same student–course pair should appear at most once.

### Step E — Decide Container Semantics

Since *date* and *grade* are properties of the **link**, not of either class, the link cannot be stored as a plain association. We promote it to an **Association Class** named `Registration`.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Storage for Registration} &= \text{Map}\langle \text{(Student, Course)}, \text{Registration} \rangle
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step F — Apply Refinements

Add ordering constraint: courses appear in chronological registration order.

$$
\text{Container} \;=\; \text{List}\langle \text{Registration} \rangle \;\; \text{ordered by } \text{dateRegistered ASC}
$$

---

## 3.2 Decision Matrix — When to Use Each Association Type

| If you observe … | Then design as … |
|------------------|------------------|
| Lifetime of part depends strictly on whole; parts cannot be shared | **Composition** (filled diamond) |
| Parts have independent lifetime but logically belong to a whole | **Aggregation** (hollow diamond) |
| Only one direction of query is required | **Unidirectional** association |
| Each object of class $A$ relates to at most $n$ of class $B$ via a key | **Qualified Association** |
| The link itself has properties (date, grade, amount) | **Association Class** |
| Both directions queried with equal frequency | **Bidirectional** association |
| Multiplicity at both ends is "many" | **Two collection references** |

---

## 3.3 Mathematical Derivation — Choosing Minimum Number of References

Let $d_A$ be the number of directions in which object of class $A$ must navigate to class $B$ ($d_A \in \{0, 1\}$). Then the **total number of references** to implement the association is:

$$
N_{\text{ref}} \;=\; d_A + d_B \;\in\; \{0, 1, 2\}
$$

Case analysis:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{ref}} = 0 &\;\Longleftrightarrow\; \text{association is non-navigable (rare, mostly theoretical)} \\
N_{\text{ref}} = 1 &\;\Longleftrightarrow\; \text{unidirectional navigation} \\
N_{\text{ref}} = 2 &\;\Longleftrightarrow\; \text{full bidirectional navigation}
\end{aligned}
$$

If multiplicity $> 1$ at any navigable end, replace the reference with a collection of size:

$$
N_{\text{elements}} \;=\; \text{cardinality of the link set at runtime}
$$

So total **storage cost** in bytes (assuming 8-byte pointer):

$$
\text{Bytes} \;=\; 8 \cdot N_{\text{ref}} \cdot N_{\text{elements}}
$$

---

## 3.4 KTU-Style Analytical Walkthrough

> **Problem (frequent pattern):**
> *"For a domain where one `Department` has exactly $5..20$ `Faculty` members, and each `Faculty` belongs to exactly one `Department`, design the association. State multiplicity, navigation, and code-level idiom."*

### Solution Walkthrough

**Step 1 — Roles:**
Department end → `employer`, Faculty end → `member`.

**Step 2 — Multiplicity:**

$$
\text{Faculty per Department} \;=\; 5..20, \qquad \text{Department per Faculty} \;=\; 1
$$

**Step 3 — Navigation:**
Usually the Faculty page on a website shows their department, but the reverse is rarely queried. Hence **unidirectional**, Faculty → Department.

**Step 4 — Idiom:**

$$
\text{In Faculty} \;=\; \text{Department dept} \;\; (\text{single reference, multiplicity} = 1)
$$

**Step 5 — Container Semantics:** Not applicable (single reference).

**Step 6 — Refinement:** Add `final` to enforce immutability of the department reference.

$$
\text{final Department dept}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Class Diagram — Three Forms of Association

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Company {
        -String name
        -Address hq
        +hire(Employee e) void
        +listEmployees() List
    }
    class Department {
        -int id
        -String name
        +addFaculty(Faculty f) void
    }
    class Employee {
        -int empId
        -String name
        +promote() void
    }
    class Project {
        -int budget
        -List tasks
        +assignMember(Employee e) void
    }
    class TeamMember {
        -Date joinDate
        -String role
    }

    Company "1" *-- "1..*" Department : owns (composition)
    Department "1" o-- "5..20" Employee : employs (aggregation)
    Employee "0..*" -- "0..*" Project : worksOn (plain association)
    Project "1" -- "0..*" TeamMember : assigns (association class)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:**
> - `*--` (filled diamond) = **Composition**
> - `o--` (hollow diamond) = **Aggregation**
> - `--` (plain line) = **Association**
> - The class `TeamMember` joined to the `Project–Employee` link is an **Association Class**

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — The Six-Step Design Decision Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Association from Analysis] --> B[Step 1: Assign Role Names]
    B --> C[Step 2: Resolve Multiplicity]
    C --> D{Multiplicity at any end greater than 1?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Step 3: Choose Collection Set List Map]
    D -- No --> F[Step 3: Use Single Reference]
    E --> G{Bidirectional query required?}
    F --> G
    G -- Yes --> H[Step 4: Add Reference in Both Classes]
    G -- No --> I[Step 4: Add Reference in One Class Only]
    H --> J[Step 5: Apply Constraints Ordering Qualifier]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Step 6: Consider Association Class for Link Attributes]
    K --> L[Final Design Specification]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Storage Mapping (Qualified Association)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Domain["Domain Layer"]
        D1["Department dept1"]
        D2["Department dept2"]
    end
    subgraph QualifierKey["Qualifier Key: deptId"]
        K1["CSE"]
        K2["ECE"]
        K3["Mech"]
    end
    subgraph Storage["Storage: Map of Map"]
        M1["deptId to Employee lookup table"]
    end
    D1 -->|indexed by| K1
    D1 -->|indexed by| K2
    D2 -->|indexed by| K3
    K1 --> M1
    K2 --> M1
    K3 --> M1
```

> [!NOTE]
> A qualified association is physically realised as a **two-level lookup** — the qualifier key restricts the target to at most one object, drastically reducing search complexity from $O(n)$ to $O(1)$ (hash-table access).

---

## 4.4 Mermaid State Diagram — Lifecycle of an Association Link

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Created : objectA connects to objectB
    Created --> Active : link object valid
    Active --> Modified : attribute on link changes
    Modified --> Active
    Active --> Terminated : one side destructor fires
    Terminated --> [*]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Composition vs Aggregation Behaviour:** In a **composition**, when `objectA` is destroyed, the link is **automatically terminated** (state jumps to `Terminated`). In an **aggregation**, the part may survive independently.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Differentiate between aggregation and composition with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Aggregation | Composition |
|--------|-------------|-------------|
| UML Notation | Hollow diamond $\diamondsuit$ | Filled diamond $\blacklozenge$ |
| Lifetime | Parts survive whole | Parts die with whole |
| Sharing | Parts may be shared | Parts exclusive to one whole |
| Example | `Team` $\diamondsuit$– `Player` (player can switch teams) | `House` $\blacklozenge$– `Room` (room destroyed with house) |

**Key line (1 mark):** *Aggregation = shared ownership; Composition = exclusive ownership with co-existent lifetime.* **(1 mark)** Example with diagram reference. **(1 mark)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is a qualified association? When is it used?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **qualified association** is one in which a special attribute (**the qualifier**) is placed at one end to *restrict* the multiplicity of the association. **(1 mark)**

- Used when objects of one class are looked up by a key from objects of another class. **(1 mark)**
- **Example:** A `Bank` has many `Account`s; qualifier `accountNo` reduces effective multiplicity to `0..1`. **(1 mark)**

---

## 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout**
> Students frequently lose marks on association design questions because of these three mistakes:
> 1. **Confusing aggregation with composition** — Diamond shape must be correct (hollow vs filled).
> 2. **Omitting role names** — KTU mandates role names at both ends when the relationship is ambiguous.
> 3. **Choosing bidirectional by default** — Always justify with a domain reason; otherwise mark only $1/7$ for that sub-part.
> 4. **Forgetting association classes** — If the link has its own attributes (date, grade, price), you **must** promote it to an association class.
> 5. **Using List when Set is correct** — Bag semantics vs Set semantics; read multiplicity carefully (unique or duplicate?).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the design steps involved in implementing a UML association in object-oriented software. Mention each step with one sentence. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following domain statement:

> *"A `Library` lends `Book`s to `Member`s. A book may be lent to at most one member at a time. A member may borrow multiple books. Each loan has a due-date."*

Design the association between `Library`, `Book`, and `Member`. Specify multiplicity, navigation, and the code-level idiom (collection type or reference) you would use. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q A (a)

The **six-step systematic procedure** for designing an association: **(1 mark for listing all six, 1 mark each for two-line explanation of any three = 3 marks, 3 marks overall)**

1. **Identify Roles** — Assign a unique role-name at each end of the association to describe how each class participates. *(Example: `borrower`, `borrowedBook`)*
2. **Resolve Multiplicity** — Determine the cardinality at each end ($0..1$, $1$, $*$, $n..m$) from the domain rules.
3. **Choose Navigation Direction** — Decide between unidirectional (one reference) and bidirectional (two references) based on query requirements.
4. **Select Implementation Idiom** — Map multiplicity to a reference (single) or a collection (Set/List/Map).
5. **Decide Container Semantics** — Choose Set (unique), Bag (allow duplicates), or Ordered List based on domain constraints.
6. **Apply Refinements** — Add qualification, ordering, association class, or constraint specifications where needed.

#### Model Solution — Q A (b)

**Analysis of the domain:**

| Class | Property |
|-------|----------|
| `Library` | lends |
| `Book` | lent to at most one Member |
| `Member` | may borrow multiple Books |
| Link attribute | due-date |

**Step 1 — Roles:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Library} &\rightarrow \text{lender (role)} \\
\text{Book} &\rightarrow \text{borrowedItem (role)} \\
\text{Member} &\rightarrow \text{borrower (role)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Multiplicity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Book per Member} &= 0..* \\
\text{Member per Book} &= 0..1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Navigation:**
The system typically needs to know *(a)* what books a member has, and *(b)* who currently holds a given book. Hence **bidirectional**. **(1 mark)**

**Step 4 — Implementation Idiom:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{In Member} &\longrightarrow \text{Set}\langle \text{Book} \rangle \;\; \text{borrowedBooks} \\
\text{In Book} &\longrightarrow \text{Member} \;\; \text{currentBorrower} \;\; (\text{single reference, may be null})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Container Semantics:**
Use a **Set** because the same book cannot be lent twice to the same member simultaneously. **(1 mark)**

**Step 6 — Refinement — Association Class:**
Because the **loan** has a `dueDate`, promote the link to an Association Class:

$$
\text{Loan} \;=\; \{\text{loanId}, \text{bookId}, \text{memberId}, \text{dueDate}, \text{issueDate}\}
$$

Storage idiom:

$$
\text{Library owns} \; \text{Map}\langle \text{String bookISBN}, \text{Loan} \rangle
$$

**(Final 1 mark for the UML diagram or its textual equivalent showing the three classes, multiplicities, and the `Loan` association class.)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Compare and contrast **unidirectional** and **bidirectional** associations. State two advantages and two disadvantages of bidirectional associations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For an online shopping portal, the use case is:

> *"A `Customer` can place multiple `Order`s. Each `Order` contains one or more `Product`s. A `Product` may appear in many orders. The quantity ordered and unit price at time of order must be recorded."*

Design the full class diagram (textual form is acceptable) showing associations, multiplicities, role names, and any required association class. Justify each design decision. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q B (a)

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Unidirectional | Bidirectional |
|--------|----------------|---------------|
| Reference count | 1 | 2 (one in each class) |
| Coupling | Looser | Tighter |
| Query flexibility | One-way only | Two-way |
| Update cost | Half | Double |
| Dangling reference risk | Lower | Higher |

**Advantages of Bidirectional (2 marks):**
1. Enables fast two-way navigation without traversal through a third class.
2. Useful in object-oriented databases where both directions are queryable.

**Disadvantages of Bidirectional (2 marks):**
1. Maintenance overhead — every change must update both references, otherwise inconsistency arises.
2. Increased coupling — each class knows the internal structure of the other.

#### Model Solution — Q B (b)

**Design Decisions:**

**(i) Customer–Order association (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Multiplicity} &: \;\; \text{Customer (1) — (0..*) Order} \\
\text{Navigation} &: \;\; \text{Bidirectional (need both: order history & order lookup)} \\
\text{Idiom in Customer} &: \;\; \text{List}\langle \text{Order} \rangle \;\; \text{orderHistory} \\
\text{Idiom in Order} &: \;\; \text{Customer} \;\; \text{placedBy}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Order–Product association (3 marks):**
This is a many-to-many with attributes on the link — must use an **association class** named `OrderLine`.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Multiplicity} &: \;\; \text{Order (1) — (1..*) OrderLine — (1..*) Product} \\
\text{OrderLine attributes} &: \;\; \text{quantity}, \text{unitPrice}, \text{lineTotal} \\
\text{Idiom in Order} &: \;\; \text{List}\langle \text{OrderLine} \rangle \;\; \text{lines} \\
\text{Idiom in Product} &: \;\; \text{Set}\langle \text{OrderLine} \rangle \;\; \text{appearsIn}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Role Names (1 mark):**
- Customer end: `buyer`
- Order end (towards Customer): `placedBy`
- Order end (towards OrderLine): `has`
- Product end (towards OrderLine): `isListedIn`

**(iv) Justification (1 mark):**
- *Why association class?* — Both `quantity` and `unitPrice` are properties of the *link*, not of Order or Product independently; this is the textbook trigger for an association class.
- *Why Set for Product end?* — A given OrderLine is uniquely identified by its `(orderId, productId)` pair, so duplicates are impossible.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (must remember):**
>
> ✅ Association is a **structural** relationship — it answers *"does A know about B?"*
>
> ✅ **Six design steps** — Roles, Multiplicity, Navigation, Idiom, Container Semantics, Refinements.
>
> ✅ Multiplicity $\le 1$ $\Rightarrow$ **single reference** (use `Optional` / `T*` / nullable field for $0..1$).
>
> ✅ Multiplicity $> 1$ $\Rightarrow$ **collection reference** (Set for unique, List for ordered/duplicates, Map for qualified).
>
> ✅ **Prefer unidirectional** — only choose bidirectional when functionally required.
>
> ✅ **Aggregation $\diamondsuit$** = shared/weak ownership; **Composition $\blacklozenge$** = exclusive/strong ownership with co-destruction.
>
> ✅ **Association class** is mandatory when the **link itself** carries attributes (date, grade, price, quantity).
>
> ✅ **Qualifier** reduces effective multiplicity from $n$ to $0..1$ via a key — implemented as a `Map<KeyType, Target>`.
>
> ✅ A bidirectional association costs **$2 \times$ storage and $2 \times$ update** versus unidirectional.
>
> ✅ Always **justify each design decision** in the KTU exam with a domain rule, never by personal preference.
>
> ✅ UML multiplicity symbols: $1, \; 0..1, \; *, \; 0..*, \; 1..*, \; n..m$. Each has a distinct implementation meaning.
>
> ✅ Bidirectional associations must be kept **consistent** — a removal operation must update both ends to avoid dangling references.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Object Representation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCNT604 - OBJECT ORIENTED MODELING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Object Representation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Object Representation** is the standardized notation mechanism used in **Object-Oriented Modeling (OOM)** to depict **object instances**, their **attributes**, their **values**, and the **links** connecting them, so that a real-world domain can be unambiguously captured for analysis, design, and implementation.

In the **Object Modeling Technique (OMT)** proposed by **James Rumbaugh**, object representation is treated as the third major model (after the **Object Model** and the **Dynamic Model**) and is the visual + textual vocabulary through which an analyst captures the *state* of a system at a given moment in time.

The KTU 2024 syllabus (PBCNT604) categorizes object representation under the broader umbrella of **UML/OMT static modeling** and expects students to master the **three complementary notations** defined by Rumbaugh.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of **Object Representation** as the **photograph** of a running system.

- A **class diagram** is like the **architect's blueprint** of a building — it shows *what the building can be*.
- An **object representation / object diagram** is like a **photograph of the building on a particular day** — it shows *exactly which tenants are in which rooms, what furniture is where, and who is talking to whom*.

So while the class tells you the *template* (a Customer *has* a name, an id, and a balance), the object representation tells you the *snapshot* (Customer "Ravi" has name="Ravi", id=101, balance=25000).

## 1.3 Why is Object Representation Needed?

| Reason | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|
| **Snapshot Analysis** | Allows designers to test if a class structure can actually hold real data |
| **Traceability** | Maps real-world entities to software objects during design |
| **Communication** | Provides a common visual language between analysts, designers, and developers |
| **Validation** | Helps verify constraints (multiplicity, links) before coding |
| **Reverse Engineering** | Allows recovery of object structure from existing code |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners frequently frame the question as: *"Distinguish between Class Diagram and Object Diagram."* The crisp answer is: **Class Diagram is a *template* (shows all possible objects); Object Representation is a *snapshot* (shows a specific set of objects and their actual values at an instant).**

## 1.4 The Three Representation Methods (Rumbaugh)

Rumbaugh's OMT methodology defines **three** equivalent ways to represent objects. Any of the three may be used depending on the level of formality required.

1. **Analytical (Textual) Representation** — purely textual, used in reports and documentation.
2. **Diagrammatic (Graphical) Representation** — purely visual, used in design tools.
3. **Mixed Representation** — combines both, used in detailed engineering documents.

Each of these is explored in exhaustive detail in the following sections.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Method 1 — Analytical (Textual) Representation

The analytical representation is a **textual shorthand** that lists every object, its class, its attribute names, and the *current* attribute values.

### 2.1.1 General Syntax

$$
\text{ObjectName : ClassName} \;\; \big[\, a_1 = v_1,\; a_2 = v_2,\; \ldots,\; a_n = v_n \,\big]
$$

Where:
- $\text{ObjectName}$ is the specific instance label (optional but recommended).
- $\text{ClassName}$ is the template the object belongs to.
- $a_i$ are attribute names, and $v_i$ are the **current** values.
