### 2.1.2 Worked Example

A university domain containing two students enrolled in a course can be written textually as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ravi} & : \text{Student} \;\; [\,\text{rollNo}=101,\; \text{name}="Ravi",\; \text{cgpa}=8.7\,] \\
\text{meera} & : \text{Student} \;\; [\,\text{rollNo}=102,\; \text{name}="Meera",\; \text{cgpa}=9.2\,] \\
\text{CS201} & : \text{Course} \;\; [\,\text{code}="CS201",\; \text{title}="OOPS",\; \text{credits}=4\,] \\
\text{enroll}(\text{ravi},\text{CS201}) & \quad \text{[Association link]} \\
\text{enroll}(\text{meera},\text{CS201}) & \quad \text{[Association link]}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 2.2 Method 2 — Diagrammatic (Graphical) Representation

The diagrammatic representation is the most widely used form. It uses **boxes, lines, and labels** to render the same information visually.

### 2.2.1 Rumbaugh's Object Icon Conventions

| Object Type | Graphical Icon | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Object Instance** | A **rectangle** with the object name and class name separated by a colon | Standard instance |
| **Anonymous Object** | A rectangle with **only** the class name preceded by `:` | Shows the class without naming the instance |
| **Class Symbol** | A **rectangle with a shaded band** at the top containing the class name | Used in class diagrams (templates) |
| **Link** | A **solid straight line** | Connects two objects that participate in an association |
| **Link to Class** | A **dashed line** with a label | Shows that an object is an instance of a class |
| **Aggregation** | A **line with a hollow diamond** at the aggregate end | Whole–part relationship |
| **Inheritance / Generalization** | A **line with a hollow triangle** at the superclass end | "is-a" relationship |

### 2.2.2 Naming Rules (KTU High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - Object names are written in **lowercase** or mixed case (e.g., `ravi`, `emp42`).
> - Class names are written in **PascalCase** (e.g., `Customer`, `BankAccount`).
> - The format inside the box is always: `objectName : ClassName`.
> - If the object is anonymous, use only `: ClassName`.
> - Underline the object name to indicate a **persistent** object (one that survives process termination).

## 2.3 Method 3 — Mixed Representation

The mixed representation **combines** the textual and graphical forms. The diagram shows the structure (links, multiplicities) while the text adjacent to each object shows the *attribute values*. This is the form most often used in **detailed KTU lab records and design documents**.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield "Formula Sheet" / Notation Cheat Table

| # | Concept | Analytical Form | Graphical Form | Mixed Form |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Object instance | `obj : Class [a=v]` | Box `obj : Class` | Both |
| 2 | Anonymous object | `: Class` | Box `: Class` | Both |
| 3 | Binary link | `link(a,b)` | Solid line a — b | Both |
| 4 | Aggregation | `contains(a,b)` | Line with hollow diamond at `a` | Both |
| 5 | Generalization | `is-a(a, ParentClass)` | Line with hollow triangle | Both |
| 6 | Persistent object | `obj : Class` (underlined) | Box with **underlined** name | Both |
| 7 | Active object | `obj : Class` (bold) | Box with **bold** name | Both |
| 8 | Ternary link | `link(a,b,c)` | Three lines meeting at a small diamond | Both |
| 9 | Qualified link | `a[qual]→b` | Box inside the association line | Both |
| 10 | Multiplicity | `1`, `*`, `m..n` | Labels on line ends | Both |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In modern UML tools (e.g., **Rational Rose, StarUML, Visual Paradigm, Lucidchart**), the mixed representation is automatically generated from the analytical form, and vice versa. Mastering all three notations is therefore essential for KTU graduates entering the IT industry.

## 2.5 Object Representation in the Three OMT Models

| OMT Model | What is represented | Role of Object Representation |
|---|---|---|
| **Object Model** | Static structure (classes, links) | Shows *snapshots* of the class diagram with actual values |
| **Dynamic Model** | Time-varying behavior (states, events) | Object diagrams are *scenarios* of state transitions |
| **Functional Model** | Data flow and transformations | Objects are the data repositories of the DFD |

Object representation is the **common glue** that ties all three models together.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 board examiner expects students to **convert** between notations and to **trace** attribute values from a class diagram to an object representation. The following derivations are exhaustive.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Class Diagram to Object Representation

### Step 1 — Identify the Classes
Given a class diagram, enumerate every class.

### Step 2 — Identify the Attributes
For each class, list the attributes declared in the class diagram.

### Step 3 — Instantiate
Create one or more object instances by assigning concrete values to each attribute.

### Step 4 — Identify the Links
Trace the associations from the class diagram and connect the relevant object instances.

### Step 5 — Encode in the Chosen Notation
Translate the result into analytical, graphical, or mixed form.

### Worked Example
**Class diagram given:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Class } & \text{Department} : \text{name} : \text{String},\; \text{deptId} : \text{Integer} \\
\text{Class } & \text{Professor} : \text{empId} : \text{Integer},\; \text{name} : \text{String},\; \text{salary} : \text{Float} \\
\text{Association} & : \text{worksIn (Professor, Department)},\; 1\ \text{to}\ 1..*
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step-by-step object representation (analytical form):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text{CS} : \text{Department} \;\; [\,\text{name}="Computer Science",\; \text{deptId}=1\,] \\
& \text{EC} : \text{Department} \;\; [\,\text{name}="Electronics",\; \text{deptId}=2\,] \\
& \text{ravi} : \text{Professor} \;\; [\,\text{empId}=501,\; \text{name}="Ravi",\; \text{salary}=75000\,] \\
& \text{meera} : \text{Professor} \;\; [\,\text{empId}=502,\; \text{name}="Meera",\; \text{salary}=82000\,] \\
& \text{anand} : \text{Professor} \;\; [\,\text{empId}=503,\; \text{name}="Anand",\; \text{salary}=68000\,] \\
& \text{worksIn}(\text{ravi},\text{CS}) \\
& \text{worksIn}(\text{meera},\text{CS}) \\
& \text{worksIn}(\text{anand},\text{EC})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Graphical equivalent** is drawn in Section 4.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — From Object Representation to Class Diagram

Reverse the process:

1. Collect objects sharing the same `: ClassName`.
2. Union the attribute set across all objects of that class.
3. Identify the multiplicity of links.
4. Promote the structure to a class.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Representing Ternary Associations

A **ternary association** involves three classes. Rumbaugh's graphical form uses a **small diamond** where three lines meet.

### Analytical form:
$$
\text{projectAssign}(\text{empId}=501,\; \text{projId}=10,\; \text{role}="Lead")
$$

### Step-by-step reading:
1. Locate the diamond.
2. Identify the three participating objects.
3. Read the labels on each line for role names and multiplicity.
4. The combination of three objects is one valid tuple of the ternary relation.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Qualified Associations

**Qualification** is used to reduce multiplicity by adding a qualifier (e.g., a hash-map key). Example: *A Department contains many Employees, but an Employee is uniquely identified within a Department by an `empId`.*

### Analytical:
$$
\text{member}(\text{CS},\,\text{empId=501}) \;\rightarrow\; \text{ravi}
$$

### Graphical:
A small box labeled `empId` is drawn on the association line near the Department end.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Conversion Patterns

| # | Given | Derive | Algorithm |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Class with attributes | Object instance | Assign values to all attributes |
| 2 | Class with association | Object diagram | Draw a line between every legal pair |
| 3 | Object diagram | Class diagram | Generalize objects into a class |
| 4 | Ternary association | Ternary link | Add a small diamond and three lines |
| 5 | Qualified association | Qualifier box | Insert qualifier box on the line |
| 6 | Inheritance chain | Sub/Super object | Reuse object with a triangle pointing up |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Three Methods of Object Representation (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Object Representation] --> B[Analytical Textual]
    A --> C[Diagrammatic Graphical]
    A --> D[Mixed Representation]
    B --> B1["obj : Class attribute=value"]
    B --> B2["link obj1 obj2"]
    C --> C1[Box with obj : Class]
    C --> C2[Line for link]
    C --> C3[Hollow diamond for aggregation]
    C --> C4[Hollow triangle for inheritance]
    D --> D1[Diagram plus text labels]
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Sample Object Diagram (Rumbaugh Graphical Notation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DEP[Departments]
        CS[("CS : Department")]
        EC[("EC : Department")]
    end
    subgraph PROF[Professors]
        ravi[("ravi : Professor")]
        meera[("meera : Professor")]
        anand[("anand : Professor")]
    end
    ravi --- CS
    meera --- CS
    anand --- EC
```

> [!NOTE]
> The cylindrical/rounded box shape is the standard UML "object" icon. The `:` separates the object name from the class.

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Three Notation Styles Side-by-Side

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph A[Analytical Form]
        A1["ravi : Professor empId=501, name='Ravi', salary=75000"]
        A2["worksIn ravi, CS"]
    end
    subgraph G[Graphical Form]
        G1[("ravi : Professor")] -- "worksIn" --> G2[("CS : Department")]
    end
    subgraph M[Mixed Form]
        M1[("ravi : Professor")] --|empId=501, salary=75000| --> M2[("CS : Department")]
    end
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Aggregation, Inheritance, Ternary, Qualified

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph AGG[Aggregation]
        Univ[("univ : University")] o--|> Dept[("cs : Department")]
    end
    subgraph GEN[Inheritance]
        Prof[("meera : Professor")] -->|> Person[("Person")]
    end
    subgraph TRN[Ternary]
        P1[("emp : Professor")] --- Diamond{Tr}
        P2[("proj : Project")] --- Diamond
        P3[("role : Role")] --- Diamond
    end
    subgraph QUA[Qualified]
        Bank[("sbi : Bank")] ---|acctNo| Cust[("c : Customer")]
    end
```

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Object Representation Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Input["Domain Snapshot (Real World)"] --> Parser["Parser Module"]
    ClassDB["Class Diagram Definitions"] --> Parser
    Parser --> Analyzer["Analytical Form Generator"]
    Parser --> Grapher["Graphical Form Renderer"]
    Parser --> Mixer["Mixed Form Composer"]
    Analyzer --> OutA["Text Report"]
    Grapher --> OutG["Object Diagram Image"]
    Mixer --> OutM["Annotated Diagram"]
    OutA --> Validator["Constraint Checker"]
    OutG --> Validator
    OutM --> Validator
    Validator --> Final["Verified Object Representation"]
```

This **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** describes how a modern CASE tool ingests a domain, parses the class definitions, and emits the three representations while validating constraints such as multiplicity and link legality.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define Object Representation. List any two notations used for object representation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) Object Representation is the standardized notation used to depict objects, their attributes and the links among them, capturing a snapshot of a system at a given moment.
- (1 mark) Notation 1 — Analytical (textual) form: `obj : Class [attribute=value]`.
- (1 mark) Notation 2 — Diagrammatic (graphical) form: rectangles for objects, lines for links, with hollow diamonds and triangles for aggregation and inheritance.

### Question 2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Differentiate between a Class Diagram and an Object Diagram.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| # | Class Diagram | Object Diagram (Object Representation) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Shows the **template** of all possible objects | Shows **specific instances** and their values |
| 2 | Contains only class names and attribute types | Contains object names, class names, and concrete values |
| 3 | Used to define the **static structure** of a system | Used to validate the structure with **real data** |
| 4 | Multiplicity shown as `1`, `*`, `m..n` | Links shown between **named** objects |
| 5 | Represents the **Object Model** in OMT | Represents a **scenario** or **snapshot** of the Object Model |

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **three notations** of object representation in detail with suitable examples. (7 marks)

**(b)** Consider a library system with the classes `Book`, `Member`, and `Loan`. A Member can borrow many Books; a Book may be on loan many times. Represent the system using:
   - (i) Analytical notation
   - (ii) Graphical notation (Mermaid/object diagram)
   - (iii) Mixed notation
Also identify any aggregation or inheritance present. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**(a) Three Notations (7 marks):**
The Rumbaugh OMT methodology defines three complementary notations.

1. **Analytical Notation (2 marks)** — purely textual.
   - General syntax: `objName : ClassName [a1=v1, a2=v2, ...]`
   - Example: `ravi : Student [rollNo=101, name="Ravi", cgpa=8.7]`
   - Useful for documentation and textual reports.

2. **Diagrammatic Notation (2 marks)** — purely graphical.
   - Object box: `obj : Class` inside a rounded rectangle.
   - Link: solid straight line.
   - Aggregation: hollow diamond.
   - Inheritance: hollow triangle.
   - Example: A diagram showing `ravi : Student` connected to `CS201 : Course` via a `enrolls` link.

3. **Mixed Notation (2 marks)** — combination.
   - Diagram provides the structure; text labels provide attribute values.
   - Example: `ravi : Student [cgpa=8.7]` shown as a box with a tail label `cgpa=8.7`.
   - (1 mark reserved for diagrams/examples drawn correctly).

**(b) Library System Representation (7 marks):**

**Analytical (2 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text{b101} : \text{Book} \;\; [\,\text{isbn}="B101",\; \text{title}="OOPS",\; \text{author}="Rumbaugh"\,] \\
& \text{b102} : \text{Book} \;\; [\,\text{isbn}="B102",\; \text{title}="UML Distilled",\; \text{author}="Fowler"\,] \\
& \text{m01} : \text{Member} \;\; [\,\text{memId}=1,\; \text{name}="Ravi"\,] \\
& \text{m02} : \text{Member} \;\; [\,\text{memId}=2,\; \text{name}="Meera"\,] \\
& \text{loan}(\text{m01},\text{b101}) \\
& \text{loan}(\text{m02},\text{b102})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Graphical (2 marks):**
(Mermaid block as shown below; explain each link.)

**Mixed (2 marks):**
Add attribute values as labels next to the boxes.

**Aggregation / Inheritance (1 mark):**
If `Loan` is treated as an association class with attributes `dateIssued` and `dueDate`, an aggregation from `Member` to `Loan` and from `Book` to `Loan` is appropriate.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BOOKS[Books]
        b1[("b101 : Book")]
        b2[("b102 : Book")]
    end
    subgraph MEMBERS[Members]
        m1[("m01 : Member")]
        m2[("m02 : Member")]
    end
    L1[("loan1 : Loan")] --- m1
    L1 --- b1
    L2[("loan2 : Loan")] --- m2
    L2 --- b2
```

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Explain with examples how **aggregation** and **inheritance** are represented in Rumbaugh's object representation. (7 marks)

**(b)** A company has `Department` and `Employee` classes. A department contains many employees, and an employee belongs to exactly one department. Show this using all three object representation methods. Also demonstrate a **ternary association** between `Employee`, `Project`, and `Role`. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**(a) Aggregation and Inheritance (7 marks):**

**Aggregation (3.5 marks):** Aggregation represents a *whole–part* relationship. In Rumbaugh's graphical notation, it is drawn as a **solid line with a hollow diamond** at the *whole* (aggregate) end.

*Example:* `Car` is an aggregate of `Engine`, `Wheel`, and `Chassis`. Each `Wheel` is a part of exactly one `Car`.

Analytical form: `aggregates(Car, Engine)`, `aggregates(Car, Wheel)`, `aggregates(Car, Chassis)`.

**Inheritance (3.5 marks):** Inheritance represents an *is-a* relationship and is drawn as a **solid line with a hollow triangle** at the *superclass* (parent) end.

*Example:* `SavingsAccount` and `CurrentAccount` both inherit from `BankAccount`.

Analytical form: `isA(SavingsAccount, BankAccount)`, `isA(CurrentAccount, BankAccount)`.

In a mixed representation, the diagram clearly shows the parent–child hierarchy, and the analytical forms are written adjacent to it.

**(b) Department–Employee + Ternary Association (7 marks):**

**Analytical (2 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text{cs} : \text{Department} \;\; [\,\text{deptId}=1,\; \text{name}="Computer Science"\,] \\
& \text{ec} : \text{Department} \;\; [\,\text{deptId}=2,\; \text{name}="Electronics"\,] \\
& \text{ravi} : \text{Employee} \;\; [\,\text{empId}=101,\; \text{name}="Ravi"\,] \\
& \text{meera} : \text{Employee} \;\; [\,\text{empId}=102,\; \text{name}="Meera"\,] \\
& \text{worksIn}(\text{ravi},\text{cs}) \\
& \text{worksIn}(\text{meera},\text{ec}) \\
& \text{assign}(\text{ravi},\text{projX},\text{Lead}) \\
& \text{assign}(\text{meera},\text{projY},\text{Developer})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Graphical (2 marks):** (Mermaid block below.)

**Mixed (2 marks):** Add text labels to the boxes for attribute values; use a small diamond for the ternary association.

**Ternary (1 mark):** A small diamond labeled `assign` connects `ravi : Employee`, `projX : Project`, and `Lead : Role`.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DEP[Departments]
        CS[("cs : Department")]
        EC[("ec : Department")]
    end
    subgraph EMP[Employees]
        Ravi[("ravi : Employee")]
        Meera[("meera : Employee")]
    end
    Ravi --- CS
    Meera --- EC
    subgraph TRN[Ternary Association]
        Tri1{Tr} --- Ravi
        Tri1 --- P1[("projX : Project")]
        Tri1 --- R1[("Lead : Role")]
    end
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students often **forget to underline** the object name for *persistent* objects — this costs a full mark.
> - Confusing **aggregation (hollow diamond)** with **composition (filled diamond)** is a common error. Composition implies ownership and lifetime dependency; aggregation does not.
> - In **ternary associations**, students frequently draw a *binary* link and *add a third box* to the line. The correct Rumbaugh form is a **small diamond** with three emanating lines.
> - Do **not** use Python or pseudo-code in OOM theory answers; KTU examiners expect **notation**, not implementation.
> - Always write the **object name first**, followed by `: ClassName`. The reverse order is incorrect.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density revision checklist for Object Representation:**

- Object Representation captures a **snapshot** of the system, not the template.
- Rumbaugh's OMT defines **three notations**: Analytical, Diagrammatic, Mixed.
- **Analytical syntax:** `obj : Class [a1=v1, a2=v2, ...]`.
- **Object icon:** Rectangle with `objectName : ClassName`.
- **Anonymous object:** Rectangle with `: ClassName` only.
- **Persistence:** Underline the object name.
- **Active object:** Bold the object name.
- **Link:** Solid line between objects.
- **Aggregation:** Hollow diamond at the *whole* end (whole–part).
- **Composition:** Filled diamond at the *whole* end (strong ownership).
- **Inheritance / Generalization:** Hollow triangle at the *superclass* end.
- **Ternary association:** Small diamond with three emanating lines.
- **Qualified association:** Small box on the association line acting as a key.
- **Multiplicity labels:** `1`, `*`, `0..1`, `m..n`.
- **Class diagram** is the **template**; **Object representation** is the **snapshot**.
- An object representation **must not contradict** its parent class diagram; validate multiplicity.
- Rumbaugh's object model uses object representation as the **bridge** between class structure and runtime state.
- In KTU exams, **diagrams carry 40–50% of the marks** — always draw clearly and label every link and multiplicity.
- Modern UML tools (Rational Rose, StarUML, Visual Paradigm, PlantUML) auto-generate all three notations; understanding them is essential for both exams and industry practice.
- **Active vs Passive objects:** Active objects (bold) initiate control flow; passive objects (normal) only respond.
- **Persistent vs Transient objects:** Persistent objects (underlined) survive process termination; transient objects do not.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Physical Packaging
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCNT604 - OBJECT ORIENTED MODELING AND DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Physical Packaging # 📦 PHYSICAL PACKAGING — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Physical Packaging** in Object-Oriented Modeling and Design refers to the **physical architecture view** of a software system. It describes how the **logical software artifacts** (classes, subsystems, packages from the design model) are organized into **physical deliverable units** such as source files, library files (`.dll`, `.jar`, `.so`), executables (`.exe`), configuration files, and database scripts, and how these units are subsequently **deployed onto physical computing nodes** (servers, workstations, mobile devices, embedded processors). In the **Unified Process (UP)** and **UML 2.5** lifecycle, Physical Packaging is the bridge between: $$\text{Logical Design (Classes \& Packages)} \longrightarrow \text{Physical Implementation (Components)} \longrightarrow \text{Deployment (Nodes)}$$ The KTU 2024 syllabus classifies Physical Packaging as a **System-Level Architectural Concern** that directly addresses **Course Outcome CO4**: *"Design and document software architecture using component and deployment diagrams."* --- ## 1.2 The Two Pillars of Physical Packaging | Pillar | UML Artifact | Primary Concern | |---|---|---| | **Component Packaging** | **Component Diagram** | HOW software files are grouped and their dependencies | | **Physical Deployment** | **Deployment Diagram** | WHERE these files run on hardware infrastructure | > [!NOTE] > **Packaging ≠ Deployment.** A *package* groups *files* logically. A *node* hosts *files* physically on hardware. Many students conflate these — examiners specifically test this distinction. --- ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Moving House" Intuition Imagine you have designed the **blueprint of a house** (your class diagram). The *logical model* says: "There is a Kitchen, a Bedroom, a Bathroom." Now you must actually **pack the house into moving boxes** (Physical Packaging — Component View): - 📦 **Box 1** = Kitchen items (pots, pans, stove) - 📦 **Box 2** = Bedroom items (bed, wardrobe, sheets) - 📦 **Box 3** = Bathroom items (toiletries, towels) Each box is a **Component** — a self-contained, replaceable unit. Once the boxes are ready, you decide **which truck carries which box to which house**: - 🚚 **Truck A** delivers Box 1 & Box 2 → **Villa** - 🚚 **Truck B** delivers Box 3 → **Apartment** The trucks travel on **roads (network)** and stop at **destinations (nodes)**. This is the **Deployment View**. > [!TIP] > **Design time = Component Diagram** (how files relate) > **Run time = Deployment Diagram** (how files execute on hardware) --- ## 1.4 Core Terminology — The KTU Must-Know Lexicon > [!NOTE] > ### 📘 Mandatory KTU Definitions > - **Component**: *"A modular, replaceable, and well-defined part of a system that encapsulates its contents and has a well-defined set of interfaces."* — Rumbaugh, Jacobson, Booch (UML Reference, foundational) > - **Node**: *"A physical element that exists at run time and represents a computational resource, typically having memory and processing capability."* — UML 2.5 Specification (canonical definition used in KTU textbooks) > - **Package**: *"A general-purpose mechanism for organizing modeling elements into groups. It provides a namespace for the grouped elements."* — UML 2.5 Specification > - **Artifact**: *"A piece of physical information that is used or produced by a software development process. Examples include source files, binaries, executables, database tables, and documentation."* — UML 2.5 Specification --- ## 1.5 Why Physical Packaging Matters in Industry | Engineering Concern | How Physical Packaging Solves It | |---|---| | **Team Scalability** | 5 teams can compile 5 different components in parallel | | **Versioning & Release** | Update `PaymentComponent.dll` without redeploying the whole app | | **Distribution & Licensing** | Sell `PremiumModule.jar` as a paid add-on | | **Fault Isolation** | A crash in one component does not necessarily kill others | | **Performance Tuning** | Heavy components placed on powerful nodes | | **Security** | Sensitive components (encryption) isolated on secure hardware | --- ## 1.6 Relationship to the 4+1 Architectural View Model Physical Packaging implements **two of the five views** in Philippe Kruchten's **4+1 View Model**: $$\underbrace{\text{Logical View}}_{\text{Class Diagram}} + \underbrace{\text{Process View}}_{\text{Sequence/Activity}} + \underbrace{\text{Physical View}}_{\textbf{Component + Deployment}} + \underbrace{\text{Development View}}_{\text{Package Diagram}} + \underbrace{\text{Use Case View}}_{\text{Scenarios}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > The **Physical View** is *literally* the technical realization of Physical Packaging in modern software engineering. The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly references this view-model mapping. # ⚙️ Deep Theoretical Analysis — The Physical Packaging Framework ## 2.1 The Component Model — Structural Theory A **Component** in UML is *not* a class. It is a **higher-level software building block** that groups one or more classes, interfaces, and other artifacts behind a coherent set of provided and required interfaces. ### 2.1.1 The Five Properties of a Well-Formed Component A component is **component-worthy** if and only if it satisfies these properties: 1. **Encapsulation** — Hides its internal classes from outside clients 2. **Substitutability** — A component can be replaced by another that implements the same interfaces 3. **Cohesion** — All internal classes serve one common purpose 4. **Reusability** — Can be deployed in multiple systems 5. **Deployability** — Has a physical realization (a file) > [!NOTE] > **Naming Convention (KTU Standard)**: Component names are written in **PascalCase** and end with the suffix `Component` (e.g., `OrderProcessingComponent`, `PaymentGatewayComponent`). ### 2.1.2 Component Stereotypes (UML 2.5 Standard) | Stereotype | UML Notation | Meaning | Example File | |---|---|---|---| | `<>` | Rectangle with `<>` | Program that can run on a node | `app.exe`, `app.jar` | | `<>` | Rectangle with `<>` | Static or dynamic library | `libmath.a`, `math.dll` | | `<>` | Rectangle with `<
>` | Database table artifact | `customers.sql` | | `<>` | Rectangle with `<>` | Documentation artifact | `README.pdf` | | `<>` | Rectangle with `<>` | Generic source/data file | `config.xml` | | `<>` | Rectangle with `<>` | Generic physical information | (default) | --- ## 2.2 The Node Model — Deployment Theory A **Node** represents a **physical computational resource** where artifacts are deployed and executed. ### 2.2.1 Two Categories of Nodes $$\text{Node} = \begin{cases} \text{Device (Processor Node)} & \text{: A physical hardware machine} \\ \text{Execution Environment (Container Node)} & \text{: A software runtime container} \end{cases}$$ | Node Type | UML Notation | Real-World Example | |---|---|---| | **Device** | 3D box | A Dell PowerEdge server, an iPhone 14, a Raspberry Pi | | **Execution Environment** | 3D box with `<>` | JVM, Node.js runtime, Docker container, IIS, Apache Tomcat | ### 2.2.2 Node Stereotypes and Properties - `<>` — Physical hardware - `<>` — Software runtime (e.g., `JRE 17`, `Node v20`, `IIS 10`) - **Association between nodes** = physical communication path (network, bus, cable) - **Deployment dependency** = dashed arrow from artifact to node, with optional multiplicity (`1`, `0..1`, `*`) > [!TIP] > If you forget the notation, remember: **Devices are drawn as 3D boxes (cubes)**. If it has `<>` written inside, it is a *container*, not a *device*. --- ## 2.3 Component Interfaces — The Contract Mechanism A component communicates with the outside world **exclusively through interfaces**. There are two types: | Interface Type | Lollipop Notation | Socket Notation | Meaning | |---|---|---|---| | **Provided Interface** (○) | Small circle attached to component | Half-circle (socket) attached to client | Services the component offers | | **Required Interface** (⌒) | Half-circle (socket) on the component side | Small circle attached to supplier | Services the component needs | ### 2.3.1 The Interface Wiring Pattern $$\text{Required Interface of Client} \xLeftrightarrow{\text{Realizes}} \text{Provided Interface of Server}$$ This is the UML **"ball-and-socket"** mechanism: a *lollipop* (○) plugs into a *socket* (⌒). --- ## 2.4 Mapping Logical to Physical — The KTU Decision Matrix This is the **heart of the KTU 14-mark question** on this topic. Given a set of classes, how do you group them into components? | Decision Criterion | Group Classes Into Same Component When... | |---|---| | **Strong Coupling** | Classes have high interdependencies (call each other often) | | **Common Purpose** | Classes collaborate to fulfill a single responsibility | | **Same Team** | Classes are developed and maintained by the same team | | **Version Co-evolution** | Classes are released and versioned together | | **Reuse Boundary** | Classes are reused together in multiple contexts | | **Granularity Limit** | Component should not be larger than ~50–100 classes | > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Mistake**: Students often group classes by *inheritance* hierarchy. ❌ Wrong. Components are grouped by *collaboration*, not inheritance. A parent class and its child can live in **different** components. --- ## 2.5 The KTU High-Yield "Formula" Sheet for Physical Packaging Since this is a design subject, the "formulas" are **architectural rules, notation rules, and decision principles**: | Rule # | Principle | KTU Statement | |---|---|---| | R1 | **Component Encapsulation Rule** | A component must hide its internal classes; only interfaces are public | | R2 | **Interface Realization Rule** | A *realize* relationship links a component to its provided interface | | R3 | **Dependency Rule** | A *dependency* arrow goes from the requiring component to the required interface | | R4 | **Node Identity Rule** | Every node must be uniquely named within a deployment diagram | | R5 | **Deployment Multiplicity** | An artifact may be deployed on `0..*` nodes, indicated at the arrow tail | | R6 | **Communication Path Rule** | Association between two devices = a physical network link | | R7 | **Artifact Conformance** | A component in the design view is *realized by* one or more artifacts in the implementation view | | R8 | **Package Containment** | A package can contain classes, but a **component contains artifacts** | | R9 | **No Direct Class on Node** | You **cannot** deploy a *class* on a *node*; only *artifacts* may be deployed | | R10 | **One Node, Many Artifacts** | A node can host multiple artifacts; an artifact is typically on one node | --- ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!IMPORTANT] > Physical Packaging is the foundation of **modern microservice architecture**. In a Netflix-style system: > - Each **microservice** is a UML **Component** with its own database (artifact) > - Each **Kubernetes pod / Docker container** is a UML **Execution Environment Node** > - Each **physical server / VM** is a UML **Device Node** > - The **REST/gRPC call** between services is a UML **dependency through provided interface** > > The Component & Deployment diagrams you draw in KTU exams are **the exact same diagrams** cloud architects draw using tools like AWS CloudFormation, Terraform, or Lucidchart. # 🧮 Step-by-Step Analysis — The "Logical → Physical" Derivation Procedure ## 3.1 The Canonical KTU 14-Mark Problem Pattern > *"Given the following class diagram / use cases of a system, design the Component Diagram and Deployment Diagram. Justify your packaging decisions."* This section provides the **exhaustive step-by-step methodology** to solve such a problem. --- ### 3.2 STEP 1: Identify Atomic Classes from the Logical Model **Input**: You are given a class diagram of, say, a **Library Management System** with these classes: - `Book`, `Member`, `Loan`, `Reservation`, `Fine` - `BookController`, `MemberController`, `LoanController` - `BookRepository`, `MemberRepository`, `LoanRepository` - `AuthenticationService`, `NotificationService` **Logical Decomposition**: $$\text{Total Classes} = \underbrace{5}_{\text{Entities}} + \underbrace{3}_{\text{Controllers}} + \underbrace{3}_{\text{Repositories}} + \underbrace{2}_{\text{Services}} = 13 \text{ classes}$$ **Output of Step 1**: A list of **13 atomic logical units**. --- ### 3.3 STEP 2: Apply Cohesion & Coupling Rules to Form Components Apply the **KTU Decision Matrix** (from §2.4): #### 3.3.1 Cluster by Domain Responsibility | Cluster Name | Classes | Justification | |---|---|---| | **BookManagementComponent** | `Book`, `BookController`, `BookRepository` | All handle book CRUD; same team (Book Team) | | **MemberManagementComponent** | `Member`, `MemberController`, `MemberRepository` | All handle members; same team (Member Team) | | **LoanManagementComponent** | `Loan`, `Reservation`, `Fine`, `LoanController`, `LoanRepository` | Loan workflow is one cohesive transaction | | **CrossCuttingComponent** | `AuthenticationService`, `NotificationService` | Reusable across all 3 components above | **Mathematical Form of the Grouping**: $$C_i = \{c_j \mid \text{responsibility}(c_j) = R_i\}$$ where $C_i$ is a component and $R_i$ is its single responsibility. #### 3.3.2 Verify the 5-Property Check | Property | Verified? | Reasoning | |---|---|---| | Encapsulation | ✅ | Each component hides its internal classes | | Substitutability | ✅ | `BookRepository` can be swapped (MySQL → MongoDB) | | Cohesion | ✅ | All classes in a component share one goal | | Reusability | ✅ | `CrossCuttingComponent` reused everywhere | | Deployability | ✅ | Each component compiles to one `.dll` / `.jar` | **Output of Step 2**: **4 components** identified. --- ### 3.4 STEP 3: Design the Interfaces Each component exposes **provided interfaces** and declares **required interfaces**. | Component | Provided Interface | Required Interface | |---|---|---| | `BookManagementComponent` | `IBookService` | `IAuthenticationService` | | `MemberManagementComponent` | `IMemberService` | `IAuthenticationService` | | `LoanManagementComponent` | `ILoanService` | `IBookService`, `IMemberService`, `INotificationService` | | `CrossCuttingComponent` | `IAuthenticationService`, `INotificationService` | *(none)* | **Interface Equation**: $$\text{Dependencies}(C_i) = \{I_k \mid C_i \text{ invokes method on } I_k\}$$ The **Loan component** is the most dependent because it orchestrates all workflows. --- ### 3.5 STEP 4: Map Components to Artifacts Each component is *realized by* one physical artifact (binary file): | Component | Artifact (Physical File) | |---|---| | `BookManagementComponent` | `BookMgmt.dll` | | `MemberManagementComponent` | `MemberMgmt.dll` | | `LoanManagementComponent` | `LoanMgmt.dll` | | `CrossCuttingComponent` | `SecurityCore.dll` | **The Component-to-Artifact Realization**: $$\text{Artifact}(C_i) \xrightarrow{\text{realizes}} C_i$$ Each artifact is also a `<>` or `<>` artifact (UML 2.5). --- ### 3.6 STEP 5: Identify Physical Nodes For a typical 3-tier Library System: | Node | Type | Role | |---|---|---| | `ClientPC` | `<>` | User's browser/laptop | | `WebServer` | `<>` | Hosts the presentation logic | | `AppServer` | `<>` | Hosts the business logic components | | `DBServer` | `<>` | Hosts the database | | `JVM_Runtime` | `<>` | Java runtime on AppServer | | `IIS_Runtime` | `<>` | Web server on WebServer | | `MySQL_Server` | `<>` | DB engine on DBServer | **Total nodes identified**: 7 (3 devices + 3 execution environments + 1 device) --- ### 3.7 STEP 6: Deploy Artifacts onto Nodes Apply the deployment multiplicity rules: | Artifact | Deployed On | Multiplicity | |---|---|---| | `WebApp.war` | `IIS_Runtime` | `1` | | `BookMgmt.dll` | `JVM_Runtime` | `1` | | `MemberMgmt.dll` | `JVM_Runtime` | `1` | | `LoanMgmt.dll` | `JVM_Runtime` | `1` | | `SecurityCore.dll` | `JVM_Runtime` | `1` | | `library_db.sql` | `MySQL_Server` | `1` | > [!WARNING] > **Do NOT deploy a component directly on a device.** You must deploy the **artifact** (the file). The artifact is *realized by* the component, and *deployed on* the node. --- ### 3.8 STEP 7: Define Communication Paths Connect devices with associations labeled by protocol: | From Node | To Node | Protocol | |---|---|---| | `ClientPC` | `WebServer` | HTTPS | | `WebServer` | `AppServer` | TCP/IP | | `AppServer` | `DBServer` | SQL over TCP | **UML Notation**: Solid line between two devices, with the protocol name as a label. --- ### 3.9 The Final Verification Matrix After completing the design, verify all KTU 2024 expectations: | KTU Expectation | How It Was Met | |---|---| | Correct component stereotypes | Used `<>` for all DLLs | | Valid interface wiring | Loan component requires Book and Member services | | Proper node hierarchy | 3-tier with clear separation | | Deployment multiplicity | All artifacts deployed exactly once | | Communication paths | All 3 network links labeled with protocol | | No classes on nodes | All nodes host artifacts, not classes | --- ## 3.10 Analytical Summary — The 7-Step Master Recipe $$\boxed{ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Enumerate all classes from the design model} \\ \text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Cluster classes by responsibility (Decision Matrix)} \\ \text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Define provided \& required interfaces per component} \\ \text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{Map each component to one or more artifacts} \\ \text{Step 5:} \quad & \text{Identify physical devices and execution environments} \\ \text{Step 6:} \quad & \text{Deploy artifacts onto nodes with multiplicities} \\ \text{Step 7:} \quad & \text{Connect nodes with labeled communication paths} \end{aligned} }$$ > [!TIP] > In the KTU exam, if you write these 7 steps as the *methodology* in your answer, examiners award full method marks even if your final diagram has minor cosmetic errors. # 🏗️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Master Architecture: From Logic to Physical ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Logical Model
Class Diagram] --> B[Design Model
Package Diagram] B --> C[Implementation Model
Component Diagram] C --> D[Deployment Model
Deployment Diagram] D --> E[Running System
Live Network] subgraph SG1[Analysis Phase] A end subgraph SG2[Design Phase] B end subgraph SG3[Implementation Phase] C end subgraph SG4[Deployment Phase] D E end classDef phase fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0,stroke-width:2px,color:#0d47a1 classDef art fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#bf360c class A,B,C,D,E art class SG1,SG2,SG3,SG4 phase ``` > **Reading Guide**: The arrow direction shows *transformation*, not *dependency*. A class becomes part of a package, which becomes realized by a component, which gets deployed as an artifact, which finally runs on a node. --- ## 4.2 Component Diagram Schematic — Library System ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph CrossCutting[CrossCuttingComponent] direction TB AuthSvc[AuthenticationService] NotifSvc[NotificationService] end subgraph BookComp[BookManagementComponent] direction TB BookCtrl[BookController] BookRepo[BookRepository] end subgraph MemberComp[MemberManagementComponent] direction TB MemberCtrl[MemberController] MemberRepo[MemberRepository] end subgraph LoanComp[LoanManagementComponent] direction TB LoanCtrl[LoanController] LoanRepo[LoanRepository] end IBook[/IBookService/] IMember[/IMemberService/] ILoan[/ILoanService/] IAuth[/IAuthenticationService/] INotif[/INotificationService/] BookComp --- IBook MemberComp --- IMember LoanComp --- ILoan CrossCutting --- IAuth CrossCutting --- INotif LoanComp -. requires .-> IBook LoanComp -. requires .-> IMember LoanComp -. requires .-> INotif BookComp -. requires .-> IAuth MemberComp -. requires .-> IAuth classDef compBox fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:2px classDef iface fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:1px classDef req fill:#ffebee,stroke:#c62828,stroke-width:1px,stroke-dasharray: 5 5 class BookComp,MemberComp,LoanComp,CrossCutting compBox class IBook,IMember,ILoan,IAuth,INotif iface ``` > **Legend**: Solid lines = *realize* (component provides interface). Dashed lines = *dependency* (component requires interface). Yellow boxes = interfaces. --- ## 4.3 Deployment Diagram Schematic — Library System (3-Tier) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Tier1[Client Tier] ClientPC[ClientPC
device] end subgraph Tier2[Presentation Tier] WebServer[WebServer
