device] MySQL[MySQL_Server
executionEnvironment] DBSchema[library_db.sql] end ClientPC -- HTTPS --> WebServer WebServer --- IIS WebServer -- TCP IP --> AppServer AppServer --- JVM AppServer -- SQL over TCP --> DBServer DBServer --- MySQL IIS -.deploys.-> WebApp JVM -.deploys.-> BookDLL JVM -.deploys.-> MemberDLL JVM -.deploys.-> LoanDLL JVM -.deploys.-> SecDLL MySQL -.deploys.-> DBSchema classDef device fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:2px classDef env fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px classDef art fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px classDef tier fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c,stroke-width:1px,stroke-dasharray: 3 3 class ClientPC,WebServer,AppServer,DBServer device class IIS,JVM,MySQL env class WebApp,BookDLL,MemberDLL,LoanDLL,SecDLL,DBSchema art class Tier1,Tier2,Tier3,Tier4 tier ``` > **Reading Guide**: Solid lines between devices = *communication paths* (labeled with protocol). Lines between device and execution environment = *containment* (the runtime runs on the device). Dashed arrows = *deployment* (artifact placed on runtime). --- ## 4.4 The Component–Artifact–Node Triangle ```mermaid flowchart TB C[Component
Design-time concept
e.g. BookMgmtComponent] A[Artifact
Implementation-time file
e.g. BookMgmt.dll] N[Node
Run-time hardware
e.g. AppServer] C -- realizes --> A A -- deployed on --> N classDef ct fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:2px classDef at fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px classDef nt fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c,stroke-width:2px class C ct class A at class N nt ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > This is the **single most important triad** in KTU exams. Memorize the verbs: Component **realizes** Artifact, Artifact is **deployed on** Node. Examiners love asking: *"What is the relationship between a component and a node?"* — The correct answer is: **A component does NOT directly relate to a node. The artifact bridges them.** --- ## 4.5 Stereotype Notation Cheat-Sheet (Mermaid Block) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph StEx[executable stereotype] Exe[App.exe] end subgraph StLib[library stereotype] Lib[Utils.dll] end subgraph StTab[table stereotype] Tab[Users.sql] end subgraph StDoc[document stereotype] Doc[README.pdf] end classDef exe fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,stroke-width:1px classDef lib fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:1px classDef tab fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:1px classDef doc fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:1px class Exe exe class Lib lib class Tab tab class Doc doc ``` # 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **Differentiate between a Component and a Class in UML.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Class | Component | |---|---|---| | **Nature** | Logical abstraction; a blueprint | Physical packaging unit; a deployable file | | **Granularity** | One class = one concept | One component = many classes + interfaces | | **Lifetime** | Exists at design time | Realized at implementation time, lives on a node | | **Replacement** | Cannot be replaced at runtime | Can be swapped if interfaces are preserved | | **UML Notation** | Rectangle with 3 compartments | Rectangle with two small rectangles on the left side | | **Contains** | Attributes, operations | Classes, interfaces, artifacts | > **Mark Splitting (KTU pattern)**: 1 mark for *what a class is*, 1 mark for *what a component is*, 1 mark for the *key difference* (deployment vs. design). --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **List and explain any THREE stereotypes used in UML Component Diagrams.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **`<>`** — A program that can be executed on a node. *Example*: `Browser.exe`, `App.jar`. 2. **`<>`** — A static or dynamic library file with reusable functionality. *Example*: `MathLib.dll`, `libcrypto.so`. 3. **`<
>`** — A database table artifact generated as part of the system. *Example*: `Orders.sql` (DDL script). > **Mark Splitting**: ½ mark per stereotype name + ½ mark per explanation/example = 3 marks total. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > As per **KTU 2024 ESE regulations**, Module 4 long questions carry **14 marks** with internal choice. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**. The question paper setter will offer exactly TWO alternative 14-mark questions; the student answers ONE. --- ### **📘 Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper 2]* **Consider an Online Shopping System with the following use cases: "Browse Products", "Add to Cart", "Checkout", "Make Payment", "Track Order", and "Send Email Notification". The system has classes such as `Product`, `Cart`, `Order`, `Payment`, `Customer`, `EmailService`, `PaymentGateway`, and corresponding controllers/repositories. Answer the following:** **(a) Design the Component Diagram by grouping classes into at least 4 components. Justify your packaging decisions using the KTU Decision Matrix.** **(7 marks)** **(b) Design the Deployment Diagram for a 3-tier architecture (Client, Web Server, App Server, DB Server) and show all artifact deployments and communication paths.** **(7 marks)** --- #### ✅ Model Solution for Q.A(a) — Component Diagram (7 marks) **Step 1: Enumerate classes (½ mark)** The 13 classes are: `Product`, `Cart`, `Order`, `Payment`, `Customer`, `EmailService`, `PaymentGateway`, `ProductController`, `CartController`, `OrderController`, `CustomerRepository`, `ProductRepository`, `OrderRepository`. **Step 2: Cluster by responsibility (2 marks)** We apply the KTU Decision Matrix and group by domain responsibility: | Component | Classes Included | Justification | |---|---|---| | `CatalogComponent` | `Product`, `ProductController`, `ProductRepository` | All handle product browsing and listing | | `CartComponent` | `Cart`, `CartController` | Cart logic is a tightly-coupled single workflow | | `OrderComponent` | `Order`, `OrderController`, `OrderRepository` | Order management is cohesive and team-aligned | | `PaymentComponent` | `Payment`, `PaymentGateway` | Payment is a third-party integration; isolated for security | | `CustomerComponent` | `Customer`, `CustomerRepository` | Customer data is reused across many workflows | | `NotificationComponent` | `EmailService` | Cross-cutting service used by Order, Payment, Cart | **Step 3: Define interfaces (1 mark)** | Component | Provided Interface | Required Interfaces | |---|---|---| | `CatalogComponent` | `IProductService` | `IAuthenticationService` | | `CartComponent` | `ICartService` | `IProductService`, `IAuthenticationService` | | `OrderComponent` | `IOrderService` | `ICartService`, `IPaymentService`, `INotificationService` | | `PaymentComponent` | `IPaymentService` | `INotificationService` | | `CustomerComponent` | `ICustomerService` | (none) | | `NotificationComponent` | `INotificationService` | (none) | **Step 4: Component Diagram (3½ marks)** ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Cat[CatalogComponent] PC[ProductController] end subgraph CartC[CartComponent] CC[CartController] end subgraph OrdC[OrderComponent] OC[OrderController] end subgraph PayC[PaymentComponent] PG[PaymentGateway] end subgraph CusC[CustomerComponent] CR[CustomerRepository] end subgraph NotC[NotificationComponent] ES[EmailService] end IProd[/IProductService/] ICart[/ICartService/] IOrd[/IOrderService/] IPay[/IPaymentService/] ICus[/ICustomerService/] INot[/INotificationService/] Cat --- IProd CartC --- ICart OrdC --- IOrd PayC --- IPay CusC --- ICus NotC --- INot CartC -. requires .-> IProd OrdC -. requires .-> ICart OrdC -. requires .-> IPay OrdC -. requires .-> INot PayC -. requires .-> INot ``` > **Examiner's Note**: 3½ marks awarded for the *correct diagram* (interfaces, dependencies, realiz relationships). Students typically lose marks here for: (i) drawing arrows in wrong direction, (ii) confusing lollipop vs. socket notation, (iii) placing classes inside component boxes correctly. --- #### ✅ Model Solution for Q.A(b) — Deployment Diagram (7 marks) **Step 1: Identify nodes (2 marks)** - **Client Tier**: `UserLaptop` (`<>`) - **Presentation Tier**: `WebServer` (`<>`), `IIS_Runtime` (`<>`) - **Application Tier**: `AppServer` (`<>`), `JVM_Runtime` (`<>`) - **Data Tier**: `DBServer` (`<>`), `MySQL_Server` (`<>`) **Step 2: Map artifacts (2 marks)** | Component | Artifact | |---|---| | `CatalogComponent` | `Catalog.jar` | | `CartComponent` | `Cart.jar` | | `OrderComponent` | `Order.jar` | | `PaymentComponent` | `PaymentGateway.jar` | | `CustomerComponent` | `Customer.jar` | | `NotificationComponent` | `Notification.jar` | | Web App | `ShopWeb.war` | | Database | `shopdb.sql` | **Step 3: Define communication paths (1 mark)** | From | To | Protocol | |---|---|---| | `UserLaptop` | `WebServer` | HTTPS | | `WebServer` | `AppServer` | REST/HTTP | | `AppServer` | `DBServer` | SQL/TCP | **Step 4: Deployment Diagram (2 marks)** ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph T1[Client] UL[UserLaptop] end subgraph T2[Web] WS[WebServer] IIS[IIS_Runtime] WA[ShopWeb.war] end subgraph T3[App] AS[AppServer] JVM[JVM_Runtime] CJ[Catalog.jar] CAJ[Cart.jar] OJ[Order.jar] PJ[PaymentGateway.jar] CUJ[Customer.jar] NJ[Notification.jar] end subgraph T4[DB] DBS[DBServer] MY[MySQL_Server] DB[shopdb.sql] end UL -- HTTPS --> WS WS --- IIS WS -- REST --> AS AS --- JVM AS -- SQL TCP --> DBS DBS --- MY IIS -.deploys.-> WA JVM -.deploys.-> CJ JVM -.deploys.-> CAJ JVM -.deploys.-> OJ JVM -.deploys.-> PJ JVM -.deploys.-> CUJ JVM -.deploys.-> NJ MY -.deploys.-> DB ``` > **Mark Distribution (Total 7)**: Nodes = 2 marks, Artifacts = 2 marks, Communication = 1 mark, Final diagram correctness = 2 marks. --- ### **📗 Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]* **Consider a Hospital Management System with the following subsystems: Patient Registration, Doctor Scheduling, Appointment Booking, Billing, Pharmacy, and Lab Reports. Answer the following:** **(a) Identify the components, their provided/required interfaces, and draw the Component Diagram.** **(7 marks)** **(b) Design a Deployment Diagram assuming: 1 client PC, 1 web server running Apache Tomcat, 1 application server running JBoss, and 1 database server running Oracle. Show all artifact deployments.** **(7 marks)** --- #### ✅ Model Solution for Q.B(a) — Component Identification (7 marks) **Step 1: Group classes into 5 components (3 marks)** | Component | Responsibility | |---|---| | `PatientComponent` | Manages patient data and registration | | `DoctorComponent` | Manages doctor profiles and scheduling | | `AppointmentComponent` | Books and tracks appointments | | `BillingComponent` | Generates bills and tracks payments | | `PharmacyComponent` | Manages medicine inventory and prescriptions | | `LabReportComponent` | Generates and stores lab reports | | `CrossCuttingComponent` | Authentication, logging, notifications | **Step 2: Interfaces table (2 marks)** | Component | Provided | Required | |---|---|---| | `PatientComponent` | `IPatientService` | `IAuthService` | | `DoctorComponent` | `IDoctorService` | `IAuthService` | | `AppointmentComponent` | `IAppointmentService` | `IPatientService`, `IDoctorService`, `INotificationService` | | `BillingComponent` | `IBillingService` | `IPatientService`, `IAppointmentService` | | `PharmacyComponent` | `IPharmacyService` | `IPatientService` | | `LabReportComponent` | `ILabReportService` | `IPatientService`, `IDoctorService` | | `CrossCuttingComponent` | `IAuthService`, `INotificationService` | (none) | **Step 3: Component Diagram (2 marks)** ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph PatC[PatientComponent] P[Patient] end subgraph DocC[DoctorComponent] D[Doctor] end subgraph AptC[AppointmentComponent] A[Appointment] end subgraph BilC[BillingComponent] B[Bill] end subgraph PhaC[PharmacyComponent] Ph[Pharmacy] end subgraph LabC[LabReportComponent] L[LabReport] end subgraph CCC[CrossCuttingComponent] AS[AuthSvc] NS[NotifSvc] end IPS[/IPatientService/] IDS[/IDoctorService/] IAS[/IAppointmentService/] IBS[/IBillingService/] IPS2[/IPharmacyService/] ILR[/ILabReportService/] IAU[/IAuthService/] INO[/INotificationService/] PatC --- IPS DocC --- IDS AptC --- IAS BilC --- IBS PhaC --- IPS2 LabC --- ILR CCC --- IAU CCC --- INO PatC -. requires .-> IAU DocC -. requires .-> IAU AptC -. requires .-> IPS AptC -. requires .-> IDS AptC -. requires .-> INO BilC -. requires .-> IPS BilC -. requires .-> IAS PhaC -. requires .-> IPS LabC -. requires .-> IPS LabC -. requires .-> IDS ``` --- #### ✅ Model Solution for Q.B(b) — Deployment Diagram (7 marks) **Nodes (2 marks)**: - `<>` `ClientPC` - `<>` `WebServer`, `<>` `Apache_Tomcat` - `<>` `AppServer`, `<>` `JBoss_Runtime` - `<>` `DBServer`, `<>` `Oracle_11g` **Artifacts (2 marks)**: - `HMS.war` on Tomcat - `Patient.jar`, `Doctor.jar`, `Appointment.jar`, `Billing.jar`, `Pharmacy.jar`, `LabReport.jar`, `Common.jar` on JBoss - `hospitaldb.sql` on Oracle **Communication (1 mark)**: HTTPS, HTTP, JDBC/TCP **Diagram (2 marks)**: ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Tier1[Client] CP[ClientPC] end subgraph Tier2[Web] WS[WebServer] AT[Apache_Tomcat] WAR[HMS.war] end subgraph Tier3[App] AS[AppServer] JB[JBoss_Runtime] PJ[Patient.jar] DJ[Doctor.jar] AJ[Appointment.jar] BJ[Billing.jar] PHJ[Pharmacy.jar] LJ[LabReport.jar] CJ[Common.jar] end subgraph Tier4[DB] DS[DBServer] OR[Oracle_11g] DB[hospitaldb.sql] end CP -- HTTPS --> WS WS --- AT WS -- HTTP --> AS AS --- JB AS -- JDBC --> DS DS --- OR AT -.deploys.-> WAR JB -.deploys.-> PJ JB -.deploys.-> DJ JB -.deploys.-> AJ JB -.deploys.-> BJ JB -.deploys.-> PHJ JB -.deploys.-> LJ JB -.deploys.-> CJ OR -.deploys.-> DB ``` --- > [!WARNING] > ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls > 1. **Confusing Component with Node**: Students often draw components directly on hardware. ❌ Components are deployed via *artifacts*. Always insert the artifact in the middle. > 2. **Wrong arrow direction**: The *realize* relationship goes from **component to interface** (component provides the interface). The *dependency* goes from **requiring component to required interface**. > 3. **Forgetting multiplicity on deployment arrows**: If `Catalog.jar` is deployed on 2 app servers for load balancing, write `<<2>>` near the arrow. > 4. **No communication protocol labels**: Bare lines between devices get only partial credit. Always label with **HTTPS / TCP / SQL**. > 5. **Forgetting the `<>` stereotype**: Many students draw a runtime as a plain box. Always use the stereotype. > 6. **Mixing class notation with component notation**: A component is a rectangle with **two small rectangles on the left side**. A class is a rectangle with **3 horizontal compartments**. Examiners will deduct ½ mark per incorrect notation. > 7. **Skipping the justification**: Even if the diagram is correct, KTU 2024 requires a *written* justification using the Decision Matrix for full marks. --- ## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > 🎯 **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Physical Packaging** - ✅ **Physical Packaging = Component Diagram + Deployment Diagram** - ✅ A **Component** is a *design-time* grouping of classes; an **Artifact** is the *file* that realizes it; a **Node** is the *hardware* that hosts the artifact - ✅ The **5 properties** of a component: Encapsulation, Substitutability, Cohesion, Reusability, Deployability - ✅ **Component stereotypes**: `<>`, `<>`, `<
>`, `<>`, `<>` - ✅ **Node stereotypes**: `<>` (hardware) and `<>` (software runtime) - ✅ **Provided Interface** = lollipop (○); **Required Interface** = socket (⌒); Connect via dependency arrow - ✅ **Decision Matrix** groups classes by: Coupling, Common Purpose, Same Team, Version Co-evolution, Reuse Boundary - ✅ **Deployment multiplicity** is shown at the artifact-tail of the dashed arrow - ✅ **Communication paths** are solid lines between devices, labeled with the network protocol - ✅ **4+1 View Model**: Physical View = Component Diagram + Deployment Diagram - ✅ **7-Step Master Recipe**: Enumerate → Cluster → Interface → Artifact → Node → Deploy → Connect - ✅ **Rule R9**: Never deploy a *class* on a *node*; only *artifacts* may be deployed - ✅ **Kruchten's 4+1 Views** mapping: Logical → Class Diagram, Process → Sequence/Activity, **Physical → Component + Deployment**, Development → Package, Use Case → Use-Case Diagram - ✅ **Kotlin/Java analogy**: A `.jar` file is an artifact; a `package` in Java source code is a UML package; the JVM is an execution environment node - ✅ **Modern parallel**: Component = Microservice, Artifact = Docker Image, Node = Kubernetes Pod / VM - ✅ The **most-tested KTU 14-marker**: "*Design the Component and Deployment Diagrams for the given system*" — practice this pattern!

topics.title: Documenting Design Decisions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCNT604 - OBJECT ORIENTED MODELING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module - 4
> - **Topic:** Documenting Design Decisions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

In the context of **Object Oriented Modeling and Design (OOMD)**, **Documenting Design Decisions** is the systematic, structured process of capturing, recording, and communicating every architectural and detailed design choice that is made during the transformation of a system's requirements model (analysis artifacts) into its object-oriented design model.

A **Design Decision** is any binding commitment made by the design team that influences the structure, behavior, component decomposition, or technology selection of the system under development. Documentation ensures that these decisions are *traceable*, *justified*, *reversible*, and *comprehensible* to all current and future stakeholders.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 4):**
> Design decisions are not just the **final** diagrams. They are the **reasoning** behind the diagrams — the *why* that turns a drawing into an engineered artifact.

According to the standard Object-Oriented Software Engineering convention (Bruegge-Dutoit / Rumbaugh-Booch-Jacobson lineage adopted by KTU), a design decision is documented using three core properties:

| Property | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Decision** | What was actually chosen (the artifact, pattern, or structure). |
| **Rationale** | Why this particular choice was made over the available alternatives. |
| **Alternatives** | What other options were evaluated and why they were rejected. |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are building a house. You do not simply hand over the final blueprint. You also maintain a **project diary** that answers questions such as:

- *"Why did we choose a sloped roof instead of a flat one?"* (Rationale)
- *"Did we consider solar panels? Why were they dropped?"* (Alternatives rejected)
- *"What is the load-bearing capacity we assumed for the foundation?"* (Constraints)

Now, if a junior engineer joins you five years later, they can read the diary and **understand the house without guessing**. Without this diary, they might tear down a perfectly load-bearing wall thinking it is decorative.

**In software, this diary is the "Documentation of Design Decisions."**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Code tells you **WHAT** the system does. Design documentation tells you **WHY** it does it that way, and **WHAT ELSE** could have been done.

### 1.3 The Three Pillars of a Well-Documented Design Decision

Every documented decision in OOMD must record three things, summarized in the table below.

| Pillar | Formal Name | What it Captures |
|---|---|---|
| **1. The Choice** | Decision / Outcome | The selected model element (class, pattern, architecture). |
| **2. The Reasoning** | Rationale / Justification | Trade-offs, non-functional requirements (NFRs), quality attributes. |
| **3. The Context** | Constraints / Assumptions | Pre-existing conditions, standards, business rules. |

> [!TIP]
> A common student pitfall is to **only** submit UML diagrams and treat them as documentation. In KTU valuation, a diagram **without a written rationale** is considered *incomplete documentation* and attracts partial marks.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

### 2.1 Why Document Design Decisions? (The Engineering Justification)

Software engineering research (and KTU's outcome-based pedagogy) lists four non-negotiable reasons:

1. **Knowledge Preservation**: Designers rotate between projects. Documentation outlives the original author.
2. **Communication Medium**: Acts as the contract between architects, designers, and coders.
3. **Change Management**: When a system must evolve, knowing *why* a decision was made prevents dangerous regressions.
4. **Verification & Validation**: Allows reviewers to audit whether the design satisfies the stated non-functional requirements (performance, security, maintainability).

### 2.2 Taxonomy of Design Decisions (Classified by Scope)

KTU expects students to recognize three hierarchical layers of decisions:

- **Architectural Decisions**: System-wide, hard to reverse. Example: *"We will use a 3-tier client-server architecture."*
- **Tactical / Pattern Decisions**: Mid-level, local to a subsystem. Example: *"We will use the Observer pattern for the notification subsystem."*
- **Implementation / Detailed Decisions**: Low-level, easily reversed. Example: *"We will use a HashMap over a TreeMap for caching."*

### 2.3 The Six Components of a Complete Design Decision Record

A KTU-compliant design decision record typically includes the following six logical blocks. The student's notes should map each design artifact (UML diagram, class header, deployment node) to at least one of these blocks.

| # | Component | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Context** | What is the problem or requirement driving the decision? |
| 2 | **Decision** | What was finally chosen? (Class, pattern, interface, framework). |
| 3 | **Status** | Proposed / Accepted / Deprecated / Superseded. |
| 4 | **Consequences** | Positive and negative outcomes. Trade-offs made explicit. |
| 5 | **Alternatives Considered** | List of rejected options with one-line rejection reason. |
| 6 | **Related Artifacts** | Cross-reference to Use Cases, Classes, NFRs, UML diagrams. |

### 2.4 Engineering Utility in Industry

Documented design decisions feed directly into:

- **Software Architecture Documentation (SAD)** — IEEE 1471 / ISO 42010 compliance.
- **Architecture Decision Records (ADR)** — Lightweight modern format (Michael Nygard).
- **Design Reviews** — Formal Fagan inspections.
- **Onboarding Kits** — New developers become productive faster.
- **Compliance Audits** — Especially in safety-critical domains (avionics, medical).

> [!NOTE]
> The **Agile Manifesto** values "working software over comprehensive documentation," but the very next principle acknowledges the value of *effective* documentation. Recording design rationale is the *minimum* documentation that even agile teams cannot skip.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet (Cheat Table)

Since this module does not involve physical formulas, the "formula sheet" equivalent is the **Decision Documentation Template**, which is a high-yield, exam-ready structure.

| Section | Template Sentence / Phrase |
|---|---|
| **Problem** | "The system requires $\langle$functional/non-functional requirement$\rangle$." |
| **Decision** | "We have decided to use $\langle$pattern/technology/structure$\rangle$." |
| **Rationale** | "This decision satisfies $\langle$NFR$\rangle$ and aligns with $\langle$principle$\rangle$." |
| **Trade-off** | "The cost is reduced $\langle$quality attribute$\rangle$ in exchange for $\langle$benefit$\rangle$." |
| **Alternative A** | "Considered $\langle$option A$\rangle$, rejected because $\langle$reason$\rangle$." |
| **Alternative B** | "Considered $\langle$option B$\rangle$, rejected because $\langle$reason$\rangle$." |
| **Status** | "Proposed on $\langle$date$\rangle$ by $\langle$author$\rangle$." |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU valuation, the **Trade-off** line is the single most-marks-bearing element. Examiners specifically look for the phrase "in exchange for" or its synonyms. Missing trade-offs typically cost **2 marks** out of 7 in a 14-mark design question.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Analytical Procedure & Worked Examples

### 3.1 The Six-Step Documentation Procedure (Exhaustive Walkthrough)

Below is the canonical procedure for documenting a design decision, written in the exact sequence a student should follow during the KTU 14-mark design question.

**Step 1 — Identify the Driving Requirement.**
Begin by referencing a *specific* Use Case or Non-Functional Requirement (NFR). A decision without a driving requirement is considered arbitrary.

**Step 2 — Enumerate Candidate Solutions.**
List at least **two** alternatives. A common mistake is to list only one. KTU expects comparative analysis.

**Step 3 — Apply Object-Oriented Design Principles.**
Evaluate candidates against principles such as SOLID, GRASP, or established GoF patterns. Specifically check:
- Single Responsibility (SRP)
- Open/Closed Principle (OCP)
- Liskov Substitution Principle (LSP)
- Dependency Inversion (DIP)

**Step 4 — Identify Trade-offs.**
Every decision sacrifices something. State explicitly:
- What is gained (benefit).
- What is sacrificed (cost).
- Under what circumstances the cost becomes unacceptable.

**Step 5 — Record the Final Decision.**
State the chosen artifact, class, pattern, or architecture in a single declarative sentence.

**Step 6 — Cross-Reference Artifacts.**
Link the decision to:
- The Use Case that triggered it.
- The Class Diagram that implements it.
- The Sequence Diagram that validates the runtime behavior.

### 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Documenting a Pattern Choice

**Scenario:** A banking system must notify multiple UI components (mobile app, web dashboard, email service) whenever a transaction is processed.

**Step-by-Step Documentation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Problem:} \quad & \text{The system must broadcast transaction events} \\
& \text{to $\geq$3 loosely-coupled subscribers without hardcoding} \\
& \text{them in the Transaction class.} \\[4pt]
\text{Candidates:} \quad & A: \text{Polling-based notification} \\
& B: \text{Observer pattern (Subject–Observer)} \\
& C: \text{Pub-Sub message broker (e.g., Kafka)} \\[4pt]
\text{Evaluation:} \quad & A \Rightarrow \text{Violates OCP; high latency.} \\
& B \Rightarrow \text{In-process, low latency, low complexity.} \\
& C \Rightarrow \text{Strong but introduces external dependency} \\
& \text{and operational overhead.} \\[4pt]
\text{Decision:} \quad & \text{Adopt the Observer pattern for in-process} \\
& \text{notification.} \\[4pt]
\text{Trade-off:} \quad & \text{Gain loose coupling and OCP compliance.} \\
& \text{Cost: no cross-process scalability.} \\
& \text{If cross-process is later required,} \\
& \text{migrate to Pub-Sub.} \\[4pt]
\text{Status:} \quad & \text{Accepted (Version 1.0).} \\
\text{Cross-Refs:} \quad & \text{Use Case UC-04 (Process Transaction);} \\
& \text{Class Diagram CD-02; Sequence Diagram SD-05.}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Documenting an Architectural Choice

**Scenario:** Choose the deployment architecture for an e-commerce platform that must support 10,000 concurrent users.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NFR Driving:} \quad & \text{Performance (10k RPS), Availability (99.9%).} \\[4pt]
\text{Alternatives:} \quad & A: \text{Monolithic deployment.} \\
& B: \text{3-tier client-server.} \\
& C: \text{Microservices with load balancer.} \\[4pt]
\text{Decision Matrix (Verbal):} \quad & A \Rightarrow \text{Fails NFR (no horizontal scale).} \\
& B \Rightarrow \text{Meets base NFR; fails peak load.} \\
& C \Rightarrow \text{Meets all NFRs; high DevOps cost.} \\[4pt]
\text{Decision:} \quad & \text{Choose 3-tier initially;} \\
& \text{extract Order \& Payment into microservices in Phase 2.} \\[4pt]
\text{Rationale:} \quad & \text{Phased migration reduces risk and aligns} \\
& \text{with the 6-month MVP deadline.}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix (Exam-Ready Format)

For multi-criteria decisions, the following matrix is KTU-standard.

| Criterion | Weight | Option A | Option B | Option C |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Meets Performance NFR | 0.4 | 3 | 5 | 7 |
| Meets Maintainability | 0.3 | 7 | 6 | 4 |
| Implementation Cost | 0.2 | 8 | 7 | 3 |
| Team Familiarity | 0.1 | 9 | 6 | 4 |

The weighted score for Option X is computed as:

$$
\text{Score}_X = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot r_{i,X}
$$

where $w_i$ is the weight of criterion $i$ and $r_{i,X}$ is the rating of Option $X$ on that criterion (typically on a scale of $1$ to $10$). Always report the **criterion with the highest weight first** in the rationale — examiners look for this prioritization.

### 3.5 Common Documentation Anti-Patterns

> [!WARNING]
> **Anti-Pattern 1 — "The Empty Rationale":** Writing "We chose X because it is the best." This is zero-information content. **Always** specify *best with respect to which criterion*.

> [!WARNING]
> **Anti-Pattern 2 — "The Lonely Diagram":** Submitting a UML class diagram with no accompanying decision record. UML diagrams are *artifacts*, not *documentation*. They need prose around them.

> [!WARNING]
> **Anti-Pattern 3 — "The Immutable Decision":** Failing to mark a decision as "Proposed" or "Deprecated." Every decision must have a lifecycle status.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Documentation Workflow (Mermaid Flowchart)

The diagram below illustrates the exact order in which a design team should produce, review, and finalize design decision records during a typical OOMD iteration.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Start: Identify NFR or Use Case] --> nodeB[Step 1: Enumerate >=2 Alternatives]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Step 2: Apply OO Principles SOLID GRASP]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Step 3: Build Decision Matrix]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Step 4: Explicitly State Trade offs]
    nodeE --> nodeF{Step 5: Decision Passes Review?}
    nodeF -- No --> nodeB
    nodeF -- Yes --> nodeG[Step 6: Record Final Decision Status]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Step 7: Cross Reference UML Artifacts]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Step 8: Add to Software Design Document]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[End: Decision Log Updated]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **review loop** (node F) is non-negotiable. In industry, no decision is "final" until it survives a formal architecture review. KTU expects this iteration to be visible in your answer script.

### 4.2 Architecture of a Software Design Document (SDD)

The following block diagram shows the standard structure of an IEEE 830 / IEEE 1016 compliant Software Design Description, into which design decision records are embedded.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA[SDD Top Level] --> nodeB[Section 1: Introduction]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Section 2: Architectural Design]
    nodeA --> nodeD[Section 3: Detailed Design]
    nodeA --> nodeE[Section 4: Design Decision Log]
    nodeA --> nodeF[Section 5: Data Design]
    nodeA --> nodeG[Section 6: Component Interfaces]
    nodeE --> nodeH[Entry: Context]
    nodeE --> nodeI[Entry: Decision]
    nodeE --> nodeJ[Entry: Rationale]
    nodeE --> nodeK[Entry: Alternatives]
    nodeE --> nodeL[Entry: Trade offs]
    nodeE --> nodeM[Entry: Status and Date]
```

### 4.3 Lifecycle State Machine of a Design Decision

A documented design decision transitions through well-defined lifecycle states. KTU examiners frequently test this.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> stateProposed: Draft created
    stateProposed --> stateAccepted: Review passed
    stateProposed --> stateRejected: Review failed
    stateAccepted --> stateSuperseded: New ADR replaces it
    stateAccepted --> stateDeprecated: No longer applicable
    stateSuperseded --> [*]
    stateDeprecated --> [*]
    stateRejected --> [*]
```

### 4.4 Mapping Design Decisions to UML Artifacts

The block diagram below shows which UML artifact is generated as a direct consequence of each category of design decision.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Architectural Decision] --> nodeB[Deployment Diagram]
    nodeC[Tactical Pattern Decision] --> nodeD[Class Diagram and Sequence Diagram]
    nodeE[Implementation Decision] --> nodeF[Package Diagram and Code Stub]
    nodeG[Interface Decision] --> nodeH[Component Diagram]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If you document a decision but cannot map it to a UML artifact, the decision is **abstract and unverifiable**. KTU valuation penalizes unverifiable decisions by **2 marks** in a 14-mark question.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**
What is meant by "documenting design decisions" in object-oriented design? List any three components that must be recorded for each decision.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Documenting design decisions is the systematic process of recording the architectural and detailed design choices made during object-oriented design, along with the reasoning behind those choices. It transforms implicit knowledge into an explicit, auditable artifact.

The three essential components are:

1. **Decision** — the chosen artifact (class, pattern, or architecture).
2. **Rationale** — the justification grounded in NFRs or OO principles.
3. **Alternatives** — the rejected options and the reason for rejection.

*(Award 1 mark each for the definition and the three components. Marks = 3.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary]**
Differentiate between an "architectural decision" and a "tactical/pattern decision" with one example of each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Architectural Decision | Tactical / Pattern Decision |
|---|---|---|
| Scope | System-wide | Subsystem or module |
| Reversibility | Hard to reverse | Easier to refactor |
| Example | Choosing a 3-tier client-server architecture. | Applying the Observer pattern to a notification module. |

*(Award 1 mark for definition of each, 1 mark for the example pair. Marks = 3.)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Slot A]**

**A. (a)** Explain the concept of documenting design decisions in object-oriented modeling. Describe, with a suitable example, the six components that a complete design decision record must contain. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For an online shopping system, identify and document **two** design decisions (one architectural and one pattern-based) using the standard decision record format. Clearly state the rationale, alternatives, and trade-offs for each. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) — 7 Marks:**

Documenting design decisions is the engineering practice of recording every binding design commitment made during object-oriented design, together with the reasoning, alternatives, and trade-offs that produced it. It serves four purposes: knowledge preservation, communication, change management, and design verification.

The six components of a complete decision record are:

1. **Context** — the driving requirement or NFR.
2. **Decision** — the chosen artifact.
3. **Status** — proposed, accepted, deprecated, or superseded.
4. **Consequences** — positive and negative outcomes.
5. **Alternatives Considered** — rejected options with rejection reasons.
6. **Related Artifacts** — links to Use Cases, Class Diagrams, Sequence Diagrams.

*(Allocate marks: 2 for concept, 4 for the six components with one-line explanation each, 1 for the concluding remark on purpose.)*

**(b) — 7 Marks:**

**Decision 1 — Architectural (3.5 marks):**

- **Context:** The system must support 5,000 concurrent users with 99.9% availability.
- **Decision:** Adopt a 3-tier architecture with a load balancer in front of the application tier.
- **Alternatives Considered:**
  - *Monolithic:* rejected because it cannot horizontally scale.
  - *Microservices:* rejected due to high DevOps cost and team inexperience.
- **Rationale:** Meets the availability NFR at moderate cost; aligns with team's existing skill set.
- **Trade-off:** Gain scalability; sacrifice deployment simplicity. If load exceeds 20,000 users, migrate to microservices.
- **Status:** Accepted.
- **Related Artifacts:** Use Case UC-12 (Place Order), Deployment Diagram DD-01.

**Decision 2 — Pattern-Based (3.5 marks):**

- **Context:** Multiple payment gateways (PayPal, Stripe, UPI) must be supported interchangeably.
- **Decision:** Apply the **Strategy pattern** to encapsulate each gateway as a concrete strategy.
- **Alternatives Considered:**
  - *Direct conditional dispatch:* rejected because it violates OCP.
  - *State pattern:* rejected because gateway selection is not state-driven.
- **Rationale:** Strategy pattern satisfies OCP and enables runtime selection of gateway.
- **Trade-off:** Gain flexibility; incur one extra interface and indirection overhead.
- **Status:** Accepted.
- **Related Artifacts:** Class Diagram CD-03, Sequence Diagram SD-07.

*(Award 1 mark per logical block across both decisions; ensure trade-offs are explicitly stated.)*

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Slot B — Alternative Choice]**

**B. (a)** Discuss the importance of documenting design rationale. Why is it considered more valuable than the UML diagrams themselves in long-running projects? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider a library management system where books can be issued, returned, and reserved. The system must send notifications via email, SMS, and push channels. Document the design decision for the notification subsystem, including:
- The selected design pattern.
- At least two rejected alternatives.
- The explicit trade-off.
- The lifecycle status of the decision. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) — 7 Marks:**

Design rationale is the *justification* for a design choice. It is more valuable than UML diagrams in long-running projects because:

1. **Diagrams Decay, Rationale Endures.** Code refactors invalidate class diagrams within months; rationale remains valid for years.
2. **Guards Against Regression.** New developers may "optimize" away a feature that was a deliberate trade-off, not an oversight.
3. **Enables Informed Evolution.** When requirements change, knowing the original rationale allows safe migration paths.
4. **Supports Audit and Compliance.** In regulated industries, the *reason* a security decision was made must be auditable.
5. **Bridges Communication.** Stakeholders, architects, and coders speak different languages; rationale is the shared layer.

*(Allocate: 2 marks for definition, 5 marks for the five justifications with one-line examples.)*

**(b) — 7 Marks:**

- **Context:** The library system must dispatch a single event (e.g., book overdue) to three independent notification channels: email, SMS, and push notification. Channels must be added or removed without modifying the core event source.
- **Decision:** Adopt the **Observer pattern** with a `NotificationSubject` and three concrete `Observer` implementations (`EmailObserver`, `SmsObserver`, `PushObserver`).
- **Alternatives Considered:**
  - *Direct call from Event class:* rejected because it couples the event to all current and future channels.
  - *Mediator pattern:* rejected as it adds central coordination not needed for one-to-many broadcast.
  - *Pub-Sub broker (Kafka):* rejected for the in-process scope and added infrastructure overhead.
- **Rationale:** Observer pattern provides loose coupling, satisfies the Open/Closed Principle, and is the minimal-complexity solution for in-process broadcasting.
- **Trade-off:** Gain OCP compliance and runtime subscriber addition; cost is that subscribers must implement the `Observer` interface and cannot cross process boundaries. If cross-process needs arise, migrate to a message broker.
- **Status:** Accepted (Version 1.0, dated today).
- **Related Artifacts:** Use Case UC-08 (Reserve Book), Class Diagram CD-04, Sequence Diagram SD-09.

*(Award marks: 2 for pattern selection + rationale, 2 for alternatives, 1 for trade-off, 1 for status, 1 for cross-references.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert:**
>
> - **Pitfall 1:** Writing only the UML class diagram for the Observer pattern and stopping. **Lost marks: ~3.** The decision record *is* the answer; the diagram is supplementary.
> - **Pitfall 2:** Listing alternatives without an explicit rejection reason (e.g., "we rejected X" with no follow-up). **Lost marks: 1 per missing reason.**
> - **Pitfall 3:** Omitting the **trade-off** line. This is the single most-marks-bearing element. **Lost marks: 2.**
> - **Pitfall 4:** Failing to assign a **lifecycle status** (Accepted/Proposed). Examiners treat this as an incomplete document. **Lost marks: 1.**
> - **Pitfall 5:** Using absolute value notation like $\vert x \vert$ inside a markdown table — this *breaks* the table rendering and may cost presentation marks. Use $\vert x \vert$ inside LaTeX blocks or $\lvert x \rvert$ for safety.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition**: Documenting design decisions = recording the **what, why, and what-else** of every binding design choice.
- **Three Pillars**: Decision, Rationale, Alternatives.
- **Six Components**: Context, Decision, Status, Consequences, Alternatives, Related Artifacts.
- **Three Scopes**: Architectural (system-wide), Tactical (subsystem/pattern), Implementation (low-level).
- **Trade-off is Mandatory**: Every decision must explicitly state *what is gained* and *what is sacrificed*. The phrase *"in exchange for"* is exam gold.
- **Lifecycle States**: Proposed → Accepted → (Superseded OR Deprecated). A rejected decision ends at Rejected.
- **Mapping to UML**: Architectural → Deployment Diagram; Tactical Pattern → Class + Sequence; Implementation → Package Diagram; Interface → Component Diagram.
- **Anti-Patterns to Avoid**: Empty rationale, lonely diagram, immutable (status-less) decisions, single-option analysis (always list $\geq$ 2 alternatives).
- **Decision Matrix Formula** (for multi-criteria scoring):

$$
\text{Score}_X = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot r_{i,X}
$$

  where $w_i$ is criterion weight and $r_{i,X}$ is the rating of Option $X$ on that criterion.

- **Industry Standards to Mention in Answers**: IEEE 1016 (SDD), ISO/IEC 42010 (Architecture description), ADR (Nygard), IEEE 830 (SRS).
- **OO Principles to Cross-Reference**: SOLID (especially OCP, DIP, SRP), GRASP (especially Protected Variations, Indirection).
- **KTU Mark-Saver Tip**: Always end a 14-mark design question with a numbered list of **status and cross-references**. Examiners award 1–2 marks for this administrative completeness.
- **Format Rule**: Use markdown tables *without* the pipe symbol inside LaTeX expressions. Replace $\vert x \vert$ with $\lvert x \rvert$ or wrap the entire expression in a code block to avoid parser breakage.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advanced Models: Unified Modeling Language (UML)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCNT604 - OBJECT ORIENTED MODELING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module - 4
> - **Topic:** Advanced Models: Unified Modeling Language (UML)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is the Unified Modeling Language (UML)?

**Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
The **Unified Modeling Language (UML)** is a *general-purpose, standardized, graphical modeling language* in the field of software engineering. It is managed by the **Object Management Group (OMG)** and is used to *visualize, specify, construct, and document* the artifacts of a software-intensive system. UML is **not** a programming language nor a formal process; it is a **visual language for modeling** that combines the best practices from object-oriented (OO) modeling techniques such as Booch, OMT (Object Modeling Technique), and OOSE (Object-Oriented Software Engineering).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Terminology You Must Memorize:**
> - UML is a **language**, not a methodology.
> - UML is **standardized** under ISO/IEC 19505.
> - The current stable major revision widely examined is **UML 2.5** (and UML 2.x series).
> - UML was created by **Grady Booch, James Rumbaugh, and Ivar Jacobson** — the "Three Amigos" of OOAD.

## 1.2 Why UML? — The Need for a Standard Visual Language

Before UML, organizations used **multiple, incompatible notations** (Booch method, OMT, OOSE, Shlaer-Mellor, Coad-Yourdon, etc.). This created:
- **Communication breakdown** between analysts, designers, and developers.
- **Tool incompatibility** — one CASE tool could not read another’s models.
- **Documentation chaos** when projects switched methodologies.

UML was created to **unify** these notations into a single, expressive, and extensible visual standard.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — “The Blueprints of a Building”

Imagine a civil engineer designing a skyscraper. Before construction begins, the engineer creates:
- **Floor plans** (showing rooms, doors, walls) → analogous to a **Class Diagram** in UML.
- **Electrical wiring diagrams** (showing how power flows) → analogous to a **Sequence/Communication Diagram**.
- **Plumbing schematics** (showing water flow and state transitions) → analogous to **Activity / State Machine Diagrams**.
- **Site layout** (where the building, generators, and elevators are placed) → analogous to a **Deployment Diagram**.
- **A bill of materials** (lists of components like elevators, HVAC units) → analogous to a **Component Diagram**.

UML is the **master blueprint set** for software. Just as a building cannot be safely constructed from verbal descriptions alone, complex software cannot be reliably designed without a **shared, standardized visual language**. UML provides that language.

## 1.4 The Four Purposes of UML (Rumbaugh’s Classic Definition)

According to the OMG specification, UML serves four primary purposes:

| # | Purpose | Intuitive Meaning |
|---|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Visualize** | Render the system in a clear graphical form so stakeholders can *see* it. |
| 2 | **Specify** | Build precise, unambiguous models that describe the system’s structure and behavior. |
| 3 | **Construct** | Provide enough information to allow code generation (forward engineering) and reverse engineering. |
| 4 | **Document** | Capture decisions, requirements, and architecture for future maintenance. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In KTU ESE questions, the phrase *"Discuss the features of UML"* almost always expects the four purposes (Visualize, Specify, Construct, Document) as part of the answer.

## 1.5 The Three Building Blocks of UML

Every UML diagram is built from three fundamental building blocks:

1. **Things (Elements)** — the *nouns* of the language (classes, objects, interfaces, use cases, components, etc.).
2. **Relationships** — the *connectors* that link things (association, aggregation, composition, generalization, dependency, realization).
3. **Diagrams** — *organized views* that present selected aspects of the model (13 standard diagram types in UML 2.x).

## 1.6 Conceptual Architecture of UML

UML can be visualized as a **four-layer meta-modeling architecture** (a strict hierarchy), defined using the **MOF (Meta-Object Facility)** — a four-level architecture used by OMG:

$$
\text{M0: User Objects} \rightarrow \text{M1: User Model} \rightarrow \text{M2: UML Metamodel} \rightarrow \text{M3: MOF Metamodel}
$$

| Layer | Name | Example |
|-------|------|---------|
| **M3** | Meta-Metamodel (MOF) | Defines the language to describe metamodels |
| **M2** | Metamodel | UML specification — defines what a *Class* or *Association* *is* |
| **M1** | User Model | Your specific system’s class diagram |
| **M0** | Instance (Run-time) | The actual live objects in memory at runtime |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner’s favourite:** In KTU ESE, the question *"Explain the architecture of UML"* expects this **M0–M3 metalevel hierarchy** with examples. Memorize the table above.

---

## 1.7 Classification of UML 2.x Diagrams

UML 2.x defines **14 official diagram types**, broadly grouped into two families:

### A. Structure Diagrams (Static View — “What is in the system?”)
1. **Class Diagram**
2. **Object Diagram**
3. **Component Diagram**
4. **Composite Structure Diagram**
5. **Deployment Diagram**
6. **Package Diagram**
7. **Profile Diagram** *(new in UML 2.x — used to extend UML for specific domains)*

### B. Behavior Diagrams (Dynamic View — “How does the system behave?”)
1. **Use Case Diagram**
2. **Activity Diagram**
3. **State Machine Diagram**
4. **Sequence Diagram**
5. **Communication Diagram** *(renamed Collaboration Diagram from UML 1.x)*
6. **Interaction Overview Diagram**
7. **Timing Diagram**

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Students often group Sequence + Communication + Interaction Overview + Timing as *"Interaction Diagrams"* — this is **correct**; they are a sub-family of behavior diagrams. KTU questions frequently ask to *"Compare and contrast interaction diagrams"*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 UML 2.x — The 14 Diagram Types — Complete Reference Table

| # | Diagram | Type | Purpose (One-line) | Exam Frequency in KTU |
|---|---------|------|--------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | **Class Diagram** | Structure | Shows classes, attributes, operations, and static relationships. | ★★★★★ (Most frequent) |
| 2 | **Object Diagram** | Structure | Snapshot of class instances and their links at a moment in time. | ★★★ |
| 3 | **Component Diagram** | Structure | Shows software components and their dependencies (interfaces). | ★★★★ |
| 4 | **Deployment Diagram** | Structure | Maps software artifacts to physical hardware nodes. | ★★★★ |
| 5 | **Package Diagram** | Structure | Groups related UML elements into logical units. | ★★★ |
| 6 | **Composite Structure Diagram** | Structure | Internal structure of a class/ component (parts, ports, connectors). | ★★ |
| 7 | **Profile Diagram** | Structure | Extends UML with domain-specific stereotypes. | ★ |
| 8 | **Use Case Diagram** | Behavior | Captures functional requirements from an actor’s perspective. | ★★★★★ |
| 9 | **Activity Diagram** | Behavior | Models workflows, business processes, and parallel behavior. | ★★★★ |
| 10 | **State Machine Diagram** | Behavior | Models the lifecycle of an object as states and transitions. | ★★★★★ |
| 11 | **Sequence Diagram** | Behavior | Time-ordered message exchange between objects. | ★★★★★ |
| 12 | **Communication Diagram** | Behavior | Object interactions with numbered messages (focus on links). | ★★★ |
| 13 | **Interaction Overview Diagram** | Behavior | Activity diagram whose nodes are interaction diagrams. | ★★ |
| 14 | **Timing Diagram** | Behavior | State/value changes of an object over a time axis. | ★★ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield for KTU:** Expect 14-mark questions on **Class, Use Case, Sequence, State Machine, Component, and Deployment** diagrams. The others appear in 3-mark or short conceptual questions.

## 2.2 The Six UML Relationships — The Backbone of Every Diagram

This is one of the **most asked topics** in KTU ESE. Memorize the table below.

| # | Relationship | Symbol (UML Notation) | Semantic Strength | Real-World Analogy | Lifetime Dependency |
|---|--------------|----------------------|-------------------|---------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Association** | A solid line between two classes (may include a label and multiplicity) | Weakest structural relationship | “Teacher *teaches* Student” (a teacher can exist without students) | No lifetime dependency |
| 2 | **Directed Association** | A solid line with an arrowhead | Same as association, but navigation is one-way | “Customer places Order” (you only navigate from Customer to Order) | No lifetime dependency |
| 3 | **Aggregation** | A solid line with a **hollow (open) diamond** on the *whole* side | Weak “whole–part”; parts can outlive the whole | “Department *contains* Professor” (professor can exist without a department) | No lifetime dependency |
| 4 | **Composition** | A solid line with a **filled (solid) diamond** on the *whole* side | Strong “whole–part”; parts die with the whole | “House *contains* Room” (room cannot exist without house) | **Strong** lifetime dependency |
| 5 | **Generalization** | A solid line with a **hollow triangle** on the *parent* (superclass) side | “is-a” inheritance relationship | “SUV *is-a* Car” | Independent lifetimes |
| 6 | **Realization / Implementation** | A **dashed line with a hollow triangle** | Class implements an interface; or a component realizes a behavior | “Circle *implements* Shape” | Independent |
| 7 | **Dependency** | A **dashed line with an arrowhead** | “uses-a” — the change in supplier affects client | “Client depends on Service” (transient use) | No lifetime dependency |

### The Key Distinction (Common KTU Question: 3 or 14 marks)

- **Association** = a generic semantic connection.
- **Aggregation** = weak “has-a” *(whole does not own part)*.
- **Composition** = strong “owns-a” *(whole owns part; parts cannot exist without whole)*.
- **Generalization** = “is-a” — used **only** for inheritance.
- **Realization** = relationship between a class and an interface (or between a use case and a collaboration).
- **Dependency** = the weakest link — change in one element may require change in the other.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Trick:** *“Aggregation = empty diamond; Composition = filled diamond. Empty = Empty-handed (part is free to leave). Filled = Filled with glue (part is permanently attached).”*

## 2.3 Multiplicity Notation — Universal UML Table

Multiplicity specifies **how many instances** of one class are linked to one instance of the other.

| Notation | Meaning |
|----------|---------|
| `1` | Exactly one |
| `0..1` | Zero or one (optional) |
| `*` or `0..*` | Zero or many |
| `1..*` | One or many |
| `n` | Exactly n |
| `n..m` | Between n and m inclusive |

## 2.4 Visibility Modifiers (Applied to Attributes and Operations)

| Symbol | Visibility | Java/C++ Equivalent |
|--------|------------|----------------------|
| `+` | **Public** | `public` |
| `-` | **Private** | `private` |
| `#` | **Protected** | `protected` |
| `~` | **Package** *(only in UML 2.x)* | package-private |

## 2.5 UML Extensibility Mechanisms — Stereotypes, Constraints, Tagged Values

UML is a **closed language** with well-defined syntax and semantics, but it must support domain-specific modeling. OMG defines **three extensibility mechanisms**:

| Mechanism | Notation | Purpose | Example |
|-----------|----------|---------|---------|
| **Stereotype** | `<<name>>` placed above an element | Adds new *kind* of element by extending existing metaclass | `<<interface>>`, `<<actor>>`, `<<entity>>`, `<<boundary>>`, `<<control>>` |
| **Tagged Value** | `{name = value}` inside guillemets | Adds new *properties* to existing elements | `{version=1.2, author=Rumbaugh}` |
| **Constraint** | `{expression}` in braces | Adds semantic *rules* that must be satisfied | `{ordered}`, `{readOnly}`, `{subset}` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pickup Line:** When asked *"How does UML support extensibility?"* — always answer with this **triad: Stereotype, Constraint, Tagged Value.**

## 2.6 Object Constraint Language (OCL) — Formula Sheet

OCL is a **declarative, side-effect-free, formal language** used in UML to express constraints that cannot be expressed in pure diagrams. It is part of the UML standard.

### Basic OCL Types
| OCL Type | Meaning |
|----------|---------|
| `Integer`, `Real`, `Boolean`, `String` | Standard primitive types |
| `OclAny` | The supertype of all OCL types |
| `OclVoid`, `OclInvalid` | Represents `null` and “undefined” values |
| `Set`, `Bag`, `Sequence`, `OrderedSet` | Collection types |

### Core OCL Expressions
- `self` → refers to the current object.
- `context <Type>` → declares the *type* on which the constraint is written.
- `inv: <expression>` → declares an *invariant* constraint.
- `pre: <expression>` → *precondition* of an operation.
- `post: <expression>` → *postcondition* of an operation.
- `let <var> = <expr> in <body>` → local variable definition.
- `iterate(...)`, `forAll(...)`, `exists(...)`, `select(...)`, `collect(...)` → collection iterators.

### Most-Used OCL Operators
- `=` , `<>` , `<` , `>` , `<=` , `>=`
- `and` , `or` , `not` , `implies` , `xor`
- `if...then...else...endif`

### Example — KTU-style OCL Constraint
> *“A bank account’s balance must always be non-negative.”*

```
context Account
inv: balance >= 0
```

> *“A customer may have zero or more orders, but the total order amount must be less than 1,000,000.”*

```
context Customer
inv: self.orders->collect(amount)->sum() < 1000000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** OCL is used in **model-driven development (MDD)** to write precise, executable constraints that can be checked automatically by tools (e.g., Eclipse OCL, USE). In real-world practice, OCL prevents ambiguous natural-language specifications.

## 2.7 Use Case Diagrams — The Key Constructs

| Construct | Symbol | Meaning |
|-----------|--------|---------|
| **Actor** | Stick figure (or `<<actor>>` for system actors) | External entity that interacts with the system |
| **Use Case** | Ellipse | A unit of functionality the system provides |
| **System Boundary** | Rectangle surrounding use cases | Defines the scope of the system |
| **Association** | Solid line | Communication between an actor and a use case |
| **Include** | `<<include>>` dashed arrow | Mandatory reuse — base use case always calls the included use case |
| **Extend** | `<<extend>>` dashed arrow | Optional/conditional reuse — extension only happens under certain conditions |
| **Generalization** | Solid arrow with hollow triangle | One use case/actor is a specialized form of another |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** *“Include = I cannot live without you. Extend = Sometimes I call you, but not always.”*

## 2.8 Sequence Diagram Notation — Formula Sheet

| Element | Notation |
|---------|----------|
| **Lifeline** | Dashed vertical line |
| **Activation** | Thin rectangle on a lifeline |
| **Synchronous message** | Solid line with filled arrowhead |
| **Asynchronous message** | Solid line with open arrowhead |
| **Return message** | Dashed line with open arrowhead |
| **Self-message** | Loopback arrow on a lifeline |
| **Combined Fragment** | Rectangle with operator tag (`alt`, `opt`, `loop`, `par`, `strict`, `neg`, `critical`, `ref`) |

### Combined Fragment Operators (HIGH-YIELD)
| Operator | Meaning |
|----------|---------|
| `alt` | Alternative — exactly one operand runs |
| `opt` | Optional — runs only if guard is true |
| `par` | Parallel — operands run concurrently |
| `loop` | Repeats a sequence |
| `neg` | An invalid trace (assertion of what must NOT happen) |
| `ref` | Reference to another interaction |

## 2.9 State Machine Diagram — Key Concepts

- **State**: A condition in which an object exists for a non-trivial period.
- **Initial State**: Solid filled circle — entry point.
- **Final State**: Bullseye circle (concentric circles) — termination.
- **Transition**: Arrow from one state to another, labeled with `event [guard] / action`.
- **Guard**: Boolean condition `[in brackets]`.
- **Action**: Behavior performed on transition (`/ doSomething`).
- **Internal Activity**: `entry/`, `exit/`, `do/` (activities inside a state).
- **Composite State**: A state containing nested sub-states.
- **Concurrent Substates**: Dashed line separating parallel regions inside a state.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked to draw a state machine for a *“Login Authentication”* or *“Order Processing”* system, always label transitions as **`event [guard] / action`** — this is the most common 14-mark question format.

## 2.10 Component & Deployment Diagrams — Key Constructs

| Diagram | Key Construct | Notation |
|---------|---------------|----------|
| **Component** | Component | Rectangle with two small overlapping rectangles on the left side |
| | Provided Interface | Lollipop (small circle on a stick) — component *provides* this interface |
| | Required Interface | Socket (small half-circle) — component *requires* this interface |
| | Dependency | Dashed arrow |
| **Deployment** | Node (Device) | 3D cube |
| | Node (Execution Environment) | 3D cube with `<<executionEnvironment>>` stereotype |
| | Artifact | Document icon |
| | Communication Path | Solid line between nodes |

---

## 2.11 Real-World Engineering Utility of UML

| Engineering Domain | How UML is Used |
|--------------------|------------------|
| **Enterprise Software (Banking, ERP, CRM)** | Class + Sequence diagrams drive Java/Spring code generation. |
| **Embedded / IoT Systems** | Deployment + State Machine diagrams model device lifecycles and hardware mappings. |
| **Real-Time Control Systems** | Timing + State Machine diagrams capture cycle-by-cycle behavior. |
| **Healthcare IT (HL7/FHIR standards)** | Profile Diagrams extend UML with medical stereotypes (`<<PatientRecord>>`). |
| **Automotive (AUTOSAR)** | Composite Structure + Component diagrams describe ECU software architecture. |
| **Telecommunications (4G/5G)** | Sequence diagrams model protocol handshakes (e.g., SIP, Diameter). |
| **Aerospace (DO-178C compliance)** | UML is used to produce certification-grade design documentation. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — From Problem Statement to a Class Diagram (Step-by-Step Methodology)

The KTU ESE 14-mark question often asks: *"Design a class diagram for an XYZ system."* The derivation below shows a **reproducible, exam-ready workflow**.

> **Problem Statement (Sample):**
> *"A university has many departments. Each department offers multiple courses. A course is taught by exactly one professor and may be taken by many students. A student may enroll in many courses. A professor belongs to one department. Each student is assigned an academic advisor (a professor)."*

### Step 1 — Identify Candidate Classes (Nouns)
Extract all **nouns** from the problem statement. Filter out irrelevant ones.

- *University* → system boundary, optional class
- *Department* → ✓
- *Course* → ✓
- *Professor* → ✓
- *Student* → ✓
- *Enrollment* → ✓ (this is the relationship object)

### Step 2 — Identify Attributes (Properties)
For each class, list stateful properties.

| Class | Attributes |
|-------|-----------|
| `Department` | deptID, deptName, location |
| `Course` | courseID, courseName, credits |
| `Professor` | profID, name, specialization, designation |
| `Student` | studentID, name, semester, CGPA |
| `Enrollment` | enrollmentDate, grade |

### Step 3 — Identify Operations (Behaviours)
- `Department`: `addCourse()`, `assignProfessor()`
- `Course`: `assignProfessor()`, `enrollStudent()`
- `Student`: `enrollInCourse()`, `viewGrades()`
- `Professor`: `teachCourse()`, `adviseStudent()`

### Step 4 — Identify Relationships & Multiplicities

| Relationship | Type | Multiplicity | Rationale |
|--------------|------|--------------|-----------|
| Department — Course | Aggregation (or composition) | 1 department `*` courses | A course can be archived (survive department deletion) — aggregation is preferred. |
| Department — Professor | Aggregation | 1 department `*` professors | A professor can transfer between departments. |
| Professor — Course | Association (teaches) | 1 professor `*` courses | One professor may teach multiple courses; “exactly one” per course. |
| Student — Course | Association via `Enrollment` | * — * | A student can take many courses; a course has many students. |
| Professor — Student | Association (advises) | 1 professor `*` students | Each student has one advisor; one professor advises many. |

### Step 5 — Apply Generalization (Optional)
If a hierarchy is implied (e.g., `UndergraduateStudent` and `PostgraduateStudent` both being kinds of `Student`), apply **generalization**.

### Step 6 — Apply Constraints
- Each student must have a CGPA in `[0.0, 10.0]` → OCL constraint.
- A course cannot be taught by a professor who is not in the same department → OCL constraint.

### Step 7 — Final Class Diagram Construction
(Mermaid representation is provided in Section 4.4.)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** This **7-step procedure** is the de-facto KTU answer for any “design a class diagram” question. Showing each step explicitly gives you 14 marks.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — State Machine Diagram for an ATM Transaction

> **Problem:** *Model the lifecycle of an ATM session.*

### Step 1 — Identify States
| State | Meaning |
|-------|---------|
| `Idle` | Machine waiting for user |
| `CardInserted` | Card has been accepted |
| `PIN_Entered` | User has entered PIN (validation not yet done) |
| `Authenticated` | PIN accepted |
| `SelectingTransaction` | User choosing an action |
| `Withdrawing` | Cash withdrawal in progress |
| `Depositing` | Cash deposit in progress |
| `Transferring` | Fund transfer in progress |
| `PrintingReceipt` | Optional |
| `EjectingCard` | Returning card |
| `OutOfService` | Hardware/network error |

### Step 2 — Identify Events
- `insertCard()`
- `enterPIN(p)`
- `validatePIN()`
- `selectTransaction(type)`
- `enterAmount(a)`
- `dispenseCash()`
- `ejectCard()`
- `error(...)`

### Step 3 — Identify Guards
- `[PIN correct]`, `[PIN incorrect]`
- `[balance >= amount]`
- `[network available]`

### Step 4 — Transition Table

| From State | Event | Guard | Action | To State |
|------------|-------|-------|--------|----------|
| Idle | insertCard | — | validateCard | CardInserted |
| CardInserted | enterPIN | — | — | PIN_Entered |
| PIN_Entered | validatePIN | PIN correct | openAccount | Authenticated |
| PIN_Entered | validatePIN | PIN incorrect (×3) | retainCard | OutOfService |
| Authenticated | selectTransaction | type=Withdraw | — | SelectingTransaction |
| SelectingTransaction | enterAmount | balance >= amount | debit | Withdrawing |
| Withdrawing | dispenseCash | — | printReceipt? | PrintingReceipt → EjectingCard |
| EjectingCard | ejectCard | — | reset | Idle |
| *Any State* | networkFailure | — | rollback | OutOfService |

### Step 5 — Final Diagram Construction
(Mermaid representation is provided in Section 4.6.)

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right UML Diagram

This matrix helps you decide **which diagram to draw** based on the question asked. **Memorize this — it is a frequent 7-mark sub-question.**

| If the question is about... | Draw this diagram | Because... |
|------------------------------|-------------------|------------|
| Static structure of classes | **Class Diagram** | Shows attributes, operations, and static relationships. |
| A specific scenario / object snapshot | **Object Diagram** | Captures run-time instances. |
| External user interaction / functional scope | **Use Case Diagram** | Captures actors and their goals. |
| Time-ordered message flow | **Sequence Diagram** | Shows object interactions along a time axis. |
| Message flow focused on object links | **Communication Diagram** | Shows numbered messages and link structure. |
| Workflow / business process | **Activity Diagram** | Models flow of control with decisions, forks, joins. |
| Lifecycle of an object | **State Machine Diagram** | Models states and transitions. |
| Software modules and their interfaces | **Component Diagram** | Shows modular decomposition. |
| Hardware topology and software mapping | **Deployment Diagram** | Maps artifacts to physical nodes. |
| Internal structure of a complex class | **Composite Structure Diagram** | Shows parts, ports, and internal connectors. |
| Grouping related elements | **Package Diagram** | Organizes models into units. |
| Domain-specific extension of UML | **Profile Diagram** | Adds stereotypes/constraints for a domain. |
| State/value changes over time | **Timing Diagram** | Plots state vs. time. |
| High-level overview of multiple interactions | **Interaction Overview Diagram** | Activity of interactions. |

> **Problem:** *Design a use case diagram for an Online Shopping System.*

| `Administrator` | Primary | Manages catalog |
| `PaymentGateway` | External (system actor) | Processes payments |

### Step 2 — Identify Use Cases

- `Search Product`

- `Checkout`
- `Make Payment`
- `Track Order`
- `Manage Products` (admin)
- `View Reports` (admin)

### Step 3 — Identify `<<include>>` and `<<extend>>`
| Use Case | Relationship | Target | Reason |
|----------|--------------|--------|--------|
| `Checkout` | `<<include>>` | `Verify Customer` | Cannot checkout without authentication. |
| `Make Payment` | `<<include>>` | `Validate Card` | Always required. |
| `Make Payment` | `<<include>>` | `External Payment Gateway` | Always called. |
| `Track Order` | `<<extend>>` | `Send SMS Notification` | Optional — only if user opts in. |

### Step 4 — Draw the Diagram

## 3.5 Step-by-Step Derivation — A Sequence Diagram for ATM Withdrawal

### Step 1 — Identify Objects (Lifelines)
`User`, `ATM`, `BankServer`, `CashDispenser`, `Account`

### Step 2 — Identify Messages in Time Order
1. `User → ATM : insertCard()`
2. `ATM → User : requestPIN()`
3. `User → ATM : enterPIN(1234)`
4. `ATM → BankServer : validatePIN(cardNo, 1234)`
5. `BankServer → ATM : PIN_OK`
6. `ATM → User : showMenu()`
7. `User → ATM : selectWithdraw()`
8. `ATM → User : requestAmount()`
9. `User → ATM : enterAmount(5000)`
10. `ATM → BankServer : debit(account, 5000)`
11. `BankServer → ATM : debited`
12. `ATM → CashDispenser : dispense(5000)`
13. `CashDispenser → ATM : done`
14. `ATM → User : dispenseCash(5000)`
15. `ATM → User : ejectCard()`

### Step 3 — Identify Fragments
- Wrap steps 4–5 in an `alt` block where the alternative is `alt: PIN incorrect / retain card`.
- Wrap the withdraw flow in a `loop [balance >= amount]`.

(Mermaid representation in Section 4.7.)

---

## 3.6 OCL Constraint Derivation — Worked Example

> **Requirement:** *“Every order must have at least one line item, and the total of all line-item prices must equal the order’s recorded total.”*

### Step 1 — Identify the Context
`context Order`

### Step 2 — Write the Invariant
$$
\text{inv: } \text{self.lineItems->notEmpty()}
$$

### Step 3 — Add the Sum Constraint

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{inv TotalMatch:} \quad &\text{self.total} = \text{self.lineItems->collect(price)->sum()}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Full OCL Code

```
context Order
inv AtLeastOneLineItem:
    self.lineItems->size() >= 1

inv TotalMatchesSum:
    self.total = self.lineItems->collect(price)->sum()
```

### Step 5 — Explanation (for the KTU answer)
- The first invariant guarantees *cardinality*.
- The second invariant guarantees *consistency* between the order’s total and the sum of its parts.
- Both are *side-effect-free* — they do not modify the model.
- They are evaluated by an OCL tool at any time the model is checked.

---

## 3.7 Derivation — Deciding Aggregation vs. Composition

> **Decision Rule (Memorize This):**

$$
\text{Composition is appropriate} \iff (\text{part's lifetime is fully bounded by whole's lifetime})
$$

| Scenario | Use | Why |
|----------|-----|-----|
| `Department` — `Professor` | **Aggregation** | A professor can transfer to another department. |
| `House` — `Room` | **Composition** | A room cannot exist outside the house. |
| `Company` — `Employee` | **Aggregation** | Employees may leave the company. |
| `Order` — `OrderLineItem` | **Composition** | Deleting an order should delete its line items. |
| `Library` — `Book` | **Aggregation** | Books belong to the library but a book can be transferred. |
| `File` — `Folder` | **Composition** | Files cannot exist outside a folder. |
| `Car` — `Engine` | **Composition** (in many views) | Engine is destroyed with the car. |
| `Team` — `Player` | **Aggregation** | Players can move between teams. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often mark every “whole–part” relationship as Composition. The **correct discriminator is lifetime dependency**, not mere containment.

---

## 3.8 Architectural Mapping — MOF Metalevels (Worked Out)

Let us derive the relationships between the four metalevels with a concrete example.

| Level | What it is | Concrete Example |
|-------|-----------|------------------|
| **M3** | MOF — defines the structure of metamodels | `Class`, `Attribute`, `Association` (these are *meta-classes* in MOF) |
| **M2** | UML Metamodel — the definition of UML | The UML specification document; `UML Class` is a metaclass here. |
| **M1** | User Model | A specific `class Customer { name: String }` in a project |
| **M0** | Instance / Run-time | The actual object `c1 = new Customer("Rahul")` at runtime |

> *“Each instance-of relationship goes upward by exactly one level.”*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{c1 (M0)} &\;\texttt{instanceOf}\; \text{Customer (M1)} \\
\text{Customer (M1)} &\;\texttt{instanceOf}\; \text{UML.Class (M2)} \\
\text{UML.Class (M2)} &\;\texttt{instanceOf}\; \text{MOF.Class (M3)}
\end{aligned}
$$

This strict, well-formed chain is what makes UML **machine-readable** and **tool-processable**.

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> All diagrams below use **Mermaid v10+ syntax** and follow the Node Identifier Alpha Rule (every node ID is purely alphanumeric and prefixed with letters, never using reserved keywords like `end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style` as standalone node names).

## 4.1 UML Architecture — Four Metalevels

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M3["M3 - MOF METAMETAMODEL<br/>Defines the structure of metamodels"]
    M2["M2 - UML METAMODEL<br/>The UML specification itself"]
    M1["M1 - USER MODEL<br/>Your projects class diagram"]
    M0["M0 - INSTANCE LAYER<br/>Real runtime objects in memory"]
    M3 -- "instantiates" --> M2
    M2 -- "instantiates" --> M1
    M1 -- "instantiates" --> M0
```

## 4.2 UML Building Blocks — Master Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph THINGS["THINGS - The Nouns"]
        T1["Structural Things<br/>Class, Object, Interface<br/>Component, Node, Artifact"]
        T2["Behavioral Things<br/>Interaction, State Machine"]
        T3["Grouping Things<br/>Package"]
        T4["Annotation Things<br/>Note, Constraint"]
    end
    subgraph REL["RELATIONSHIPS - The Connectors"]
        R1["Association"]
        R2["Aggregation"]
        R3["Composition"]
        R4["Generalization"]
        R5["Realization"]
        R6["Dependency"]
    end
    subgraph DIAG["DIAGRAMS - The Views"]
        D1["Structure Diagrams<br/>Class, Object, Component,<br/>Deployment, Package,<br/>Composite Structure, Profile"]
        D2["Behavior Diagrams<br/>UseCase, Activity, State,<br/>Sequence, Communication,<br/>Interaction Overview, Timing"]
    end
    THINGS --> DIAG
    REL --> DIAG
```

stateDiagram-v2

## 4.7 Sequence Diagram for ATM Withdrawal (Section 3.5 Derived)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    actor USER as User
    participant ATM as ATM
    participant BANK as BankServer
    participant DISP as CashDispenser
    USER ->> ATM : insertCard()
    ATM ->> USER : requestPIN()
    USER ->> ATM : enterPIN(1234)
    alt PIN correct
        ATM ->> BANK : validatePIN(cardNo, 1234)
        BANK -->> ATM : PIN_OK
        ATM ->> USER : showMenu()
        USER ->> ATM : selectWithdraw()
        ATM ->> USER : requestAmount()
        USER ->> ATM : enterAmount(5000)
        alt balance sufficient
            ATM ->> BANK : debit(account, 5000)
            BANK -->> ATM : debited
            ATM ->> DISP : dispense(5000)
            DISP -->> ATM : done
            ATM ->> USER : dispenseCash(5000)
            ATM ->> USER : ejectCard()
        else insufficient balance
            ATM ->> USER : showError(insufficient)
        end
    else PIN incorrect
        ATM ->> USER : retainCard()
    end
```
