# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> All questions below are modeled on the **KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examination (ESE)** pattern: Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks with internal choice).

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List and briefly explain the four purposes of UML.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
According to the OMG specification, UML serves four purposes:
1. **Visualize** — Renders the system in a clear, graphical form so all stakeholders can see the structure and behaviour.
2. **Specify** — Builds precise, unambiguous models that describe the structure and behaviour of the system.
3. **Construct** — Provides enough information to allow forward engineering (code generation) and reverse engineering (model recovery from code).
4. **Document** — Captures requirements, design decisions, and architectural information for maintenance and certification.

*(Each point carries 0.75 marks — 1 mark for the introduction / 1 mark for the list / 1 mark for the brief explanation.)*

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between aggregation and composition in UML with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Aggregation | Composition |
|--------|-------------|-------------|
| Notation | Hollow diamond on the whole | Filled diamond on the whole |
| Strength | Weak whole–part | Strong whole–part |
| Lifetime | Part may outlive whole | Part dies with whole |
| Example | Department ◇── Professor | Order ◆── LineItem |

- **Aggregation:** A `Department` aggregates `Professor` objects. A professor may transfer to another department — so the professor’s lifetime is *not* tied to the department.
- **Composition:** An `Order` is composed of `LineItem` objects. If the order is deleted, all its line items must also be deleted — they cannot exist independently.

*(1 mark each for the table row contents + 1 mark for the example.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **Note:** KTU ESE Part B questions in the 14-mark slot always have an internal choice — you must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### Question A. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(14 Marks)**

**“Unified Modeling Language is a language, not a methodology.” Critically analyze this statement and discuss the architecture, building blocks, and the 14 diagram types of UML 2.x.**

#### (a) UML is a Language, not a Methodology — Critical Analysis (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

UML is fundamentally a **visual modeling language** standardized by the **Object Management Group (OMG)**. It provides a set of notations (symbols), semantics (meaning), and rules for constructing models. It does **not** prescribe *how* you should perform analysis, design, and implementation — that is the role of a **methodology** (e.g., Rational Unified Process, Agile, Scrum).

A *methodology* defines the **phases, workflows, deliverables, and activities** required to build a system, while a *language* provides the **vocabulary and grammar** for describing the system.

- **Analogy:** UML is to a software engineer what *English* is to a writer. English provides the words and grammar; a writing methodology (e.g., “write an outline first”) is a separate process. UML, similarly, can be used with RUP, Agile, Waterfall, or any other methodology.
- **Implication:** Because UML is methodology-agnostic, it can be used in **any** development process, making it universally applicable across industries.

**Why this matters in KTU:** The question tests whether you understand the *boundary* between notation and process — a classic OOAD distinction. Marks are awarded for: stating the language-vs-methodology difference (2), giving the OMG context (2), using an analogy (1), and listing consequences (2).

#### (b) Architecture, Building Blocks, and 14 Diagrams of UML 2.x (7 marks)

**Architecture (2 marks):** UML is defined using a four-layer metamodel hierarchy based on the MOF (Meta-Object Facility):
- **M3 – Meta-Metamodel (MOF):** Defines the structure of metamodels.
- **M2 – UML Metamodel:** The definition of UML itself (the specification).
- **M1 – User Model:** Your project’s class/use-case/sequence diagrams.
- **M0 – Instances:** The run-time objects in memory.

**Building Blocks (2 marks):** Every UML diagram is built from:
- **Things** — Structural (Class, Interface, Component, Node, Artifact, Use Case, Actor), Behavioural (Interaction, State Machine), Grouping (Package), Annotation (Note).
- **Relationships** — Association, Aggregation, Composition, Generalization, Realization, Dependency.
- **Diagrams** — 14 standardized views.

**14 Diagram Types (3 marks):**

| Structure Diagrams (7) | Behaviour Diagrams (7) |
|------------------------|--------------------------|
| Class, Object, Component, Composite Structure, Deployment, Package, Profile | Use Case, Activity, State Machine, Sequence, Communication, Interaction Overview, Timing |

---

### Question B. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(14 Marks)**

**“Design a class diagram and a sequence diagram for an Online Book Store system. The system should allow customers to register, browse books, add to cart, and place orders. The system should also handle payment processing through an external payment gateway.”**

#### (a) Class Diagram for Online Book Store (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Classes identified:** `Customer`, `Account`, `Book`, `Cart`, `CartItem`, `Order`, `OrderItem`, `Payment`, `PaymentGateway` (external).

**Step 2 — Attributes & Operations (sample):**

| Class | Key Attributes | Key Operations |
|-------|----------------|----------------|
| Customer | customerID, name, email, phone | register(), login(), placeOrder() |
| Account | username, password, status | authenticate(), updateProfile() |
| Book | bookID, title, author, price, ISBN | viewDetails(), updateStock() |
| Cart | cartID, totalAmount | addItem(), removeItem(), checkout() |
| CartItem | quantity, subtotal | — |
| Order | orderID, orderDate, status, total | placeOrder(), cancelOrder() |
| Payment | paymentID, amount, status | processPayment() |
| PaymentGateway | — | validateCard(), debit(), credit() |

**Step 3 — Relationships (with multiplicities):**
- `Customer 1 — 1 Account : has`
- `Customer 1 — * Order : places`
- `Customer 1 — 1 Cart : owns`
- `Cart 1 — * CartItem : contains` *(composition — line items die with cart)*
- `Order 1 — * OrderItem : contains` *(composition)*
- `CartItem * — 1 Book : refers to`
- `OrderItem * — 1 Book : refers to`
- `Order 1 — 1 Payment : settled by` *(composition)*
- `Payment 1 — 1 PaymentGateway : uses` *(dependency)*

**Mermaid Class Diagram:**

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Customer {
        -String customerID
        -String name
        -String email
        -String phone
        +register() void
        +login() void
        +placeOrder() void
    }
    class Account {
        -String username
        -String password
        -String status
        +authenticate() boolean
        +updateProfile() void
    }
    class Book {
        -String bookID
        -String title
        -String author
        -double price
        -String ISBN
        +viewDetails() void
        +updateStock() void
    }
    class Cart {
        -String cartID
        -double totalAmount
        +addItem() void
        +removeItem() void
        +checkout() void
    }
    class CartItem {
        -int quantity
        -double subtotal
    }
    class Order {
        -String orderID
        -Date orderDate
        -String status
        -double total
        +placeOrder() void
        +cancelOrder() void
    }
    class OrderItem {
        -int quantity
        -double subtotal
    }
    class Payment {
        -String paymentID
        -double amount
        -String status
        +processPayment() void
    }
    Customer "1" -- "1" Account : has
    Customer "1" -- "*" Order : places
    Customer "1" -- "1" Cart : owns
    Cart "1" *-- "*" CartItem : contains
    Order "1" *-- "*" OrderItem : contains
    CartItem "*" --> "1" Book : refers to
    OrderItem "*" --> "1" Book : refers to
    Order "1" *-- "1" Payment : settled by
    Payment ..> "1" PaymentGateway : uses
```

*(7 marks: 2 marks for class identification, 2 for attributes/operations, 3 for relationships with correct multiplicities.)*

#### (b) Sequence Diagram for Placing an Order (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Objects:** `Customer`, `WebApp`, `OrderService`, `PaymentGateway`, `BookDB`.

**Time-ordered messages:**
1. `Customer → WebApp : login(username, password)`
2. `WebApp → WebApp : authenticate()`
3. `WebApp → Customer : loginOK`
4. `Customer → WebApp : browseBooks()`
5. `WebApp → BookDB : fetchBooks()`
6. `BookDB → WebApp : listOfBooks`
7. `Customer → WebApp : addToCart(bookID)`
8. `Customer → WebApp : checkout()`
9. `WebApp → OrderService : createOrder(cart)`
10. `OrderService → OrderService : calculateTotal()`
11. `OrderService → WebApp : amountToPay`
12. `WebApp → Customer : confirmPayment(amount)`
13. `Customer → WebApp : confirmPayment`
14. `WebApp → PaymentGateway : processPayment(cardDetails, amount)`
15. `PaymentGateway → PaymentGateway : validateCard()`
16. `PaymentGateway → WebApp : paymentSuccess`
17. `WebApp → OrderService : confirmOrder(orderID)`
18. `OrderService → BookDB : updateStock()`
19. `OrderService → WebApp : orderConfirmed`
20. `WebApp → Customer : orderConfirmation`

**Mermaid Sequence Diagram:**

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    actor CUST as Customer
    participant WEB as WebApp
    participant OS as OrderService
    participant PG as PaymentGateway
    participant DB as BookDB
    CUST ->> WEB : login(u, p)
    WEB ->> WEB : authenticate()
    WEB -->> CUST : loginOK
    CUST ->> WEB : browseBooks()
    WEB ->> DB : fetchBooks()
    DB -->> WEB : bookList
    WEB -->> CUST : showBooks()
    CUST ->> WEB : addToCart(bookID)
    CUST ->> WEB : checkout()
    WEB ->> OS : createOrder(cart)
    OS ->> OS : calculateTotal()
    OS -->> WEB : amountToPay
    WEB -->> CUST : confirmPayment(amount)
    CUST ->> WEB : confirmPayment
    WEB ->> PG : processPayment(card, amount)
    PG ->> PG : validateCard()
    PG -->> WEB : paymentSuccess
    WEB ->> OS : confirmOrder(orderID)
    OS ->> DB : updateStock()
    OS -->> WEB : orderConfirmed
    WEB -->> CUST : orderConfirmation
```

*(7 marks: 2 marks for objects/lifelines, 3 marks for ordered messages, 1 mark for alt/loop fragment, 1 mark for correct use of return arrows.)*

---

## 5.3 Common 7-Mark Sub-Questions Frequently Asked in KTU ESE

1. **Differentiate between association, aggregation, and composition** with a class diagram. *(Most common — appears in almost every KTU paper.)*
2. **Explain the four-layer metamodel architecture of UML.** *(M0 to M3 with example.)*
3. **Compare sequence diagrams and communication diagrams.**
4. **Discuss the role of stereotypes, tagged values, and constraints in UML extensibility.**
5. **Write OCL constraints** for: (i) unique email ID; (ii) age ≥ 18; (iii) total order amount calculation.
6. **Draw a state machine diagram** for: ATM, login authentication, traffic light, or order lifecycle.
7. **Distinguish between `<<include>>` and `<<extend>>` in use case diagrams.**

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner’s Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks — Examiner’s Pet Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Confusing aggregation with composition** — Students draw a *filled* diamond for *every* “has-a” relationship. The correct discriminator is *lifetime dependency*, not mere containment. A `Department–Professor` link is **aggregation**, not composition.
>
> 2. **Missing multiplicities** — A class diagram without multiplicities on every association loses 2–3 marks instantly. KTU examiners *always* expect `1`, `*`, `0..1`, or `1..*` on **both ends** of every relationship.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the difference between `<<include>>` and `<<extend>>`** — Many students mark every dependency as `<<extend>>`. The rule: **`<<include>>` is mandatory and always executes; `<<extend>>` is optional and conditional.**
>
> 4. **Drawing messages in the wrong order in sequence diagrams** — Time flows **downward**. Messages below must be later in time. KTU examiners mark time-ordering strictly.
>
> 5. **Missing OCL `context` clause** — An OCL constraint without a `context` declaration is incomplete. Always write `context <TypeName>` first.
>
> 6. **Confusing Activity Diagram with State Machine Diagram** — Activity diagrams show *flow of control across multiple objects*; State Machine diagrams show *lifecycle of a single object*. Do not draw decisions in a state machine unless the object itself branches.
>
> 7. **Naming the wrong UML architects** — UML was developed by **Grady Booch, James Rumbaugh, and Ivar Jacobson** (the *Three Amigos*). Writing OMT or OOSE as the developer’s name loses marks.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **UML = Unified Modeling Language**, a *visual language* (not a methodology), standardized by the **OMG** under **ISO/IEC 19505**.
- **Four purposes:** Visualize, Specify, Construct, Document.
- **Three building blocks:** Things, Relationships, Diagrams.
- **Four metalevels:** M0 (instance) → M1 (user model) → M2 (UML metamodel) → M3 (MOF).
- **14 diagram types** — 7 structure + 7 behavior.
- **Six relationships** — Association, Aggregation (hollow diamond), Composition (filled diamond), Generalization (hollow triangle), Realization (dashed triangle), Dependency (dashed arrow).
- **Multiplicity notation:** `1`, `0..1`, `*`, `1..*`, `n..m`.
- **Visibility:** `+` public, `-` private, `#` protected, `~` package.
- **Extensibility triad:** Stereotype, Constraint, Tagged Value.
- **OCL keywords:** `context`, `inv`, `pre`, `post`, `self`, `->`, `let...in`.
- **Use case relationships:** `<<include>>` (mandatory reuse), `<<extend>>` (optional reuse).
- **Sequence diagram message types:** synchronous (filled arrow), asynchronous (open arrow), return (dashed open arrow), self-message (loopback).
- **Combined fragments:** `alt`, `opt`, `loop`, `par`, `neg`, `ref`.
- **State machine notation:** initial = filled circle, final = bullseye, transition = `event [guard] / action`.
- **Component diagram notations:** component (rectangle with two tabs), provided interface (lollipop), required interface (socket).
- **Deployment diagram notations:** node (3D cube), artifact (document), execution environment (cube with `<<executionEnvironment>>`).
- **UML 1.x vs 2.x:** *Collaboration Diagram* is renamed to **Communication Diagram** in UML 2.x; *Profile Diagram* is new in UML 2.x.
- **Aggregation vs Composition decision rule:** Composition ↔ Part’s lifetime is fully bounded by Whole’s lifetime.
- **Memory trick for diamonds:** *“Empty diamond = Empty-handed (part is free); Filled diamond = Filled with glue (part is permanent).”*
- **Memory trick for `<<include>>` vs `<<extend>>`:** *“Include = cannot live without you; Extend = sometimes, but not always.”*
- **UML creators:** **Grady Booch, James Rumbaugh, Ivar Jacobson** — the **Three Amigos** of OOAD.

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Advanced Models: Unified Modeling Language (UML)**
> *— KTU 2024 Scheme | PBCNT604 — Object Oriented Modeling and Design*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Suggestions on Project Topics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCNT604 - OBJECT ORIENTED MODELING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module - 4
> - **Topic:** Suggestions on Project Topics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **PBCNT604 – Object Oriented Modeling and Design (OOMD)** under the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, the topic *"Suggestions on Project Topics"* refers to a curated list of application-oriented software engineering projects that allow students to apply the **Unified Modeling Language (UML 2.5)**, **Object-Oriented Analysis and Design (OOAD)** principles, and the **Unified Software Development Process** to build a complete, working, and well-documented software system. These projects form the practical backbone of the OOMD curriculum, integrating all four modules: Object Modeling, Dynamic Modeling, Functional Modeling, and System Design.

A "project topic" in OOMD is a real-world problem statement that is **decomposed, analyzed, designed, and implemented** using OO concepts such as **encapsulation, inheritance, polymorphism, abstraction, and modularity**, along with design patterns and architectural styles.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** As per the revised Outcome-Based Education (OBE) curriculum, the project is mapped to **Course Outcome CO4 / CO5** depending on the module, and the project evaluation carries significant weightage in the Continuous Internal Evaluation (CIE) and End Semester Examination (ESE) components.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have just finished learning all the **tools of a master carpenter** — the saws, hammers, chisels, sanders, and measuring tools. Knowing each tool individually is necessary, but it is **insufficient** to prove mastery. The carpenter must build a **complete piece of furniture** — a chair, a table, a wardrobe — to demonstrate the integrated use of every tool.

Similarly, in OOMD, the project topic is the **"furniture"** that the student (carpenter) must build using UML diagrams (tools). The project topic selection is thus the **first strategic decision** that determines whether the student can showcase the entire OOMD lifecycle — from requirements (Use Case Diagram) to deployment (Component/Deployment Diagram).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** A good OOMD project must traverse through **all 5 UML views** — User view, Structural view, Behavioral view, Implementation view, and Environment view — making the choice of project critical.

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of a Valid OOMD Project

| Pillar | Description | UML Artifact Used |
|---|---|---|
| **Domain Complexity** | The problem must have at least **8–12 classes** with realistic relationships | Class Diagram |
| **Dynamic Behavior** | Must have at least **3–4 key scenarios** that change state | Sequence / State Chart / Activity Diagram |
| **Functional Flow** | Must demonstrate data transformation and process flow | Data Flow Diagram (DFD), Activity Diagram |

A project lacking any one of these pillars is considered **academically weak** for KTU evaluation purposes.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Criteria Sheet

## 2.1 Selection Criteria for a Strong OOMD Project

A KTU-quality OOMD project is judged on the following **5 parameters**, each of which the student must address consciously during topic selection.

### 2.1.1 Domain Relevance (Weight: 20%)
The chosen topic must belong to a domain that **genuinely requires** object modeling. Domains like:
- Banking & Finance
- Healthcare Management
- E-Commerce
- Library/Inventory Systems
- Transportation & Logistics
- Education/Learning Management

are considered **high-relevance** because they have clear *boundaries, actors, persistent entities, and business rules*.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Heuristic:** If your project can be solved with a single flat table in Excel, it is **NOT** a good OOMD project.

### 2.1.2 Object Density (Weight: 25%)
The problem must naturally yield **at least 8 concrete classes** and **2–3 abstract/interface classes**. The class diagram should display:
- Generalization (Inheritance)
- Association, Aggregation, Composition
- Multiplicity (1..1, 1..*, *..*)
- Dependency relationships

### 2.1.3 Dynamic Depth (Weight: 20%)
There must be at least **one state-dependent object** (e.g., Order: Placed → Confirmed → Shipped → Delivered) and **at least three complex sequence flows** that involve conditional branching, loops, or concurrent actors.

### 2.1.4 Architectural Maturity (Weight: 20%)
The system should be designable using a recognized **architectural pattern** such as:
- **MVC (Model-View-Controller)** — for interactive applications
- **Layered Architecture** — for enterprise systems
- **Microservices** — for distributed systems
- **Client-Server** — for network applications

### 2.1.5 Implementation Feasibility (Weight: 15%)
The project must be **implementable in a 16-week semester window** by a team of 3–4 students, using languages like **Java, Python, C++, or C#** and a database like **MySQL, MongoDB, or PostgreSQL**.

## 2.2 KTU Project Topic Cheat Sheet — High-Yield Categories

| Category | Example Topics | Minimum UML Diagrams Expected | Recommended Architecture |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Management Systems** | Library Mgmt, Hospital Mgmt, Hostel Mgmt | UC, Class, Seq, State, Activity, Component, Deployment | Layered (3-tier) |
| **E-Commerce Platforms** | Online Shopping, Food Delivery, Auction System | UC, Class, Seq, State, Communication | MVC |
| **Reservation Systems** | Railway, Flight, Movie Ticket Booking | UC, Class, Seq, State, Timing, Component | Client-Server |
| **Workflow Systems** | Leave Approval, Loan Processing, Recruitment | UC, Activity, State, Class, Seq | Layered with State Machine |
| **Educational Tech** | LMS, Online Exam, Attendance Tracker | UC, Class, Seq, State, Package | MVC |
| **IoT / Embedded** | Smart Home, Traffic Control, Wearable Health | UC, Component, Deployment, Class | Event-driven |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Condition:** A project must use **at least 7 out of 14 UML 2.5 diagrams** to be considered comprehensive under KTU 2024 evaluation norms.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the **software industry**, the ability to translate a vague real-world problem into a structured OO model is the **single most-valued skill** in a software architect's toolkit. The KTU OOMD project trains students in this exact translation process. The same methodology is used by:
- **TCS, Infosys, Wipro** during their initial training programs
- **ISRO, DRDO** for embedded system modeling
- **Startups** for MVP (Minimum Viable Product) design

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix — How to Evaluate a Project Topic

Since this topic does not involve mathematical derivations, this section provides a **rigorous step-by-step analytical framework** that students must apply to *evaluate, select, and execute* a project topic.

## 3.1 The 7-Step Topic Selection Methodology

### Step 1: Domain Brainstorming
List **5 candidate domains** that interest you and have real-world applicability. For each, write a one-sentence problem statement.

> Example: *"Patients struggle to book specialist appointments across multiple hospitals in a city."*

### Step 2: Actor Identification
Identify the **primary, secondary, and external actors**. A healthy project has **at least 4 distinct actors**.

> Example Actors: Patient, Doctor, Receptionist, Admin, Payment Gateway (external).

### Step 3: Use Case Enumeration
Enumerate **8–12 use cases** that cover the actor interactions. Group them using `<<include>>` and `<<extend>>` stereotypes.

### Step 4: Class Identification (Noun Extraction)
Apply the **Noun-Phrase Analysis** technique on the problem statement. Filter out:
- Synonyms (retain one)
- Ambiguous nouns
- Nouns outside system scope (actors)
- Attributes masquerading as classes

> Example: From *"The library lends books to members and tracks fines"* → Classes: Library, Book, Member, Loan, Fine.

### Step 5: Relationship Mapping
For every pair of classes, determine the relationship:
- **"is-a"** → Generalization
- **"has-a" (strong ownership)** → Composition
- **"has-a" (weak ownership)** → Aggregation
- **"uses-a"** → Dependency
- **"knows-about"** → Association

### Step 6: Dynamic Scenario Selection
Pick **3 critical scenarios** (e.g., "Place Order", "Cancel Booking", "Generate Report") and develop full **Sequence Diagrams** for each, identifying object lifelines, messages, and activations.

### Step 7: State Modeling
Identify the object whose lifecycle is **most complex** (e.g., Order, Ticket, Appointment) and build its **State Chart Diagram** with at least **4 states and 6 transitions**.

## 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix — Topic Comparison

Below is an evaluation matrix that a student can use to compare multiple project topics. The score is on a **scale of 1–5** (5 = best).

$$
\text{Project Score} = \sum_{i=1}^{5} w_i \cdot s_i
$$

Where:
- $w_i$ = weight of criterion $i$ (from Section 2.1)
- $s_i$ = score (1–5) awarded to the topic
- Maximum possible score = **5.0**

> [!NOTE]
> **Recommended Threshold:** A score $\geq 4.0$ is considered a strong OOMD project topic for KTU 2024 evaluation.

### Sample Evaluation Table

| Criterion | Weight $w_i$ | Library Mgmt (score) | Online Shopping (score) | Smart Home IoT (score) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Domain Relevance | 0.20 | 4 | 5 | 4 |
| Object Density | 0.25 | 4 | 5 | 3 |
| Dynamic Depth | 0.20 | 3 | 5 | 4 |
| Architectural Maturity | 0.20 | 4 | 5 | 5 |
| Implementation Feasibility | 0.15 | 5 | 3 | 2 |
| **Weighted Total** | **1.00** | **3.95** | **4.65** | **3.55** |

### Interpretation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Library Mgmt Score} &= (0.20)(4) + (0.25)(4) + (0.20)(3) + (0.20)(4) + (0.15)(5) \\
&= 0.80 + 1.00 + 0.60 + 0.80 + 0.75 \\
&= \mathbf{3.95}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Online Shopping Score} &= (0.20)(5) + (0.25)(5) + (0.20)(5) + (0.20)(5) + (0.15)(3) \\
&= 1.00 + 1.25 + 1.00 + 1.00 + 0.45 \\
&= \mathbf{4.70}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** *Online Shopping* scores **4.70**, making it the strongest candidate by this analytical method. The student should pick topics scoring **≥ 4.0**.

## 3.3 Mapping of Project Phases to KTU Modules

| Project Phase | KTU Module | UML Artifact Produced |
|---|---|---|
| Requirements Gathering | Module 1 | Use Case Diagram |
| Object Modeling | Module 2 | Class Diagram, Object Diagram |
| Dynamic Modeling | Module 3 | Sequence, Collaboration, State Chart, Activity Diagram |
| Functional Modeling | Module 3 | Data Flow Diagram (DFD) |
| System Design & Implementation | Module 4 | Component, Deployment, Package Diagrams |

This alignment is **critical** because KTU evaluators will look for **explicit module-wise traceability** in the project report.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Project Topic Evaluation Workflow

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the complete workflow a student should follow from **topic ideation to project submission**, mapped to the OOMD lifecycle.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Domain Brainstorm] --> B[Identify Candidate Topics]
    B --> C[Apply 7-Step Selection Method]
    C --> D{Score >= 4.0?}
    D -- No --> B
    D -- Yes --> E[Finalize Project Title]
    E --> F[Module 1: Requirements Analysis]
    F --> G[Use Case Diagram + SRS Doc]
    G --> H[Module 2: Object Modeling]
    H --> I[Class Diagram + Object Diagram]
    I --> J[Module 3: Dynamic Modeling]
    J --> K[Sequence + State Chart + Activity]
    K --> L[Module 4: System Design]
    L --> M[Component + Deployment + Package]
    M --> N[Implementation Phase]
    N --> O[Testing Phase]
    O --> P[Project Report + Presentation]
    P --> Q[End: KTU Evaluation]
```

## 4.2 Typical Architecture of a KTU OOMD Project

This block diagram shows the **3-tier Layered Architecture** that the majority of KTU OOMD projects follow. It satisfies the *Architectural Maturity* criterion.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph tier1[Presentation Tier]
        UI1[Web Browser Interface]
        UI2[Mobile App Interface]
    end
    subgraph tier2[Business Logic Tier]
        CTRL[Controller / Servlet]
        SVC1[User Service]
        SVC2[Order Service]
        SVC3[Payment Service]
    end
    subgraph tier3[Data Tier]
        DB1[(MySQL Database)]
        CACHE[(Redis Cache)]
    end
    UI1 --> CTRL
    UI2 --> CTRL
    CTRL --> SVC1
    CTRL --> SVC2
    CTRL --> SVC3
    SVC1 --> DB1
    SVC2 --> DB1
    SVC3 --> DB1
    SVC2 --> CACHE
```

## 4.3 UML Diagram Coverage Checklist

The following diagram provides a **visual coverage map** of all UML diagrams a comprehensive OOMD project should contain. The shaded nodes are **mandatory**; the rest are **recommended**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph M1[Module 1]
        UC[Use Case Diagram]
    end
    subgraph M2[Module 2]
        CD[Class Diagram]
        OD[Object Diagram]
        PKG[Package Diagram]
    end
    subgraph M3[Module 3]
        SD[Sequence Diagram]
        COL[Collaboration Diagram]
        ST[State Chart Diagram]
        AC[Activity Diagram]
    end
    subgraph M4[Module 4]
        CMP[Component Diagram]
        DEP[Deployment Diagram]
    end
    M1 --> M2
    M2 --> M3
    M3 --> M4
    style UC fill:#FFD700
    style CD fill:#FFD700
    style SD fill:#FFD700
    style ST fill:#FFD700
    style AC fill:#FFD700
    style CMP fill:#FFD700
    style DEP fill:#FFD700
    style OD fill:#90EE90
    style COL fill:#90EE90
    style PKG fill:#90EE90
```

> **Legend:** *Yellow nodes* = Mandatory diagrams; *Green nodes* = Recommended additional diagrams.

## 4.4 Domain-to-Project Mapping Matrix (Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DOMAINS[Application Domains]
        D1[Healthcare]
        D2[Banking]
        D3[Education]
        D4[Retail]
        D5[Transport]
        D6[Government]
    end
    subgraph PROJECTS[Suggested Project Topics]
        P1[Hospital Mgmt System]
        P2[Loan Processing System]
        P3[Learning Mgmt System]
        P4[E-Commerce Platform]
        P5[Flight Booking System]
        P6[E-Governance Portal]
    end
    D1 --- P1
    D2 --- P2
    D3 --- P3
    D4 --- P4
    D5 --- P5
    D6 --- P6
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Q: List any four criteria that should be considered while selecting a project topic for an Object Oriented Modeling and Design course.**

**Model Answer (4 points × ~0.75 marks each):**

1. **Domain Relevance:** The problem should belong to a real-world domain that genuinely requires object modeling (e.g., banking, healthcare), not a trivial flat-data problem.
2. **Object Density:** The topic must yield at least 8–10 classes with clear relationships (inheritance, association, composition, aggregation).
3. **Dynamic Depth:** The system must have state-dependent objects and complex interactions requiring sequence and state chart diagrams.
4. **Architectural Maturity:** The project should be implementable using a recognized pattern such as MVC, Layered, or Client-Server architecture.

*(A 5th acceptable point: Implementation Feasibility — must be completable in one semester by a 3–4 member team.)*

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Q: Differentiate between a "Management System" type project and a "Real-Time/Embedded" type project in terms of UML diagram usage.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Management System | Real-Time / Embedded System |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Diagrams** | Use Case, Class, Sequence, Activity | Component, Deployment, State Chart |
| **Architectural Focus** | Layered (3-tier) | Event-driven, Client-Server with sensors |
| **Object Behavior** | Transactional, CRUD-heavy | State-machine driven, time-triggered |
| **Implementation Stack** | Java/Python + MySQL | C/C++ + RTOS + Hardware interfaces |
| **Use Case Volume** | High (8–12 use cases) | Moderate (4–6 use cases) but complex |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students often **submit a project report that is a dump of code screenshots**. This earns **0 marks** for design. Always lead with **UML diagrams** and explain the design rationale.
> - A common mistake is to draw a **Class Diagram without multiplicities or relationship stereotypes**. This is treated as a **major design error** and costs up to **3–4 marks**.
> - Many students **omit the State Chart Diagram** for objects like Order, Ticket, or Loan. Since Module 3 is heavily tested, this omission can drop your score by **4–5 marks**.
> - Avoid copying diagrams from the internet. Evaluators cross-check diagram **consistency** (e.g., class names in Class Diagram must match those in Sequence Diagram).

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**Q: A team of B.Tech students is required to select a project topic for the Object Oriented Modeling and Design course (PBCNT604).**

**(a)** Discuss the **five key criteria** that should guide the selection of a good OOMD project topic. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Suggest **any one suitable project topic** and justify your choice. For the suggested topic, list the **UML diagrams** that you would prepare, mapping each to its corresponding KTU module. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question A:

#### Part (a) — 5 Key Criteria (7 marks, 1.4 marks each)

1. **Domain Relevance & Real-World Applicability:** The chosen problem must come from a domain where OO modeling offers clear advantages — i.e., the domain has well-defined entities, relationships, and business rules. Examples: banking, healthcare, e-commerce. A problem that can be solved with a single spreadsheet does not qualify.

2. **Object Density & Class Count:** The topic should naturally yield at least 8–10 candidate classes with rich relationships — generalization (inheritance), association, aggregation, composition, and dependency. This ensures a substantive Class Diagram.

3. **Dynamic Complexity:** The system should have at least one state-dependent object with 4+ states (e.g., Order: Placed → Confirmed → Shipped → Delivered → Cancelled) and 3+ complex sequence flows involving conditional messaging.

4. **Architectural Maturity:** The project must be designable using a recognized architectural style — preferably **MVC, 3-tier Layered, or Client-Server**. This demonstrates Module 4 competency in Component and Deployment diagrams.

5. **Implementation Feasibility:** The scope must be realistic for a 3–4 member team in a 16-week semester. Over-ambitious projects (e.g., full-scale Google Maps clone) often fail to demonstrate design quality due to incomplete implementation.

#### Part (b) — Suggested Project with Justification (7 marks)

**Suggested Project:** *Online Food Ordering and Delivery Management System*

**Justification (3 marks):**
- The domain has **clear actors** (Customer, Restaurant, Delivery Agent, Admin, Payment Gateway).
- The object density is **high**: classes include Customer, Restaurant, MenuItem, Order, OrderItem, DeliveryAgent, Payment, Address, Review, Cart.
- The **Order object** is inherently state-dependent, making it perfect for a State Chart Diagram.
- The system can be **architecturally modeled** as a 3-tier web application using MVC.

**UML Diagram Mapping (4 marks):**

| UML Diagram | KTU Module | Purpose in this Project |
|---|---|---|
| Use Case Diagram | Module 1 | Capture all customer/restaurant/delivery interactions |
| Class Diagram | Module 2 | Model entities like Customer, Order, Restaurant |
| Object Diagram | Module 2 | Show snapshot of a placed order |
| Sequence Diagram | Module 3 | Trace "Place Order" message flow |
| State Chart Diagram | Module 3 | Model Order lifecycle |
| Activity Diagram | Module 3 | Model restaurant order-fulfillment workflow |
| Component Diagram | Module 4 | Show modular decomposition (UI, Service, DAO) |
| Deployment Diagram | Module 4 | Show server-client distribution |

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 – Alternative]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the **role of project topic selection** in achieving the Course Outcomes (COs) of PBCNT604. Describe any **three project categories** suitable for OOMD with one example each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a **"University Course Registration System"** project, identify the **actors, use cases, and key classes**. Draw a high-level **Use Case Diagram description** and a **Class Diagram description** in textual/narrative form. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question B:

#### Part (a) — Role in CO Achievement & 3 Project Categories (7 marks)

**Role in CO Achievement (3 marks):**
The OOMD project topic serves as the **integrative vehicle** that allows students to demonstrate mastery of all Course Outcomes:
- **CO1–CO2** (Understanding OO concepts, UML notation) → demonstrated via Class & Use Case Diagrams.
- **CO3** (Dynamic modeling) → demonstrated via Sequence, State, Activity diagrams.
- **CO4–CO5** (System design, architectural thinking) → demonstrated via Component & Deployment diagrams.

Without a well-chosen project, students cannot produce evidence of CO attainment, which affects both CIE and ESE scores.

**Three Project Categories (4 marks, ~1.3 marks each):**

1. **Management Information Systems** — e.g., *Hostel Allocation Management System*. These projects emphasize CRUD operations, layered architecture, and clear actor hierarchies.
2. **Reservation / Booking Systems** — e.g., *Movie Ticket Booking System*. These emphasize state machines (Ticket lifecycle), concurrency considerations, and time-based constraints.
3. **Workflow Automation Systems** — e.g., *Employee Leave Approval System*. These emphasize activity flows, role-based access, and decision branching — ideal for Activity and State Chart diagrams.

#### Part (b) — University Course Registration System (7 marks)

**Actors (2 marks):**
- **Primary:** Student, Faculty Advisor, Admin
- **Secondary:** Registration Office Staff
- **External:** Payment Gateway, Email Notification System

**Key Use Cases (2 marks):**
- *Register for Course*
- *Drop Course*
- *View Timetable*
- *Approve Registration* (Faculty)
- *Generate Report* (Admin)
- *Make Payment*
- *Send Notification* (External)
- *Login/Logout*

**Key Classes (2 marks):**
- `Student`
- `FacultyAdvisor`
- `Course`
- `Registration`
- `Payment`
- `Timetable`
- `Notification`
- `Admin` (inherits from `User`)

**Textual Class Diagram Description (1 mark):**
> A *Student* `registers for` multiple *Courses* through a *Registration* object. Each *Registration* is `approved by` a *FacultyAdvisor*. A *Registration* is `associated with` a *Payment*. The *Student* has access to a *Timetable* that aggregates *Course* objects. `Student` and `FacultyAdvisor` inherit from the abstract class `User` which defines common attributes like `userId`, `name`, and `email`.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Suggestions on Project Topics**

- ✅ A **good OOMD project** must have: **8+ classes**, **3+ dynamic scenarios**, **1+ state-dependent object**, and use **at least 7 of 14 UML diagrams**.
- ✅ **Selection Criteria** (mnemonic: **DOA-I-A**): **D**omain relevance, **O**bject density, **A**rchitectural maturity, **I**mplementation feasibility, **A** (dynamic depth).
- ✅ **Recommended architectures**: Layered (3-tier), MVC, Client-Server. Avoid unstructured monolithic designs.
- ✅ **Module-wise mapping is mandatory** in the project report. Evaluators specifically look for traceability from Module 1 → 4.
- ✅ **Avoid**: (1) Projects solvable in one flat table, (2) Missing multiplicities in class diagrams, (3) Copying diagrams from the internet, (4) Omitting state chart diagrams.
- ✅ **High-scoring project domains** (in current KTU trends): Online Shopping, Hospital Management, Learning Management System, Banking & Loan Systems, Food Delivery Platforms.
- ✅ **Bonus marks** can be earned by including: Design Patterns (Observer, Factory, Strategy), Package Diagrams, and a brief discussion of GRASP principles.
- ✅ **Final formula for topic selection:**
$$
\text{Project Score} = \sum_{i=1}^{5} w_i \cdot s_i \quad ; \quad \text{Threshold} \geq 4.0
$$
- ✅ Always pair your project with a clear **problem statement**, **scope document**, and **SRS (Software Requirements Specification)** — this is a Module 1 deliverable.
- ✅ Remember that the project is a **team activity (typically 3–4 members)** under KTU regulations. Choose a topic that supports division of labor along Module boundaries.
